e.g. C C in ethylene and acetylene] SIGMA --> EX1[Example H H bond
1 sigma only
Bond order equals 1] SIGMA --> EX2[Example C C in C2H4
1 sigma plus 1 pi
Bond order equals 2] SIGMA --> EX3[Example C C in C2H2
1 sigma plus 2 pi
Bond order equals 3] PI --> EX2 PI --> EX3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"Differentiate between ionic and covalent bonding with suitable examples."** ### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Parameter | Ionic Bonding | Covalent Bonding | |:---|:---|:---| | **Mechanism** | Complete transfer of valence electrons from one atom to another (3 marks for 3 distinct correct points) | Mutual sharing of valence electron pairs between atoms | | **Electronegativity Difference** | Large, $\Delta EN \geq 1.7$ | Small, $\Delta EN < 1.7$ | | **Bond Directionality** | Non-directional (electrostatic field) | Highly directional (specific orbital overlap) | | **Examples** | NaCl, MgO, CaF₂ | CH₄, H₂O, Diamond (C), SiC | **Example Explanation (1 mark):** In NaCl, sodium donates its 3s¹ electron to chlorine (3p⁵), forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held in a face-centered cubic lattice. In CH₄, carbon shares one electron with each of four hydrogen atoms via sp³ hybrid orbitals, forming a tetrahedral molecule. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"State Fajans' rules and explain how they account for partial covalent character in ionic bonds."** ### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 mark):** Fajans' rules are qualitative guidelines that predict the degree of **covalent character** in otherwise ionic bonds, based on the **polarizing power of the cation** and the **polarizability of the anion**. **The Four Rules (2 marks):** 1. **Small cation** → High charge density → Greater polarization of anion → More covalent character 2. **Large anion** → More diffuse electron cloud → More polarizable → More covalent character 3. **High charge on cation and anion** → Greater electrostatic distortion → More covalent character 4. **Cation with non-noble gas configuration** (e.g., Cu⁺, Ag⁺ with 18 e⁻) → Stronger polarizer than noble-gas-config cations (e.g., Na⁺, K⁺) → More covalent character **Application Example:** LiI is more covalent than NaCl because Li⁺ is smaller than Na⁺ (more polarizing) and I⁻ is larger than Cl⁻ (more polarizable). --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) Define lattice energy. Derive the Born-Landé equation for the lattice energy of an ionic crystal. (7 Marks)** ### Model Solution: **Definition (2 marks):** > Lattice energy is defined as the **amount of energy released when one mole of an ionic crystalline solid is formed from its constituent gaseous ions**, brought together from infinity to their equilibrium positions in the crystal lattice. $$M^{+}(g) + X^{-}(g) \rightarrow MX(s) \quad \quad \Delta H = -U$$ **Derivation (5 marks):** Consider a crystalline lattice composed of alternating cations and anions. **Step 1:** The Coulombic attractive potential energy for one ion pair: $$U_{\text{attr}} = -\dfrac{z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}$$ **Step 2:** For the entire crystal (per mole), summing over all ion pairs introduces the **Madelung constant (M)**: $$U_{\text{Coulomb}} = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}$$ **Step 3:** The **Born short-range repulsion** term (due to Pauli exclusion at close approach): $$U_{\text{rep}} = \dfrac{B}{r_0^n}$$ **Step 4:** Total energy: $$U_{\text{total}} = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0} + \dfrac{B}{r_0^n}$$ **Step 5:** Equilibrium condition $\left(\dfrac{dU_{\text{total}}}{dr_0} = 0\right)$ yields: $$B = \dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2 r_0^{n-1}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 n}$$ **Step 6:** Substituting back: $$\boxed{U = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)}$$ where $M$ = Madelung constant, $z^+, z^-$ = ionic charges, $r_0$ = equilibrium interionic distance, $n$ = Born exponent (5–12). --- **(b) Calculate the lattice energy of NaCl using the Born-Landé equation. Given: $M = 1.7476$, $r_0 = 2.82 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $n = 9$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. (7 Marks)** ### Model Solution: **Given (1 mark):** NaCl with $z^+ = +1$, $z^- = -1$, $M = 1.7476$, $r_0 = 2.82 \times 10^{-10}$ m, $n = 9$. **Step 1: Substitute into the Born-Landé equation (2 marks):** $$U = -\dfrac{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.7476)(1)(1)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2}{4 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.82 \times 10^{-10})} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)$$ **Step 2: Numerator computation (1 mark):** $$(6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.7476)(2.5664 \times 10^{-38}) = 2.7007 \times 10^{-14}$$ **Step 3: Denominator computation (1 mark):** $$4 \pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(2.82 \times 10^{-10}) = 3.1378 \times 10^{-20}$$ **Step 4: Prefactor (1 mark):** $$\dfrac{2.7007 \times 10^{-14}}{3.1378 \times 10^{-20}} = 8.6068 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J/mol}$$ **Step 5: Apply correction factor (1 mark):** $$U = -(8.6068 \times 10^{5})\left(\dfrac{8}{9}\right) = -7.65 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J/mol}$$ $$\boxed{U_{\text{NaCl}} \approx -765 \; \text{kJ/mol}}$$ The negative sign indicates that the process is **exothermic** and the lattice is energetically stable. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) Explain the formation of covalent bonds in methane (CH₄) with reference to sp³ hybridization. Discuss the geometry and bond angle. (7 Marks)** ### Model Solution: **Ground State Configuration of Carbon (1 mark):** C (Z=6): $1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^2$. Carbon has only **2 unpaired electrons** (in 2p), yet it forms 4 equivalent bonds in CH₄. This anomaly is resolved by hybridization. **Step 1: Electronic Excitation (1 mark):** One 2s electron is promoted to the empty 2p orbital: $$1s^2 \, 2s^2 \, 2p^2 \xrightarrow{+409 \; \text{kJ/mol}} 1s^2 \, 2s^1 \, 2p^3$$ **Step 2: sp³ Hybridization (2 marks):** The one 2s orbital and three 2p orbitals **mix (hybridize)** to form **4 equivalent sp³ hybrid orbitals**: $$\text{1 (2s)} + \text{3 (2p)} \rightarrow \text{4 sp}^3 \text{ hybrid orbitals}$$ Each sp³ orbital has: - **25% s-character, 75% p-character** - **One lobe large** (for bonding) and one small **Step 3: Geometry and Bond Angle (1 mark):** The four sp³ orbitals orient themselves toward the corners of a **regular tetrahedron** to minimize electron-pair repulsion (VSEPR theory). - **Bond angle:** $109.5°$ (or $109°28'$) - **Geometry:** Tetrahedral - **Symmetry:** $T_d$ **Step 4: Overlap and Bond Formation (1 mark):** Each sp³ hybrid orbital of carbon overlaps **head-on (σ overlap)** with the 1s orbital of a hydrogen atom, forming **4 equivalent C–H σ bonds** of length **109 pm** and bond energy **413 kJ/mol**. **Step 5: Net Energy Gain (1 mark):** The energy released by forming 4 strong C–H bonds (4 × 413 = 1652 kJ/mol) far exceeds the excitation energy (409 kJ/mol), making CH₄ highly stable. --- **(b) Compare the properties of ionic and covalent compounds. Discuss the percentage ionic character of a bond using Pauling's equation. (7 Marks)** ### Model Solution: **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Property | Ionic Compounds | Covalent Compounds | |:---|:---|:---| | Melting/Boiling Point | High (strong Coulombic forces) | Low to moderate (van der Waals) | | Electrical Conductivity | Conducts only in molten/aqueous state | Generally does not conduct | | Hardness | Hard but brittle | Variable (soft to very hard) | | Solubility | Polar solvents (water) | Non-polar solvents (benzene) | | Structure | Crystalline lattice | Discrete molecules or network | | Bond Directionality | Non-directional | Highly directional | **Pauling's Equation for Percentage Ionic Character (3 marks):** Pauling proposed that the **ionic character of a bond** depends exponentially on the electronegativity difference: $$\% \text{ Ionic Character} = \left(1 - e^{-\Delta EN^2/4}\right) \times 100$$ **Interpretation:** - $\Delta EN = 0 \rightarrow 0\%$ ionic → **Pure covalent** (e.g., H–H, Cl–Cl) - $\Delta EN = 1.7 \rightarrow 50\%$ ionic → **50-50 ionic-covalent** - $\Delta EN = 3.0 \rightarrow 89\%$ ionic → **Predominantly ionic** (e.g., Na–F) **Numerical Example:** For HCl: $EN_H = 2.20$, $EN_{Cl} = 3.16$, so $\Delta EN = 0.96$ $$\%IC = \left(1 - e^{-0.96^2/4}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - e^{-0.2304}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.794) \times 100 = 20.6\%$$ Thus, HCl is **20.6% ionic** and **79.4% covalent** in character — a clear demonstration that no bond is purely ionic or purely covalent. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the Born-Landé equation → Lose 1 mark. Always write $U = -(\ldots)$ to indicate exothermic stabilization. > 2. **Confusing $z^+$ and $z^-$ with $e$** → The ionic charges are dimensionless; $e$ is the elementary charge in Coulombs. > 3. **Skipping the Madelung constant** → Many students use $M = 1$ which is **wrong**. Remember $M_{NaCl} = 1.7476$. > 4. **Mixing up σ and π bonds** → In a **double bond, ONE is σ and ONE is π**; in a **triple bond, ONE is σ and TWO are π**. A common error is writing "2σ + 1π" for double bonds. > 5. **Saying "carbon has 4 unpaired electrons"** in CH₄ → The ground state has only 2 unpaired! The 4 bonds arise **after excitation and hybridization**. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 6. **Units inconsistency** → Final lattice energy must be in **kJ/mol**, not J/mol. Convert by dividing by 1000. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist:** ### **Ionic Bonding** - [ ] Driven by **complete electron transfer** from metal to non-metal; $\Delta EN \geq 1.7$ - [ ] **Three energetic steps:** Ionization Energy (IE) + Electron Affinity (EA) + Lattice Energy (U) - [ ] **Born-Landé Equation:** $U = -\dfrac{N_A M z^+ z^- e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ - [ ] **Madelung Constants (Memorize):** NaCl = 1.7476, CsCl = 1.7627, ZnS = 1.6381 - [ ] **Born Exponent (n):** 5–12; for NaCl, n = 9 - [ ] **Lattice energy is always negative** (exothermic, stabilizing) - [ ] **Fajans' Rules:** Small cation + Large anion + High charge = More covalent character - [ ] Properties: **High MP/BP, Hard, Brittle, Conducts in molten state, Soluble in polar solvents** ### **Covalent Bonding** - [ ] Driven by **electron pair sharing**; $\Delta EN < 1.7$ - [ ] **Two bond types:** σ (head-on, stronger) and π (sideways, weaker) - [ ] **Hybridization summary:** - sp → Linear, 180° - sp² → Trigonal planar, 120° - sp³ → Tetrahedral, 109.5° - sp³d → Trigonal bipyramidal, 90°/120° - sp³d² → Octahedral, 90° - [ ] **Carbon's ground state has only 2 unpaired electrons**; 4 bonds need excitation + hybridization - [ ] **Pauling's % Ionic Character:** $\%IC = (1 - e^{-\Delta EN^2/4}) \times 100$ - [ ] **Dipole moment** ($\mu = qd$) indicates bond/molecular polarity - [ ] **Network covalent solids** (diamond, SiC) have very high MP — exception to the "low MP" generalization ### **Engineering Materials to Remember** - [ ] **Ceramics** = predominantly ionic/covalent network (Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂) - [ ] **Semiconductors** = covalent (Si, Ge, GaAs) - [ ] **Polymers** = covalent (C–C, C–H backbones) - [ ] **Glasses** = mixed ionic-covalent (SiO₂ network with ionic modifiers)

topics.title: Properties Based on Atomic Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties Based on Atomic Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In materials science, the **properties based on atomic bonding** refer to the macroscopic physical, mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical characteristics of a material that emerge directly from the type, strength, and nature of the interatomic forces binding its constituent atoms together. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, atomic bonding is the foundational parameter that dictates almost every observable engineering property — from **melting point** and **modulus of elasticity** to **electrical conductivity** and **ductility**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Atomic bonding is the *root cause* of all material behavior. Whether a material is ductile or brittle, conductive or insulating, soft or hard, is fundamentally determined by the electron configuration and the resulting bond type.

The five primary bond types studied in this module are:

| Bond Type | Bonding Mechanism | Bond Energy (kJ/mol) | Example Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ionic** | Electrostatic attraction between opposite charges | 600 – 1500 | NaCl, MgO |
| **Covalent** | Shared electron pairs between adjacent atoms | 100 – 1100 | Diamond, Si, Ge |
| **Metallic** | Delocalized "sea" of valence electrons | 100 – 800 | Cu, Fe, Al, Au |
| **Van der Waals** | Transient dipoles / induced polarization | 1 – 40 | Solid Argon, Polymers |
| **Hydrogen** | Polar attraction involving H atom | 10 – 40 | H₂O, DNA, Nylon |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of atoms as people, and the bonds between them as the *glue* and *ropes* connecting them in a room:

- **Metallic bond** = a group of people all holding onto a shared *communal bag of marbles* (the electron sea). The marbles can move freely, so people can slide past each other (ductility) and the marbles can carry messages (electrical conductivity).
- **Ionic bond** = two people locked in a *rigid handshake* because one is positively charged and the other is negatively charged. They hold tight (high strength, high melting point) but if you force them to slide, the whole structure shatters (brittle).
- **Covalent bond** = people holding hands by *sharing a specific item between them* — very strong and directional. They form rigid networks (diamond) or strong chains (polymers).
- **Van der Waals** = two people standing close just because of *gentle air currents* — easy to separate (low melting point), like the soft graphite that lets its layers slide.
- **Hydrogen bond** = like a *Velcro strap* between people holding H atoms — strong for a "secondary" bond, but still much weaker than the primary ionic/covalent ropes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Stronger bonds → higher melting points, higher stiffness, lower thermal expansion. Weaker bonds → softer, more deformable, lower melting.

---

## 1.3 Bond Strength and Property Mapping

The **bond energy** (also called *cohesive energy*) is the energy required to separate two bonded atoms to infinity. It is the single most important predictor of mechanical and thermal properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Bond Energy (U):** The energy required to completely dissociate one mole of a solid into its constituent free atoms at absolute zero. It is measured in **kJ/mol** or **eV/atom** (1 eV/atom ≈ 96.485 kJ/mol).

The relationship between **bond energy** and **macroscopic properties** can be summarized by the empirical rule:

$$E \propto T_m \propto E_{modulus} \propto \frac{1}{\alpha_L}$$

where:
- $E$ = bond energy
- $T_m$ = melting temperature (K)
- $E_{modulus}$ = Young's modulus (GPa)
- $\alpha_L$ = linear coefficient of thermal expansion (K⁻¹)

This single proportionality captures why diamond (covalent, $E \approx 713$ kJ/mol) has the highest melting point and modulus, while solid argon (van der Waals, $E \approx 7.7$ kJ/mol) melts at just **84 K**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Bonding Mechanisms (Logical Breakdown)

### 2.1.1 Ionic Bonding
- **Mechanism:** Complete transfer of valence electron(s) from electropositive atom (metal) to electronegative atom (non-metal).
- **Driving Force:** Large electronegativity difference (typically $\Delta EN > 1.7$ on Pauling scale).
- **Resulting Force:** Coulombic attraction governed by Coulomb's Law.
- **Properties Consequence:**
  - High melting point (e.g., NaCl = 801 °C, MgO = 2852 °C)
  - Hard but brittle
  - Electrical insulators in solid state; conductors in molten/aqueous state
  - Optically transparent to visible light (for many ionic crystals)
  - Non-directional bonding → close-packed structures

### 2.1.2 Covalent Bonding
- **Mechanism:** Equal (or near-equal) sharing of valence electrons between two atoms.
- **Driving Force:** Mutual reduction of system energy through electron pair localization.
- **Directionality:** Highly directional — bond forms along specific crystallographic directions (e.g., $sp^3$ tetrahedral in diamond).
- **Properties Consequence:**
  - Very high melting point (diamond sublimes at ~3900 K)
  - Extremely high hardness and stiffness
  - Variable electrical behavior: insulator (diamond), semiconductor (Si, Ge)
  - Brittle in network solids; flexible in long-chain polymers

### 2.1.3 Metallic Bonding
- **Mechanism:** Valence electrons are delocalized into a "sea" or "gas" surrounding positively charged ion cores.
- **Driving Force:** Reduction in electron kinetic energy via electron delocalization; also electrostatic attraction of ions to the electron sea.
- **Non-directionality:** Electrons are smeared uniformly → bonding is non-directional.
- **Properties Consequence:**
  - Excellent electrical and thermal conductivity (free electrons transport charge/heat)
  - Ductile and malleable (ions can slide without breaking the bond)
  - Lustrous (free electrons absorb and re-emit photons of all wavelengths)
  - Wide range of melting points (Hg: −39 °C; W: 3422 °C)

### 2.1.4 Van der Waals (Secondary) Bonding
- **Mechanism:** Arises from transient or permanent dipoles — three contributions:
  1. **Dispersion (London) forces** — universal, from instantaneous dipoles
  2. **Dipole-dipole (Keesom) forces** — between permanently polar molecules
  3. **Dipole-induced dipole (Debye) forces** — between a permanent dipole and an induced one
- **Properties Consequence:**
  - Low melting points
  - Soft, mechanically weak
  - Responsible for slip between graphite layers, polymer chain slippage
  - Enable solidification of noble gases (Ne, Ar) at cryogenic temperatures

### 2.1.5 Hydrogen Bonding
- **Mechanism:** Special dipole-dipole interaction where H is bonded to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, or N), creating a strong local dipole.
- **Properties Consequence:**
  - Anomalously high boiling point of water
  - High tensile strength of biological materials (DNA, proteins)
  - Responsible for cross-linking in nylon and Kevlar

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table** — these are the high-yield formulas KTU examiners repeatedly test for Module 1.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{ionic} = -\dfrac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ | Madelung energy for ionic crystals | kJ/mol | Ionic bond strength |
| 2 | $E_{vanderWaals} = -\dfrac{A}{r^6} + \dfrac{B}{r^{12}}$ | Lennard-Jones potential | eV | Secondary bonding |
| 3 | $T_m \approx \dfrac{E_{bond}}{k_B}$ | Melting point vs. bond energy | K | Empirical, $k_B$ = Boltzmann const |
| 4 | $E_{cohesive} = \sum_i E_i \cdot N_A$ | Cohesive energy per mole | kJ/mol | Total bond energy |
| 5 | $\alpha_L \approx \dfrac{k_B}{E_{bond} \cdot a_0^3}$ | Thermal expansion vs. bond energy | K⁻¹ | Inverse proportionality |
| 6 | $\rho_{theoretical} = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_C \cdot N_A}$ | Theoretical density | g/cm³ | Crystal structure |
| 7 | $E \approx 2 G \cdot \frac{(1+\nu)}{1-2\nu} \cdot (1-\nu)$ | Elastic modulus relation | GPa | For isotropic materials |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ engineering stress | Stress | MPa | Mechanical testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Note:** Absolute value notations such as $\vert x \vert$ are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Never use plain `|` inside a table cell.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Material Used | Bonding Type | Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Titanium alloys (Ti-6Al-4V) | Metallic | High strength-to-weight |
| Cutting tools | Tungsten carbide (WC) | Covalent + Metallic | Extreme hardness |
| Electronics | Silicon (Si) wafers | Covalent (semiconductor) | Controlled conductivity |
| Cryogenics | Solid Argon / Nitrogen | Van der Waals | Low-temperature solids |
| Medical implants | Hydroxyapatite ceramics | Ionic | Biocompatibility |
| Polymer industry | Polyethylene, Nylon | Covalent (chain) + vdW (between) | Flexibility, toughness |
| Power transmission | Copper conductors | Metallic | High electrical conductivity |

---

## 2.4 Key Property Trends Across Bond Types

| Property | Ionic | Covalent | Metallic | Van der Waals |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Melting Point** | High | Very High | Variable | Very Low |
| **Hardness** | High | Very High | Variable | Very Low |
| **Ductility** | Brittle | Brittle (network) / Flexible (chains) | High | Low |
| **Electrical Conductivity (solid)** | Insulator | Insulator/Semi | Excellent | Insulator |
| **Directionality** | Non-directional | Highly directional | Non-directional | Non-directional |
| **Optical** | Transparent | Variable | Opaque, lustrous | Transparent |
| **Solubility in Water** | High (ionic) | Low | None | None |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ionic Bond Energy (Born–Landé Equation)

The lattice energy of an ionic crystal is derived from the Born–Landé equation, which accounts for the electrostatic attraction and the short-range Born repulsion.

### Step 1 — Define the Electrostatic (Coulomb) Energy

The potential energy between two point charges $z^+ e$ and $z^- e$ separated by distance $r$ is:

$$
U_{coulomb} = -\frac{z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r}
$$

This is negative because energy is *released* when oppositely charged ions come together (stable configuration).

### Step 2 — Sum Over All Ion Pairs in the Crystal

In a real crystal, each ion interacts with *all* other ions. The summation is captured by the **Madelung constant** $A$, which depends only on crystal geometry:

$$
U_{coulomb}^{total} = -\frac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r_0}
$$

For NaCl (rock-salt structure), $A = 1.748$. For CsCl, $A = 1.763$. For ZnS (zinc blende), $A = 1.638$.

### Step 3 — Add the Short-Range Born Repulsion

When electron clouds of adjacent ions overlap, strong repulsion arises. This is empirically modeled as:

$$
U_{repulsion} = \frac{B}{r^n}
$$

where $B$ is a constant and $n$ is the **Born exponent** (typically $n \approx 9$ for alkali halides, with $n = 9$ for Ne shells, $n = 10$ for Na, $n = 12$ for K).

### Step 4 — Combine the Two Contributions

$$
U_{total} = -\frac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r_0} + \frac{B}{r_0^n}
$$

### Step 5 — Minimize the Energy to Find Equilibrium

At equilibrium ($r = r_0$), $\dfrac{dU}{dr} = 0$. Solving:

$$
\frac{dU}{dr}\bigg|_{r_0} = 0 \Rightarrow B = \frac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2 \cdot r_0^{n-1}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot n}
$$

Substituting back into $U_{total}$:

$$
U_{lattice} = -\frac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r_0}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)
$$

This is the **Born–Landé equation**, the cornerstone of ionic bonding energetics.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ accounts for the Born repulsion; for $n = 9$, this factor is $8/9 \approx 0.889$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Property vs. Bond Energy

Using the proportionality $T_m \propto E_{bond}$ and $E_{modulus} \propto E_{bond}$:

| Material | Bond Type | $E_{bond}$ (kJ/mol) | $T_m$ (K) | $E$ (GPa) | $\alpha_L$ ($\times 10^{-6}$/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Diamond (C) | Covalent | 713 | 3823 (subl.) | 1000 | 1.0 |
| Tungsten (W) | Metallic | 859 | 3695 | 411 | 4.5 |
| Iron (Fe, α) | Metallic | 413 | 1811 | 211 | 11.8 |
| Copper (Cu) | Metallic | 336 | 1358 | 130 | 16.5 |
| NaCl | Ionic | 750 | 1074 | 40 | 40 |
| MgO | Ionic | 3794 | 3123 | 345 | 10.5 |
| Solid Ar | vdW | 7.7 | 84 | 2.7 | — |
| Polyethylene | Covalent+vdW | ~150 (per CH₂) | ~400 (softens) | 0.2 | 100+ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how $E$ and $T_m$ both rise with bond energy, while $\alpha_L$ falls — this confirms the inverse proportionality between bond strength and thermal expansion.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** Estimate the lattice energy of NaCl given:
- $z^+ = z^- = 1$
- $r_0 = 0.282$ nm
- Madelung constant $A = 1.748$
- Born exponent $n = 9$
- $\dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 2.307 \times 10^{-28}$ J·m

**Step 1:** Convert $r_0$ to meters: $r_0 = 0.282 \times 10^{-9}$ m

**Step 2:** Compute the Coulombic term:

$$
U_{coulomb} = -\frac{1.748 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 2.307 \times 10^{-28}}{0.282 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
U_{coulomb} = -\frac{4.033 \times 10^{-28}}{2.82 \times 10^{-10}} = -1.430 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J per ion pair}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the $(1 - 1/n)$ correction for $n = 9$:

$$
U_{lattice} = -1.430 \times 10^{-18} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right) = -1.430 \times 10^{-18} \times 0.889
$$

$$
U_{lattice} = -1.271 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J per ion pair}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to kJ/mol using Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$:

$$
U_{lattice}^{molar} = -1.271 \times 10^{-18} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = -7.654 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{lattice}^{molar} \approx -765.4 \text{ kJ/mol}}
$$

The experimental value is approximately **−787 kJ/mol**, confirming the model's accuracy. The small discrepancy arises from neglected zero-point energy and covalent character of the Na–Cl bond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Practice:** Examiners expect you to show **all** numerical steps, including unit conversions and the final Avogadro multiplication. Marks are awarded for clearly stating each substitution.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Proof: Why Strong Bonds → Low Thermal Expansion

The mean interatomic separation $r(T)$ can be approximated by the asymmetric shape of the interatomic potential $U(r)$. If $U(r) = U_0 + a(r - r_0)^2 - b(r - r_0)^3$ (asymmetric Morse-like potential), thermal vibration gives:

$$
\langle r - r_0 \rangle = \frac{3 b k_B T}{2 a^2}
$$

Since $a \propto E_{bond}$ and $b$ is roughly constant:

$$
\alpha_L = \frac{1}{r_0}\frac{d\langle r \rangle}{dT} = \frac{3 b k_B}{2 a^2 r_0} \propto \frac{1}{E_{bond}^2}
$$

This quadratic inverse dependence explains why diamond ($\alpha_L \approx 1 \times 10^{-6}$/K) has nearly **40× lower** thermal expansion than polyethylene ($\alpha_L \approx 100 \times 10^{-6}$/K).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Bond Type Decision Tree

This diagram helps students identify the dominant bond type for a given material based on electronegativity difference and electronic structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Material] --> B{Check Elemental Composition}
    B -->|Metal + Non-metal| C{EN difference > 1.7?}
    B -->|Two Non-metals| D{EN difference < 1.7?}
    B -->|Pure Metal or Alloy| E[Metallic Bonding]
    B -->|Molecule with H-F, H-O, H-N| F[Hydrogen Bonding]
    B -->|Noble gas or polymer chains| G[Van der Waals]

    C -->|Yes| H[Ionic Bonding]
    C -->|No| D

    D -->|Yes| I[Covalent Bonding]
    D -->|No, both non-metals small ATOM| I

    H --> H1[Properties: High Tm, Brittle, Insulator solid]
    I --> I1[Properties: Variable, Network Solid Strong]
    E --> E1[Properties: Conductive, Ductile, Lustrous]
    F --> F1[Properties: High Tm for secondary bond, directional]
    G --> G1[Properties: Low Tm, Soft, Insulator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrams in Mermaid** use alphanumeric node identifiers (`A`, `B`, `C1`...) and plain uppercase labels without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    PROC1 -->|High T| P1[High Bond Energy Needed]
    PROC1 -->|Conductivity| P2[Free Electrons Needed]

    PROC1 -->|Mechanical| P4[Directional Bond Control]

    P1 --> B1[Choose Covalent or Ionic]
    P2 --> B2[Choose Metallic]
    P3 --> B3[Choose Covalent Network]
    P4 --> B4[Choose Covalent Network or Metallic]

    B1 --> OUT1[Diamond, SiC, MgO, Al2O3]
    B2 --> OUT2[Cu, Al, Ag, Au]
    B3 --> OUT3[Si, Ge, GaAs, Diamond]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between primary and secondary bonds in materials. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Bonds | Secondary Bonds |
|---|---|---|
| Strength | Strong (100–1500 kJ/mol) | Weak (1–40 kJ/mol) |
| Origin | Direct electron participation | Polarization / dipole effects |
| Existence | Hold atoms together in a molecule/crystal | Hold molecules/groups together |
| Example | Covalent bond in Diamond | Van der Waals in solid Argon |
| Directionality | Directional (covalent) / Non-directional (ionic, metallic) | Non-directional |

**Examples:**
- Primary: Covalent bond in Si, Ionic bond in NaCl
- Secondary: Hydrogen bond in water, Van der Waals in polyethylene

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note (1 mark each):** Definition, Example, and the Strength comparison table.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why are metals ductile while ionic crystals are brittle? Relate your answer to atomic bonding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **metallic bonding**, valence electrons form a delocalized "sea" surrounding positively charged ion cores. When a metallic crystal is subjected to stress, one plane of ions can slide over another without breaking the bonding because the electron sea simply redistributes around the displaced ions. This gives metals their characteristic **ductility** and **malleability**.

In **ionic crystals**, the bonding is highly directional in the sense that each cation is surrounded by anions and vice versa. When a shear stress is applied, ions of like charge come into close proximity, leading to strong **electrostatic repulsion** that fractures the crystal. This makes ionic materials **brittle**.

(3 marks: 1 for metallic explanation, 1 for ionic explanation, 1 for the role of bonding in determining the property.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Born–Landé equation** for the lattice energy of an ionic crystal. Define each term clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the Born–Landé equation, calculate the **lattice energy of NaCl** in kJ/mol, given:
- Madelung constant $A = 1.748$
- Interionic distance $r_0 = 0.282$ nm
- Born exponent $n = 9$
- Product of charges $z^+ \cdot z^- = 1$
- Coulomb constant factor $\dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 2.307 \times 10^{-28}$ J·m (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

The **Born–Landé equation** gives the total lattice (potential) energy of an ionic solid:

$$
U_{lattice} = -\frac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r_0}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)
$$

**Term definitions (each term = 1 mark, with derivation context):**
- $U_{lattice}$ = Lattice energy (energy released when gaseous ions form 1 mole of solid crystal) — kJ/mol
- $A$ = **Madelung constant**, geometry-dependent (NaCl: 1.748, CsCl: 1.763, ZnS: 1.638)
- $z^+, z^-$ = Numerical charge magnitudes of cation and anion
- $e$ = Elementary charge ($1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Vacuum permittivity ($8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m)
- $r_0$ = Equilibrium interionic spacing
- $n$ = **Born exponent**, related to electronic configuration of ions (typically 5–12)
- $\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$ = **Madelung correction factor** for Born repulsion

The equation combines **Coulombic attraction** (first term) with **short-range repulsion** (correction factor).

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Substitute all values:

$$
U_{lattice} = -\frac{(1.748)(1)(1)(2.307 \times 10^{-28})}{0.282 \times 10^{-9}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{9}\right) \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Coulombic numerator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.748 \times 2.307 \times 10^{-28} = 4.033 \times 10^{-28} \text{ J·m}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Coulombic term:

$$
\frac{4.033 \times 10^{-28}}{0.282 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.430 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J per ion pair}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Born correction $(1 - 1/9) = 0.889$:

$$
U_{lattice} = -1.430 \times 10^{-18} \times 0.889 = -1.271 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J per ion pair}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to kJ/mol using $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹:

$$
U_{lattice}^{molar} = -1.271 \times 10^{-18} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = -7.654 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol}
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{lattice}^{molar} \approx -765.4 \text{ kJ/mol}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with the experimental value of approximately **−787 kJ/mol**. The small deviation (~3%) arises from zero-point vibrational energy and partial covalent character of the Na–Cl bond, which the Born–Landé model neglects.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the **properties of metals, ceramics, and polymers** based on their atomic bonding. Discuss melting point, electrical conductivity, ductility, and stiffness. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain **why graphite is a solid lubricant**, while diamond (both being pure carbon) is the hardest natural material. Relate to bonding. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

| Property | Metals (Metallic Bond) | Ceramics (Ionic/Covalent) | Polymers (Covalent chain + vdW) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Melting Point** | Moderate–High (Hg: −39°C; W: 3422°C) | Very High (Al₂O₃: 2072°C) | Low (PE: ~115°C) |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Excellent (10⁷ S/m) | Insulator (10⁻¹⁰ S/m) | Insulator (10⁻¹⁴ S/m) |
| **Ductility** | High (ions slide in electron sea) | Brittle (no slip mechanism) | Moderate (chain uncoiling) |
| **Stiffness (E)** | 70–400 GPa | 70–1000 GPa | 0.1–5 GPa |
| **Bond Type** | Non-directional electron sea | Directional (covalent) or Coulombic (ionic) | Covalent along chain, vdW between |

**Discussion:**
- **Metals** owe their ductility to non-directional metallic bonding — planes of atoms slide without bond rupture.
- **Ceramics** are stiff and brittle because ionic/covalent bonds are strong but the slip systems are constrained by charge neutrality (ionic) or bond directionality (covalent).
- **Polymers** consist of long covalently bonded chains held together weakly by van der Waals forces, leading to low stiffness and low melting points.

(7 marks: 1 for the table, 3 for the explanatory discussion, 1 for the bonding-property link, 2 for examples.)

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Diamond structure:**
- Each carbon atom is **sp³ hybridized** and forms four strong **covalent bonds** with neighbors in a **tetrahedral** arrangement.
- This creates a **3D rigid network** of bonds.
- Result: **Extremely high hardness** (~10000 HV), **highest thermal conductivity** of any natural material, and **electrical insulator** (no free electrons).
- All bond energy is concentrated in strong directional covalent links.

**Graphite structure:**
- Each carbon atom is **sp² hybridized** and forms three strong covalent bonds in a **planar hexagonal layer**.
- The fourth electron is delocalized within the layer (π-electron system).
- Layers are stacked and held together by **weak van der Waals forces** (~7 kJ/mol between layers).
- Result: **Layers can slide easily** over one another → **excellent solid lubricant**.
- It is also an **electrical conductor** (delocalized π-electrons move within layers).

**Conclusion:**
Both diamond and graphite are made of pure carbon — the dramatic property difference arises **entirely from the bonding arrangement**:
- Diamond: 3D covalent network → hard, insulating.
- Graphite: 2D covalent layers + vdW interlayer → soft, conducting, lubricating.

(7 marks: 2 marks for diamond bonding diagram + explanation, 2 marks for graphite structure, 2 marks for the comparative conclusion, 1 mark for the bonding-property link.)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Properties-from-Bonding Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the Born–Landé calculation** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if the final answer is not in kJ/mol. Always multiply by Avogadro's number explicitly.
> 2. **Conflating metallic bonding with covalent bonding** — Students often say metals share electrons. They do NOT; metals have a delocalized *sea* of electrons, not localized pairs. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not citing the Madelung constant's physical meaning** — A bare substitution of $A = 1.748$ without explaining it represents the geometry-dependent lattice sum → 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Confusing "cohesive energy" with "bond energy"** — Cohesive energy is the *total* energy to atomize the solid; bond energy refers to a *single* bond. Use the correct term based on context.
> 5. **Skipping the $(1 - 1/n)$ factor in the Born–Landé equation** — This is a frequent mistake worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Writing diamond/graphite comparison without referring to sp² vs sp³ hybridization** — A 2-mark deduction because the hybridization *is* the bonding mechanism.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

### 🎯 Core Concepts to Memorize
- ☐ Atomic bonding is the **root cause** of all material properties.
- ☐ There are **three primary bond types** (Ionic, Covalent, Metallic) and **two secondary types** (Van der Waals, Hydrogen).
- ☐ **Bond energy** is measured in **kJ/mol** or **eV/atom** (1 eV/atom = 96.485 kJ/mol).

### 🎯 Key Proportionalities
- ☐ Higher bond energy → Higher melting point.
- ☐ Higher bond energy → Higher elastic modulus.
- ☐ Higher bond energy → Lower thermal expansion coefficient.
- ☐ Higher bond energy → Higher hardness (in general).

### 🎯 Bond Type Identification Rules
- ☐ **Metal + Non-metal + ΔEN > 1.7 → Ionic.**
- ☐ **Two Non-metals with shared electrons → Covalent.**
- ☐ **Pure metal or alloy → Metallic.**
- ☐ **Inert gas / polymer chain layers → Van der Waals.**
- ☐ **H bonded to F, O, or N in a molecule → Hydrogen bond.**

### 🎯 Must-Know Examples
- ☐ Diamond (C) — Covalent network — hardest natural material.
- ☐ NaCl — Ionic — brittle, high melting point (801 °C).
- ☐ Cu — Metallic — excellent conductor, ductile.
- ☐ Graphite — Covalent layers + vdW — solid lubricant.
- ☐ Solid Ar — Van der Waals — melts at 84 K.
- ☐ H₂O — Hydrogen bonding — anomalously high boiling point.

### 🎯 Critical Formula to Memorize
- ☐ **Born–Landé equation:** $U = -\dfrac{A \cdot z^+ \cdot z^- \cdot e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \cdot r_0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)$

### 🎯 Property-Property Trends
- ☐ Ceramics: high stiffness, brittle, insulating.
- ☐ Metals: ductile, conductive, lustrous.
- ☐ Polymers: low stiffness, insulating, low melting.

### 🎯 Exam-Specific Tips
- ☐ Always include **units** in numerical answers.
- ☐ Always show **all substitution steps** in derivations.
- ☐ Always state the **physical meaning** of each term in an equation.
- ☐ Always link a **property to its bonding origin** — not just state the property.
- ☐ For diamond vs. graphite, always mention **sp² vs. sp³ hybridization**.

> **Good luck with your KTU 2024 exam preparation!** Master these concepts, and the Module 1 question on "Properties Based on Atomic Bonding" will be straightforward. 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Crystallography - SC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Crystallography** is the branch of materials science that studies the arrangement of atoms, ions, or molecules in crystalline solids. A **crystal structure** is defined as the regular, periodic, and three-dimensional arrangement of atoms (or basis of atoms) in space, where every atom is surrounded by an identical environment.

The **Simple Cubic (SC)** crystal system, also known as the **Primitive Cubic (P)** system in international crystallographic notation, is the most elementary of the 14 Bravais lattices. It forms the foundational building block of the cubic crystal family.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The SC structure is treated as the pedagogical entry-point into crystallography because it is the simplest, most symmetric cubic arrangement, allowing students to grasp lattice concepts (Bravais lattice, basis, unit cell) before progressing to BCC and FCC structures.

In a Simple Cubic lattice:
- The unit cell is a **cube** with edge length $a$ (the lattice parameter).
- Atoms (or lattice points) reside **only at the eight corners** of the cube.
- Each corner atom is shared equally among **8 adjacent unit cells**.

**The crystallographic notation** of the Simple Cubic lattice is given by the Pearson symbol **cP** (cubic Primitive) and the Strukturbericht designation **A** (in the case of α-Polonium).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Beehive Stack

Imagine a **neatly stacked pile of oranges** at a grocery shop where each orange is placed exactly at the corner of an invisible cube. Every orange touches **six other oranges** — one each above, below, left, right, front, and back. This is exactly how atoms arrange themselves in a Simple Cubic lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study SC if it's rare in nature?**
> The Simple Cubic structure is the *conceptual skeleton* of crystallography. All other cubic structures (BCC, FCC) and indeed all higher-symmetry lattices are built by **adding interior and face-centered atoms** to this primitive cube. Mastering SC first means you automatically understand the geometry of every cubic derivative.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics for Simple Cubic (SC)

| Metric | Value for SC |
|---|---|
| Lattice parameter | $a$ |
| Atomic radius | $R = a/2$ |
| Atoms per unit cell | **1** |
| Coordination Number (CN) | **6** |
| Atomic Packing Factor (APF) | **0.5236 (52.36 %)** |
| Nearest-Neighbour Distance | $2R = a$ |
| Number of 2nd Nearest Neighbours | 12 |
| Density (theoretical) | $\rho = \dfrac{n A}{V_C N_A}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** **Polonium (Po)** is the only naturally occurring element that crystallizes in the Simple Cubic structure (in its α-phase at room temperature). Most other elements adopt denser BCC or FCC packings.

---

## 1.4 The Three Foundational Concepts of Crystallography

Before diving into calculations, three terms must be crystal-clear (no pun intended):

1. **Crystal Lattice** — An infinite array of discrete mathematical points in space, arranged so that the environment (sum and orientation of vectors) at every point is identical.
2. **Basis (or Motif)** — The atom, ion, or group of atoms that is identically attached to every lattice point.
3. **Crystal Structure = Lattice + Basis** — The complete, physical 3-D arrangement of atoms in a real crystal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 14 Bravais lattices arise from the combination of **7 crystal systems** × **(primitive, base-centred, body-centred, face-centred) centring modes**, but only the cubic system possesses all four centring types: **P (Simple)**, **I (Body-centred)**, **F (Face-centred)**, and (rarely) **C**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Crystal Systems — Foundation Review

The seven crystal systems differ by the relationship between their three unit-cell edge lengths ($a$, $b$, $c$) and the three inter-axial angles ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$). The Simple Cubic belongs to the **Cubic** system, where:

$$
a = b = c \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha = \beta = \gamma = 90^\circ
$$

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Simple Cubic Unit Cell

A unit cell is the smallest repeating volume that, when translated along the three crystallographic axes, reproduces the entire crystal. For the Simple Cubic structure:

- **Vertices (corners):** 8 atoms, each shared by 8 unit cells → contribution = $8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1$ atom.
- **Faces:** 0 atoms.
- **Body centre (interior):** 0 atoms.

Therefore, the **effective number of atoms per unit cell (n)** for SC is:

$$
n_{\text{SC}} = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1
$$

## 2.3 Coordination Number (CN)

The **coordination number** is the number of nearest equidistant atoms surrounding a given atom. In the SC structure, any corner atom is in direct contact with **six** nearest neighbours:

- **+x, −x** (right and left, distance $a$)
- **+y, −y** (front and back, distance $a$)
- **+z, −z** (top and bottom, distance $a$)

$$
\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{SC}} = 6}
$$

This is the **lowest** coordination number of all cubic lattices (BCC = 8, FCC = 12, HCP = 12).

## 2.4 Atomic Radius and Lattice Parameter Relationship

Atoms are modelled as **rigid spheres** that touch along the cube edge in the SC structure. Since the cube edge contains two atomic radii (one from each corner atom sharing the edge):

$$
a = 2R \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R = \frac{a}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse the *direction* of contact. In SC, contact is along the **cube edge** $<100>$, not along the body diagonal (as in BCC) or face diagonal (as in FCC). The general rule: identify the **direction of closest packing** (highest linear density) for each structure before writing $a$ vs $R$ relationships.

## 2.5 Atomic Packing Factor (APF) — The Core Performance Metric

The **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)** is the fraction of the unit-cell volume that is actually occupied by atoms, defined as:

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms inside cell}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3}
$$

For the Simple Cubic lattice, this gives the **canonical 52.36 %** (derived step-by-step in Section 3).

$$
\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236}
$$

## 2.6 Theoretical Density Formula

The mass of a unit cell is the product of the number of atoms per cell ($n$), the atomic mass ($A$ in amu), and the mass of a unified atomic mass unit ($1 \text{ amu} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg). Dividing by the volume of the cell ($V_C = a^3$) and using Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ atoms/mol:

$$
\rho = \frac{n A}{V_C N_A} = \frac{n A}{a^3 N_A}
$$

This formula is one of the **highest-weightage formulas** in KTU Module 1 problems.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — SC Quick Reference

| # | Quantity | Formula | Value for SC | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-------------|:------|
| 1 | Lattice parameter | $a$ | given | nm, Å, pm |
| 2 | Atomic radius | $R = a/2$ | $a/2$ | nm, Å, pm |
| 3 | Atoms per unit cell | $n = 8 \times \tfrac{1}{8}$ | **1** | dimensionless |
| 4 | Coordination number | CN | **6** | dimensionless |
| 5 | Nearest-neighbour distance | $2R$ | $a$ | nm, Å, pm |
| 6 | 2nd nearest-neighbour distance | $\sqrt{2}\,a$ | $1.414\,a$ | nm, Å, pm |
| 7 | 3rd nearest-neighbour distance | $\sqrt{3}\,a$ | $1.732\,a$ | nm, Å, pm |
| 8 | Volume of unit cell | $V_C = a^3$ | $a^3$ | $\text{nm}^3$, $\text{m}^3$ |
| 9 | Volume occupied by atoms | $n \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | $\tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | $\text{nm}^3$, $\text{m}^3$ |
| 10 | Atomic Packing Factor | $\text{APF} = n \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \,/\, a^3$ | $\pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Theoretical density | $\rho = nA / (a^3 N_A)$ | — | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 12 | Packing efficiency (volumetric) | $\text{APF} \times 100\,\%$ | **52.36 %** | percent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The APF value determines the **theoretical upper bound of mass that can be packed into a given volume** for a given element. SC's 52.36 % is the *least efficient* cubic packing — that's why only one element (Po) adopts it. Materials with higher APF (FCC, HCP at 74 %) are favoured for **high-density structural applications** like aerospace counterweights, ball bearings, and armour plating.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Property Implication | Consequence for SC Materials |
|---|---|
| Low APF (52.36 %) | High porosity, low density, soft mechanically |
| Low CN (6) | Fewer bonds → low strength, low melting point |
| Open structure | Favours diffusion of guest atoms (used in some catalysts) |
| Rare in nature (only Po) | Limited engineering use; mainly pedagogical |

The **take-home engineering message** is: SC is *conceptually* indispensable but *practically* rare — yet every other crystal structure you will study in this module is a *systematic modification* of SC (atoms added to body-centre in BCC, atoms added to face-centres in FCC).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Atoms per Unit Cell of an SC Structure

A cube has **8 corners**, and each corner atom is shared by the **8 unit cells** that meet at that corner. This is because in 3-D space, three mutually perpendicular sets of parallel planes intersect at every corner, creating **$2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ identical sub-cubes**, one for each unit cell.

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total corner atoms (full count)} &= 8 \\
\text{Contribution of each corner atom} &= \frac{1}{8} \\
\text{Effective atoms in unit cell} &= 8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** $n_{\text{SC}} = 1$ atom per unit cell.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Atomic Radius ($R$) and Lattice Parameter ($a$)

The atoms are assumed to be **rigid spheres** in contact along the **cube edge** of length $a$. Two atoms (one at each end of an edge) touch along this edge, so the edge length equals the sum of the two atomic radii:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= R + R \\
a &= 2R \\
\therefore R &= \frac{a}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For SC, $R = a/2$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coordination Number of SC

Pick a corner atom at the origin $(0,0,0)$. The six nearest-neighbour atoms are located at the centres of the six cube edges that radiate outward:

- $(a, 0, 0)$ and $(-a, 0, 0)$ → along $\pm x$
- $(0, a, 0)$ and $(0, -a, 0)$ → along $\pm y$
- $(0, 0, a)$ and $(0, 0, -a)$ → along $\pm z$

The distance from the origin to each of these six atoms is exactly $a$. Therefore, the **coordination number is 6**.

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{SC}} = 6
$$

> **Verification using position vectors:** All six vectors have magnitude $\vert \vec{r} \vert = a$ (one component equals $a$, the other two are 0).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of SC

The APF derivation is the **single most important derivation** in Module 1 of PCMET205. We compute it in five explicit steps.

**Step 1 — Define APF.**

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{\text{Volume of atoms inside the unit cell}}{\text{Volume of the unit cell}} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the volume of the unit cell.**

$$
V_{\text{cell}} = a \cdot a \cdot a = a^3
$$

**Step 3 — Write the volume occupied by atoms.**

For an SC cell with $n = 1$ effective atom, modelled as a sphere of radius $R$:

$$
V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = 1 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3
$$

**Step 4 — Express $R$ in terms of $a$ using the SC relation $R = a/2$.**

$$
R^3 = \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{a^3}{8}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into APF and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} &= \frac{\dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\dfrac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \dfrac{a^3}{8}}{a^3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4\pi}{3 \times 8} \cdot \frac{a^3}{a^3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4\pi}{24} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\pi}{6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.5236 \\[6pt]
\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} \,(\%) &\approx 52.36\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Approximately **52.36 %** of the unit cell is occupied by atoms, and the remaining **47.64 %** is empty (void) space. This is the lowest APF among the three cubic structures.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Theoretical Density of a Hypothetical SC Element

**Problem statement:** An element (hypothetical) crystallizes in the SC structure. Its atomic mass is $A = 50 \text{ g/mol}$ and the lattice parameter is $a = 0.4 \text{ nm}$. Compute its theoretical density.

**Step 1 — Identify known values.**

$$
n = 1, \quad A = 50 \,\text{g/mol}, \quad a = 0.4 \,\text{nm} = 0.4 \times 10^{-9} \,\text{m}, \quad N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \,\text{mol}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the unit-cell volume.**

$$
V_C = a^3 = (0.4 \times 10^{-9})^3 = 0.064 \times 10^{-27} \,\text{m}^3 = 6.4 \times 10^{-29} \,\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the density formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{n A}{V_C N_A} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1 \times 50 \,\text{g/mol}}{6.4 \times 10^{-29} \,\text{m}^3 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \,\text{mol}^{-1}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50}{6.4 \times 6.022 \times 10^{-6}} \,\text{g/m}^3 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50}{3.8541 \times 10^{-5}} \,\text{g/m}^3 \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.297 \times 10^{6} \,\text{g/m}^3 \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.297 \,\text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The theoretical density is approximately **1.30 g/cm³**. The result is in the typical density range of light metallic elements, confirming the validity of the calculation.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SC, BCC, FCC

This **master comparison table** is the single most useful revision tool for KTU Module 1. The SC, BCC, and FCC structures form a logical progression in which interior and face-centred atoms are *added* to the basic SC framework.

| Parameter | **SC (Primitive)** | **BCC (Body-Centred)** | **FCC (Face-Centred)** |
|:----------|:------------------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------------------:|
| Atoms per cell, $n$ | $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1$ | $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} + 1 = 2$ | $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} + 6 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 4$ |
| Coordination number, CN | 6 | 8 | 12 |
| Relation $R : a$ | $R = a/2$ | $R = \sqrt{3}\,a/4$ | $R = \sqrt{2}\,a/4$ |
| Nearest-neighbour distance | $a$ | $\sqrt{3}\,a/2$ | $\sqrt{2}\,a/2$ |
| Direction of contact | cube edge $<100>$ | body diagonal $<111>$ | face diagonal $<110>$ |
| $\text{APF} = n V_{\text{atom}} / a^3$ | $\pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ | $\sqrt{3}\pi/8 \approx 0.6802$ | $\sqrt{2}\pi/6 \approx 0.7405$ |
| $\text{APF}$ in percent | **52.36 %** | **68.02 %** | **74.05 %** |
| Void fraction | 47.64 % | 31.98 % | 25.95 % |
| Example element | α-Polonium (Po) | Fe (α), Cr, W, Mo | Cu, Al, Ni, Ag, Au, γ-Fe |

> **Pattern recognition insight (KTU high-yield):** Notice that as we add atoms to the SC framework (one at the body centre in BCC, six at the face centres in FCC), the **APF monotonically increases** from 52.36 % → 68.02 % → 74.05 %. Nature prefers denser packings because they lower the total energy of the system — this is why SC is rare in nature and FCC/HCP dominate metallic bonding.

## 3.7 Worked Example — Multi-Step KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem (12-mark KTU style):** An element with atomic mass 70.0 g/mol and density 1.55 g/cm³ crystallizes in the SC structure. Compute:
(a) the lattice parameter $a$, and
(b) the atomic radius $R$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Starting from the theoretical-density formula:

$$
\rho = \frac{n A}{a^3 N_A}
$$

Rearrange for $a^3$:

$$
a^3 = \frac{n A}{\rho N_A}
$$

Substitute $n = 1$, $A = 70 \text{ g/mol}$, $\rho = 1.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$:

$$
a^3 = \frac{1 \times 70}{1.55 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \,\text{cm}^3
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
1.55 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 9.3341 \times 10^{23}
$$

So:

$$
a^3 = \frac{70}{9.3341 \times 10^{23}} = 7.498 \times 10^{-23} \,\text{cm}^3
$$

Take the cube root:

$$
a = (7.498 \times 10^{-23})^{1/3} \,\text{cm}
$$

Computing the cube root:

$$
a \approx 4.22 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{cm} = 4.22 \,\text{Å} = 0.422 \,\text{nm}
$$

**Part (b):**

Using $R = a/2$ for SC:

$$
R = \frac{0.422}{2} = 0.211 \,\text{nm} = 2.11 \,\text{Å}
$$

> **Conclusion:** $a = 0.422$ nm and $R = 0.211$ nm. Both values lie in the typical metallic-atom range, confirming the plausibility of the answer.

Use this decision tree to determine **which formula** to apply when relating $R$ to $a$ for any cubic structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    q1{"Atoms Touch Along Which Direction?"}
    ans1["Cube Edge : a = 2R : SC"]
    ans2["Body Diagonal : 4R = sqrt(3) a : BCC"]
    ans3["Face Diagonal : 4R = sqrt(2) a : FCC"]
    result["Write Final Equation for R in Terms of a"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|"Edge less than 100"| ans1
    q1 -->|"Body Diagonal less than 111"| ans2
    q1 -->|"Face Diagonal less than 110"| ans3
    ans1 --> result
    ans2 --> result
    ans3 --> result
```
