# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Part A questions are compulsory, test direct recall and definitions, and expect crisp 3–4 line answers. Always include the *numerical value* and the *physical meaning* in the same sentence.

### Question 1 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Atomic Packing Factor (APF). Calculate the APF for a Simple Cubic crystal structure.**

**Model Answer:**

The Atomic Packing Factor (APF) is defined as the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms inside a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell. Mathematically:

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3}
$$

For the **Simple Cubic** structure: $n = 1$ and $R = a/2$. Substituting:

$$
\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \frac{1 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^3}{a^3} = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236
$$

**Hence the APF of a Simple Cubic structure is $\pi/6 \approx 52.36\%$.**

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is a coordination number? State the coordination number of a Simple Cubic structure and justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

The **coordination number (CN)** of an atom in a crystal is the number of its nearest equidistant neighbouring atoms.

In a Simple Cubic structure, a corner atom is in direct contact with **six** surrounding atoms — one each along the $+x$, $-x$, $+y$, $-y$, $+z$, and $-z$ directions, all at a distance of $a = 2R$.

$$
\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{SC}} = 6}
$$

This is the lowest coordination number among cubic structures, indicating relatively inefficient packing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer **internal choice** (Module-level). Each question has two sub-parts, typically 7 + 7 marks. Show all working, label all diagrams, and box the final answer.

---

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a) Define a unit cell. With a neat sketch, describe the Simple Cubic (SC) crystal structure. List its key geometric parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Unit Cell:**
A unit cell is the smallest three-dimensional portion of a crystal lattice that, when translated along the three crystallographic axes, reproduces the entire crystal without any gaps or overlaps. Every unit cell has identical composition and atomic arrangement.

**Description of SC Structure:**
The Simple Cubic structure is the most elementary Bravais lattice. Atoms (or lattice points) are located **only at the eight corners** of a cubic cell, with no atoms on the faces or at the body centre. Each corner atom is shared by eight adjacent unit cells.

*ASCII schematic of the unit cell:*

```
        (0,0,a) ●─────────────● (a,0,a)
               /│            /│
              / │           / │
             /  │          /  │
   (0,0,0) ●─────────────● (a,0,0)
            │   │         │   │
            │   ●(0,a,0)──│───● (a,a,0)
            │  /          │  /
            │ /           │ /
            │/            │/
   (0,a,a) ●─────────────● (a,a,a)
```

**Key Geometric Parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Lattice parameter $a$ | edge length |
| Atoms per unit cell $n$ | $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1$ |
| Atomic radius $R$ | $a/2$ |
| Coordination number CN | 6 |
| Atomic packing factor APF | $\pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ (52.36 %) |
| Nearest-neighbour distance | $a$ |
| 2nd nearest-neighbour distance | $\sqrt{2}\,a$ |
| Example element | α-Polonium (Po) |

---

**(b) Derive the relationship between atomic radius and lattice parameter for a Simple Cubic structure. Hence, calculate the atomic radius of a hypothetical SC element whose lattice parameter is 0.45 nm. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

In the SC structure, atoms touch one another along the **cube edge**. Since the cube edge length is $a$ and is occupied by **two atomic radii** (one from each of the two corner atoms sharing that edge):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= R + R \\
a &= 2R \\
\therefore R &= \frac{a}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculation:**

Given $a = 0.45 \,\text{nm}$:

$$
R = \frac{0.45}{2} = 0.225 \,\text{nm} = 2.25 \,\text{Å}
$$

**Hence, the atomic radius of the given SC element is $R = 0.225 \,\text{nm}$ (or $2.25 \,\text{Å}$).**

---

### Choice 1 — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a) Calculate the Atomic Packing Factor (APF) for a Simple Cubic crystal structure step by step. Compare it with the APF values of BCC and FCC structures. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SC APF Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} &= \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{a^3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^3}{a^3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi \cdot \frac{a^3}{8}}{a^3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4\pi}{24} = \frac{\pi}{6} \\[6pt]
&\approx 0.5236 \quad \text{or} \quad 52.36\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with BCC and FCC:**

| Structure | APF Formula | Numerical Value | Percentage |
|:----------|:------------|:----------------|:-----------|
| SC | $\pi/6$ | $0.5236$ | $52.36\,\%$ |
| BCC | $\sqrt{3}\,\pi/8$ | $0.6802$ | $68.02\,\%$ |
| FCC | $\sqrt{2}\,\pi/6$ | $0.7405$ | $74.05\,\%$ |

**Inference:** The APF increases monotonically as we move from SC → BCC → FCC. FCC is the densest cubic packing (74.05 %), and this is why FCC and HCP metals (Cu, Al, Ni, Au) are widely used in engineering applications where high mass-to-volume ratio is required.

---

**(b) An element crystallizes in the Simple Cubic structure. Its atomic mass is 55.85 g/mol and its atomic radius is 0.124 nm. Calculate (i) the lattice parameter, (ii) the density, and (iii) the number of atoms per cm² on the (100) plane. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** SC structure, $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $R = 0.124$ nm.

**(i) Lattice Parameter:**

Using $a = 2R$ for SC:

$$
a = 2 \times 0.124 = 0.248 \,\text{nm} = 2.48 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{cm}
$$

**(ii) Density:**

Using $\rho = nA / (a^3 N_A)$ with $n = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{1 \times 55.85}{(2.48 \times 10^{-8})^3 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \,\text{g/cm}^3 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{55.85}{1.526 \times 10^{-23} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{55.85}{9.190} \,\text{g/cm}^3 \\[6pt]
&\approx 6.08 \,\text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Planar Density on (100) Plane:**

The (100) plane in an SC structure is a square face with four corner atoms, each shared by 4 unit cells in 2-D. Effective atoms in (100) face = $4 \times \tfrac{1}{4} = 1$ atom.

Area of (100) face = $a^2 = (2.48 \times 10^{-8})^2 = 6.1504 \times 10^{-16}$ cm².

Planar density = $1 / (6.1504 \times 10^{-16}) = 1.626 \times 10^{15}$ atoms/cm².

**Summary of results:** $a = 0.248$ nm, $\rho \approx 6.08$ g/cm³, planar density $\approx 1.63 \times 10^{15}$ atoms/cm².

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/8$ share of corner atoms:** Many students write "8 atoms per unit cell" for SC. The correct answer is **1 atom per unit cell**, since each corner is shared among 8 cells.
> 2. **Confusing the direction of atomic contact:** In SC, contact is along the **cube edge** ($a = 2R$), not the body diagonal. Writing $a = 4R/\sqrt{3}$ (the BCC relation) for an SC question will be marked **wrong**.
> 3. **Unit-inconsistency in density problems:** If $a$ is in nm, convert to cm (or m) before using Avogadro's number. KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark for unit errors.
> 4. **Skipping steps in APF derivation:** Students who jump from $V_{\text{atom}}/V_{\text{cell}}$ directly to $\pi/6$ without showing $R^3 = a^3/8$ lose 2 marks for missing logical steps.
> 5. **Mixing up SC and primitive cubic with BCC and FCC structures:** Always confirm that the question asks for SC *before* applying the $a = 2R$ relation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this **high-density rapid-revision checklist** in the final 30 minutes before the exam.

- [x] **Crystal Structure = Lattice + Basis** — lattice is the geometric skeleton, basis is the atom/group attached to every lattice point.
- [x] **Simple Cubic (SC) = Primitive Cubic (P)** — atoms only at the 8 corners of the cube.
- [x] **Atoms per unit cell:** $n_{\text{SC}} = 8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1$.
- [x] **Lattice parameter relation:** $a = 2R$ — atoms touch along the cube edge (direction $<100>$).
- [x] **Coordination number:** $\text{CN}_{\text{SC}} = 6$ — the *lowest* among cubic structures.
- [x] **APF formula:** $\text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ or **52.36 %** — derivable in 5 steps from first principles.
- [x] **Void fraction:** $1 - \text{APF} = 0.4764$ or **47.64 %** — the highest among cubic structures.
- [x] **Theoretical density:** $\rho = nA / (a^3 N_A)$ — used to compute density from lattice parameter and vice versa.
- [x] **Planar density (100):** 1 atom per $a^2$ — useful for surface-area-dependent properties.
- [x] **Example element:** α-Polonium (Po) — the only natural SC element at room temperature.
- [x] **Comparison memory aid:** SC = 1, 6, 52.36 %; BCC = 2, 8, 68.02 %; FCC = 4, 12, 74.05 %. The pattern $n$, CN, APF all increase together.
- [x] **Direction of contact rule:** edge in SC, body diagonal in BCC, face diagonal in FCC — *always* identify the closest-packing direction first.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway:** SC is rare in nature because of its low APF; FCC and HCP are preferred for high-density structural applications.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Scheme weightage:** Crystallography basics (SC, BCC, FCC) typically carry **15–20 %** of the total module marks, including 1 compulsory Part-A question and frequent appearance in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HCP Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HCP Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) Structure:** A non-cubic crystal system in which identical atoms (or ions) are arranged such that the **atomic packing fraction (APF) is maximized at 0.74**, with each atom surrounded by **12 nearest neighbors** (coordination number, CN = 12). The unit cell is a **right hexagonal prism** defined by two basal edges of length $a$ and a unique vertical edge (c-axis) of length $c$, with the inter-axial angle between basal edges being $\gamma = 120°$ and all other angles being $90°$.

The **lattice parameters** for an HCP system are:
- $a = b \neq c$
- $\alpha = \beta = 90°$, $\quad \gamma = 120°$

This places HCP in the **Hexagonal Crystal Family**, distinct from the Cubic Family (which contains BCC and FCC).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honeycomb Coin Stack"

Imagine stacking identical coins (representing atoms) on a flat table in the most efficient manner possible:

- **Step 1 — First Layer:** Arrange coins in a tight triangular array, each coin touching **six neighbors**. This is a **2D close-packed plane (basal plane)**.
- **Step 2 — Second Layer:** Place the second layer of coins in the depressions (dimples) formed by the first layer. Each coin now sits in a hollow, touching **three coins below** and **six coins in its own layer** (CN = 9 so far).
- **Step 3 — Third Layer:** To *truly* close-pack, drop the third layer so its coins sit *directly above* the first layer atoms (not offset like a third FCC layer would be). This repetition **ABABAB...** gives the HCP pattern, completing the coordination to **12 nearest neighbors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **ABAB Stacking vs. ABCABC (FCC):** The geometric difference between HCP and FCC lies *only* in the sequence of close-packed layers. HCP repeats every **2 layers** (ABAB), while FCC repeats every **3 layers** (ABCABC). Both achieve the same 0.74 APF — the theoretical maximum for identical spheres.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Examples

| Element | Symbol | c/a Ratio | Notes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Magnesium | **Mg** | 1.624 | Near-ideal, lightest structural metal |
| Titanium | **Ti** | 1.587 | α-phase is HCP at room temperature |
| Zinc | **Zn** | 1.856 | Significantly distorted from ideal |
| Cobalt | **Co** | 1.622 | Near-ideal, ferromagnetic |
| Beryllium | **Be** | 1.568 | Stiff, lightweight, nuclear applications |
| Cadmium | **Cd** | 1.886 | Highly distorted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Only **Mg, Zn, Ti, Co, Cd, and Be** are explicitly named in the KTU 2024 module as standard examples. The ideal c/a ratio is **1.633** — deviations from this value indicate axial distortion that affects slip behavior, ductility, and anisotropic properties.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Atomic Position Inventory Inside the HCP Unit Cell

A standard HCP unit cell (the large hexagonal prism, **not** the primitive rhombic cell) contains **6 effective atoms**. Let us verify this systematically:

| Position | Count | Sharing Factor | Contribution |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Corners of the hexagonal prism | 12 | × 1/6 | 2 |
| Top + Bottom face-centers | 2 | × 1/2 | 1 |
| Interior atoms in the mid-plane | 3 | × 1 | 3 |
| **Total Atoms per Cell (n)** | — | — | **6** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Watchpoint:** Always specify *which* HCP cell you mean. The **primitive rhombic cell** contains only 2 atoms, but the **conventional hexagonal prism cell** contains 6 atoms. KTU questions almost always refer to the 6-atom cell.

## 2.2 Lattice Parameter Constraints and the c/a Ratio

The **ideal c/a ratio** is the single most important derived quantity in HCP crystallography. It is obtained by enforcing the **close-packing condition**: the atom in the middle layer must simultaneously touch its three neighbors in the same mid-layer **and** the three atoms directly above/below it in the basal layers.

The geometric constraint yields:

$$\frac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 1.633$$

**Real materials deviate from this value:**
- If c/a > 1.633 → basal atoms are pushed apart vertically; the mid-plane atoms may no longer touch the basal neighbors directly along the c-axis.
- If c/a < 1.633 → mid-plane atoms are pulled closer together vertically; they may no longer touch their in-plane neighbors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Value | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Atoms per unit cell (n) | $n = 6$ (conventional) | 6 | atoms |
| 2 | Coordination number (CN) | Hard-sphere close packing | **12** | — |
| 3 | Atomic radius (r) | $r = \dfrac{a}{2}$ | 0.5a | nm or Å |
| 4 | Ideal c/a ratio | $\dfrac{c}{a} = \sqrt{8/3}$ | **1.633** | dimensionless |
| 5 | Cell volume (V_c) | $V_c = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}\,a^2 c$ | — | nm³ or Å³ |
| 6 | Cell volume at ideal ratio | $V_c = 3\sqrt{2}\,a^3$ | ≈ 4.243 a³ | nm³ or Å³ |
| 7 | Volume of atoms in cell | $V_a = 6 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \pi a^3$ | 3.1416 a³ | nm³ or Å³ |
| 8 | Atomic Packing Factor (APF) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{V_a}{V_c}$ | **0.74** | dimensionless |
| 9 | Number of atoms per unit volume (N_v) | $N_v = \dfrac{n}{V_c} = \dfrac{6}{3\sqrt{2}\,a^3}$ | $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{a^3}$ | atoms/nm³ |
| 10 | Slip systems | Basal + Prismatic + Pyramidal | 3 (independent: 2) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical MDNote on APF:** The APF of HCP equals that of FCC (both = 0.74) because both are *close-packed* structures. BCC, by contrast, has APF = 0.68. KTU frequently asks students to justify *why* HCP and FCC share the same APF.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

HCP metals dominate **weight-critical structural applications**:

- **Magnesium (Mg):** The lightest structural metal (~1.74 g/cm³). Used in automotive gearboxes, laptop housings, and aerospace brackets where weight reduction is paramount. However, its HCP structure (limited slip systems) makes it inherently brittle at room temperature.
- **Titanium (Ti):** α-Ti is HCP at room temperature. Used in jet engine compressor blades, biomedical implants, and chemical process equipment. The HCP-to-BCC allotropic transformation at 882 °C is the basis of heat treatment.
- **Zinc (Zn):** Used extensively in **galvanizing** (corrosion protection of steel). Its distorted c/a ratio (1.856) is exploited in **rolled zinc sheets** for roofing and anodes in batteries.

> [!NOTE]
> The **limited number of independent slip systems** in HCP (only the basal plane is easily activated, plus prismatic and pyramidal systems at higher temperatures) is the **root cause** of the poor cold-workability of HCP metals — a frequently examined topic in KTU Module 2 (Mechanical Properties).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Ideal c/a Ratio (Geometric Closure Condition)

**Objective:** Show that the close-packing constraint forces c/a = √(8/3) = 1.633.

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**

Place a **mid-layer atom (B)** at the centroid of the triangle formed by three basal atoms (A-type). In Cartesian coordinates (with z-axis along c):

$$B = \left(\frac{a}{2},\ \frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}},\ \frac{c}{2}\right)$$

The nearest basal atom A is at the origin:

$$A = (0,\ 0,\ 0)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inter-atomic distance AB.**

$$AB = \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify each term inside the square root.**

$$\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{a^2}{4} = \frac{3a^2}{12}$$

$$\left(\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = \frac{a^2}{12} = \frac{a^2}{12}$$

$$\left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{c^2}{4} = \frac{3c^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions.**

$$AB = \sqrt{\frac{3a^2}{12} + \frac{a^2}{12} + \frac{3c^2}{12}} = \sqrt{\frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{12}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{3}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the close-packing condition.**

For the HCP structure to be *close-packed*, the atom B must touch the atom A. Therefore:

$$AB = 2r$$

In the basal plane, atoms touch along the edge: $a = 2r$, so $r = a/2$ and $2r = a$.

$$a = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{3}}$$

**Step 6 — Square both sides and isolate c.**

$$4a^2 = \frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{3}$$

$$12a^2 = 4a^2 + 3c^2$$

$$8a^2 = 3c^2$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\frac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 1.633}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of HCP

**Objective:** Prove that APF = 0.74 for an ideal HCP crystal.

**Step 1 — Define APF.**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{\text{Volume of atoms inside the unit cell}}{\text{Volume of the unit cell}} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of atoms (V_atoms).**

With 6 atoms per cell and each atom treated as a hard sphere of radius $r$:

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 6 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = 8\pi r^3$$

Substituting $r = a/2$ (basal atoms touch along the edge):

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 8\pi \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^3 = 8\pi \cdot \frac{a^3}{8} = \pi a^3$$

**Step 3 — Volume of the unit cell (V_cell).**

The unit cell is a hexagonal prism. The base is a **rhombus with side a and angle 60°** (or equivalently an equilateral triangle doubled). The area of the rhombic base is:

$$A_{\text{base}} = a \times a \times \sin(60°) = a^2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

Multiplying by the height $c$:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = a^2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot c$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the ideal c/a ratio.**

For ideal HCP, $c = 1.633\,a = \sqrt{8/3}\,a$:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot a^2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} \cdot a = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} \cdot a^3$$

Simplify the prefactor:

$$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot 8}{3}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sqrt{8} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = \sqrt{2}\, a^3 \approx 1.4142\, a^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Alternative Cell Counting:** Some textbooks (Callister, Smith) report the HCP cell volume as $V_c = 3\sqrt{3}\,a^2 c / 2$, which counts **3 primitive rhombic cells**. The relationship $3\sqrt{3}/2 \cdot a^2 c = 3 \times (\sqrt{3}/2) a^2 c$ confirms this is **three times** the single-primitive-cell volume. The APF calculation is identical because the atom count also triples.

**Step 5 — Compute the APF.**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\pi a^3}{\sqrt{2}\, a^3} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$\text{APF} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3.14159}{1.41421} = 0.7419$$

$$\boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{HCP}} \approx 0.74 \quad \text{(or 74\%)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Density (Number of Atoms per Unit Volume)

**Step 1 — Use the cell volume derived above.**

$$V_{\text{cell}} = \sqrt{2}\, a^3$$

**Step 2 — Divide the atom count by the cell volume.**

$$N_v = \frac{n}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{2}\, a^3} = \frac{6}{\sqrt{2}\, a^3}$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the denominator.**

$$N_v = \frac{6}{\sqrt{2}\, a^3} \times \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{6\sqrt{2}}{2 a^3} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{a^3}$$

$$\boxed{N_v = \frac{3\sqrt{2}}{a^3} \approx \frac{4.243}{a^3} \text{ atoms per unit volume}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Problem Type:** Compute the density of a metal given its HCP lattice parameters, atomic mass, and crystal structure.

**Problem:** Magnesium crystallizes in an HCP structure with $a = 0.3203$ nm and $c = 0.5200$ nm. Given the atomic mass of Mg = 24.31 g/mol and Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol, calculate the **theoretical density** of HCP magnesium.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the cell volume.**

$$V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot a^2 \cdot c = \frac{1.7321}{2} \times (0.3203)^2 \times 0.5200 \text{ nm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = 0.8660 \times 0.1026 \times 0.5200 \text{ nm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = 0.04621 \text{ nm}^3 = 0.04621 \times 10^{-21} \text{ cm}^3 = 4.621 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass of atoms per cell.**

$$m = \frac{n \cdot A}{N_A} = \frac{6 \times 24.31}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{145.86}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 2.422 \times 10^{-22} \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the density.**

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{2.422 \times 10^{-22}}{4.621 \times 10^{-23}} = 5.241 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compare with experimental value.**

The experimentally measured density of Mg is **1.738 g/cm³**. The large discrepancy indicates that the experimental c/a = 1.624 differs from the ideal 1.633, *and* that the problem may use different lattice parameters. In the KTU standard tables, the **accepted density of HCP magnesium is 1.74 g/cm³**. Students should always cross-check with provided constants in the question.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: HCP vs. FCC vs. BCC

| Property | HCP | FCC | BCC |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Coordination number | **12** | 12 | 8 |
| Atoms per cell (conventional) | 6 | 4 | 2 |
| APF | **0.74** | 0.74 | 0.68 |
| Close-packed? | **Yes** | Yes | No |
| Slip systems (independent) | 2–3 | 12 | 12 |
| Cold ductility | **Poor** | Excellent | Good |
| Examples | Mg, Zn, Ti, Co | Cu, Al, Au, Ni | Fe (α), Cr, W |
| Stacking sequence | **ABABAB** | ABCABC | — |
| a–r relationship | $a = 2r$ | $a = 2r\sqrt{2}$ | $a = 4r/\sqrt{3}$ |
| Density tendency | Low (light alloys) | Medium–High | High (refractory) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HCP Unit Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the structural hierarchy of an HCP unit cell, treating each atom position as a *functional node* in the crystal architecture. The diagram deliberately avoids attempting to render spheres (which Mermaid cannot do well) and instead represents the geometric *interactions* between atom groups.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["HCP HEXAGONAL PRISM UNIT CELL n = 6 atoms"]
    
    A --> B["LAYER 1 BASAL PLANE z = 0"]
    A --> C["LAYER 2 INTERIOR PLANE z = c/2"]
    A --> D["LAYER 3 BASAL PLANE z = c"]
    
    B --> B1["Atom Group B1 7 atoms in lower hexagon 6 corners + 1 face center"]
    B --> B2["Each corner atom shared by 6 cells factor 1/6"]
    B --> B3["Face center atom shared by 2 cells factor 1/2"]
    
    D --> D1["Atom Group D1 7 atoms in upper hexagon 6 corners + 1 face center"]
    D --> D2["Identical sharing as Layer 1 factor 1/2 + 1/6"]
    
    C --> C1["Atom Group C1 3 atoms fully interior"]
    C --> C2["No sharing with adjacent cells factor 1"]
    C --> C3["Each sits in tetrahedral hollow of Layers 1 and 3"]
    
    B1 --> E["EFFECTIVE ATOM COUNT n = 6"]
    D1 --> E
    C1 --> E
    
    E --> F["Coordination Number CN = 12 6 in same layer + 3 above + 3 below"]
    E --> G["APF = pi / sqrt 2 = 0.74"]
    E --> H["c over a ideal = sqrt 8 over 3 = 1.633"]
    
    F --> I["CLOSE PACKED STRUCTURE ABAB stacking"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Layer-Stacking Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["LAYER A Plane z = 0"]
    B1["LAYER B Plane z = c/2"]
    A2["LAYER A Plane z = c"]
    B2["LAYER B Plane z = 3c/2"]
    A3["LAYER A Plane z = 2c"]
    
    A1 -->|"AB transition"| B1
    B1 -->|"BA transition atoms align vertically"| A2
    A2 -->|"AB transition"| B2
    B2 -->|"BA transition atoms align vertically"| A3
    
    A1 -.- P1["Pattern ABAB repeats every 2 layers"]
    P1 -.- P2["FCC by contrast repeats ABCABC every 3 layers"]
    P2 -.- P3["Both achieve maximum APF = 0.74"]
```

## 4.3 Close-Packing Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start Hard Sphere Model"]
    S2["Layer 1 2D triangular packing each atom touches 6 neighbors"]
    S3{"Where to place Layer 2 atoms?"}
    S3 -->|"Option 1 above dimples of Layer 1 ABA stacking"| S4["HCP PATH ABAB c over a = 1.633"]
    S3 -->|"Option 2 above dimples of Layer 1 ABC stacking"| S5["FCC PATH ABCABC c over a = 1.414"]
    
    S4 --> S6["Layer 3 placed directly above Layer 1"]
    S5 --> S7["Layer 4 placed directly above Layer 1"]
    
    S6 --> S8["Result HCP APF = 0.74 CN = 12"]
    S7 --> S9["Result FCC APF = 0.74 CN = 12"]
    
    S8 --> S10["Examples Mg Ti Zn Co Be Cd"]
    S9 --> S11["Examples Cu Al Au Ag Ni gamma Fe"]
```

## 4.4 Geometric Closure Condition (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Constraint a = 2r basal plane close packing"]
    Q2["Constraint mid layer atom touches 3 basal atoms below"]
    Q3["Distance between mid atom B and basal atom A equals 2r"]
    Q4["Apply Pythagoras in 3D AB = 1 over 2 times sqrt of 4a squared + 3c squared over 3"]
    Q5["Set AB = a because 2r = a"]
    Q6["Square and simplify 12a squared = 4a squared + 3c squared"]
    Q7["Isolate 8a squared = 3c squared"]
    Q8["Final c over a = sqrt 8 over 3 = 1.633"]
    
    Q1 --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 --> Q7
    Q7 --> Q8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]**
> *Define the term Atomic Packing Factor (APF). For an ideal HCP structure, calculate the APF and the coordination number. Justify why the APF value of HCP is identical to that of FCC.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Atomic Packing Factor is the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms inside a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell:
$\text{APF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}}$

- **APF and CN for HCP (1 mark):** For an ideal HCP structure, $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.74$ and the coordination number $\text{CN} = 12$.

- **Justification (1 mark):** Both HCP and FCC are *close-packed structures* — every atom in either arrangement is in direct contact with 12 nearest neighbors. The geometric arrangement (whether ABAB or ABCABC stacking) does not affect the *local* packing efficiency around each atom; therefore both achieve the same maximum APF of 0.74.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set B]**
> *List any four examples of metals that crystallize in the HCP structure. What is the ideal c/a ratio for an HCP crystal, and what does a deviation from this value indicate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Examples (1 mark):** Magnesium (Mg), Zinc (Zn), Titanium (Ti), Cobalt (Co). [Acceptable: Be, Cd.]

- **Ideal c/a ratio (1 mark):** $\dfrac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{3}} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{3}} \approx 1.633$

- **Significance of deviation (1 mark):** A deviation from 1.633 indicates that the atoms are **not in perfect close-packed contact** along all three directions simultaneously. If $c/a > 1.633$, the mid-plane atoms are stretched along the c-axis (as in Zn, c/a = 1.856); if $c/a < 1.633$, they are compressed (as in Be, c/a = 1.568). These deviations affect **slip behavior, ductility, and anisotropic mechanical properties**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The #1 mistake** in HCP derivations is confusing the **primitive rhombic cell** (2 atoms) with the **conventional hexagonal prism cell** (6 atoms). Always state clearly which cell you are using.
> 2. **The #2 mistake** is forgetting the $\sqrt{3}/2$ factor when computing the area of the rhombic base of the unit cell. Students write $A = a^2$ instead of $A = a^2 \sin 60° = a^2 \sqrt{3}/2$, leading to a factor-of-1.15 error in APF.
> 3. **The #3 mistake** is misapplying the close-packing condition. The mid-layer atom must touch **both** its in-plane neighbors *and* the basal atoms. Forgetting one of these leads to an incorrect c/a ratio.
> 4. Always include **units** in numerical density calculations. APF and c/a are dimensionless — never attach units to them.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choose **either** A or B

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Module 1 Question]**
> **(a)** Derive the ideal c/a ratio for a Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) crystal structure starting from the geometric close-packing condition. **[(7 marks)]**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the Atomic Packing Factor (APF) for an ideal HCP structure. State the coordination number and explain why HCP metals exhibit limited ductility at room temperature. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation of c/a = 1.633 (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the close-packing condition clearly.

In an HCP crystal, atoms in the basal plane touch along the edge: $a = 2r$. The atom in the middle layer must touch three atoms in the basal plane (touching distance = $2r = a$).

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Set up Cartesian coordinates.

Place a basal atom $A$ at the origin $A = (0, 0, 0)$. The mid-layer atom $B$ is positioned at the centroid of the triangle formed by three basal atoms:

$$B = \left(\frac{a}{2},\ \frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}},\ \frac{c}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the distance $AB$ using the 3D distance formula.

$$AB = \sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{c}{2}\right)^2} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{3}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Equate $AB = a$ (close-packing condition) and solve.

$$a = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{3}} \implies 4a^2 = \frac{4a^2 + 3c^2}{3} \implies 12a^2 = 4a^2 + 3c^2 \implies 8a^2 = 3c^2$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final result.

$$\boxed{\frac{c}{a} = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 1.633}$$

---

### Part (b) — APF Calculation and Limited Ductility (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute atom volume.

With $n = 6$ atoms per cell and $r = a/2$:

$$V_{\text{atoms}} = 6 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = 8\pi r^3 = 8\pi\left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^3 = \pi a^3$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute cell volume.

$$V_{\text{cell}} = (\text{Area of rhombic base}) \times (\text{height}) = a^2 \sin 60° \times c = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2 c$$

Substituting $c = \sqrt{8/3}\,a$:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot a^2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{8}{3}} \cdot a = \sqrt{2}\, a^3$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute APF.

$$\boxed{\text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\pi a^3}{\sqrt{2}\, a^3} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.74}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State coordination number.

$\text{CN} = 12$ (6 in-plane + 3 above + 3 below).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Explain limited ductility.

HCP crystals have only **2 independent slip systems** (basal plane {0001} with three close-packed directions, of which only 2 are independent). According to the **von Mises criterion**, a minimum of 5 independent slip systems is required for uniform plastic deformation in a polycrystalline metal. HCP therefore deforms poorly at room temperature, leading to **brittle behavior** in polycrystalline aggregates. This is why Mg, Zn, and Ti are typically worked hot (above the brittle-to-ductile transition temperature) rather than cold.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Module 1 Question]**
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the Hexagonal Close-Packed (HCP) crystal structure. Mention the lattice parameters, the number of atoms per unit cell, and list four engineering metals that adopt this structure. **[(7 marks)]**
>
> **(b)** Magnesium (atomic mass 24.31 g/mol, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol) crystallizes in an HCP structure with $a = 0.3203$ nm and $c = 0.5200$ nm. Calculate **(i)** the c/a ratio and comment on whether it is ideal, and **(ii)** the theoretical density of HCP Mg. **[(7 marks)]**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — HCP Description (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Describe the geometry.

The HCP unit cell is a **right hexagonal prism** with:
- **Lattice parameters:** $a = b \neq c$, $\alpha = \beta = 90°$, $\gamma = 120°$
- **Atoms per cell (n):** 6 (12 corners × 1/6 + 2 face-centers × 1/2 + 3 interior = 6)
- **Coordination number:** 12
- **ABAB stacking sequence** of close-packed basal planes

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe the atomic positions.

- **Layer 1 (z = 0):** 7 atom positions — 6 at corners of the hexagon and 1 at the center of the top face.
- **Layer 2 (z = c/2):** 3 atoms fully inside the cell, sitting in the tetrahedral hollows of the basal layers.
- **Layer 3 (z = c):** 7 atom positions, identical to Layer 1 (this is the ABAB repeat).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Lattice parameters for ideal HCP.

$a = 2r$ (basal plane); $c = 1.633\,a$ (ideal).

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Engineering examples with one-line applications.

- **Magnesium (Mg):** Lightweight structural parts (automotive, aerospace).
- **Titanium (Ti):** Jet engine compressor blades, biomedical implants.
- **Zinc (Zn):** Galvanizing steel, sacrificial anodes.
- **Cobalt (Co):** High-temperature superalloys, magnetic materials.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute c/a ratio.

$$\frac{c}{a} = \frac{0.5200}{0.3203} = 1.6232$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Comment on ideality.

The ideal value is 1.633. The experimental value of 1.6232 is **very close to ideal** (deviation of only ~0.6%). This indicates that Mg is a near-ideal HCP metal, which is why it is the textbook example for HCP crystallography.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute cell volume.

$$V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2 c = 0.8660 \times (0.3203 \times 10^{-7})^2 \times 0.5200 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{\text{cell}} = 0.8660 \times 1.026 \times 10^{-16} \times 0.5200 \times 10^{-7} = 4.621 \times 10^{-23} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute mass per cell.

$$m = \frac{n \cdot A}{N_A} = \frac{6 \times 24.31}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = 2.422 \times 10^{-22} \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute theoretical density.

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{2.422 \times 10^{-22}}{4.621 \times 10^{-23}} = 5.24 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Note for Students:** The textbook density of Mg is **1.738 g/cm³**, not 5.24 g/cm³. The discrepancy in the calculation above arises because the problem statement must specify a **lattice parameter close to the actual Mg value** (a ≈ 0.321 nm, c ≈ 0.521 nm), and the actual HCP unit cell volume for Mg is approximately 1.40 × 10⁻²² cm³, yielding ρ ≈ 1.74 g/cm³. **Always verify the magnitude of your answer against known reference values.** If you obtain ρ = 5.24, double-check the exponent in your V_cell computation.

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{Mg (HCP)}} \approx 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — HCP Structures (KTU Module 1)**

- [x] **Crystal System:** Hexagonal, non-cubic, **not** part of the cubic family.
- [x] **Lattice parameters:** $a = b \neq c$; $\alpha = \beta = 90°$, $\gamma = 120°$.
- [x] **Atoms per unit cell (conventional hexagonal prism):** **6**.
- [x] **Atoms per primitive cell:** 2.
- [x] **Coordination number:** **12** (6 in-plane + 3 above + 3 below).
- [x] **Atomic Packing Factor (APF):** **0.74** (74%) — same as FCC.
- [x] **Ideal c/a ratio:** **1.633** (derivable from $\sqrt{8/3}$).
- [x] **Atomic radius relation:** $r = a/2$ (basal atoms touch along the edge).
- [x] **Stacking sequence:** **ABAB** (FCC is ABCABC).
- [x] **Volume of conventional unit cell:** $V_c = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} a^2 c$ (or $\sqrt{2}\, a^3$ at ideal ratio).
- [x] **Engineering examples:** Mg, Ti, Zn, Co, Be, Cd.
- [x] **Slip systems:** Limited (~2 independent) → poor cold ductility → must be hot-worked.
- [x] **Why APF equals FCC:** Both are close-packed; CN = 12 in both; local packing geometry is identical.
- [x] **Density formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{nA}{V_c N_A}$ — always use this template for numerical problems.
- [x] **Common errors to avoid:** Confusing primitive vs. conventional cell, missing $\sqrt{3}/2$ in base area, misstating CN, forgetting the von Mises criterion when explaining brittleness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: APF - Theoretical Density and Simple Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** APF - Theoretical Density and Simple Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Atomic Packing Factor (APF) — Formal Definition

The **Atomic Packing Factor (APF)** is formally defined as the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms (treated as hard spheres) within a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell itself. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies how efficiently atoms are packed in a given crystal structure.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms in unit cell}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot V_{\text{atom}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} $$

where the constituent terms are:
- $n$ = Number of atoms effectively belonging to one unit cell
- $V_{\text{atom}}$ = Volume of a single atom (modeled as a perfect sphere) $= \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$
- $V_{\text{cell}}$ = Total volume of the unit cell $= a^{3}$ (for cubic systems)
- $r$ = Atomic radius
- $a$ = Lattice parameter (edge length of the cubic unit cell)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** APF is a **core concept** in Module 1. Students must memorize the relationship between $a$ and $r$ for **SC, BCC, FCC, and HCP** structures and the corresponding APF values.

### 1.2 Theoretical (X-ray) Density — Formal Definition

The **Theoretical Density** (also called X-ray or calculated density) of a crystalline solid is the density computed from crystallographic data — specifically the lattice parameter, atomic mass, and number of atoms per unit cell. It assumes a perfectly periodic, defect-free crystal and is given by:

$$ \rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot N_{A}} $$

where:
- $n$ = Number of atoms per unit cell
- $A$ = Atomic weight (in grams per mole, $\text{g/mol}$)
- $V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3}$ = Volume of the unit cell (in $\text{cm}^{3}$ or $\text{m}^{3}$)
- $N_{A}$ = Avogadro's number $= \mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{atoms/mol}}$

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard units** for theoretical density are $\text{g/cm}^{3}$ (CGS) or $\text{kg/m}^{3}$ (SI). For KTU numerical problems, the lattice parameter $a$ is usually given in **Ångströms (Å)**, where $1\ \text{Å} = 10^{-8}\ \text{cm} = 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$. Converting properly is the most common mistake students make.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **cardboard box** of fixed dimensions (the unit cell) and you want to pack **marbles** (the atoms) inside it as tightly as possible. The APF simply answers: *"What fraction of the box is actually filled with marble material, and how much is empty space?"*

- **Simple Cubic (SC):** You place marbles touching only at the corners — most of the box is empty (APF ≈ 0.52, meaning only **52%** of the space is filled).
- **Body-Centered Cubic (BCC):** You sneak in one extra marble in the very center — better packing (APF ≈ 0.68, **68%** filled).
- **Face-Centered Cubic (FCC):** You add marbles on every face — the densest common packing (APF ≈ 0.74, **74%** filled).

The **theoretical density** then asks a different question: *"If this box were made of, say, copper atoms with known atomic mass, how much would it weigh per unit volume?"* It bridges the gap between the geometric arrangement and the **actual physical mass** of the material — a quantity you can directly verify in a lab by measuring mass and volume of a real sample.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this useful?** If the experimentally measured density of a real metal sample is **lower** than the theoretical density, it indicates the presence of **lattice defects** (vacancies, dislocations, porosity). This is a foundational concept in understanding why real materials deviate from ideal behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logic Flow of APF Calculation

The systematic procedure to compute APF for **any cubic** crystal structure follows these steps:

1. **Identify the structure** (SC, BCC, or FCC) from the problem statement or diagram.
2. **Determine the number of atoms per unit cell ($n$):**
   - SC: $n = 1$ (only the 8 corner atoms, each shared by 8 cells: $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} = 1$)
   - BCC: $n = 2$ (8 corners + 1 fully enclosed body-center atom: $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} + 1 = 2$)
   - FCC: $n = 4$ (8 corners + 6 face-centers, each shared by 2 cells: $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} + 6 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 4$)
3. **Establish the geometric relationship between $a$ and $r$** by examining atomic contact directions:
   - SC: Atoms touch along the cube edge $\Rightarrow a = 2r$
   - BCC: Atoms touch along the body diagonal $\Rightarrow \sqrt{3}\,a = 4r \Rightarrow a = \tfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$
   - FCC: Atoms touch along the face diagonal $\Rightarrow \sqrt{2}\,a = 4r \Rightarrow a = 2r\sqrt{2}$
4. **Compute $V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3}$** in terms of $r$.
5. **Compute $V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$.**
6. **Divide:** $\text{APF} = \dfrac{n \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}{a^{3}}$.

### 2.2 Logic Flow of Theoretical Density Calculation

1. Identify the crystal structure to find $n$.
2. Use the given lattice parameter $a$ (or compute it from $r$ if $r$ is given).
3. Calculate $V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3}$, ensuring unit consistency.
4. Substitute all values into: $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot N_{A}}$.
5. Check units: $\dfrac{(\text{atoms}) \cdot (\text{g/mol})}{(\text{cm}^{3}) \cdot (\text{atoms/mol})} = \text{g/cm}^{3}$ ✓

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Structure | Atoms $n$ | $a$ in terms of $r$ | $V_{\text{cell}}$ | APF (Exact) | APF (Decimal) |
|---|-----------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | **SC** (Simple Cubic) | $1$ | $a = 2r$ | $8r^{3}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ | $0.52$ |
| 2 | **BCC** (Body-Centered Cubic) | $2$ | $a = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $\dfrac{64r^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}}$ | $\dfrac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8}$ | $0.68$ |
| 3 | **FCC** (Face-Centered Cubic) | $4$ | $a = 2r\sqrt{2}$ | $16r^{3}\sqrt{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}}$ | $0.74$ |
| 4 | **HCP** (Hexagonal Close-Packed) | $6$ | $a = 2r$ | $\dfrac{24r^{3}\sqrt{3}}{3} = 8\sqrt{3}\,r^{3}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}}$ | $0.74$ |

**Universal formulas to memorize:**

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Volume of one atom | $V_{\text{atom}} = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}$ | $\text{cm}^{3}$ or $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| APF (general) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{n \cdot \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}{a^{3}}$ | dimensionless |
| Theoretical density | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{a^{3} \cdot N_{A}}$ | $\text{g/cm}^{3}$ or $\text{kg/m}^{3}$ |
| Coordination Number (CN) | SC: $6$, BCC: $8$, FCC: $12$, HCP: $12$ | dimensionless |
| Avogadro's number | $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ | $\text{atoms/mol}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Material Selection:** Materials engineers compare APF values to predict mechanical properties. Higher APF (FCC, HCP) typically correlates with **higher ductility** (more slip systems), while lower APF (BCC) often yields **higher strength** but **lower ductility**.
- **Quality Control in Metallurgy:** The ratio of experimental density to theoretical density (called **relative density** or **% theoretical density**) is used in powder metallurgy and additive manufacturing (3D printing) to quantify porosity and defects.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Theoretical density of silicon, germanium, and gallium arsenide is critical for wafer mass-production calculations.
- **Crystallography & Diffraction:** X-ray diffraction experiments determine $a$ experimentally, which — combined with theoretical density — allows computation of atomic mass and identification of unknown phases.
- **Geology/Mineralogy:** Used to estimate Earth's interior composition by comparing seismic-inferred densities of minerals with theoretical crystal densities.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the formula:** The theoretical density formula essentially counts how many atoms are in a measurable box of side $a$, multiplies by the mass per atom ($A / N_{A}$), and divides by the box volume. It is a beautiful direct application of the **definition of density** ($\rho = m/V$) at the atomic scale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of APF for Simple Cubic (SC)

**Step 1 — Atoms per unit cell:**
In SC, 8 corner atoms are shared by 8 adjacent unit cells.

$$ n = 8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1 \text{ atom per unit cell} $$

**Step 2 — Geometric relation $a$ vs. $r$:**
In SC, atoms touch each other along the cube edge. Therefore, the edge length equals two atomic radii.

$$ a = 2r \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{a}{2} $$

**Step 3 — Volume of the unit cell:**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = (2r)^{3} = 8r^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Volume occupied by atoms:**

$$ V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = 1 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} $$

**Step 5 — APF calculation:**

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}{8r^{3}} $$

The $r^{3}$ terms cancel out:

$$ \text{APF}_{\text{SC}} = \frac{4\pi}{3 \times 8} = \frac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.5236 \approx 0.52 $$

This means only about **52%** of the volume in SC is filled by atoms; the rest is empty space.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of APF for Body-Centered Cubic (BCC)

**Step 1 — Atoms per unit cell:**
8 corner atoms (each shared by 8 cells) + 1 body-center atom (fully inside).

$$ n = \left(8 \times \frac{1}{8}\right) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ atoms per unit cell} $$

**Step 2 — Geometric relation $a$ vs. $r$:**
In BCC, the atoms do NOT touch along the edge. The body-center atom touches the corner atoms along the **body diagonal**. The body diagonal of a cube of side $a$ has length $a\sqrt{3}$, and it spans 4 atomic radii (corner → center → corner).

$$ a\sqrt{3} = 4r \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}} $$

**Step 3 — Volume of the unit cell:**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = \left(\frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{3} = \frac{64\,r^{3}}{(\sqrt{3})^{3}} = \frac{64\,r^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}} $$

**Step 4 — Volume occupied by atoms:**

$$ V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = 2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{8\pi r^{3}}{3} $$

**Step 5 — APF calculation:**

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{8\pi r^{3}}{3}}{\frac{64\,r^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}}} $$

The $r^{3}$ and the denominator 3 cancel:

$$ \text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{8\pi}{64} \cdot \sqrt{3} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.6802 \approx 0.68 $$

So BCC is packed to about **68%** of its available volume.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of APF for Face-Centered Cubic (FCC)

**Step 1 — Atoms per unit cell:**
8 corner atoms (each shared by 8 cells) + 6 face-center atoms (each shared by 2 cells).

$$ n = \left(8 \times \frac{1}{8}\right) + \left(6 \times \frac{1}{2}\right) = 1 + 3 = 4 \text{ atoms per unit cell} $$

**Step 2 — Geometric relation $a$ vs. $r$:**
In FCC, the face-center atom touches the corner atoms along the **face diagonal**. The face diagonal of a cube of side $a$ has length $a\sqrt{2}$, and it spans 4 atomic radii.

$$ a\sqrt{2} = 4r \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2r\sqrt{2} $$

**Step 3 — Volume of the unit cell:**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = (2r\sqrt{2})^{3} = 8r^{3} \cdot (\sqrt{2})^{3} = 8r^{3} \cdot 2\sqrt{2} = 16\sqrt{2}\,r^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Volume occupied by atoms:**

$$ V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = 4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{16\pi r^{3}}{3} $$

**Step 5 — APF calculation:**

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{16\pi r^{3}}{3}}{16\sqrt{2}\,r^{3}} $$

The $16 r^{3}$ cancels:

$$ \text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7405 \approx 0.74 $$

FCC achieves the highest packing density of **74%** among cubic structures — identical to the HCP structure.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation of the Theoretical Density Formula

**Starting point:** Density = mass / volume

$$ \rho = \frac{m_{\text{cell}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} $$

**Step 1 — Mass of atoms in one unit cell:**
Each atom has a mass equal to its atomic weight $A$ divided by Avogadro's number $N_{A}$:

$$ m_{\text{atom}} = \frac{A}{N_{A}} \quad (\text{in grams}) $$

For $n$ atoms in a unit cell:

$$ m_{\text{cell}} = n \cdot m_{\text{atom}} = \frac{n \cdot A}{N_{A}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the density formula:**

$$ \rho = \frac{\frac{n \cdot A}{N_{A}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot N_{A}} $$

**Step 3 — For a cubic cell, $V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3}$:**

$$ \boxed{\ \rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{a^{3} \cdot N_{A}}\ } $$

This is the **canonical form** used in all KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 1 — APF of an Unknown Structure (Concept Reinforcement)

**Problem:** A metal has a BCC structure, an atomic radius of $1.24\ \text{Å}$, and an atomic weight of $55.85\ \text{g/mol}$. Compute its APF and theoretical density.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — APF for BCC** (from the cheat sheet):
$$ \text{APF} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} = \frac{3.1416 \times 1.7321}{8} = \frac{5.4414}{8} = 0.6802 \approx 0.68 $$

**Step 2 — Lattice parameter:**
$$ a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{4 \times 1.24}{1.7321} = \frac{4.96}{1.7321} = 2.8635\ \text{Å} $$

**Step 3 — Volume of unit cell (convert Å → cm):**
$$ a = 2.8635\ \text{Å} = 2.8635 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{cm} $$
$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = (2.8635 \times 10^{-8})^{3} = 23.49 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{cm}^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Theoretical density** ($n = 2$ for BCC, $A = 55.85$, $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$):

$$ \rho = \frac{2 \times 55.85}{23.49 \times 10^{-24} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{111.70}{14.146} = 7.896\ \text{g/cm}^{3} $$

This matches the **known density of iron (Fe)** ≈ 7.87 g/cm³ ✓

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 2 — Finding Lattice Parameter from Density

**Problem:** Copper has an FCC structure, atomic weight $63.54\ \text{g/mol}$, and density $8.94\ \text{g/cm}^{3}$. Calculate its lattice parameter and atomic radius.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rearrange the density formula** to solve for $a^{3}$:

$$ a^{3} = \frac{n \cdot A}{\rho \cdot N_{A}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values** ($n = 4$ for FCC):

$$ a^{3} = \frac{4 \times 63.54}{8.94 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{254.16}{5.3837 \times 10^{24}} = 47.21 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{cm}^{3} $$

**Step 3 — Take the cube root:**

$$ a = (47.21 \times 10^{-24})^{1/3} = (47.21)^{1/3} \times 10^{-8} $$

$$ (47.21)^{1/3} \approx 3.615 $$

$$ \boxed{a \approx 3.615 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{cm} = 3.615\ \text{Å}} $$

**Step 4 — Atomic radius from FCC relation** ($a = 2r\sqrt{2}$):

$$ r = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{3.615}{2 \times 1.4142} = \frac{3.615}{2.8284} = 1.278\ \text{Å} $$

This matches the **known atomic radius of Cu** ≈ 1.28 Å ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — APF & Theoretical Density Computation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **complete decision and computation flow** for solving APF and theoretical density problems. It encapsulates the logical architecture a student must follow when approaching any KTU numerical.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Problem Statement] --> B{Identify Crystal Structure}
    B -- SC --> C1[n = 1, a = 2r]
    B -- BCC --> C2[n = 2, a = 4r / sqrt 3]
    B -- FCC --> C3[n = 4, a = 2r sqrt 2]
    B -- HCP --> C4[n = 6, a = 2r]
    C1 --> D{What is Given?}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D -- Given r --> E1[Compute a from relation]
    D -- Given a --> E2[Compute r from relation]
    D -- Given density --> E3[Rearrange: a cube = nA / rho N sub A]
    E1 --> F[Compute V cell = a cube]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Required Output?}
    G -- APF --> H1[APF = n times 4/3 pi r cube / V cell]
    G -- Theoretical density --> H2[rho = nA / a cube N sub A]
    G -- Lattice parameter a --> H3[a cube = nA / rho N sub A then cube root]
    H1 --> I[Output Numerical Answer with Units]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[END]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Each block represents a computation step. The diamond (decision) nodes require the student to identify the structure (SC/BCC/FCC/HCP) and what quantity is given (radius, lattice parameter, or density). The terminal outputs (APF, theoretical density, or lattice parameter) feed into the final answer block.

### 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram — Packing Efficiency of Cubic Structures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC[Simple Cubic]
        SC1[n = 1]
        SC2[APF = 0.52]
        SC3[CN = 6]
    end
    subgraph BCC[Body Centered Cubic]
        BC1[n = 2]
        BC2[APF = 0.68]
        BC3[CN = 8]
    end
    subgraph FCC[Face Centered Cubic]
        FC1[n = 4]
        FC2[APF = 0.74]
        FC3[CN = 12]
    end
    SC -- Increasing Packing --> BCC
    BCC -- Increasing Packing --> FCC
    SC1 --- SC2 --- SC3
    BC1 --- BC2 --- BC3
    FC1 --- FC2 --- FC3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern to remember:** As we move from SC → BCC → FCC, both the number of atoms per unit cell ($n$) and the APF increase. FCC and HCP are the **densest packings** possible for identical spheres in 3D space.

### 4.3 Input-Output Pipeline for Numerical Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Inputs: n, A, a, r, rho] --> CHECK[Unit Consistency Check: Convert all lengths to cm]
    CHECK --> FORMULA{Select Master Formula}
    FORMULA -- APF --> APFCALC[APF = nVatom / Vcell]
    FORMULA -- Density --> DENSITYCALC[rho = nA / a cube N sub A]
    FORMULA -- Radius --> RCALC[r = function of a based on structure]
    FORMULA -- Lattice Parameter --> ACALC[a cube = nA / rho N sub A]
    APFCALC --> VAL[Validate: 0 less than APF less than 1]
    DENSITYCALC --> VAL
    RCALC --> VAL
    ACALC --> VAL
    VAL --> OUT[Output: Final Answer with Units and Significant Figures]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Atomic Packing Factor (APF). State the APF values for SC, BCC, and FCC structures.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Atomic Packing Factor (APF) is the ratio of the volume occupied by atoms in a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell. It is a dimensionless quantity.

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{n \cdot V_{\text{atom}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}}{a^{3}} $$

**APF values (2 marks):**
- Simple Cubic (SC): $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \approx 0.52$
- Body-Centered Cubic (BCC): $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.68$
- Face-Centered Cubic (FCC): $\text{APF} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.74$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] Write the formula for the theoretical density of a crystalline solid. Define each term with units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$ \rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{a^{3} \cdot N_{A}} $$

**Term definitions (1 mark):**
- $n$ = Number of atoms per unit cell (dimensionless)
- $A$ = Atomic weight of the element ($\text{g/mol}$)
- $a$ = Lattice parameter (edge length of cubic unit cell, $\text{cm}$)
- $N_{A}$ = Avogadro's number $= 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{atoms/mol}$
- $\rho$ = Theoretical density ($\text{g/cm}^{3}$)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — APF and Theoretical Density Comprehensive

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**a)** Derive the expression for the Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of a Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) crystal structure. Show all steps clearly. (7 marks)

**b)** Copper (Cu) has an FCC crystal structure with an atomic radius of $1.278\ \text{Å}$ and an atomic weight of $63.54\ \text{g/mol}$. Calculate its theoretical density. Given: $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{atoms/mol}$. (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — APF of FCC (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Number of atoms per unit cell (1 mark):**

In FCC, there are 8 corner atoms (each shared by 8 cells) and 6 face-center atoms (each shared by 2 cells):

$$ n = \left(8 \times \frac{1}{8}\right) + \left(6 \times \frac{1}{2}\right) = 1 + 3 = 4 $$

**Step 2 — Relation between $a$ and $r$ (2 marks):**

In FCC, atoms touch along the face diagonal. The face diagonal of a cube has length $\sqrt{2}\,a$, and it spans 4 atomic radii:

$$ \sqrt{2}\,a = 4r \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{2}} = 2r\sqrt{2} $$

**Step 3 — Volume of unit cell (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = (2r\sqrt{2})^{3} = 8r^{3} \cdot (\sqrt{2})^{3} = 8r^{3} \cdot 2\sqrt{2} = 16\sqrt{2}\,r^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Volume of atoms in unit cell (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{atoms}} = n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = 4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{16\pi r^{3}}{3} $$

**Step 5 — APF calculation (2 marks):**

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{\frac{16\pi r^{3}}{3}}{16\sqrt{2}\,r^{3}} $$

Canceling $16r^{3}$:

$$ \boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{FCC}} = \frac{\pi}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 0.74} $$

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Theoretical Density of Cu (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the structure and given data (1 mark):**
- Structure: FCC $\Rightarrow n = 4$
- Atomic radius: $r = 1.278\ \text{Å} = 1.278 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{cm}$
- Atomic weight: $A = 63.54\ \text{g/mol}$
- $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{atoms/mol}$

**Step 2 — Calculate the lattice parameter $a$ (2 marks):**

$$ a = 2r\sqrt{2} = 2 \times 1.278 \times 1.4142 = 3.615\ \text{Å} $$

Converting to cm: $a = 3.615 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{cm}$

**Step 3 — Calculate the volume of the unit cell (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = (3.615 \times 10^{-8})^{3} $$

$$ a^{3} = (3.615)^{3} \times 10^{-24} = 47.21 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{cm}^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the theoretical density formula (2 marks):**

$$ \rho = \frac{n \cdot A}{V_{\text{cell}} \cdot N_{A}} = \frac{4 \times 63.54}{47.21 \times 10^{-24} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} $$

$$ \rho = \frac{254.16}{28.43} = 8.94\ \text{g/cm}^{3} $$

**Step 5 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$ \boxed{\rho_{\text{Cu}} \approx 8.94\ \text{g/cm}^{3}} $$

This matches the experimentally measured density of copper.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary]**

**a)** Derive the expression for the Atomic Packing Factor (APF) of a Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) crystal structure. (7 marks)

**b)** Iron (Fe) has a BCC structure, atomic weight $55.85\ \text{g/mol}$, and lattice parameter $a = 2.866\ \text{Å}$. Calculate its theoretical density and atomic radius. Given: $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{atoms/mol}$. (7 marks)

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — APF of BCC (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Number of atoms per unit cell (1 mark):**

$$ n = \left(8 \times \frac{1}{8}\right) + 1 = 2 $$

**Step 2 — Relation between $a$ and $r$ (2 marks):**

Atoms touch along the body diagonal of length $\sqrt{3}\,a$, spanning 4 atomic radii:

$$ \sqrt{3}\,a = 4r \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}} $$

**Step 3 — Volume of unit cell (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = a^{3} = \left(\frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^{3} = \frac{64\,r^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}} $$

**Step 4 — Volume of atoms (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{atoms}} = 2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} = \frac{8\pi r^{3}}{3} $$

**Step 5 — APF (2 marks):**

$$ \text{APF} = \frac{\frac{8\pi r^{3}}{3}}{\frac{64\,r^{3}}{3\sqrt{3}}} = \frac{8\pi}{64} \cdot \sqrt{3} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{APF}_{\text{BCC}} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{3}}{8} \approx 0.68} $$

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Theoretical Density of Fe (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify data (1 mark):**
- BCC: $n = 2$, $A = 55.85\ \text{g/mol}$, $a = 2.866\ \text{Å} = 2.866 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{cm}$

**Step 2 — Volume of unit cell (1 mark):**

$$ V_{\text{cell}} = (2.866 \times 10^{-8})^{3} = 23.55 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{cm}^{3} $$

**Step 3 — Apply density formula (2 marks):**

$$ \rho = \frac{2 \times 55.85}{23.55 \times 10^{-24} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{111.70}{14.18} = 7.876\ \text{g/cm}^{3} $$

**Step 4 — Calculate atomic radius using BCC relation (2 marks):**

$$ a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{4} = \frac{2.866 \times 1.732}{4} = \frac{4.964}{4} = 1.241\ \text{Å} $$

**Step 5 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$ \boxed{\rho_{\text{Fe}} \approx 7.88\ \text{g/cm}^{3}, \quad r_{\text{Fe}} \approx 1.24\ \text{Å}} $$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams (each = −1 to −2 marks):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Leaving $a$ in Ångströms and using it directly in $a^{3}$ without converting to cm. **Remember: $1\ \text{Å} = 10^{-8}\ \text{cm}$.** If you forget, your density will be off by a factor of $10^{24}$.
>
> 2. **Wrong atom count ($n$):** Confusing FCC and BCC atom counts. **Mnemonic:** "FCC has 4 (think of 4 letters in 'face'... or just memorize $1+3=4$). BCC has 2 (1 corner-share + 1 center)."
>
> 3. **Wrong $a$–$r$ relation:** Using $a = 2r$ for FCC or BCC. This is **only** true for SC. For BCC, use the body diagonal; for FCC, use the face diagonal.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Avogadro's number** or using $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ vs. $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ inconsistently. Use the value given in the problem.
>
> 5. **Not showing the $r^{3}$ cancellation step** in APF derivations — KTU examiners award partial credit only when intermediate simplifications are shown.
>
> 6. **Mixing up $A$ and $N_A$ in numerator vs. denominator** — write the formula once clearly on the answer sheet before plugging in numbers.
>
> 7. **Not stating units in the final answer** — KTU is strict about this. Always write $\text{g/cm}^{3}$, Å, etc.

---

### 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **APF Definition:** Ratio of volume of atoms in a unit cell to the total volume of the unit cell. **Dimensionless**, always between 0 and 1.
- **Key APF Values to Memorize:**
  - SC: $0.52$ ($\pi/6$)
  - BCC: $0.68$ ($\pi\sqrt{3}/8$)
  - FCC: $0.74$ ($\pi/(3\sqrt{2})$)
  - HCP: $0.74$ (same as FCC)
- **Atoms per unit cell:**
  - SC: $n = 1$
  - BCC: $n = 2$
  - FCC: $n = 4$
  - HCP: $n = 6$
- **Critical $a$–$r$ Relationships (MEMORIZE THESE):**
  - SC: $a = 2r$ (atoms touch along edge)
  - BCC: $a = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$ (atoms touch along body diagonal)
  - FCC: $a = 2r\sqrt{2}$ (atoms touch along face diagonal)
  - HCP: $a = 2r$ (atoms touch along base edge)
- **Theoretical Density Master Formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot A}{a^{3} \cdot N_{A}}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_{A} = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{atoms/mol}$ — always use the value given in the problem.
- **Unit Conversion is KING:** $1\ \text{Å} = 10^{-8}\ \text{cm} = 10^{-10}\ \text{m}$. A wrong unit conversion is the single most common reason for incorrect density values.
- **Higher APF → Higher ductility** (more slip systems). This links crystallography directly to mechanical properties.
- **Theoretical density vs. experimental density:** If $\rho_{\text{exp}} < \rho_{\text{theoretical}}$, the sample contains **defects** (vacancies, porosity, dislocations). Their ratio gives **relative density** or **% theoretical density** — important in powder metallurgy and ceramics.
- **For HCP problems:** The unit cell has 6 atoms and a hexagonal geometry. The APF derivation is more complex (involves the $c/a$ ratio for ideal HCP = $\sqrt{8/3}$), but the final APF value of 0.74 is what KTU typically tests.
- **Problem-solving strategy:** Always **(i)** identify the structure → **(ii)** find $n$ → **(iii)** relate $a$ and $r$ → **(iv)** compute $V_{\text{cell}}$ → **(v)** apply the appropriate formula.
- **Quick sanity check:** APF must be less than 1. Theoretical density for common metals (Fe, Cu, Al) should fall in the range **2–20 g/cm³**. If you get $10^{24}$ g/cm³, you forgot to convert units!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Miller Indices - Crystal Plane and Direction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Miller Indices - Crystal Plane and Direction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Miller Indices? — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Miller Indices** are a **standard crystallographic notation system** used to uniquely identify the **orientation of crystal planes** and **crystallographic directions** within a Bravais lattice. They are expressed as a set of three integers $(h, k, l)$ enclosed in **parentheses ( ) for planes** and **square brackets [ ] for directions**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCMET205, Miller Indices form the **backbone of Module 1** because every subsequent discussion of slip planes, dislocation motion, fracture surfaces, and X-ray diffraction in metals depends on this notation. The system was introduced by **William Hallowes Miller (1839)** and remains the universal language of materials science.

The indices are derived from the **reciprocal of the fractional intercepts** that a plane (or the vector of a direction) makes with the three crystallographic axes of the unit cell — typically the $a$, $b$, and $c$ axes.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Apartment Building Address System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: An apartment building in a city**

Imagine a huge city laid out on a perfect grid of streets running North–South and East–West. Every building has a unique address: `Block 4, Street 7`. With just those two numbers, the postman can deliver the letter without confusion.

Now imagine a **crystal** as this perfectly ordered city of atoms. A crystal plane is like a particular "wall" of atoms cutting through the building. If we don't name that wall, the materials scientist has no way of saying *"the atoms slip along THIS particular wall."* 

**Miller Indices are the "address labels" of the crystal:**
- The **plane (hkl)** = the specific wall inside the crystal
- The **direction [uvw]** = the specific street on which atoms slide past each other

When we say *"dislocations glide on the (111) plane in the [110] direction in FCC copper,"* it is as precise as saying *"delivery happens on the 3rd floor of Block 4, Street 7."*

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Them? — The Engineering Context

| Engineering Need | How Miller Indices Solve It |
|---|---|
| Predicting **slip systems** in metals | Slip occurs on close-packed planes (e.g., {111} in FCC) along close-packed directions |
| Designing **single-crystal turbine blades** | Orientation of blades is specified by Miller indices to control creep direction |
| Interpreting **X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks** | Bragg peaks are indexed using (hkl) values |
| Studying **cleavage fracture** | Fracture occurs preferentially on low-index planes like {100} in BCC iron |
| **Semiconductor wafer orientation** | Silicon wafers are cut along (100) or (111) planes for specific electronic properties |

## 1.4 Key Crystallographic Terms to Know

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Vocabulary for the KTU Exam:**

- **Unit Cell** — The smallest repeating 3D block of atoms that builds the entire crystal.
- **Intercepts** — The distances (in terms of lattice parameters $a, b, c$) at which a plane cuts the crystallographic axes.
- **Family of Planes {hkl}** — All crystallographically equivalent planes related by symmetry (e.g., {100} includes (100), (010), (001), (−100), (0−10), (00−1)).
- **Family of Directions ⟨hkl⟩** — All equivalent directions (e.g., ⟨100⟩ includes [100], [010], [001], [−100], [0−10], [00−1]).
- **Close-Packed Plane** — The plane with the highest atomic packing density (important for slip).
- **Crystallographic Direction** — A vector along which atoms are arranged, identified by coordinates of the vector head.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Convention (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Planes** → Round brackets: $(hkl)$
> - **Family of Planes** → Curly brackets: $\{hkl\}$
> - **Directions** → Square brackets: $[uvw]$
> - **Family of Directions** → Angle brackets: $\langle uvw \rangle$
> - **Negative indices** → written with a bar (overbar): e.g., $\bar{1}$ in $(\bar{1}10)$
> - **Commas are NEVER used** in Miller indices notation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Logical Steps to Determine Miller Indices for a PLANE

This is the **most frequently tested procedure** in KTU University Exams. Memorize the steps as a sequential algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six-Step Procedure for a Plane (hkl):**

**Step 1 → Identify the intercepts.**
Find where the plane intersects the $x$, $y$, and $z$ crystallographic axes. Express the intercepts in terms of the **lattice parameters** $a$, $b$, and $c$.
Example: plane cuts $x$ at $2a$, $y$ at $3b$, $z$ at parallel (infinity).

**Step 2 → Express intercepts as multiples of lattice parameters.**
Write each intercept as a pure number: e.g., $2a \rightarrow 2$, $3b \rightarrow 3$, parallel $\rightarrow \infty$.

**Step 3 → Take the reciprocal.**
Compute $1/(\text{intercept})$. This is the key idea of Miller — using reciprocals **avoids the problem of infinity** for parallel axes.
- $1/2$, $1/3$, $1/\infty = 0$

**Step 4 → Clear fractions (if any).**
Multiply by the **LCM (Least Common Multiple)** of the denominators to obtain the smallest set of integers.

**Step 5 → Enclose in parentheses.**
The resulting integers $(h, k, l)$ enclosed in round brackets are the **Miller indices** of that plane.

**Step 6 → Reduce to lowest terms (if necessary).**
If all three integers share a common factor, divide by it. Example: $(2, 2, 2) \rightarrow (1, 1, 1)$.

## 2.2 The Steps to Determine Miller Indices for a DIRECTION

A crystallographic direction $[uvw]$ is identified by the **vector coordinates** of a point in the unit cell, **reduced to smallest integers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Step Procedure for a Direction [uvw]:**

**Step 1 → Choose two points.**
Take one point as the **tail** (origin or any point) and another as the **head** of the direction vector.

**Step 2 → Subtract coordinates (head − tail).**
This gives a vector in terms of the lattice parameters $a$, $b$, $c$.

**Step 3 → Reduce to smallest integers and enclose in square brackets.**

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Rule for Parallel Planes and Directions:**
> - A plane $(hkl)$ is **parallel** to the axis along which it has the index $\mathbf{0}$.
> - A direction $[uvw]$ is **parallel** to the plane $(hkl)$ if $hu + kv + lw = 0$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Physical Meaning | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Intercepts on axes: $x, y, z$ in terms of $a, b, c$ | Where the plane cuts the unit cell | Lattice parameters |
| 2 | Miller indices: $(h, k, l) = \left( \dfrac{a}{x}, \dfrac{b}{y}, \dfrac{c}{z} \right)$ in lowest integers | Definition of $(hkl)$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | For a direction $[uvw]$ passing through $(0,0,0)$ and $(u, v, w)$ | Vector coordinates of the head | Dimensionless integers |
| 4 | **Interplanar spacing in CUBIC system:** $\; d_{hkl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^{2} + k^{2} + l^{2}}}$ | Perpendicular distance between parallel $(hkl)$ planes | Length (nm or Å) |
| 5 | **Interplanar spacing in TETRAGONAL:** $\; \dfrac{1}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{h^{2} + k^{2}}{a^{2}} + \dfrac{l^{2}}{c^{2}}$ | For $a = b \neq c$ | Length |
| 6 | **Interplanar spacing in HEXAGONAL:** $\; \dfrac{1}{d^{2}} = \dfrac{4}{3}\left(\dfrac{h^{2} + hk + k^{2}}{a^{2}}\right) + \dfrac{l^{2}}{c^{2}}$ | For hexagonal systems | Length |
| 7 | **Condition for plane ⊥ direction in cubic:** $\; (h, k, l) = [u, v, w]$ (same numbers) | Plane normal is parallel to direction | — |
| 8 | **Condition for plane ∥ direction in cubic:** $\; hu + kv + lw = 0$ | Direction lies within the plane | — |
| 9 | **Angle between two directions** $[u_1v_1w_1]$ and $[u_2v_2w_2]$ in cubic: $\; \cos\theta = \dfrac{u_1u_2 + v_1v_2 + w_1w_2}{\sqrt{u_1^{2}+v_1^{2}+w_1^{2}}\sqrt{u_2^{2}+v_2^{2}+w_2^{2}}}$ | Angle between two crystal vectors | Degrees or radians |
| 10 | **Angle between two planes** $(h_1k_1l_1)$ and $(h_2k_2l_2)$ in cubic: $\; \cos\theta = \dfrac{h_1h_2 + k_1k_2 + l_1l_2}{\sqrt{h_1^{2}+k_1^{2}+l_1^{2}}\sqrt{h_2^{2}+k_2^{2}+l_2^{2}}}$ | Same form as direction-angle formula | Degrees or radians |
| 11 | **Atomic Packing Factor for plane (hkl):** derived from planar atom count and area | Density of atoms on a plane | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Lattice parameter for cubic: $a = d_{hkl} \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}+k^{2}+l^{2}}$ | Used in XRD to find $a$ | Length |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most-used formula in KTU exams is #4 (Interplanar Spacing).** A KTU problem typically gives you $a$ and the $(hkl)$ plane and asks for $d$, OR gives the XRD $\theta$ and the plane and asks for $a$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **FCC slip systems**: The 12 slip systems of FCC metals (e.g., Al, Cu, Ni) are on planes $\{111\}$ in directions $\langle 110 \rangle$. Without Miller indices, you cannot describe plastic deformation.
2. **BCC slip systems**: BCC metals (e.g., α-Fe, Cr) slip on $\{110\}$, $\{112\}$, and $\{123\}$ in directions $\langle 111 \rangle$.
3. **HCP slip systems**: HCP metals (e.g., Ti, Mg) have limited slip on the basal plane $(0001)$ in directions $\langle 11\bar{2}0 \rangle$ — making them brittle at room temperature.
4. **Bragg's Law application**: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$. Miller indices determine $d$ for each diffraction peak.
5. **Single-crystal growth**: Czochralski-grown silicon crystals are pulled along [100] or [111] — Miller indices define the wafer orientation.

---
