**Step 6 (1 mark):** Difference: Schottky involves vacancies **only** (cations and anions both leave the crystal → density drops); Frenkel involves a vacancy + interstitial **of the same ion** → no density change, no stoichiometry loss.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Typical examples — Frenkel: AgCl, ZnS; Schottky: NaCl, KCl.

---

#### Model Solution for B2(b) — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert temperature: $T = 600 + 273 = 873$ K.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute total atomic sites per m³:

$$
N = \frac{N_0 \cdot \rho}{M} = \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 2.70 \times 10^3}{26.98 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.024 \times 10^{28} \text{ sites/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the exponential term:

$$
\frac{Q_v}{k_B T} = \frac{0.75}{8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 873} = \frac{0.75}{0.07523} = 9.969
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Exponential:

$$\exp(-9.969) = 4.62 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Vacancy concentration:

$$N_v = 6.024 \times 10^{28} \times 4.62 \times 10^{-5} = 2.78 \times 10^{24} \text{ vacancies/m}^3$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Comparison with $N$: ratio $N_v / N = 4.62 \times 10^{-5}$ → **about 1 vacancy per 21,600 sites**.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Comment — even though the fractional concentration is tiny, in **1 m³ of aluminium** there are approximately $2.78 \times 10^{24}$ vacancies actively diffusing at 600 °C, controlling the rates of annealing, creep and precipitation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Point-Defect Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Frenkel/Schottky exponents.** A Frenkel/Schottky event creates **two** defects (vacancy + interstitial, or cation + anion vacancy), so $Q$ in the exponent must be divided by $2 k_B T$, not $k_B T$. Examiners explicitly check this.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Frenkel and Schottky density behaviour.** Frenkel = no density change; Schottky = density **decreases**. Confusing these costs 2 marks instantly.
>
> 3. **Wrong units for $k_B$.** If $Q_v$ is in **eV**, use $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K. If $Q_v$ is in **J**, use $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. Do not mix.
>
> 4. **Skipping Stirling's approximation in the derivation.** Examiners allocate **1 mark** explicitly for invoking Stirling's approximation $\ln N! \approx N \ln N - N$ — omitting it loses that mark.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to state the assumption $Q_v \gg k_B T$.** This is the justification for dropping the "1" in $N/N_v - 1 \approx N/N_v$. Always state it.
>
> 6. **In numericals, not converting °C to K.** A 2-mark penalty applies if temperature is left in Celsius in the exponential.
>
> 7. **Drawing Frenkel/Schottky diagrams without arrows or labels.** Schematic clarity is worth 1–2 marks; label the cation, anion, vacancy, interstitial clearly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module-2 Point-Defect Cheat-Sheet**

- ✅ **Point defects** = 0-D imperfections localized to one atomic site.
- ✅ **Three families** — Intrinsic, Extrinsic, Electronic.
- ✅ **Four intrinsic types** — Vacancy, Self-interstitial, Frenkel, Schottky.
- ✅ **Frenkel** = vacancy + interstitial of *same* species; **no density change**; AgCl, ZnS.
- ✅ **Schottky** = paired cation + anion vacancy migrating to surface; **density decreases**; NaCl, KCl.
- ✅ **Substitutional impurity** — Hume-Rothery rule: radius within 15%, similar structure, similar valency, similar electronegativity.
- ✅ **Interstitial impurity** — Solute radius < 0.41 × host radius (small atoms: H, C, N, O, B).
- ✅ **Master equation** — $N_v = N \exp(-Q_v / k_B T)$ (Arrhenius form).
- ✅ **Frenkel/Schottky** — $n = \sqrt{N N_i} \exp(-Q_F / 2 k_B T)$ or $N \exp(-Q_S / 2 k_B T)$.
- ✅ **Concentration ratio** — $N_{v2}/N_{v1} = \exp\left[-\frac{Q_v}{k_B}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$ — must-convert formula for KTU numericals.
- ✅ **Constants** — $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K; $N_0 = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol.
- ✅ **Typical $Q_v$ values** — Al: 0.75 eV; Cu: 1.5 eV; Fe: 2.0 eV; Ni: 1.4 eV.
- ✅ **Engineering utilities** — Doping (semiconductors), carburizing (steel), F-centres (optics), oxygen vacancies (YSZ in fuel cells), radiation damage (nuclear).
- ✅ **Rule of thumb** — At melting point, $N_v / N \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$.
- ✅ **Solid-solution strengthening** — quantified by Fleischer relation $\sigma_{ss} \propto G \varepsilon^{3/2} \sqrt{c}$.
- ✅ **Hume-Rothery rules for substitutional solid solutions** (4 rules) — frequently asked as 3-mark question.

---

*End of Module 2 — Crystal Imperfections: Point Defects. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!* 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Line Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Line Defects (1-Dimensional Crystalline Imperfections)**
> Line defects, also called **dislocations**, are one-dimensional (1D) linear crystallographic defects or irregularities within a crystal structure, around which some of the atoms are misaligned. The misalignment extends along a line (the **dislocation line**) that may be straight, curved, or form a closed loop. They are responsible for the phenomenon of **plastic deformation** in crystalline solids at stresses far below the theoretical shear strength predicted for a perfect crystal.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2 (Imperfections in Crystals), line defects are broadly classified into three primary categories:

1. **Edge Dislocation** (also called *Taylor-Orowan dislocation* or *extra half-plane dislocation*).
2. **Screw Dislocation** (also called *Burgers dislocation* or *spiral dislocation*).
3. **Mixed Dislocation** (combination of edge and screw components — the most common in real crystals).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thick stack of papers on a desk representing perfectly stacked atomic planes. A line defect is like **sliding a folded carpet** — to move the entire carpet by 1 cm, you don't push the whole thing at once. Instead, you create a small wave (a "ruck") that travels across the carpet, and the carpet moves progressively. Similarly, plastic deformation in a crystal occurs not by breaking all atomic bonds simultaneously, but by the **stepwise motion of dislocations** under applied shear stress, requiring orders of magnitude lower force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Recall Box**
> - **Dimension of line defect:** 1D (length scale ~ $10^{-8}$ m = tens of atomic spacings).
> - **Burgers vector** $\vec{b}$: Magnitude and direction of lattice distortion (the "signature" of a dislocation).
> - **Dislocation line direction** $\vec{\xi}$: Defines the orientation of the line itself.
> - **Relationship:** $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$ → Edge dislocation; $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$ → Screw dislocation; otherwise → Mixed.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Magnitude of Burgers vector** for a simple cubic crystal: $b = a$ (lattice parameter), and for FCC/BCC it equals the shortest lattice translation vector, $b = \dfrac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$ (FCC) or $b = \dfrac{a}{2}\langle 111 \rangle$ (BCC).
- **Typical dislocation density** in annealed metals: $\rho \approx 10^{10}$ to $10^{12}$ **lines/m²**.
- **Typical dislocation density** in heavily cold-worked metals: $\rho \approx 10^{14}$ to $10^{16}$ **lines/m²**.
- **Shear modulus** $G$ is the most relevant elastic constant in dislocation mechanics (order **25–200 GPa** for metals).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

#### A. Edge Dislocation
1. Visualize a perfect crystal lattice. Insert an **extra half-plane of atoms** from the top, terminating it somewhere inside the crystal.
2. The lower edge of this extra half-plane is the **dislocation line**, denoted by the symbol **⊥**.
3. Just above the dislocation line: atoms are **compressed** (hydrostatic compression zone).
4. Just below the dislocation line: atoms are **in tension** (tensile zone).
5. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is perpendicular to the dislocation line $\vec{\xi}$.

#### B. Screw Dislocation
1. Imagine a perfect crystal and apply a **helical shear** along a slip plane.
2. The atomic planes are transformed into a **spiral ramp** (like a parking garage ramp).
3. The dislocation line passes through the center of this spiral.
4. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line $\vec{\xi}$.

#### C. Mixed Dislocation
1. A curved dislocation line, in general, has both edge and screw character at different points.
2. The edge component predominates where the line is **perpendicular to $\vec{b}$**.
3. The screw component predominates where the line is **parallel to $\vec{b}$**.
4. In a dislocation loop, the character continuously varies from pure edge → mixed → pure screw → mixed → pure edge.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Plastic Deformation (Slip)
1. Applied shear stress $\tau$ acts on a slip plane.
2. When $\tau$ exceeds the **Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS)** $\tau_{CRSS}$, the dislocation begins to move.
3. Motion on the slip plane = **glide** (conservative motion).
4. Motion perpendicular to slip plane = **climb** (non-conservative, requires diffusion of vacancies, happens at high temperatures).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Formula Table for Line Defects (KTU Module 2)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{L}{V}$ | $L$ = total dislocation line length, $V$ = crystal volume | lines/m² | Dislocation density |
| 2 | $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}{\text{factor}}$ (FCC) $b = \dfrac{a}{2}\sqrt{2}$ | $a$ = lattice parameter | m | Magnitude of Burgers vector (FCC) |
| 3 | $b = \dfrac{a}{2}\sqrt{3}$ (BCC) | $a$ = lattice parameter | m | Magnitude of Burgers vector (BCC) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi K}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_0}\right)$ | $E/L$ = strain energy per unit length, $K = 1$ (screw) or $(1-\nu)$ (edge) | J/m | Strain energy of a dislocation |
| 5 | $\tau_{FR} = \dfrac{Gb}{L}$ | $L$ = length of pinned dislocation segment | Pa | Stress to operate Frank-Read source |
| 6 | $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma_{y}\cos\phi\cos\lambda$ | $\phi$ = angle between tensile axis & slip plane normal, $\lambda$ = angle between tensile axis & slip direction | Pa | **Schmid's Law** — resolved shear stress |
| 7 | $m = \cos\phi\cos\lambda$ | $m$ = Schmid factor (max = 0.5) | dimensionless | Determines ease of slip activation |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{Gb}{2\pi r}$ | $r$ = radial distance from dislocation core | Pa | Stress field around screw dislocation |
| 9 | $F/L = Gb^2 \cdot f(\theta, \nu)$ | Force per unit length between dislocations | N/m | Dislocation-dislocation interaction force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In cold-worked metals, $\rho$ rises to $10^{16}$ m$^{-2}$. The increased dislocation interactions act as barriers to further motion — this is the **strain-hardening (work-hardening)** mechanism. The Taylor relationship quantifies it: $\tau \propto \sqrt{\rho}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Metallurgy & Manufacturing:** Controlling dislocation density via cold working, annealing, and alloying directly controls yield strength (Hall-Petch, Taylor strengthening).
- **Semiconductors:** Dislocation-free silicon wafers are critical — dislocations act as recombination centers degrading electronic performance.
- **Aerospace Alloys:** Single-crystal turbine blades are designed with low dislocation density to resist creep at high temperatures.
- **Materials Selection:** The number of active slip systems determines ductility — FCC (12 systems) is ductile, HCP (3 systems) is brittle.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Magnitude of Burgers Vector in a Simple Cubic Crystal

**Statement:** For a simple cubic crystal of lattice parameter $a$, the smallest lattice translation vector has magnitude $a$.

**Step 1 — Define the Burgers Vector Concept:**
The Burgers vector is defined by constructing a **Burgers circuit**: a closed loop of atom-to-atom steps taken in a perfect reference lattice. The closure failure of an equivalent circuit around a real dislocation defines $\vec{b}$.

**Step 2 — Set Up the Circuit:**
In a perfect simple cubic crystal, a square Burgers circuit of $n$ atoms per side has a perimeter vector sum of zero:

$$\vec{b}_{perfect} = n\hat{x} - n\hat{x} + n\hat{y} - n\hat{y} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the FS/RH Convention (Finish-Start / Right-Hand):**
For an edge dislocation, the same circuit drawn in the real (defective) crystal fails to close. The vector required to close it (from Finish to Start) gives $\vec{b}$.

**Step 4 — Compute Magnitude:**
For a simple cubic crystal, the smallest translation is exactly one lattice spacing:

$$b = a \quad (\text{simple cubic})$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to FCC:**
In an FCC crystal, the shortest lattice vector is the vector from a corner atom to the center of the face:

$$
b_{FCC} = \frac{a}{2}\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 0^2} = \frac{a\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalize to BCC:**
In a BCC crystal, the shortest lattice vector is the body diagonal projection:

$$
b_{BCC} = \frac{a}{2}\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2} = \frac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}
$$

**Final Note:** These magnitudes are critical because the **self-energy of a dislocation is proportional to $b^2$** (see Section 3.2).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Strain Energy per Unit Length of an Edge Dislocation

**Statement:** The elastic strain energy stored in a cylindrical volume of radius $R$ around an edge dislocation is given by $\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_0}\right)$.

**Step 1 — Stress Field Components of an Edge Dislocation:**
For an edge dislocation with Burgers vector along $x$-axis and line along $z$-axis, the stress components in cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta)$ around the dislocation are:

$$
\sigma_{rr} = \sigma_{\theta\theta} = -\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\cdot\frac{\sin\theta}{r}
$$

$$
\sigma_{r\theta} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\cdot\frac{\cos\theta}{r}
$$

$$
\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{rr} + \sigma_{\theta\theta})
$$

**Step 2 — Strain Energy Density:**
The elastic strain energy per unit volume is:

$$
u = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over the Cylindrical Volume:**
Integrate $u$ from the dislocation core radius $r_0$ to outer cutoff $R$, and around $\theta$ from 0 to $2\pi$:

$$
\frac{E}{L} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_{r_0}^{R} u \cdot r\,dr\,d\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Carry Out the Integration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E}{L} &= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_{r_0}^{R} \frac{1}{2G}\left[\sigma_{rr}^2 + \sigma_{\theta\theta}^2 + 2\sigma_{r\theta}^2 + \frac{2\nu}{1-2\nu}\left(\sigma_{rr}+\sigma_{\theta\theta}\right)^2\right] r\,dr\,d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Using the Divergence Theorem:**
The integration reduces to a boundary integral via the energy-momentum tensor. The dominant term in $\frac{1}{r}$ yields:

$$
\frac{E}{L} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — For a Screw Dislocation (replace $\nu$ factor):**
Because the stress field is isotropic for a screw dislocation:

$$
\frac{E}{L}\bigg|_{screw} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The factor $\dfrac{1}{4\pi(1-\nu)}$ for edge and $\dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ for screw shows that an edge dislocation stores ~ $\dfrac{1}{(1-\nu)} \approx 1.5$ times more energy than a screw dislocation. This is why in real crystals, dislocations prefer to align in **screw orientation** to minimize total energy.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Stress to Operate a Frank-Read Source

**Statement:** The critical shear stress to bow out a pinned dislocation segment of length $L$ into a semicircle and generate a new dislocation loop is $\tau_{FR} = \dfrac{Gb}{L}$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Bowing:**
A dislocation segment of length $L$ is pinned at two nodes (e.g., by precipitates or other dislocations). Under shear stress $\tau$, the line bows into an arc of radius $r$.

**Step 2 — Line Tension Model:**
Treat the dislocation as an elastic string with line tension $T \approx \dfrac{Gb^2}{2}$ (half the self-energy).

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of Curved Dislocation:**
The shear stress produces a force per unit length $F = \tau b$. The line tension produces a restoring force $\dfrac{T}{r}$. Equilibrium gives:

$$
\tau b = \frac{T}{r} = \frac{Gb^2}{2r}
$$

**Step 4 — Critical Condition:**
The minimum radius of curvature occurs when the segment bows into a semicircle, $r_{min} = \dfrac{L}{2}$. Substituting:

$$
\tau_{FR} = \frac{Gb}{2r_{min}} = \frac{Gb}{L}
$$

**Step 5 — Subsequent Loop Formation:**
When $\tau$ just exceeds $\tau_{FR}$, the segment wraps around the pinning points, the two arms meet on the far side, annihilate, and a closed loop is released. The segment then regenerates and the process repeats — this is the **Frank-Read source**, the primary mechanism of dislocation multiplication.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Application:** Frank-Read sources explain why cold working multiplies dislocations rapidly. The number of active sources is what makes plastic deformation continuous and not catastrophic.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Schmid's Law (Critical Resolved Shear Stress)

**Statement:** The resolved shear stress on a slip system is $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$, where $\sigma$ is the applied uniaxial tensile stress.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**
Let the tensile axis make angle $\phi$ with the slip plane normal and angle $\lambda$ with the slip direction.

**Step 2 — Resolve Force into Slip Direction:**
The component of $\sigma$ along the slip direction:

$$
\sigma_{slip} = \sigma \cos\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Resolve Stress onto the Slip Plane:**
The component of this force acting on the slip plane (area factor):

$$
\tau_{RSS} = \frac{F_{slip}}{A_{slip}} = \sigma \cos\lambda \cos\phi
$$

**Step 4 — Critical Condition:**
Slip begins when $\tau_{RSS} = \tau_{CRSS}$ (a material constant for a given slip system at a given temperature).

**Step 5 — Schmid Factor:**
Define $m = \cos\phi\cos\lambda$ (max = 0.5 when $\phi = \lambda = 45°$).

$$
\tau_{CRSS} = m \cdot \sigma_y
$$

**Step 6 — Implication:**
Slip orientation with $m = 0.5$ requires the **lowest applied stress** to yield. This is why single-crystal tensile tests on FCC metals show the famous "Schmid factor" dependence.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Edge vs. Screw Dislocation Comparison

| Parameter | Edge Dislocation | Screw Dislocation |
|---|---|---|
| Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ direction | $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$ | $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$ |
| Extra half-plane | Present (above or below slip plane) | Absent |
| Slip plane | Unique (defined by $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{\xi}$) | Not unique (any plane containing $\vec{\xi}$) |
| Stress field symmetry | Hydrostatic + shear | Pure shear, axisymmetric |
| Self-energy factor | $\dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)}$ | $\dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi}$ |
| Cross-slip capability | No (locked) | Yes (can change slip plane) |
| Mobility | Lower | Higher (3D glide) |
| Symbol | ⊥ (perpendicular) | ∪ (curve/U-shape) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dislocation Activity in a Crystal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Perfect Crystal Lattice] --> B[Apply Shear Stress tau]
    B --> C{tau exceeds CRSS?}
    C -- No --> D[Elastic Deformation Only]
    C -- Yes --> E[Dislocation Nucleation]
    E --> F[Edge Dislocation Loop Forms]
    F --> G[Frank Read Source Activates]
    G --> H[Dislocation Glide on Slip Plane]
    H --> I[Encounter Obstacle]
    I --> J{Obstacle Type}
    J --> P1[Precipitate]
    J --> P2[Other Dislocation]
    J --> P3[Grain Boundary]
    P1 --> K1[Orowan Looping]
    P1 --> K2[Particle Cutting]
    P2 --> K3[Forest Dislocation Cutting]
    P2 --> K4[Junction Formation]
    P3 --> K5[Dislocation Pile Up]
    K1 --> L[Increased CRSS]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    K5 --> L
    L --> M[Strain Hardening]
    M --> N[Higher Yield Strength]
    H --> O[Dislocation Climb]
    O --> Q[High Temperature Diffusion of Vacancies]
    Q --> R[Recovery and Annihilation]
    R --> S[Reduced Dislocation Density]
    S --> T[Softening / Annealing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Dislocation Classification by Burgers Vector Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line Defect Dislocation] --> B{Orientation of b relative to xi}
    B -- Perpendicular --> C[Edge Dislocation]
    B -- Parallel --> D[Screw Dislocation]
    B -- Angle theta --> E[Mixed Dislocation]
    C --> C1[Extra Half Plane Present]
    C --> C2[Slip Plane Unique]
    C --> C3[High Strain Energy]
    D --> D1[Helical Shear Geometry]
    D --> D2[Cross Slip Possible]
    D --> D3[Low Strain Energy]
    E --> E1[Curved Dislocation Loop]
    E --> E2[Continuously Varying Character]
    E --> E3[Most Common in Real Crystals]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Force Interactions Between Parallel Dislocations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Parallel Edge Dislocations] --> B{Same Sign of b}
    A --> C{Opposite Sign of b}
    B -- Yes --> D[Repulsive Force F equals Gb squared over 2 pi r times 1 minus nu]
    B -- No --> E[Same slip plane alignment]
    C -- Yes --> F[Attractive Force]
    C -- No --> G[Annihilation Possible]
    D --> H[Dislocation Pile Up]
    F --> I[Dislocation Dipole Formation]
    G --> J[Reduction in Dislocation Density]
    H --> K[Strain Hardening]
    I --> K
    J --> L[Recovery]
    K --> M[Strengthened Material]
    L --> N[Softened Material]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The diagrams above represent the *block-level functional flow* of how dislocations nucleate, glide, interact with obstacles, and cause either hardening or recovery. This is a conceptual process-map rather than a literal atomic-scale drawing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define the term "Burgers vector" of a dislocation. Explain how the Burgers vector distinguishes between an edge and a screw dislocation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is a vector that characterizes the magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion associated with a dislocation. It is defined by the closure failure of a Burgers circuit drawn around the dislocation in the real crystal, referenced to a perfect crystal.
- **Edge vs Screw (2 marks):**
  - For an **edge dislocation**, $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$ (Burgers vector is perpendicular to the dislocation line). It represents the displacement required to insert the extra half-plane of atoms.
  - For a **screw dislocation**, $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$ (Burgers vector is parallel to the dislocation line). It represents the helical displacement of the crystal lattice.
  - For a **mixed dislocation**, $\vec{b}$ makes an arbitrary angle with $\vec{\xi}$.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **What is a Frank-Read source? Write the expression for the critical shear stress required to operate it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A Frank-Read source is a dislocation multiplication mechanism. A dislocation segment pinned at two points (by precipitates, grain boundaries, or other dislocations) bows out under applied shear stress, forms a closed loop, and regenerates the original segment. This produces a continuous stream of dislocations and is the primary cause of plastic deformation.
- **Expression (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{FR} = \frac{Gb}{L}
$$

where $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude, $L$ = length of pinned segment.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define edge dislocation and screw dislocation with neat labeled sketches. Derive the strain energy per unit length of an edge dislocation. (7 marks)

**(b)** State and derive Schmid's Law. What is the significance of the Schmid factor? Calculate the Schmid factor for a slip system oriented such that $\phi = 45°$ and $\lambda = 30°$. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Definitions and Strain Energy Derivation (7 marks)**

**Edge Dislocation (1 mark):** An edge dislocation is a line defect in which an extra half-plane of atoms is inserted into the crystal lattice. The dislocation line lies at the edge of this extra half-plane, and the Burgers vector is perpendicular to the dislocation line. The region above the dislocation line is in compression, while the region below is in tension.

**Screw Dislocation (1 mark):** A screw dislocation is a line defect in which the crystal planes are displaced in a helical manner, producing a spiral ramp. The Burgers vector is parallel to the dislocation line, and pure shear deformation characterizes the stress field.

**Strain Energy Derivation (5 marks):**

The stress components around an edge dislocation in polar coordinates $(r, \theta)$ are:

$$
\sigma_{rr} = \sigma_{\theta\theta} = -\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\cdot\frac{\sin\theta}{r}
$$

$$
\sigma_{r\theta} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\cdot\frac{\cos\theta}{r}
$$

The elastic strain energy per unit volume is:

$$
u = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sigma_{rr}\varepsilon_{rr} + \sigma_{\theta\theta}\varepsilon_{\theta\theta} + 2\sigma_{r\theta}\varepsilon_{r\theta}\right)
$$

Integrating over a cylindrical volume from core radius $r_0$ to outer radius $R$ and over the full angle $\theta$ from 0 to $2\pi$:

$$
\frac{E}{L} = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_{r_0}^{R} u \cdot r\,dr\,d\theta
$$

After performing the integration and simplifying (using Hooke's law for plane strain and the dominant $\frac{1}{r}$ stress terms):

$$
\frac{E}{L} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)
$$

where $G$ = shear modulus, $b$ = Burgers vector, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio, $R$ = outer cutoff radius, $r_0$ = dislocation core radius.

---

**(b) Schmid's Law Derivation (7 marks)**

**Statement (1 mark):** The Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS) is the component of the applied uniaxial tensile stress acting on the slip plane in the slip direction. Slip occurs when this resolved shear stress equals the critical value for the slip system.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $\sigma$ be the applied uniaxial tensile stress, $\phi$ = angle between tensile axis and slip plane normal, $\lambda$ = angle between tensile axis and slip direction.

Force along slip direction: $F_{slip} = \sigma \cdot A \cdot \cos\lambda$

Area of slip plane: $A_{slip} = \dfrac{A}{\cos\phi}$

Therefore:

$$
\tau_{RSS} = \frac{F_{slip}}{A_{slip}} = \frac{\sigma A \cos\lambda}{A/\cos\phi} = \sigma \cos\phi\cos\lambda
$$

Slip occurs when $\tau_{RSS} = \tau_{CRSS}$.

**Schmid Factor and Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**
The Schmid factor is $m = \cos\phi\cos\lambda$.

For $\phi = 45°$ and $\lambda = 30°$:

$$
m = \cos(45°)\cos(30°) = \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{4} \approx 0.612
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum possible Schmid factor is 0.5** (when $\phi = \lambda = 45°$). A value greater than 0.5 is physically inadmissible (the two angles are constrained by the slip-system geometry in a real crystal: $\phi + \lambda \leq 90°$ for the standard convention). The obtained value 0.612 indicates a non-physical angle combination — students should recognize this in exam conditions.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the geometry of an edge dislocation and a screw dislocation. Discuss how the Burgers vector is determined using the FS/RH convention. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is dislocation density? Explain how the Frank-Read source operates and derive the critical shear stress expression. Discuss the role of dislocation multiplication in strain hardening. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Geometry of Edge and Screw Dislocation (7 marks)**

**Edge Dislocation Geometry (2 marks):**
Imagine a perfect crystal lattice. Insert an additional half-plane of atoms from the top of the crystal such that it terminates inside the crystal. The lower boundary of this extra half-plane is the **dislocation line**, denoted ⊥. The region above the dislocation line is under hydrostatic compression (atoms squeezed together), while the region below is under hydrostatic tension (atoms pulled apart). The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is perpendicular to the dislocation line and lies along the slip direction.

**Screw Dislocation Geometry (2 marks):**
Consider a perfect crystal. Apply a shear displacement parallel to a slip plane, but only over a portion of the crystal. The resulting lattice takes the form of a **spiral ramp** or **parking garage helix**. The dislocation line is the central axis of this helix, and the Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is parallel to the dislocation line.

**FS/RH Burgers Vector Convention (3 marks):**
The Burgers vector is defined using the **Finish-Start Right-Hand (FS/RH)** convention:
1. Choose a **direction vector** $\vec{\xi}$ along the dislocation line (right-hand rule).
2. Construct a closed loop in the **real (defective) crystal** by stepping atom-to-atom around the dislocation.
3. Note the **Start** and **Finish** points of this loop.
4. Construct an **identical loop in a perfect reference crystal** (same number and direction of steps).
5. The vector required to close the loop in the perfect crystal (from Finish to Start) is the **Burgers vector** $\vec{b}$.

For an edge dislocation, $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$. For a screw dislocation, $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$. For a mixed dislocation, $\vec{b}$ makes an arbitrary angle with $\vec{\xi}$.

---

**(b) Dislocation Density and Frank-Read Source (7 marks)**

**Dislocation Density Definition (1 mark):**
The dislocation density $\rho$ is defined as the total length of dislocation lines per unit volume of crystal:

$$
\rho = \frac{L}{V} \quad \text{(units: lines/m}^2\text{)}
$$

Typical values: $10^{10}$–$10^{12}$ m$^{-2}$ for annealed metals, $10^{14}$–$10^{16}$ m$^{-2}$ for heavily cold-worked metals.

**Frank-Read Source Operation (3 marks):**
1. A dislocation segment of length $L$ is pinned at two points (e.g., by insoluble precipitates, grain boundaries, or network nodes).
2. Under applied shear stress $\tau$, the segment bows out into a circular arc.
3. As $\tau$ increases, the radius of curvature decreases until $r = L/2$ (semicircular configuration).
4. At this point, the segment wraps around the pinning points. The two arms meet on the far side, annihilate, and **release a closed dislocation loop**.
5. The original segment is regenerated and can produce more loops — this is the **Frank-Read source**, a self-sustaining dislocation multiplication mechanism.

**Critical Shear Stress Derivation (2 marks):**
The line tension model treats the dislocation as an elastic string with tension $T \approx \dfrac{Gb^2}{2}$. At the critical semicircular configuration, $r = L/2$:

$$
\tau_{FR}\cdot b = \frac{T}{r_{min}} = \frac{Gb^2/2}{L/2} = \frac{Gb}{L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{FR} = \frac{Gb}{L}}
$$

**Strain Hardening Role (1 mark):**
Frank-Read sources exponentially multiply dislocations during plastic deformation. The increased dislocation density leads to frequent interactions, jog formation, and forest-dislocation cutting — all of which act as obstacles to further motion. This raises the CRSS, manifesting as the macroscopic phenomenon of **strain hardening (work hardening)** observed in tensile tests of ductile metals.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong slip-plane statement for screw dislocations:** Many students write "screw dislocations have a unique slip plane." This is WRONG — screw dislocations can cross-slip onto any plane containing the line, which is why they are more mobile.
> 2. **Confusing $\phi$ and $\lambda$ in Schmid's Law:** The angle $\phi$ is with the slip-plane **normal**, and $\lambda$ is with the slip **direction**. Marks deducted for swap.
> 3. **Forgetting $\nu$ in the edge dislocation energy formula:** The $(1-\nu)$ denominator is critical for edge but absent for screw — examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Not specifying units in dislocation density:** Always state **lines/m²** (or m/m³) — bare numerical answers lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing "glide" and "climb":** Glide = conservative motion on the slip plane. Climb = non-conservative motion perpendicular to the slip plane, requires vacancy diffusion. Examiners want this distinction explicitly stated.
> 6. **Drawing Burgers circuit incorrectly:** The FS/RH convention requires both the real and reference crystal circuits to be drawn with **the same number of steps in the same direction**. Marks lost if the circuit is malformed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Line Defects (Module 2)**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - Line defects = dislocations; dimension = 1D; length ~ $10^{-8}$ m.
> - Three types: **edge** ($\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$), **screw** ($\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$), **mixed** (curved, most common).
> - Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ = magnitude and direction of lattice distortion.
> - Slip = conservative motion on slip plane. Climb = non-conservative, requires vacancy diffusion.
>
> **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
> - $\rho = L/V$ (dislocation density)
> - $b_{FCC} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2}$, $b_{BCC} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$
> - $\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)}\ln(R/r_0)$ (edge)
> - $\dfrac{E}{L} = \dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi}\ln(R/r_0)$ (screw)
> - $\tau_{FR} = \dfrac{Gb}{L}$ (Frank-Read source)
> - $\tau_{CRSS} = \sigma\cos\phi\cos\lambda$ (Schmid's Law)
> - Schmid factor max = 0.5 (at $\phi = \lambda = 45°$)
>
> **High-Yield Engineering Implications:**
> - Cold working ↑ $\rho$ → ↑ yield strength (strain hardening).
> - Annealing ↓ $\rho$ → ↓ hardness (recovery, recrystallization).
> - Frank-Read source = primary dislocation multiplication mechanism.
> - Edge dislocations store ~ 1.5× more energy than screw dislocations (Poisson's ratio effect).
> - Slip in FCC: 12 systems → ductile. Slip in HCP: 3 systems → brittle.
>
> **Common Exam Traps:**
> - Screw dislocation: NO unique slip plane (can cross-slip).
> - Edge dislocation: HAS a unique slip plane (locked to one plane).
> - Schmid factor cannot exceed 0.5 for a physically admissible slip system.
> - Burgers vector is invariant along the dislocation line (conserved quantity).
> - Total Burgers vector of all dislocations meeting at a node = 0 (Kirchhoff's law for dislocations).
>
> **Quick Mnemonic:** "**E**dge has **E**xtra half-plane, **S**crew has **S**piral" — visualize both, then derive.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surface Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Defects (also called Planar Defects or Interfacial Defects)** are two-dimensional imperfections in the crystalline lattice that span across a plane within a material. They represent boundaries across which the crystallographic orientation, composition, or atomic stacking sequence of the lattice changes abruptly. These defects are critical because they directly influence the **mechanical strength, ductility, electrical conductivity, corrosion resistance, and diffusional behavior** of engineering materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Surface defects are 2D imperfections that occur at the boundary between two distinct regions of a crystal — including grain boundaries, sub-grain boundaries, twin boundaries, stacking faults, anti-phase boundaries, and free external surfaces."

### 1.2 Position within the Defect Classification Hierarchy

Crystallographic defects are classified by their **dimensionality** (D = 0, 1, 2, 3):

| Dimension | Defect Type | Examples |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **0D** (Point Defects) | Atomic scale | Vacancies, Interstitials, Substitutional atoms |
| **1D** (Line Defects) | One-dimensional | Edge, Screw, and Mixed Dislocations |
| **2D** (Surface/Planar Defects) | Two-dimensional | Grain boundaries, Twin boundaries, Stacking faults, Free surfaces |
| **3D** (Volume Defects) | Three-dimensional | Voids, Precipitates, Inclusions, Cracks |

> [!NOTE]
> The current module focuses on the **2D category**. However, for KTU examinations, students are frequently asked comparative questions across all four categories.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Brick Wall" Model

Imagine a freshly built **brick wall** where each brick is an atom perfectly stacked:

- **A missing brick** = a *vacancy* (0D point defect)
- **A brick that slipped one row** = a *dislocation* (1D line defect)
- **A crack running across the wall** between two separately-built sections = a *surface defect* (2D planar defect)
- **A large air-pocket / hollow region** inside the wall = a *volume defect* (3D)

A **surface defect** is essentially a "seam" where two different crystal regions meet. Just as the seam between two adjacent bricks-built-by-different-masons might be weaker, the boundary between two grains of a metal is usually a region of misaligned atoms — and this misalignment dictates the macroscopic strength of the entire wall.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Most engineering failures (e.g., a fractured gear tooth, a ruptured boiler tube) initiate at grain boundaries. Controlling surface defects is the foundation of **heat treatment, alloying, and grain-size engineering** in metallurgy.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

- **Grain Size (d)** — measured in **micrometers (µm)** or via the **ASTM grain size number (G)**
- **Disorientation Angle (θ)** — measured in **degrees (°)**
- **Burgers Vector (b)** — measured in **nanometers (nm)**
- **Stacking Fault Energy (SFE / γ_SF)** — measured in **mJ/m²** or **ergs/cm²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Surface Defects

Surface defects in crystalline materials are categorized based on the **nature of the discontinuity** at the interface. Below is a structured analysis of every major subtype expected in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 Grain Boundaries (GB)

A **grain boundary** is the interface separating two adjacent grains (crystallites) in a polycrystalline material. Since adjacent grains have different crystallographic orientations, the atomic bonding across the boundary is imperfect, giving rise to a region of **distorted, higher-energy atoms**.

**Types of Grain Boundaries:**

**(a) High-Angle Grain Boundaries (HAGB)**
- Misorientation angle: **θ > 15°**
- Atoms are highly disordered at the interface
- Generally higher energy
- Most grain boundaries in conventional metals are HAGBs

**(b) Low-Angle Grain Boundaries (LAGB) — Sub-grain Boundaries**
- Misorientation angle: **θ < 15°**
- Can be visualized as an **array of edge dislocations** stacked along the boundary
- Composed of two subtypes:
  - **Tilt Boundary**: rotation axis is **parallel** to the boundary plane
  - **Twist Boundary**: rotation axis is **perpendicular** to the boundary plane

**(c) Coincident Site Lattice (CSL) Boundaries**
- Special boundaries where a fraction of lattice points coincide across the interface
- Designated by the **Σ (Sigma) value** = reciprocal of the fraction of coincident sites
- Lower energy, more resistant to corrosion and sliding
- Examples: Σ3 (twin), Σ5, Σ7

### 2.1.2 Twin Boundaries

A **twin boundary** is a special, mirror-symmetric planar defect across which the lattice on one side is the **mirror image** of the lattice on the other side. There is no atomic misfit — atoms are perfectly bonded across the interface.

**Two major types:**

| Type | Mechanism | Example Material |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **Annealing Twin** | Forms during recrystallization at high temperatures | FCC metals (Cu, Brass, Austenitic SS) |
| **Mechanical / Deformation Twin** | Forms under applied stress (especially at low temperatures or high strain rates) | HCP metals (Zn, Mg, Ti), BCC (α-Fe at low T) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Twin boundaries do **not** extend the crystal across the interface — they are mirror-symmetric. This is what distinguishes them from ordinary grain boundaries.

### 2.1.3 Stacking Faults

A **stacking fault** is a planar defect arising from an **error in the stacking sequence** of atomic planes.

- For FCC metals, the normal stacking sequence is **ABCABCABC…**
  - **Intrinsic Stacking Fault (ISF)**: a layer is missing → **ABC A~~C~~ ABC**
  - **Extrinsic Stacking Fault (ESF)**: an extra layer is inserted → **ABC A B~~A~~ BC**
- Energy associated: **Stacking Fault Energy (SFE / γ_SF)**
  - **Low SFE** → wide stacking faults, deformation by twinning (e.g., Austenitic SS ~ 20 mJ/m²)
  - **High SFE** → narrow stacking faults, deformation by slip (e.g., Aluminum ~ 200 mJ/m²)

### 2.1.4 Anti-Phase Boundaries (APB)

- Occur in **ordered alloys / intermetallic compounds** (e.g., Ni₃Al, CuAu, CuZn)
- At the APB, the regular ordering of unlike atoms is disrupted
- Bounded by a pair of **partial dislocations** known as a *super-dislocation*
- Increases the energy of the alloy

### 2.1.5 Phase Boundaries

The interface between two distinct **phases** in a material (e.g., ferrite-austenite interface in steel, α-β interface in Ti-alloys).

- **Coherent**: lattice planes match perfectly across the boundary
- **Semi-Coherent**: periodic misfit dislocations accommodate lattice mismatch
- **Incoherent**: no lattice matching — high-energy interface

### 2.1.6 Free (External) Surfaces

- The outermost atomic plane of a crystal exposed to the environment
- Each surface atom has **dangling/un-saturated bonds**
- This creates **surface energy (γ)** — typically **1–3 J/m²** for metals
- Surface energy drives phenomena like adsorption, surface diffusion, and sintering

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{b}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$ | Low-angle tilt boundary dislocation spacing | m |
| 2 | $G = \text{ASTM grain size number}$ | Empirical grain size designation (linear scale) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_L = \dfrac{\text{Number of grains}}{\text{Unit length}}$ | Lineal intercept density of grains | mm⁻¹ |
| 4 | $d^{-1} = \dfrac{N_L}{2}$ | Inverse relation: grain size $\propto$ intercept count | — |
| 5 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | **Hall-Petch Equation** (grain-boundary strengthening) | MPa |
| 6 | $\Delta \sigma = k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Strengthening contribution from grain refinement | MPa |
| 7 | $N = 2^{G-1}$ | Number of grains per square inch at 100× magnification | grains/in² |
| 8 | $\gamma_{GB} = \gamma_s \cdot \sin(\theta/2) \cdot (1 - \sin(\theta/2))$ | Read-Shockley energy for low-angle GB | J/m² |
| 9 | $\Sigma = \dfrac{\text{Total lattice sites}}{\text{Coincident sites}}$ | CSL boundary Sigma value | dimensionless |
| 10 | $b = \dfrac{a}{6} \langle 112 \rangle$ | Shockley partial Burgers vector (FCC) | nm |
| 11 | $\gamma_{SF} \uparrow \;\Rightarrow\; W_{SF} \downarrow$ | Stacking fault width inversely related to SFE | mJ/m² |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Surface defects are not just academic curiosities — they are **engineered features** in modern industry:

1. **Steel Industry** — Fine-grained steel (via thermomechanical processing) leverages the Hall-Petch relation to achieve high strength.
2. **Turbine Blades** — Single-crystal superalloys (used in jet engines) deliberately **eliminate grain boundaries** in the load direction to prevent creep failure at 1000 °C+.
3. **Semiconductor Industry** — CSL grain boundaries with low Σ-values are used in polysilicon solar cells for higher carrier mobility.
4. **Nanotechnology** — Nanocrystalline materials (grain size < 100 nm) exhibit exceptional strength but reduced ductility due to grain-boundary sliding.
5. **Corrosion Engineering** — Sensitization of austenitic stainless steel is a grain-boundary phenomenon (Cr-carbide precipitation at GB).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Low-Angle Tilt Boundary Geometry

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Consider two adjacent sub-grains misoriented by a small angle **θ** about an axis lying in the boundary plane. The boundary is composed of a **vertical array of edge dislocations**, each having Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ perpendicular to the dislocation line.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Derivation

Let:
- $h$ = vertical spacing between successive dislocations in the array
- $b$ = magnitude of the Burgers vector
- $\theta$ = tilt angle (in radians, for small angles)

For a small tilt angle, the geometry yields the relation:

$$
\theta \;\approx\; \dfrac{b}{h}
$$

Rearranging for the dislocation spacing $h$:

$$
h \;=\; \dfrac{b}{\theta}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For larger tilt angles where the small-angle approximation breaks down, the exact relationship is given by the **Read-Shockley formula**:
>
> $$D = \dfrac{b}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Example
**Question:** A low-angle tilt boundary in copper (Burgers vector $b = 0.256$ nm) has a tilt angle of $\theta = 1°$. Find the dislocation spacing.

**Given:**
- $b = 0.256 \text{ nm} = 0.256 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $\theta = 1° = \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ rad $\approx 0.01745$ rad

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
h = \dfrac{b}{\theta}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
h = \dfrac{0.256 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}{0.01745}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator/denominator ratio:**

$$
h = \dfrac{0.256 \times 10^{-9}}{0.01745}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
h \approx 14.67 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 14.67 \text{ nm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The edge dislocations are spaced approximately **14.67 nm apart** along the boundary. This very small spacing explains why low-angle boundaries are not usually visible under an optical microscope.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hall-Petch Strengthening Relation

### 3.2.1 Conceptual Foundation
A dislocation pile-up against a grain boundary generates a stress concentration at the boundary tip. The applied stress needed to propagate slip into the next grain scales inversely with the square-root of the grain size.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Consider a slip band of length $L = d$ (the grain diameter) piling up $n$ dislocations. The shear stress at the head of the pile-up is amplified by a factor of $n$:

$$
\tau_{head} = n \cdot \tau_{applied}
$$

From dislocation pile-up theory (Eshelby–Frank–Nabarro):

$$
n = \dfrac{k \pi d \tau_{applied}}{G b}
$$

where:
- $k$ = a constant (~1)
- $G$ = shear modulus
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude
- $d$ = grain size

Yielding occurs when the head stress equals a critical value $\tau_c$:

$$
\tau_c = n \cdot \tau_y = \dfrac{k \pi d \tau_y^2}{G b}
$$

Solving for the yield stress $\tau_y$:

$$
\tau_y^2 = \dfrac{\tau_c \cdot G b}{k \pi d}
$$

Taking the square root:

$$
\tau_y = \tau_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

In normal tensile stress notation:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_0$ = friction stress (lattice resistance)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (material-dependent)
- $d$ = average grain diameter

### 3.2.3 Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hall-Petch Insight:** Halving the grain size (e.g., from 100 µm to 50 µm) increases the yield strength by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is the fundamental reason why **fine-grained metals are stronger** than coarse-grained ones — and it is the mathematical basis of every grain-refinement process (e.g., normalizing, controlled rolling, addition of grain refiners like Nb/Ti in HSLA steel).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stacking Fault Width and Partial Dislocations

In FCC metals, a perfect dislocation $\vec{b}_{perfect} = \dfrac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle$ dissociates into two **Shockley partial dislocations** separated by a ribbon of stacking fault of width $w$:

$$
\dfrac{a}{2}\langle 110 \rangle \;\longrightarrow\; \dfrac{a}{6}\langle 211 \rangle \;+\; \dfrac{a}{6}\langle 21\bar{1} \rangle
$$

Force balance at equilibrium — the **repulsive elastic force** between the two partials equals the **attractive surface-tension force** of the stacking fault:

$$
F_{elastic} = F_{surface\ tension}
$$

The elastic repulsion per unit length between two parallel partials separated by distance $w$ is:

$$
F_{elastic} = \dfrac{G b_p^2}{8 \pi w} \cdot \dfrac{2 - \nu}{1 - \nu}
$$

where $b_p = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{6}}$ is the magnitude of each partial Burgers vector.

Equating to the stacking fault energy $\gamma_{SF}$:

$$
\dfrac{G b_p^2}{8 \pi w} \cdot \dfrac{2 - \nu}{1 - \nu} = \gamma_{SF}
$$

Solving for the equilibrium width $w$:

$$
w = \dfrac{G b_p^2}{8 \pi \gamma_{SF}} \cdot \dfrac{2 - \nu}{1 - \nu}
$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Example
**Question:** In a copper crystal, $G = 48$ GPa, $b_p = 0.148$ nm, $\nu = 0.34$, $\gamma_{SF} = 78$ mJ/m². Calculate the equilibrium stacking fault width.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the equation:**

$$
w = \dfrac{(48 \times 10^9) \cdot (0.148 \times 10^{-9})^2}{8 \pi \cdot (78 \times 10^{-3})} \cdot \dfrac{2 - 0.34}{1 - 0.34}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared Burgers vector:**

$$
b_p^2 = (0.148)^2 \times 10^{-18} = 0.0219 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
\text{Num} = 48 \times 10^9 \times 0.0219 \times 10^{-18} = 1.052 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
\text{Den} = 8 \pi \times 0.078 = 1.961
$$

**Step 5 — Combine with the Poisson correction factor:**

$$
w = \dfrac{1.052 \times 10^{-9}}{1.961} \cdot \dfrac{1.66}{0.66}
$$

$$
w = 5.365 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 2.515
$$

$$
\boxed{w \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 1.35 \text{ nm}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Copper has a relatively high SFE, so the two partials are bound tightly together (only ~1.35 nm apart), making perfect dislocation behavior dominant. Austenitic stainless steel, with a much lower SFE, exhibits **wide stacking fault ribbons** facilitating deformation twinning.

---

| Defect Type | Dimensionality | Energy Scale | Effect on Strength | KTU Frequency |
|-------------|----------------|--------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Vacancy | 0D | ~1 eV | Marginal (solution hardening) | High |
| Edge Dislocation | 1D | ~5 eV/b | Major (work hardening) | Very High |
