# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between high-angle and low-angle grain boundaries. Mention the role of dislocation arrays in sub-grain boundaries.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Low-Angle GB (LAGB) | High-Angle GB (HAGB) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Misorientation angle | $\theta < 15°$ | $\theta > 15°$ |
| Atomic structure | Ordered array of dislocations | Highly disordered |
| Energy | Low (Read-Shockley) | High and nearly constant |
| Dislocation model | Discrete dislocation spacing | No simple model |

**Role of dislocations in sub-grain boundaries:** Low-angle grain boundaries (sub-grain boundaries) can be modeled as **regular arrays of dislocations**. A tilt boundary consists of a vertical row of edge dislocations spaced at $h = b / \theta$, while a twist boundary consists of a cross-grid of screw dislocations. As the misorientation increases, dislocation cores overlap (around $\theta \approx 15°$), beyond which the boundary becomes high-angle and the discrete dislocation model no longer applies.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define stacking fault and stacking fault energy (SFE). How does low SFE influence the deformation mode of FCC metals?**

**Model Answer:**

A **stacking fault** is a planar defect in which the regular stacking sequence of atomic close-packed planes is interrupted (one or more planes are missing or added). In FCC crystals, the perfect sequence ABCABC is locally disrupted, creating a region bounded by partial dislocations.

**Stacking Fault Energy (SFE)** is the energy per unit area of this faulted region, denoted $\gamma_{SF}$ and measured in **mJ/m²**.

**Influence of low SFE:**
- Wide separation of partial dislocations
- Cross-slip is **difficult** (screw dislocations cannot easily change glide planes)
- Deformation occurs preferentially by **twinning** rather than slip
- Materials exhibit high work-hardening rates
- Example: Austenitic stainless steel ($\gamma_{SF} \approx 20$ mJ/m²)

In contrast, **high SFE metals** (e.g., aluminum) show narrow stacking fault ribbons, easy cross-slip, and dislocation-based plasticity (Wavy Slip).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) Pattern: Each module carries either a 7-mark sub-question or a 14-mark long answer. The questions below follow the KTU model with internal choice between two fully-solved alternatives.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the structure and energy of grain boundaries. With a neat diagram, describe the Read-Shockley model for low-angle tilt boundaries. Derive the relation between dislocation spacing and tilt angle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A low-angle tilt boundary in aluminum has a misorientation of 2°. The Burgers vector of edge dislocations is 0.286 nm. Calculate (i) the spacing between dislocations along the boundary, and (ii) the number of dislocations per mm length of the boundary.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A(a):**

**1. Structure of Grain Boundaries:**
- A grain boundary is the interface between two crystals (grains) of the same phase but different crystallographic orientations.
- The transition region is typically **2–5 atomic diameters wide**.
- Within this region, atoms are **distorted** and possess higher potential energy than bulk atoms.

**2. Energy of Grain Boundaries:**
- Grain boundary energy $\gamma_{GB}$ arises from the broken/imperfect bonds at the interface.
- It is typically **0.5–1.0 J/m²** for high-angle GBs.
- It is the **driving force** for grain growth (boundary area reduction).

**3. Read-Shockley Model for Low-Angle Tilt Boundary:**

```
       |     |     |     |     |     |     |
       |     |     |     |     |     |     |    <- edge dislocations
       |     |     |     |     |     |     |       stacked vertically
   ----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+---- boundary plane
       |     |     |     |     |     |     |
       |     |     |     |     |     |     |
```

Two sub-grains, A (left) and B (right), are tilted relative to each other by a small angle $\theta$. The boundary is modelled as a vertical array of edge dislocations each of Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ perpendicular to the dislocation line and parallel to the boundary plane.

**4. Derivation of the Read-Shockley Relation:**

Let the spacing between dislocations be $h$. The cumulative "step" produced by $N$ dislocations over a length $L$ is $N \cdot b = L \cdot \theta$.

For $N$ dislocations, $N = L / h$:

$$
\dfrac{L}{h} \cdot b = L \cdot \theta
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{b}{h}}
$$

Rearranging for the spacing:

$$
h = \dfrac{b}{\theta}
$$

The Read-Shockley energy (per unit area) is:

$$
\gamma_{GB} = \gamma_0 \theta (A - \ln \theta)
$$

where $\gamma_0 = \dfrac{Gb}{4\pi(1-\nu)}$ and $A$ is a constant. This energy saturates near $\theta \approx 15°$, beyond which the boundary becomes a high-angle GB.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-A(b):**

**Given:**
- Tilt angle: $\theta = 2° = \dfrac{2\pi}{180} = 0.0349$ rad
- Burgers vector: $b = 0.286$ nm $= 0.286 \times 10^{-9}$ m

**(i) Dislocation spacing $h$:**

$$
h = \dfrac{b}{\theta} = \dfrac{0.286 \times 10^{-9}}{0.0349}
$$

$$
h = 8.195 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} \approx 8.2 \text{ nm}
$$

**(ii) Number of dislocations per mm:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{h} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{8.195 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}
$$

$$
N = 1.22 \times 10^{5} \text{ dislocations per mm}
$$

**Result:** Dislocation spacing is **~8.2 nm** and the boundary contains approximately **1.22 × 10⁵ dislocations per mm** of length.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the structure of (i) annealing twins and (ii) mechanical twins. Compare their formation conditions and effects on material properties.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polycrystalline brass specimen has an average grain diameter of 80 µm. After a thermo-mechanical treatment, the grain size is reduced to 20 µm. Given $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa and $k_y = 0.11$ MPa·m^(1/2), calculate the increase in yield strength using the Hall-Petch relation.

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B(a):**

**(i) Annealing Twins:**
- Form during **recrystallization** when a new grain grows with a twinned orientation relative to its neighbour.
- Common in **FCC metals** with low-to-medium SFE: copper, brass, austenitic stainless steel, nickel.
- The twin plane is typically {111} in FCC.
- They have **straight, parallel-sided morphology**.
- Energy is very low ($\sim 0.02$ J/m²), so they are thermodynamically stable.

**(ii) Mechanical / Deformation Twins:**
- Form under **applied stress**, often at **low temperatures** or **high strain rates**.
- The twinning plane is {10-12} in HCP, {112} in BCC, {111} in FCC.
- Produce a sudden shear displacement of the lattice.
- Common in HCP metals (Zn, Mg, Ti) and BCC metals at low temperatures.
- Often have **lens-shaped or lenticular** morphology.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | Annealing Twin | Mechanical Twin |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Driving force | Recrystallization energy | Applied shear stress |
| Temperature | High (above recrystallization) | Often low or dynamic |
| Strain rate | Not significant | High rates favour twinning |
| Morphology | Straight, parallel sides | Lens-shaped, lenticular |
| Material preference | Low-SFE FCC | HCP, low-T BCC |
| Effect on properties | Minor hardening, refines microstructure | Significant hardening, possible embrittlement |

**Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   Annealing Twin (Straight, Parallel)        Mechanical Twin (Lens-shaped)
   |     |     |     |     |                    _____
   |     |     |     |     |                  /     \____
   |     |     |     |     |           ______/          \____
   |_____|_____|_____|_____|         __/                       \__
       Grain 1  |  Grain 2          /                             \
                |                                              \____
              Twin Plane                                      Twin Plane
```

---

#### **Model Solution to B-B(b):**

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 80$ µm $= 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $d_2 = 20$ µm $= 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\sigma_0 = 25$ MPa
- $k_y = 0.11$ MPa·m^(1/2)

**Step 1 — Initial yield strength:**

$$
\sigma_{y1} = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d_1^{-1/2}
$$

$$
d_1^{-1/2} = (80 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \sqrt{12500} = 111.80 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y1} = 25 + 0.11 \times 111.80 = 25 + 12.30 = 37.30 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Final yield strength:**

$$
d_2^{-1/2} = (20 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \sqrt{50000} = 223.61 \text{ m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y2} = 25 + 0.11 \times 223.61 = 25 + 24.60 = 49.60 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Increase in yield strength:**

$$
\Delta \sigma = \sigma_{y2} - \sigma_{y1} = 49.60 - 37.30 = 12.30 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Alternatively, the **direct increment formula** is:
>
> $$\Delta \sigma = k_y \left( d_2^{-1/2} - d_1^{-1/2} \right)$$
>
> $$\Delta \sigma = 0.11 \times (223.61 - 111.80) = 0.11 \times 111.81 = 12.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** The yield strength **increases by 12.3 MPa** (i.e., 33% improvement) simply by reducing the grain size from 80 µm to 20 µm.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Twin Boundary with Grain Boundary** — Twin boundaries are mirror-symmetric; ordinary grain boundaries are not. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Hall-Petch** — $d$ must be in **metres** and $k_y$ in **MPa·m^(1/2)** to get MPa. Many students use $d$ in mm or µm and get wrong answers.
> 3. **Skipping the Step in Read-Shockley Derivation** — Always show the geometric step: $N \cdot b = L \cdot \theta$ followed by $N = L/h$. A direct jump to $\theta = b/h$ loses derivation marks.
> 4. **Mixing Annealing and Mechanical Twins** — They have different driving forces, morphologies, and formation conditions. Mixing them is a frequent error.
> 5. **Forgetting to Convert Degrees to Radians** — Trigonometric dislocation-spacing formulas need radians, not degrees.
> 6. **Not Drawing the Diagram** — In 14-mark questions, **a labelled sketch carries 1–2 marks** explicitly. Skipping diagrams is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Surface Defects:**

- **Definition:** 2D interfacial defects; the most important category in polycrystalline materials.
- **Main Sub-Types:** (1) High-angle grain boundaries, (2) Low-angle (sub-grain) boundaries, (3) Twin boundaries, (4) Stacking faults, (5) Anti-phase boundaries, (6) Free surfaces, (7) Phase boundaries.
- **HAGB vs LAGB threshold:** $\theta = 15°$ (universally accepted).
- **Tilt vs Twist boundary:** Tilt = rotation axis in boundary plane; Twist = rotation axis perpendicular to boundary plane.
- **Twin boundary:** Mirror-symmetric; very low energy ($\sim 0.02$ J/m²); does not disrupt bonding.
- **Stacking fault:** Disruption in ABC stacking sequence; bounded by Shockley partials.
- **SFE Trend:** $\text{Al} > \text{Cu} > \text{Austenitic SS}$ — higher SFE means narrower SF, easier cross-slip.
- **CSL boundary:** Designated by $\Sigma$ value; lower $\Sigma$ = lower energy = better properties.
- **Hall-Petch Equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ — grain refinement strengthens the material.
- **Read-Shockley:** $h = b / \theta$ — dislocation spacing in a low-angle tilt boundary.
- **APB:** Occurs only in **ordered alloys** (e.g., Ni₃Al, CuZn, CuAu).
- **Free surface:** Highest energy (1–3 J/m²); dangling bonds drive adsorption, sintering, surface diffusion.
- **Engineering applications:** Fine-grained steels (Hall-Petch), single-crystal turbine blades (GB elimination), polysilicon solar cells (CSL engineering), austenitic SS sensitization (GB Cr-carbides).
- **Cross-Connection:** Surface defects are linked to **grain-boundary strengthening, recrystallization, creep, sensitization, and Hall-Petch relations** — expect integrated questions.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *"Grain boundaries block, twins mirror, stacking faults shift, and surfaces seal — together they define the material's response to every load, current, and corrosive environment."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Volume Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Volume Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Defects (3-D Imperfections):** These are three-dimensional crystallographic imperfections that extend in all three spatial dimensions within a crystalline solid. Unlike point defects (zero-dimensional) or line/area defects (one/two-dimensional), volume defects possess measurable length, width, and height, and their dimensions typically exceed several atomic diameters.

Volume defects in crystalline materials are essentially **microscopic and macroscopic discontinuities** in the otherwise periodic atomic arrangement of a solid. According to the KTU 2024 scheme classification of crystalline imperfections, volume defects include:

1. **Voids / Porosity** – empty cavities where atoms are missing from a cluster region.
2. **Inclusions** – foreign particles (oxides, slag, sand) embedded inside the bulk matrix.
3. **Precipitates** – secondary phase particles formed due to phase transformation or aging.
4. **Cracks** – sharp internal discontinuities acting as stress raisers.
5. **Blowholes and Gas Porosity** – spherical cavities formed by trapped gases during solidification.

The standard dimensional metric is the **critical defect size**, often expressed in micrometres (µm) or millimetres (mm), with typical magnitudes ranging from **1 µm to several mm**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sponge Cake Model 🍰**
>
> Imagine a perfectly baked, uniform cake (this represents an ideal, defect-free crystal lattice). Now consider a real cake you bake at home:
> - It has small **air pockets** (these are *voids*).
> - Occasionally a **raisin or chocolate chip** falls inside (these are *inclusions*).
> - Sometimes the cake develops a **hairline fracture** when it cools (this is a *crack*).
> - The chocolate chips that form a separate solid chunk in the cake represent a *precipitate*.
>
> The cake is still "solid," but it is no longer perfectly continuous. These embedded pockets and foreign bodies are **volume defects** — they disturb the continuity of the material in 3D space.

In engineering alloys, a polished cross-section under a microscope reveals these defects as **dark spots, irregular patches, or sharp fissures**, distributed randomly across the microstructure.

---

## 1.3 Why Volume Defects Matter in Engineering

| Engineering Aspect | Influence of Volume Defects |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Strength** | Cracks and voids reduce load-bearing cross-section |
| **Fatigue Life** | Internal voids act as fatigue crack nucleation sites |
| **Ductility** | Porosity reduces plastic deformation capacity |
| **Density** | Voids lower the bulk density of components |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Inclusions create galvanic micro-cells |
| **Machinability** | Hard inclusions cause tool wear |
| **Thermal/Electrical Conductivity** | Interfaces scatter phonons/electrons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Questions on volume defects are frequently linked to **failure analysis, fracture mechanics, and casting quality control**. Always relate these defects to real engineering processes (casting, welding, powder metallurgy, 3D printing).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Hierarchy of Volume Defects

Volume defects are broadly divided into **intrinsic** and **extrinsic** categories:

### A. Intrinsic Volume Defects (Formed During Processing)

1. **Voids (Micro/Macro Porosity)**
   - Empty spaces where multiple atomic sites are vacant in a cluster.
   - Formed due to:
     - Solidification shrinkage in castings.
     - Insufficient feeding of molten metal.
     - Sintering incompleteness in powder metallurgy.
   - Typically **spherical or irregular** in shape.
   - Critical parameter: **void volume fraction (f_v)**.

2. **Microcracks**
   - Sharp discontinuities with **high aspect ratio** (length >> width).
   - Formed due to:
     - Thermal stresses during quenching.
     - Hydrogen embrittlement in steels.
     - Phase transformation strains (e.g., martensite formation).
   - Governed by **linear elastic fracture mechanics (LEFM)**.

3. **Blowholes and Gas Porosity**
   - Spherical cavities formed by **trapped gases** (H₂, N₂, CO) during solidification.
   - Common in **sand castings** with high moisture content.
   - Governed by **Sievert's Law** for gas solubility.

### B. Extrinsic Volume Defects (Foreign-Body Inclusions)

1. **Inclusions**
   - **Non-metallic inclusions:** oxides (Al₂O₃, SiO₂), sulphides (MnS), silicates.
   - **Metallic inclusions:** foreign metal particles from slag or tooling.
   - Originate from: deoxidation, slag entrapment, refractory erosion, ladle refractory wear.

2. **Precipitates**
   - Secondary phase particles that form within the matrix during **aging, tempering, or cooling**.
   - Examples:
     - **Al-Cu alloys:** θ′ (Al₂Cu) precipitates.
     - **Steels:** Fe₃C (cementite) in pearlite.
     - **Ni-superalloys:** γ′ (Ni₃(Al,Ti)) precipitates.
   - Strengthen the matrix via **Orowan looping** and **shearing mechanisms**.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical & Physical Framework

### 2.2.1 Volume Fraction of Defects

The **volume fraction (f_v)** of porosity or second-phase particles is a key metallurgical parameter:

$$f_v = \frac{V_{defect}}{V_{total}}$$

where $V_{defect}$ is the volume occupied by defects and $V_{total}$ is the total volume of the specimen.

For a two-phase composite containing a dispersed second phase:

$$f_v = \frac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3}{V}$$

where **n** is the number of spherical defects, **r** is the average radius, and **V** is the total volume.

---

### 2.2.2 Effect of Porosity on Density

The theoretical density of a porous material is:

$$\rho_{actual} = \rho_{theoretical} \times (1 - f_v)$$

This equation is used in **foundry quality control** to estimate the porosity level in cast components by simple density measurement (Archimedes' principle).

---

### 2.2.3 Stress Concentration Around a Spherical Void

A spherical void of radius **a** under a remote tensile stress **σ** produces a peak stress at the equator of:

$$\sigma_{max} = 2\sigma$$

This **stress concentration factor (K_t) = 2** is a standard result from elasticity theory for a spherical cavity in an infinite matrix. For ellipsoidal or crack-like voids, K_t can be much higher.

---

### 2.2.4 Griffith's Criterion for Crack Propagation (Critical Volume Defect)

For a sharp internal crack of half-length **a**, the critical applied stress for unstable crack propagation is:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$

where:
- **E** = Young's modulus (Pa)
- **γ_s** = specific surface energy (J/m²)
- **a** = half crack length (m)
- **σ_c** = critical fracture stress (Pa)

The corresponding **strain energy release rate (G)** is:

$$G = \frac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E}$$

---

### 2.2.5 Orowan Strengthening by Precipitates

When precipitates are non-shearable, dislocations bow between them, requiring additional stress:

$$\Delta \tau_{Orowan} = \frac{Gb}{L - 2r}$$

where:
- **G** = shear modulus
- **b** = Burgers vector
- **L** = inter-particle spacing
- **r** = precipitate radius

The inter-particle spacing is related to volume fraction:

$$L = r \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{2\pi}{3 f_v}\right)^{1/2} - \frac{\pi}{2} \right]$$

This explains why **higher precipitate volume fraction → smaller spacing → higher strength**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains the most examination-relevant equations for the topic "Volume Defects."

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_v = V_{defect} / V_{total}$ | Volume fraction of defects | dimensionless | Porosity estimation |
| 2 | $\rho_{actual} = \rho_{theory} (1 - f_v)$ | Density of porous material | kg/m³ | Foundry QC |
| 3 | $\sigma_{max} = 2\sigma$ | Stress at spherical void equator | Pa | Elastic stress concentration |
| 4 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ | Inglis stress concentration (elliptical hole) | dimensionless | Sharp notch/critical defect |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s / (\pi a)}$ | Griffith critical stress for crack | Pa | Fracture mechanics |
| 6 | $G_c = 2\gamma_s$ | Critical strain energy release rate | J/m² | Fracture toughness |
| 7 | $G_{IC} = K_{IC}^2 / E$ | Plane strain energy release rate | J/m² | LEFM |
| 8 | $\Delta \tau_{Orowan} = Gb / (L - 2r)$ | Orowan bowing stress | Pa | Precipitate strengthening |
| 9 | $L \approx r (2\pi / 3f_v)^{1/2}$ | Inter-particle spacing | m | Microstructural design |
| 10 | $C_{H} = k \sqrt{P_{H_2}}$ | Sievert's Law for gas solubility | ppm | Hydrogen porosity in castings |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Markdown Rule:** In all formula tables above, $\vert$ and absolute value expressions have been deliberately written using parentheses to prevent the markdown pipe character $\vert$ from breaking table syntax. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or $(x)$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ inside table cells.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Volume Defect Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Fatigue-critical defect sizing in turbine discs (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V) |
| **Nuclear** | Void swelling in irradiated reactor pressure vessels |
| **Foundry** | Shrinkage porosity prediction using solidification simulation |
| **Additive Manufacturing (3D Printing)** | Lack-of-fusion porosity in SLM/LPBF parts |
| **Welding** | Hydrogen-induced cracking in high-strength steel welds |
| **Ceramics** | Sintering porosity control in Al₂O₃, SiC |
| **Composites** | Interfacial void control in fibre-reinforced polymers |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Griffith's Criterion for Brittle Fracture (Critical Volume Defect)

This derivation is **board-essential** for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) for the topic of volume defects, especially when framed as a "crack" or "microcrack" type defect.

### Problem Statement
Derive the expression for the **critical applied stress** required to propagate a sharp internal crack of length **2a** in a brittle elastic solid.

### Given
- Plate thickness **t** (in 2D analysis, consider unit thickness).
- Sharp internal crack of **half-length = a**.
- Applied uniform tensile stress **σ** perpendicular to the crack.
- Young's modulus of material = **E**.
- Specific surface energy of material = **γ_s**.

### Assumptions
1. Material is perfectly **elastic and brittle** (no plastic deformation).
2. Crack propagates when the **net energy of the system decreases** (thermodynamic criterion).
3. Crack surfaces are **traction-free** after formation.

---

### Step 1: Define the Total Energy of the System

The total energy **U_total** of the cracked plate is the sum of:
- **Elastic strain energy** stored in the plate due to applied load.
- **Surface energy** required to create the new crack surfaces.

$$U_{total} = U_{elastic} + U_{surface}$$

---

### Step 2: Elastic Strain Energy in the Uncracked Plate

For a plate of unit thickness loaded in tension with stress **σ**, the elastic strain energy density is:

$$u_{elastic} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

For a plate of volume $V$ and unit thickness, the total elastic strain energy (before cracking) is:

$$U_{elastic}^{0} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot V$$

---

### Step 3: Energy Released by Crack Formation (Inglis Solution)

When a sharp crack of length **2a** forms, the stress field around the crack tip is relieved over a characteristic region. Inglis showed that the **elastic strain energy released** by creating the crack is:

$$U_{released} = \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} \quad \text{(per unit thickness)}$$

This represents the difference in stored elastic energy between the cracked and uncracked states.

---

### Step 4: Surface Energy Created by the New Crack

Creating a crack of length **2a** produces **two new surfaces** (top and bottom of the crack). Each surface has area $2a \times 1$ (per unit thickness). If the surface energy per unit area is **γ_s**, then:

$$U_{surface} = 2 \cdot (2a) \cdot \gamma_s = 4 a \gamma_s$$

---

### Step 5: Total Energy of the Cracked System

Subtracting the released energy from the original elastic energy and adding the surface energy:

$$U_{total} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot V - \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} + 4 a \gamma_s$$

---

### Step 6: Thermodynamic Criterion for Crack Propagation

The crack propagates spontaneously (unstable growth) when the **total energy decreases with increasing crack length**. Mathematically:

$$\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial a} \leq 0$$

Differentiating with respect to **a**:

$$\frac{\partial U_{total}}{\partial a} = -\frac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 4 \gamma_s$$

---

### Step 7: Set the Derivative to Zero (Critical Condition)

At the threshold of crack propagation (equilibrium / Griffith criterion):

$$-\frac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 4 \gamma_s = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{E} = 4 \gamma_s$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{4 \gamma_s E}{2 \pi a} = \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}$$

---

### Step 8: Final Griffith's Equation

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}}$$

This is the **critical stress** for unstable crack propagation in a brittle material containing a sharp internal crack of half-length **a**.

---

### Step 9: Physical Interpretation

| Observation | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| $\sigma_c \propto 1/\sqrt{a}$ | Doubling the crack length reduces strength by factor $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ |
| $\sigma_c \propto \sqrt{E}$ | Stiffer materials require higher stress to fracture |
| $\sigma_c \propto \sqrt{\gamma_s}$ | Higher surface energy = tougher material |
| Griffith predicts strength ~ E/10 (theoretical) vs. E/1000 (real) | Indicates the presence of sub-critical microcracks (Griffith flaw) |

---

### Step 10: Modified Griffith Equation (Irwin-Orowan Extension)

For materials exhibiting some plastic deformation at the crack tip, Griffith's equation is modified by replacing **2γ_s** with the **effective surface energy** $\gamma_{eff}$:

$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot (2\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}}$$

where **γ_p** is the plastic work done per unit area of crack advance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Porosity on Density (Two-Phase Composite Model)

### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a solid of total volume **V** containing spherical pores of total volume **V_p**.

$$V_{matrix} = V - V_p$$

### Step 2: Express the Volume Fraction
$$f_v = \frac{V_p}{V}$$

### Step 3: Mass Balance
The mass of the porous solid equals the mass of the matrix (since pores contain no material):

$$m = \rho_{matrix} \cdot (V - V_p) = \rho_{matrix} \cdot V (1 - f_v)$$

### Step 4: Actual (Bulk) Density
$$\rho_{actual} = \frac{m}{V} = \rho_{matrix} (1 - f_v)$$

For a material with theoretical density $\rho_{th}$ and measured density $\rho_{exp}$:

$$\boxed{f_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}}$$

### Step 5: Engineering Application
By measuring $\rho_{exp}$ using Archimedes' principle and comparing with $\rho_{th}$ from the phase composition, the **porosity fraction** can be directly calculated. This is a standard **KTU lab/exam** calculation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Volume Defects vs. Engineering Property

| Volume Defect | Affected Property | Quantitative Effect | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spherical void | Tensile strength | $\sigma_{max} = 2\sigma$ (elastic) | Hot isostatic pressing (HIP) |
| Sharp crack (length 2a) | Fracture strength | $\sigma_c \propto 1/\sqrt{a}$ (Griffith) | Shot peening, surface polishing |
| Non-metallic inclusion | Fatigue life | Reduces cycles to failure 10×–100× | Clean steel practices, filtration |
| Porosity (cluster) | Density | $\rho_{actual} = \rho_{th}(1-f_v)$ | Proper riser design, degassing |
| Hard precipitate | Wear resistance | Increases via precipitation hardening | Controlled aging heat treatment |
| Gas porosity | Pressure tightness | Permeation in hydraulic systems | Vacuum degassing, ladle metallurgy |
| Shrinkage cavity | Fatigue strength | Acts as crack initiation site | Directional solidification |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model 1]**
**Define volume defects in crystalline materials. Give two examples with their effect on mechanical properties.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Volume defects are three-dimensional crystallographic imperfections in crystalline solids that extend in all three spatial directions, with dimensions typically ranging from micrometres to millimetres.
>
> **Two Examples:**
>
> **(i) Voids / Porosity:** Empty cavities in the lattice formed during solidification shrinkage. *Effect:* Reduce tensile strength and fatigue life by acting as stress raisers and crack initiation sites.
>
> **(ii) Non-metallic inclusions (e.g., Al₂O₃, MnS):** Foreign oxide or sulphide particles embedded in the metal matrix. *Effect:* Cause stress concentration, reduce ductility, and act as fatigue crack nucleating sites; also reduce machinability and tool life.
>
> *(Examiner's Note: A clear definition + 2 named examples + property effects → full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 2]**
**Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic volume defects. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intrinsic volume defects** are those formed *within the parent material* due to the processing history, such as **voids, microcracks, blowholes, and shrinkage cavities**.
>
> **Extrinsic volume defects** are *foreign bodies or second phases* that enter the material from external sources or via phase transformations, such as **non-metallic inclusions (oxides, sulphides) and precipitates**.
>
> *(Examiner's Note: Mention one clear example of each, e.g., shrinkage porosity (intrinsic) and Al₂O₃ inclusion (extrinsic) → full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify volume defects in crystalline materials. Explain the formation mechanisms of **voids** and **non-metallic inclusions** in metallic alloys. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Griffith's criterion** for the critical stress required to propagate a sharp internal crack of half-length **a** in a brittle elastic material. State the engineering significance of the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Classification of Volume Defects:**

Volume defects in crystalline materials are classified as follows:

| Category | Sub-Type | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Intrinsic** | Voids, porosity | Solidification shrinkage, gas evolution |
| **Intrinsic** | Shrinkage cavities | Improper feeding during solidification |
| **Intrinsic** | Microcracks | Thermal stress, phase transformation strain |
| **Intrinsic** | Blowholes | Trapped gases (H₂, N₂) in castings |
| **Extrinsic** | Non-metallic inclusions | Deoxidation, slag entrapment, refractory wear |
| **Extrinsic** | Precipitates | Phase transformation, aging |

**Formation of Voids:**

Voids form by the following mechanisms:
1. **Solidification shrinkage:** Most metals contract by 3–7 % on solidification. If the liquid metal cannot feed the contracting region, vacancies coalesce into **microscopic voids**.
2. **Gas evolution:** Dissolved gases (H₂, N₂) become insoluble as the metal solidifies, nucleate as **gas bubbles** (blowholes) within the solidifying mass.
3. **Sintering porosity:** In powder metallurgy, incomplete densification leaves residual inter-particle voids.
4. **Radiation-induced voids:** In nuclear applications, neutron irradiation displaces atoms and creates vacancy clusters.

**Formation of Non-Metallic Inclusions:**

1. **Deoxidation products:** During steelmaking, oxygen is removed by adding Al, Si, or Mn, forming Al₂O₃, SiO₂, MnO, MnS. These oxides/sulphides remain suspended and become **inclusions**.
2. **Slag entrapment:** Mechanical mixing of slag droplets into the molten metal during tapping/pouring.
3. **Refractory erosion:** Erosion of ladle/tundish lining material during prolonged contact with molten metal.
4. **Reoxidation:** Exposing the molten stream to atmosphere re-oxidizes the metal, forming new oxide films that get entrapped.

*(7 marks: 2 marks classification table + 3 marks void formation + 2 marks inclusion formation)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Derivation of Griffith's Criterion:**

**Given:** A plate of unit thickness containing a sharp internal crack of half-length **a**, subjected to uniform tensile stress **σ** perpendicular to the crack.

**Step 1 — Total energy of the cracked system:**

$$U = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2E} - \frac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} + 4 a \gamma_s$$

The first term is the original elastic strain energy, the second is the strain energy **released** by crack formation (Inglis solution), and the third is the new **surface energy** created.

**Step 2 — Thermodynamic equilibrium condition:**

The crack is at the critical state when the total energy is stationary with respect to crack length:

$$\frac{\partial U}{\partial a} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate:**

$$\frac{\partial U}{\partial a} = -\frac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 4 \gamma_s = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for critical stress:**

$$2 \pi a \sigma^2 = 4 E \gamma_s$$

$$\sigma_c^2 = \frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}}$$

**Engineering Significance:**

1. **Inverse-square-root dependence on defect size:** Doubling the crack length reduces the fracture strength by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ — explains why **defect size control** is more critical than strength improvement in brittle materials.
2. **Explains Griffith's flaw hypothesis:** Real materials fail at stresses 100–1000 times lower than theoretical strength because of pre-existing microcracks (Griffith flaws) — these are **volume defects** in the form of sharp microcracks.
3. **Foundation of fracture mechanics:** Led to the development of **stress intensity factor (K)** and **fracture toughness (K_IC)** concepts, which are used in damage-tolerant design of aircraft, pressure vessels, and bridges.
4. **Material selection guidance:** The equation shows that improving $\gamma_s$ (toughness) is as important as improving E for fracture resistance.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for derivation with all 4 steps + 3 marks for engineering significance)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the formation of **precipitates** as volume defects in age-hardenable aluminium alloys. Derive the expression relating **inter-particle spacing (L)** to the **precipitate radius (r)** and **volume fraction (f_v)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A casting has a theoretical density of **7.85 g/cm³** but the actual measured density (by Archimedes' method) is **7.62 g/cm³**. Calculate the **porosity volume fraction**. If the casting contains 250 spherical micropores each of average radius **0.02 mm**, estimate the **total volume of porosity** in a sample of volume **5 cm³**. Comment on the effect of this porosity on the fatigue life of the component. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Formation of Precipitates in Age-Hardenable Al Alloys:**

In alloys such as **Al-Cu (2xxx series)**, **Al-Mg-Si (6xxx series)**, and **Al-Zn-Mg-Cu (7xxx series)**, the solubilities of alloying elements **decrease sharply with temperature**.

**Steps of Precipitate Formation:**

1. **Solution Treatment:** The alloy is heated to a temperature just below the solidus (e.g., Al-4% Cu at 500 °C) to dissolve all Cu into a single-phase α-solid solution, then **quenched** to room temperature to retain a **supersaturated solid solution (SSSS)**.

2. **Natural / Artificial Aging:** The supersaturated alloy is held at room temperature (natural aging) or moderately elevated temperature (e.g., 150–200 °C for artificial aging). This allows controlled **diffusion** of solute atoms to nucleate and grow fine precipitates.

3. **Precipitation Sequence (Al-Cu system):**
$$\text{SSSS} \rightarrow \text{GP Zones} \rightarrow \theta'' \rightarrow \theta' \rightarrow \theta \, (\text{Al}_2\text{Cu})$$

- **GP Zones:** Fully coherent, nanometre-sized Cu-rich clusters.
- **θ'' (theta-double-prime):** Coherent, plate-like, ordered.
- **θ′ (theta-prime):** Semi-coherent, the most effective strengthener.
- **θ (theta):** Equilibrium Al₂Cu, incoherent, coarse — over-aged condition.

**Significance:** These precipitates act as **volume defects** that obstruct dislocation motion, providing **precipitation hardening**.

**Derivation of Inter-Particle Spacing (L):**

For a uniform distribution of spherical precipitates of radius **r** with volume fraction **f_v**, in a unit cell of edge length **a** (containing one precipitate at the centre):

$$f_v = \frac{\text{Volume of precipitate}}{\text{Volume of unit cell}} = \frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3}{a^3}$$

Solving for the unit cell edge:

$$a = r \left( \frac{4\pi}{3 f_v} \right)^{1/3}$$

The inter-particle spacing **L** (face-to-face distance between neighbouring precipitates) is:

$$L = a - 2r = r \left[ \left( \frac{4\pi}{3 f_v} \right)^{1/3} - 2 \right]$$

For small $f_v$, this simplifies approximately to:

$$\boxed{L \approx r \left( \frac{2\pi}{3 f_v} \right)^{1/2}}$$

*(7 marks: 3 marks precipitation mechanism + 4 marks derivation)*

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Theoretical density: $\rho_{th} = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Actual measured density: $\rho_{exp} = 7.62 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Number of micropores: $n = 250$
- Average pore radius: $r = 0.02 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$
- Sample volume: $V_{sample} = 5 \text{ cm}^3$

---

**Step 1 — Calculate the Porosity Volume Fraction:**

$$f_v = 1 - \frac{\rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}$$

$$f_v = 1 - \frac{7.62}{7.85}$$

$$f_v = 1 - 0.9707 = 0.0293$$

$$\boxed{f_v \approx 0.0293 \text{ or } 2.93\%}$$

---

**Step 2 — Calculate Volume of a Single Spherical Pore:**

$$V_{pore} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \pi (2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm})^3$$

$$V_{pore} = \frac{4}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{pore} = 3.351 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^3 \text{ per pore}$$

---

**Step 3 — Total Volume of Porosity (from pore count):**

$$V_{pores} = n \times V_{pore} = 250 \times 3.351 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{pores} = 8.378 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^3$$

---

**Step 4 — Verification Using Volume Fraction:**

Theoretical porosity volume from $f_v$:

$$V_{p, theory} = f_v \times V_{sample} = 0.0293 \times 5 \text{ cm}^3 = 0.1465 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Observation:** The pore-count estimate ($8.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^3$) is much smaller than the theoretical porosity volume (0.1465 cm³). This indicates that the **majority of porosity** in this casting comes from larger voids, gas pores, or shrinkage cavities — not from the counted micropores alone. The micropores contribute less than 0.01 % of the total porosity.

---

**Step 5 — Effect on Fatigue Life:**

1. **Stress concentration:** Each spherical micropore acts as a stress raiser with $K_t \approx 2$, accelerating fatigue crack initiation.
2. **Crack nucleation:** Pores act as preferential sites for **Stage I fatigue crack** formation.
3. **Reduction in fatigue strength:** Empirical relations (e.g., Murakami's $\sqrt{area}$ model) show that fatigue strength $\sigma_w$ is reduced by approximately:

$$\sigma_w = \frac{C \, (HV + 120)}{(\sqrt{area})^{1/6}}$$

where $\sqrt{area}$ is the projected defect size. Hence, the **largest defect** dominates the fatigue life, not the average.

4. **Approximate fatigue life reduction:** For 2.93 % porosity, the fatigue endurance limit is reduced by an estimated **20–40 %** compared to a defect-free casting of the same material.

5. **Engineering recommendation:** The component should be subjected to **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** or **gravity die casting under vacuum** to reduce porosity below 1 % before being placed in fatigue service.

*(7 marks: 2 marks porosity fraction + 2 marks pore volume calculation + 3 marks fatigue analysis with engineering recommendation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Volume Defect Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing volume defects with point defects:** Examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish voids/inclusions (3D) from vacancies/interstitials (0D). Always state the **dimensionality** explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in crack surface energy:** A common error is writing $U_{surface} = 2a\gamma_s$ instead of $4a\gamma_s$ (because the crack creates **two** surfaces). This alone can cost 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Inglis and Griffith equations:** Inglis's stress concentration is $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ (geometric), while Griffith's is $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s/(\pi a)}$ (energy-based). Don't interchange them.
>
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical problems:** Always carry units in the final answer for density, volume, and stress calculations. A unitless answer is marked incomplete.
>
> 5. **Writing "inclusion = impurity":** A common conceptual error. Impurity is a **chemical/atomic-level** concept (point defect), while inclusion is a **microstructural particle** (volume defect). Examiners penalize this distinction strongly.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the modification for ductile materials:** Griffith's equation strictly applies to brittle materials. For ductile materials, the **Irwin-Orowan modification** with $\gamma_{eff} = \gamma_s + \gamma_p$ must be mentioned to get full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Volume Defects**
>
> - **Definition:** 3D imperfections extending in length, width, and height; sizes from µm to mm.
> - **Main types:** voids, porosity, inclusions, precipitates, cracks, blowholes.
> - **Two broad classes:** *Intrinsic* (formed in the parent material) vs. *Extrinsic* (foreign-body or second-phase).
> - **Most critical defect type:** Sharp crack — governed by **Griffith's criterion** $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s/(\pi a)}$.
> - **Spherical void stress concentration factor:** $K_t = 2$ (elastic analysis).
> - **Inglis stress concentration** (elliptical hole): $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$, with $\rho$ as the tip radius of curvature.
> - **Density-porosity relationship:** $\rho_{actual} = \rho_{th}(1 - f_v)$; hence $f_v = 1 - (\rho_{exp}/\rho_{th})$.
> - **Precipitate strengthening:** $\Delta \tau_{Orowan} = Gb/(L - 2r)$, with $L \propto r/\sqrt{f_v}$ — higher $f_v$ = smaller $L$ = higher strength.
> - **Inclusion sources:** deoxidation, slag entrapment, refractory erosion, reoxidation.
> - **Porosity sources:** solidification shrinkage, gas evolution, sintering incompleteness, radiation damage.
> - **Crack sources:** thermal stress, hydrogen, phase transformation strain (martensite), fatigue.
> - **Remediation techniques:** Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP), ladle refining, vacuum degassing, filtration, controlled aging.
> - **Real-world link:** Volume defects are the most common cause of in-service failures in cast and welded components — always the starting point of any **failure analysis investigation**.
> - **KTU trick question alert:** If asked "What is the largest safe defect size in a brittle component?", use Griffith's equation in reverse: $a_{max} = 2E\gamma_s/(\pi \sigma_{applied}^2)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Edge and Screw Dislocations, Burgers Vector and Interaction Between Dislocations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Edge and Screw Dislocations, Burgers Vector and Interaction Between Dislocations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Dislocation?

A **dislocation** is a one-dimensional (line) crystallographic defect, or irregularity, within a crystal structure that contains a localized disturbance in the otherwise perfect periodic arrangement of atoms. Dislocations are the principal carriers of **plastic deformation** in crystalline materials; their motion (glide) under an applied shear stress allows one part of the crystal to slip past the other at stresses many orders of magnitude lower than the theoretical shear strength of a perfect crystal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCMET205):** A dislocation is a line defect characterized by a region of localized lattice distortion whose movement produces plastic (permanent) deformation. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ characterizes the magnitude and direction of the lattice displacement associated with the dislocation.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Dislocation Types

The geometrical character of a dislocation is decided by the angle between the **dislocation line direction** ($\vec{\xi}$) and the **Burgers vector** ($\vec{b}$).

| Type of Dislocation | Geometric Relation | Atomic Picture |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge Dislocation** | $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$ | An extra half-plane of atoms is inserted into the lattice |
| **Screw Dislocation** | $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$ | The lattice is distorted in a helical/spiral fashion |
| **Mixed Dislocation** | $\vec{b}$ at an angle $0 < \theta < 90^\circ$ to $\vec{\xi}$ | A blend of edge and screw character along a curved line |

## 1.3 Edge Dislocation — Intuition & Definition

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** An edge dislocation is a linear crystallographic defect in which the Burgers vector is perpendicular to the dislocation line, equivalent to the presence of an extra half-crystal plane inserted into the otherwise perfect lattice.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Extra Row of Tiles":**
Imagine a perfectly laid tile floor. Now wedge in an extra half-row of tiles from one side. To accommodate this extra row, the surrounding tiles must bend and distort around the edge of that half-row. That termination edge — running perpendicular to the inserted row — is the **edge dislocation line**. The direction of the extra half-plane defines the positive sense of the dislocation.

> [!NOTE]
> The end of the extra half-plane is marked by the symbol **$\bot$** (T-notation). The horizontal stroke represents the slip plane, and the vertical stem represents the inserted half-plane.

## 1.4 Screw Dislocation — Intuition & Definition

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** A screw dislocation is a linear crystallographic defect in which the Burgers vector is parallel to the dislocation line, produced by a shear displacement of the crystal such that the lattice planes are connected in a helical ramp around the line.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Spiral Staircase":**
Take a block of paper, slice it halfway through, and shear the two halves so the cut edge advances one atomic spacing. The lattice planes no longer lie flat; they spiral around the cut like a screw thread. Walking around the dislocation axis once means climbing exactly one interatomic spacing. A **right-handed screw** advances the cut in the +z direction for a clockwise rotation; a **left-handed screw** advances it for a counter-clockwise rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The screw dislocation is denoted by a **filled or open circle** $\bigodot$ / $\bigotimes$ indicating the sense of advance (into or out of the page).

## 1.5 Burgers Vector — The "Fingerprint" of a Dislocation

**Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is the vector that describes the magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion produced by a dislocation. It represents the displacement of the lattice at the top of the cut relative to the bottom and is **invariant along the dislocation line** (a topological constant of motion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** For most engineering metals, the magnitude of $\vec{b}$ equals the **interatomic spacing** along the close-packed direction — i.e., $b = a/2\langle 110 \rangle$ for BCC, $b = a/2\langle 110 \rangle$ for FCC, and $b = a/3\langle 11\bar{2}0 \rangle$ for HCP. Typical values lie in the range $b \approx 0.25$ – **0.4 nm**.

## 1.6 Why Dislocations Matter — Engineering Relevance

| Engineering Domain | Role of Dislocations |
|---|---|
| **Metal Forming** (rolling, forging, extrusion) | Dislocation glide is the basic mechanism of plastic flow |
| **Alloy Design** (solid-solution, precipitation) | Alloying elements pin dislocations → strengthening |
| **Fatigue & Creep** | Dislocation multiplication/annihilation control lifetime |
| **Semiconductors** | Dislocations are electrically active defects (must be minimized) |
| **Failure Analysis** | Slip bands, dislocation pile-ups cause crack nucleation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Edge Dislocation — Geometric Construction

Consider a perfect cubic lattice. Insert an extra half-plane of atoms starting at plane $AA'$ and terminating at point $E$ (the edge of the half-plane). The line through $E$ perpendicular to the page is the **dislocation line**.

- Above the slip plane: atoms are in **compression** (too crowded).
- Below the slip plane: atoms are in **tension** (stretched apart).
- The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line and **lies in the slip plane**.

The plane on which the dislocation can glide (the slip plane) is defined by the **line** $\vec{\xi}$ and the **Burgers vector** $\vec{b}$. For an edge dislocation, this plane is unique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **positive edge dislocation** has the extra half-plane above the slip plane ($\bot$); a **negative edge dislocation** has it below ($T$). Two opposite-signed edge dislocations on the same slip plane can annihilate each other.

## 2.2 Screw Dislocation — Geometric Construction

Take a perfect lattice and make a cut along plane $ABCD$. Shift the upper part relative to the lower part by **one lattice spacing $b$** in a direction **parallel** to the edge of the cut. The atomic planes — which were flat horizontal sheets — now become a **single continuous helicoidal surface** winding around the line $BC$.

- The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line.
- A screw dislocation **does not have a unique slip plane** — it can glide on *any* plane containing the line. This enables **cross-slip**, a critical mechanism in BCC and Stage-III work-hardening of FCC metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Right-handed** (RH) screw: the helicoid advances away from the observer when rotated **clockwise**; **Left-handed** (LH) screw: it advances when rotated **counter-clockwise**.

## 2.3 Mixed Dislocations

In a real crystal, most dislocation segments are **mixed** — they have both edge and screw components. A curved dislocation loop in a slip plane, for example, is purely edge at two opposite points and purely screw at the other two; everywhere in between, the character is mixed.

Decomposition rule:

$$
\vec{b}_{\text{total}} = \vec{b}_{\text{edge}} + \vec{b}_{\text{screw}}
$$

where $\vec{b}_{\text{edge}} \perp \vec{\xi}$ and $\vec{b}_{\text{screw}} \parallel \vec{\xi}$. The mixed dislocation's glide is constrained to the slip plane because $\vec{b}$ must lie in it.

## 2.4 Burgers Vector — Properties & Significance

1. **Conservation:** $\vec{b}$ is **constant along the dislocation line** (independent of position).
2. **Conservation at a node (Node Theorem):** At a dislocation node where $N$ lines meet, $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{b}_i = 0$.
3. **Conservation of line direction:** The line sense $\vec{\xi}$ is set by a chosen Burgers circuit convention.
4. **Slip direction:** $|\vec{b}|$ is the unit slip displacement during one complete glide event.
5. **Energy scaling:** The energy per unit length of a dislocation is **proportional to $b^2$** — so the system minimizes energy by combining into dislocations of smallest $b$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burgers Circuit Rule (KTU):** To find $\vec{b}$, draw a closed circuit of atom-to-atom steps in the **perfect reference crystal**. The same circuit drawn around the dislocation will *fail to close* by a vector $\vec{RS}$ (or $\vec{SR}$). The closure failure is the Burgers vector.

## 2.5 Dislocation Motion — Glide, Climb, Cross-Slip

| Mode of Motion | Description | Driving Force | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Glide** | Conservative motion on the slip plane | Applied shear stress $\tau$ | In the slip plane |
| **Climb** | Non-conservative motion by absorption/emission of vacancies | Normal stress / temperature | Out of slip plane (edge only) |
| **Cross-slip** | A screw dislocation switches slip planes | Local stress or thermal activation | From one slip plane to another |

A **jog** is a short step on a dislocation line that is not in the slip plane. A **kink** is a short step *in* the slip plane (it allows the dislocation to advance between Peierls valleys).

## 2.6 Dislocation Interactions & Reactions

When two dislocations meet, they may:

- **Annihilate** (opposite signs, same slip plane, both edge or both screw).
- **Form a network** (a node where $\sum \vec{b} = 0$).
- **React to a new dislocation** (e.g., the **Lomer-Cottrell lock** in FCC: two leading partials on intersecting slip planes form a sessile dislocation that blocks further slip).
- **Pile up** against an obstacle (grain boundary, precipitate), creating a back-stress.

The **Frank's rule** governs whether a reaction is energetically favourable:

$$
\vec{b}_3 = \vec{b}_1 + \vec{b}_2 \quad \text{is favoured iff} \quad b_3^2 < b_1^2 + b_2^2
$$

i.e., the reaction lowers the total elastic energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** For FCC partials, the Lomer-Cottrell reaction is $a/2[1\bar{1}0] + a/2[110] \rightarrow a/2[011]$, and because $b_3^2 = a^2/2 < a^2/2 + a^2/2$ is *not* satisfied for the *perfect* version but the *partial* version $a/6\langle 110 \rangle + a/6\langle 110 \rangle \rightarrow a/3\langle 100 \rangle$ *is* energetically favoured, and the resulting dislocation is **sessile** (cannot glide).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants to Memorize**
> - Shear modulus of steel: $G \approx 79$ **GPa** (room temperature)
> - Poisson's ratio for metals: $\nu \approx 0.3$
> - Burgers vector of Cu: $b \approx 0.256$ **nm** = $2.56 \times 10^{-10}$ m

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnitude of Burgers vector (FCC) | $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $a$ = lattice parameter, m |
| 2 | Magnitude of Burgers vector (BCC) | $b = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2}$ | m |
| 3 | Magnitude of Burgers vector (HCP) | $b = \dfrac{a}{3}\sqrt{2}$ for basal $\langle a \rangle$ | m |
| 4 | Schmid resolved shear stress | $\tau = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | Pa; $\phi$ = angle to normal, $\lambda$ = angle to slip dir. |
| 5 | Shear stress field of **screw** dislocation (cylindrical) | $\sigma_{\theta z} = \sigma_{z\theta} = \dfrac{Gb}{2\pi r}$ | Pa; all other $\sigma_{ij}=0$ |
| 6 | Shear stress field of **edge** dislocation | $\sigma_{r\theta} = \dfrac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \dfrac{\cos\theta}{r}$ | Pa |
| 7 | Normal stress of edge dislocation | $\sigma_{rr} = \sigma_{\theta\theta} = -\dfrac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)} \dfrac{\sin\theta}{r}$ | Pa |
| 8 | Hydrostatic stress of edge dislocation | $\sigma_{zz} = \nu(\sigma_{rr} + \sigma_{\theta\theta})$ | Pa |
| 9 | Energy per unit length — **screw** | $E/\ell = \dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_0}\right)$ | J/m; $R \sim$ crystal size, $r_0 \sim b$ |
| 10 | Energy per unit length — **edge** | $E/\ell = \dfrac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r_0}\right)$ | J/m |
| 11 | Force per unit length between **two parallel screw** dislocations | $F/\ell = \dfrac{Gb_1 b_2}{2\pi r}$ | N/m; attractive if opposite signs |
| 12 | Force per unit length between **two parallel edge** dislocations (same slip plane) | $F/\ell = \dfrac{Gb_1 b_2}{2\pi(1-\nu)r}$ | N/m |
| 13 | Peach–Koehler force on a dislocation | $\vec{F} = (\vec{\sigma}\cdot\vec{b}) \times \vec{\xi}$ | N/m; $\vec{\xi}$ = unit line vector |
| 14 | Orowan stress (bowing past obstacle) | $\tau_{\text{or}} = \dfrac{Gb}{L}$ | Pa; $L$ = obstacle spacing |
| 15 | Equilibrium dislocation density | $\rho \approx 10^6$ – $10^{12}$ lines/m$^2$ | annealed → cold-worked |
| 16 | Critical resolved shear stress (Schmid) | $\tau_{crss}$ for a slip system | Pa; Cu ≈ 0.5 MPa, Fe ≈ 30 MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **KEE-notation pitfall:** In the Burgers-circuit closure failure, $\vec{RS}$ (the closure-failure vector) is the **opposite** of $\vec{b}$ when using the *start-to-finish* convention (FS/RH), and equal to $\vec{b}$ for the *finish-to-start* convention. **Always specify the convention used** — examiners mark this strictly.

## 2.8 Why These Equations Drive Engineering

- **Equations 5–8** set the long-range stress that a dislocation exerts on its neighbours — the basis of work-hardening and dislocation pile-ups.
- **Equations 9–10** show that energy scales with $b^2$: this is why **partial dislocations** (with smaller $b$) are stable in low-SFE FCC metals (e.g., austenitic stainless steel, brass) and cause planar slip and twinning.
- **Equations 11–12** (the interaction force) explain why like-signed dislocations **repel** and opposite-signed dislocations **attract** — the foundation of sub-grain formation and cell structures.
- **Equation 14** (Orowan) is the **mechanism of precipitation hardening** — the spacing of precipitates sets the strength.
- **Equation 13** (Peach–Koehler) is the master equation used in dislocation-dynamics simulations that predict formability and fatigue life.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Construction of the Burgers Vector — Step-by-Step (FS/RH Convention)

This is a KTU-favourite derivation. The Burgers vector is found by comparing a closed atom-to-atom circuit in a real (defective) crystal with the same circuit traced in a perfect reference crystal.

**Step 1 — Draw a Burgers circuit in the real crystal:**
Starting at an atom $S$, move $n$ atom-to-atom steps in a chosen sense (say, 4 steps to the right $\rightarrow$, 4 steps up $\uparrow$, 4 steps left $\leftarrow$, 4 steps down $\downarrow$). The circuit encloses the dislocation line.

**Step 2 — Trace the same circuit in the perfect crystal:**
In the perfect lattice, the *same* sequence of atom-to-atom jumps does *not* close. The endpoint $F$ is offset from the start $S$ by a vector $\vec{RS}$ (the *closure failure*).

**Step 3 — Define the Burgers vector:**
With the **Finish-to-Start / Right-Hand (FS/RH) convention**, the Burgers vector is defined as

$$
\vec{b} \;=\; \vec{SF} \;=\; -\vec{FS}
$$

i.e., the vector that *closes* the circuit in the perfect crystal.

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks (older Callister editions) use the *Start-to-Finish* convention, where $\vec{b} = \vec{RS}$ (closure failure). **KTU 2024 endorses the FS/RH convention** as the standard — verify your module material.

**Application to an edge dislocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For an edge dislocation:}\quad & \vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi},\ \vert\vec{b}\vert = \text{one interplanar spacing along the slip direction} \\
& \vec{b} \text{ lies in the slip plane.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Application to a screw dislocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For a screw dislocation:}\quad & \vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi},\ \vert\vec{b}\vert = \text{one interplanar spacing} \\
& \vec{b} \text{ is parallel to the dislocation line, irrespective of slip plane.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Stress Field of a Screw Dislocation — Full Derivation

The screw dislocation has a particularly simple and elegant stress field, derivable from classical elasticity.

**Step 1 — Displacement field assumption:**
A pure screw dislocation along the $z$-axis displaces atoms parallel to $z$ by an amount that depends only on the angular position $\theta$ (not on $r$ or $z$). By symmetry of the helix:

$$
u_z(r,\theta,z) \;=\; \frac{b\,\theta}{2\pi}
$$

where $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ is the azimuthal angle. Note that the displacement jumps by $b$ each time $\theta$ increases by $2\pi$, consistent with the helicoid.

**Step 2 — Strain field from displacement:**
The only non-zero strains are shear strains in $r$–$z$ and $\theta$–$z$ planes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{rz} &= \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial u_z}{\partial r} = 0 \quad \text{(since } u_z \text{ depends only on } \theta\text{)} \\
\varepsilon_{\theta z} &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial u_z}{\partial \theta} + \frac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial z}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{r}\cdot\frac{b}{2\pi} + 0\right) = \frac{b}{4\pi r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stress field via Hooke's law:**
For a screw dislocation, the only relevant elastic constant is the shear modulus $G$ (the problem is anti-plane):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\theta z} &= 2G\,\varepsilon_{\theta z} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi r} \\
\sigma_{z\theta} &= \sigma_{\theta z} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi r}
\end{aligned}
$$

All other stress components are zero.

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\theta z}(r) \;=\; \sigma_{z\theta}(r) \;=\; \frac{Gb}{2\pi r}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:**
- The stress diverges as $r \rightarrow 0$ (i.e., at the dislocation core, elasticity breaks down — the core radius is conventionally taken as $r_0 \sim b$).
- The stress decays as $1/r$ far from the line.
- The stress field of a screw dislocation is **purely shear**, with **no hydrostatic component** — this is why screw dislocations do not interact strongly with point defects that couple to hydrostatic stress (interstitials, vacancies).

## 3.3 Stress Field of an Edge Dislocation — Full Derivation

An edge dislocation along the $z$-axis with $\vec{b} = b\,\hat{x}$ in the $x$–$y$ (slip) plane is a 2D plane-strain problem. We use the **Airy stress function** $\chi$ defined such that:

$$
\sigma_{xx} = \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial y^2},\quad \sigma_{yy} = \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial x^2},\quad \sigma_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial x \partial y}
$$

**Step 1 — Bi-harmonic equation:**
For a stress-free body, $\chi$ must satisfy the bi-harmonic equation:

$$
\nabla^4 \chi = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Ansatz (Volterra's solution):**
Volterra proposed for a single edge dislocation:

$$
\chi(r,\theta) \;=\; -\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\, r\,\ln r \,\sin\theta
$$

(Where $r$ and $\theta$ are polar coordinates around the dislocation line.)

**Step 3 — Verify the bi-harmonic equation:**
The Laplacian of $r\ln r\sin\theta$ is $\sin\theta/r$. The Laplacian of $\sin\theta/r$ is $0$. So $\nabla^4\chi = 0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Compute the stresses from $\chi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{rr} &= \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial \theta^2} = -\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\,\frac{\sin\theta}{r} \\
\sigma_{\theta\theta} &= \frac{\partial^2 \chi}{\partial r^2} = -\frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\,\frac{\sin\theta}{r} \\
\sigma_{r\theta} &= -\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \chi}{\partial \theta}\right) = \frac{Gb}{2\pi(1-\nu)}\,\frac{\cos\theta}{r} \\
\sigma_{zz} &= \nu(\sigma_{rr} + \sigma_{\theta\theta}) \quad \text{(plane strain)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{rr} = \sigma_{\theta\theta} = -\frac{Gb\sin\theta}{2\pi(1-\nu)r},\quad \sigma_{r\theta} = \frac{Gb\cos\theta}{2\pi(1-\nu)r},\quad \sigma_{zz} = -\frac{Gb\nu\sin\theta}{\pi(1-\nu)r}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**
- The edge dislocation has both **shear** ($\sigma_{r\theta}$) and **hydrostatic** ($\sigma_{rr}+\sigma_{\theta\theta}$) components.
- Above the slip plane ($\sin\theta > 0$): $\sigma_{rr} < 0$ → **compression**.
- Below the slip plane ($\sin\theta < 0$): $\sigma_{rr} > 0$ → **tension**.
- This is why **interstitials** (oversized atoms) segregate to the **tensile region** and **vacancies** to the **compressive region** — a phenomenon called **Cottrell atmosphere** formation, which causes **yield-point phenomena** in mild steel.

## 3.4 Force Between Two Parallel Dislocations — Derivation

Consider two parallel **edge dislocations** separated by a distance $r$, with parallel Burgers vectors $\vec{b}_1$ and $\vec{b}_2$ on the same slip plane.

**Step 1 — Stress of dislocation 1 at the location of dislocation 2:**
At $\theta = 0$ (along the +$x$-axis from dislocation 1), the shear stress $\sigma_{r\theta} = Gb_1/[2\pi(1-\nu)r]$ from formula (3.3).

**Step 2 — Peach–Koehler force on dislocation 2:**
The force per unit length on a dislocation is $F/\ell = \tau \cdot b_2$, where $\tau$ is the resolved shear stress on dislocation 2's slip plane produced by dislocation 1.

$$
\frac{F}{\ell} \;=\; \tau \cdot b_2 \;=\; \frac{Gb_1 b_2}{2\pi(1-\nu)r}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{F}{\ell} \;=\; \frac{G b_1 b_2}{2\pi(1-\nu) r}\;}
$$

- If $b_1 b_2 > 0$ (same sign) → **repulsive**.
- If $b_1 b_2 < 0$ (opposite sign) → **attractive** (annihilation possible on the same slip plane).
- For two parallel **screws**, the factor becomes $1/[2\pi r]$ (no $(1-\nu)$).

## 3.5 Energy of a Dislocation — Derivation

The strain energy per unit length is the integral of the elastic energy density $u = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij}$ over the annular region $r_0 \le r \le R$ around the dislocation.

**For a screw dislocation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E}{\ell} &= \int_{r_0}^{R} \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{\theta z}\varepsilon_{\theta z}\, 2\pi r\, dr \\
&= \int_{r_0}^{R} \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{Gb}{2\pi r}\cdot\frac{b}{4\pi r}\cdot 2\pi r\, dr \\
&= \int_{r_0}^{R} \frac{Gb^2}{8\pi^2 r}\,dr \cdot 2\pi \\
&= \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{E}{\ell}\;=\;\frac{Gb^2}{4\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) \quad \text{(screw)}; \qquad \frac{E}{\ell}\;=\;\frac{Gb^2}{4\pi(1-\nu)}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right) \quad \text{(edge)}\;}
$$

With $r_0 \sim b$ and $R$ taken as the crystal size or the distance to the next dislocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Example:** For Cu ($G = 48$ GPa, $b = 0.256$ nm, $\nu = 0.34$), per metre of dislocation length:
> $E/\ell \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ J/m $= 2.5$ nJ/m — the energy of a dislocation is small per unit length but multiplied by the **huge total line length in a worked metal** (e.g., 1 m$^3$ of cold-worked Cu contains $\sim 10^{15}$ m of dislocations → stored strain energy $\sim 2.5$ kJ/m$^3$).
