# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> Define the **Burgers vector** of a dislocation. Mention any **two properties** of the Burgers vector.

**Model Answer (3 marks — examiner allocation: 1 + 1 + 1):**

The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ of a dislocation is the vector that represents the magnitude and direction of the lattice displacement associated with the dislocation. It is determined by the closure failure of a Burgers circuit drawn around the dislocation line.

**Two important properties:**

1. **Invariance along the line:** The Burgers vector is constant in magnitude and direction at every point along a given dislocation line.
2. **Conservation at a node (Burgers vector sum rule):** At a dislocation node where $N$ dislocation lines meet, the vector sum of all incoming Burgers vectors equals the vector sum of all outgoing Burgers vectors; equivalently, $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vec{b}_i = 0$ when all line directions are taken consistently.

*(Optional 3rd point for full credit: $\vec{b}$ determines the slip direction and the magnitude of slip produced by one glide event.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> Differentiate between an **edge dislocation** and a **screw dislocation**. State **any four points** of distinction.

**Model Answer (3 marks — examiner allocation: 4 × 0.75 ≈ 1 mark per valid point):**

| S.No. | Edge Dislocation | Screw Dislocation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$ | $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$ |
| 2 | Slip plane is uniquely defined by $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{\xi}$ | Slip plane is *not* unique — any plane containing the line is a possible slip plane |
| 3 | Can **climb** (non-conservative motion) | Cannot climb — only glide (and cross-slip) |
| 4 | Has both shear and hydrostatic stress components | Stress field is **purely shear** (no hydrostatic component) |
| 5 | Symbol: $\bot$ (T-notation) | Symbol: $\bigodot$ or $\bigotimes$ (sense of advance) |
| 6 | Extra half-plane of atoms is present | No extra half-plane — helicoidal arrangement of atoms |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** (either Question A **OR** Question B). Each question is split into sub-parts typically valued at 7 marks each. Students must answer **one** full 14-mark question. Show all derivations and label all diagrams.

### 📘 QUESTION A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** With the help of neat **block diagrams**, explain the geometry of an **edge dislocation** and a **screw dislocation** in a crystal. Discuss how the Burgers vector is oriented relative to the dislocation line in each case. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is a **Burgers vector**? Describe step-by-step the construction of the Burgers vector for **(i)** an edge dislocation and **(ii)** a screw dislocation, clearly stating the convention used. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Q.A (a) (7 marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 split):**

**(i) Edge Dislocation Geometry — 2 marks**

An edge dislocation is created when an extra half-plane of atoms is introduced into a perfect crystal lattice. The terminating edge of this half-plane inside the crystal is the **dislocation line**. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line and lies in the slip plane. Above the slip plane, atoms experience compression; below it, tension.

```
   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
--------|---|---|---|---|---     <-- Slip plane
   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
   |   |   |   |   |   |   |
              ↑
        dislocation line (E)
        b is ⊥ to line (in slip plane)
```

Symbol: $\bot$ (T) — the vertical stroke is the extra half-plane, the horizontal is the slip plane.

**(ii) Screw Dislocation Geometry — 2 marks**

A screw dislocation is created by shearing a crystal block parallel to the cut edge by one interatomic spacing $b$. The atomic planes wrap continuously around the dislocation line like a spiral staircase. The Burgers vector $\vec{b}$ is **parallel** to the dislocation line.

**Cross-sectional view (atoms denoted by A, B, C, D, E, F):**

```
   A→A→A→A→A→A
   ↓
   B   B   B   B   B   B
   ↓
   C   C   C   C   C   C
   ↓       (one circuit around line advances by b)
   D   D   D   D   D   D
   ↓
   E   E   E   E   E   E
   ↓
   F   F   F   F   F   F
```

The line direction $\vec{\xi}$ and $\vec{b}$ are co-linear.

**(iii) Comparison of $\vec{b}$ Orientation — 2 marks**

| Feature | Edge | Screw |
|---|---|---|
| Angle $\angle(\vec{b}, \vec{\xi})$ | $90^\circ$ | $0^\circ$ |
| Slip plane uniqueness | Unique | Not unique |
| Climb possible? | Yes | No |
| Cross-slip possible? | No | Yes |

**(iv) Conclusion — 1 mark**

The relative orientation of $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{\xi}$ fully determines the dislocation character and its modes of motion.

---

**Model Answer — Q.A (b) (7 marks — 2 + 2.5 + 2.5 split):**

**Definition — 2 marks**

The **Burgers vector** $\vec{b}$ of a dislocation is defined as the vector that closes the Burgers circuit drawn around the dislocation in a perfect reference crystal. It is the **closure-failure vector** of the same circuit drawn in the dislocated crystal. $\vec{b}$ characterises the magnitude and direction of the lattice distortion caused by the dislocation.

**Convention used:** **Finish-to-Start / Right-Hand (FS/RH) convention** — recommended by KTU 2024.

**Construction for an edge dislocation — 2.5 marks**

1. Place the dislocation line vertically (the extra half-plane is to the left of the line).
2. Choose a starting atom $S$ several atom-spacings away from the line.
3. Move $n$ equal atom-to-atom steps in a clockwise circuit (e.g., 4 right, 4 up, 4 left, 4 down) enclosing the line.
4. Transfer the same circuit to a perfect reference crystal. The circuit *fails to close* by a vector $\vec{SF}$ pointing horizontally to the right.
5. This closure vector is the Burgers vector $\vec{b}$; it is **perpendicular** to the dislocation line and **parallel to the slip direction**.

**Construction for a screw dislocation — 2.5 marks**

1. Place the screw dislocation line vertically (helicoid runs parallel to the line).
2. Draw a Burgers circuit in a plane perpendicular to the line, going around the line in a chosen sense (say clockwise when viewed from above).
3. In the perfect reference crystal, the same circuit fails to close by a vector parallel to the line direction.
4. The closure vector $\vec{SF}$ is the Burgers vector $\vec{b}$; it is **parallel** to the dislocation line.

**Result:** $|\vec{b}|$ equals one interatomic spacing along the close-packed direction. For Cu (FCC), $b = a/\sqrt{2} = 0.256$ nm.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **stress field of a screw dislocation** using classical elasticity theory. State the final expressions for all non-zero stress components. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **interaction between dislocations**. Derive the **force per unit length between two parallel edge dislocations** lying on the **same slip plane**, and discuss the physical significance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Q.B (a) (7 marks — 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 split):**

**Assumption (Anti-plane strain) — 1 mark**

Consider a screw dislocation of Burgers vector $\vec{b} = b\,\hat{z}$ along the $z$-axis. By symmetry, the only displacement is $u_z$ and it depends only on the angular coordinate $\theta$ (not on $r$ or $z$):

$$
u_z(r,\theta) = \frac{b\theta}{2\pi}
$$

**Strain field — 2 marks**

The only non-zero strain is

$$
\varepsilon_{\theta z} = \frac{1}{2r}\frac{\partial u_z}{\partial \theta} = \frac{b}{4\pi r}
$$

**Stress field via Hooke's law — 2 marks**

Since the deformation is a pure shear, $\sigma_{\theta z} = 2G\varepsilon_{\theta z}$:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{\theta z} = \sigma_{z\theta} = \frac{Gb}{2\pi r}}
$$

All other $\sigma_{ij} = 0$ (no normal stresses, no hydrostatic pressure).

**Core cutoff — 1 mark**

The linear-elastic solution diverges as $r \rightarrow 0$. We define a **core radius** $r_0 \sim b$ within which non-linear atomic effects dominate. Outside the core, the equation is valid.

**Physical interpretation — 1 mark**

- The stress decays as $1/r$ — the screw dislocation has a long-range stress field.
- The stress is **purely shear**; this means a screw dislocation does **not** interact with point defects via hydrostatic coupling (no Cottrell atmosphere on screws).
- The strain energy per unit length is

$$
\frac{E}{\ell} = \frac{Gb^2}{4\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_0}\right)
$$

---

**Model Answer — Q.B (b) (7 marks — 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 2 split):**

**Introduction to Dislocation Interactions — 1.5 marks**

When two dislocations approach each other, they exert forces on one another through their elastic stress fields. These interactions govern:

- **Work-hardening** (repulsion between like-signed dislocations).
- **Recovery and recrystallisation** (annihilation of opposite-signed dislocations).
- **Sub-grain formation** (organisation into low-energy arrays).
- **Solute strengthening / Cottrell locking** (interaction with point defects).

**Force Between Two Parallel Edge Dislocations — 2 marks**

Consider two parallel edge dislocations on the same slip plane, separated by a distance $r$, with **same-sign** Burgers vectors. Dislocation 1 produces a shear stress at the location of dislocation 2 (using $\theta = 0$ in formula 3.3):

$$
\tau = \sigma_{r\theta}\big\vert_{\theta = 0} = \frac{Gb_1}{2\pi(1-\nu)r}
$$

**Peach–Koehler force — 1.5 marks**

The force per unit length on dislocation 2 is the resolved shear stress times its Burgers vector:

$$
\frac{F}{\ell} = \tau \cdot b_2 = \frac{Gb_1 b_2}{2\pi(1-\nu)r}
$$

**Generalising to like vs. opposite signs — 2 marks**

- $b_1 b_2 > 0$ (same sign) → $F/\ell > 0$ → **repulsive**. Like-signed dislocations on the same slip plane push each other apart and tend to form ordered arrays (Taylor lattices).
- $b_1 b_2 < 0$ (opposite sign) → $F/\ell < 0$ → **attractive**. The dislocations glide towards each other and **annihilate** when $r \to 0$ (with emission of energy as heat or phonons).

**Physical Significance:**

- The inverse-distance dependence ($1/r$) explains the long-range nature of dislocation interactions.
- This force is the basis of **Taylor hardening** and **forest hardening**.
- Numerical estimate for Cu ($G = 48$ GPa, $\nu = 0.34$, $b_1 = b_2 = b = 0.256$ nm, $r = 100b = 25.6$ nm):

$$
\frac{F}{\ell} = \frac{48 \times 10^9 \times (0.256 \times 10^{-9})^2}{2\pi \times 0.66 \times 25.6 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{N/m}
$$

— small per unit length, but multiplied by the total dislocation line length in a real material gives substantial forces.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Convent blunder (–2 marks):** Students write $\vec{b} = \vec{RS}$ (closure failure) without stating the convention. KTU expects the **FS/RH convention**; simply naming the symbol without defining it loses full credit.
> 2. **Confusing slip plane with climb plane (–1 to 2 marks):** The slip plane is the plane **containing both $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{\xi}$**. An edge dislocation has exactly one such plane; a screw has infinitely many.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu)$ factor for edge dislocations (–1 mark):** Mixing up the screw and edge stress-field formulas is a frequent mistake. Memorise: **screw = $1/2\pi r$**, **edge = $1/[2\pi(1-\nu)r]$**.
> 4. **Sign of interaction force (–1 mark):** Always state whether the force is attractive or repulsive. Examiners explicitly look for the **sign analysis** of $b_1 b_2$.
> 5. **Diagram labelling (–1 mark):** A diagram without labelled axes ($\vec{b}$, $\vec{\xi}$, slip plane) is treated as a "decorative sketch" and gets only partial credit. Always **label** every line and vector.
> 6. **Mixing dislocation line direction $\vec{\xi}$ with Burgers vector $\vec{b}$:** The line is the locus of the defect; $\vec{b}$ is the slip vector. They are *different* concepts. Examiners will deduct for confusing them.
> 7. **Forgetting to state $r_0 \sim b$ in energy integrals (–1 mark):** The lower limit of integration in the energy derivation is the core radius, not zero.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Exam Hall**
>
> ✅ **Dislocation = line defect** with vector $\vec{b}$ describing lattice distortion.
>
> ✅ **Edge dislocation:** $\vec{b} \perp \vec{\xi}$, extra half-plane, unique slip plane, can climb.
>
> ✅ **Screw dislocation:** $\vec{b} \parallel \vec{\xi}$, helicoidal, any plane is a slip plane, can cross-slip, no climb.
>
> ✅ **Mixed dislocation:** $\vec{b}$ at $0 < \angle(\vec{b}, \vec{\xi}) < 90^\circ$ — a continuous blend.
>
> ✅ **Burgers vector properties:** (a) constant along a line, (b) conserved at nodes ($\sum \vec{b} = 0$), (c) determines slip direction and magnitude.
>
> ✅ **Burgers circuit:** draw a closed atom-to-atom loop around the dislocation; transfer to perfect crystal; closure failure = $\vec{b}$ (FS/RH convention).
>
> ✅ **Stress field of a screw:** $\sigma_{\theta z} = Gb/(2\pi r)$; purely shear; $1/r$ decay.
>
> ✅ **Stress field of an edge:** $\sigma_{rr} = \sigma_{\theta\theta} = -Gb\sin\theta/[2\pi(1-\nu)r]$; $\sigma_{r\theta} = Gb\cos\theta/[2\pi(1-\nu)r]$; hydrostatic component exists.
>
> ✅ **Strain energy per unit length:** screw = $Gb^2/[4\pi]\ln(R/r_0)$; edge = $Gb^2/[4\pi(1-\nu)]\ln(R/r_0)$.
>
> ✅ **Force between parallel dislocations:** $F/\ell = Gb_1b_2/[2\pi(1-\nu)r]$ (edge); $F/\ell = Gb_1b_2/(2\pi r)$ (screw). Like signs repel; unlike signs attract.
>
> ✅ **Modes of dislocation motion:** **glide** (conservative, in slip plane), **climb** (non-conservative, out of slip plane, edge only), **cross-slip** (screw, between slip planes).
>
> ✅ **Peach–Koehler force:** $\vec{F} = (\vec{\sigma} \cdot \vec{b}) \times \vec{\xi}$ — the master equation of dislocation mechanics.
>
> ✅ **Frank's rule:** a reaction $\vec{b}_1 + \vec{b}_2 \rightarrow \vec{b}_3$ is energetically favoured if $b_3^2 < b_1^2 + b_2^2$.
>
> ✅ **Engineering impact:** dislocation density controls yield strength (Hall–Petch: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k/\sqrt{d}$); dislocation motion is the mechanism of **plastic deformation, work-hardening, creep, and fatigue**.
>
> ✅ **Typical $b$ values:** Cu, Al, Ni (FCC) ≈ 0.25–0.36 nm; $\alpha$-Fe (BCC) ≈ 0.248 nm; Mg, Ti (HCP) ≈ 0.32 nm.
>
> ✅ **Typical dislocation densities:** annealed = $10^6$ – $10^8$ lines/m²; cold-worked = $10^{14}$ – $10^{16}$ lines/m².
>
> ✅ **Remember the units:** Stress in Pa (or GPa = $10^9$ Pa); energy in J/m (per unit length); force per unit length in N/m.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polishing and Etching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polishing and Etching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Polishing and Etching in Metallography?

In materials science, **metallography** is the study of the microstructure of metals and alloys through microscopic examination. To observe a sample under a microscope, its surface must be transformed from a rough, cut, or damaged state into a **mirror-smooth, scratch-free, flat surface** that reveals the true internal grain structure of the metal. The final two critical operations in metallographic specimen preparation are **Polishing** and **Etching**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Polishing):** Polishing is the process of removing the surface deformation, scratches, and artifacts introduced during the cutting and grinding stages, by using progressively finer abrasive particles, until a mirror-like, reflective surface (typically with surface roughness below 0.5 µm) is obtained for microscopic examination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (Etching):** Etching is the controlled chemical (or electrochemical) attack of a polished metal surface using a suitable reagent (etchant) to preferentially dissolve grain boundaries, second phases, and other microstructural features, making them visible under the optical microscope.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic sits within Module 2 of PCMET205 and is a fundamental **experimental** topic — students are expected to know the procedure, the chemicals, the safety aspects, and the underlying reason why each step is performed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wooden Table and Stain" Metaphor

Imagine you are restoring an old wooden table:
- **Cutting & Mounting** → Sawing the wood to a workable size.
- **Grinding** → Sanding the wood with coarse sandpapers (60, 120, 400 grit) to remove major roughness and make the surface flat.
- **Polishing** → Sanding with very fine sandpaper (1000, 2000 grit) and applying a finishing wax until the wood reflects your face like a mirror. The wood is now perfectly smooth, **but you cannot see the grain pattern yet**.
- **Etching** → Applying a dark wood-stain (the *etchant*). The stain penetrates the soft grain boundaries and porous regions more than the dense late-wood. When you wipe off the excess, the **hidden annual rings and grain boundaries become visible** as dark lines.

**In metals, exactly the same logic applies:**
- A polished metal surface looks like a flat, featureless mirror under the microscope.
- An *etchant* is a mild acid that preferentially attacks the higher-energy **grain boundaries**, **phase boundaries**, and **inclusions**.
- The etched surface, when viewed under oblique light, shows up the **true microstructure** as a network of dark lines and contrasting shades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** Polishing and etching are *not* two separate unconnected steps — they form a **continuous sequence** in metallographic preparation. A poorly polished surface will give a *false microstructure* after etching. Therefore, both quality and procedure are evaluated together.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

| Standard Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Surface Roughness (Ra)** | Average surface roughness in micrometres; should be < 0.5 µm for optical microscopy. |
| **Abrasive Particle Size** | Measured in **micrometres (µm)** or **mesh number** (higher mesh = finer abrasive). |
| **Etching Time** | Typically **5 to 60 seconds**; over-etching destroys the structure. |
| **Grain Boundary Energy** | The driving force for preferential attack during etching; measured in **J/m²**. |
| **Magnification Range** | Optical metallography typically uses **50× to 1000×**. |

> [!TIP]
> Remember the **"3-S Rule"** of metallographic preparation: **S**ectioning → **S**urface Preparation (Grinding) → **S**urface Finishing (Polishing & Etching).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Matrix

## 2.1 The Complete Metallographic Specimen Preparation Chain

The logical flow from a raw metal sample to a micrograph is shown below. Each step is a prerequisite for the next; skipping any one leads to artefacts.

**Step 1 — Sectioning:** Cutting a representative piece from the bulk material using an abrasive cutoff wheel. Liquid coolant is mandatory to avoid heat damage.

**Step 2 — Mounting:** Encapsulating the specimen in a thermoplastic or thermosetting resin (e.g., Bakelite) so that small/irregular samples can be handled safely.

**Step 3 — Grinding (Planarising):** Sequential abrasion on SiC papers of **decreasing grit size** — typically **120 → 240 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200**. Water is used as a lubricant/coolant. The direction of grinding is rotated by **90°** between successive grits to remove the scratches of the previous step completely.

**Step 4 — Polishing:** Final surface finishing using fine abrasives (diamond, alumina, or silica) on a soft cloth-covered rotating disc. Produces a **mirror finish** (mirror-like reflectivity).

**Step 5 — Etching:** Controlled chemical attack to reveal the microstructure.

**Step 6 — Microscopic Examination:** Observation under optical or electron microscope; recording of micrographs.

## 2.2 Polishing — Theory, Methods, and Abrasives

Polishing is essentially a **fine-scale grinding** operation. Material removal occurs through **micro-cutting, micro-fatigue, and micro-fracture** at the point of contact between abrasive particles and the metal surface.

### 2.2.1 Types of Polishing

**A. Mechanical Polishing (Most Common)**
- Performed on a rotating disc covered with a **polishing cloth** (e.g., felt, velvet, synthetic fabric).
- The cloth is charged with an **abrasive slurry** of fine particles.
- Polishing sequence: **Rough polishing → Fine polishing → Final polishing**.
- Lubricant: distilled water or special oil-based extenders.

**B. Electrolytic Polishing (Electropolishing)**
- The specimen is made the **anode** in an electrolytic cell.
- Controlled anodic dissolution preferentially removes surface asperities because the current density is higher at peaks than at valleys → surface becomes **atomically flat**.
- **Advantages:** no mechanical deformation layer, faster, ideal for very soft metals.
- **Disadvantages:** requires specific electrolytes, more expensive.

**C. Chemical Polishing**
- Simple immersion in a chemical solution that dissolves the surface layer.
- Less control than electropolishing; used for non-critical applications.

**D. Automatic / Vibratory Polishing**
- Uses a vibratory bowl with abrasive slurry; multiple specimens are polished simultaneously.
- Ideal for routine quality-control labs.

### 2.2.2 Abrasives Used in Mechanical Polishing

| Abrasive | Particle Size Range | Application Stage | Hardness (Mohs) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** | 60 µm down to 5 µm | Grinding papers | 9.5 |
| **Diamond Paste** | 6 µm, 3 µm, 1 µm, 0.25 µm | Fine to final polishing | 10 (hardest) |
| **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** | 0.3 µm to 0.05 µm | Final polishing of steels, Al, Cu | 9.0 |
| **Silica (SiO₂) Suspension (Colloidal Silica)** | 0.05 µm to 0.02 µm | Final polishing of Al, Ti, superalloys | 7.0 |
| **Iron Oxide (Fe₂O₃ — Rouge)** | 1 µm | Final polish of non-ferrous metals | 6.0 |
| **Chromium Oxide (Cr₂O₃)** | < 1 µm | Final polish of hard alloys | 8.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The hardness of the abrasive must always be **higher than the hardness of the metal being polished**. This is why **diamond** (Mohs 10) is the universal premium abrasive, while **alumina (Mohs 9)** is the standard for most ferrous metals.

## 2.3 Etching — Theory and Practice

A freshly polished metal surface is essentially optically featureless. This is because:
- All grains are at the same level.
- There is no optical contrast between adjacent grains.
- Grain boundaries are too narrow to scatter light.

**Etching** introduces a **tiny amount of controlled relief** (a few atomic layers) on the polished surface. When viewed under the microscope:
- **Grain boundaries** → etched deeper because they have higher free energy → appear as **dark lines**.
- **Different grains** → etched at different rates depending on their crystallographic orientation → appear as **different shades of grey/colour**.
- **Second-phase particles** → may be dissolved, remain in relief, or be coloured by the etchant → appear as **distinct shapes**.
- **Inclusions** → appear as **rounded or angular dark particles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Underlying Physics:** The atoms at a grain boundary have a higher chemical potential than atoms within the bulk of a grain. Hence, the chemical reaction rate (dissolution) at the boundary is much faster. The etchant effectively *amplifies* the pre-existing energy differences into visible topography.

### 2.3.1 Classification of Etching Methods

**A. Chemical Etching (Most Common)**
- **Immersion Etching:** The specimen is fully dipped in the etchant for a controlled time, then immediately rinsed with water, followed by alcohol, and dried with a hot air blower.
- **Swab Etching:** A cotton swab soaked in etchant is gently rubbed on the surface. Used for very reactive etchants or large components.
- **Drop Etching:** A few drops are placed on a horizontal specimen surface; useful for small specimens.

**B. Electrolytic (Electrolytic) Etching**
- The specimen is the **anode** in an electrolytic cell.
- A suitable electrolyte and low current/voltage dissolve the surface selectively.
- Excellent for **stainless steels, superalloys, and refractory metals** that are difficult to etch chemically.
- Examples: **10% Oxalic acid** for austenitic stainless steel, **10% KOH** for ferritic stainless steel.

**C. Thermal Etching**
- The specimen is heated in a vacuum or inert atmosphere. Surface diffusion preferentially removes atoms at grain boundaries, producing grooves. Used for **ceramics and high-melting-point metals**.

**D. Ion Etching / Plasma Etching**
- Used in **SEM preparation** where chemical etching is unsuitable.

### 2.3.2 Standard Etchants for Common Metals (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!WARNING]
> **Etchant Safety:** All etchants listed below are corrosive. Nital is flammable (ethanol-based). Use a fume hood, gloves, and eye protection. Always add acid to water, never water to acid.

| Metal / Alloy | Etchant Name | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plain Carbon Steel** | **Nital** | 1–5% HNO₃ in Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Reveals ferrite grain boundaries, pearlite |
| **Tool Steel / Martensitic Steel** | **Picral** | 4% Picric Acid + Ethanol | Reveals carbides, prior-austenite grain boundaries |
| **High-Speed Steel** | **Kalling's No. 2** | 5 g CuCl₂ + 40 mL HCl + 30 mL Ethanol + 30 mL Water | Reveals martensite, retained austenite |
| **Austenitic Stainless Steel** | **Glyceregia** | 3 parts Glycerol + 2 parts HCl + 1 part HNO₃ | Reveals austenite grain boundaries |
| **Austenitic Stainless Steel** | **10% Oxalic Acid (Electro)** | Electrolytic at 6 V DC | Reveals carbides, sensitisation |
| **Cast Iron** | **Nital (2–5%)** | Same as for steel | Reveals ferrite, pearlite, graphite flakes |
| **Aluminium & Alloys** | **Keller's Reagent** | 1% HF + 1.5% HCl + 2.5% HNO₃ + 95% Water | General microstructural reveal |
| **Aluminium & Alloys** | **Barker's Reagent (Electro)** | 2.5% HBF₄ in Water, electrolytic at 30 V | Reveals grain orientation (colour metallography) |
| **Copper & Brasses** | **NH₄OH + H₂O₂** | Equal parts, fresh mix | Reveals grain boundaries in Cu |
| **Titanium** | **Kroll's Reagent** | 1–3% HF + 2–6% HNO₃ in Water | Reveals alpha-beta structure |
| **Magnesium Alloys** | **Acetic-Picral** | 5 mL Acetic Acid + 6 g Picric Acid + 10 mL Water + 100 mL Ethanol | Reveals grain structure |
| **Zinc** | **Palmerton's Reagent** | 200 g CrO₃ + 15 g Na₂SO₄ in 1000 mL Water | Reveals grain boundaries |

## 2.4 Etch Figures and Special Microstructural Features

> [!NOTE]
> **Etch Pits:** Tiny geometric cavities formed at the location where a single dislocation intersects the polished surface, after prolonged or specific etching. They are used to determine the **orientation of single crystals** and to estimate **dislocation density**.

- **Twin Boundaries** appear as straight lines that do not deflect and end at grain boundaries.
- **Sub-grain Boundaries** (low-angle) appear as rows of aligned etch pits.
- **Precipitates** appear as small dots, needles, or plates of contrasting shade.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control in Foundries** → verifying the grain size, dendrite arm spacing, and porosity in cast components.
- **Failure Analysis** → identifying the origin of cracks, fatigue striations, and overload fractures.
- **Heat-Treatment Verification** → confirming correct hardening, tempering, or annealing of steels (e.g., presence of martensite vs. pearlite).
- **Weld Inspection** → revealing the heat-affected zone (HAZ), weld metal, and base metal boundaries.
- **Phase-Diagram Verification** → experimentally confirming the predicted microstructural phases in an alloy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners love to ask:** "Why do different grains of the same phase appear with different shades after etching?" The correct answer: *different crystallographic orientations of grains lead to different rates of chemical attack, producing different surface roughnesses, which reflect light differently into the microscope objective.*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedures, Mechanisms & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Mechanical Polishing Procedure (Step-by-Step)

The following sequential procedure is what is followed in a standard metallography lab. The student must be able to write this in the KTU exam in the correct order.

**Step 1 — Coarse Grinding (Planarising)**
- Start with **120 or 240 grit SiC paper**.
- Hold the specimen firmly and grind in one direction only.
- Apply uniform pressure; rotate the specimen 90° only when moving to the next finer grit.
- Use running water as a coolant and lubricant.

**Step 2 — Intermediate Grinding**
- Move to **400 → 600 → 800 grit** sequentially.
- Rotate the specimen by **90°** to remove all scratches from the previous paper.
- Continue until all deep scratches are replaced by a uniform set of fine scratches in a single direction.

**Step 3 — Fine Grinding (Optional for Soft Metals)**
- Use **1000 and 1200 grit** SiC paper.
- Some labs skip 1200 and move directly to rough polishing on a cloth.

**Step 4 — Rough Polishing**
- Polishing cloth: **Billiard or synthetic rayon cloth**.
- Abrasive: **6 µm diamond paste** with diamond extender fluid.
- Polishing speed: **200–300 rpm**.
- Apply light pressure for 2–4 minutes.
- Result: surface is now mirror-like but may show fine scratches.

**Step 5 — Fine Polishing**
- Polishing cloth: **Microcloth or Vel-Chemo-Pol**.
- Abrasive: **1 µm diamond paste** or **0.3 µm alumina slurry**.
- Duration: 1–2 minutes.
- Result: scratch-free mirror finish.

**Step 6 — Final Polishing**
- Polishing cloth: **Chemo-Pol or Microcloth (soft)**.
- Abrasive: **0.05 µm colloidal silica** or **0.02 µm alumina**.
- Duration: 30–60 seconds.
- Result: optically perfect mirror surface ready for etching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Between every stage, the specimen and the operator's hands must be **washed thoroughly** to prevent cross-contamination of coarser abrasive particles into finer stages. This is a very common cause of persistent scratches in student lab work.

## 3.2 Detailed Etching Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Surface Cleaning**
- Before etching, the polished surface must be **degreased** with acetone or alcohol to remove fingerprints, polishing compound residue, and water films.
- Do not touch the surface with bare fingers after this step.

**Step 2 — Selection of Etchant**
- Choose the etchant based on the **alloy system** and the **microstructural feature** to be revealed.
- Reference: Standard ASTM E407 (Standard Practice for Microetching Metals and Alloys).

**Step 3 — Etching Application**
- Three methods: **immersion, swabbing, or drop**.
- Maintain consistent **etching time** (typically 5–30 seconds for nital on steel).

**Step 4 — Immediate Rinsing**
- Rinse under **running water** to stop the chemical reaction.
- Then rinse with **ethanol or methanol** to displace the water.
- This step is critical — water left on the surface can cause staining and rusting.

**Step 5 — Drying**
- Dry with a **warm air blast** (hair dryer or laboratory hot air blower).
- Do not rub the surface; let the air stream do the work.

**Step 6 — Microscopic Examination**
- Observe under the optical microscope at the required magnification.
- If under-etched: re-polish lightly and etch again.
- If over-etched: re-polish from the fine grinding stage and etch again.

## 3.3 Mechanism of Chemical Etching — Analytical Derivation

The rate at which an etchant attacks a metal surface is governed by the **Arrhenius-type chemical kinetics** combined with the **crystallographic orientation dependence** of the dissolution rate.

Let us model the dissolution rate $R$ at any point on the surface as:

$$
R = R_0 \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_a}{k_B T} \right) \cdot f(\theta)
$$

where:
- $R_0$ is the pre-exponential factor (chemistry-controlled constant).
- $E_a$ is the **activation energy** for the dissolution reaction (lower at grain boundaries).
- $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant** = $1.381 \times 10^{-23} \; \text{J/K}$.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in **kelvin**.
- $f(\theta)$ is an orientation factor that depends on the angle $\theta$ between the etchant attack direction and the crystal plane.

**At a grain boundary**, the atoms have higher free energy due to broken bonds. Hence, the effective activation energy is reduced by an amount equal to the grain boundary energy per atom, $\gamma_{gb}$:

$$
E_a^{\text{boundary}} = E_a^{\text{grain interior}} - \gamma_{gb} \cdot \Omega
$$

where $\Omega$ is the **atomic volume** of the metal (in m³/atom).

Substituting:

$$
R_{\text{boundary}} = R_0 \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_a - \gamma_{gb} \Omega}{k_B T} \right) = R_{\text{grain}} \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{\gamma_{gb} \Omega}{k_B T} \right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The dissolution rate at a grain boundary is **exponentially larger** than that inside a grain. This is why the boundary is etched deeper, scatters light out of the microscope objective, and appears as a **dark line** in the final micrograph.

**Quantitative Estimate for Iron (α-Fe) at Room Temperature (T = 298 K) in Nital:**

$$
\Omega_{Fe} \approx 1.18 \times 10^{-29} \; \text{m}^3/\text{atom}
$$

$$
\gamma_{gb} \approx 0.78 \; \text{J/m}^2 \quad \text{(typical high-angle grain boundary in Fe)}
$$

$$
\frac{\gamma_{gb} \cdot \Omega}{k_B T} = \frac{0.78 \times 1.18 \times 10^{-29}}{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298} = \frac{9.2 \times 10^{-30}}{4.12 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 2.23 \times 10^{-9}
$$

This dimensionless number is tiny, but when summed over many atomic layers of the boundary, it produces a **measurable groove depth of a few tens of nanometres** — exactly the right order of magnitude to be visible under an optical microscope.

> [!NOTE]
> **For Exam Purposes:** The student is **not** expected to memorise the Arrhenius equation. However, the *qualitative concept* — *grain boundaries dissolve faster because they have higher energy* — is a high-weightage answer for both **3-mark** and **14-mark** questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Etching Time vs. Outcome

The following matrix shows how etching time affects the quality of microstructural revelation, and is a very common KTU exam reasoning question.

| Etching Time | Resulting Microstructure | Suitable Magnification |
|---|---|---|
| **Under-etched (< 5 s for steel in 2% Nital)** | Surface appears featureless; grain boundaries not visible | — (re-etch required) |
| **Properly Etched (10–20 s for steel in 2% Nital)** | Clear grain boundaries; phases distinct | 100× – 500× |
| **Over-etched (> 60 s)** | Heavy relief; grain boundaries become wide grooves; pitting; false structure | Higher magnification difficult |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always start with a short etch (5 s)**, then observe under the microscope, and re-etch in small increments if required. **Etching is irreversible** — you cannot un-etch a specimen; you can only re-polish and try again.

## 3.5 Diagnostic Table — What Microstructural Features Look Like Under the Microscope

| Microstructural Feature | Appearance After Etching | Etchant of Choice |
|---|---|---|
| **Ferrite** | Light, smooth regions | 2% Nital |
| **Pearlite** | Dark, lamellar (layered) structure | 2% Nital / 4% Picral |
| **Martensite** | Needle-like or acicular (sharp-edged) structure | 4% Picral |
| **Austenite** | Smooth, featureless (often only visible by twin lines) | Glyceregia / Electrolytic |
| **Cementite (Fe₃C)** | White, raised, un-attacked needles/particles | 4% Picral |
| **Graphite (in cast iron)** | Black flakes or spheroids | Unetched (visible as-is) |
| **Inclusions (MnS, silicates)** | Grey-blue rounded or angular particles | As-polished observation |
| **Twin Boundaries** | Straight, parallel-sided lines | Most chemical etchants |
| **Prior-Austenite Grain Boundaries** | Continuous network in quenched steels | Picral + Teepol |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Complete Metallographic Specimen Preparation Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram traces the **full sequence** from a raw metal sample to a finished micrograph. Note the strict linear flow, the **decision diamond** for whether the polished surface is scratch-free, and the feedback loop for re-polishing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Metal Sample] --> B[Sectioning using Abrasive Cutoff Wheel]
    B --> C[Cleaning with Acetone]
    C --> D{Mounting Required?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Mounting in Bakelite Resin]
    D -- No --> F[Direct Grinding]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Coarse Grinding: 120 to 240 grit SiC]
    G --> H[Intermediate Grinding: 400 to 800 grit SiC]
    H --> I[Fine Grinding: 1000 to 1200 grit SiC]
    I --> J[Rough Polishing: 6 micron Diamond on Billiard Cloth]
    J --> K[Fine Polishing: 1 micron Diamond or 0.3 micron Alumina]
    K --> L[Final Polishing: 0.05 micron Colloidal Silica]
    L --> M{Surface Scratch Free?}
    M -- No --> J
    M -- Yes --> N[Degrease with Acetone]
    N --> O[Select Appropriate Etchant from ASTM E407]
    O --> P[Apply Etchant by Immersion or Swabbing]
    P --> Q[Etching Time: 5 to 30 seconds]
    Q --> R[Immediate Rinse: Water then Ethanol]
    R --> S[Hot Air Drying]
    S --> T[Microscopic Examination at 50X to 1000X]
    T --> U{Microstructure Clear?}
    U -- No --> P
    U -- Yes --> V[Record Micrograph and Report]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Polishing-Etching System

This block diagram shows the **functional interactions** between the major subsystems of a metallographic preparation lab, mapping the **inputs, processes, outputs, and feedback** of each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS[Input Materials]
        raw[Raw Metal Specimen]
        abrasives[Abrasives: SiC, Diamond, Alumina]
        etchants[Etchants: Nital, Picral, Krolls]
        cloth[Polishing Cloths]
    end

    subgraph PREP[Preparation Subsystem]
        cut[Sectioning Machine]
        mount[Mounting Press]
        grind[Grinding Station]
    end

    subgraph POLISH[Polishing Subsystem]
        roughP[Rough Polisher]
        fineP[Fine Polisher]
        finalP[Final Polisher]
    end

    subgraph ETCH[Etching Subsystem]
        etchantApp[Etchant Application Unit]
        rinse[Rinse Station]
        dryer[Hot Air Dryer]
    end

    subgraph QC[Quality Control Subsystem]
        micro[Optical Microscope]
        micrograph[Recorded Micrograph]
    end

    raw --> cut
    cut --> mount
    mount --> grind
    abrasives --> grind
    abrasives --> roughP
    abrasives --> fineP
    abrasives --> finalP
    cloth --> roughP
    cloth --> fineP
    cloth --> finalP
    grind --> roughP
    roughP --> fineP
    fineP --> finalP
    finalP --> etchantApp
    etchants --> etchantApp
    etchantApp --> rinse
    rinse --> dryer
    dryer --> micro
    micro --> micrograph
    micrograph --> qcfeedback{QC Acceptable?}
    qcfeedback -- No --> etchantApp
    qcfeedback -- Yes --> archive[Archived in Lab Records]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Mechanism of Preferential Etching at Grain Boundaries

The following diagram shows a 2-D schematic of how the etchant attacks a polished surface, producing the dark lines that we see as grain boundaries in the microscope.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STAGE1[Stage 1: Polished Surface]
        a1[Grain A] --- g1((Grain Boundary)) --- a2[Grain B]
        a1 -.->|flat| surf1[Mirror Surface]
    end

    subgraph STAGE2[Stage 2: Etchant Applied]
        b1[Grain A] --- g2((Grain Boundary)) --- b2[Grain B]
        etchant[Etchant Solution]
        etchant ==>|rapid attack| g2
        b1 -.->|slight attack| surf2[Surface]
    end

    subgraph STAGE3[Stage 3: Etched Surface]
        c1[Grain A] --- g3((Groove)) --- c2[Grain B]
        c1 -.->|plane| surf3[Light Reflected into Objective]
        c2 -.->|plane| surf3
        g3 -.->|shadow| surf3
    end

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2 --> STAGE3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define the terms "polishing" and "etching" as used in metallographic specimen preparation. Why is etching necessary even after obtaining a mirror-polished surface?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Polishing** is the process of removing surface deformation and scratches from a metallographic specimen using progressively finer abrasives, until a mirror-like, scratch-free surface (Ra < 0.5 µm) is obtained.
- **Etching** is the controlled chemical (or electrochemical) attack of the polished surface using a suitable reagent, in order to reveal the true microstructure.
- **Why etching is necessary:** A perfectly polished surface is optically flat and featureless; all grains reflect light uniformly, so no microstructure is visible. Etching preferentially dissolves grain boundaries (which have higher free energy) and grains of different orientations at different rates, producing tiny surface relief and contrast. This relief scatters light differently from different grains, making the **grain boundaries, phase boundaries, and inclusions** visible under the optical microscope.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**List any three commonly used etchants for plain carbon steel and state the specific microstructural feature each one reveals.**

**Model Answer:**

| Etchant | Composition | Microstructural Feature Revealed |
|---|---|---|
| **2% Nital** | 2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL Ethanol | Ferrite grain boundaries, pearlite colonies |
| **4% Picral** | 4 g Picric Acid + 96 mL Ethanol | Carbide network, prior-austenite grain boundaries in tool steels |
| **Kalling's Reagent** | 5 g CuCl₂ + 40 mL HCl + 30 mL Ethanol + 30 mL Water | Martensite needles and retained austenite contrast |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)
**A. (a)** Explain in detail the various stages involved in the metallographic preparation of a metal specimen, with emphasis on grinding and polishing operations. (7 Marks)
**A. (b)** Describe the principles and procedures of chemical etching. Discuss the role of etchant composition and etching time on the quality of the resulting microstructure. (7 Marks)

### Model Answer for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

The metallographic preparation involves a **strict sequential procedure**:

**1. Sectioning (1 mark):** A representative sample is cut from the bulk using an abrasive cutoff wheel with continuous liquid coolant to avoid heat-induced phase changes.

**2. Mounting (1 mark):** The specimen is encapsulated in a thermosetting resin (Bakelite) for easy handling, especially for small or irregular samples. Hot compression mounting at ~150 °C and ~3000 psi is standard.

**3. Grinding (3 marks):** Sequential abrasion on **silicon carbide (SiC) papers** of progressively finer grit: **120 → 240 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200**. Water is the lubricant. The specimen is rotated by **90°** between grits to ensure removal of prior scratches. Final scratch pattern should be uniform and unidirectional.

**4. Polishing (2 marks):** Performed on rotating discs covered with soft cloth:
- **Rough polish** — 6 µm diamond paste on billiard cloth.
- **Fine polish** — 1 µm diamond or 0.3 µm alumina on microcloth.
- **Final polish** — 0.05 µm colloidal silica on chemo-pol cloth.
- Result: optically flat, scratch-free mirror surface suitable for etching.

### Model Answer for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**1. Principle of Etching (2 marks):** Etching exploits the **difference in chemical potential** between atoms at grain boundaries and those within a grain. The atoms at boundaries have **higher free energy** (broken bonds), so they dissolve faster in a chemical etchant. This produces a small **topographical relief** on the surface, which scatters light and reveals microstructure under oblique illumination.

**2. Etching Procedure (2 marks):**
- Degrease the polished surface with acetone.
- Apply the etchant by **immersion, swabbing, or drop method**.
- Etch for **5–30 seconds** (depends on the etchant and alloy).
- **Immediately** rinse under running water, then with alcohol, and dry with warm air.

**3. Effect of Etchant Composition (1.5 marks):** Different alloys require different etchants. For example, **2% Nital** (HNO₃ in ethanol) is used for carbon steels, while **Keller's reagent** (HF + HCl + HNO₃ + water) is required for aluminium alloys. The composition controls the **selectivity** — which phases are attacked and which are not.

**4. Effect of Etching Time (1.5 marks):**
- **Under-etching (< 5 s):** grain boundaries not visible.
- **Proper etching (10–30 s):** clear microstructure.
- **Over-etching (> 60 s):** heavy pitting, false structure, grain pull-out. Etching is irreversible; re-polishing is required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**B. (a)** Discuss in detail the mechanical polishing method used in metallography. Describe the role and properties of common abrasives such as diamond, alumina, and colloidal silica. (7 Marks)
**B. (b)** Explain the electrolytic polishing and electrolytic etching methods. What are their advantages and limitations compared to mechanical methods? (7 Marks)

### Model Answer for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Mechanical Polishing Process (2 marks):** Performed on a rotating polishing disc (200–300 rpm) covered with a soft cloth. The cloth is impregnated with an abrasive slurry. The specimen is held against the rotating disc with light, uniform pressure. Lubricants (water or extender fluid) are used to wash away debris and prevent heating.

**2. Polishing Stages (2 marks):**
- **Rough polishing** — 6 µm diamond, removes deep deformation.
- **Fine polishing** — 1 µm diamond or 0.3 µm alumina, removes fine scratches.
- **Final polishing** — 0.05 µm colloidal silica, produces a mirror surface.

**3. Common Abrasives (3 marks):**
- **Diamond (Mohs 10):** The hardest, most efficient abrasive. Used as paste or suspension. Ideal for ceramics, carbides, and hard steels.
- **Alumina (Al₂O₃, Mohs 9):** The standard fine abrasive. Used in slurry form for ferrous and non-ferrous metals.
- **Colloidal Silica (SiO₂, ~0.05 µm):** Used for final polishing of aluminium, titanium, and superalloys. Produces a chemically active surface that etches and polishes simultaneously.

### Model Answer for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**1. Electrolytic Polishing (3 marks):** The specimen is connected as the **anode** in an electrochemical cell with a stainless-steel cathode. A suitable electrolyte (e.g., **perchloric acid + ethanol** for steels; **phosphoric acid + chromic acid** for aluminium) is used. A DC voltage of **20–60 V** is applied. Anodic dissolution removes surface asperities preferentially (higher current density at peaks), producing an **atomically flat, deformation-free surface**. It is ideal for very soft metals and for samples that cannot tolerate mechanical stress.

**2. Electrolytic Etching (2 marks):** Similar setup, but with a **weaker electrolyte and lower voltage** (e.g., 10% oxalic acid at 6 V for austenitic stainless steel). Selective dissolution of grain boundaries and phases occurs. Used when chemical etchants are ineffective, e.g., for highly corrosion-resistant alloys.

**3. Advantages and Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Advantages:** No mechanical deformation layer, fast, ideal for very hard or very soft metals, no scratches, can reveal subtle features.
- **Limitations:** Requires specific electrolytes, expensive equipment, parameters (voltage, time, temperature) must be precisely controlled, less suitable for multi-phase alloys.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Polishing & Etching Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong order of operations:** Many students write "etching first, then polishing" — this is **wrong**; polishing is always performed *before* etching.
> 2. **Confusing polishing and grinding:** Grinding removes bulk material using bonded abrasives (SiC papers), while polishing produces a mirror surface using loose abrasives on soft cloth.
> 3. **Forgetting safety:** Nital is flammable (ethanol-based); HF is extremely dangerous. Examiners deduct marks for not mentioning fume hoods and PPE.
> 4. **Writing "Nital" without composition:** Always state the **percentage of HNO₃** (e.g., 2% Nital) — examiners are strict about this.
> 5. **Single etchant for all metals:** A common error is stating that Nital works for all metals. Aluminium requires **Keller's reagent**, stainless steel requires **Glyceregia** or **electrolytic etching**, etc.
> 6. **Skipping rinsing step:** Etching is reversible only by re-polishing. Always mention immediate **water + alcohol rinse + hot air dry**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Polishing and Etching:**

- The metallographic sequence is: **Sectioning → Mounting → Grinding → Polishing → Etching → Microscopy**.
- **Grinding grits follow 120 → 240 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200** (SiC papers).
- Always rotate the specimen by **90°** between successive grits.
- **Polishing abrasives** in order of typical use: **6 µm diamond → 1 µm diamond → 0.3 µm alumina → 0.05 µm colloidal silica**.
- **Polishing produces a mirror surface; etching produces the microstructure.**
- **Etching mechanism** = preferential chemical attack at higher-energy sites (grain boundaries).
- **Nital (2% HNO₃ + Ethanol)** is the universal etchant for **plain carbon steel**.
- **Picral (4% Picric Acid + Ethanol)** is preferred for **tool steels and carbide networks**.
- **Keller's reagent (HF + HCl + HNO₃ + Water)** is the standard for **aluminium alloys**.
- **Kroll's reagent (HF + HNO₃ + Water)** is the standard for **titanium alloys**.
- **Glyceregia (Glycerol + HCl + HNO₃)** is used for **austenitic stainless steel**.
- **Electrolytic polishing/etching** uses the specimen as the **anode** in an electrolytic cell.
- **Etching time is short** (5–30 s) and **irreversible** — under-etch rather than over-etch.
- After etching, always **rinse with water → alcohol → hot air dry**.
- **Electrolytic polishing** produces no mechanical deformation layer, ideal for soft metals.
- **Hardness of abrasive (Mohs)** must be **higher** than the metal being polished.
- **ASTM E407** is the international standard reference for etchant selection.
- **Etch pits** reveal dislocations and crystal orientation in single crystals.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Polishing and Etching**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metallographic Characterisations of Metallic Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metallographic Characterisations of Metallic Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Metallographic characterisation** is the systematic, scientific study of the internal microstructure of metallic materials through carefully prepared specimens, optical/electron microscopy, and quantitative image analysis. The term *metallography* literally means the *graphical (or descriptive) study of metals*. It is the **bridge between processing, structure, and properties** — the central tenet of Materials Science and Engineering.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, metallographic characterisation is not merely "looking at metals under a microscope." It is a **standardised engineering protocol** comprising four sequential operations:

1. **Specimen preparation** (sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, etching)
2. **Microscopic examination** (optical, SEM, TEM)
3. **Microstructural feature identification** (grains, phases, inclusions, defects)
4. **Quantitative metallography** (grain size, phase fraction, ASTM rating)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Metallographic characterisation is examined as a *process-control tool*. The examiner expects students to link each characterisation step to a specific engineering outcome (e.g., grain size → yield strength via the Hall-Petch relationship).

## 1.2 The Three-Tier Characterisation Hierarchy

Metallographic data is collected at three resolution levels, each answering a different question:

| Level | Technique | Magnification | Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Macro** | Visual / Low-power stereo | 1× – 50× | "Does the casting have shrinkage porosity or surface cracks?" |
| **Micro** | Optical Microscopy (OM) | 50× – 1000× | "What is the grain size and phase distribution?" |
| **Nano / Sub-micro** | SEM / TEM | 1000× – 1,000,000× | "What is the dislocation substructure or precipitate morphology?" |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bakery Inspector's Toolkit"

Imagine you are a **quality inspector in a bakery** tasked with confirming whether a chocolate cake is properly baked. You would:

- **Slice a small piece** (→ *sectioning* the metal specimen)
- **Embed it in wax for handling** (→ *mounting* in bakelite/resin)
- **Smooth it down with progressively finer sandpaper** (→ *grinding* with SiC papers)
- **Polish until it shines like a mirror** (→ *polishing* with diamond/alumina paste)
- **Apply a chemical stain to reveal the internal crumb structure** (→ *etching* with nital/picral)
- **Examine under a magnifying glass** (→ *optical microscopy*)
- **Count the air bubbles per cm²** (→ *quantitative metallography*)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Specimen:** A **metallographic specimen** (or *metallographic mount*) is a small, geometrically precise sample of a metal, prepared such that the plane of interest is flat, scratch-free, and chemically etched to reveal the true microstructure. Standard specimen diameter is typically **25 mm or 30 mm**.

## 1.4 Why Metallography Matters in Mechanical Engineering

For a Mechanical Engineer, metallography is the **forensic tool of failure analysis** and the **diagnostic tool of heat-treatment verification**. Every component in a turbine, gearbox, or pressure vessel is metallographically inspected at least once during its lifecycle. The grain size alone (quantified by the **ASTM number $G$**) can change the yield strength by **over 50%** through the Hall-Petch mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **yield strength** $\sigma_y$ of a polycrystalline metal is empirically related to its **average grain diameter $d$** by the **Hall-Petch equation**:
>
> $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$
>
> where $\sigma_0$ is the friction stress, $k_y$ is the Hall-Petch coefficient, and $d$ is the mean grain diameter. This is the single most important reason why metallographic grain-size measurement is a KTU high-yield topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Specimen Preparation Sequence

A metallographic specimen must be prepared in a strictly ordered sequence. Skipping or reversing any stage contaminates the next. The five stages are:

### Stage 1 — Sectioning
- A small piece is cut from the bulk component using an **abrasive cut-off wheel** (for hard metals) or a **precision saw** (for soft metals).
- Goal: obtain a representative sample with **minimal deformation, overheating, or burn damage**.
- Coolant is used continuously to avoid tempering the heat-affected zone.

### Stage 2 — Mounting
- The irregular specimen is encapsulated in a **mounting resin** (phenolic, epoxy, or acrylic) to provide a uniform geometry for automated polishing.
- Two methods:
  - **Hot compression mounting** (bakelite, ~150 °C, ~25 MPa)
  - **Cold mounting** (epoxy casting for heat-sensitive or porous specimens)

### Stage 3 — Grinding
- Abrasive papers of **progressively decreasing grit size** are used to remove sectioning damage and create a planar surface.
- Standard SiC grit sequence: **180 → 240 → 320 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1200** (grit number = mesh openings per inch).
- Water is used as a lubricant/coolant.
- Each grinding step rotates the specimen **90°** relative to the previous scratches — this is a **critical KTU exam point**.

### Stage 4 — Polishing
- Final surface preparation using **diamond paste** (6 µm, 3 µm, 1 µm) or **colloidal silica** (0.05 µm for OPS polish).
- Goal: a **scratch-free, mirror-finish surface** suitable for etching and microscopy.
- Performed on a rotating cloth-covered wheel (150–300 rpm).

### Stage 5 — Etching
- The mirror-finish surface is chemically attacked by an **etchant** to reveal the microstructure.
- Etching exploits the fact that **grain boundaries** and **different phases** have different chemical potentials, so they dissolve at different rates.
- **Common etchants (KTU must-know):**

| Echant | Composition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Nital** | 2–5% HNO₃ in ethanol | Plain carbon steels, ferrite, pearlite |
| **Picral** | 4% picric acid in ethanol | Carbides, tempered martensite |
| **Kalling's** | CuCl₂ + HCl + ethanol | Stainless steels, austenite |
| **Keller's** | HF + HCl + HNO₃ + H₂O | Aluminium alloys |
| **Marble's** | CuSO₄ + HCl + H₂O | Copper alloys |
| **Oberhoffer's** | FeCl₃ + CuCl₂ + HCl | Cast iron, phosphorus segregation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Etching Principle:** Atoms at **grain boundaries** have higher energy (broken bonds, segregation) → dissolve faster → produce a **groove**. Light reflects off this groove at an angle → grain boundaries appear **dark** under vertical illumination. The bulk grain interior appears **bright**.

## 2.2 Optical Microscopy (Metallurgical Microscope)

The workhorse instrument of metallography. Key components:

- **Light source:** tungsten-halogen or LED
- **Objective lens** (5×, 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×) — determines primary magnification
- **Eyepiece (ocular)** — typically 10×, total magnification = objective × ocular
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** controls **resolving power** $R$

The **Abbe diffraction-limited resolution** of an optical microscope is:

$$R = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light (~550 nm for green) and $NA = n \cdot \sin\theta$, with $n$ being the refractive index of the medium (air ≈ 1.0, immersion oil ≈ 1.515).

**Practical resolution limit of optical microscopy ≈ 0.2 µm (200 nm).** This is why nano-scale features (precipitates, dislocations) require SEM or TEM.

## 2.3 Quantitative Metallography — The Core Engineered Outputs

Quantitative metallography converts visual microstructure into **numerical parameters** that can be used in design equations. The four most important parameters are:

### Parameter A — Average Grain Diameter ($d$)
- Measured by the **linear intercept method** (Heyn line intercept).
- A test line of known length $L_T$ is drawn on the micrograph; the number of grain boundaries intersected $N_L$ is counted.
- Mean intercept length $\bar{L}$ is:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{L_T}{N_L \cdot M}$$

where $M$ is the magnification. For a 2D random section, $\bar{L} \approx 0.785 \cdot d$, where $d$ is the true 3D grain diameter.

### Parameter B — ASTM Grain Size Number ($G$)
The **ASTM E112 standard** defines the grain size number $G$ by:

$$N_{AE} = 2^{G-1}$$

where $N_{AE}$ is the number of grains per square inch at **100× magnification**. Equivalently:

$$n_L = 2^{G-1} \cdot 0.01 \quad \text{[grains/mm at 1×]}$$

The mean grain area at 100× is:

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1 \text{ in}^2}{N_{AE}} = \frac{645.16 \text{ mm}^2}{2^{G-1}}$$

The mean linear intercept $\bar{L}$ in mm at 100× is:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100} \sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}}$$

A **high ASTM number = fine grain = high strength** (Hall-Petch). Typical values: $G = 1$ to 12.

### Parameter C — Volume Fraction of a Phase ($V_f$)
- Measured by **point counting** (ASTM E562) — a grid of points is overlaid, the fraction of points falling on the phase of interest gives $V_f$.
- For a 2D random section, **Delesse's principle (1847)** states:

$$V_{V,\alpha} = A_{A,\alpha} = L_{L,\alpha} = P_{P,\alpha}$$

i.e., the volume fraction equals the areal fraction, equals the lineal fraction, equals the point fraction. This is the **fundamental theorem of stereology**.

### Parameter D — Phase Area (Manual Planimetry)
- The area of a specific phase region is measured with a **digitising tablet** or **image analysis software** (e.g., ImageJ), and divided by the total field area.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch equation | Predicting yield strength from grain size |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ | Abbe resolution limit | Optical microscope resolution |
| 3 | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{L_T}{N_L \cdot M}$ | Mean linear intercept | Grain size measurement |
| 4 | $N_{AE} = 2^{G-1}$ | ASTM grain count at 100× | Standardised grain size rating |
| 5 | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{1}{100}\sqrt{\dfrac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}}$ [mm] | Intercept vs. ASTM G | Inter-conversion |
| 6 | $V_{V} = A_A = L_L = P_P$ | Delesse stereological equality | Phase fraction measurement |
| 7 | $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{oc}$ | Total magnification | Optical microscopy |
| 8 | $n_m = 2^{G+3}$ (alternate form) | Grains/mm² at 1× | Alternate ASTM form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Metallographic Characterisation:**
> 1. **Quality control:** verify that heat treatment (annealing, quenching, tempering) was performed correctly by examining phase content and grain size.
> 2. **Failure analysis:** identify the failure mode (ductile cup-cone, brittle cleavage, fatigue striations, creep voids) from the fracture surface and microstructure.
> 3. **Process development:** correlate casting/rolling/forging parameters with the resulting microstructure.
> 4. **Alloy development:** confirm that a new composition produces the desired phase balance.
> 5. **Weld inspection:** check the heat-affected zone (HAZ) and fusion zone of welded joints.

## 2.5 Inclusion and Defect Rating Standards

- **ASTM E45:** Standard practice for determining the inclusion content in steel. Rates **Type A (sulfide), B (aluminate), C (silicate), D (globular oxide)** on severity levels **1 (thin) to 5 (heavy)**.
- **ASTM E112:** Grain size.
- **ASTM E562:** Volume fraction by point count.
- **ASTM E883:** Metallographic photography.

These standards are routinely asked in KTU viva and short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean Linear Intercept Formula

**Starting assumption:** A metallographic micrograph is captured at magnification $M$. A straight test line of length $L_T$ (measured on the micrograph, in mm) is drawn across the image. The number of grain boundaries intersected is $N_L$. We wish to determine the **true mean linear intercept length $\bar{L}$** of the grains in the actual specimen.

**Step 1 — Identify the units and the magnification correction.**

The line drawn on the micrograph is in magnified space. To get the true length on the specimen:

$$L_{true} = \frac{L_T}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Define the mean linear intercept.**

The mean linear intercept is the **average true chord length** of a grain measured along a random line. If there are $N_L$ grain boundaries intercepted along the true line length $L_{true}$, the number of grains intercepted is approximately $N_L$ (one boundary per grain transition). The mean chord length is therefore:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{L_{true}}{N_L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the magnification correction.**

$$\bar{L} = \frac{L_T / M}{N_L} = \frac{L_T}{M \cdot N_L}$$

**Step 4 — Final canonical form.**

$$\boxed{\bar{L} = \frac{L_T}{M \cdot N_L}}$$

This is the working formula. When $L_T$ is measured in mm and $M$ is dimensionless, $\bar{L}$ is in **mm**.

**Step 5 — Convert to true 3D grain diameter $d$.**

For an equiaxed polycrystal with spherical grains intersected by a random plane, the geometric relationship is:

$$d = \frac{4 \bar{L}}{\pi} \approx 1.273 \bar{L}$$

This conversion is sometimes approximated as $d \approx 1.128 \bar{L}$ for cubic grains. KTU typically uses the more common engineering approximation $d \approx 1.128 \bar{L}$ — both are accepted, but the **exact stereological factor is $4/\pi$**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the ASTM Grain Size Number Relationship

**Starting definition (ASTM E112):** The grain size number $G$ is defined such that the number of grains per square inch visible at 100× magnification is:

$$N_{AE} = 2^{G-1}$$

**Step 1 — Express mean grain area at 100×.**

At 100×, one square inch on the micrograph corresponds to $1/10000$ sq. inch on the specimen. The mean grain area at 100× is:

$$\bar{A}_{100} = \frac{1 \text{ in}^2}{N_{AE}} = \frac{1}{2^{G-1}} \text{ in}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mm² for SI usage.**

1 inch = 25.4 mm, so 1 in² = 645.16 mm². Thus:

$$\bar{A}_{100} = \frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}} \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Relate mean area to mean linear intercept.**

For roughly equiaxed grains, the mean linear intercept is geometrically related to the mean area by:

$$\bar{L} = \sqrt{\bar{A}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the magnification correction.**

At 100×, the field area on the micrograph is $100\times$ the field area on the specimen. The true mean linear intercept (at 1×) is:

$$\bar{L}_{true} = \frac{\bar{L}_{100}}{100} = \frac{1}{100} \sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}} \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Final canonical form.**

$$\boxed{\bar{L}_{true} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}} \text{ mm} \quad \text{at magnification} = 1\times}$$

**Worked numerical example for KTU problem-solving:**

If $G = 8$, compute $\bar{L}$:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{7}}} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{\frac{645.16}{128}} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{5.040}$$

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100} \times 2.2451 = 0.02245 \text{ mm} = 22.45 \text{ µm}$$

So an ASTM 8 grain has a mean intercept of about **22 µm**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hall-Petch Strength Contribution

**Empirical starting point:** Experimental data shows that for many metals, yield strength $\sigma_y$ scales as $d^{-1/2}$ where $d$ is the mean grain diameter.

**Step 1 — Assume a linear relation.**

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the friction stress (lattice resistance to dislocation motion) and $k_y$ is the Hall-Petch coefficient (a material constant).

**Step 2 — Justification from dislocation pile-up theory.**

A dislocation gliding on a slip plane in a grain of size $d$ piles up at the grain boundary, generating a back-stress $\tau_{back}$. The number of dislocations in the pile-up is $n \propto d \cdot \tau_{applied}$. The stress concentration at the pile-up tip activates slip in the adjacent grain when:

$$\tau_{applied} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{1/2} = \tau_{critical}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the yield stress.**

Rearranging:

$$\tau_{applied} = 2 \tau_{critical} \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

In terms of normal yield strength $\sigma_y$:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Practical implication.**

If grain diameter is reduced from 100 µm ($G \approx 3$) to 10 µm ($G \approx 10$), $d^{-1/2}$ increases by $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$, meaning yield strength rises by **more than 3×** due to grain-boundary strengthening alone.

For mild steel, $\sigma_0 \approx 70$ MPa and $k_y \approx 0.74$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$.

**Worked numerical example:**

A low-carbon steel has $\sigma_0 = 70$ MPa, $k_y = 0.74$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, and grain diameter $d = 25$ µm = $25 \times 10^{-6}$ m.

Compute $d^{-1/2}$:

$$d^{-1/2} = (25 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{25 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$

Compute $\sigma_y$:

$$\sigma_y = 70 + 0.74 \times 200 = 70 + 148 = 218 \text{ MPa}$$

The Hall-Petch contribution is **148 MPa** — nearly **70% of the total yield strength**. This is why grain refinement is the most cost-effective strengthening mechanism in steels.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Workflow

| Step | Action | Equation / Tool | Common Error to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the micrograph magnification | $M = M_{obj} \times M_{oc}$ | Forgetting to convert mm to µm |
| 2 | Draw test lines, count boundaries | $N_L$ | Counting triple points as 1.5 boundaries |
| 3 | Compute mean intercept | $\bar{L} = L_T / (M N_L)$ | Using $L_T$ on micrograph without dividing by $M$ |
| 4 | Convert intercept to ASTM G | $\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{645.16/2^{G-1}}$ | Forgetting the 1/100 factor |
| 5 | Use grain size in strength equation | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ | Mixing mm and m in $d$ |
| 6 | Compute phase fraction | $V_f = P_P$ from grid | Counting grid points partially |
| 7 | Identify phases via etchant | Etchant chart | Using nital on stainless steel (passivates Cr₂O₃) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** A common error is to use the intercept length $\bar{L}$ (which is a 1D measurement) directly in the Hall-Petch equation, which technically requires the true 3D grain diameter $d$. The conversion $d = 1.273 \bar{L}$ must be applied for rigorous KTU scoring.
