# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define metallography. List the five sequential stages of metallographic specimen preparation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Metallography** is the systematic study of the internal microstructure of metals and alloys using prepared specimens, microscopes, and quantitative image analysis. It links **processing → structure → properties** in the central paradigm of Materials Science.

**Five stages of specimen preparation:**
1. **Sectioning** — cutting a representative sample using an abrasive wheel.
2. **Mounting** — encapsulating the sample in bakelite/epoxy for handling.
3. **Grinding** — planarisation using SiC papers of progressively finer grit (180 → 1200).
4. **Polishing** — mirror finish using diamond paste (6 → 1 µm) and colloidal silica.
5. **Etching** — chemical attack to reveal grain boundaries and phases.

**[3 Marks]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the ASTM grain size number? Write the standard relation between $G$ and the number of grains per square inch at 100×.**

**Model Answer:**

The **ASTM grain size number $G$** (per ASTM E112) is a standardised rating index that quantifies grain size in a polycrystalline metal.

The defining relation is:

$$N_{AE} = 2^{G-1}$$

where $N_{AE}$ = number of grains visible in one square inch of a micrograph taken at **100× magnification**.

Equivalently, the mean grain area at 100× is:

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1 \text{ in}^2}{2^{G-1}}$$

A higher $G$ corresponds to a **finer** grain structure.

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **metallographic specimen preparation procedure** for a low-carbon steel sample, specifying the purpose of each stage and the role of water/coolant. List **two suitable etchants** and explain the chemical principle by which etching reveals the microstructure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A metallographic micrograph of a low-carbon steel is captured at 400× magnification. A test line of length 100 mm drawn on the micrograph intersects **62 grain boundaries**. Compute:
  (i) the mean linear intercept length $\bar{L}$
  (ii) the approximate ASTM grain size number $G$
  (iii) the expected yield strength using Hall-Petch with $\sigma_0 = 70$ MPa, $k_y = 0.74$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, and $d = 1.273 \bar{L}$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Specimen Preparation for Low-Carbon Steel**

1. **Sectioning:** A small piece is cut from the bulk using an abrasive cut-off wheel with continuous water cooling. Purpose: obtain a representative sample without overheating (which would alter the microstructure) or mechanical deformation (which would create a false work-hardened layer).

2. **Mounting:** The specimen is encapsulated in **bakelite** by hot compression mounting (~150 °C, ~25 MPa, 5 min). Purpose: provide a uniform cylindrical geometry of standard size (25 mm or 30 mm dia.) for automated grinding/polishing and easy handling.

3. **Grinding:** Abrasive SiC papers are used in the sequence **180 → 240 → 320 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1200** grit, with water as lubricant. Purpose: remove sectioning damage and progressively flatten the surface. The specimen is rotated **90°** between successive grits so that the old scratches are visible perpendicular to the new ones — this confirms complete removal of the previous layer.

4. **Polishing:** Diamond paste (6 µm, 3 µm, 1 µm) on a rotating cloth wheel produces a mirror finish. Colloidal silica (0.05 µm) gives the final polish. Purpose: eliminate all scratches to obtain a deformation-free, mirror-finish surface suitable for etching.

5. **Etching:** The polished surface is swabbed or immersed in an etchant.

**Two suitable etchants for low-carbon steel:**
- **Nital (2–5% HNO₃ in ethanol):** general-purpose etchant for plain carbon steel, reveals ferrite grain boundaries and the ferrite–pearlite structure.
- **Picral (4% picric acid in ethanol):** reveals carbide networks and prior-austenite grain boundaries, preferred for tempered martensite.

**Chemical principle of etching:**

Grain boundaries contain atoms with **higher free energy** (broken bonds, solute segregation). These atoms have a **higher chemical potential** and thus **dissolve faster** in the etchant than atoms in the ordered grain interior. The preferential dissolution creates a **groove** along each grain boundary. Under the vertical illumination of the optical microscope, light reflects off the flat grain interior into the objective (bright), but is scattered by the grooves (dark). This **differential reflectivity** produces the dark grain-boundary network and the bright grain interior that constitutes the visible microstructure.

**[7 Marks: 4 for procedure + purpose, 2 for etchants, 1 for chemical principle]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- $L_T = 100$ mm (on micrograph)
- $N_L = 62$ (boundary intersections)
- $M = 400\times$

**(i) Mean linear intercept $\bar{L}$:**

$$\bar{L} = \frac{L_T}{M \cdot N_L} = \frac{100}{400 \times 62} = \frac{100}{24800}$$

$$\bar{L} = 4.032 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 4.032 \text{ µm}$$

**(ii) ASTM grain size number $G$:**

Convert $\bar{L}$ to mm: $\bar{L} = 4.032 \times 10^{-3}$ mm at 1×.

Use the canonical formula:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}}$$

Rearrange:

$$100 \bar{L} = \sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}}$$

Square both sides:

$$(100 \bar{L})^2 = \frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}$$

$$2^{G-1} = \frac{645.16}{(100 \bar{L})^2}$$

Substitute $\bar{L} = 4.032 \times 10^{-3}$ mm:

$$100 \bar{L} = 100 \times 4.032 \times 10^{-3} = 0.4032$$

$$(100 \bar{L})^2 = 0.1626$$

$$2^{G-1} = \frac{645.16}{0.1626} = 3968.6$$

Take $\log_2$:

$$G - 1 = \log_2(3968.6) = \frac{\ln(3968.6)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{8.286}{0.6931} \approx 11.95$$

$$G \approx 12.95 \approx 13$$

This is an **extremely fine grain** (ASTM 13 is the practical upper limit; typical fine-grained commercial steels are ASTM 8–10).

**(iii) Yield strength via Hall-Petch:**

True 3D grain diameter $d$:

$$d = 1.273 \bar{L} = 1.273 \times 4.032 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5.133 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

Compute $d^{-1/2}$:

$$d^{-1/2} = (5.133 \times 10^{-6})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5.133 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2.266 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$d^{-1/2} \approx 441.3 \text{ m}^{-1/2}$$

Apply Hall-Petch:

$$\sigma_y = 70 + 0.74 \times 441.3 = 70 + 326.6 = 396.6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_y \approx 397 \text{ MPa}}$$

**[7 Marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii)]**

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **linear intercept method** for measuring grain size. Derive the relationship between the mean intercept length and the ASTM grain size number. Why is a high ASTM number desirable in structural steels?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **etching mechanism** in metallography. Differentiate between macro-etching and micro-etching. Mention two applications of each.

---

**Model Solution (concise):**

**(a)** The linear intercept method is the most widely used quantitative metallographic technique (ASTM E112). A line of known length $L_T$ is drawn on the micrograph; the number of grain boundaries intersected $N_L$ is counted. The mean intercept is:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{L_T}{M \cdot N_L}$$

**Derivation of ASTM relation:** *(as shown in Section 3.2 above)*. The canonical formula is:

$$\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{\frac{645.16}{2^{G-1}}} \text{ mm}$$

**Why high ASTM is desirable:** Per the Hall-Petch equation $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$, a fine grain (high $G$, small $d$) increases yield strength linearly in $d^{-1/2}$. Also, fine grains improve **toughness** (lower ductile-to-brittle transition temperature), **fatigue strength**, and **creep resistance** in many alloys. Fine-grained structural steels (ASTM ≥ 8) are therefore mandatory in pressure vessels, bridges, and ship hulls.

**(b) Etching mechanism:** *(as in Section 4.2 and Question A above)*. Grain boundaries are higher-energy regions → faster chemical attack → grooves → dark under vertical illumination.

**Macro-etching vs. Micro-etching:**

| Feature | Macro-etching | Micro-etching |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface** | As-cut, ground, or polished | Mirror-polished |
| **Magnification** | 1× – 50× (visual or stereo) | 50× – 1000× (optical microscope) |
| **Echant strength** | Strong (e.g., 10% nital, HCl + HNO₃ aqua regia variants) | Mild (2–5% nital, Kalling's, etc.) |
| **Time** | Minutes to hours | Seconds to minutes |
| **Purpose** | Reveal gross flow lines, weld zones, cracks, segregation, porosity | Reveal grain boundaries, phases, precipitates |

**Macro-etching applications:** (i) Weld inspection (HAZ identification), (ii) Forging flow-line check, (iii) Casting shrinkage porosity mapping.

**Micro-etching applications:** (i) Grain size measurement (ASTM G), (ii) Phase identification in heat-treated steels, (iii) Inclusion rating (ASTM E45).

**[7 Marks each sub-part]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Metallography Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide $L_T$ by $M$ in the intercept formula.** Students frequently write $\bar{L} = L_T / N_L$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units in Hall-Petch.** $k_y$ is in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** but $d$ is often given in **mm or µm** — convert $d$ to **metres** before substituting.
> 3. **Using $d^{-1/2}$ as a scalar when it should be $m^{-1/2}$.** The Hall-Petch equation is dimensionally consistent only if $d$ is in metres.
> 4. **Conflating linear intercept $\bar{L}$ with grain diameter $d$.** The conversion $d = 1.273 \bar{L}$ is mandatory for KTU full marks.
> 5. **Naming "nital" as an etchant for stainless steel.** Stainless steels passivate in nital — use **Kalling's or Vilella's** reagent.
> 6. **Forgetting to rotate the specimen 90° between grinding grits.** This is a KTU favourite 1-mark question.
> 7. **Mis-stating the ASTM relation** as $N_{AE} = 2^G$ instead of $N_{AE} = 2^{G-1}$. The "−1" is a frequent trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall Points for KTU Module 2 — Metallographic Characterisation:**

- **Five preparation stages:** Sectioning → Mounting → Grinding → Polishing → Etching. *Do not skip or reorder.*
- **Grinding grit sequence:** 180 → 240 → 320 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1200. **Rotate 90° between grits.**
- **Final polish:** Diamond paste 6 → 3 → 1 µm, then colloidal silica 0.05 µm.
- **Standard etchant for plain carbon steel:** Nital (2–5% HNO₃ in ethanol). **Stainless steel** → Kalling's. **Aluminium** → Keller's. **Copper** → Marble's.
- **Etching principle:** Grain boundary atoms have higher free energy → faster dissolution → grooves → dark lines under vertical illumination.
- **Linear intercept formula:** $\bar{L} = L_T / (M N_L)$.
- **ASTM grain count at 100×:** $N_{AE} = 2^{G-1}$.
- **ASTM → intercept conversion:** $\bar{L} = \frac{1}{100}\sqrt{645.16 / 2^{G-1}}$ mm.
- **3D grain diameter:** $d \approx 1.273 \bar{L}$.
- **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ with $d$ in **metres**.
- **Optical resolution limit:** $R = 0.61 \lambda / NA \approx 0.2$ µm.
- **Delesse's principle:** $V_V = A_A = L_L = P_P$ — volume, area, line, and point fractions are equal for a random 2D section.
- **ASTM E45:** Inclusion rating. **ASTM E112:** Grain size. **ASTM E562:** Volume fraction by point count.
- **Hall-Petch contributes up to 70% of yield strength in fine-grained steels** — making grain refinement the most cost-effective strengthening mechanism.
- **Fine grain (high G) gives high strength + high toughness + good fatigue life.**
- **Three-tier characterisation:** Macro (visual), Micro (optical, OM), Nano (SEM/TEM).
- **Finer than ASTM 10 is rare in commercial steels**; the practical upper limit is around ASTM 13.
- **ASTM G = 1 → coarse (≈ 250 µm); ASTM G = 10 → fine (≈ 11 µm).**
- **Specimen diameter is standardised at 25 or 30 mm.**
- **Optical microscope total magnification = objective × eyepiece (typically 100× max useful).**
- **Numerical aperture (NA) governs resolution**, not magnification. Higher NA → finer detail.
- **Etching time is critical** — over-etching produces artefacts; under-etching hides the microstructure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SEM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is an advanced electron-optical characterization technique that produces high-resolution, three-dimensional topographical images of a specimen's surface by scanning it with a focused beam of accelerated electrons in a raster (line-by-line) pattern and detecting the resulting secondary and backscattered electron signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) – Principle, construction, working, applications, advantages and limitations.*

In SEM, a **thermionic or field-emission electron gun** emits electrons that are accelerated through a potential difference of typically **1 kV to 30 kV**. These electrons are then focused into a fine probe (≈ 1 nm to 10 nm diameter) by a series of **electromagnetic lenses** and made to scan the specimen surface in synchronism with a **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) display**, forming a magnified image.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Flashlight & Sand-Art" Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a completely dark room and you want to study a sand sculpture on a table. You cannot use sunlight because you want to see *every* tiny grain of sand.

- You take a **super-fine laser pointer** (the **electron beam** in SEM) and sweep it across the sculpture line by line.
- Wherever the laser hits a grain of sand, the grain **bounces back tiny sparks of light** (the **secondary electrons**) in a unique pattern — some grains are bright, some dim, depending on their shape and angle.
- A **light sensor** (the **Everhart-Thornley detector**) reads the brightness of these sparks.
- A **paint-by-numbers artist** (the **CRT screen**) simultaneously draws a matching picture, dot by dot, line by line, exactly synchronized with your laser's sweep.
- Because the laser is super-fine and the room is in a **vacuum** (so nothing disturbs it), you can see grains 100,000× smaller than what your naked eye could ever see.

The resulting picture is not a photograph of light reflecting off the object — it is a **map of how the surface responded to being hit by electrons**. This is the essence of SEM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why vacuum?** Electrons are tiny charged particles that are easily deflected by air molecules. To prevent the electron beam from scattering and losing its focus, the entire column and specimen chamber are maintained at a high vacuum (typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa**).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Accelerating Voltage | **1 kV – 30 kV** (up to 200 kV in specialized SEMs) |
| Wavelength of electron (λ) | **0.001 nm – 0.01 nm** (at 30 kV) |
| Magnification range | **10× to 1,000,000×** |
| Resolution (best) | **≈ 1 nm** (field-emission SEM) |
| Working vacuum | **10⁻³ – 10⁻⁶ Pa** |
| Specimen size | Up to ~ 100 mm in diameter |

> [!NOTE]
> The extremely small de Broglie wavelength of electrons (compared to visible light at **400–700 nm**) is precisely why SEM achieves resolutions about **1000× better** than optical microscopy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The working of SEM is governed by the **electron–specimen interaction**. When the primary electron beam strikes the specimen surface, several types of signals are generated from a teardrop-shaped volume known as the **interaction volume** (typically **1 µm – 5 µm** deep).

The primary signals of interest are:

1. **Secondary Electrons (SE)** — Low-energy electrons (< 50 eV) ejected from the top few nanometres of the surface. Used for **topographical imaging**.
2. **Backscattered Electrons (BSE)** — High-energy electrons (> 50 eV) reflected from deeper regions. Used for **compositional contrast (Z-contrast)**.
3. **Characteristic X-rays** — Used in **Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS/EDX)** for elemental analysis.
4. **Auger Electrons** — Used in surface-specific chemical analysis.
5. **Cathodoluminescence (CL)** — Light emitted from the specimen; used in mineralogy and semiconductor analysis.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Image Formation

- **Step 1 — Electron Generation:** A tungsten filament (or LaB₆ or field-emission tip) is heated to ~ 2700 K, emitting electrons via thermionic emission.
- **Step 2 — Acceleration:** A high voltage (1–30 kV) between the cathode and anode accelerates the electrons into a beam.
- **Step 3 — Condenser Lens Focusing:** The first electromagnetic lens (condenser) demagnifies the electron source to ~ 50 µm.
- **Step 4 — Objective Lens & Aperture:** A fine aperture (typically 10–100 µm) clips the beam to a small diameter, and the objective lens focuses it to a **probe size of 1–10 nm** on the specimen.
- **Step 5 — Scanning Coils:** Two pairs of electromagnetic deflection coils steer the beam in a **raster pattern** (left-to-right, top-to-bottom) across the specimen.
- **Step 6 — Signal Generation:** Beam–specimen interaction produces secondary and backscattered electrons.
- **Step 7 — Detection:** The Everhart-Thornley detector (+300 V bias on collector) attracts secondary electrons. They strike a scintillator, producing photons detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT).
- **Step 8 — Image Display:** The amplified PMT signal modulates the brightness of a CRT pixel whose position on the screen is **synchronized** with the beam position on the specimen.
- **Step 9 — Scan Repetition:** The beam is scanned repeatedly (typically 30–60 frames per second) to build a real-time, stable image.

### 2.3 Why the "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why use electrons instead of light?** Because of the **Abbe diffraction limit**, optical microscopes are limited to resolutions of ~ 200 nm. Electrons have de Broglie wavelengths of picometres, so the diffraction limit is far smaller.
- **How is magnification controlled?** The **scan amplitude** on the specimen is the lever. If the scan coils sweep the beam across **1 mm** of specimen but the CRT displays it across **100 mm**, the magnification = 100/0.001 = **100,000×**.
- **How is focus achieved?** By tuning the current through electromagnetic lens coils, which changes the magnetic field strength and thus the focal length of the lens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnification in SEM is NOT achieved by lens power** (unlike in optical microscopy). It is purely a ratio between the **size of the raster on the specimen** and the **size of the raster on the display screen**. This is a high-yield exam point!

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | de Broglie Wavelength of electron | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}$ | $h$ = Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s); $m$ = electron mass (9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg); $e$ = electron charge (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C); $V$ = accelerating voltage (V) | Determines the theoretical **resolution limit** of the SEM |
| 2 | Relativistic Wavelength (high V) | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m c^2}\right)}}$ | $c$ = speed of light (3 × 10⁸ m/s) | Used for V > 50 kV |
| 3 | Magnification | $M = \dfrac{L_{CRT}}{L_{specimen}}$ | $L_{CRT}$ = display scan length; $L_{specimen}$ = specimen scan length | Image size on the monitor |
| 4 | Resolution (Rayleigh Criterion) | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{N A}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength; $NA$ = numerical aperture | Theoretical minimum resolvable distance |
| 5 | Beam Current | $I_b \propto \beta \cdot \alpha^2$ | $\beta$ = brightness; $\alpha$ = convergence semi-angle | Controls signal-to-noise ratio |
| 6 | Kanaya-Okayama Range (interaction depth) | $R_{KO} = \dfrac{0.0276 A E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \rho}$ | $A$ = atomic weight; $E$ = beam energy (keV); $Z$ = atomic number; $\rho$ = density (g/cm³) | Predicts the **penetration depth** of electrons |
| 7 | Everhart-Hoffman Range (practical) | $R_{nm} = \dfrac{25.6 E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \rho}$ | E in keV, ρ in g/cm³ | Commonly used practical interaction depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All vertical bars in the table above (e.g., between fractions) have been replaced with `\dfrac` or contextual separators to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SEM is the workhorse of **failure analysis** in mechanical engineering — examining fracture surfaces to identify the mode of failure (ductile, brittle, fatigue, creep). It is also used to study **grain size, phase distribution, inclusion analysis, surface coatings, weld microstructures, and nano-composites** in research and industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of de Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron

The wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is found by equating the kinetic energy gained by the electron to its quantum-mechanical momentum.

**Step 1:** Write the kinetic energy gained by the electron as it moves from cathode to anode:

$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 2:** Equate this kinetic energy to the non-relativistic momentum–energy relation $E_k = p^2 / (2m)$:

$$\frac{p^2}{2m} = eV$$

**Step 3:** Solve for momentum $p$:

$$p = \sqrt{2 m e V}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the de Broglie relation $\lambda = h / p$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}$$

This is the **non-relativistic** de Broglie wavelength of the electron.

**Step 5:** Plug in standard constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

**Step 6:** Compute for an accelerating voltage of $V = 30{,}000$ V:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 30{,}000}}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify the denominator:

$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3 \times 10^{4}} = \sqrt{8.7456 \times 10^{-45}}$$

**Step 8:** Take the square root:

$$= 9.352 \times 10^{-23} \text{ kg·m/s}$$

**Step 9:** Compute $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{9.352 \times 10^{-23}} = 7.08 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 0.00708 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 10:** Compare with visible light (≈ 550 nm). The electron's wavelength is roughly **77,000 times smaller** than visible light, which is why SEM dramatically exceeds the resolution of optical microscopy.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Magnification Equation in SEM

**Step 1:** Define magnification as the ratio of the size of the image on the viewing screen to the actual size scanned on the specimen:

$$M = \frac{\text{Dimension on display screen}}{\text{Dimension scanned on specimen}}$$

**Step 2:** Let the specimen be scanned over a length $L_s$ and the CRT display the same scan over a length $L_d$. Then:

$$M = \frac{L_d}{L_s}$$

**Step 3:** As a numerical example — suppose the beam scans across $L_s = 0.001$ mm of the specimen, and the CRT displays this across $L_d = 100$ mm:

$$M = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{0.001 \text{ mm}} = 100{,}000 \times$$

**Step 4:** Thus, **decreasing the scan amplitude on the specimen increases the magnification**, while keeping the display size constant. This is a critical KTU exam point — magnification in SEM is not determined by lens power, but by the **scan-coil deflection amplitude**.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Kanaya-Okayama Electron Range

The Kanaya-Okayama range predicts the depth of electron penetration into the specimen:

**Step 1:** Start with the empirical expression:

$$R_{KO} = \frac{0.0276 \cdot A \cdot E^{1.67}}{Z^{0.89} \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 2:** Consider iron (Fe): $A = 55.85$ g/mol, $Z = 26$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³, $E = 20$ keV.

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator:

$$0.0276 \times 55.85 \times 20^{1.67} = 0.0276 \times 55.85 \times 200.0 \approx 308.2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator:

$$26^{0.89} \times 7.87 = 19.85 \times 7.87 \approx 156.2$$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$R_{KO} = \frac{308.2}{156.2} \approx 1.97 \text{ µm}$$

This means a 20 keV electron beam penetrates about **2 µm** into iron — a critical value for setting the depth of focus in failure analysis.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of SEM with Optical Microscopy (OM) and TEM

| Parameter | Optical Microscope (OM) | SEM | TEM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Source of radiation | Visible light | Electron beam | Electron beam |
| Wavelength | 400–700 nm | 0.001–0.01 nm | 0.001–0.01 nm |
| Lenses | Glass | Electromagnetic | Electromagnetic |
| Specimen | Opaque/transparent | Opaque, surface only | Ultra-thin (< 100 nm) |
| Maximum useful magnification | ~ 1000× | ~ 1,000,000× | ~ 5,000,000× |
| Resolution | ~ 200 nm | ~ 1–10 nm | ~ 0.1 nm |
| Image type | True colour, real-time | Greyscale, 3D-looking surface | 2D projection, internal structure |
| Vacuum required | No | Yes (10⁻³ – 10⁻⁶ Pa) | Yes (10⁻⁵ – 10⁻⁷ Pa) |
| Specimen prep | Minimal | Coating (Au/Pt) for non-conductors | Extremely tedious (ion milling) |
| Depth of field | Shallow | Very deep | Very shallow |
| Cost | Low | High | Very high |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an SEM (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional signal and beam flow** of a Scanning Electron Microscope, from the electron source to the final image. This is preferred over an attempted physical drawing because of the system's distributed, column-based architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC High Voltage Power Supply 1 to 30 kV] --> B[Electron Source Tungsten LaB6 or FEG Cathode]
    B --> C[Wehnelt Cylinder and Anode Electron Acceleration Stage]
    C --> D[Condenser Lens 1 First Demagnification]
    D --> E[Condenser Aperture 10 to 100 micrometre]
    E --> F[Condenser Lens 2 Final Beam Shaping]
    F --> G[Objective Lens Final Focusing to 1 to 10 nm Probe]
    G --> H[Scan Coils X and Y Deflection Raster Pattern Generator]
    H --> I[Specimen Stage Vacuum Chamber 10 to the power minus 3 to 10 to the power minus 6 Pa]
    I --> J[Interaction Volume Signal Generation]
    J --> K[Secondary Electrons SE Detector Everhart Thornley]
    J --> L[Backscattered Electrons BSE Detector Solid State]
    J --> M[EDS Detector Characteristic X rays]
    K --> N[Signal Amplifier Photomultiplier Tube]
    L --> N
    M --> O[Multichannel Analyser Elemental Spectrum]
    N --> P[Image Processor and Frame Buffer]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[CRT or Digital Display Synchronized with Scan Coils]
    H --> Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> The double arrow between the **Scan Coils (H)** and **Display (Q)** is the *time-base synchronization* line. This is the backbone of SEM imaging — the beam position on the specimen and the electron beam on the display must move in lock-step.

### 4.2 Schematic of the Electron–Specimen Interaction Volume

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Primary Electron Beam 1 to 30 keV] --> S[Specimen Surface]
    S --> V[Teardrop Interaction Volume 1 to 5 micrometre]
    V --> SE[Secondary Electrons Top 5 to 50 nm Topography]
    V --> BSE[Backscattered Electrons Top 0.5 micrometre Z Contrast]
    V --> X[Characteristic X rays Whole Volume EDS Analysis]
    V --> A[Auger Electrons Top 1 nm Surface Chemistry]
    V --> CL[Cathodoluminescence Optical]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation of the Teardrop:** As the primary electron beam penetrates the specimen, it spreads sideways due to multiple elastic scattering — producing the characteristic "teardrop" or "pear" shape. The depth and width of this volume depend on the **atomic number Z** and the **accelerating voltage**.

### 4.3 Block Diagram of a Typical SEM Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COLUMN [Electron Column]
        direction TB
        A1[Electron Gun] --> A2[Condenser Lens]
        A2 --> A3[Objective Lens]
        A3 --> A4[Scan Coils]
    end
    A4 --> A5[Specimen]
    A5 --> A6[Detectors SE BSE EDS]
    A6 --> A7[Amplifier and Processor]
    A7 --> A8[Display]
    A4 -.-> A8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Secondary Electrons" in the context of SEM. How are they generated and what type of information do they provide about the specimen?*

**Model Answer:**

Secondary electrons (SE) are low-energy electrons (energy < 50 eV) generated when the primary electron beam ejects loosely-bound conduction-band electrons from the outermost few nanometres (5–50 nm) of the specimen surface. They are produced by inelastic scattering events within the top surface layer.

- **Information provided:** They are highly sensitive to **surface topography**. Surfaces tilted toward the detector emit more secondary electrons (edge effect), giving images a strong three-dimensional, shadowed appearance.
- **Detection:** Collected by the **Everhart-Thornley detector** biased at +300 V.

**(3 marks — KTU marking scheme: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for energy range and depth, 1 mark for application.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Why is a high vacuum (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa) maintained inside the SEM column and specimen chamber?*

**Model Answer:**

A high vacuum is essential in SEM for the following reasons:

1. **Preventing electron scattering:** Electrons travelling from the gun to the specimen would otherwise collide with air molecules, scattering the beam and destroying its focus.
2. **Preventing filament oxidation:** The hot tungsten filament would rapidly burn out in the presence of oxygen.
3. **Preventing specimen contamination:** Residual gas molecules can deposit as hydrocarbon contamination on the specimen surface, degrading the image.
4. **Maintaining detector stability:** High-energy electrons in the detector must travel through a controlled environment.

A typical SEM uses a **rotary pump** (for low vacuum, ~ 10⁻¹ Pa) followed by an **oil diffusion pump** or **turbomolecular pump** (for high vacuum, 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ Pa).

**(3 marks — 1 mark per correct reason, up to 3.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a Scanning Electron Microscope.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the various signals generated during electron–specimen interaction in SEM. How are secondary and backscattered electrons used to obtain different types of contrast in the image?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of SEM (7 marks):**

**Construction — Major Components:**

1. **Electron Source (Electron Gun):** A tungsten hairpin filament (cathode) heated to ~ 2700 K emits electrons via thermionic emission. The electrons are accelerated by a high voltage (1–30 kV) between the cathode and anode. A Wehnelt cylinder shapes the beam and controls emission current.
2. **Electromagnetic Lenses:** Two condenser lenses and one objective lens (each consisting of copper coils inside a soft-iron shroud with a pole-piece) demagnify the electron source into a fine 1–10 nm probe.
3. **Apertures:** Thin platinum apertures (typically 10–100 µm) clip the beam to remove off-axis electrons, improving beam coherence.
4. **Scanning Coils:** Two pairs of electromagnetic coils (one for X, one for Y) deflect the focused beam in a raster scan across the specimen. The scan signal is also fed to the CRT for synchronization.
5. **Specimen Stage:** A multi-axis stage (X, Y, Z, tilt, rotation) inside a vacuum chamber. The specimen is mounted on an aluminium stub, often coated with a thin layer of gold or carbon to make it conductive.
6. **Detectors:**
   - **Everhart-Thornley detector** (for secondary electrons) — biased scintillator + photomultiplier tube.
   - **Solid-state (semiconductor) detector** (for backscattered electrons).
   - **EDS detector** (Li-drifted Si or SDD) for X-rays.
7. **Vacuum System:** Combination of rotary + diffusion/turbomolecular pumps achieving 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa.
8. **Display System:** CRT or modern digital monitor with synchronized scan.

**Working (5 logical steps):**

1. The electron gun produces a high-energy, monochromatic electron beam.
2. The condenser and objective lenses focus the beam to a fine 1–10 nm probe.
3. The scan coils sweep the probe in a raster pattern across the specimen.
4. Beam–specimen interaction generates SE, BSE, X-rays, etc.
5. The detector signal modulates the brightness of a CRT pixel, in synchronism with the scan coils — building a real-time, magnified image.

**(7 marks — KTU marking scheme: 2 marks for construction components, 3 marks for working steps, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for neatness.)**

---

**(b) Electron–Specimen Interaction Signals and Contrast (7 marks):**

When the primary electron beam strikes the specimen, the following signals are generated (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

1. **Secondary Electrons (SE):** Low energy (< 50 eV), escape from top 5–50 nm. Provide **topographic contrast** because the SE yield depends on the local surface tilt — edges and protrusions appear bright. Resolution: ~ 1–5 nm.
2. **Backscattered Electrons (BSE):** High energy (> 50 eV), originate from top ~ 0.5 µm. The BSE yield increases monotonically with atomic number Z, providing **compositional (Z-) contrast** — heavier elements appear brighter. Resolution: ~ 50 nm.
3. **Characteristic X-rays:** Emitted when inner-shell electrons are ejected and outer-shell electrons drop down. The X-ray energies are characteristic of the element, enabling **elemental analysis via EDS**.
4. **Auger Electrons:** Surface-specific (< 1 nm) chemical information; useful for light elements.
5. **Cathodoluminescence:** Light emitted from semiconductors, minerals, and phosphors.
6. **Transmitted Electrons:** Only for very thin specimens.

**Contrast Mechanisms:**

- **Topographic contrast (SE):** Surface tilts toward detector → more SE collected → bright region. This produces the "3D shadow" look.
- **Compositional contrast (BSE):** Local variation in mean atomic number → variation in BSE intensity. A heavy carbide in a steel matrix will appear bright.
- **Voltage contrast:** Variations in surface potential show up in SE image.
- **Magnetic contrast:** Magnetic domains deflect electrons, producing domain-wall contrast.

**(7 marks — KTU marking scheme: 2 marks for listing signals, 3 marks for explaining SE and BSE contrast, 2 marks for resolution and applications.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential V. Why is this wavelength important in electron microscopy?** **(7 marks)**

**(b) What are the advantages, limitations, and major applications of SEM? Compare SEM with optical microscopy.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of de Broglie Wavelength (7 marks):**

(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation.)

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}$$

**Numerical value at 30 kV:**

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 30{,}000}} = 0.00708 \text{ nm}$$

**Importance in electron microscopy:**

1. The resolving power of any microscope is **fundamentally limited by the wavelength** of the radiation used (Abbe diffraction limit).
2. Optical microscopes use visible light ($\lambda \approx 550$ nm) → resolution limit ≈ 200 nm.
3. Electrons accelerated at 30 kV have $\lambda \approx 0.007$ nm — about **80,000 times smaller** than visible light.
4. This theoretically allows a resolving power below 1 Å, although practical resolutions are limited by **lens aberrations** (spherical and chromatic) to ~ 1 nm in SEM.

**(7 marks — KTU marking scheme: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for numerical substitution, 2 marks for importance.)**

---

**(b) Advantages, Limitations, Applications, and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Advantages of SEM:**

1. **High resolution** — down to 1 nm (FE-SEM).
2. **Large depth of field** — about 300× that of an optical microscope; whole rough specimens can be in focus at once.
3. **Wide magnification range** — from 10× to 1,000,000×.
4. **3D appearance** of surface images from SE imaging.
5. **Compositional information** via BSE and EDS.
6. Minimal specimen preparation compared to TEM.

**Limitations of SEM:**

1. Specimens must be **vacuum-compatible** and **electrically conductive** (non-conductors require gold coating).
2. The specimen chamber is small — limited sample size.
3. **Cost** is high (₹ lakhs to crores).
4. Cannot observe **internal structure** (only surface and near-surface).
5. Sensitive to **specimen contamination** and **charging artifacts**.
6. Cannot image **wet or hydrated** samples (no environmental SEM without special detectors).

**Applications of SEM:**

1. **Fractography** — identifying ductile/brittle/fatigue failure modes in mechanical components.
2. **Microstructural analysis** of metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites.
3. **Failure analysis** of welds, castings, and machined parts.
4. **Coating and thin-film characterization.**
5. **Nanoparticle and nanofiber morphology.**
6. **Forensic science** (gunshot residue, hair analysis).
7. **Biological specimens** (insects, plant tissue, bone, microorganisms).
8. **Semiconductor device inspection.**

**Comparison with Optical Microscopy:**

| Feature | Optical Microscope | SEM |
|---|---|---|
| Magnification | up to ~ 1000× | up to ~ 1,000,000× |
| Resolution | ~ 200 nm | ~ 1 nm |
| Depth of field | shallow | very deep |
| Specimen | any | conductive, dry, vacuum-compatible |
| Image | colour, 2D | greyscale, 3D-looking |
| Cost | low | high |

**(7 marks — KTU marking scheme: 2 marks for advantages, 2 marks for limitations, 2 marks for applications, 1 mark for comparison.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing SE and BSE:** Students often say "secondary electrons show composition" — **WRONG**. SE = topography; BSE = composition. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Stating "Magnification is changed by lens power":** In SEM, magnification is purely a function of **scan-coil deflection amplitude**, not lens strength. This is a 2-mark KTU question almost every year.
> 3. **Forgetting the vacuum requirement:** Many students omit the vacuum pump system. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Specimen preparation skipped:** Always mention the **gold sputter coating** for non-conductive samples.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the diagram:** A neat block diagram is mandatory for full marks in 7-mark construction questions. Missing it = 1–2 marks lost.
> 6. **Writing "electron microscope uses light":** Always state "electron beam" — never say "light" or "rays" in a way that confuses with optical microscopy.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SEM** = Scanning Electron Microscope; uses a focused electron beam scanned in a raster pattern.
- **Resolution** of SEM is governed by the **de Broglie wavelength** of the electron, given by $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m e V}$, giving λ ≈ 0.007 nm at 30 kV.
- **Magnification** in SEM = **display scan length / specimen scan length** — NOT a function of lens power.
- **Major components:** Electron gun, electromagnetic lenses (condenser + objective), apertures, scan coils, specimen stage, Everhart-Thornley detector, vacuum system, display.
- **Everhart-Thornley detector** collects **secondary electrons** for topography; **Solid-state detector** collects **backscattered electrons** for Z-contrast.
- **Five main signals** generated: SE, BSE, characteristic X-rays, Auger electrons, cathodoluminescence.
- **SE** come from the top 5–50 nm → topography; **BSE** come from top ~ 0.5 µm → composition.
- **EDS** (Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy) is integrated with SEM for **elemental analysis**.
- **Vacuum level:** typically **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa**, achieved by rotary + turbomolecular/diffusion pumps.
- **Specimen preparation:** Non-conductive samples are **sputter-coated** with gold or platinum (~ 10 nm thick).
- **Advantages:** high resolution (1 nm), large depth of field, 3D appearance, wide magnification range.
- **Limitations:** vacuum requirement, small chamber, high cost, conductive specimen only (unless coated), no internal structure.
- **Applications:** fractography, failure analysis, microstructural analysis, coating characterization, nanoparticle morphology, forensic science, biology.
- **Always remember:** SEM ≠ TEM. SEM images **surface**; TEM images **internal** structure of ultra-thin specimens.
- **Always synchronize** the scan coils with the display scan — this is the essence of SEM imaging.
- **Magnification formula** $M = L_{CRT} / L_{specimen}$ is a high-yield KTU problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TEM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** TEM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)** is a high-resolution imaging and analytical technique in which a beam of accelerated electrons is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (typically less than **100 nm** thick). The interaction of the transmitted electrons with the atomic lattice of the specimen produces a magnified image, diffraction patterns, and spectroscopic signals that reveal the **microstructure, crystal structure, morphology, and composition** of materials down to the **atomic scale** (resolving power ≈ **0.05 nm to 0.2 nm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 2 – Microscopy):**
> TEM is taught alongside Optical Microscopy (OM) and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). Students must clearly distinguish:
> - **OM** → uses *visible light*, max resolution ≈ 200 nm.
> - **SEM** → uses *scattered/secondary electrons*, surface imaging, resolution ≈ 1–10 nm.
> - **TEM** → uses *transmitted electrons*, internal imaging, resolution < 0.2 nm (atomic scale!).

The fundamental working principle is based on the **de Broglie hypothesis** (1924), which states that electrons possess wave-like properties with wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

where $h$ is Planck's constant and $p$ is the momentum of the electron. By accelerating electrons through potentials of **60 kV to 300 kV**, their effective wavelength shrinks to **picometers (pm)**, which is **100,000× smaller** than visible light — this is what gives TEM its extraordinary resolving power.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of TEM as an "Ultra-Thin Slice Movie Projector."**
>
> Imagine you have a solid steel block and you want to *see the atoms inside it*. You can't use a regular light microscope because the wavelength of light (~550 nm) is far too large to resolve atoms (~0.2 nm) — analogous to trying to feel the texture of a coin using a football. So you:
>
> 1. **Slice the block** into a wafer thinner than a soap bubble (≈ 50–100 nm).
> 2. **Shoot electrons** (wavelength ≈ 0.0025 nm) through this slice instead of light.
> 3. The electrons pass *through* the atoms and get deflected by them.
> 4. A magnetic lens system (acting like the glass lenses in a movie projector) magnifies the resulting electron "shadow" onto a fluorescent screen or camera.
> 5. The result? A **giant, high-resolution image of the atomic arrangement!**
>
> In essence: **Light Microscope uses photons + glass lenses; TEM uses electrons + electromagnetic lenses.** Both produce a magnified image, but the electron microscope operates at a wavelength 5 orders of magnitude smaller.

## 1.3 Salient Features of TEM

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Source of radiation | Electron beam (thermionic or field emission) |
| Accelerating voltage | **40 kV – 300 kV** (commonly 100/200 kV) |
| Effective wavelength | **0.00197 nm (200 kV) to 0.0087 nm (40 kV)** |
| Resolving power | **0.05 nm – 0.2 nm** (lattice imaging) |
| Useful magnification | **50× to 1,500,000×** |
| Specimen requirement | Ultra-thin, electron-transparent (< 100 nm) |
| Imaging mode | Bright-field, Dark-field, Diffraction |
| Lens type | Electromagnetic (magnetic field–focused) |
| Vacuum | **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ Pa** (high vacuum) |
| Specimen environment | Room temp., cryo-TEM, in-situ heating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always remember the **two fundamental requirements** of any microscope (Abbe's criterion):
> 1. **Short wavelength** of the probing radiation.
> 2. **High numerical aperture** of the lens system.
> TEM satisfies both: picometer-scale wavelength + tightly focused magnetic lenses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operational Principles

The operation of a TEM rests on **four sequential physics principles**, each of which is examinable:

### Step 1 — Electron Generation (The "Gun")
- A **thermionic emission source** (tungsten filament W, or LaB₆ crystal) or a **field emission gun (FEG)** releases electrons.
- Tungsten filament is heated to ≈ **2800 K**, liberating electrons via thermal excitation.
- FEG provides a much brighter, more coherent source — critical for high-resolution imaging.

### Step 2 — Acceleration and Wavelength Reduction
- Electrons are accelerated by a potential difference $V$ (typically 100–300 kV).
- The kinetic energy acquired is $E = eV = \frac{1}{2}m_0v^2$ (non-relativistic).
- For high voltages ($V > 50$ kV), the electron velocity approaches a significant fraction of $c$ (speed of light), so **relativistic corrections are mandatory** in KTU problems.

### Step 3 — Electromagnetic Focusing (The "Lenses")
- Magnetic lenses (electromagnetic solenoids with iron pole-pieces) focus the electron beam.
- The focal length $f$ of a magnetic lens is given by:
$$f \approx \frac{K \cdot V}{(N \cdot I)^2}$$
  where $K$ is a constant, $V$ is the accelerating voltage, $N$ is the number of turns, and $I$ is the lens current.
- **Key insight:** Unlike optical lenses, the focal length of an EM lens is **continuously variable** by changing the coil current $I$.

### Step 4 — Image Formation
- The image is formed by electrons that are either:
  - **Unscattered** (direct beam) → forms the **bright-field image**.
  - **Elastically scattered** (diffracted beam) → forms the **dark-field image**.
  - **Inelastically scattered** → used for EELS (Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy).

## 2.2 Types of Contrast in TEM

| Contrast Type | Origin | Image Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Mass-thickness contrast** | Variation in density and thickness | Heavier/thicker regions appear darker |
| **Diffraction contrast** | Bragg diffraction from crystal planes | Dislocations, stacking faults, grain boundaries visible |
| **Phase contrast** | Interference of diffracted + direct beams | Atomic lattice fringes, high-resolution imaging |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Compare Bright-Field and Dark-Field imaging in TEM."* This is a 7-mark KTU standard. Bright-Field uses the direct (unscattered) beam — empty holes in aperture — regions diffracting strongly appear *dark*. Dark-Field uses a diffracted beam — those same diffracting regions appear *bright* against a dark background.

## 2.3 Specimen Preparation (Critical for KTU)

Because electrons have **very limited penetration** in solids (mean free path ~ 10–100 nm in metals), specimens must be extraordinarily thin:

1. **Mechanical thinning** — grinding/polishing to ≈ 100 μm.
2. **Dimpling** — concave grinding to ≈ 10–20 μm at the center.
3. **Ion-beam milling (Ion milling)** — Ar⁺ ions at 3–6 kV sputter the specimen to electron transparency (< 100 nm).
4. **Electropolishing** — electrochemical dissolution (common for metals).
5. **Replica technique** — for non-conductive or powder samples.
6. **FIB (Focused Ion Beam) lift-out** — site-specific preparation for SEM/TEM cross-sections.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are HIGH-FREQUENCY in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions. Memorize each with its variable meanings and units.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{mv}$ | de Broglie wavelength (non-relativistic) | m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V}}$ | Non-relativistic wavelength of accelerated electron | m |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$ | **Relativistic wavelength** (use for V > 50 kV) | m |
| 4 | $eV = m_0 c^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}} - 1\right)$ | Relativistic kinetic energy | J or eV |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{m_0}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}$ | Relativistic mass | kg |
| 6 | $d_{min} \approx 0.61 \dfrac{\lambda}{n \sin \alpha}$ | Abbe's diffraction-limited resolution | m |
| 7 | $2 d \sin \theta = n \lambda$ | **Bragg's law** (for electron diffraction in TEM) | — |
| 8 | $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{int} \times M_{proj}$ | Total magnification (obj + intermediate + projector lens) | — |
| 9 | $f \approx \dfrac{K V}{(N I)^2}$ | Focal length of magnetic lens | m |
| 10 | $r = \dfrac{m v}{e B}$ | Radius of electron in magnetic field (B = lens strength) | m |
| 11 | $\Delta f = C_s \alpha^2$ | Spherical aberration contribution to defocus | m |
| 12 | $f_{obj} = \dfrac{\text{constant}}{I_{lens}}$ | Lens current controls focal length (qualitative) | m |

**Numerical values to memorize:**

| Constant | Value |
|---|---|
| $h$ (Planck's constant) | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| $m_0$ (electron rest mass) | $9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |
| $e$ (electron charge) | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| $c$ (speed of light) | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| $m_0 c^2$ (rest energy) | **511 keV** = $8.187 \times 10^{-14}$ J |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> TEM is **the workhorse of modern materials R&D** and failure analysis:
> - **Semiconductor industry**: identifying dislocations in Si wafers, dopant segregation, gate-oxide defects.
> - **Aerospace alloy development**: precipitation hardening in Ni-superalloys, γ′ phase identification.
> - **Battery research**: Li-ion electrode SEI layer characterization, Li plating/stripping.
> - **Biomedical**: cryo-TEM for virus structures, protein folding.
> - **Steel industry**: carbide identification, dislocation density measurement.
> - **Nanotechnology**: visualizing individual nanoparticles, CNTs, graphene edges.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Non-Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength of an Accelerated Electron

> **KTU Importance:** This is the derivation most commonly asked in Part A (3-mark) and short Part B (7-mark) questions.

**Given:**
- An electron of mass $m_0$ and charge $e$ is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$.

**Step 1 — Energy gained by the electron:**
The work done by the electric field on the electron equals its kinetic energy:
$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 2 — Express kinetic energy in terms of momentum:**
For a non-relativistic particle:
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m_0 v^2 = \frac{p^2}{2 m_0}$$

(using the identity $p = m_0 v$, so $p^2 = m_0^2 v^2$, hence $\frac{p^2}{2m_0} = \frac{1}{2}m_0 v^2$).

**Step 3 — Solve for momentum:**
$$p^2 = 2 m_0 eV$$
$$p = \sqrt{2 m_0 eV}$$

**Step 4 — Apply de Broglie relation:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV}}$$

**Numerical values** (substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C):

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2.919 \times 10^{-49} \times V}}$$

$$\lambda \approx \frac{12.27 \times 10^{-10}}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ m} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$

**Step 5 — Example for $V = 100$ kV = $10^5$ V:**
$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{10^5}} = \frac{1.227}{316.23} = 0.00388 \text{ nm} = 3.88 \text{ pm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare this to green light ($\lambda = 550$ nm). The electron wavelength is **~140,000× smaller** — that's why TEM can resolve atomic features.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relativistic Wavelength Correction

> **KTU Importance:** Used when $V > 50$ kV. Standard 200 kV TEM requires relativistic formula.

**Step 1 — Relativistic kinetic energy:**
From Einstein's special relativity, the total energy is $E = \gamma m_0 c^2$, where $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}$.
The kinetic energy is:
$$E_k = (\gamma - 1) m_0 c^2 = eV$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\gamma$:**
$$\gamma = 1 + \frac{eV}{m_0 c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Relativistic momentum:**
$$p = \gamma m_0 v = m_0 v \cdot \gamma$$

A more direct route uses the relativistic energy-momentum relation:
$$E^2 = (p c)^2 + (m_0 c^2)^2$$

The total energy $E = eV + m_0 c^2$, so:
$$(eV + m_0 c^2)^2 = (p c)^2 + (m_0 c^2)^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $p$:**
$$(p c)^2 = (eV + m_0 c^2)^2 - (m_0 c^2)^2$$

Expanding:
$$(p c)^2 = eV(eV + 2 m_0 c^2)$$

$$p = \frac{1}{c}\sqrt{eV(eV + 2 m_0 c^2)} = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}{1}$$

**Step 5 — de Broglie wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$$

**Step 6 — Verification for $V = 200$ kV:**

Compute the correction factor:
$$\frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2} = \frac{200 \times 10^3 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}$$

$$= \frac{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}{1.6396 \times 10^{-13}} = 0.1954$$

So the relativistic wavelength is:
$$\lambda_{rel} = \frac{\lambda_{non-rel}}{\sqrt{1.1954}} = \frac{\lambda_{non-rel}}{1.0934}$$

Non-relativistically for 200 kV:
$$\lambda_{non-rel} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{2 \times 10^5}} = \frac{1.227}{447.2} = 0.00274 \text{ nm}$$

Relativistically:
$$\lambda_{rel} = \frac{0.00274}{1.0934} = 0.00251 \text{ nm} = 2.51 \text{ pm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the relativistic wavelength is **smaller** than the non-relativistic value because the relativistic mass is greater, making the momentum (and thus 1/λ) larger. This **8% correction** is non-negligible and KTU examiners expect you to mention it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Magnification of TEM

**Step 1 — TEM has a three-stage lens system:**
- **Objective lens** (forms the first intermediate image, $M_1$)
- **Intermediate lens** (further magnifies, $M_2$)
- **Projector lens** (final magnification to screen/camera, $M_3$)

**Step 2 — Each lens magnification:**
For a thin lens, magnification is:
$$M_i = \frac{L_i}{f_i}$$
where $L_i$ is the image distance and $f_i$ is the focal length.

**Step 3 — Total magnification:**
Since the lenses are in series (image of one becomes object of next):
$$M_{total} = M_1 \times M_2 \times M_3 = \frac{L_1}{f_1} \cdot \frac{L_2}{f_2} \cdot \frac{L_3}{f_3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical 200 kV TEM, the objective lens magnification alone is ≈ 50×, and the total can reach **1,000,000×**. The screen acts as the final "image plane" at ≈ 50 cm from the projector lens.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Resolving Power (Abbe's Criterion Applied to TEM)

**Step 1 — Abbe's resolution limit** for a microscope is:
$$d_{min} = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{n \sin \alpha} \approx \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

where $n \sin \alpha$ is the numerical aperture (NA).

**Step 2 — In TEM, the objective aperture controls $\alpha$** (semi-angle of collection), and the wavelength $\lambda$ is determined by $V$.

For 200 kV electrons ($\lambda \approx 2.51$ pm) with $\alpha \approx 10$ mrad:
$$d_{min} \approx \frac{0.61 \times 2.51 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 0.01} = 1.53 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} = 0.153 \text{ nm}$$

This is **comparable to atomic spacings** (e.g., d-spacing in {110} planes of Fe is 0.203 nm).

**Step 3 — Practical resolution is also limited by spherical aberration** $C_s$ of the magnetic objective lens:
$$d_{sph} = 0.66 (C_s \lambda^3)^{1/4}$$

For $C_s \approx 1$ mm and $\lambda = 2.5$ pm:
$$d_{sph} = 0.66 (10^{-3} \times (2.5 \times 10^{-12})^3)^{1/4} = 0.66 \times 10^{-10} = 0.066 \text{ nm} = 0.66 \text{ Å}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why modern "aberration-corrected TEMs" (with $C_s$ correctors) achieve sub-Ångström resolution (~0.05 nm), allowing **direct imaging of atomic columns**.

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Equation

| Situation in KTU Problem | Equation to Use |
|---|---|
| $V < 30$ kV | Non-relativistic $\lambda = 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm |
| $V \geq 50$ kV | Relativistic $\lambda$ (mandatory) |
| Asked for electron velocity | $v = c\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{(1 + eV/m_0c^2)^2}}$ |
| Resolving power given NA | Abbe's formula $d = 0.61\lambda / NA$ |
| Diffraction pattern indexing | Bragg's law $2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$ |
| Specimen thickness estimate | Mean free path ≈ 10–100 nm for 100 keV electrons |
| Camera length constant $L\lambda$ | Used to index SAD (Selected Area Diffraction) patterns |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a TEM (Top-Down Block Flow)

This Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional architecture of a TEM column**, mapping the electron path, lens functions, and signal outputs. It is designed as a fallback to physical drawings and renders directly in Mermaid-compatible markdown viewers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EGUN[Electron Source W Filament or LaB6 FEG]
    ACCEL[Acceleration Anode 60 to 300 kV]
    CL1[Condenser Lens C1 Primary Beam Size Control]
    CL2[Condenser Lens C2 Beam Convergence and Aperture]
    SPEC[Specimen Stage Ultra Thin lt 100 nm]
    OBJ[Objective Lens Primary Image Formation]
    OAP[Objective Aperture BF or DF Selection]
    SAD[Selected Area Aperture Limits Diffraction Region]
    INT[Intermediate Lens Magnification Control]
    PROJ[Projector Lens Final Image to Screen]
    SCR[Fluorescent Screen Visual Image]
    CAM[CCD or CMOS Camera Digital Recording]
    DIFF[SAED Pattern Diffraction Mode Output]
    EDS[EDS Detector Chemical Analysis]
    EELS[EELS Spectrometer Inelastic Scattering]

    EGUN --> ACCEL --> CL1 --> CL2 --> SPEC --> OBJ --> OAP
    OAP --> INT --> PROJ --> SCR
    OAP --> DIFF
    PROJ --> CAM
    SPEC -.-> EDS
    SPEC -.-> EELS
    OBJ -.-> SAD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows depict the *primary electron beam path*. The dotted arrows depict *secondary signals* (X-rays for EDS, energy-loss electrons for EELS) that exit the specimen column laterally. The Objective Aperture is the **switching point** — selecting the direct beam gives Bright-Field, selecting a scattered beam gives Dark-Field, removing the aperture gives Diffraction mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four "outputs" (IM1–IM4) are the four primary analytical channels of a TEM: **imaging (BF/DF), diffraction, spectroscopy (EELS), and composition (EDS)**. A modern TEM is therefore both a microscope and a complete materials analysis laboratory in a single column.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse SEM and TEM in exam answers.** SEM scans the surface with a focused probe; TEM transmits electrons *through* the specimen. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks immediately if these are swapped.
> 2. **Always state the assumption of relativity** in wavelength problems. If the question gives $V = 100$ kV or higher, you must use the relativistic formula. Using the non-relativistic one is a 2-mark penalty in numerical problems.
> 3. **Specimen preparation is mandatory** in TEM — many students forget to mention that specimens must be < 100 nm thick, leading to a -1 mark deduction.
> 4. **Bright-field vs Dark-field confusion:** BF = unscattered/direct beam (diffracting regions appear *dark*). DF = scattered beam (diffracting regions appear *bright*).
> 5. **Numerical units:** $\lambda$ in **m or nm**, $V$ in **V (not kV)**, mass in **kg** (not grams). Examiners reject answers with mixed units.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]

**Define Transmission Electron Microscopy. State any two advantages of TEM over Optical Microscopy.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) is a characterization technique in which a high-energy beam of electrons (typically 60–300 kV) is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (< 100 nm), and the transmitted/scattered electrons are focused by electromagnetic lenses to form a highly magnified image or diffraction pattern of the internal structure at near-atomic resolution.
>
> **Advantages of TEM over Optical Microscopy:**
> 1. **Higher resolution:** TEM achieves resolution of 0.05–0.2 nm versus ~200 nm for optical microscopes, because the de Broglie wavelength of electrons (~0.0025 nm) is ~10⁵ times smaller than visible light.
> 2. **Dual information capability:** TEM can simultaneously provide a magnified image (real-space) and a diffraction pattern (reciprocal space), enabling correlation of microstructure with crystal structure — impossible with optical microscopy.
> 3. *Optional third:* Direct visualization of lattice planes, dislocations, and atomic columns.

*(Self-Assessment: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for two distinct advantages. Mentioning the wavelength comparison is the high-value key word.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]

**Explain the need for high vacuum ($10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ Pa) inside the TEM column.**

**Model Answer:**

> A high vacuum is essential in the TEM column for **three reasons**:
>
> 1. **Mean free path of electrons:** Electrons must travel from the gun to the screen (≈ 1–2 m) without colliding with gas molecules. At atmospheric pressure, the mean free path is only ~100 nm; at 10⁻⁶ Pa, it exceeds several meters — ensuring the beam reaches the specimen and screen unperturbed.
>
> 2. **Filament/source protection:** The tungsten filament operates at ~2800 K; any residual gas would oxidize or erode it rapidly.
>
> 3. **Specimen integrity:** Reactive gases would contaminate or oxidize the ultra-thin specimen, and hydrocarbons would polymerize on the specimen forming a "contamination spot" that obscures imaging.
>
> Therefore, the entire column from gun to camera is evacuated to a high vacuum of 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ Pa using rotary, diffusion, and ion pumps.

*(Self-Assessment: Each reason ~1 mark. Examiners specifically look for the "mean free path" argument.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Derive an expression for the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential $V$. Hence, calculate the wavelength for $V = 60$ kV. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a Transmission Electron Microscope. Compare its resolving power with that of an optical microscope. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Work-energy theorem:**
The work done on an electron accelerated through potential $V$ becomes its kinetic energy:
$$E_k = eV$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of momentum:**
For non-relativistic motion: $E_k = \dfrac{p^2}{2m_0}$, where $m_0$ is the electron rest mass.

**Step 3 — Solve for momentum:**
$$p^2 = 2 m_0 eV \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \sqrt{2 m_0 eV}$$

**Step 4 — de Broglie relation:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the standard values** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times V}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{12.27 \times 10^{-10}}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ m} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$

**Step 6 — For $V = 60$ kV $= 6 \times 10^4$ V:**
$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{6 \times 10^4}} = \frac{1.227}{244.95} = 5.01 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 5.01 \text{ pm}$$

**Conclusion:** $\lambda \approx 0.005$ nm for 60 kV electrons — about **100,000× smaller** than visible light, hence the superior resolution.

*(Marks distribution: Derivation = 4 marks, numerical substitution = 2 marks, conclusion = 1 mark.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Construction (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):**
A TEM consists of:
1. **Electron source** (electron gun) — W filament, LaB₆, or FEG.
2. **Acceleration anode** — 60 to 300 kV high-voltage supply.
3. **Condenser lens system** (C1, C2) — controls beam size and convergence.
4. **Specimen stage** — ultra-thin (<100 nm) specimen, often with tilt/rotation.
5. **Objective lens + objective aperture** — forms the primary image; aperture selects BF or DF beam.
6. **Intermediate and projector lenses** — further magnify the image.
7. **Fluorescent screen + CCD/CMOS camera** — viewing and recording.
8. **Vacuum system** — 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ Pa throughout.

**Working Principle:**
- Electrons emitted from the source are accelerated and focused into a thin, coherent beam by the condenser lenses.
- The beam strikes the ultra-thin specimen; some electrons pass through (transmitted), some are scattered.
- The objective lens forms a magnified intermediate image; the objective aperture selects which electrons contribute (BF = direct, DF = scattered).
- Intermediate and projector lenses further magnify the image onto a fluorescent screen, where it is viewed and recorded.

**Resolving Power Comparison:**

| Parameter | Optical Microscope | TEM |
|---|---|---|
| Radiation | Visible light (λ ≈ 550 nm) | Electron beam (λ ≈ 0.005 nm at 60 kV) |
| Resolving power $d_{min}$ | $\approx 200$ nm | $\approx 0.1$–$0.2$ nm |
| Useful magnification | ≈ 1000× | up to 1,000,000× |
| Lens | Glass | Electromagnetic |

By Abbe's criterion, $d_{min} = 0.61 \lambda / (n \sin \alpha)$. Since $\lambda_{TEM} \ll \lambda_{light}$, the resolving power of TEM is ~1000× greater.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the various modes of imaging in TEM. With suitable diagrams, distinguish between Bright-Field, Dark-Field, and Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) modes. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A TEM is operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Calculate (i) the velocity of the electrons, and (ii) the relativistic de Broglie wavelength. Compare this wavelength with that of visible light. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Three Primary Imaging Modes in TEM:**

**1. Bright-Field (BF) Imaging (3 marks):**
- The **objective aperture** is positioned to allow only the **direct (unscattered) transmitted beam** to form the image.
- Regions of the specimen that scatter electrons strongly (e.g., thick or high-density regions, grains oriented for strong Bragg diffraction) appear **dark** on a bright background.
- BF gives **mass-thickness contrast** and **diffraction contrast**.

**2. Dark-Field (DF) Imaging (2 marks):**
- The objective aperture is shifted to admit **only one diffracted beam**.
- Only those regions of the specimen satisfying the Bragg condition for the chosen reflection appear **bright** on a dark background.
- DF is especially useful for imaging dislocations, precipitates, and stacking faults.

**3. Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) (2 marks):**
- The intermediate lens is focused on the **back-focal plane of the objective lens** instead of the image plane.
- A **selected-area aperture** restricts the diffraction pattern to a chosen region (~1 μm diameter) of the specimen.
- The resulting pattern is an array of spots (single crystal) or rings (polycrystalline), used for crystal structure determination, d-spacing measurement, and orientation analysis.
- **Camera equation:** $R \cdot d = L \lambda$ (where $R$ = spot/ring radius, $L$ = camera length, $\lambda$ = wavelength).

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrams** (as in Section 4.3) are essential. In a KTU 14-mark answer, allocating ~1 mark for sketches is standard.

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $V = 200 \text{ kV} = 2 \times 10^5$ V; electron rest energy $m_0 c^2 = 511$ keV.

**(i) Relativistic velocity of the electron (3 marks):**

The total relativistic energy is $E = m_0 c^2 + eV$.

The Lorentz factor:
$$\gamma = \frac{E}{m_0 c^2} = 1 + \frac{eV}{m_0 c^2} = 1 + \frac{200}{511} = 1 + 0.3914 = 1.3914$$

Velocity:
$$v = c\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{\gamma^2}} = c\sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{1.9360}} = c\sqrt{0.4835}$$

$$v = 0.6953 \, c = 0.6953 \times 3 \times 10^8 = 2.086 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Note:** This is **0.7 c** (70% the speed of light) — confirming the **relativistic nature** of the electron and justifying the use of relativistic equations.

**(ii) Relativistic de Broglie wavelength (3 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_0 eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2}\right)}}$$

Compute the correction term:
$$\frac{eV}{2 m_0 c^2} = \frac{200}{2 \times 511} = \frac{200}{1022} = 0.1957$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 1.1957}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{6.978 \times 10^{-44}}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{2.6416 \times 10^{-22}}$$

$$\lambda = 2.51 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 2.51 \text{ pm} = 0.00251 \text{ nm}$$

**Comparison with visible light (1 mark):**
$$\frac{\lambda_{light}}{\lambda_{TEM}} = \frac{550 \text{ nm}}{0.00251 \text{ nm}} \approx 2.19 \times 10^5$$

The electron wavelength is **~220,000 times smaller** than visible light, which is the fundamental reason TEM can resolve atomic-scale features.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — TEM:**

- **TEM = Transmission Electron Microscopy.** Electrons pass *through* the specimen.
- **Specimen must be < 100 nm thick** (electron-transparent). Thicker specimens are opaque to electrons.
- **Operating voltage:** 60–300 kV (common 100, 200, 300 kV). Higher V → smaller λ → higher resolution.
- **Non-relativistic wavelength:** $\lambda = 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ nm (use for V < 30 kV).
- **Relativistic wavelength:** $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_0 eV (1 + eV / 2 m_0 c^2)}$ (use for V ≥ 50 kV — **mandatory in 200 kV problems**).
- **Resolution:** 0.05–0.2 nm — ~1000× better than optical microscopy.
- **Magnification:** up to 1,000,000×. Total $M = M_{obj} \times M_{int} \times M_{proj}$.
- **Electromagnetic lenses** (solenoid + iron pole-piece) replace glass lenses. Focal length varies with coil current.
- **High vacuum (10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ Pa)** is essential to prevent electron scattering by gas molecules.
- **Three imaging modes:**
  - **BF (Bright-Field):** direct beam; diffracting regions appear dark.
  - **DF (Dark-Field):** one diffracted beam; diffracting regions appear bright.
  - **SAED:** diffraction pattern from a selected area; spots = single crystal, rings = polycrystalline.
- **Two contrast mechanisms:** mass-thickness contrast and diffraction contrast; HRTEM adds phase contrast.
- **Bragg's law** in TEM: $2 d \sin \theta = n \lambda$ — central to SAED analysis.
- **Camera equation:** $R \cdot d = L \lambda$ — for indexing diffraction patterns.
- **Auxiliary detectors:** EDS (chemical analysis via X-rays), EELS (inelastic energy loss).
- **Specimen preparation methods:** mechanical thinning → dimpling → ion milling / electropolishing / FIB lift-out.
- **TEM vs SEM vs OM:** OM uses light (surface, 200 nm), SEM scans with electrons (surface, 1–10 nm), TEM transmits electrons (internal, 0.05–0.2 nm).
- **Key constants to remember:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $m_0 c^2 = 511$ keV.
- **Crossover check question:** *"Why can't an optical microscope resolve atoms?"* — Because $\lambda_{light} \approx 200$ nm >> atomic spacing ~ 0.2 nm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grain Size Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Grain Size Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Grain Size Determination** is the quantitative metallographic procedure used to measure the average diameter, area, or number of grains per unit volume in a polycrystalline metal or alloy, as standardized under **ASTM E112** ("Standard Test Methods for Determining Average Grain Size"). Grain size is a **microstructural metric** that directly governs the mechanical, physical, and chemical response of engineering materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A single polycrystalline specimen contains thousands of grains (also called **crystallites**). Each grain is a single crystal with a unique lattice orientation, separated from its neighbours by a **grain boundary** — a 2-D defect of width typically 2–5 atomic diameters.

The **ASTM Grain Size Number (G)** is defined by the relation:

$$N = 2^{G-1}$$

where $N$ is the number of grains per square inch observed at **100× magnification**. A higher $G$ means a finer grain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Raisin Bread" Model

Imagine two identical loaves of bread:
- **Loaf A** has 10 huge raisins randomly embedded.
- **Loaf B** has 1000 tiny raisins packed densely.

Both loaves weigh the same, but their **internal texture** is wildly different. The "small-raisin loaf" (fine-grained) is **stronger, tougher, and less likely to tear**, just as a **fine-grained metal is stronger, harder, and more ductile** than a coarse-grained one of the same composition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The grain boundary acts like a "speed bump" for dislocations. More boundaries = more speed bumps = more resistance to plastic deformation. This is the heart of the **Hall-Petch Strengthening Mechanism**.

## 1.3 Why Grain Size Determination Matters in KTU Context

- **Mechanical Behaviour:** Yield strength, hardness, and toughness scale inversely with $\sqrt{d}$ (grain diameter).
- **Heat Treatment Verification:** Confirms whether annealing, normalizing, or grain-refinement treatments succeeded.
- **Failure Analysis:** Coarse grains often correlate with brittle fracture in service.
- **Quality Control in Foundries & Forges:** Mandatory in aerospace, nuclear, and pressure-vessel codes (ASME Sec. III, IS 2825).
- **Phase-Transform & Recrystallization Studies:** Tracks grain growth kinetics.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Terminology (Board-Exam Favourite):**
> - **Polycrystal** – aggregate of many crystals.
> - **Grain Boundary (GB)** – interface between two grains of differing orientation.
> - **Twin Boundary** – special mirror-symmetric GB (e.g., in annealing twins of brass).
> - **ASTM** – American Society for Testing and Materials.
> - **Etchant** – chemical solution (e.g., Nital, Kalling's) that reveals grain boundaries by preferential attack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standardized Methods under ASTM E112

ASTM E112 recognizes **three principal methods** for grain size measurement. Each is tuned to a different accuracy / time tradeoff, and KTU board questions routinely test the comparison and procedural differences.

### 2.1.1 Comparison Method (Procedure A)
- Uses **standard reference chart plates** (usually 8–10 photomicrographs at 100×).
- The metallographer visually matches the unknown microstructure to the closest standard plate.
- Reports the **ASTM Grain Size Number (G)** directly.
- **Pros:** Fastest (< 2 minutes).
- **Cons:** Subjective; ±0.5 G accuracy only.

### 2.1.2 Planimetric (Jeffries) Method (Procedure B)
- Counts the **number of grains within a known area** $A$ (typically a 5000 mm² circle on a ground glass).
- Uses the Jeffries multiplier formula to correct for grains touching the boundary.
- **Pros:** Most accurate; ±0.1 G.
- **Cons:** Tedious for very fine grains.

### 2.1.3 Heyn's Linear Intercept Method (Procedure C)
- Draws one or more **test lines of known length** $L$ across the micrograph.
- Counts the number of grains **intercepted** by the line.
- Computes the mean linear intercept $\bar{L}_3$.
- **Pros:** Best for non-equiaxed (elongated) grains; widely used in industry.
- **Cons:** Slight underestimation due to grains not lying on a true great circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three methods?** A coarse-grained cast structure is easiest by comparison; a fine-grained, equiaxed wrought structure by Jeffries; a deformed/banded structure by intercepts. KTU examiners expect students to **justify the choice of method**, not just list it.

## 2.2 The Hall–Petch Relationship (Foundational Equation)

The yield strength of a polycrystalline metal depends on grain size via:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

where:
- $\sigma_y$ = yield stress (MPa)
- $\sigma_0$ = friction stress (resistance of lattice to dislocation motion)
- $k_y$ = Hall–Petch coefficient (material-dependent strengthening constant)
- $d$ = mean grain diameter (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important equation** in the grain-size module. Plotting $\sigma_y$ vs $d^{-1/2}$ gives a straight line; the slope and intercept yield $k_y$ and $\sigma_0$ respectively.

## 2.3 ASTM Grain Size Number — Exact Definition

The number of grains per square inch at 100× is:

$$N = 2^{G-1}$$

The corresponding number per mm² at 1× (i.e., the actual grain count) is:

$$n_L = \frac{N}{(645.16 \text{ mm}^2/\text{in}^2) \times 10^4} = 2^{G+3} \text{ grains/mm}^2$$

> This is at 1×, but a 100× micrograph shows an area 10,000× smaller, so the per-area count is divided by $10^4$. Many KTU problems use the **per mm² at 1×** convention.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard / Method | Typical Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $N = 2^{G-1}$ | Grains/in² @ 100× | ASTM E112 | dimensionless |
| 2 | $n_0 = 2^{G+3}$ | Grains/mm² @ 1× | ASTM E112 | grains/mm² |
| 3 | $\bar{L}_3 = \frac{L_T}{N_L}$ | Mean linear intercept | Heyn | mm or μm |
| 4 | $N_L = \frac{M \cdot N_i}{L_T}$ | Intercepts per mm | Heyn (M = mag.) | mm⁻¹ |
| 5 | $N_A = f \cdot (N_{\text{inside}} + 0.5 N_{\text{on boundary}})$ | Grains per unit area | Jeffries | — |
