Always cite the **sampling length $l_r$** per ISO 4288. > 3. **Confusing $Rz$ (mean of 5 pairs) with $Rt$ (single max)** — Both are peak-to-valley heights but differ in definition. > 4. **Skipping the mean-line condition** — The centerline must satisfy $\int z(x)\,dx = 0$. Students often forget to mention this in derivations. > 5. **Wrong assumption** — Applying $Rq = 1.11 \times Ra$ for **non-Gaussian** profiles (e.g., honed, lapped) — the ratio can deviate up to $\pm 20\%$. > 6. **No mention of evaluation length** — KTU expects $l_n = 5 \times l_r$ mention whenever $Ra$ is reported. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Roughness** = micro-scale (high-frequency) surface irregularities, distinct from **waviness** (meso) and **form** (macro). - [x] **Primary standard:** ISO 4287 (parameters), ISO 4288 (sampling), ISO 1302 (drawing indication). - [x] **$Ra$** is the default parameter on engineering drawings (most reproducible). - [x] **$Ra$** = arithmetic mean of $\vert z(x) \vert$ over sampling length $l_r$. - [x] **$Rq$** = RMS value of $z(x)$; equals standard deviation of height distribution. - [x] **$Rz$** = mean of 5 highest peak-valley pairs in $l_r$. - [x] **$Rt$ (or $Rmax$)** = single maximum peak-to-valley height over evaluation length $l_n$. - [x] **$l_n = 5 \times l_r$** always — this is the evaluation length. - [x] **Cutoff $\lambda_c$** is selected per ISO 4288 table based on $Ra$ range. - [x] **Stylus tip radius:** 2 µm (smooth) to 10 µm (rough). - [x] **Stylus material:** Diamond (extreme hardness, no wear). - [x] **Transducers used:** LVDT or piezoelectric. - [x] **$Rq \approx 1.11 \times Ra$** for Gaussian surfaces — memorize the constant. - [x] **$Rz \approx 4$ to $6 \times Ra$** empirically — typically use 5. - [x] **Drawing symbols:** `√` = roughness required; `(√)` bar = material removal; `√○` = no material removal. - [x] **Lay symbols:** `=` parallel, `⊥` perpendicular, `✕` crossed, `C` circular, `R` radial, `M` multidirectional. - [x] **Feed rate dominates** $Ra$ in machining; $Ra \approx f^2 / 8R$ (turning). - [x] **Practical correlations:** Lower $Ra$ → better fatigue life, lower friction, better sealing, higher cost. - [x] **Skewness $Rsk$:** negative = valleys dominant (good for lubrication); positive = peaks dominant (brittle failure risk). - [x] **Kurtosis $Rku > 3$** = sharp peaks (poor for fatigue); **$Rku < 3$** = rounded/flat (good for sealing). - [x] **Always state** $l_r$, $\lambda_c$, and the parameter being reported. ---

topics.title: Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 MODULE 2 — CORROSION: COMPREHENSIVE STUDY NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Corrosion** is the gradual degradation of a metal (or alloy) caused by a chemical or electrochemical reaction with its surrounding environment. It is essentially the **reverse process of extractive metallurgy** — the metal tends to revert to its thermodynamically stable oxidized state (ore form) such as oxides, hydroxides, sulphides, or carbonates.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, corrosion is defined as:
> *"The destruction or deterioration of a metal by chemical or electrochemical reaction with its environment."* — Fontana, M.G. (adopted in PCMET205 module notes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** Corrosion is **NOT** a pure chemical process in most engineering cases. It is predominantly an **electrochemical phenomenon** involving the simultaneous occurrence of an anodic (oxidation) and a cathodic (reduction) reaction on a metal surface in the presence of an electrolyte.

### 📊 Economic & Engineering Significance

- The **global annual cost of corrosion** is estimated to be roughly **3–4% of a nation's GDP** (often cited as ~3.5%).
- In India, this translates to losses of several thousand crore rupees per year in infrastructure, oil & gas pipelines, marine structures, and chemical plants.
- **Every Mechanical Engineer** must understand corrosion because it directly affects:
  - **Design life** of components
  - **Maintenance costs**
  - **Safety** (e.g., boiler tube failure, bridge collapse)
  - **Material selection** decisions

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"Corrosion is to metals what aging is to humans."**
> Just as a human body slowly degrades over time due to interaction with oxygen, water, and stress, a metal slowly "ages" and "decays" when exposed to moisture, oxygen, salts, and acids. The metal "remembers" its original ore state (where the energy was lower) and tries to return there — releasing energy in the process.

**Real-world examples of corrosion:**
- 🟤 **Rust on iron** — familiar reddish-brown Fe₂O₃·H₂O
- 🟢 **Green patina on copper roofs** — basic copper carbonate
- ⚪ **White patches on aluminium utensils** — Al₂O₃ formation
- 🚢 **Hull damage in ships** — galvanic corrosion between Cu and Fe
- 🛢️ **Underground pipeline leaks** — soil + stray current corrosion
- 🏗️ **Spalling of reinforced concrete** — rebar corrosion due to chloride ingress

### 🔑 Key Terms to Memorize

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Anode** | Electrode where **oxidation** occurs (electrons released). The metal dissolves here. |
| **Cathode** | Electrode where **reduction** occurs (electrons consumed). Protected. |
| **Electrolyte** | Ion-conducting medium (water, salt solution, soil moisture) |
| **Metallic path** | Electrical contact required to complete the cell |
| **EMF of cell** | Driving force for corrosion (E_cathode − E_anode) |
| **Passivity** | State where a metal resists corrosion due to a thin, adherent, protective oxide film |

### 📐 Important Physical Constants (Bold for memorization)

- **Faraday's constant, F = 96,485 C/equivalent ≈ 96,500 C/mol**
- **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Temperature, T (in Kelvin)** — typically **298 K (25 °C)** is used as the standard reference
- **Standard electrode potentials** — measured relative to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**, defined as **E° = 0.000 V at 25 °C, 1 atm H₂, [H⁺] = 1 M**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical (Galvanic) Mechanism of Corrosion

The **electrochemical theory** is the foundation of wet corrosion. It states that corrosion occurs through the formation of a **short-circuited galvanic cell** on the metal surface. Four essential components are required:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Electrochemical Corrosion (Tafel-Galvanic Model)**
> 1. **Anode** — the site of metal dissolution (oxidation)
> 2. **Cathode** — the site of reduction (e.g., O₂ + H₂O → OH⁻)
> 3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conduction path (e.g., moisture, salt water)
> 4. **Electrical connection (metallic path)** — electron conduction path

If any ONE of these four is missing, corrosion will **NOT** proceed. This is the basis of corrosion prevention techniques.

### ⚙️ Anodic Reaction (Oxidation)

At the anode, the metal loses electrons and goes into solution as ions:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^{-}$$

**Common examples:**
- Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
- Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻
- Al → Al³⁺ + 3e⁻
- Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻

### ⚙️ Cathodic Reactions (Reduction)

The most common cathodic reactions in corrosion (in neutral/alkaline aerated solutions — the most common scenario):

$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2OH^{-} \quad \text{(Oxygen reduction — neutral/alkaline)}$$

In acidic solutions:

$$2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2 \quad \text{(Hydrogen evolution)}$$

In aerated acidic solutions:

$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2O \quad \text{(Oxygen reduction — acid)}$$

### ⚙️ Overall Corrosion Reaction (Rusting of Iron)

For iron in aerated neutral water, the overall process is:

$$4Fe + 3O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O \quad \text{(Hydrated ferric oxide — rust)}$$

Intermediate steps:
- Anode: Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
- Cathode: ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻
- Combined: Fe²⁺ + 2OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂
- Further oxidation: 4Fe(OH)₂ + O₂ + 2H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃
- Dehydration: 2Fe(OH)₃ → Fe₂O₃ + 3H₂O

## 2.2 The Galvanic Series (Marine) — High-Yield KTU Topic

Unlike the **standard EMF series** (measured under standard conditions), the **Galvanic Series** ranks metals/alloys by their **actual corrosion behaviour in seawater**. Metals at the top are **anodic** (corrode), metals at the bottom are **cathodic** (protected).

> [!NOTE]
> **Abridged Galvanic Series (from anodic to cathodic):**
> **Most Anodic (most prone to corrode)**
> - Magnesium
> - Zinc
> - Aluminium alloys
> - Cadmium
> - Mild steel, Cast iron
> - Stainless steels (active)
> - Lead, Tin
> - Nickel (active)
> - Brasses, Copper bronzes
> - Copper
> - Stainless steels (passive)
> - Silver, Graphite
> - Platinum, Gold
> **Most Cathodic (most noble — least prone to corrode)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **EMF (Electrochemical) Series** with the **Galvanic Series**. The EMF series gives precise voltage values under standard conditions, while the galvanic series is empirical (no exact values) but reflects **real** behaviour in service.

## 2.3 Types of Corrosion — KTU Module 2 Syllabus Coverage

### (a) Uniform / General Corrosion
- Even loss of material across the entire exposed surface.
- Most common type; predictable; easiest to design against (use a corrosion allowance).
- Example: Rusting of an exposed steel tank.

### (b) Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion
- Occurs when two **dissimilar metals** are in electrical contact in an electrolyte.
- The **more anodic metal corrodes** faster; the **more cathodic** is protected.
- Example: Cu–Fe couple in a hot water tank causes pitting of steel.

### (c) Pitting Corrosion
- Highly localized, produces deep narrow pits.
- Occurs when the protective film is locally broken and the rest remains passive.
- **Most dangerous** because it causes sudden failure with little mass loss.
- Common in stainless steels in chloride (Cl⁻) environments.

### (d) Crevice Corrosion
- Localized corrosion inside crevices, gaskets, lap joints, under deposits.
- Driven by **differential aeration cell** — oxygen-depleted (anodic) interior vs. oxygen-rich (cathodic) exterior.

### (e) Intergranular Corrosion
- Corrosion along **grain boundaries** of alloys.
- Classic example: **Sensitization of austenitic stainless steel (e.g., 304)** where Cr-depleted zones form at grain boundaries due to Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation, causing "weld decay."

### (f) Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)
- Simultaneous action of **tensile stress** + **specific corrosive environment** → cracking.
- Examples: "Season cracking" of brass in ammonia; caustic embrittlement of steel in boilers; SCC of stainless steel in Cl⁻.

### (g) Erosion Corrosion
- Accelerated corrosion due to **mechanical wear** + chemical attack.
- Common in pipes carrying abrasive slurries, pump impellers.

### (h) Concentration Cell Corrosion
- Caused by **differences in electrolyte concentration** (e.g., oxygen concentration cell, metal ion concentration cell).
- Subtypes: differential aeration, salt concentration cells.

## 2.4 Dry / Chemical Corrosion

Occurs in the **absence of an electrolyte**, typically in high-temperature gaseous environments. It is purely chemical and follows **parabolic, linear, or logarithmic** rate laws.

**Examples:** Oxidation of metals in air at elevated temperatures, e.g., scale formation on hot-rolled steel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pilling-Bedworth Ratio (PBR):**
> $$\text{PBR} = \frac{\text{Volume of oxide formed}}{\text{Volume of metal consumed}}$$
> - **PBR < 1** → oxide is porous, non-protective (e.g., Mg, Ca)
> - **PBR ≈ 1 to 2** → protective, adherent oxide (e.g., Al, Cr, stainless steels) — **good corrosion resistance**
> - **PBR > 2** → oxide spalls off (e.g., Fe — rust flakes off)

## 2.5 Factors Affecting Corrosion

| Factor | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| **Nature of metal** | Position in galvanic series; passive film presence |
| **Purity of metal** | Impurities create micro-galvanic cells |
| **Nature of environment** | pH, dissolved O₂, Cl⁻, H₂S, temperature |
| **Temperature** | Higher T → faster reaction (Arrhenius), but reduces O₂ solubility |
| **Velocity of fluid** | Higher → more O₂ + erosion (often worsens corrosion) |
| **Stress** | Promotes SCC, corrosion fatigue |
| **Microbial activity** | MIC (microbiologically influenced corrosion) |

## 2.6 Corrosion Prevention & Control Methods

> [!TIP]
> **The Three Master Strategies (from Fontana) — KTU Favorite**
> 1. **Modify the metal** — alloying (e.g., adding Cr to make stainless steel)
> 2. **Modify the environment** — inhibitors, deaeration, pH control
> 3. **Modify the design / add a barrier** — coatings, cathodic protection

### Cathodic Protection (CP) — Most Tested Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cathodic Protection:** The metal to be protected is **forced to act as the cathode** by supplying electrons from an external source.

**Two methods:**
1. **Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic Anode) Method**
   - Attach a more active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) to the structure.
   - Sacrificial anode corrodes preferentially.
   - Used for: ship hulls, underground pipelines (small installations).

2. **Impressed Current Method**
   - External DC power source + **inert anode** (e.g., graphite, Ti, scrap Fe).
   - The structure is connected to the **negative** terminal.
   - Used for: large pipelines, water tanks, marine structures.

**Anodic Protection** (less common): Metal made anodic by applying positive potential; relies on forming a passive film. Used for stainless steels in H₂SO₄.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — KTU frequently tests these directly.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[M^{n+}]}{1}$ | **Nernst Equation** (general form) | V |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{[M^{n+}]}{1}$ | Nernst equation at **T = 298 K** (2.303 RT/F = 0.0591) | V |
| 3 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | EMF of corrosion cell | V |
| 4 | $W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = Z \cdot I \cdot t$ | **Faraday's First Law** (mass of metal dissolved) | grams |
| 5 | $\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$ | **Faraday's Second Law** (ratio of masses) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E = \frac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight (M = molar mass) | g/equiv |
| 7 | $R_c = \frac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate (g·m⁻²·h⁻¹) | g/m²·h |
| 8 | $\text{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Corrosion rate in **mils per year** (mpy); D = density in g/cm³ | mpy |
| 9 | $\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}$ | Pilling–Bedworth ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $E^{\circ} = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log K$ | Relation of standard EMF to equilibrium constant | V |

Where:
- $E$ = electrode potential, $E^{\circ}$ = standard electrode potential
- $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = absolute temperature, $F$ = 96,485 C
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per ion
- $W$ = mass of metal dissolved, $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s)
- $M$ = atomic mass, $E$ (in formula 5) = equivalent weight
- $Z = \frac{M}{nF}$ = electrochemical equivalent (kg/C)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion shortcut for mpy (memorize for KTU):**
> $$\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t \cdot (\text{hrs})}$$
> where W is mass loss in mg, D is density in g/cm³, A is area in in², t is exposure in hours.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (for M → Mⁿ⁺ + n e⁻)

The Nernst equation is the **cornerstone of corrosion thermodynamics** and is **routinely asked in KTU exams**. Below is the complete, exhaustive derivation.

### Setup

Consider a reversible electrode reaction at equilibrium:

$$M^{n+} + n e^{-} \rightleftharpoons M$$

The Gibbs free energy change is related to the electrical work done:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. At equilibrium ($\Delta G = 0$, $Q = K$):

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

### Electrical Work

The reversible electrical work done in transferring $n$ moles of electrons at potential $E$ is:

$$W_{elec} = -nFE$$

For a cell reaction, $\Delta G = -nFE$, hence:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

### Combining the Equations

Substitute $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ into $\Delta G = -nFE$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Rearranging:

$$nFE = nFE^{\circ} - RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### Special Case: Pure Metal in Contact with its Ions

For the half-reaction $M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$, activity of solid $M$ is 1. Thus $Q = \frac{1}{[M^{n+}]}$, and:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{1}{[M^{n+}]}$$

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [M^{n+}]$$

### Conversion to Base-10 Logarithm

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log [M^{n+}]$$

At standard temperature $T = 298$ K:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.0591 \text{ V}$$

So the **Nernst equation at 25 °C** simplifies to:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$$

This is the working equation KTU expects in numericals.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Nernst Equation (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 1:** The standard electrode potential of the Cu²⁺/Cu couple is **+0.34 V**. Calculate the electrode potential when $[Cu^{2+}] = 0.01$ M at 25 °C.

**Solution (step-by-step):**

For the reaction $Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu$, $n = 2$.

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0591}{n} \log [Cu^{2+}]$$

$$E = 0.34 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log (0.01)$$

$$E = 0.34 + 0.02955 \times (-2)$$

$$E = 0.34 - 0.0591 = 0.2809 \text{ V}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Faraday's First Law (for KTU Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

**Derivation:**

The charge $Q$ passed is:

$$Q = I \times t$$

Number of moles of electrons transferred = $Q / F$.

Each ion requires $n$ electrons, so number of moles of metal deposited:

$$\text{moles of } M = \frac{Q}{nF}$$

Multiplying by atomic mass $M$ (g/mol):

$$W = \frac{M \cdot Q}{nF} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF} = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

where $Z = \frac{M}{nF}$ is the **electrochemical equivalent** (mass deposited per coulomb).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Faraday's Law (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 2:** A current of **2 A** is passed through a solution of CuSO₄ for **1 hour** using copper electrodes. Calculate:
> (a) the mass of copper deposited,
> (b) the corrosion rate in **mpy** if the exposed area is **100 cm²** and copper density is **8.96 g/cm³**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$W = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF} = \frac{63.5 \times 2 \times 3600}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$W = \frac{457200}{192970} = 2.369 \text{ g}$$

**Part (b):**

Using $R_c = \frac{I \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot t}$ (in g·m⁻²·h⁻¹):

- $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$
- $t = 1 \text{ h}$

$$R_c = \frac{2 \times 63.5}{2 \times 96485 \times 0.01 \times 1} = \frac{127}{1929.7} = 0.0658 \text{ g/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$$

Converting to mpy (multiply by appropriate factors):
- 1 g/m²·h = 0.0134 mpy approximately → 0.0658 × 0.0134 = ~**0.88 mpy**

Or using the direct formula:

$$\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \times W (\text{mg})}{D \times A (\text{in}^2) \times t (\text{hr})}$$

- W = 2369 mg
- A = 100 cm² = 15.5 in²
- D = 8.96 g/cm³
- t = 1 h

$$\text{mpy} = \frac{534 \times 2369}{8.96 \times 15.5 \times 1} = \frac{1,265,046}{138.88} = 9108 \text{ mpy}$$

(This is the theoretical rate if 100% of current goes to metal loss.)

## 3.5 EMF & Cell Potential Calculation — Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 3:** A galvanic couple is formed between **Zn** ($E^{\circ} = -0.76$ V) and **Cu** ($E^{\circ} = +0.34$ V) in an electrolyte. Determine:
> (a) which metal corrodes,
> (b) the cell EMF,
> (c) the direction of electron flow.

**Step-by-step solution:**

(a) The metal with the **more negative** $E^{\circ}$ is the anode (corrodes). So **Zinc corrodes**; **Copper is protected**.

(b) EMF of the cell:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = E^{\circ}_{Cu} - E^{\circ}_{Zn}$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10 \text{ V}$$

(c) Electrons flow from **Zn (anode) → Cu (cathode)** through the external metallic path.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** **"AN OX, RED CAT"** — **AN**ode is **OX**idation; **RED**uction occurs at **CAT**hode.

## 3.6 Standard Electrode Potentials Table (EMF Series) — KTU High-Yield

| Electrode Reaction | Standard Potential $E^{\circ}$ (V) |
|--------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Au³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Au | **+1.50** (most noble) |
| O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O | **+1.23** |
| Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag | **+0.80** |
| Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu | **+0.34** |
| 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ | **0.00** (SHE reference) |
| Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb | **−0.13** |
| Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Fe | **−0.44** |
| Cr³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Cr | **−0.74** |
| Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Zn | **−0.76** |
| Al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → Al | **−1.66** |
| Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Mg | **−2.37** (most active) |

## 3.7 Tafel Equation (Briefly for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tafel Equation** (for corrosion kinetics):
> $$\eta = a + b \log i$$
> where $\eta$ is the **overpotential**, $i$ is the **current density**, $a$ and $b$ are Tafel constants. The intersection of the anodic and cathodic Tafel lines gives the **corrosion current density** $i_{corr}$, from which the corrosion rate is calculated.

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define corrosion. List any four types of corrosion with one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Corrosion:** Corrosion is the destruction or gradual deterioration of a metal by chemical or electrochemical reaction with its surrounding environment. It is essentially the reverse of extractive metallurgy.

**Four types of corrosion:**

| # | Type | Example |
|---|------|---------|
| 1 | **Uniform corrosion** | Rusting of an exposed steel tank |
| 2 | **Galvanic corrosion** | Cu–Fe couple in a hot water tank |
| 3 | **Pitting corrosion** | Stainless steel pits in chloride-rich seawater |
| 4 | **Intergranular corrosion** | Weld decay in sensitized 304 stainless steel |

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State Faraday's laws of electrolysis. Write the mathematical form of the first law.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.
>
> **Mathematical form:**
> $$W \propto Q \quad \text{or} \quad W = Z \cdot I \cdot t = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF}$$
> where W = mass deposited (g), I = current (A), t = time (s), M = atomic mass, n = valency, F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C), Z = electrochemical equivalent.

> **Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights.
> $$\frac{W_1}{W_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme provides internal choice. We present two alternative questions.

### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **electrochemical mechanism of corrosion** of iron in aerated neutral water. Write the relevant anodic, cathodic, and overall reactions. State the four conditions necessary for corrosion to occur.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **cathodic protection**? With neat sketches, explain the **sacrificial anode method** and the **impressed current cathodic protection method**. Compare the two methods.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Electrochemical Mechanism of Corrosion of Iron:**

Corrosion of iron in aerated neutral water is a classic example of **wet (electrochemical) corrosion**. The mechanism is explained using the **electrochemical cell theory** as follows:

**Step 1: Formation of the galvanic cell**

When iron is exposed to aerated water, microscopic regions on the iron surface become the **anode** and **cathode** due to heterogeneity (impurities, inclusions, surface roughness, differential aeration, etc.).

**Step 2: Anodic reaction (oxidation)**

At the anode, iron atoms lose electrons and enter the solution as Fe²⁺ ions:

$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 3: Cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction)**

At the cathode, dissolved oxygen from the water is reduced by the electrons liberated at the anode:

$$\frac{1}{2} O_2 + H_2O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2OH^{-}$$

**Step 4: Formation of corrosion product**

The Fe²⁺ ions and OH⁻ ions migrate toward each other through the electrolyte and combine:

$$Fe^{2+} + 2OH^{-} \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2 \text{ (ferrous hydroxide)}$$

**Step 5: Further oxidation to rust**

The Fe(OH)₂ is further oxidized by dissolved oxygen to form hydrated ferric oxide (rust):

$$4Fe(OH)_2 + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3$$

$$2Fe(OH)_3 \rightarrow Fe_2O_3 \cdot 3H_2O \text{ (Hydrated ferric oxide — rust)}$$

**Overall Reaction:**

$$4Fe + 3O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O$$

**Four Essential Conditions for Corrosion (the absence of ANY ONE stops corrosion):**

1. **Anode** — site of metal dissolution
2. **Cathode** — site of reduction
3. **Electrolyte** — ionic conduction path (e.g., moisture, salt solution)
4. **Metallic (electrical) path** — electron conduction path between anode and cathode

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Cathodic Protection (CP):**

> Cathodic protection is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the **metal to be protected is made the cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing its dissolution.

**1. Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic Anode) Method:**

In this method, a more **electrochemically active metal** (like Zn, Mg, or Al) is connected to the structure to be protected. The active metal (anode) corrodes preferentially and supplies electrons to the protected structure, keeping it as the cathode.

**Construction details:**
- Sacrificial anodes (Zn blocks) are welded to the steel structure (e.g., ship's hull).
- Anode must be in electrical contact with the structure and immersed in the electrolyte (seawater/soil).
- Periodic replacement of consumed anodes is required.

**2. Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) Method:**

In this method, an **external DC power source** is used. The structure to be protected is connected to the **negative terminal** of the DC source (making it the cathode), while an **inert auxiliary anode** (graphite, scrap steel, or titanium) is connected to the **positive terminal**.

**Construction details:**
- A rectifier converts AC to DC.
- Reference electrodes (e.g., Cu/CuSO₄) monitor the potential.
- Current is continuously applied and adjusted to maintain protective potential.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode | Impressed Current |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Power source | None (natural EMF) | External DC rectifier |
| Anode material | Active metal (Zn, Mg) | Inert (graphite, Ti, scrap Fe) |
| Anode consumption | High (must be replaced) | Low (long life) |
| Cost (initial) | Low | High |
| Cost (long-term) | Higher (anode replacement) | Lower |
| Current capacity | Limited | Adjustable, high |
| Applications | Small installations, ships, domestic | Large pipelines, marine platforms |
| Suitability for high-resistivity electrolytes | Limited | Good |

---

### **Question B [Alternative 14-Mark Question — KTU Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the different **types of corrosion**? Explain **galvanic corrosion** and **pitting corrosion** in detail with suitable examples and prevention methods.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The standard electrode potential of the **Cu²⁺/Cu** half-cell is **+0.34 V** and that of the **Zn²⁺/Zn** half-cell is **−0.76 V**. (i) Calculate the **EMF of the Cu–Zn galvanic cell**. (ii) Identify the **anode and cathode**. (iii) State **Faraday's first law of electrolysis** and calculate the mass of copper deposited when a current of **5 A** is passed through a CuSO₄ solution for **2 hours** (atomic mass of Cu = 63.5 g/mol, n = 2, F = 96,485 C).

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

**Types of Corrosion (Table — Draw as needed):**

| S.No. | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| 1 | Uniform | Even attack over whole surface |
| 2 | Galvanic | Two dissimilar metals in electrolyte |
| 3 | Pitting | Localized deep cavities |
| 4 | Crevice | Inside crevices/gaps |
| 5 | Intergranular | Along grain boundaries |
| 6 | Stress corrosion cracking | Tensile stress + corrosive environment |
| 7 | Erosion corrosion | Wear + chemical attack |

**Galvanic Corrosion (Detailed):**

Galvanic corrosion occurs when **two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in the presence of an electrolyte**. The metal with the **more negative (more active) electrode potential** becomes the anode and corrodes preferentially; the other becomes the cathode and is protected.

- **Example:** Cu–Fe couple in a hot water tank. The iron pipes corrode rapidly because copper (cathodic) and iron (anodic) are connected electrically in hot water (electrolyte).
- **Prevention:**
  1. Use metals close together in the galvanic series.
  2. Avoid coupling of metals with large EMF difference.
  3. Use insulating gaskets or sleeves at joints.
  4. Apply protective coatings.
  5. Install a third (sacrificial) anode.

**Pitting Corrosion (Detailed):**

Pitting corrosion is a **highly localized form of attack** that produces deep, narrow cavities (pits) on the metal surface. It is one of the most dangerous forms because it causes **failure with minimal mass loss**.

- **Mechanism:** Local breakdown of the passive film (e.g., Cr₂O₃ on stainless steel) creates a small anodic area, while the surrounding large passive area acts as cathode — leading to an extremely high anodic current density and rapid pit penetration.
- **Example:** Stainless steel 304 in chloride-containing water develops pits at the Cl⁻ attack sites.
- **Prevention:**
  1. Use metals with higher pitting resistance (e.g., 316 SS with Mo).
  2. Keep Cl⁻ concentration low.
  3. Avoid stagnant conditions.
  4. Use cathodic protection.
  5. Use higher Cr, Mo, N alloys.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:**
- $E^{\circ}_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V
- $E^{\circ}_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V
- $I = 5$ A
- $t = 2$ hours = 7200 s
- $M_{Cu} = 63.5$ g/mol
- $n = 2$ (for Cu²⁺)
- $F = 96,485$ C

**(i) EMF of the cell:**

Since Cu has a higher (more positive) potential, it becomes the **cathode**, and Zn becomes the **anode**.

$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = E^{\circ}_{Cu} - E^{\circ}_{Zn}$$

$$E_{cell} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Anode and Cathode:**

- **Anode (corrodes):** Zinc — $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^{-}$
- **Cathode (protected):** Copper — $Cu^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow Cu$

Electron flow: **Zn (anode) → Cu (cathode)** through the external metallic path.

**(iii) Faraday's First Law:**

> *"The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte."*

$$W = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

$$W = \frac{63.5 \times 5 \times 7200}{2 \times 96485}$$

$$W = \frac{2,286,000}{192,970}$$

$$W \approx 11.85 \text{ g}$$

**Therefore, ~11.85 g of copper is deposited.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Corrosion problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Students often write $E_{cell} = E_{anode} - E_{cathode}$, giving a *negative* EMF. The correct formula is **$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$**, which is always positive for a spontaneous corrosion cell.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert hours to seconds** in Faraday's law. Use **t (s)**, not t (hr). 1 hour = **3600 s**.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Galvanic Series with the EMF Series.** The EMF series is for pure metals in standard solutions; the galvanic series is for commercial alloys in real environments. Mark downgrades of **1–2 marks** are common.
>
> 4. **Incomplete reactions:** Always write the anodic, cathodic, and **overall** reactions. The examiner awards partial marks for each.
>
> 5. **Mis-identifying anode/cathode:** Remember the mnemonic **"AN OX, RED CAT"** — **AN**ode is **OX**idation; **RED**uction at **CAT**hode. The anode always **corrodes**.
>
> 6. **Cathodic protection diagram:** When drawing, always show the **external wire connection** AND the **electrolyte path** (otherwise it's not a complete cell). Neglecting either costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **PBR interpretation:** Students forget that **PBR ≈ 1 to 2 is the protective range**, not PBR > 1. PBR > 2 leads to spalling.
>
> 8. **Units:** Always state units. $E$ in **Volts**, W in **grams**, $R_c$ in **mm/year or mpy**, current density in **A/cm²**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board-Ready Summary — Read this 1 hour before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Concepts

- [ ] Corrosion = **reverse of extractive metallurgy**; metals return to oxide form.
- [ ] **Wet corrosion = electrochemical**; dry corrosion = chemical (high temperature).
- [ ] Four essentials: **Anode + Cathode + Electrolyte + Metallic path**.
- [ ] Mnemonic: **"AN OX, RED CAT"** (Anode = Oxidation; Cathode = Reduction).
- [ ] Galvanic Series ≠ EMF Series (Galvanic is empirical, EMF is thermodynamic).

### ✅ Key Reactions

- [ ] Anodic: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$
- [ ] Cathodic (neutral/alkaline): $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2OH^{-}$
- [ ] Cathodic (acid): $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow$
- [ ] Overall rust: $4Fe + 3O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O$

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas

- [ ] **Nernst:** $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{1}{[M^{n+}]}$ (at 25 °C)
- [ ] **EMF of cell:** $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$
- [ ] **Faraday's 1st Law:** $W = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{nF} = Z \cdot I \cdot t$
- [ ] **Corrosion rate (mpy):** $\frac{534 \cdot W_{mg}}{D \cdot A_{in^2} \cdot t_{hr}}$
- [ ] **PBR:** $\frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}$ → protective when **1 < PBR < 2**

### ✅ Standard Electrode Potentials (Top 5 to remember)

- [ ] Au: **+1.50 V** (most noble)
- [ ] Cu: **+0.34 V**
- [ ] H₂: **0.00 V** (reference)
- [ ] Fe: **−0.44 V**
- [ ] Zn: **−0.76 V**
- [ ] Mg: **−2.37 V** (most active)

### ✅ Corrosion Types — One-line Identification

- [ ] **Uniform:** Even attack → corrosion allowance suffices
- [ ] **Galvanic:** Two dissimilar metals → use similar metals or insulate
- [ ] **Pitting:** Localized, deep → use Mo-containing SS
- [ ] **Crevice:** Inside gaps → design to avoid crevices
- [ ] **Intergranular:** Grain boundary → solution annealing (e.g., 304 SS)
- [ ] **SCC:** Stress + specific corrodent → reduce stress, change environment
- [ ] **Erosion:** Wear + corrosion → reduce velocity, harder materials

### ✅ Cathodic Protection Quick-Pick

- [ ] **Sacrificial Anode** = No power source; uses Zn/Mg/Al; for small structures.
- [ ] **Impressed Current** = External DC; inert anode; for large structures.
- [ ] **Anodic Protection** = Uses passivation; only for alloys that passivate (e.g., SS in H₂SO₄).

### ✅ Pilling-Bedworth Rule

- [ ] **PBR < 1:** Porous, non-protective (Mg, Ca)
- [ ] **1 ≤ PBR ≤ 2:** Protective, adherent (Al, Cr, Ti — **good**)
- [ ] **PBR > 2:** Spalling (Fe, Ni — **poor**)

### ✅ Final Golden Rule for KTU Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always check units:** Current in **A**, time in **seconds**, mass in **grams**, area in **m² or cm²**, F = **96,485 C**. Failure to convert units = 1-mark deduction per KTU valuation pattern.

---
