topics.title: Diffusion in Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diffusion in Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Diffusion in Solids** is a time-dependent **mass transport phenomenon** in which atoms, ions, or molecules migrate through the crystal lattice of a solid by random thermal motion (atomic jumps) under the influence of a chemical potential gradient, concentration gradient, or stress gradient.

In the context of KTU 2024 Material Science syllabus, diffusion is classified as:

- **Self-Diffusion**: Migration of atoms in a pure material (e.g., Cu atoms moving through pure Cu lattice). This cannot be observed directly without radioactive tracer isotopes.
- **Inter-Diffusion (Chemical Diffusion)**: Migration of atoms of one metal into another (e.g., Ni atoms diffusing into Cu). This is observable and industrially significant.
- **Volume Diffusion (Lattice Diffusion)**: Atom movement through the bulk crystal interior.
- **Grain Boundary Diffusion**: Faster atom movement along grain boundaries (short-circuit path).
- **Surface Diffusion**: Movement of atoms along the free surface of a solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Diffusion in Solids is a **2-mark to 14-mark weightage topic**. It is a high-frequency topic in Part A (definitions) and Part B (numerical problems based on Fick's laws).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a crowded **tea-coffee party** where you drop a single drop of red ink into a glass of still water. Initially, the red color stays concentrated. But after a few hours, the entire glass turns a uniform light pink. **What happened?** The ink molecules physically moved from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration through random molecular motion until equilibrium was achieved.

In a solid, atoms are locked in a rigid crystal lattice. Yet, given enough **thermal energy** and **vacancies** (empty lattice sites), atoms can "hop" from one site to another. The driving force is the **chemical potential gradient**, not just the concentration gradient alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Intuition**: Think of diffusion in solids as a "leaky parking lot" — atoms constantly leave their parking spots, and the empty spots (vacancies) act as gaps into which neighboring atoms can hop. The hotter the parking lot (temperature), the more frequent the hopping events.

## 1.3 Fundamental Constants and Metrics

- **Boltzmann Constant (k)** = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ)** = **6.022 × 10²³ /mol**
- Standard unit of **Diffusion Coefficient (D)** = **m²/s** or **cm²/s**
- Standard unit of **Diffusion Flux (J)** = **atoms/(m²·s)** or **kg/(m²·s)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Diffusion in Solids

### 2.1.1 Vacancy Diffusion (Substitutional Diffusion)
- Atoms migrate by **hopping into adjacent vacant lattice sites**.
- The number of vacancies increases exponentially with temperature (Arrhenius behavior).
- **Most common mechanism** in substitutional solid solutions.
- Requires the existence of a vacancy — hence, this mechanism is **temperature dependent**.
- **Why it matters**: This is the dominant mechanism in pure metals and substitutional alloys (e.g., Cu-Ni, Cu-Zn brass).

### 2.1.2 Interstitial Diffusion
- Small atoms (H, C, N, O) fit into the **interstitial spaces** between host atoms.
- These atoms hop from one interstitial site to the next without requiring a vacancy.
- **Much faster** than vacancy diffusion because there are many empty interstitial sites.
- **Why it matters**: This is the mechanism behind **carburization of steel** (C diffusion into Fe), **nitriding**, and **hydrogen embrittlement**.

### 2.1.3 Interstitialcy Mechanism
- A self-interstitial atom **displaces** a lattice atom into another interstitial position.
- The displaced atom then pushes another atom, and so on, in a chain.
- Common in ionic crystals (e.g., halides like NaCl).

### 2.1.4 Direct Exchange Mechanism
- Two adjacent atoms **simultaneously swap positions**.
- Requires high distortion energy; rarely observed in pure metals.
- No vacancy is needed.

### 2.1.5 Ring Mechanism
- A group of **3 or 4 atoms** rotates simultaneously in a ring-like motion.
- Lower distortion than direct exchange, but still energetically unfavorable for most metals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point**: Out of all five mechanisms, **vacancy diffusion** and **interstitial diffusion** are the only ones frequently asked in KTU exams. Memorize their differences carefully.

## 2.2 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Steady-State)

**Fick's First Law** states that the flux of diffusing atoms is proportional to the concentration gradient, with the diffusion coefficient as the constant of proportionality.

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Where:**
- **J** = diffusion flux (atoms/m²·s) — direction of mass flow
- **D** = diffusion coefficient (m²/s) — material property
- **dC/dx** = concentration gradient (atoms/m⁴)
- The **negative sign** indicates flow occurs **from high to low concentration** (downhill).

**Steady-state** means ∂C/∂t = 0. The concentration at any point does not change with time.

## 2.3 Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Non-Steady-State)

When the concentration at a point changes with time, Fick's First Law is combined with the **continuity equation** (mass conservation) to yield:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

This is a **second-order partial differential equation** that must be solved with appropriate boundary and initial conditions.

## 2.4 The Arrhenius Equation for Diffusion

The diffusion coefficient is **strongly temperature-dependent** and follows an Arrhenius-type relationship:

$$D = D_0 \, \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$

**Where:**
- **D₀** = pre-exponential constant (frequency factor) — units of m²/s
- **Q** = activation energy for diffusion (J/mol or eV/atom)
- **R** = universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**: A plot of **ln D vs 1/T** gives a straight line with **slope = -Q/R** and **y-intercept = ln D₀**. This is the standard **Arrhenius plot** asked in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.5 Error Function Solution to Fick's Second Law

For a semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration, the solution is:

$$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

**Where:**
- **Cₛ** = surface concentration (constant)
- **Cₓ** = concentration at depth x and time t
- **C₀** = initial uniform concentration
- **x** = depth below surface
- **t** = diffusion time
- **erf** = Gauss error function (tabulated)

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation Name** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **When to Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fick's First Law | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | J [atoms/m²·s], D [m²/s], C [atoms/m³], x [m] | Steady-state diffusion problems |
| Fick's Second Law | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | t [s] | Non-steady state, time-dependent |
| Arrhenius Equation | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | D₀ [m²/s], Q [J/mol], R [J/mol·K], T [K] | Find D at given temperature |
| Log Form Arrhenius | $\ln D = \ln D_0 - \dfrac{Q}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}$ | — | Two-temperature problems, Arrhenius plot |
| Error Function Solution | $\dfrac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | x [m], t [s] | Carburization, doping depth |
| Diffusion Distance | $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$ | x [m] | Quick estimation of penetration depth |
| Slope of Arrhenius Plot | $\text{slope} = -Q/R$ | Q [J/mol] | Experimental data interpretation |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Carburization of Steel**: Carbon diffuses into low-carbon steel surface to harden it (used in gears, crankshafts).
- **Nitriding**: Nitrogen diffusion into steel surfaces (used in diesel engine crankshafts).
- **Doping of Semiconductors**: Phosphorus or boron atoms diffuse into silicon wafers to create p-n junctions.
- **Sintering of Powder Metallurgy Parts**: Atomic diffusion bonds metal powder particles.
- **Creep Resistance**: Diffusion along grain boundaries causes high-temperature creep; adding alloying elements (like Cr in Ni superalloys) reduces diffusion.
- **Hot Corrosion and Oxidation**: Metal ions diffuse outward through oxide layers.
- **Welding and Brazing**: Interdiffusion across joint interfaces creates a strong metallurgical bond.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law

This is a **high-yield 7-mark derivation** in KTU exams. We must start from Fick's First Law and apply the **law of conservation of mass** within a control volume.

### Step 1: Set Up the Control Volume

Consider a thin slab of solid between positions **x** and **x + dx** with a unit cross-sectional area (A = 1 m²).

**Flux entering the slab at x**: $J_x$ [atoms/m²·s]

**Flux leaving the slab at x + dx**: $J_{x+dx}$ [atoms/m²·s]

### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass

The rate of accumulation of atoms inside the slab equals the **net inflow** of atoms:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \cdot (dx)(1) = J_x - J_{x+dx}$$

The left side represents the change in concentration times the volume (dx × 1 = dx). The right side is the net flux into the control volume.

### Step 3: Expand the Outflow Term Using Taylor Series

We expand $J_{x+dx}$ around x using a first-order Taylor series:

$$J_{x+dx} = J_x + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} dx + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 J}{\partial x^2}(dx)^2 + \dots$$

For an infinitesimally small dx, we truncate higher-order terms:

$$J_{x+dx} \approx J_x + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} dx$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back into the Mass Balance

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \cdot dx = J_x - \left(J_x + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} dx\right)$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \cdot dx = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} dx$$

Dividing both sides by dx:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

### Step 5: Substitute Fick's First Law

From Fick's First Law, $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$.

Differentiating with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

(assuming D is constant with respect to x and t for a binary dilute system).

### Step 6: Final Form of Fick's Second Law

Substituting this into the continuity equation:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\left(-D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}}$$

This is **Fick's Second Law** of diffusion for constant D.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note**: When deriving Fick's Second Law, students often forget to state the assumption that **D is constant** (independent of concentration). Always mention this assumption explicitly to avoid losing 1 mark.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Error Function Solution

We want to solve Fick's Second Law with the following boundary and initial conditions:

**Initial Condition (IC):** $C(x, 0) = C_0$ for all x > 0

**Boundary Conditions (BC):**
- $C(0, t) = C_s$ (constant surface concentration) for t > 0
- $C(\infty, t) = C_0$ (far from surface, concentration is unchanged)

### Step 1: Introduce a New Variable η

Define a similarity variable:

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

Using chain rule, the partial derivatives transform as:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \left(-\frac{x}{4t\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = -\frac{\eta}{2t} \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} = \frac{d}{d\eta}\left(\frac{dC}{dx}\right) \cdot \frac{d\eta}{dx} = \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Fick's Second Law

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t} \frac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

Simplifying:

$$-\frac{\eta}{2} \frac{dC}{d\eta} = \frac{1}{4} \frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2}$$

Multiplying both sides by 4:

$$\frac{d^2 C}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve the ODE

Let $p = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}$. Then:

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta p = 0$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Separating variables:

$$\frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta \, d\eta$$

Integrating:

$$\ln p = -\eta^2 + A_1$$

$$p = A \exp(-\eta^2)$$

where $A = e^{A_1}$.

### Step 4: Integrate Once More

$$\frac{dC}{d\eta} = A \exp(-\eta^2)$$

$$C(\eta) = A \int_0^\eta \exp(-u^2) \, du + B$$

### Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions

**At η = 0** (i.e., x = 0): $C(0) = C_s$, so $B = C_s$.

**At η = ∞**: $C(\infty) = C_0$, so:

$$C_0 = A \int_0^\infty \exp(-u^2) \, du + C_s = A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + C_s$$

Solving for A:

$$A = -\frac{2(C_s - C_0)}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

### Step 6: Final Error Function Solution

$$C(\eta) - C_s = -\frac{2(C_s - C_0)}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^\eta \exp(-u^2) du$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^\eta \exp(-u^2) du$$

By definition, the **Gauss error function** is:

$$\mathrm{erf}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^z \exp(-u^2) du$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Two-Temperature Arrhenius Problem

In KTU problems, students are often given D at two temperatures and asked to find D at a third temperature or to find Q.

**Starting equation:**

$$D_1 = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT_1}\right)$$

$$D_2 = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT_2}\right)$$

**Dividing:**

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = \exp\left[\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

**Taking natural log:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Solving for Q:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{R \ln(D_1/D_2)}{\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}}$$

This is the **most asked numerical** in KTU 2024 Module 2.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Diffusion Mechanisms

| **Mechanism** | **Vacancy Required?** | **Activation Energy** | **Speed** | **Example System** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vacancy | Yes | High (≈ lattice self-diffusion) | Slow | Cu in Cu, Ni in Cu |
| Interstitial | No | Low | Very Fast | C in Fe, H in Pd, N in Ti |
| Interstitialcy | No | Moderate | Moderate | AgBr, ionic crystals |
| Direct Exchange | No | Very High | Very Slow | Theoretically, rare |
| Ring | No | High | Slow | Theoretical for FCC metals |
| Grain Boundary | Yes (loosely) | Lower than bulk | Fast | All polycrystalline metals |
| Surface | Yes (loosely) | Lowest | Fastest | Thin films, catalysts |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diffusion Mechanism Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Solid State Diffusion in Materials"] --> B{"Type of Diffusing Atom?"}
    B -- "Substitutional Atoms" --> C["Vacancy Diffusion Mechanism"]
    B -- "Small Interstitial Atoms" --> D["Interstitial Diffusion Mechanism"]
    B -- "Ionic Crystals" --> E["Interstitialcy Mechanism"]

    C --> C1["Atom hops into adjacent vacant lattice site"]
    C1 --> C2["Requires thermal vacancies"]
    C2 --> C3["Activation energy: Q_v high"]
    C3 --> C4["Example: Cu in Ni, Ni in Cu"]

    D --> D1["Atom moves through interstitial voids"]
    D1 --> D2["No vacancy required"]
    D2 --> D3["Activation energy: Q_i low"]
    D3 --> D4["Example: C in gamma-Fe, H in Pd"]

    E --> E1["Displaces lattice atom into interstitial site"]
    E1 --> E2["Chain reaction possible"]
    E2 --> E3["Example: Ag in AgBr"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Diffusion Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["INPUT: Concentration Gradient dC/dx"] --> B1["DIFFUSION MEDIUM: Crystal Lattice"]
    B1 --> B2["Diffusion Coefficient D"]
    B2 --> C1["Ficks First Law: J = -D dC/dx"]
    C1 --> D1["MASS FLUX J atoms per m2 per s"]
    D1 --> E1["OUTPUT: Time-Dependent Profile"]
    E1 --> F1["Ficks Second Law Solution"]
    F1 --> G1["Error Function Profile"]
    G1 --> H1["FINAL: Depth vs Concentration Curve"]
    H1 --> H2["Used for Carburization Depth Design"]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Carburization of Steel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Low Carbon Steel Surface C0 equals 0.2 percent"] --> P2["Heat to 950 degrees Celsius in CO or CH4 atmosphere"]
    P2 --> P3["Surface Carbon Concentration Cs equals 1.0 percent"]
    P3 --> P4["Carbon Atoms Diffuse Along Interstitial Voids"]
    P4 --> P5["Error Function Concentration Profile Develops"]
    P5 --> P6["Case Hardened Layer Forms"]
    P6 --> P7["Quench to Form Martensite"]
    P7 --> P8["Final Hard Wear Resistant Surface"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Chart of Steady-State vs Non-Steady-State Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Diffusion Problem Type"] --> M2{"Is dC by dt equals zero?"}
    M2 -- Yes --> S1["Steady State Diffusion"]
    S1 --> S2["Ficks First Law Applies"]
    S2 --> S3["Concentration Profile Linear in x"]
    S3 --> S4["Example: H2 gas through Pd membrane"]

    M2 -- No --> N1["Non Steady State Diffusion"]
    N1 --> N2["Ficks Second Law Applies"]
    N2 --> N3["Error Function Solution"]
    N3 --> N4["Example: Carburization of steel"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Fick's First Law of Diffusion. State the units of the diffusion coefficient.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the mass flux of diffusing atoms per unit cross-sectional area per unit time is directly proportional to the concentration gradient along the direction of diffusion.

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Where **J** is the diffusion flux [atoms/m²·s], **D** is the diffusion coefficient [m²/s], and **dC/dx** is the concentration gradient [atoms/m⁴].

**The negative sign** indicates that atomic flow occurs from regions of higher concentration to regions of lower concentration (i.e., down the concentration gradient).

**Units of D**: m²/s (in SI) or cm²/s (in CGS).

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 1 mark: Correct equation
- 1 mark: Explanation of variables and negative sign
- 1 mark: Units of D

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between vacancy diffusion and interstitial diffusion mechanisms.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **S. No.** | **Vacancy Diffusion** | **Interstitial Diffusion** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Atom migrates by hopping into a neighboring vacant lattice site | Small atom moves through interstitial voids in the lattice |
| 2 | Requires the presence of a vacancy | Does not require a vacancy |
| 3 | Activation energy is high | Activation energy is low |
| 4 | Slower process | Faster process |
| 5 | Common in substitutional solid solutions (e.g., Cu-Zn) | Common for small atoms like H, C, N, O (e.g., C in Fe) |

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 1 mark: Definition of vacancy diffusion
- 1 mark: Definition of interstitial diffusion
- 1 mark: At least two distinguishing features

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion from Fick's First Law. State the assumptions clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Assumption**: The diffusion coefficient D is constant (independent of concentration and position). The system is one-dimensional. There are no chemical reactions or sources/sinks.

**Step 1**: Consider an elemental volume of thickness **dx** and unit cross-sectional area.

**Step 2**: Net rate of atomic accumulation in the volume = Flux in − Flux out.

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} dx = J_x - J_{x+dx}$$

**Step 3**: Expanding $J_{x+dx}$ in Taylor series (up to first order):

$$J_{x+dx} = J_x + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} dx$$

**Step 4**: Substituting back:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} dx = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} dx$$

**Step 5**: From Fick's First Law: $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$

**Step 6**: Differentiating with respect to x (since D is constant):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 7**: Final result:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 1 mark: Stating assumption
- 2 marks: Setting up the continuity equation
- 2 marks: Taylor series expansion and substitution
- 1 mark: Differentiating Fick's First Law
- 1 mark: Final equation with proper boxing

---

**(b) The diffusion coefficient of carbon in BCC iron at 500°C is 4.5 × 10⁻¹² m²/s, and at 700°C it is 2.7 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s. Calculate the activation energy for diffusion. Also, find the diffusion coefficient at 1000°C. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 4.5 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s at $T_1 = 500°C = 773$ K
- $D_2 = 2.7 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s at $T_2 = 700°C = 973$ K
- Find: Q and $D_3$ at $T_3 = 1000°C = 1273$ K

**Step 1: Use the two-temperature Arrhenius equation**

$$\ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2: Calculate the LHS**

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-12}}{2.7 \times 10^{-10}} = 0.01667$$

$$\ln(0.01667) = -4.094$$

**Step 3: Calculate the RHS bracket**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{973} - \frac{1}{773}$$

$$= 0.001028 - 0.001294 = -2.66 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Solve for Q**

$$-4.094 = \frac{Q}{8.314} \times (-2.66 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$Q = \frac{-4.094 \times 8.314}{-2.66 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$Q = \frac{-34.04}{-2.66 \times 10^{-4}} = 127{,}970 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 128 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5: Find D₀**

$$D_1 = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT_1)$$

$$4.5 \times 10^{-12} = D_0 \exp\left(\frac{-127970}{8.314 \times 773}\right)$$

$$4.5 \times 10^{-12} = D_0 \exp(-19.92)$$

$$4.5 \times 10^{-12} = D_0 \times 2.22 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$D_0 = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-12}}{2.22 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.03 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Find D at 1000°C (1273 K)**

$$D_3 = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT_3)$$

$$D_3 = 2.03 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp\left(\frac{-127970}{8.314 \times 1273}\right)$$

$$D_3 = 2.03 \times 10^{-3} \times \exp(-12.10)$$

$$D_3 = 2.03 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.55 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{D_3 \approx 1.13 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 1 mark: Correct equation
- 1 mark: Correct LHS evaluation
- 1 mark: Correct RHS bracket
- 1 mark: Final Q value
- 1 mark: Correct D₀ calculation
- 1 mark: Final D₃ value
- 1 mark: Units and presentation

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the different mechanisms of diffusion in solids with neat sketches. Compare their activation energies. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The five primary mechanisms of diffusion in crystalline solids are:

**1. Vacancy Diffusion:**
- Atoms move into adjacent vacant lattice sites.
- Requires pre-existing vacancies.
- **Activation Energy Q_v** is high because it includes vacancy formation energy + atom migration energy.
- Most common in pure metals and substitutional alloys.

**2. Interstitial Diffusion:**
- Small atoms (H, C, N, O) move through the small voids between host atoms.
- Vacancy is NOT required since many interstitial sites are naturally empty.
- **Activation Energy Q_i** is low (only migration energy needed).
- Examples: Carbon in iron, hydrogen in palladium.

**3. Interstitialcy Mechanism:**
- A self-interstitial atom displaces a host atom into a nearby interstitial site, creating a chain reaction.
- **Activation Energy** is moderate.
- Common in ionic crystals like NaCl, AgCl.

**4. Direct Exchange:**
- Two adjacent atoms simultaneously swap positions.
- **Activation Energy is very high** due to high lattice distortion.
- Rarely observed experimentally.

**5. Ring Mechanism:**
- 3 or 4 atoms rotate cooperatively in a ring.
- Lower distortion than direct exchange but still rare.
- Activation energy is moderately high.

**Comparison of Activation Energies (Lowest to Highest):**
$$Q_{interstitial} < Q_{grain\ boundary} < Q_{vacancy} < Q_{ring} < Q_{direct\ exchange}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 2 marks: Explaining vacancy and interstitial mechanisms
- 2 marks: Explaining other 3 mechanisms
- 2 marks: Comparison of activation energies
- 1 mark: Sketches / diagrams (mermaid)

---

**(b) A steel gear is to be case-hardened by carburization. The initial carbon concentration is 0.20 wt%. The carbon concentration at the surface is maintained at 1.00 wt%. The treatment is done at 927°C, where the diffusion coefficient of C in γ-iron is 1.28 × 10⁻¹¹ m²/s. Determine the time required to achieve a carbon concentration of 0.50 wt% at a depth of 0.7 mm below the surface. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 0.20$ wt% (initial)
- $C_s = 1.00$ wt% (surface, constant)
- $C_x = 0.50$ wt% (target at depth)
- $x = 0.7$ mm $= 7 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $D = 1.28 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s (at 927°C)
- Find: t

**Step 1: Write the error function solution**

$$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$\frac{1.00 - 0.50}{1.00 - 0.20} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

$$\frac{0.50}{0.80} = \mathrm{erf}\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

$$0.625 = \mathrm{erf}(z) \quad \text{where } z = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

**Step 3: Look up the inverse error function**

From standard error function tables:

$$\mathrm{erf}(0.626) \approx 0.625$$

So, $z \approx 0.626$

**Step 4: Solve for t**

$$z = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

$$0.626 = \frac{7 \times 10^{-4}}{2\sqrt{1.28 \times 10^{-11} \times t}}$$

$$2 \times 0.626 \times \sqrt{1.28 \times 10^{-11} \times t} = 7 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$1.252 \times \sqrt{1.28 \times 10^{-11} \times t} = 7 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5: Square both sides**

$$1.252^2 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-11} \times t = (7 \times 10^{-4})^2$$

$$1.568 \times 1.28 \times 10^{-11} \times t = 4.9 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$2.007 \times 10^{-11} \times t = 4.9 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$t = \frac{4.9 \times 10^{-7}}{2.007 \times 10^{-11}} = 24{,}415 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 6: Convert to hours**

$$t = \frac{24{,}415}{3600} \approx 6.78 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 6.78 \text{ hours}}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 1 mark: Correct formula identification
- 1 mark: Correct substitution
- 1 mark: Correct LHS evaluation = 0.625
- 1 mark: Correct inverse error function value
- 1 mark: Algebraic manipulation
- 1 mark: Squaring step
- 1 mark: Final answer with correct units

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls - Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion is Critical**: Temperature MUST be in Kelvin (add 273 to °C). Distance MUST be in meters. Marks deducted for mixing units.
> 2. **Error Function Table Required**: Students often forget to state the error function value. Always carry the table value in your solution steps.
> 3. **Sign Convention**: The negative sign in Fick's First Law is mandatory. Marks lost if omitted.
> 4. **Assumption of Constant D**: When deriving Fick's Second Law, always state "D is constant" assumption explicitly.
> 5. **LHS Calculation in Arrhenius**: Common mistake is using $D_2/D_1$ instead of $D_1/D_2$. The formula sign matters.
> 6. **Order of Magnitude Check**: Verify your final D value lies between $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-12}$ m²/s for solids at high temperature. If not, recheck.
> 7. **No Direct Quote from Textbook**: KTU 2024 expects self-explanatory answers with neat sketches, not verbatim text reproduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ **Diffusion**: Mass transport by atomic motion in solids driven by chemical potential gradient.
- ☐ **Steady-State**: Concentration at a point does not change with time (∂C/∂t = 0).
- ☐ **Non-Steady-State**: Concentration changes with both position and time.

**Fick's Laws:**
- ☐ **First Law**: $J = -D(dC/dx)$ — used for steady-state problems.
- ☐ **Second Law**: $\partial C/\partial t = D(\partial^2 C/\partial x^2)$ — used for time-dependent problems.
- ☐ **Assumption**: D is constant (does not depend on x, t, or C).

**Mechanisms of Diffusion:**
- ☐ Vacancy diffusion (substitutional atoms, high Q)
- ☐ Interstitial diffusion (small atoms like C, H, N, O, low Q, fastest)
- ☐ Interstitialcy, direct exchange, ring (less common)

**Arrhenius Equation:**
- ☐ $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$
- ☐ $\ln D$ vs $1/T$ plot is a straight line (slope = $-Q/R$)
- ☐ Two-temperature formula: $Q = R \ln(D_1/D_2) / (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$

**Error Function Solution:**
- ☐ $(C_s - C_x)/(C_s - C_0) = \mathrm{erf}(x / 2\sqrt{Dt})$
- ☐ Applicable to semi-infinite solid with constant $C_s$.
- ☐ Always use SI units (m, s, K).

**Numerical Mnemonics:**
- ☐ Diffusion coefficient for solids at high T ≈ $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-12}$ m²/s.
- ☐ Approximate diffusion distance: $x \approx \sqrt{Dt}$
- ☐ Atoms diffuse faster at higher temperatures (exponentially).

**Industrial Applications (must know for 3-mark questions):**
- ☐ Carburization of steel (C in Fe — interstitial)
- ☐ Nitriding (N in steel — interstitial)
- ☐ Semiconductor doping (P, B in Si — interstitial)
- ☐ Sintering, creep, hot corrosion (vacancy and grain boundary)

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ☐ Mixing up units of D (m²/s) and J (atoms/m²·s).
- ☐ Forgetting to convert °C to K in Arrhenius.
- ☐ Using $D_2/D_1$ instead of $D_1/D_2$.
- ☐ Omitting the negative sign in Fick's First Law.
- ☐ Confusing $C_s$, $C_0$, and $C_x$ in the error function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip**: For maximum marks in diffusion problems, **always show all units clearly, draw a labeled diagram of concentration vs depth, and box the final answer**. KTU examiners are specifically trained to award step marks, so even if your final value is wrong, intermediate steps will fetch you 4-5 out of 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fick's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fick's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fick's Laws of Diffusion — Formal Definitions

**Fick's Laws** are the fundamental governing equations of **atomic diffusion** in solids, formulated by the German physiologist **Adolf Fick** in **1855**. They are the mass-transport analog of **Fourier's Law of heat conduction** and **Ohm's Law of electrical conduction**, and they form the mathematical backbone of all mass-transfer operations in materials engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> Diffusion in solids — Fick's First and Second Laws, steady-state and non-steady-state solutions, and engineering applications such as **carburization**, **decarburization**, and **semiconductor doping**.

### 1.1.1 Fick's First Law (Steady-State Diffusion)

**Fick's First Law** states that the **diffusion flux** $J$ of atoms across a plane is proportional to the **concentration gradient** normal to that plane:

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

where:
- $J$ = diffusion flux $\left[\text{atoms} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}\right]$
- $D$ = **diffusion coefficient** or **diffusivity** $\left[\text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}\right]$
- $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ = concentration gradient in the $x$-direction $\left[\text{atoms} \cdot \text{m}^{-4}\right]$
- The **negative sign** indicates that atomic flow proceeds from **high to low concentration** (i.e., down the gradient).

### 1.1.2 Fick's Second Law (Non-Steady-State Diffusion)

When the concentration within the diffusion zone **changes with time** (i.e., the system is **non-steady**), Fick's First Law is recast as a partial differential equation:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Dimensional Form:** For isotropic media, $D$ is treated as a **scalar** (constant or position-dependent). For anisotropic media (e.g., hexagonal crystals), $D$ becomes a **tensor** $\mathbf{D}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ink in Water" Intuition

Imagine a single drop of **red ink** released into a still glass of clear water. The molecules do not "know" where the high-concentration region is, but every molecule undergoes **random thermal motion** (Brownian motion). Statistically, more molecules wander from the dense (red) region to the dilute (clear) region than the reverse. The **net visible flow** of color spreads outward until the water turns uniformly pink — a state of **equilibrium**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Mapping to Fick's Laws:**
> - **First Law** → Predicts the *instantaneous rate* of spreading when the colour profile is already established (steady glow rate).
> - **Second Law** → Predicts *how the colour profile itself evolves* with time (the spreading cloud).
> - The **diffusivity $D$** plays the role of *thermal diffusivity $\alpha$* in heat conduction, or *hydraulic conductivity* in groundwater flow.

---

## 1.3 The Diffusion Coefficient — Arrhenius Behaviour

The temperature dependence of $D$ follows the **Arrhenius equation**, a vital concept for KTU problems:

$$D = D_{0} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_{d}}{RT}\right)$$

where:
- $D_{0}$ = **pre-exponential (frequency) factor** $\left[\text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}\right]$
- $Q_{d}$ = **activation energy for diffusion** $\left[\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}\right]$
- $R$ = **universal gas constant** = $8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- $T$ = **absolute temperature** $\left[\text{K}\right]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Constants to Memorise:**
> - $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$
> - $1 \, \text{eV/atom} = 96.485 \, \text{kJ/mol}$
> - Typical $D$ values at **room temperature**: $10^{-20}$ to $10^{-16} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ (very slow in solids)
> - Typical $D$ values **near melting**: $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-10} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fick's First Law — Underlying Logic

The first law is a **constitutive equation** derived from the postulate that the driving force for diffusion is the **chemical potential gradient**. For an ideal solution:

1. **Step 1 — Net Atomic Drift:** Atoms in a concentration gradient execute random walks. On average, more atoms cross a reference plane from the high-$C$ side than from the low-$C$ side.
2. **Step 2 — Define Flux:** The flux $J$ is the *net number* of atoms crossing a unit area per unit time.
3. **Step 3 — Linearisation (Fickian Postulate):** For small gradients, $J$ is linearly proportional to $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ with proportionality constant $D$.
4. **Step 4 — Sign Convention:** Diffusion proceeds *down* the gradient, hence the **minus sign**.

### 2.1.1 Boundary Conditions for Fick's First Law

For a **steady-state** problem across a slab of thickness $L$ with surface concentrations $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$:

$$J = -D \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta x} = -D \frac{C_{2} - C_{1}}{L} = D \frac{C_{1} - C_{2}}{L}$$

The **time-independent** concentration profile is **linear**:

$$C(x) = C_{1} + \left(\frac{C_{2} - C_{1}}{L}\right) x$$

### 2.1.2 Engineering Utility of the First Law

- **Hydrogen permeation through metal membranes** (e.g., Pd-alloy purifiers).
- **Leaching of dopants** from semiconductor wafers.
- **Steady-state carburization depth** in industrial furnaces.
- **Gas-barrier design** in polymer packaging.

---

## 2.2 Fick's Second Law — The Time-Dependent Equation

When the concentration field is **not** at steady state, the first law is combined with **Fick's equation of continuity** (mass conservation):

1. **Step 1 — Apply continuity:** $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J}$
2. **Step 2 — Substitute Fick's First Law:** $\mathbf{J} = -D \, \nabla C$
3. **Step 3 — Assume $D$ is constant:** $\nabla \cdot (D \nabla C) = D \nabla^{2} C$
4. **Step 4 — Combine:** $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \nabla^{2} C$

For the **1-D Cartesian** case, this collapses to the iconic form:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### 2.2.1 Common Boundary-Condition Families

| **Problem Type** | **Surface BC** | **Initial Condition** | **Physical Process** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Carburization (constant $C_{s}$) | $C(0,t) = C_{s}$ | $C(x,0) = C_{0}$ | Gas carburizing, pack carburizing |
| Decarburization (zero $C_{s}$) | $C(0,t) = 0$ | $C(x,0) = C_{0}$ | Decarburizing anneal |
| Pair diffusion | $C(\pm L, t) = C_{s}$ | $C(x,0) = C_{0}$ | Homogenization of alloys |
| Thin-film source | $\int_{0}^{\infty} C \, dx = Q$ | $C(x,0) = 0$ | Dopant implantation anneal |

### 2.2.2 Error-Function Solution (Semi-Infinite Solid, Constant Surface Concentration)

The most-examined KTU solution to Fick's Second Law assumes a **semi-infinite solid** ($x \geq 0$), initially at uniform concentration $C_{0}$, with the surface suddenly held at $C_{s}$ for $t > 0$. The solution is:

$$\frac{C(x,t) - C_{0}}{C_{s} - C_{0}} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

The **error function** is defined as:

$$\operatorname{erf}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_{0}^{z} e^{-u^{2}} \, du$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** The form $(C - C_0)/(C_s - C_0) = \operatorname{erfc}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})$ is *the* formula you must know for **carburization** numericals.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | **Equation / Statement** | **Symbol / Form** | **Typical Use** |
|:--:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law (1-D) | $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Steady-state flux calculation |
| 2 | Steady-state concentration profile | $C(x) = C_{1} + \dfrac{(C_{2} - C_{1})}{L} \, x$ | Linear profile in a slab |
| 3 | Fick's Second Law (1-D, const. $D$) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Time-dependent problems |
| 4 | Arrhenius diffusivity | $D = D_{0} \exp(-Q_{d}/RT)$ | Temperature dependence |
| 5 | Error function solution | $\dfrac{C(x,t) - C_{0}}{C_{s} - C_{0}} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Carburization, surface saturation |
| 6 | Complementary error function | $\operatorname{erfc}(z) = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(z)$ | Surface problems |
| 7 | Gaussian thin-film source | $C(x,t) = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^{2}}{4Dt}\right)$ | Dopant diffusion from thin layer |
| 8 | Carburization depth at $C = C_{0}$ | $x \approx 4\sqrt{Dt}$ | Rule-of-thumb penetration depth |
| 9 | Permeability (Hagen analogue) | $P = D \, S$ | Steady-state permeation through membranes |
| 10 | Einstein diffusion relation | $D = \dfrac{k_{B} T}{\gamma}$ | Random-walk link to mobility $\gamma$ |

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Applications of Fick's Laws

| **Domain** | **Application** |
|:---|:---|
| **Steel Heat Treatment** | Carburization, nitriding, decarburization, homogenization of castings |
| **Semiconductor Industry** | Boron, phosphorus, arsenic diffusion doping of Si wafers |
| **Nuclear Engineering** | Hydrogen/tritium permeation through reactor walls |
| **Polymer Processing** | Solvent diffusion, plasticizer migration, barrier design |
| **Corrosion Science** | Oxygen/nitrogen inward diffusion forming oxide/nitride scales |
| **Batteries / Fuel Cells** | Li-ion diffusion in electrodes; H$^+$ diffusion in Nafion membranes |
| **Additive Manufacturing** | Homogenization of SLM-printed multi-alloy components |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Short-Cut Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition, every boundary substitution, and every limit is shown explicitly. **No step is skipped.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's First Law from Random-Walk Statistics

Consider a plane at $x = 0$ separating two regions of concentrations $C_{1}$ (at $x = -\lambda$) and $C_{2}$ (at $x = +\lambda$), where $\lambda$ is the **mean free path** of a diffusing atom. Each atom performs a **random walk** with step length $\lambda$ and time $\tau$ between jumps.

**Step 1 — Atoms crossing left to right per unit area per unit time:**

$$\Phi_{L \to R} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, n_{1} \, v_{\text{avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_{1} \, \frac{\lambda}{\tau}$$

> *Half* of the atoms in the left plane jump right; the concentration is $C_{1}$ and average jump velocity is $\lambda/\tau$.

**Step 2 — Atoms crossing right to left per unit area per unit time:**

$$\Phi_{R \to L} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, n_{2} \, v_{\text{avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, C_{2} \, \frac{\lambda}{\tau}$$

**Step 3 — Net flux (left → right, positive $x$):**

$$J = \Phi_{L \to R} - \Phi_{R \to L} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\tau} \, (C_{1} - C_{2})$$

**Step 4 — Express $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ in terms of the gradient:**

Since $C_{1} = C(0) - \lambda \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ and $C_{2} = C(0) + \lambda \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$:

$$C_{1} - C_{2} = -2\lambda \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$J = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\tau} \left(-2\lambda \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = -\frac{\lambda^{2}}{\tau} \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6 — Define the diffusion coefficient:**

$$D \;\equiv\; \frac{\lambda^{2}}{\tau}$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\,}$$

> This confirms Fick's First Law from atomic-scale statistics. The constant $D$ depends on $\lambda$ and $\tau$ — both thermally activated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law

Consider a thin slab of thickness $dx$ between $x$ and $x + dx$ with cross-sectional area $A$.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation (continuity):** The rate of accumulation of solute inside the slab equals the difference between inflow and outflow:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \, A \, dx = A \, J_{\text{in}} - A \, J_{\text{out}} = A \left[ J(x) - J(x+dx) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand $J(x+dx)$ using a Taylor series:**

$$J(x + dx) = J(x) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^{2} J}{\partial x^{2}} (dx)^{2} + \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Neglect higher-order terms (infinitesimal slab):**

$$J(x) - J(x + dx) \;\approx\; -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the conservation equation:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \, dx = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Fick's First Law, $J = -D \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Assume $D$ is constant in space (and time):**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Solution of Fick's Second Law for the Semi-Infinite Solid

**Problem:** A semi-infinite solid ($x \geq 0$) is initially at concentration $C_{0}$. At $t = 0$, the surface $x = 0$ is suddenly brought to a constant concentration $C_{s}$ and held there. Find $C(x, t)$.

**Governing PDE:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

**Boundary conditions:**

$$C(0, t) = C_{s} \quad \text{(constant surface concentration)}$$

$$C(\infty, t) = C_{0} \quad \text{(far field unchanged)}$$

$$C(x, 0) = C_{0} \quad \text{(uniform initial concentration)}$$

### 3.3.1 Method — Boltzmann Substitution (Similarity Variable)

**Step 1 — Introduce the similarity variable $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2 \sqrt{Dt}}$$

The PDE is reduced to an **ordinary differential equation** (ODE) because the solution depends on $x$ and $t$ only through the combination $\eta$.

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \left(-\frac{x}{4\sqrt{D} \, t^{3/2}}\right) = -\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{dC}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta}\right) = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \cdot \frac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the PDE:**

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify (multiply both sides by $4t$):**

$$-2\eta \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = \frac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite as a standard ODE:**

$$\frac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta \cdot \frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 6 — First integration:** Let $p = \dfrac{dC}{d\eta}$. Then:

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta \, p = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta \, d\eta$$

Integrate:

$$\ln p = -\eta^{2} + \ln A_{1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = A_{1} \, e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Second integration:**

$$\frac{dC}{d\eta} = A_{1} \, e^{-\eta^{2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C(\eta) = A_{1} \int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}} \, du + A_{2}$$

**Step 8 — Apply the boundary conditions in $\eta$-form:**

At $x \to \infty$: $\eta \to \infty$, so $C \to C_{0}$:

$$C_{0} = A_{1} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^{2}} \, du + A_{2} = A_{1} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + A_{2}$$

At $x = 0$: $\eta = 0$, so $C = C_{s}$:

$$C_{s} = A_{1} \cdot 0 + A_{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A_{2} = C_{s}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $A_{1}$:**

$$A_{1} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} (C_{0} - C_{s}) = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} (C_{s} - C_{0})$$

**Step 10 — Assemble the final solution:**

$$C(\eta) = C_{s} - \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}(C_{s} - C_{0}) \int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}} \, du$$

Using the definition of the **error function** $\operatorname{erf}(\eta) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}} du$:

$$C(\eta) = C_{s} - (C_{s} - C_{0}) \operatorname{erf}(\eta)$$

**Step 11 — Re-substitute $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$:**

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C(x,t) - C_{0}}{C_{s} - C_{0}} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = \operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\,}$$

### 3.3.2 Numerical Verification (KTU-Style Sanity Check)

| $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$ | $\operatorname{erf}(\eta)$ | $\operatorname{erfc}(\eta)$ | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0.00 | 0.0000 | 1.0000 | Surface concentration $C_{s}$ |
| 0.20 | 0.2227 | 0.7773 | Near-surface region |
| 0.50 | 0.5205 | 0.4795 | Mid-diffusion zone |
| 0.80 | 0.7421 | 0.2579 | Deeper region |
| 1.00 | 0.8427 | 0.1573 | Penetration front ($\approx C_{0}$) |
| 1.50 | 0.9661 | 0.0339 | Effectively at $C_{0}$ |
| 2.00 | 0.9953 | 0.0047 | Fully back to $C_{0}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **The penetration depth is conventionally defined at $x$ where $C - C_{0} \approx 0.01 (C_{s} - C_{0})$, corresponding to $\eta \approx 2$, giving $x \approx 4\sqrt{Dt}$.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Fick's Laws with Analogous Transport Laws

| **Aspect** | **Fick's Law (Mass)** | **Fourier's Law (Heat)** | **Ohm's Law (Charge)** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Driving potential | Concentration gradient $\nabla C$ | Temperature gradient $\nabla T$ | Voltage gradient $-\nabla V$ |
| Flux | Mass flux $J_{m} \;[\text{kg/m}^{2}\text{s}]$ | Heat flux $q \;[\text{W/m}^{2}]$ | Current density $J \;[\text{A/m}^{2}]$ |
| Transport coefficient | Diffusivity $D$ | Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ | Electrical conductivity $\sigma$ |
| Constitutive equation | $J = -D \nabla C$ | $q = -k \nabla T$ | $J = -\sigma \nabla V$ |
| Time-dependent form | $\partial C/\partial t = D \nabla^{2} C$ | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \nabla^{2} T$ | $\partial \rho/\partial t = \text{(Laplace)}$ |
| Field analogy | $C \leftrightarrow T$ | — | $V \leftrightarrow T$ |
| Boundary condition style | Dirichlet on $C$ | Dirichlet on $T$ | Dirichlet on $V$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Fickian Diffusion Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Initial Condition C x 0] --> n2[Apply Ficks First Law]
    n3[Boundary Condition C 0 t] --> n2
    n4[Diffusivity D Arrhenius] --> n2
    n2 --> n5{Steady State Check}
    n5 -- Yes --> n6[Solve Linear Profile Cx]
    n5 -- No --> n7[Formulate PDE Ficks Second Law]
    n7 --> n8[Choose Solution Method]
    n8 --> n9[Error Function Solution]
    n8 --> n10[Gaussian Thin Film Solution]
    n8 --> n11[Finite Difference Numerical]
    n9 --> n12[Compute Concentration Profile]
    n10 --> n12
    n11 --> n12
    n6 --> n12
    n12 --> n13[Engineering Application]
    n13 --> nA[Carburization of Steel]
    n13 --> nB[Semiconductor Doping]
    n13 --> nC[Hydrogen Permeation]
    n13 --> nD[Polymer Barrier Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Concentration Profile Evolution (Schematic Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA[Surface at C sub s] -->|Holds Constant| nB[Diffusion Zone]
    nB -->|Depth x grows as sqrt Dt| nC[Bulk at C sub 0]
    nC -->|Far Field Undisturbed| nA
    nD[Time t sub 1] -->|Shorter Penetration| nB
    nE[Time t sub 2 Greater] -->|Deeper Penetration| nB
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Tree for Choosing the Correct Fick Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nQ1{Is the Concentration Profile Time Independent?}
    nQ1 -- Yes --> nA1[Use Ficks First Law]
    nA1 --> nA2[Linear Profile Cx C1 plus gradient times x]
    nQ1 -- No --> nQ2{Is the System Semi Infinite?}
    nQ2 -- Yes --> nB1[Use Error Function Solution]
    nB1 --> nB2[Equation C minus C0 over Cs minus C0 equals erfc of x over 2 sqrt Dt]
    nQ2 -- No --> nQ3{Boundary Condition Type?}
    nQ3 -- Constant Surface Cs --> nC1[Error Function or Series Solution]
    nQ3 -- Zero Surface --> nC2[Decarburization Series]
    nQ3 -- Thin Film Initial --> nC3[Gaussian Solution]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nMF[Mass Diffusion Ficks] --> n1[D Coefficient]
    nHT[Heat Conduction Fourier] --> n2[Thermal Diffusivity alpha]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State Fick's First Law of diffusion. Define the terms involved and mention the condition under which it is applicable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Fick's First Law states that the diffusion flux $J$ across a plane is directly proportional to the concentration gradient normal to that plane.
>
> $$J = -D \, \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$
>
> - **$J$** — diffusion flux (atoms per unit area per unit time)
> - **$D$** — diffusion coefficient / diffusivity (m²/s)
> - **$\partial C / \partial x$** — concentration gradient
>
> **Applicable condition:** The system must be in a **steady state** (i.e., $\partial C / \partial t = 0$) and $D$ is assumed constant. *(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for terms, 1 mark for condition)*

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the diffusion coefficient. Show that it has the units of m²/s.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The diffusion coefficient $D$ is the proportionality constant in Fick's First Law that quantifies the rate at which atoms diffuse through a medium per unit concentration gradient. From the equation $J = -D (\partial C/\partial x)$:
>
> $$\dim(D) = \frac{\dim(J)}{\dim(\partial C/\partial x)} = \frac{\text{atoms} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}}{\text{atoms} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{m}^{-1}} = \text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$$
>
> Hence, $D$ has the units of **m²/s**. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for unit derivation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must attempt *one* of the two alternatives.

---

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of diffusion starting from Fick's First Law. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel gear is to be carburized in a furnace at $927^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($1200 \text{ K}$). The surface concentration is held at $1.2 \, \text{wt\% C}$ and the initial uniform carbon content is $0.2 \, \text{wt\% C}$. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in $\gamma$-iron at this temperature is $D = 1.28 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. Calculate the time required to achieve a carbon concentration of $0.8 \, \text{wt\% C}$ at a depth of $0.5 \, \text{mm}$ from the surface. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Fick's Second Law (7 marks):**

1. **State the conservation of mass** for a thin slab of thickness $dx$ and area $A$: *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} \, A \, dx = A \left[ J(x) - J(x + dx) \right]$$

2. **Taylor-expand $J(x+dx)$** and neglect higher-order terms: *(1 mark)*

   $$J(x + dx) \approx J(x) + \frac{\partial J}{\partial x} \, dx$$

3. **Obtain the continuity equation:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J}{\partial x}$$

4. **Substitute Fick's First Law** $J = -D \, (\partial C/\partial x)$: *(2 marks)*

   $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right)$$

5. **Assume $D$ is constant** in space and time, giving: *(2 marks)*

   $$\boxed{\,\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \, \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}\,}$$

   **Assumptions:** (i) One-dimensional diffusion, (ii) constant $D$, (iii) no chemical reaction, (iv) no bulk motion, (v) isotropic medium.

**Part (b) — Carburization Time Calculation (7 marks):**

1. **Given data:** $C_{s} = 1.2 \, \text{wt\%}$, $C_{0} = 0.2 \, \text{wt\%}$, $C(x,t) = 0.8 \, \text{wt\%}$, $x = 0.5 \, \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$, $D = 1.28 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. *(1 mark)*

2. **Apply the error-function solution for a semi-infinite solid:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{C(x,t) - C_{0}}{C_{s} - C_{0}} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

3. **Substitute the values:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{0.8 - 0.2}{1.2 - 0.2} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

   $$\frac{0.6}{1.0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

   $$0.6 = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(\eta) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \operatorname{erf}(\eta) = 0.4$$

4. **From standard tables, $\eta = \operatorname{erf}^{-1}(0.4) \approx 0.3708$:** *(1 mark)*

5. **Solve for $t$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \sqrt{Dt} = \frac{x}{2\eta}$$

   $$Dt = \frac{x^{2}}{4\eta^{2}} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^{2}}{4 \times (0.3708)^{2}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{0.5500} = 4.545 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

6. **Compute the time:** *(1 mark)*

   $$t = \frac{4.545 \times 10^{-7}}{1.28 \times 10^{-11}} = 3.55 \times 10^{4} \, \text{s} \approx 9.86 \, \text{hours}$$

7. **Final answer with units:** $t \approx 9.86$ hours (≈ 9 h 52 min). *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the error-function solution of Fick's Second Law for a semi-infinite solid with a constant surface concentration. State clearly the initial and boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The diffusion coefficient of nitrogen in iron at $700^{\circ}\text{C}$ is $D = 4.0 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. At what depth will the nitrogen concentration be **half** of the surface concentration after 5 hours of nitriding, assuming the initial nitrogen content is negligible? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B:

**Part (a) — Derivation of the Error-Function Solution (7 marks):**

1. **State the governing PDE and the boundary/initial conditions:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

   - $C(x, 0) = C_{0}$ (uniform initial)
   - $C(0, t) = C_{s}$ (constant surface)
   - $C(\infty, t) = C_{0}$ (far field)

2. **Introduce the Boltzmann similarity variable $\eta = \dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$** and reduce the PDE to an ODE: *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{d^{2}C}{d\eta^{2}} + 2\eta \frac{dC}{d\eta} = 0$$

3. **First integration with $p = dC/d\eta$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta p = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = A_{1} e^{-\eta^{2}}$$

4. **Second integration:** *(1 mark)*

   $$C(\eta) = A_{1} \int_{0}^{\eta} e^{-u^{2}} du + A_{2}$$

5. **Apply boundary conditions** $C(0) = C_{s}$ and $C(\infty) = C_{0}$: *(1 mark)*

   - At $\eta = 0$: $A_{2} = C_{s}$.
   - At $\eta \to \infty$: $A_{1} = \dfrac{2(C_{0} - C_{s})}{\sqrt{\pi}}$.

6. **Substitute and recognise the error function:** *(1 mark)*

   $$C(\eta) = C_{s} - (C_{s} - C_{0}) \operatorname{erf}(\eta)$$

7. **Re-substitute $\eta = x/(2\sqrt{Dt})$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\boxed{\,\frac{C(x,t) - C_{0}}{C_{s} - C_{0}} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\,}$$

**Part (b) — Nitriding Depth Calculation (7 marks):**

1. **Given:** $D = 4.0 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$, $t = 5 \, \text{h} = 18000 \, \text{s}$, $C_{0} \approx 0$, $C(x,t) = 0.5 \, C_{s}$. *(1 mark)*

2. **Apply the error-function formula** (with $C_{0} = 0$): *(1 mark)*

   $$\frac{C}{C_{s}} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(\eta) = 0.5$$

   $$\operatorname{erf}(\eta) = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta = \operatorname{erf}^{-1}(0.5) \approx 0.4769$$

3. **Set up the equation for $x$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x = 2\eta \sqrt{Dt}$$

4. **Compute $Dt$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$Dt = (4.0 \times 10^{-12})(18000) = 7.2 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

5. **Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{7.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.683 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}$$

6. **Compute $x$:** *(1 mark)*

   $$x = 2 \times 0.4769 \times 2.683 \times 10^{-4} = 2.560 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} \approx 0.256 \, \text{mm}$$

7. **Final answer with units:** $x \approx 0.256 \, \text{mm}$ (≈ 256 μm). *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Fick's Law Numericals (Valuator Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (Cost: 1–2 marks):** Depth is given in mm or μm but $D$ is in m²/s. Always convert $x$ to **metres** before substitution. Forgetting to convert gives a wrong answer by factors of $10^{6}$ or $10^{12}$.
>
> 2. **Time Unit Mismatch (Cost: 1 mark):** Furnace times are given in hours, but $D$ uses seconds. Convert $t$ to **seconds** explicitly.
>
> 3. **Wrong BC Selection (Cost: 2–3 marks):** Confusing **carburization** ($C_{s}$ constant) with **decarburization** ($C_{s} = 0$) or **thin-film source** (Gaussian). The formula changes — read the problem statement twice.
>
> 4. **No Statement of Assumptions (Cost: 1–2 marks):** In derivation questions, omitting the "**constant $D$**" or "**semi-infinite solid**" assumption loses easy marks. KTU examiners specifically look for these.
>
> 5. **Error-Function Table Lookup (Cost: 1 mark):** If the problem gives a target concentration leading to $\operatorname{erf}^{-1}$, **show the table value** explicitly. Do not write "from tables" without quoting the value.
>
> 6. **Sign Error in First Law (Cost: 1 mark):** Writing $J = +D(\partial C/\partial x)$ instead of $J = -D(\partial C/\partial x)$ — physical direction is wrong. Always retain the **negative sign**.
>
> 7. **Differentiating $C$ vs $C_{s} - C_{0}$ in Numerator (Cost: 1–2 marks):** The dimensionless ratio must be $\dfrac{C(x,t) - C_{0}}{C_{s} - C_{0}}$, not $\dfrac{C(x,t)}{C_{s}}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fick's First Law** $J = -D \, (\partial C/\partial x)$ applies to **steady-state** diffusion only.
- **Fick's Second Law** $\partial C/\partial t = D \, \partial^{2} C/\partial x^{2}$ governs **time-dependent** (non-steady) diffusion.
- The **negative sign** in Fick's First Law is a *physical* statement: atoms flow *down* the concentration gradient.
- $D$ has units of **m²/s**; the SI unit of flux is **atoms·m⁻²·s⁻¹**.
- The Arrhenius form $D = D_{0} \exp(-Q_{d}/RT)$ governs the **temperature dependence** of $D$.
- The **error-function solution** applies to a **semi-infinite solid** with a **constant surface concentration** $C_{s}$ — memorize it!
- Penetration depth scales as $x \propto \sqrt{Dt}$ — doubling the time increases depth by only **40%**, not 100%.
- The **complementary error function** $\operatorname{erfc}(z) = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(z)$ gives the fraction of concentration change $(C - C_0)/(C_s - C_0)$.
- The **Gaussian solution** applies when the source is a **thin film** of total dose $Q$, not a constant reservoir.
- The **Einstein relation** $D = k_{B}T/\gamma$ links atomic mobility $\gamma$ to diffusivity.
- Numerical conversion: $1 \, \text{eV/atom} = 96.485 \, \text{kJ/mol}$; $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$.
- Always convert $x$ to **metres** and $t$ to **seconds** before substituting into $x^{2}/4Dt$.
- The carburization "rule of thumb" gives penetration depth $x \approx 4\sqrt{Dt}$ (where $C \approx 0.01 C_{s}$).
- Fickian diffusion is *analogous* to Fourier heat conduction and Ohm's law — use the analogy to remember the form.
- Typical $D$ in solids at **room temperature**: $10^{-20}$ to $10^{-16}$ m²/s (sluggish); near **melting**: $10^{-12}$ m²/s.
- **Assumptions** in the standard solutions: (i) constant $D$, (ii) one-dimensional geometry, (iii) no chemical reaction, (iv) no bulk convection.
- For **anisotropic media** (e.g., HCP crystals), $D$ becomes a **tensor** with different values along $a$- and $c$-axes.
- KTU's most-tested numerical setup: **carburization of steel** — a 3-parameter problem with $C_{s}$, $C_{0}$, and target $C(x,t)$.
- Always state the **boundary conditions and initial conditions** explicitly in derivation answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Mechanisms # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are "Mechanisms" in Materials Science? In the context of **PCMET205 – Material Science and Engineering**, the term *"Mechanisms"* refers to the **microscopic physical processes** by which engineering materials (metals, polymers, ceramics, composites) respond to external stimuli such as stress, temperature, chemical environment, or electrical fields. Unlike "mechanisms" in kinematics (linkages, cams, gears), the Materials Science mechanisms describe the **atomic-scale or microstructural pathways** that govern properties like strength, ductility, toughness, and failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):** > A *mechanism* in materials science is a sequence of atomic, crystallographic, and microstructural events that collectively produce an observed macroscopic phenomenon such as plastic deformation, fracture, diffusion, or phase transformation. The five principal mechanism families covered in KTU Module 2 are: 1. **Deformation Mechanisms** – Slip and Twinning 2. **Strengthening Mechanisms** – Solid solution, strain, grain boundary, precipitation 3. **Fracture Mechanisms** – Ductile, brittle, fatigue, creep 4. **Diffusion Mechanisms** – Vacancy and interstitial 5. **Phase Transformation Mechanisms** – Nucleation and growth ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: "The Crowd in a Stadium"** > Imagine a packed cricket stadium where every person represents an atom arranged in perfect rows. When a force is applied (a strong push at the boundary), people do not all move at once. Instead: > - Some **slide in rows** (this is *slip* in metals). > - A small section of rows suddenly **re-orients as a mirror image** (this is *twinning*). > - Where rows meet (aisle dividers), the movement is *blocked* (this is a *grain boundary barrier* – the essence of grain-boundary strengthening). > > The "mechanism" is the *specific orderly way* the crowd rearranges under push. Studying mechanisms means asking: *exactly how do the rows move, where do they get stuck, and what microscopic event controls the overall strength?* ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Critical Resolved Shear Stress (CRSS) | τ_CRSS | 0.5 – 50 | MPa | | Boltzmann constant | k_B | **1.38 × 10⁻²³** | J/K | | Gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | | Avogadro's number | N_A | **6.022 × 10²³** | /mol | | Burgers vector magnitude (Cu) | b | **0.256** | nm | | Typical activation energy (self-diffusion, Al) | Q | **142** | kJ/mol | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Deformation Mechanism: Slip Plastic deformation in crystalline solids occurs primarily by **slip** – the movement of one atomic plane over another along specific crystallographic planes (slip planes) and directions (slip directions). The combination of a slip plane and a slip direction defines a **slip system**. - **Slip Plane:** The plane of highest atomic density (e.g., {111} in FCC, {110} in BCC, basal plane in HCP). - **Slip Direction:** The closest-packed direction lying within the slip plane (e.g., ⟨110⟩ in FCC, ⟨111⟩ in BCC). - **Slip System:** Plane + Direction = 1 slip system. > [!IMPORTANT] > FCC metals (Cu, Al, Au, Ni) have **12 slip systems** → highly ductile. > BCC metals (Fe-α, W, Cr) have **48 slip systems** but require thermal activation → ductile at high T. > HCP metals (Mg, Zn, Ti) have only **3 slip systems** → generally brittle at room temperature. ### Schmid's Law (Critical Resolved Shear Stress) When a uniaxial tensile stress σ is applied to a single crystal, the shear stress resolved on the slip plane in the slip direction is: $$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$ where: - φ = angle between tensile axis and normal to slip plane - λ = angle between tensile axis and slip direction - τ_CRSS = the constant value of τ_RSS at which slip begins (material property) **Slip initiates when:** $\tau_{RSS} \geq \tau_{CRSS}$ The orientation factor (Schmid factor) is **m = cos φ cos λ**; maximum value is 0.5 (when φ = λ = 45°). ## 2.2 Deformation Mechanism: Twinning Twinning is a mirror-image reorientation of a portion of the crystal lattice across a **twin plane (composition plane)**. Unlike slip, twinning: - Involves **partial displacement** of many atoms simultaneously. - Produces a **mirror-symmetric** lattice arrangement. - Is **polar** (sense of shear matters) and produces a **lattice reorientation**. Twinning dominates in: - **HCP metals** (Mg, Zn, Ti) at low temperatures. - **BCC metals** (Fe, Cr) at low T or high strain rates. - When slip systems are restricted. ## 2.3 Strengthening Mechanisms Metals are strengthened by **hindering dislocation motion**. Four classical mechanisms (the "4 Horsemen of Strengthening"): ### (a) Grain-Boundary Strengthening (Hall-Petch) Smaller grains → more grain boundaries → more obstacles to dislocation motion. $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$ where σ₀ = lattice friction stress, k_y = Hall-Petch coefficient, d = average grain diameter. ### (b) Solid-Solution Strengthening Solute atoms create local stress fields that impede dislocation motion. For small concentrations: $$\sigma_{ss} \propto \sqrt{c}$$ where c = atomic concentration of solute. ### (c) Strain (Work) Hardening Increasing dislocation density ρ increases the stress required for further plastic flow (Taylor relation): $$\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$$ where G = shear modulus, b = Burgers vector, α ≈ 0.3 – 0.5. ### (d) Precipitation (Second-Phase) Hardening Dislocations must either cut through precipitates (small, coherent) or loop around them (large, incoherent – Orowan looping). Peak hardening occurs at the critical precipitate radius. ## 2.4 Fracture Mechanisms ### (a) Ductile Fracture Three stages: **Neck formation → Cavity nucleation at inclusions → Cavity growth and coalescence → Cup-and-cone fracture surface.** Energy absorbed: **high**. ### (b) Brittle Fracture **Cleavage** along specific crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} in BCC Fe). Essentially no plastic deformation. Fracture surface shows **river patterns** and **cleavage steps**. ### (c) Fatigue Fracture Cyclic loading → **crack initiation** at surface defects → **Stage I (Stage I) slow crack growth** along slip planes → **Stage II fast crack growth** with characteristic **striations** → final overload fracture. Basquin's law (high-cycle fatigue): $$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$ where σ_a = stress amplitude, N_f = cycles to failure, σ'_f and b = fatigue strength coefficient and exponent. ### (d) Creep Fracture Time-dependent deformation at high T (T > 0.4 T_m). Three stages: **primary (decreasing rate) → secondary/steady (constant rate) → tertiary (accelerating to rupture).** Norton-Bailey steady-state creep law: $$\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^n \exp\left(-\frac{Q_c}{RT}\right)$$ ## 2.5 Diffusion Mechanisms **Fick's First Law (steady state):** $$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$$ **Fick's Second Law (non-steady state):** $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ Temperature dependence of diffusivity (Arrhenius form): $$D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ Two principal atomic mechanisms: - **Vacancy (substitutional) diffusion:** Atom exchanges with a neighboring vacancy. Slower; Q = Q_f (vacancy formation) + Q_m (migration). - **Interstitial diffusion:** Small atoms (C, N, H in Fe) jump between interstitial sites. Faster; Q = Q_m only. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Mechanism | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Key Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Slip (Schmid) | $\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$ | σ (MPa), φ, λ (deg) | τ_RSS ≥ τ_CRSS | | 2 | Hall-Petch | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y / \sqrt{d}$ | σ₀ (MPa), k_y (MPa·μm^½), d (μm) | d in range 1–100 μm | | 3 | Taylor (strain hardening) | $\tau = \tau_0 + \alpha G b \sqrt{\rho}$ | τ, G (MPa), b (m), ρ (m⁻²) | ρ > 10¹² m⁻² | | 4 | Solid solution | $\sigma_{ss} \propto \sqrt{c}$ | c = atomic fraction | dilute limit | | 5 | Fick's I | $J = -D \, dC/dx$ | J (atoms/m²·s), C (atoms/m³) | steady state | | 6 | Fick's II | $\partial C / \partial t = D \, \partial^2C / \partial x^2$ | D (m²/s) | transient | | 7 | Arrhenius diffusion | $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | D₀ (m²/s), Q (J/mol) | T > 0.5 T_m | | 8 | Norton-Bailey creep | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q_c/RT)$ | n = 3–8 typical | high T | | 9 | Basquin fatigue | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | b ≈ -0.05 to -0.12 | N_f > 10³ | | 10 | Coffin-Manson | $\Delta \varepsilon_p / 2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | c ≈ -0.5 to -0.7 | low-cycle | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Schmid's Law explains why single-crystal turbine blades are oriented so that the highest τ is on the most active slip system. Hall-Petch justifies the use of fine-grained microalloyed steels in automobiles. The Arrhenius form of D underpins every heat-treatment calculation in industry (carburizing, nitriding, annealing). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Schmid's Law Consider a cylindrical single crystal of cross-sectional area A₀ subjected to axial tensile force F. **Step 1 — Force balance on the slip plane.** The slip plane has area A = A₀ / cos φ, where φ is the angle between the tensile axis and the slip-plane normal. The component of F acting normal to the slip plane is F cos φ; the component acting parallel to the slip direction (lying within the slip plane) is F cos λ, where λ is the angle between tensile axis and slip direction. **Step 2 — Resolve into shear stress on slip system.** The shear force on the slip plane in the slip direction is: $$F_{shear} = F \cos\lambda$$ **Step 3 — Divide by the slip-plane area to get resolved shear stress.** $$\tau_{RSS} = \frac{F \cos\lambda}{A} = \frac{F \cos\lambda}{A_0 / \cos\phi} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$ Since σ = F/A₀, we obtain Schmid's Law: $$\tau_{RSS} = \sigma \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$ **Step 4 — Identify the Schmid factor.** The product cos φ cos λ is the **Schmid factor m**. Substituting: $$\tau_{RSS} = m\sigma, \quad \text{where } m = \cos\phi \cos\lambda$$ **Step 5 — Maximize the Schmid factor.** $$\frac{dm}{d\phi} = -\sin\phi \cos\lambda + \cos\phi \sin\lambda \cdot \frac{d\lambda}{d\phi} = 0$$ Using the geometric constraint that tensile axis, slip-plane normal, and slip direction are coplanar, this simplifies to the well-known result that **m is maximum when φ = λ = 45°**, giving m_max = 0.5. **Step 6 — Yield condition.** Single-crystal yielding occurs at the applied stress: $$\sigma_y^{sc} = \frac{\tau_{CRSS}}{m}$$ Hence, for the same material, the crystal oriented with φ = λ = 45° yields *first*, while orientations with m ≈ 0 (axial alignment with slip direction) are the *strongest*. ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Hall-Petch Equation (Dislocation Pile-Up Model) Consider a grain of diameter d containing a Frank–Read source emitting dislocations that pile up against a grain boundary. **Step 1 — Number of dislocations in the pile-up.** A pile-up of n dislocations under applied shear stress τ produces a stress concentration at the tip. The number n of dislocations in the pile-up of length L = d/2 is: $$n = \frac{k\pi d \tau}{2 G b}$$ **Step 2 — Stress concentration at the grain boundary.** The shear stress concentrated at the head of the pile-up is: $$\tau_{tip} = n \tau = \frac{k\pi d \tau^2}{2 G b}$$ **Step 3 — Yielding condition.** Yielding in the next grain requires τ_tip to equal the local lattice friction τ₀: $$\frac{k\pi d \tau_y^2}{2 G b} = \tau_0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for τ_y in terms of d.** $$\tau_y^2 = \frac{2 G b \tau_0}{k\pi d} \;\Rightarrow\; \tau_y = \sqrt{\frac{2 G b \tau_0}{k\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{d}}$$ **Step 5 — Convert shear yield stress to tensile yield stress (σ_y = 2 τ_y, Taylor factor).** $$\sigma_y = 2\tau_y = \underbrace{\left(2\sqrt{\frac{2 G b \tau_0}{k\pi}}\right)}_{\sigma_0} + 0 \quad \text{... but the constant term comes from direct-lattice resistance.}$$ In its standard empirical form: $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$ The k_y is a material constant (≈ 0.11 MPa·m^½ for mild steel). **Step 6 — Consequence.** Halving the grain size d increases σ_y by 41 % (since √2 = 1.414). This is the **strongest** strengthening mechanism available without changing composition. ## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Arrhenius Diffusion Equation **Step 1 — Statistical mechanics of activated jumps.** An atom on a lattice site oscillates with frequency ν ≈ 10¹³ Hz (Debye frequency). To move to a neighboring site, it must overcome an energy barrier of height Q. The probability of having sufficient energy is given by the Boltzmann factor. **Step 2 — Express the jump frequency.** $$\Gamma = \nu \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Relate jump frequency to diffusivity.** For a random walk on a 3D lattice, the mean-square displacement after N jumps is: $$\langle R^2 \rangle = N \alpha^2$$ where α = jump distance (≈ b). For an FCC lattice, α = a/√2. The diffusivity is: $$D = \frac{\Gamma \alpha^2}{6}$$ (The factor 6 is the dimensionality term for 3D random walk.) **Step 4 — Combine.** $$D = \frac{\nu \alpha^2}{6} \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Rewrite in molar form using R = N_A k_B.** $$D = \underbrace{\frac{\nu \alpha^2}{6}}_{D_0} \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right) = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ This is the famous **Arrhenius form** for diffusivity, valid from low T up to the melting point. **Step 6 — Take logarithm to extract activation energy from experiments.** $$\ln D = \ln D_0 - \frac{Q}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$ A plot of ln D vs 1/T is a straight line; slope = -Q/R, intercept = ln D₀. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary of Mechanisms | Mechanism | Driving Force | Rate-Limiting Step | Strongest T-Dependence | Engineering Lever | |---|---|---|---|---| | Slip | Applied shear ≥ τ_CRSS | Dislocation glide | Weak | Alloying, orientation | | Twinning | Shear at low T / high strain rate | Atom reorientation | Moderate | Texture control | | Grain-boundary strengthening | Dislocation pile-up | Crossing of GB | Weak | Grain refinement (thermomechanical) | | Solid-solution | Solute-dislocation elastic interaction | Dislocation motion through stress field | Weak | Alloy composition | | Strain hardening | Dislocation-dislocation interaction | Dislocation entanglement | Weak | Cold working % | | Precipitation | Dislocation-precipitate interaction | Cutting or Orowan looping | Strong (T controls precipitate size) | Heat treatment schedule | | Vacancy diffusion | Concentration gradient | Vacancy exchange | **Strong (Arrhenius)** | Temperature, time | | Creep | σ at high T | Diffusion + dislocation climb | **Very strong** | T limitation, alloy selection | | Fatigue | Cyclic ΔK | Crack propagation | Moderate | Surface finish, residual stress | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define slip. What is a slip system? Give two examples of slip systems in FCC and BCC metals.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Slip (1 mark):** Slip is the plastic deformation mechanism in crystalline solids in which one block of the crystal glides over an adjacent block along a specific crystallographic plane (slip plane) in a specific crystallographic direction (slip direction). The motion is mediated by the glide of dislocations. - **Slip system (1 mark):** A slip system is the combination of a slip plane and a slip direction that together constitute the unit of plastic deformation. In FCC, the slip plane is {111} and slip direction is ⟨110⟩; in BCC, the slip plane is {110} and slip direction is ⟨111⟩. - **Examples (1 mark):** FCC Cu: {111}⟨110⟩ — 12 slip systems.
