- At $\eta \to \infty$: $\theta = 0$
  $$0 = A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot 1 + 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad A = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}$$

Substituting $A$ and $B$ back:

$$\theta = -\operatorname{erf}(\eta) + 1 = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(\eta)$$

### Step 7 — Final Form

Restoring $\theta$ and $\eta$ in physical terms:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\;}$$

This is the **famous error-function solution** used universally for carburizing, nitriding, decarburization, and semiconductor doping problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Carburizing Case-Depth Calculation

> **Problem:** A low-carbon steel gear with initial carbon content $0.20\%$ is gas-carburized at $1173 \text{ K}$. The surface carbon content is maintained at $1.0\%$. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in austenite at $1173 \text{ K}$ is $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the time required to achieve a carbon content of $0.40\%$ at a depth of $0.5 \text{ mm}$ from the surface.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities

- $C_0 = 0.20\%$
- $C_s = 1.0\%$
- $C_x = 0.40\%$
- $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $x = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

### Step 2 — Apply the Error-Function Solution

$$\frac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

Substitute the numbers:

$$\frac{0.40 - 0.20}{1.0 - 0.20} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

$$\frac{0.20}{0.80} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

$$0.25 = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

$$\operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 0.75$$

### Step 3 — Invert the Error Function

From standard error-function tables:

$$\operatorname{erf}(0.80) \approx 0.7421$$
$$\operatorname{erf}(0.85) \approx 0.7707$$

Interpolating linearly to find the value where $\operatorname{erf}(z) = 0.75$:

$$z = 0.80 + \frac{0.75 - 0.7421}{0.7707 - 0.7421} \times 0.05 \approx 0.80 + 0.0140 = 0.814$$

So:

$$\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = 0.814$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $t$

$$2\sqrt{Dt} = \frac{x}{0.814} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.814} = 6.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\sqrt{Dt} = 3.071 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$Dt = (3.071 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 9.43 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

$$t = \frac{9.43 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-11}} = 9432 \text{ s}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Hours

$$t = \frac{9432}{3600} \approx 2.62 \text{ hours}$$

> **Final Answer:** The gear must be carburized for approximately **2.6 hours** to achieve $0.40\%$ carbon at a depth of 0.5 mm. ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Effect of Doubling Treatment Time

> **Question:** Using the same data as Example 1, what is the carbon concentration at $x = 0.5 \text{ mm}$ if the time is **doubled** to $t = 4 \times 9432 = 37728 \text{ s}$?

### Step 1 — Compute $Dt$

$$Dt = 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \times 37728 = 3.77 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{Dt}$

$$\sqrt{Dt} = 6.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Argument

$$\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 6.14 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.0}{12.28} = 0.407$$

### Step 4 — Look up erf(0.407)

$$\operatorname{erf}(0.40) \approx 0.4284$$
$$\operatorname{erf}(0.41) \approx 0.4379$$

Interpolating:

$$\operatorname{erf}(0.407) \approx 0.4284 + 0.7 \times (0.4379 - 0.4284) \approx 0.435$$

### Step 5 — Compute Carbon Concentration

$$\frac{C_x - 0.20}{1.0 - 0.20} = 1 - 0.435 = 0.565$$

$$C_x - 0.20 = 0.565 \times 0.80 = 0.452$$

$$C_x = 0.20 + 0.452 = 0.652\%$$

> **Final Answer:** Doubling the time raises the carbon content at 0.5 mm from $0.40\%$ to **$0.65\%$**. This shows that **deeper carbon penetration does NOT scale linearly with time** — it scales with $\sqrt{t}$. ✅

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Temperature Effect on Diffusion

> **Question:** For carbon diffusion in austenite:
> - $D_0 = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
> - $Q_d = 148{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$
>
> Calculate $D$ at $T = 1273 \text{ K}$ and at $T = 1173 \text{ K}$. What is the ratio?

### Step 1 — Arrhenius Equation

$$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_d}{RT}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Calculate at $T = 1273 \text{ K}$

$$\frac{Q_d}{RT} = \frac{148000}{8.314 \times 1273} = \frac{148000}{10583.6} = 13.98$$

$$D_{1273} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times e^{-13.98} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 8.58 \times 10^{-7} = 1.97 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate at $T = 1173 \text{ K}$

$$\frac{Q_d}{RT} = \frac{148000}{8.314 \times 1173} = \frac{148000}{9752.3} = 15.18$$

$$D_{1173} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times e^{-15.18} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.56 \times 10^{-7} = 5.89 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Ratio

$$\frac{D_{1273}}{D_{1173}} = \frac{1.97 \times 10^{-11}}{5.89 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 3.35$$

> **Final Answer:** A $100 \text{ K}$ temperature increase **triples** the diffusion coefficient. This is why carburizing furnaces are tightly temperature-controlled — a small T change has a large effect on the diffusion process. ✅

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix for KTU Numerical Problems

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation |
|--------------|-------|------|--------------|
| Time for specified $C_x$ at given $x$ | $C_s, C_0, C_x, x, D$ | $t$ | $\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ |
| Depth of $C_x$ at given time | $C_s, C_0, C_x, t, D$ | $x$ | Solve the erf equation for $x$ |
| Concentration at depth $x$ at time $t$ | $C_s, C_0, x, t, D$ | $C_x$ | Direct substitution |
| $D$ at a new temperature | $D_0, Q_d, T$ | $D$ | $D = D_0 e^{-Q_d/RT}$ |
| Time to double case depth | Existing $t_1, x_1$ | $t_2$ | $x \propto \sqrt{t}$, so $t_2 = 4 t_1$ |

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **"List any three industrial applications of diffusion and explain the role of Fick's Second Law in one of them." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Carburizing**: Diffusion of carbon into low-carbon steel at $\sim 1173 \text{ K}$ to produce a hard surface case while keeping the core tough.
- (1 Mark) **Semiconductor Doping**: Diffusion of B, P, or As into Si wafer at $1273\text{–}1473 \text{ K}$ to form p-n junctions.
- (1 Mark) **Role of Fick's Second Law in Carburizing**: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ with the solution $\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ is used to predict the carbon concentration $C_x$ at any depth $x$ after time $t$, enabling engineers to design the carburizing schedule for a specified case depth.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> **"Differentiate between carburizing and nitriding. Mention any two industrial components where these processes are applied." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Carburizing**: Diffuses *carbon* into low-carbon steel at $\sim 1173 \text{ K}$; requires quenching for hardness; uses CO, CH₄, or charcoal atmosphere.
- (1 Mark) **Nitriding**: Diffuses *nitrogen* into alloy steels at $\sim 773\text{–}823 \text{ K}$; no quenching needed; uses NH₃ atmosphere; forms hard Fe-nitrides.
- (1 Mark) **Applications**: Carburizing → *automotive gears, camshafts*; Nitriding → *aircraft crankshafts, diesel engine cylinder liners*.

---

## 5.2 Part B: ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert mm to m** in carburizing problems. This causes the final answer to be wrong by a factor of $\mathbf{10^6}$.
> - Many students incorrectly assume $D$ is constant for a given material. Always check whether the temperature is given — if it is, **first compute $D$ from the Arrhenius equation**, then apply the error-function solution.
> - For error-function problems, **don't** simply set the argument to 0.5 or 1.0 — **invert the erf using tables or numerical methods** to get the correct depth.
> - The **sign convention** in Fick's First Law: students often miss the negative sign. The flux flows from **high to low** concentration, so the gradient is **negative**, and the flux is **positive** only because of the minus sign in $J = -D \cdot dC/dx$.

---

### 📝 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Past Paper Pattern]**

> **Part (a)** — Explain the three methods of carburizing with neat diagrams. Discuss the role of the error function in determining the carbon concentration profile in a carburized steel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- (2 Marks) **Pack Carburizing**: Steel parts are packed in a sealed container along with solid carbonaceous material (charcoal) and an energizer (BaCO₃, Na₂CO₃). Heated to $1173 \text{ K}$ for several hours. Slowest but cheapest. CO released by BaCO₃ provides the active carbon: $\text{BaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + \text{CO}_2$, and $\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$, which decomposes on the steel surface to release carbon atoms.

- (2 Marks) **Gas Carburizing**: Steel is heated in a furnace with a controlled atmosphere of methane, propane, or carbon monoxide at $1173 \text{ K}$. The reactions are: $\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C(Fe)} + 2\text{H}_2$ and $2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{C(Fe)} + \text{CO}_2$. This method gives precise control of surface carbon content and is widely used industrially.

- (1.5 Marks) **Liquid Carburizing**: Steel is dipped in a molten cyanide salt bath (NaCN) at $973\text{–}1073 \text{ K}$. Reaction: $\text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{NaCNO}$, then $2\text{NaCNO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + 2\text{C(Fe)} + \text{N}_2$. Produces simultaneous C and N diffusion. Very fast but toxic — almost obsolete.

- (1.5 Marks) **Role of the Error Function**: Fick's Second Law for the semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration gives the error-function solution $\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$. By inverting the erf, engineers can predict the carbon concentration at any depth $x$ after time $t$, which is essential for designing the case depth and the carburizing schedule.

> **Part (b)** — A low-carbon steel component with $0.15\%$ initial carbon is gas-carburized at $1173 \text{ K}$. The surface carbon content is maintained at $1.10\%$. The diffusion coefficient of carbon in $\gamma$-iron at $1173 \text{ K}$ is $7.0 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate **(i)** the carbon content at a depth of $0.8 \text{ mm}$ after $5$ hours of treatment, and **(ii)** the time required to reach a carbon content of $0.50\%$ at $0.5 \text{ mm}$ depth. Given: $\operatorname{erf}(0.80) = 0.7421$, $\operatorname{erf}(1.50) = 0.9661$, $\operatorname{erf}(1.20) = 0.9103$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- (1 Mark) **Given:** $C_0 = 0.15\%$, $C_s = 1.10\%$, $D = 7.0 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $T = 1173 \text{ K}$.

- **(i) Carbon Content at $x = 0.8 \text{ mm}$ after $t = 5$ h** (3 Marks):

  Convert: $x = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, $t = 5 \times 3600 = 18{,}000 \text{ s}$.

  Compute $Dt = 7.0 \times 10^{-12} \times 18{,}000 = 1.26 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$.

  Compute $\sqrt{Dt} = 3.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$.

  Compute argument: $\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 3.55 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{8.0}{7.10} = 1.127$.

  Interpolate: $\operatorname{erf}(1.10) \approx 0.8802$, $\operatorname{erf}(1.13) \approx 0.8896$. Approximate $\operatorname{erf}(1.127) \approx 0.888$.

  Apply: $\dfrac{C_x - 0.15}{1.10 - 0.15} = 1 - 0.888 = 0.112$.

  $C_x - 0.15 = 0.112 \times 0.95 = 0.1064$.

  $C_x \approx 0.15 + 0.106 = \mathbf{0.256\%}$.

- **(ii) Time for $0.50\%$ at $x = 0.5 \text{ mm}$** (3 Marks):

  Convert: $x = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$.

  Apply error-function: $\dfrac{0.50 - 0.15}{1.10 - 0.15} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$.

  $0.368 = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(z)$ → $\operatorname{erf}(z) = 0.632$.

  From tables: $\operatorname{erf}(0.68) = 0.6638$, $\operatorname{erf}(0.65) = 0.6420$. Interpolating: $z \approx 0.645$.

  So $\dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = 0.645$ → $\sqrt{Dt} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.290} = 3.876 \times 10^{-4}$.

  $Dt = (3.876 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 1.502 \times 10^{-7}$.

  $t = \dfrac{1.502 \times 10^{-7}}{7.0 \times 10^{-12}} = 21{,}457 \text{ s} \approx 5.96 \text{ hours}$.

  **Final Answer: $t \approx 5.96 \text{ hours}$.**

---

### 📝 Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Past Paper Pattern]**

> **Part (a)** — With the help of neat sketches, describe the **nitriding** and **carbonitriding** processes. Compare their operating temperatures, atmospheres, and resulting case properties. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- (2 Marks) **Nitriding**:
  - Steel (alloy steel containing Al, Cr, Mo) is heated to $773\text{–}823 \text{ K}$ in an atmosphere of dissociated ammonia ($\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{N} + \tfrac{3}{2}\text{H}_2$).
  - Atomic nitrogen diffuses into the surface, forming hard **iron nitrides** (Fe₂N, Fe₃N, Fe₄N) and **alloy nitrides** (AlN, CrN).
  - No quenching required — surface is already very hard (~1000 HV).
  - Very low distortion due to low temperature.
  - **Disadvantage:** Long process time (20–90 hours).
  - **Applications:** Aircraft crankshafts, gears, dies, gauges.

- (2 Marks) **Carbonitriding**:
  - Steel is heated to $\sim 1123 \text{ K}$ in a mixed atmosphere of carburizing gas (CH₄) and ammonia (NH₃).
  - Both **carbon and nitrogen** diffuse simultaneously into the surface.
  - Faster than carburizing alone; produces harder case.
  - Thin case depth compared to carburizing.
  - Quenching is required to develop full hardness.

- (3 Marks) **Comparison Table**:

  | Parameter | Nitriding | Carbonitriding |
  |-----------|-----------|----------------|
  | Temperature | $773\text{–}823 \text{ K}$ | $1073\text{–}1173 \text{ K}$ |
  | Diffusing Species | Nitrogen only | Carbon and Nitrogen |
  | Atmosphere | NH₃ gas | CH₄ + NH₃ |
  | Quenching | Not required | Required |
  | Distortion | Very low | Moderate |
  | Case Hardness | $900\text{–}1100 \text{ HV}$ | $700\text{–}900 \text{ HV}$ |
  | Process Time | $20\text{–}90 \text{ hours}$ | $1\text{–}4 \text{ hours}$ |
  | Steel Used | Alloy steel with Al, Cr | Low-carbon and plain carbon steels |

> **Part (b)** — A plain carbon steel gear with initial carbon content of $0.18\%$ is to be carburized at $1200 \text{ K}$. The diffusion data for carbon in austenite at $1200 \text{ K}$ are: $D_0 = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ and $Q_d = 142{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$. The surface carbon content is fixed at $1.0\%$. Calculate **(i)** the diffusion coefficient at $1200 \text{ K}$, and **(ii)** the depth (in mm) at which the carbon content drops to $0.40\%$ after $6$ hours of treatment. Take $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$ and $\operatorname{erf}(0.85) = 0.7707$, $\operatorname{erf}(0.80) = 0.7421$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **(i) Diffusion Coefficient** (3 Marks):

  $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_d}{RT}\right) = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{142000}{8.314 \times 1200}\right)$.

  Compute exponent: $\dfrac{142000}{9976.8} = 14.234$.

  $D = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times e^{-14.234} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 6.60 \times 10^{-7} = 1.32 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

- **(ii) Depth at $C_x = 0.40\%$** (4 Marks):

  Given: $t = 6 \times 3600 = 21{,}600 \text{ s}$, $D = 1.32 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

  Apply: $\dfrac{0.40 - 0.18}{1.0 - 0.18} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$.

  $0.268 = 1 - \operatorname{erf}(z)$ → $\operatorname{erf}(z) = 0.732$.

  From tables: $\operatorname{erf}(0.80) = 0.7421$, $\operatorname{erf}(0.81) = 0.7480$, $\operatorname{erf}(0.79) = 0.7361$. Interpolating for $0.732$:

  $z = 0.79 + \dfrac{0.7361 - 0.732}{0.7361 - 0.7421} \times 0.01 = 0.79 + 0.685 \times 0.01 \approx 0.797$.

  So $\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}} = 0.797$.

  Compute $\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{1.32 \times 10^{-11} \times 21{,}600} = \sqrt{2.851 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.34 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$.

  $x = 0.797 \times 2 \times 5.34 \times 10^{-4} = 0.797 \times 1.068 \times 10^{-3} = 8.51 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$.

  **Final Answer: $x \approx 0.851 \text{ mm}$.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Applications of Diffusion**

- **Diffusion** = mass transport of atoms in solids driven by concentration gradient, temperature, or stress.
- **Fick's First Law** (steady state): $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$.
- **Fick's Second Law** (non-steady): $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$.
- **Solution for semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration:** $\dfrac{C_x - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$.
- **Arrhenius temperature dependence:** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q_d/RT)$.
- **Carburizing**: adds C to low-carbon steel at $\sim 1173 \text{ K}$ — case hardening.
- **Nitriding**: adds N to alloy steel at $\sim 773 \text{ K}$ — no quench needed.
- **Carbonitriding**: adds C + N at $\sim 1123 \text{ K}$ — combined effect.
- **Cyaniding**: thin case, uses NaCN salt bath — toxic.
- **Decarburization**: carbon *leaves* steel at high T in oxidizing atmosphere — *bad*.
- **Semiconductor doping**: B, P, As, Sb diffuse into Si at $1273\text{–}1473 \text{ K}$ to make p-n junctions.
- **Sintering**: powder consolidation by surface, grain-boundary, and volume diffusion.
- **Diffusion bonding**: solid-state joining at $0.5\,T_m\text{–}0.8\,T_m$ under pressure.
- **Case depth** scales with $\sqrt{t}$ — doubling the time does **not** double the depth.
- **Diffusion coefficient** increases roughly 2–3× per $100 \text{ K}$ temperature rise.
- **Erf values to remember**: $\operatorname{erf}(0.5) = 0.5205$, $\operatorname{erf}(1.0) = 0.8427$, $\operatorname{erf}(2.0) = 0.9953$.
- **Unit conversion** is the #1 reason students lose marks in numerical problems — always convert mm → m and hours → seconds.
- **Furnace atmosphere control** is critical — small leaks or impurities can cause decarburization, ruining the entire batch.
- **Quenching** is required after carburizing and carbonitriding, but **not** after nitriding.
- **Dopant depth** in semiconductor industry is measured in micrometers — case depth in gears is measured in millimeters — but both use the **same** Fickian error-function solution.
- **Surface concentration** $C_s$ is set by the atmosphere chemistry (carbon potential), not by the material.
- **Activation energy $Q_d$** is the energy barrier an atom must overcome to hop to a neighbouring site — high $Q_d$ means slower diffusion.
- **Vacancy diffusion** is dominant for substitutional atoms (Cu in Ni, Al in Cu); **interstitial diffusion** is dominant for small atoms (C, N, H in Fe).

> ✅ **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Applications of Diffusion. All the best for your KTU 2024 Examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tensile Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tensile Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Tensile Properties

**Tensile Properties** are a set of mechanical characteristics that describe how a material responds to an **axial tensile (pulling) load** applied uniaxially along its longitudinal axis. These properties are evaluated using the standardized **Uniaxial Tensile Test** (ASTM E8/E8M for metals), where a specimen of known original cross-sectional area $A_0$ and original gauge length $L_0$ is subjected to a gradually increasing axial load $F$ until fracture occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Tensile properties quantify a material's resistance to deformation and fracture under uniaxial tensile loading, encompassing elastic behavior, plastic deformation, strain hardening, and ultimate failure characteristics. They are fundamental inputs for design of load-bearing mechanical components.

The primary tensile properties derived from this test are:
- **Engineering Stress ($\sigma$)** — Force per unit **original** cross-sectional area.
- **Engineering Strain ($\epsilon$)** — Elongation per unit **original** gauge length.
- **True Stress ($\sigma_T$)** — Force per unit **instantaneous** cross-sectional area.
- **True Strain ($\epsilon_T$)** — Instantaneous change in length divided by **instantaneous** length.
- **Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)** — Slope of the linear elastic region (Young's Modulus).
- **Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$)** — Stress at which plastic deformation initiates.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_{uts}$)** — Maximum stress the material sustains.
- **Fracture Strength ($\sigma_f$)** — Stress at which the specimen breaks.
- **Percent Elongation (%EL)** — Measure of ductility.
- **Percent Reduction in Area (%RA)** — Measure of ductility.
- **Resilience Modulus ($U_r$)** — Energy absorbed per unit volume in the elastic region.
- **Toughness Modulus ($U_t$)** — Total energy absorbed per unit volume up to fracture.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** — Lateral strain to axial strain ratio.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Stretching a Rubber Band vs. a Steel Wire**

Imagine you are **stretching a rubber band** between your two hands. When you pull gently, the rubber band elongates, but when you release it, it springs back to its original length — this is the **elastic region** governed by Hooke's Law. If you pull harder, the rubber band becomes much easier to stretch but never returns to its original length — this is the **plastic region**. Eventually, at some maximum stretch, the band begins to thin at one spot (called **necking**) and finally snaps — this is **fracture**.

Now replace the rubber band with a **ductile metal wire** (say, mild steel or copper). The wire behaves similarly, but the forces are vastly larger, and the elongations are much smaller (a few percent compared to hundreds of percent for rubber). A **brittle material** like a piece of **chalk or cast iron** would simply snap almost immediately after elastic deformation with negligible plastic stretch — analogous to pulling a piece of **dried spaghetti** which resists loading until it suddenly breaks with little warning.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Significance:** Tensile properties are the **first filter** in materials selection. A bridge cable must have high strength and toughness; a spring must have high resilience; a car chassis panel must be ductile enough to absorb crash energy. **Every** load-bearing design begins with tensile property data.

---

## 1.3 Why Tensile Testing? — Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Uniaxial Tensile Test is considered the **"mother of all mechanical tests"** because:
> 1. It is **simple**, repeatable, and standardized worldwide.
> 2. It yields **multiple** material constants from a **single** experiment.
> 3. Other properties (fatigue, creep, hardness) are empirically correlated to tensile data.
> 4. The **stress-strain curve** is a "fingerprint" of the material's mechanical identity.

The international standards governing tensile testing are:
- **ASTM E8 / E8M** — Standard Test Methods for Tension Testing of Metallic Materials.
- **ISO 6892-1** — Metallic materials — Tensile testing — Part 1: Method of test at room temperature.
- **ASTM D638** — Tension testing of plastics.

The **strain rate** (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1} \ \text{s}^{-1}$) and **temperature** must be reported alongside tensile data, as both strongly influence the response.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Engineering Stress-Strain Curve — Step-by-Step Analysis

The engineering stress-strain ($\sigma$-$\epsilon$) curve is generated by plotting **engineering stress** on the y-axis and **engineering strain** on the x-axis as the specimen is loaded to failure. Below is the structural breakdown of this curve from origin to fracture.

### Step 1: Linear Elastic Region (Hooke's Law Region)

In this initial region, stress is **directly proportional** to strain. Atomic bonds stretch elastically — when the load is removed, the specimen returns to its original dimensions. The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)**, also called **Young's Modulus**.

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon}$$

The slope of this linear segment is the **stiffness** of the material. **A high $E$ means a stiff material** (e.g., diamond $E \approx 1000$ GPa, steel $E \approx 200$ GPa, aluminum $E \approx 70$ GPa, magnesium $E \approx 45$ GPa).

### Step 2: Proportional Limit ($\sigma_{pl}$)

This is the **maximum stress at which the stress-strain curve remains a perfectly straight line**. Beyond this, the relationship becomes nonlinear but may still be elastic (recoverable). The proportional limit is a **mathematical ideal** — difficult to measure precisely.

### Step 3: Elastic Limit ($\sigma_{el}$)

The **maximum stress that can be applied without causing any permanent (plastic) deformation** upon unloading. In practice, the elastic limit and proportional limit are nearly identical for most engineering materials, and the term **"yield strength"** is used in design instead.

### Step 4: Yielding — Initiation of Plastic Deformation

At the **yield point**, the material begins to **permanently** deform. Atomic dislocations begin to move in large numbers, allowing planes of atoms to slide past one another. Two practical definitions are used:

- **Upper Yield Point ($\sigma_{uy}$):** Stress at which plastic deformation first begins (often with a load drop).
- **Lower Yield Point ($\sigma_{ly}$):** Stress at which plastic deformation continues at a relatively constant value.
- **Offset Yield Strength ($\sigma_{0.2}$ or $\sigma_y$):** For materials without a distinct yield point (e.g., aluminum, copper), it is the stress at which a **0.2% plastic strain** occurs. Determined by drawing a line parallel to the elastic slope, offset by $\epsilon = 0.002$, until it intersects the curve.

### Step 5: Strain Hardening (Work Hardening) Region

After yielding, the material continues to deform plastically, but with an **increasing stress** requirement. This phenomenon is called **strain hardening** — dislocations multiply and tangle, impeding each other's motion, thereby increasing the material's resistance to further deformation. The slope of this region is the **strain hardening rate** $\frac{d\sigma}{d\epsilon}$.

### Step 6: Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, $\sigma_{uts}$)

The **maximum engineering stress** the material can sustain. Beyond this point, the reduction in cross-sectional area (necking) accelerates faster than the strain-hardening rate, so the engineering stress **decreases** (even though the actual stress in the neck continues to rise).

### Step 7: Necking

A **localized reduction** in cross-sectional area begins at some point along the gauge length, typically at the geometrically weakest location. All subsequent plastic deformation is concentrated in this neck region.

### Step 8: Fracture

The specimen separates into two pieces. The **engineering stress at fracture** is the **fracture strength** $\sigma_f$, and the corresponding strain is the **engineering strain at fracture** $\epsilon_f$.

---

## 2.2 Ductility Metrics

Ductility quantifies the degree of **plastic deformation** a material can undergo before fracture. Two measures are reported:

$$\% \text{EL} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{RA} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$

where $L_f$ and $A_f$ are the final gauge length and final cross-sectional area at the fracture surface, respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **%EL** is sensitive to gauge length $L_0$ (longer specimens show lower %EL). **%RA** is a more reliable ductility indicator because it depends only on the cross-section.

A material with **%EL > 5%** is generally classified as **ductile**; below 5% is considered **brittle**.

---

## 2.3 True Stress & True Strain

Engineering stress-strain values become physically misleading **beyond the onset of necking** because they are referenced to the original (unchanging) dimensions. The **true** values account for the **instantaneous** dimensions.

$$\sigma_T = \sigma \left(1 + \epsilon\right)$$

$$\epsilon_T = \ln\left(1 + \epsilon\right)$$

True stress-strain curves **continue to rise** until fracture (no apparent decrease), and are essential for:
- Metal forming simulations (forging, rolling, drawing).
- Predicting large-strain plastic behavior.
- Finite Element Analysis (FEA) of plastic deformation.

The relationship between true stress and true strain in the plastic region can be approximated by the **Hollomon Equation**:

$$\sigma_T = K \cdot \epsilon_T^n$$

where $K$ is the **strength coefficient** and $n$ is the **strain hardening exponent** ($0 \le n \le 1$). For an ideally plastic material, $n = 0$; for a perfectly elastic material (up to fracture), $n = 1$.

---

## 2.4 Resilience and Toughness — Energy Absorption

These properties measure a material's ability to **absorb energy** prior to failure.

**Resilience ($U_r$):** Energy absorbed per unit volume in the **elastic region**. Equals the area under the linear portion of the $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve.

$$U_r = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \epsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

**Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$):** Same as resilience for linear elastic behavior; it represents the **maximum energy per unit volume** that can be absorbed without permanent deformation.

**Toughness ($U_t$):** Total energy absorbed per unit volume up to **fracture**. Equals the **total area under the $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve**.

$$U_t = \int_{0}^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \ d\epsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resilience vs. Toughness:**
> - **Resilience** = area up to **yield** point → relevant for **springs, elastic components**.
> - **Toughness** = area up to **fracture** → relevant for **impact-resistant structures, crash zones**.

---

## 2.5 Poisson's Ratio

When a material is stretched axially, it contracts laterally. The ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to axial (longitudinal) strain is **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$:

$$\nu = -\frac{\epsilon_{lat}}{\epsilon_{axial}}$$

The negative sign ensures $\nu$ is positive (since lateral strain is negative when axial strain is positive). Typical values:
- **Metals:** $0.25$ to $0.35$.
- **Concrete:** $0.10$ to $0.20$.
- **Rubber (elastomers):** $\approx 0.50$ (incompressible limit).
- **Cork:** $\approx 0$ (nearly zero lateral contraction — why cork fits bottle necks).

The **theoretical range** is $-1 \le \nu \le 0.5$ for isotropic, thermodynamically stable materials.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU exam on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Key | Typical Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | $F$ = axial load, $A_0$ = original area | MPa or GPa |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\epsilon = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | $L$ = instantaneous length | dimensionless (or %) |
| 3 | Young's Modulus (Hooke's Law) | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \dfrac{F L_0}{A_0 \Delta L}$ | slope of elastic region | GPa |
| 4 | Offset Yield Strength (0.2%) | $\sigma_{0.2}$ at $\epsilon_{plastic} = 0.002$ | offset method | MPa |
| 5 | Percent Elongation | $\% \text{EL} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | $L_f$ = final length | % |
| 6 | Percent Reduction in Area | $\% \text{RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | $A_f$ = final fracture area | % |
| 7 | True Stress | $\sigma_T = \sigma (1 + \epsilon)$ | accounts for necking | MPa |
| 8 | True Strain | $\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon)$ | natural log | dimensionless |
| 9 | Hollomon Equation (Plastic Region) | $\sigma_T = K \cdot \epsilon_T^{\,n}$ | $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = hardening exponent | MPa, dimensionless |
| 10 | Modulus of Resilience | $U_r = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \epsilon_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E}$ | area under elastic region | MJ/m³ or MPa |
| 11 | Modulus of Toughness | $U_t = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \ d\epsilon$ | total area under $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ | MJ/m³ or MPa |
| 12 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\epsilon_{lat}}{\epsilon_{axial}}$ | lateral-to-axial strain ratio | dimensionless |
| 13 | Shear Modulus (from E and $\nu$) | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | valid for isotropic materials | GPa |
| 14 | Bulk Modulus (from E and $\nu$) | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ | volumetric response | GPa |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Aerospace structures** (aluminum alloys 2024, 7075): high $\sigma_{uts}/E$ (specific strength), moderate ductility.
> - **Pressure vessels & pipelines** (X-65, X-80 line-pipe steels): high $\sigma_y$ and high $U_t$ to prevent catastrophic brittle fracture.
> - **Sheet metal forming** (deep drawing of steel): high $n$ (typically 0.20–0.25) for uniform plastic deformation and resistance to localized necking.
> - **Springs & elastomers**: very high $U_r$ (e.g., spring steel has $U_r \approx 0.7$–$1.0$ MJ/m³).
> - **Bridge cables & pre-stressed concrete tendons**: high $\sigma_y$ and high $E$ to minimize elastic stretch under service load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: True Stress from Engineering Stress

We start from the definition of engineering stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

where $A_0$ is the **original** cross-sectional area. As the specimen elongates, the **instantaneous** area $A$ decreases due to plastic deformation (assuming constant volume plastic flow in the gauge section).

**Assumption (incompressible plastic flow):** Volume is conserved in the plastic region:

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A \cdot L$$

Solving for the instantaneous area:

$$A = \frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L}$$

The **true stress** is force divided by the instantaneous area:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A}$$

Substituting the expression for $A$:

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A_0 \cdot L_0 / L} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L}{L_0}$$

Since $F/A_0 = \sigma$ and $L/L_0 = 1 + \epsilon$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_T = \sigma \left(1 + \epsilon\right)}$$

This equation is valid up to the onset of necking. Beyond necking, it is still used as an engineering approximation when $A$ is measured directly at the neck.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: True Strain from Engineering Strain

Starting with engineering strain:

$$\epsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{L}{L_0} - 1$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{L}{L_0} = 1 + \epsilon$$

True strain is defined as the **summation (integral) of infinitesimal strain increments** over the path from $L_0$ to $L$:

$$\epsilon_T = \int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL'}{L'}$$

Evaluating the natural log integral:

$$\epsilon_T = \ln L \ \Big\vert_{L_0}^{L} = \ln L - \ln L_0 = \ln\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right)$$

Substituting the previous relation:

$$\boxed{\epsilon_T = \ln\left(1 + \epsilon\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why true strain uses a logarithm:** Because the denominator (instantaneous length) **changes** as the material deforms, we must integrate. For small strains ($\epsilon \ll 1$), $\ln(1+\epsilon) \approx \epsilon$, so engineering and true values are nearly equal in the elastic region.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Modulus of Resilience from a Triangular Area

The elastic region of the $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve is a **triangle** with base $\epsilon_y$ (strain at yield) and height $\sigma_y$ (stress at yield). The area of this triangle represents the **strain energy density** (energy per unit volume), or **modulus of resilience**:

$$U_r = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \epsilon_y \cdot \sigma_y$$

Since Hooke's Law gives $\epsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$:

$$U_r = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_y}{E} \cdot \sigma_y = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

$$\boxed{U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}}$$

This is one of the most-tested formulas in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Shear Modulus and Bulk Modulus from E and $\nu$

For an **isotropic, homogeneous, linear elastic** material, only **two independent elastic constants** are needed. If $E$ and $\nu$ are known, the other moduli follow.

**Shear Modulus $G$:** The relation is:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

**Proof sketch:** The shear modulus relates shear stress $\tau$ to shear strain $\gamma$ in a pure shear test. For isotropic materials, the elastic strain energy in terms of all three normal stresses and strains can be decomposed, and the pure-shear component yields the above relation.

**Bulk Modulus $K$:** Relates hydrostatic pressure $p$ to volumetric strain $\Delta V/V$:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Proof sketch:** Apply a hydrostatic pressure $p$ to a cube. The normal stress on each face is $-p$. The resulting strain in each direction is $\epsilon_x = \epsilon_y = \epsilon_z = -p(1-2\nu)/E$. The volumetric strain is the sum: $\Delta V / V = 3\epsilon_x = -3p(1-2\nu)/E$. Inverting $p = -K(\Delta V/V)$ gives the result.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 1: Engineering & True Stress/Strain Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU University Exam - May 2024 pattern):**
> A cylindrical brass specimen with original diameter $d_0 = 12$ mm and original gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm is pulled in tension. At an applied load $F = 35$ kN, the gauge length increases to $L = 51.5$ mm and the diameter reduces to $d = 11.85$ mm. Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, true stress, and true strain at this load.

**Step 1: Original and instantaneous areas.**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (12)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 144}{4} = 36 \pi \ \text{mm}^2 \approx 113.10 \ \text{mm}^2$$

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (11.85)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 140.4225}{4} \approx 110.28 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Engineering stress.**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{35 \times 10^3 \ \text{N}}{113.10 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{35{,}000}{1.1310 \times 10^{-4}} \ \text{Pa} \approx 3.095 \times 10^8 \ \text{Pa} \approx 309.5 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3: Engineering strain.**

$$\epsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{51.5 - 50}{50} = \frac{1.5}{50} = 0.030 = 3.0\%$$

**Step 4: True stress (using the formula $\sigma_T = \sigma(1+\epsilon)$).**

$$\sigma_T = 309.5 \times (1 + 0.030) = 309.5 \times 1.030 = 318.8 \ \text{MPa}$$

**Verification using instantaneous area:**

$$\sigma_T = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{35{,}000}{110.28 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 317.4 \ \text{MPa}$$

The small difference (≈0.5%) is due to rounding in area calculation — both methods agree.

**Step 5: True strain.**

$$\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon) = \ln(1.030) = 0.02956 \approx 0.0296 = 2.96\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** For small strains (< 5%), engineering and true values are nearly identical. They diverge significantly only at large plastic strains (e.g., necking region).

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 2: Modulus of Resilience Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 pattern):**
> A quenched and tempered alloy steel has a yield strength $\sigma_y = 1240$ MPa and Young's modulus $E = 210$ GPa. A spring made from this material must absorb 1.8 J of elastic energy per cubic centimeter. Is this material suitable?

**Step 1: Compute the modulus of resilience of the material.**

$$U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(1240 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times (210 \times 10^9)}$$

$$U_r = \frac{1.5376 \times 10^{18}}{4.20 \times 10^{11}}$$

$$U_r = 3.661 \times 10^6 \ \text{J/m}^3 = 3.661 \ \text{MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Convert to J/cm³.**

$$1 \ \text{J/cm}^3 = 10^6 \ \text{J/m}^3$$

$$U_r = \frac{3.661 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 3.661 \ \text{J/cm}^3$$

**Step 3: Compare to requirement.**

Required: $1.8 \ \text{J/cm}^3$. Available: $3.661 \ \text{J/cm}^3$.

Since $3.661 > 1.8$, the material **is suitable** for the spring application.

$$\boxed{U_r = 3.66 \ \text{J/cm}^3 \ \Rightarrow \ \text{Material is suitable.}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Material Comparison

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $\sigma_{uts}$ (MPa) | %EL | $U_r$ (MJ/m³) | Behavior |
|----------|-----------|------------------|----------------------|-----|---------------|----------|
| Diamond | 1000 | — | — | 0 | — | Brittle |
| Tungsten carbide | 600 | 600 | 1500 | 0 | 0.30 | Brittle |
| AISI 1040 Steel (Q&T) | 210 | 1240 | 1580 | 12 | 3.66 | Ductile |
| Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) | 114 | 1100 | 1150 | 10 | 5.31 | Ductile |
| Aluminum 2024-T3 | 73 | 345 | 485 | 18 | 0.82 | Ductile |
| Copper (annealed) | 110 | 70 | 220 | 45 | 0.022 | Highly ductile |
| Magnesium AZ31 | 45 | 200 | 260 | 15 | 0.444 | Ductile |
| Cast Iron (gray) | 100 | — | 200 | 0.6 | 0.022 | Brittle |
| Nylon 6,6 | 3.0 | 55 | 80 | 60 | 0.504 | Tough polymer |
| Natural rubber | 0.001–0.01 | 2 | 15 | 700 | 0.0002 | Hyperelastic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that the **modulus of resilience $U_r$** depends on **both** strength and stiffness. A high yield strength alone is not enough — the material must also be stiff (high $E$) relative to its strength. Titanium's high $U_r$ explains its use in springs and aerospace components.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Engineering Stress-Strain Curve (Block Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how a stress-strain curve is constructed, the various regions, and the key parameters associated with each. This serves as a visual flowchart of the material's response to increasing tensile load.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Apply Tensile Load F] --> B[Record Force and Elongation Continuously]
    B --> C{Stress vs Strain Calculated}
    C --> D[Region I: Linear Elastic Region]
    D --> E[Slope equals Young's Modulus E]
    E --> F[Region II: Proportional and Elastic Limit]
    F --> G[Region III: Yielding and Plastic Deformation]
    G --> H{Yield Point Visible?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use Upper and Lower Yield Points]
    H -->|No| J[Use 0.2 Percent Offset Method]
    I --> K[Region IV: Strain Hardening Zone]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Region V: Ultimate Tensile Strength UTS]
    L --> M[Region VI: Necking Localized]
    M --> N[Region VII: Fracture]
    N --> O[END: Specimen Separated]
    D --> P[Modulus of Resilience equals area under Region I]
    N --> Q[Modulus of Toughness equals total area under curve]
    K --> R[Hollomon Equation applies in Region IV]
    L --> S[True Stress and True Strain from this point]
```

---

## 4.2 Tensile Test Specimen Geometry (Block Schematic)

The standardized tensile test specimen (per ASTM E8) has a precisely defined geometry. The following block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of the specimen and the naming of each region.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Head Section M16 Threaded] --> B[Shoulder Fillet Radius]
    B --> C[Reduced Gauge Section Diameter d0]
    C --> D[Original Gauge Length L0]
    D --> E[Reduced Gauge Section Diameter d0]
    E --> F[Shoulder Fillet Radius]
    F --> G[Head Section M16 Threaded]
    C -.->|After Test| H[Final Gauge Length Lf]
    E -.->|After Test| I[Final Diameter df at Fracture]
    H --> J[Percent Elongation percentEL]
    I --> K[Percent Reduction in Area percentRA]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Ductile vs Brittle Material Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Subjected to Tensile Test] --> B[Plastic Strain at Fracture less than 5 percent?]
    B -->|Yes| C[Brittle Material]
    B -->|No| D[Ductile Material]
    C --> E[No Significant Necking]
    C --> F[Fracture Surface: Flat and Shiny]
    C --> G[Engineering Stress Strain Curve: Linear to Fracture]
    D --> H[Visible Necking Before Fracture]
    D --> I[Fracture Surface: Cup and Cone]
    D --> J[Engineering Stress Strain Curve: Distinct Yield and UTS]
    E --> K[Examples: Cast Iron, Ceramics, Glass]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> L[Examples: Mild Steel, Copper, Aluminum]
    I --> L
    J --> L
```

---

## 4.4 Offset Yield Strength Method (0.2% Offset) — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stress Strain Data Plotted] --> B[Identify Linear Elastic Region]
    B --> C[Draw Line Parallel to Elastic Slope]
    C --> D[Offset Line by Strain equals 0.002]
    D --> E[Locate Intersection with Stress Strain Curve]
    E --> F[Read Corresponding Stress Value]
    F --> G[This Stress is Yield Strength Sigma 0.2]
    G --> H[Use as Design Stress for Ductile Materials]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 0.2% offset method is the **industry standard** for materials that do not exhibit a sharp yield point (most non-ferrous metals such as aluminum alloys, copper alloys, titanium alloys, and stainless steels).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define (i) Engineering Stress, (ii) Engineering Strain, and (iii) Modulus of Elasticity. State the SI units of each.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Engineering Stress ($\sigma$):** The instantaneous axial tensile load $F$ applied to a specimen divided by its **original** cross-sectional area $A_0$:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

Units: $\text{N/m}^2 = \text{Pa}$ (practically MPa or GPa).

(ii) **Engineering Strain ($\epsilon$):** The change in gauge length $\Delta L$ divided by the **original** gauge length $L_0$:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$

Units: dimensionless (commonly expressed as % or m/m).

(iii) **Modulus of Elasticity ($E$):** The slope of the linear (elastic) region of the engineering stress-strain curve; it is the constant of proportionality in Hooke's Law, $\sigma = E \epsilon$. It represents the **stiffness** of the material.

Units: GPa (or MPa).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Q2. Distinguish between **Resilience** and **Toughness** of a material. Which one is more relevant for designing (a) a spring, and (b) a crash barrier?

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Definition | Region of Curve |
|----------|------------|-----------------|
| **Resilience** | Capacity of a material to absorb energy when deformed elastically and release it upon unloading. | Area under the **elastic region** of the $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve = $\dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$. |
| **Toughness** | Capacity of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform **before fracturing**. | Total area under the **entire** $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve up to fracture. |

(a) **Spring:** **Resilience** is critical — the spring must absorb and release energy elastically without permanent set.

(b) **Crash barrier:** **Toughness** is critical — the barrier must absorb large amounts of energy through plastic deformation before fracture, in order to protect occupants.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw a schematic engineering stress-strain curve for a medium-carbon steel, clearly labeling the following points: (i) Proportional limit, (ii) Elastic limit, (iii) Upper yield point, (iv) Lower yield point, (v) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (vi) Fracture point. Also indicate the regions corresponding to elastic deformation, plastic deformation, strain hardening, and necking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical specimen of a ductile cast iron has an original diameter of 18 mm and a gauge length of 60 mm. It is subjected to a tensile test, and the following data are recorded:

| Load (kN) | Gauge Length (mm) |
|-----------|-------------------|
| 0 | 60.000 |
| 25 | 60.075 |
| 55 | 60.150 |
| 85 | 60.225 |
| 110 | 60.300 |
| 125 (max) | 60.450 |
| 115 (fracture) | 61.380 |

Final diameter at fracture = 14.6 mm.

Compute: (i) Modulus of Elasticity, (ii) Engineering stress at maximum load (UTS), (iii) Percent Elongation, (iv) Percent Reduction in Area. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**(i) Modulus of Elasticity:** Use data from the linear elastic region. From 0 to 55 kN, the data is linear:

$$\Delta L = 60.150 - 60.000 = 0.150 \ \text{mm}, \quad F = 55 \ \text{kN}$$

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi (18)^2}{4} = 81 \pi \approx 254.47 \ \text{mm}^2 = 2.5447 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$\sigma = \frac{55{,}000}{2.5447 \times 10^{-4}} = 216.1 \ \text{MPa}$$

$$\epsilon = \frac{0.150}{60.000} = 0.0025$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon} = \frac{216.1 \ \text{MPa}}{0.0025} = 86{,}440 \ \text{MPa} \approx 86.4 \ \text{GPa}$$

**(ii) UTS:** At maximum load 125 kN:

$$\sigma_{uts} = \frac{125{,}000}{2.5447 \times 10^{-4}} = 491.2 \ \text{MPa}$$

**(iii) Percent Elongation:**

$$\% \text{EL} = \frac{61.380 - 60.000}{60.000} \times 100 = \frac{1.380}{60.000} \times 100 = 2.30\%$$

**(iv) Percent Reduction in Area:**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi (14.6)^2}{4} = 53.29 \pi \approx 167.41 \ \text{mm}^2$$

$$\% \text{RA} = \frac{254.47 - 167.41}{254.47} \times 100 = \frac{87.06}{254.47} \times 100 = 34.2\%$$

**Note:** Even with low %EL (2.3%), the high %RA (34.2%) confirms localized necking — a characteristic of ductile cast iron.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the significance of the following tensile properties for material selection: (i) Modulus of Resilience, (ii) Toughness, (iii) Ductility, (iv) Poisson's Ratio. For each, give **one real-world engineering example** where it is the most critical property. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

(i) **Modulus of Resilience ($U_r$):** Indicates the energy a material can absorb **elastically** per unit volume. Critical for **spring applications** (e.g., mechanical watch springs, vehicle suspension coil springs) where energy must be stored and released without permanent deformation. A high $U_r$ requires high yield strength and low modulus (so the spring flexes easily).

(ii) **Toughness ($U_t$):** The total energy absorbed before fracture. Critical for **structural components subjected to impact or shock loading** (e.g., automobile bumpers, helicopter rotor blades, ship hulls, earthquake-resistant building beams). A high toughness is required to prevent catastrophic brittle fracture.

(iii) **Ductility (%EL, %RA):** The ability to undergo large plastic deformation before failure. Critical for **metal forming operations** (e.g., deep drawing of automobile body panels, wire drawing, forging, extrusion). A ductile material can be shaped into complex geometries without cracking. Also essential for **structural safety** — ductile materials give visible warning (necking) before failure, whereas brittle materials fail without warning.

(iv) **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** The ratio of lateral to axial strain under uniaxial loading. Critical for **multi-axial stress analysis** (e.g., pressure vessel design, where axial and hoop stresses interact), and for **composite laminate design** where mismatched $\nu$ between plies causes interlaminar stresses. It also governs the **incompressibility** of elastomers ($\nu \to 0.5$) used in seals and gaskets.

---

**(b)** A brass specimen with original gauge length $L_0 = 40$ mm and original diameter $d_0 = 8$ mm yields at a load of $F_y = 8500$ N. The corresponding elongation at yield is $\Delta L_y = 0.040$ mm. The maximum load sustained is $F_{uts} = 15{,}200$ N. At fracture, the gauge length is $L_f = 46.4$ mm and the diameter is $d_f = 5.5$ mm. Calculate: (i) Young's Modulus, (ii) Yield Strength, (iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (iv) Percent Elongation, (v) Percent Reduction in Area, and (vi) True Stress and True Strain at the maximum load point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Setup:**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi (8)^2}{4} = 16 \pi \approx 50.265 \ \text{mm}^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi (5.5)^2}{4} = 7.5625 \pi \approx 23.758 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**(i) Young's Modulus:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{8500}{5.0265 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.691 \times 10^8 \ \text{Pa} = 169.1 \ \text{MPa}$$

$$\epsilon_y = \frac{\Delta L_y}{L_0} = \frac{0.040}{40} = 0.00100$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma_y}{\epsilon_y} = \frac{169.1 \ \text{MPa}}{0.00100} = 169{,}100 \ \text{MPa} = 169.1 \ \text{GPa}$$

**(ii) Yield Strength:** $\sigma_y = 169.1 \ \text{MPa}$ (computed above).

**(iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$\sigma_{uts} = \frac{F_{uts}}{A_0} = \frac{15{,}200}{5.0265 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.024 \times 10^8 \ \text{Pa} = 302.4 \ \text{MPa}$$

**(iv) Percent Elongation:**

$$\% \text{EL} = \frac{46.4 - 40.0}{40.0} \times 100 = \frac{6.4}{40.0} \times 100 = 16.0\%$$

**(v) Percent Reduction in Area:**

$$\% \text{RA} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{50.265 - 23.758}{50.265} \times 100 = \frac{26.507}{50.265} \times 100 = 52.7\%$$

**(vi) True Stress and True Strain at Maximum Load:**

Engineering strain at maximum load is not given directly, but we can use the elongation at yield (0.040 mm) as an approximate reference. However, since we want true values **at** UTS, we use the engineering strain estimate at UTS. From the elongation at yield (small elastic strain) and the given fracture data, the engineering strain at UTS in the original data is implied to be small. For estimation:

Assuming engineering strain at UTS $\epsilon_{uts} \approx 0.04$ (typical for brass at UTS):

$$\sigma_T = \sigma_{uts}(1 + \epsilon_{uts}) = 302.4 \times (1 + 0.04) = 302.4 \times 1.04 = 314.5 \ \text{MPa}$$

$$\epsilon_T = \ln(1 + \epsilon_{uts}) = \ln(1.04) = 0.0392 \approx 0.039$$

(If the problem intends us to use the value at the **fracture** point instead: $\epsilon_f = 0.16$, then $\sigma_{Tf} = 302.4 \times 1.16 = 350.8$ MPa and $\epsilon_{Tf} = \ln(1.16) = 0.1484$.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently:** $A$ in $\text{mm}^2$ must be converted to $\text{m}^2$ when $F$ is in N. Forgetting the $10^{-6}$ factor is the **#1 source of mark loss** in KTU numericals.
> 2. **Confusing engineering and true quantities at large strains:** At 20% engineering strain, true strain is only 18.2%, and true stress is 20% higher. Students often apply the true-stress formula in the elastic region, where engineering and true values are virtually identical — both methods give full credit, but the formula must be **correctly identified** for the regime.
> 3. **Confusing toughness with resilience:** Toughness = area to **fracture**; resilience = area to **yield**. Examiners specifically check whether you used $\epsilon_f$ or $\epsilon_y$ in the integral.
> 4. **Misapplying %EL formula:** The denominator is **$L_0$**, not $L_f$. Writing $\frac{L_f - L_0}{L_f}$ is a common error that costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Poisson's ratio sign convention:** Always include the **negative sign** to ensure $\nu > 0$. Writing $\nu = \epsilon_{lat}/\epsilon_{axial}$ without the minus sign gives a negative value and is technically wrong.
> 6. **0.2% offset definition:** Examiners expect "the stress corresponding to 0.2% **plastic** (permanent) strain," not 0.2% **total** strain. The offset line starts at $\epsilon = 0.002$ on the strain axis, not at the origin.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Tensile Properties**

- [x] **Engineering stress** uses **original** area $A_0$; **true stress** uses **instantaneous** area $A$.
- [x] **Engineering strain** is $\Delta L / L_0$; **true strain** is $\ln(L/L_0) = \ln(1+\epsilon)$.
- [x] **Young's Modulus $E$** is the slope of the **linear elastic** region of the $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve.
- [x] **Yield strength** is the stress at which **plastic** deformation begins. For non-ferrous metals, use the **0.2% offset method**.
- [x] **UTS** is the **maximum engineering stress**; beyond UTS, necking begins.
- [x] **Fracture strength** is the engineering stress at which the specimen actually breaks (always $\le$ UTS for ductile materials).
- [x] **%EL** depends on gauge length $L_0$; **%RA** is more reliable as a ductility metric.
- [x] **Resilience** = $\sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ — energy absorbed **elastically** per unit volume.
- [x] **Toughness** = $\int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma \, d\epsilon$ — energy absorbed up to **fracture**.
- [x] **Hollomon equation** $\sigma_T = K \epsilon_T^{\,n}$ governs the **plastic flow** region.
- [x] **Poisson's ratio** $\nu = -\epsilon_{lat}/\epsilon_{axial}$; typical range for metals is 0.25 to 0.35.
- [x] For isotropic materials: $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ and $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$.
- [x] **Ductile** materials: %EL $> 5\%$, exhibit yield, strain hardening, necking, and cup-and-cone fracture.
- [x] **Brittle** materials: %EL $< 5\%$, fracture with no appreciable plastic deformation.
- [x] **Volume conservation** during plastic flow: $A_0 L_0 = A L$ — used to derive the true-stress formula.
- [x] Always report **strain rate and temperature** alongside tensile data.
- [x] **Standards:** ASTM E8/E8M (metals), ISO 6892-1 (international), ASTM D638 (plastics).
- [x] **Units cheat sheet:** Stress in MPa ($1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$), $E$ in GPa ($1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa}$), area in $\text{m}^2$ when force is in N.
- [x] **True stress-strain curve never decreases** — it always rises to fracture (engineering curve decreases after UTS due to necking).
- [x] **Strain hardening exponent $n$** indicates formability: high $n$ = good for sheet metal forming.
- [x] **Cork** has $\nu \approx 0$ — hence its use in wine bottle stoppers.
- [x] **Rubber** has $\nu \approx 0.5$ — hence its near-incompressibility.
- [x] **Young's modulus is a structure-insensitive property** — it is governed by atomic bonding and is virtually unaffected by heat treatment or cold work (unlike $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_{uts}$, which are structure-sensitive).
- [x] **Toughness depends on both strength and ductility** — a strong-but-brittle material can have lower toughness than a weaker-but-ductile material.
- [x] **Area under $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve** = energy per unit volume (strain energy density).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardness and Hardness Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hardness and Hardness Measurement # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Hardness? — The Formal KTU Definition **Hardness** is the resistance of a material to **localized plastic deformation**, typically caused by mechanical indentation, abrasion, or scratching. It is fundamentally a surface-level property that quantifies a material's ability to resist permanent shape change when a concentrated external force is applied via a sharp object (the *indenter*). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Rigid):** > *"Hardness is defined as the resistance of a material to indentation, scratching, abrasion, or cutting. It is not a fundamental material property in the thermodynamic sense, but rather an empirical, easily measurable engineering parameter that correlates strongly with other mechanical properties such as yield strength and wear resistance."* Mathematically, hardness $H$ is expressed as the **applied load $P$ divided by the projected contact area $A$** of the indentation: $$H = \frac{P}{A}$$ where $P$ is in **N** (Newtons) and $A$ is in **mm²**, yielding hardness in **MPa** or **GPa** (when expressed in SI units). ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **Plain English Analogy: "The Wall vs. The Sponge"** > > Imagine you press your thumb hard against a **solid concrete wall** versus a **soft sponge**. > - The **concrete wall** barely dents — it is *hard*. The wall's surface atoms form a rigid, tightly-bonded crystal lattice that strongly resists the thumb's pressure. > - The **sponge** easily yields, gets a permanent dent — it is *soft*. Its internal bonds are weak and rearrange easily under load. > > In a hardness test, an engineered "thumb" (the indenter — a hardened steel ball or diamond pyramid) presses into the metal with a known force. The smaller and shallower the dent left behind, the harder the material. The size of that dent is the inverse fingerprint of how tightly the material's atoms are bonded. ## 1.3 Why Is Hardness Important? (Engineering Utility) Hardness is one of the most **routinely measured** properties in any materials or mechanical engineering lab for three practical reasons: 1. **Speed and Simplicity** — Hardness tests are *non-destructive* (or only slightly destructive), fast (seconds to minutes), and require only a small, polished sample. 2. **Strong Correlation with Strength** — A harder material almost always has higher yield strength and tensile strength. This empirical relationship (e.g., $UTS \approx 3.45 \times HB$ for steels) allows engineers to *estimate* strength without destroying tensile-test specimens. 3. **Wear and Durability Prediction** — Hard surfaces resist scratching, galling, and abrasive wear — critical for gears, bearings, cutting tools, dies, and cam shafts. ## 1.4 Classification of Hardness Tests There are **three broad families** of hardness tests in engineering practice: | Family | Mechanism | Typical Tests | Common Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Scratch Hardness** | Resistance to scratching by a harder point | Mohs Scale | Mineralogy, ceramics ranking | | **Rebound / Dynamic Hardness** | Height of bounce of a dropped indenter | Shore Scleroscope, Leeb | Large/forging components, on-site checks | | **Indentation Hardness** | Depth or area of permanent indent | Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell, Knoop | Metals, alloys, heat-treated parts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Focus Zone:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 places heavy emphasis on **Brinell, Vickers, and Rockwell** hardness tests, their formulas, indenter geometries, load selection, and conversion relationships. Memorize the indenter shapes, standard loads, and dwell times for each. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Indentation Tests — A Logic Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Brinell Hardness Test (HB) — Johan August Brinell, 1900 **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. A **hardened steel ball** (or tungsten carbide ball) of diameter $D$ is pressed into a *polished, flat* specimen surface. 2. A **standard load $P$** is applied perpendicular to the surface, held for a fixed **dwell time** (typically 10–30 s for metals). 3. The load is removed, leaving a *circular* indent of diameter $d$ (measured using a calibrated microscope with a graduated scale). 4. The **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN or HB)** is computed as the load divided by the **curved surface area** of the indentation (not the projected area). **Why a Steel Ball?** Because the indenter must always be *harder* than the test material — a fundamental prerequisite of any indentation test. **Why Load/Diameter Ratios Matter:** To compare BHN values across different materials and specimen sizes, the load must be scaled with the square of the indenter diameter. This is the **load ratio principle** (see Section 2.2). ### 2.1.2 Vickers Hardness Test (HV) — Robert L. Smith & George E. Sandland, 1921 **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. A **square-based diamond pyramid indenter** with a face angle of **136°** between opposite faces is pressed into the specimen. 2. A **standard load $P$** (typically 1 gf to 100 kgf, depending on application) is applied and held for a dwell time. 3. On removal, a *square-shaped* indent with diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$ is left. 4. The **Vickers Hardness Number (HV)** is computed as load divided by the **projected contact area** (not the slanted surface area). **Why 136°?** This face angle was deliberately chosen because it produces HV numbers that closely match Brinell HB values up to approximately 300 HB. Beyond this, the Vickers test is more accurate for very hard materials (HV can scale indefinitely). **The Killer Advantage:** Since the Vickers indent is geometrically *similar* at all loads, the HV number is **load-independent** — unlike Brinell where the load ratio matters. This makes Vickers the most universally applicable hardness test. ### 2.1.3 Rockwell Hardness Test (HR) — Stanley P. Rockwell, 1919 **Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. A diamond cone (Brale indenter) or a steel ball indenter is brought into **minor contact** with the surface, applying a small **preliminary load (10 kgf)**. This establishes a *zero reference depth* (eliminates surface roughness and seating errors). 2. The **major load** is then applied (60, 100, or 150 kgf, depending on scale). 3. After dwell time, the major load is removed, leaving the **preliminary load still applied**. 4. The **depth difference $e$** (in units of 0.002 mm, called a *Rockwell unit*) is measured. 5. The **Rockwell Hardness Number (HR)** is read directly off the dial — *no manual calculation needed*. **Why Preliminary Load?** Without it, surface roughness, dirt, or non-parallel surfaces would produce wildly varying readings. The minor load is a clever engineering "zeroing" trick. **Why Different Scales?** Different materials require different indenters and loads. A diamond Brale would shatter a soft aluminum sample, while a steel ball would not leave a measurable mark on a hardened tool steel. Hence **Rockwell scales** (A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, K, etc.). ## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is your exam lifeline.** Memorize this table. Every hardness problem in KTU ESE reduces to picking the correct row and plugging in numbers. Note: The vertical bar character is written as `\vert` in the table to avoid Markdown parser conflicts. | Test | Hardness Number (Definition) | Formula | Indenter | Standard Loads | Dwell Time | Key Range | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Brinell (HB)** | Load / Curved Surface Area of Indent | $HB = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}$ | Hardened steel / WC ball, $D$ = 10, 5, 2.5, 1 mm | 500, 1500, 3000 kgf (steel); 500 kgf (soft metals) | 10–30 s (harder = longer) | Soft to medium metals ($HB \le 450$ for steel ball) | | **Vickers (HV)** | Load / Projected Contact Area of Indent | $HV = \dfrac{2P \sin(\theta/2)}{d^2} = \dfrac{1.8544 \, P}{d^2}$ | Square diamond pyramid, $\theta = 136°$ | 1 gf – 100 kgf (micro: < 1 kgf) | 10–15 s | All metals, ceramics, thin sheets, case-hardened layers | | **Rockwell – C (HRC)** | Direct dial reading (depth-based) | $HRC = 100 - \dfrac{e}{0.002 \text{ mm}}$ | Diamond Brale cone (120° apex, 0.2 mm tip radius) | Major: 150 kgf; Minor: 10 kgf | ~10 s | Hardened steels, carbides ($HRC \ge 20$) | | **Rockwell – B (HRB)** | Direct dial reading (depth-based) | $HRB = 130 - \dfrac{e}{0.002 \text{ mm}}$ | Steel ball, 1/16 inch (1.588 mm) | Major: 100 kgf; Minor: 10 kgf | ~10 s | Soft steels, brass, aluminum ($HRB \le 100$) | | **Rockwell – A (HRA)** | Direct dial reading (depth-based) | $HRA = 100 - \dfrac{e}{0.002 \text{ mm}}$ | Diamond Brale cone | Major: 60 kgf; Minor: 10 kgf | ~10 s | Very hard materials, cemented carbides, thin sheets | | **Mohs (Scratch)** | Scratch resistance rating 1–10 | Comparison with 10 reference minerals | Mineral pick / fingernail | None | Manual | Mineralogy, ceramics, qualitative ranking | | **Knoop (HK)** | Load / Projected Area of long-diagonal indent | $HK = \dfrac{14.229 \, P}{L^2}$ | Elongated rhombic diamond pyramid | 25 gf – 5 kgf (micro range) | 10–20 s | Micro-hardness, brittle materials, coatings, fracture toughness | **Universal Constants to Remember:** - $\pi \approx 3.1416$ — but in Brinell problems, use $\pi$ symbolically. - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (only if you must convert kgf → N). - $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$. - The factor **1.8544** in Vickers comes from $2 \sin(136°/2) = 2 \sin(68°) = 1.8544$. - The factor **14.229** in Knoop comes from the long-diagonal rhombic geometry. ## 2.3 The Load Ratio Principle (Brinell) — A Critical KTU Concept > [!NOTE] > **George's Empirical Rule:** For Brinell tests to give consistent, comparable BHN values, the load-to-diameter-squared ratio must be **constant for a given material class**. $$\frac{P}{D^2} = \text{constant}$$ | Material Class | Recommended $P/D^2$ Ratio (kgf/mm²) | |---|---| | Steel and hard alloys | 30 | | Cast iron | 10 | | Copper, brass, soft bronzes | 5 | | Aluminum, lead, tin, magnesium | 2.5 | **Real-world utility:** If you want to test a small steel sample and can only fit a 5 mm ball, the load must be reduced from 3000 kgf (for 10 mm ball) to $P = 30 \times 5^2 = 750$ kgf. Same BHN will be obtained. ## 2.4 The Famous Hardness–Strength Correlation (KTU Hot Topic) > [!TIP] > **For Steels, the empirical Tabor–Meyer relation holds:** > > $$UTS (\text{MPa}) \approx 3.45 \times HB$$ > > This means if a steel measures 200 HB, its ultimate tensile strength is approximately $3.45 \times 200 = 690$ MPa. This correlation is hugely useful for quality control of incoming stock. A similar but more conservative relation: $$\sigma_y \approx \frac{HV}{3} \quad (\text{approx. for ductile metals})$$ This arises because plastic deformation in indentation is governed by the same dislocation mechanics as tensile yielding. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Brinell Hardness Formula **Starting Point:** The Brinell Hardness Number is *defined* as the applied load divided by the **lateral (curved) surface area** of the spherical-cap indentation. **Step 1 — Geometry of the Spherical Cap** When a sphere of diameter $D$ indents a flat surface and creates a circular indent of diameter $d$, the *depth of the indent* $h$ is: $$h = \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2} = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$ *Why?* The sphere's center is at height $D/2$ above the original surface. After indentation, the chord of the sphere at the surface level is at depth $h$ below the original surface. By Pythagoras on the right triangle (sphere center → indent edge → indent bottom), the distance from sphere center to indent edge is $D/2$, and the horizontal half-chord is $d/2$. Therefore the vertical distance from sphere center down to the indent bottom is $\sqrt{(D/2)^2 - (d/2)^2}$. Subtracting from $D/2$ gives $h$. **Step 2 — Curved Surface Area of a Spherical Cap** The curved lateral surface area of a spherical cap of height $h$ cut from a sphere of diameter $D$ is: $$A = \pi \, D \, h$$ *Why?* Standard geometric result for a spherical cap: $A = 2 \pi R h$ where $R = D/2$, so $A = 2 \pi (D/2) h = \pi D h$. ✓ **Step 3 — Substitute the Expression for $h$** $$A = \pi D \cdot \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} = \frac{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Definition of HB** $$HB = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{\dfrac{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}{2}}$$ $$\boxed{HB = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}}$$ **Numerical Simplification Trick (for KTU exams):** A common approximation when $d \ll D$ (i.e., shallow indents, $h$ is small): $$h \approx \frac{d^2}{4D}$$ Substituting back into $A = \pi D h$: $$A \approx \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$ This gives the *simpler* (but approximate) Brinell formula: $$HB \approx \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$ This is actually the **projected area** formula, and is acceptable for *small indents* ($d < D/3$). ## 3.2 Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Formula **Step 1 — Geometry of the Diamond Pyramid Indent** The Vickers indenter is a square-based pyramid with an included face angle of $\theta = 136°$ between *opposite* faces. The half-angle from the vertical axis to a face is $136°/2 = 68°$. When the pyramid penetrates to a depth $h$, the diagonal of the square base indent is $d$. The relationship between $d$ and $h$ comes from the right-triangle cross-section: $$\tan(68°) = \frac{d/2}{h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = 2h \tan(68°)$$ Alternatively, $h = \dfrac{d}{2 \tan(68°)} = \dfrac{d}{2 \tan(68°)}$. **Step 2 — Projected Area of the Square Indent** The square indent has diagonal $d$, so its side length is $a = d / \sqrt{2}$. $$A_{\text{projected}} = a^2 = \left(\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 = \frac{d^2}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Relate to Pyramid Depth and Apply the Definition** The depth of the pyramid is: $$h = \frac{d/2}{\tan(68°)} = \frac{d}{2 \tan(68°)}$$ So the projected area can also be written as: $$A = \frac{d^2}{2} = h^2 \cdot 2 \tan^2(68°)$$ But the cleanest form uses the diagonal directly. The Vickers definition is $HV = P / A$: $$HV = \frac{P}{d^2 / 2} = \frac{2P}{d^2}$$ **Step 4 — The Standard 1.8544 Factor** In conventional engineering practice, the face angle of 136° is used to introduce a numerical constant. From the full slanted-area definition: $$A_{\text{slanted}} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)} = \frac{d^2}{1.8544}$$ Therefore, if we use the **slanted surface area** (not the projected area) — which is the *true* contact area between indenter and specimen: $$HV = \frac{P}{A_{\text{slanted}}} = \frac{1.8544 \, P}{d^2}$$ $$\boxed{HV = \frac{1.8544 \, P}{d^2}}$$ where $P$ is in **kgf**, $d$ is in **mm**, and $HV$ is dimensionless (kgf/mm²). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Pitfall Alert:** Many textbooks differ on whether Vickers uses *projected area* or *slanted area*. The **official Vickers standard (ISO 6507)** uses the *slanted contact area*, yielding the $1.8544 \, P / d^2$ formula. KTU examiners will use this convention. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Rockwell Hardness Number Rockwell hardness is defined *experimentally* by depth measurement, not by a closed-form geometric formula. The derivation is therefore an operational one: **Step 1 — Apply Minor Load (10 kgf)** The indenter sinks a small depth $e_1$ into the surface. This depth is *zeroed* on the dial — it is our reference point. (Eliminates surface roughness and elastic deflection of the testing machine.) **Step 2 — Apply Major Load (60, 100, or 150 kgf)** The indenter sinks an *additional* depth $e_2$ (elastic + plastic). The total depth is $e_1 + e_2$. **Step 3 — Remove Major Load (Minor Load Still On)** The elastic portion of $e_2$ recovers, leaving only the *permanent plastic depth* increment. The final depth below the reference is: $$e = e_{\text{plastic}} = e_2 - e_2^{\text{elastic}}$$ **Step 4 — Convert Depth to a Hardness Number** For the **C scale (diamond Brale, 150 kgf major load):** $$HRC = 100 - \frac{e}{0.002 \text{ mm}}$$ The constant 100 is chosen so that a perfectly hard material ($e = 0$) reads 100, and a soft material gives a smaller number. The unit 0.002 mm is one *Rockwell unit*. For the **B scale (1/16" steel ball, 100 kgf major load):** $$HRB = 130 - \frac{e}{0.002 \text{ mm}}$$ The constant 130 is used because the steel-ball indent is shallower for the same material, so the number starts higher. > [!NOTE] > **No mathematical derivation of $e$ exists** — it is *measured directly* by the depth-measuring dial on the Rockwell machine. The "formula" is essentially a calibrated scale that converts depth to a dimensionless number. ## 3.4 Worked Analytical Examples (KTU Exam Practice) ### Example 1: Brinell Hardness Calculation **Given:** A Brinell test on a steel sample uses a 10 mm hardened steel ball, an applied load of 3000 kgf, and the measured indent diameter is 4.5 mm. Find the BHN. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} D &= 10 \text{ mm} \\ P &= 3000 \text{ kgf} \\ d &= 4.5 \text{ mm} \\ D^2 - d^2 &= 100 - 20.25 = 79.75 \text{ mm}^2 \\ \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} &= \sqrt{79.75} \approx 8.9306 \text{ mm} \\ D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} &= 10 - 8.9306 = 1.0694 \text{ mm} \\ \pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}) &= 3.1416 \times 10 \times 1.0694 \approx 33.598 \\ HB &= \frac{2 \times 3000}{33.598} \approx \frac{6000}{33.598} \approx 178.6 \text{ HB} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $HB \approx 178.6$ (or rounded to 179). **Check the load ratio:** $P/D^2 = 3000/100 = 30$ ✓ (correct for steel). ### Example 2: Vickers Hardness from a 5 kgf Load **Given:** A Vickers test on a hardened tool steel uses a 5 kgf load. The two diagonals of the indent are measured as $d_1 = 0.082$ mm and $d_2 = 0.080$ mm. Find HV. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} d_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.082 + 0.080}{2} = 0.081 \text{ mm} \\ d_{\text{avg}}^2 &= (0.081)^2 = 0.006561 \text{ mm}^2 \\ HV &= \frac{1.8544 \times P}{d^2} = \frac{1.8544 \times 5}{0.006561} \\ HV &= \frac{9.272}{0.006561} \approx 1413.2 \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $HV \approx 1413$. This corresponds to a very hard tool steel (matches hardened high-speed steel). ### Example 3: Tensile Strength from Brinell Hardness **Given:** A batch of steel components has an average BHN of 220. Estimate the UTS. **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} UTS (\text{MPa}) &\approx 3.45 \times HB \\ UTS &\approx 3.45 \times 220 = 759 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $UTS \approx 759$ MPa. ### Example 4: Required Load for a Smaller Ball (Brinell) **Given:** A soft brass sample must be tested using a 5 mm Brinell ball (instead of the standard 10 mm). What load should be applied to maintain the standard $P/D^2 = 5$ ratio for brass? **Solution:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{P}{D^2} &= 5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2 \\ P &= 5 \times D^2 = 5 \times (5)^2 = 5 \times 25 = 125 \text{ kgf} \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $P = 125$ kgf. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Brinell Hardness Test — Indenter Geometry and Indent Profile The schematic below captures the **physical relationships** between the indenter ball, the indent impression, and the measured parameters $D$ and $d$. ```mermaid graph TD A[Brinell Test Setup] --> B[Hardened Steel or WC Ball
Diameter D = 10 mm standard] A --> C[Apply Load P
500, 1500, or 3000 kgf] A --> D[Dwell Time 10 to 30 seconds] A --> E[Remove Load] E --> F[Measure Indent Diameter d
Using Calibrated Microscope] F --> G[Apply Formula
HB = 2P divided by pi times D times D minus sqrt of D squared minus d squared] G --> H[Report Brinell Hardness Number HB] B --> I[Indent Geometry
Spherical Cap of Depth h] I --> J[h equals D divided by 2 minus sqrt of D squared over 2 minus d squared over 2] I --> K[Curved Area A equals pi times D times h] I --> L[Load Ratio Rule
P divided by D squared is constant for material class] ``` **Physical Sketch (Conceptual):** ``` Applied Load P (kgf) ↓ ↓ ↓ ┌──────────────┐ / ╲ / ╲ ← Hardened steel ball / ╲_______/ ╲ (diameter D) / |d/2| ╲ / |←──→| ╲ ← Indent diameter d / ___ ╲ / / ╲ ╲ ──────────/─────╲─────────── ← Original flat specimen surface ↕ h ← Indent depth h ───────────────────────────────── ▔▔▔▔▔▔▔ ← Plastic zone in specimen ``` ## 4.2 Vickers Hardness Test — Diamond Pyramid Indent The Vickers indenter produces a **square-shaped indent** with diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$. The face angle of 136° is the key geometric driver. ```mermaid graph TD A1[Vickers Test Setup] --> B1[Diamond Pyramid Indenter
Face Angle 136 degrees] A1 --> C1[Apply Load P
1 gf to 100 kgf] A1 --> D1[Dwell Time 10 to 15 seconds] A1 --> E1[Remove Load] E1 --> F1[Measure Two Diagonals d1 and d2

HV equals 1.8544 times P divided by d squared] G1 --> H1[Report Vickers Hardness Number HV] B1 --> I1[Geometry of Pyramid
