Square base side a equals d over sqrt of 2] I1 --> J1[Projected Area equals d squared over 2] I1 --> K1[Slanted Area equals d squared over 1.8544] I1 --> L1[Geometric Similarity
HV is independent of load magnitude] ``` **3D Conceptual View of Vickers Indent:** ``` Diamond Pyramid Indenter ╱▔▔▔╲ ╱▔▔▔▔▔╲ ← Square pyramid, face angle 136° ╱▔▔▔▔▔▔▔╲ ╱___▔▔▔▔___╲ ───────┼─────── ← Square base of indent │ d (diagonal of square) Top View of the Indent Impression: ┌────────────────┐ │ │ │ ╲ ╱ │ ← Diagonal d1 │ ╲ ╱ │ (one direction) │ ╲ ╱ │ │ ╲╱ │ │ ╱╲ │ │ ╱ ╲ │ ← Diagonal d2 │ ╱ ╲ │ (perpendicular direction) │ ╱ ╲ │ │ │ └────────────────┘ ``` ## 4.3 Rockwell Hardness Test — Block-Level Functional Architecture The Rockwell test is unique because of its **two-stage loading** (minor → major → remove major). The schematic below captures this *operational state machine*. ```mermaid graph TD R1[Start: Position Specimen] --> R2[Apply Minor Load 10 kgf] R2 --> R3[Wait 2 to 3 seconds for seating] R3 --> R4[Zero the Depth Dial at reference point] R4 --> R5[Apply Major Load 60 to 100 to 150 kgf
Depends on selected Rockwell Scale] R5 --> R6[Wait for Dwell Time about 10 seconds] R6 --> R7[Remove Major Load Only
Minor Load Remains Applied] R7 --> R8[Read Final Depth e in mm] R8 --> R9[Compute Hardness Number using Scale Specific Formula] R9 --> R10A[Scale C: HRC equals 100 minus e divided by 0.002 mm] R9 --> R10B[Scale B: HRB equals 130 minus e divided by 0.002 mm] R9 --> R10C[Scale A: HRA equals 100 minus e divided by 0.002 mm] R10A --> R11[Report HR Value] R10B --> R11 R10C --> R11 R5 --> R12{Choose Indenter and Load} R12 --> R13A[Diamond Brale plus 150 kgf gives Scale C] R12 --> R13B[Steel Ball 1 over 16 inch plus 100 kgf gives Scale B] R12 --> R13C[Diamond Brale plus 60 kgf gives Scale A] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Decision Flowchart — Which Hardness Test to Use? ```mermaid graph TD S1[Need to Measure Hardness] --> S2{Type of Material?} S2 -->|Very Hard Carbide or Tool Steel| T1[Use Rockwell C Scale or Vickers] S2 -->|Soft Aluminum or Copper Alloy| T2[Use Brinell or Rockwell B or F] S2 -->|Thin Sheet or Coating| T3[Use Vickers Micro or Knoop] S2 -->|Small Heat Treated Region| T4[Use Vickers Micro Hardness] S2 -->|Mineral or Ceramic Ranking| T5[Use Mohs Scale] S2 -->|Large Forging on Site| T6[Use Leeb Rebound Test] S1 --> S3{Required Precision?} S3 -->|High Laboratory Precision| P1[Use Vickers HV] S3 -->|Quick Industrial QC| P2[Use Rockwell HR] S3 -->|Approximate Bulk Strength| P3[Use Brinell HB] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short-Answer Practice ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]` **Define hardness. List the three principal indentation hardness tests and state one advantage of each.** **Model Answer:** Hardness is defined as the resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation, typically measured by the size of an indent produced under a known load applied by a standardized indenter. The three principal indentation tests are: 1. **Brinell Hardness Test (HB):** Uses a hardened steel ball indenter (typically 10 mm diameter) with loads up to 3000 kgf. *Advantage:* The large ball indenter gives a representative average hardness over a wide area, making it suitable for materials with coarse grains or heterogeneous microstructures (e.g., cast iron, forgings). 2. **Vickers Hardness Test (HV):** Uses a square-based diamond pyramid with 136° face angle. *Advantage:* HV values are **load-independent** due to geometric similarity, and the same scale can be used from very soft (HV = 5) to extremely hard (HV > 1000) materials, including ceramics and thin sheets. 3. **Rockwell Hardness Test (HR):** Uses depth measurement directly with a diamond Brale or steel ball. *Advantage:* The hardness number is **read directly from a dial** with no optical measurement required, making it the fastest, most operationally convenient test for high-volume production quality control. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]` **State the load-ratio principle in Brinell hardness testing. Why is it important?** **Model Answer:** The load-ratio principle states that for consistent, comparable Brinell Hardness Numbers, the ratio of the applied load $P$ to the square of the ball diameter $D$ must be **constant for a given material class**. $$\frac{P}{D^2} = \text{constant (per material class)}$$ Recommended values: - Steel and hard alloys: 30 kgf/mm² - Cast iron: 10 kgf/mm² - Copper, brass: 5 kgf/mm² - Aluminum, lead, magnesium: 2.5 kgf/mm² **Importance:** This principle ensures that when comparing BHN values across samples of different sizes (e.g., a small laboratory specimen vs. a large production part) tested with different ball diameters, the results are directly comparable. It also prevents two common errors: using too small a load (which gives too shallow, unreliable indents) and using too large a load (which destroys the indenter or causes cracking in brittle materials). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Brinell hardness test** procedure. Derive the expression for the Brinell Hardness Number. State two limitations of this test. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Brinell test is performed on a sample of cast iron with a ball of diameter 5 mm and a load of 750 kgf. The measured indent diameter is 1.5 mm. Calculate the BHN. If the same sample is tested with a 10 mm ball, what load should be used to give the same BHN? Comment on the relationship between hardness and ultimate tensile strength for this material. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Brinell Hardness Test — Procedure, Derivation, and Limitations:** **Procedure:** 1. The specimen surface is ground flat and polished. 2. A **hardened steel ball** of diameter $D$ (commonly 10 mm) is brought into firm contact with the specimen. 3. A **standard load $P$** is applied smoothly (without shock) perpendicular to the surface, held for a fixed **dwell time** of 10–30 s. 4. The load is removed; the indent is a circular impression of diameter $d$. 5. The **diameter $d$** is measured using a low-power microscope with a graduated scale (typically to ±0.05 mm precision). Two readings are taken at right angles and averaged. 6. The Brinell Hardness Number is calculated from the formula. **Derivation of HB Formula:** The Brinell Hardness Number is defined as the applied load divided by the **curved lateral surface area** of the spherical-cap indent. Geometry of the indent (spherical cap of height $h$, sphere of diameter $D$): $$h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}$$ The curved lateral surface area of a spherical cap: $$A = \pi D h = \frac{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}{2}$$ Applying the definition: $$\boxed{HB = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right)}}$$ **Two Limitations of Brinell Test:** 1. **Restricted to soft-medium materials:** The hardened steel ball indenter itself deforms when testing materials harder than ~450 HB. For hard steels, carbides, or ceramics, tungsten carbide balls are used (limited to ~650 HB), and beyond that Brinell becomes unreliable — Vickers or Rockwell C must be used. 2. **Slow and not suitable for very small or very thin specimens:** The large indent (typically 2–6 mm diameter) destroys the surface, requires a substantial flat area, and is unsuitable for thin sheets, small parts, or case-hardened layers. Optical measurement of $d$ also introduces human reading error. --- **(b) Numerical Problem Solution:** **Part 1 — Calculate BHN:** $$\begin{aligned} D &= 5 \text{ mm} \\ P &= 750 \text{ kgf} \\ d &= 1.5 \text{ mm} \\ D^2 - d^2 &= 25 - 2.25 = 22.75 \text{ mm}^2 \\ \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} &= \sqrt{22.75} \approx 4.7697 \text{ mm} \\ D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} &= 5 - 4.7697 = 0.2303 \text{ mm} \\ \pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}) &= 3.1416 \times 5 \times 0.2303 \approx 3.617 \\ HB &= \frac{2 \times 750}{3.617} = \frac{1500}{3.617} \approx 414.8 \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $HB \approx 415$. **Check load ratio:** $P/D^2 = 750/25 = 30$ ✓ (this is the *steel* ratio, but cast iron typically uses 10 — so this is a slightly higher load than the typical cast iron standard, but the calculation proceeds correctly). **Part 2 — Load for 10 mm ball to give same BHN:** For the same BHN, the **load-ratio principle** requires $P/D^2$ to remain the same: $$\frac{P_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{new}}^2} = \frac{750}{25} = 30 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$ $$P_{\text{new}} = 30 \times (10)^2 = 30 \times 100 = 3000 \text{ kgf}$$ **Answer:** $P_{\text{new}} = 3000$ kgf. **Part 3 — Hardness to UTS Relation:** For steels (and to a reasonable approximation, cast irons), the empirical Tabor relation gives: $$UTS \text{ (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times HB$$ For our cast iron with $HB \approx 415$: $$UTS \approx 3.45 \times 415 \approx 1432 \text{ MPa}$$ **Note on cast iron specifically:** Cast iron is a *brittle* material with graphite flakes/nodules acting as stress concentrators. The Tabor relation is *less accurate* for cast irons than for wrought steels because cast iron rarely reaches a true UTS in the conventional sense — it tends to fracture before necking. The estimate should be treated cautiously. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Vickers hardness test**. Derive the Vickers Hardness Number expression and explain why the 136° face angle was chosen. Compare Vickers and Rockwell hardness tests. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Vickers hardness test on a ceramic uses a load of 2 kgf. The measured diagonals are $d_1 = 0.105$ mm and $d_2 = 0.115$ mm. Calculate HV. Estimate the material's yield strength using the empirical Tabor relation. A subsequent Rockwell C test on a similar sample gives 62 HRC. Convert this to an approximate Vickers equivalent. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Vickers Hardness Test — Description, Derivation, and Comparison:** **Description:** 1. The specimen surface is ground and polished (mirror finish is preferable for low-load tests). 2. A **square-based diamond pyramid indenter** with face angle 136° (between opposite faces) is pressed into the surface. 3. A **load $P$** is applied smoothly — ranging from 25 gf (micro-hardness) to 100 kgf (macro-hardness). 4. The load is held for a fixed **dwell time** of 10–15 s. 5. The load is removed, leaving a **square-shaped indent** with two perpendicular diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$. 6. The two diagonals are measured optically, averaged to give $d = (d_1 + d_2)/2$, and HV is computed. **Derivation of HV Formula:** The face angle of 136° means the half-angle (between face and vertical axis) is 68°. The diagonal $d$ of the square indent and the indent depth $h$ are related by: $$\tan(68°) = \frac{d/2}{h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{d}{2 \tan(68°)}$$ The **projected (horizontal) contact area** is: $$A_{\text{projected}} = \frac{d^2}{2}$$ The **slanted contact area** (along the pyramid face, which is the *true* contact area between indenter and specimen) is: $$A_{\text{slanted}} = \frac{A_{\text{projected}}}{\sin(68°)} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)} = \frac{d^2}{1.8544}$$ Applying the Vickers definition (load / slanted area): $$\boxed{HV = \frac{P}{A_{\text{slanted}}} = \frac{1.8544 \, P}{d^2}}$$ **Why 136°?** The 136° face angle was *empirically chosen* so that for soft-to-medium hardness ranges, the **Vickers number numerically matches the Brinell number** (HB). This was a deliberate engineering choice for ease of cross-referencing older Brinell data. Additionally, this angle produces an indent that is geometrically similar across all load levels, making HV *load-independent*. **Vickers vs Rockwell — Comparison:** | Feature | Vickers (HV) | Rockwell (HR) | |---|---|---| | Indenter | Diamond pyramid (136°) | Diamond Brale *or* steel ball | | Measurement | Optical (diagonal) | Mechanical (depth dial) | | Speed | Slower (optical) | Fastest (direct reading) | | Range | 5 to 3000+ (all materials) | Scale-dependent (e.g., HRC: 20–70) | | Surface prep | Polished essential | Lightly ground acceptable | | Best for | Lab precision, micro-hardness, ceramics, thin layers | Production QC, large parts, fast checks | --- **(b) Numerical Problem Solution:** **Part 1 — Calculate HV:** $$\begin{aligned} d_1 &= 0.105 \text{ mm}, \quad d_2 = 0.115 \text{ mm} \\ d_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{0.105 + 0.115}{2} = 0.110 \text{ mm} \\ d_{\text{avg}}^2 &= (0.110)^2 = 0.0121 \text{ mm}^2 \\ P &= 2 \text{ kgf} \\ HV &= \frac{1.8544 \times 2}{0.0121} = \frac{3.7088}{0.0121} \approx 306.5 \end{aligned}$$ **Answer:** $HV \approx 307$. **Part 2 — Estimate Yield Strength:** Using the Tabor relation: $$\sigma_y \approx \frac{HV}{3} = \frac{306.5}{3} \approx 102.2 \text{ (in HV units, kgf/mm}^2\text{)}$$ Converting to MPa (1 kgf/mm² ≈ 9.81 MPa): $$\sigma_y \approx 102.2 \times 9.81 \approx 1003 \text{ MPa}$$ **Answer:** $\sigma_y \approx 1.0$ GPa. (This is consistent with a high-performance engineering ceramic such as partially-stabilized zirconia or hot-pressed silicon carbide.) **Part 3 — Convert 62 HRC to Vickers equivalent:** The empirical conversion (from ISO 18265 and ASTM E140 tables) gives: $$HV \approx 80 + 8 \times HRC \quad \text{(approximate, for } HRC = 20 \text{ to } 65\text{)}$$ $$HV \approx 80 + 8 \times 62 = 80 + 496 = 576 \text{ HV}$$ **Answer:** 62 HRC $\approx$ 576 HV. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 1.8544 factor in Vickers** — a one-mark deduction per error. > 2. **Mixing up the Brinell and Vickers indenter shapes** in sketches — examiners allocate at least 1 mark for a correct labeled diagram. > 3. **Using the wrong load ratio for non-steel materials** in Brinell — always state which material class applies. > 4. **Forgetting to average the two diagonals** in Vickers — a common 1-mark loss. > 5. **Using $UTS \approx 3.45 HB$ for non-ferrous metals** — this relation is calibrated for steels; for aluminum alloys, the multiplier is closer to 2.5–3.0. > 6. **Confusing the Rockwell minor load (10 kgf) with the major load (60/100/150 kgf)** — this is tested every year. > 7. **Not stating the dwell time** in a procedure question — examiners explicitly look for it. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardness Module — Last-Minute Revision Bullets (Read These Twice Before the Exam):** > > - **Definition:** Hardness = resistance to localized plastic deformation; measured by indenting the surface with a harder object under a known load. > > - **Brinell (HB):** Steel or WC ball, large area average, $HB = 2P / [\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$, max ~450 HB for steel ball, requires optical measurement of $d$, slow, used for castings/forgings. > > - **Vickers (HV):** Diamond pyramid (136°), load-independent, $HV = 1.8544 P / d^2$, works on all materials including ceramics, micro-hardness variant for thin layers, requires optical measurement. > > - **Rockwell (HR):** Depth-based, *direct dial reading*, two-stage loading (10 kgf minor + 60/100/150 kgf major), multiple scales (A, B, C, D…), fastest test, no optical measurement. > > - **Rockwell C (HRC):** Diamond Brale, 150 kgf major load — for hard steels. > - **Rockwell B (HRB):** 1/16" steel ball, 100 kgf major load — for soft metals. > - **Rockwell A (HRA):** Diamond Brale, 60 kgf major load — for very hard materials and thin sheets. > > - **Load Ratio Principle (Brinell):** $P/D^2$ = 30 (steel), 10 (cast iron), 5 (brass/copper), 2.5 (aluminum). > > - **UTS Correlation (Steels):** $UTS \text{ (MPa)} \approx 3.45 \times HB$. > > - **Yield Strength (Tabor):** $\sigma_y \approx HV/3$ in consistent units. > > - **Why 136° in Vickers?** Chosen to make HV ≈ HB in the common hardness range. > > - **Why preliminary load in Rockwell?** To zero out surface roughness and elastic seating errors — a *very* common 3-mark KTU question. > > - **Mohs Scale:** Scratch test, 1 (talc) to 10 (diamond), used for minerals, not metals. > > - **Knoop (HK):** Micro-hardness for brittle materials and coatings, elongated rhombic diamond, $HK = 14.229 P/L^2$ where $L$ is the long diagonal. > > - **Leeb / Shore:** Rebound (dynamic) hardness, used for large forges where portable testing is needed. > > - **Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$; $1 \text{ HV} \text{ (kgf/mm}^2) \approx 9.81 \text{ MPa}$. > > - **Common conversion benchmarks (memorize!):** 60 HRC ≈ 700 HV; 200 HB ≈ 700 MPa UTS for steel. > > - **Dwell Times:** Brinell 10–30 s (longer for harder materials), Vickers 10–15 s, Rockwell ~10 s.

topics.title: Impact Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impact Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Impact Loading?

**Impact loading** is a *dynamic*, *high-strain-rate* mechanical loading condition where a material is subjected to a sudden, concentrated force or stress over a very short time interval (typically $< 10^{-3}$ seconds). Unlike static or quasi-static loading, impact loading does not give the material time to redistribute stresses through plastic deformation, so the response is governed by the material's **notch toughness** rather than its static strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PCMET205):**
> *Impact properties* refer to the ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform *fracture* under a rapidly applied dynamic load before catastrophic failure occurs. It is quantified by the **impact energy** (in **Joules, J**) absorbed during fracture, measured using a standardized pendulum-type impact test (Charpy or Izod).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cricket Ball vs Bullet" Concept

Imagine you are catching a ball thrown gently at you — easy, the hand deforms and absorbs the energy. Now imagine a cricket ball thrown at 140 km/hr, or worse, a bullet — your hand will fracture almost instantly because it cannot deform fast enough.

| Scenario | Loading Type | Material Response | Property Tested |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Slow stretch of a rubber band | Quasi-static | Large plastic deformation | Ductility |
| Hammering a nail | **Impact** | Sudden fracture | **Toughness** |
| Dropping a glass beaker | **Impact** | Shatters (no time to deform) | **Brittleness** |
| Pressing a car body panel | Quasi-static | Bends slowly | Formability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A material that is *strong* (high UTS) is **NOT** automatically *tough* (high impact energy). Cast iron, for instance, has high strength but extremely low impact energy. This is the central paradox that impact testing resolves.

### 1.3 Why KTU Includes This Topic

Impact properties are governed under:
- **ASTM E23** — Standard Test Methods for Notched Bar Impact Testing of Metallic Materials
- **ISO 148-1:2016** — Metallic materials — Charpy pendulum impact test
- **IS 1757** — Indian Standard equivalent for Charpy impact test

These standards define specimen geometry, notch geometry, support span, and striker velocity to ensure *reproducibility* across laboratories worldwide — a critical aspect for KTU's outcome-based education focus on **standardized engineering practice**.

### 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

As per KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic maps to:
- **CO3:** *Characterize mechanical properties of engineering materials using standard testing procedures and interpret the data for engineering design decisions.*
- **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply) → L4 (Analyze)
- **Cognitive Skill Tested:** The ability to correlate microstructure (ferrite/pearlite/bainite/martensite content) with macroscopic impact energy and transition temperature behavior.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Principal Impact Tests

KTU syllabus specifically mandates both **Charpy V-Notch (CVN)** and **Izod** tests. Let us dissect their working principles.

#### 2.1.1 Charpy Impact Test

- The specimen is a **55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm** bar with a **45° V-notch of 2 mm depth and 0.25 mm root radius**.
- It is placed as a **simply supported beam** (supported on anvils at a span of **40 mm**).
- The pendulum strikes the specimen on the side *opposite* the notch.
- The notch creates a **triaxial stress state** at the root, simulating the worst-case stress concentration.

#### 2.1.2 Izod Impact Test

- The specimen is again **55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm** with a **V-notch or U-notch**.
- It is clamped as a **cantilever beam** (one end fixed, the other free).
- The pendulum strikes the specimen on the *same side* as the notch.
- The notch is typically **22.4 mm from the top of the specimen** (clamp line).

### 2.2 The Physics of Energy Absorption

The fundamental principle is **conservation of energy**:

> The difference between the potential energy of the pendulum at its release height and its swing-height *after* fracturing the specimen equals the **energy absorbed in fracture**.

This is conceptually similar to a swinging clock pendulum — we measure how much "swing" the pendulum loses due to fracture work.

### 2.3 The Ductile-to-Brittle Transition (DBTT)

This is **the single most important concept** in impact testing for KTU examinations.

**Definition (DBTT):** The temperature range over which a material's fracture mode changes from **ductile** (energy-absorbing, with significant plastic deformation) to **brittle** (low-energy, cleavage fracture) is called the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** BCC metals (ferritic steels, tungsten, chromium) exhibit a *sharp* DBTT. FCC metals (austenitic stainless steel, copper, aluminum) show *no* DBTT and remain ductile even at cryogenic temperatures. This is why **LNG tanker ships use austenitic stainless steel** but **Arctic pipelines use special low-carbon nickel steels**.

### 2.4 Notch Sensitivity

A material is **notch sensitive** if its impact energy drops drastically in the presence of a stress concentrator. The **Notch Sensitivity Ratio (NSR)** is defined as:

$$\text{NSR} = \frac{\text{Impact Energy of Notched Specimen}}{\text{Impact Energy of Unnotched Specimen}}$$

- NSR $\to 1$: Notch **insensitive** (ductile FCC materials)
- NSR $\to 0$: Highly notch sensitive (brittle BCC materials at low T)

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear almost every year in Part A or Part B derivations.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|----------------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $E_{impact} = mg(h_1 - h_2)$ | $m$ = pendulum mass, $g$ = gravity, $h_1, h_2$ = initial & final heights | **J (Joules)** | Direct energy calculation |
| 2 | $E_{impact} = \frac{1}{2}m v^2 - \frac{1}{2}m v_f^2$ | $v$ = striker velocity at impact, $v_f$ = velocity after fracture | J | Velocity-based energy |
| 3 | $\text{NSR} = \dfrac{E_{notched}}{E_{unnotched}}$ | Energy ratio | Dimensionless | Notch sensitivity |
| 4 | $K_{IC} \approx \sqrt{2 E \sigma_{ys} \left(\dfrac{\pi a}{1-\nu^2}\right)}$ | Fracture toughness (Irwin) | **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** | Linear elastic fracture mechanics |
| 5 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{2P L}{b h^2}$ | Charpy bending stress | MPa | Peak stress at notch root |
| 6 | $T_{DBTT} \propto \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{\dot{\varepsilon}}\right)$ | Strain-rate sensitivity | K | Shift in transition with rate |
| 7 | $\text{CVN Lateral Expansion} \propto \text{Ductility}$ | Empirical correlation | mm | Quality control of steel |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of Impact Testing

- **Selection of materials** for low-temperature service (Arctic, LNG, space).
- **Quality control** in steel manufacturing — Charpy tests are mandatory per ASME Section VIII for pressure vessels.
- **Welded joint evaluation** — HAZ (Heat Affected Zone) toughness is critical in shipbuilding.
- **Failure analysis** — the classic 1944 *Liberty Ship* failures (fractured in cold North Atlantic) led to mandatory Charpy testing in shipbuilding codes worldwide.
- **Heat treatment verification** — tempering embrittlement is detected by Charpy tests.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Absorbed by Pendulum Impact Tester

**Setup:** A pendulum of mass $m$ is released from initial height $h_1$. After fracturing the specimen, it swings up to final height $h_2$. The striker velocity at the lowest point is $v$.

**Step 1:** Apply conservation of mechanical energy between release point and bottom of swing.

$$mgh_1 = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

$$\Rightarrow v = \sqrt{2 g h_1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply conservation of energy between the bottom (post-fracture) and the final height $h_2$.

$$\frac{1}{2} m v_f^2 = m g h_2$$

$$\Rightarrow v_f = \sqrt{2 g h_2}$$

**Step 3:** Energy lost by the pendulum = Energy absorbed by the specimen during fracture.

$$E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 - \frac{1}{2} m v_f^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions for $v$ and $v_f$ into the energy loss equation.

$$E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2} m (2 g h_1) - \frac{1}{2} m (2 g h_2)$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the algebraic terms to obtain the compact height form.

$$E_{absorbed} = m g h_1 - m g h_2$$

**Step 6:** Final compact form of the absorbed energy.

$$E_{absorbed} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

This is the **fundamental energy-balance equation** used by every commercial impact testing machine. The dial gauge on the machine is calibrated to read this difference directly.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Concentration at the Notch Root

For a Charpy specimen (bending beam with notch), the nominal stress at the notch root is enhanced by a **theoretical stress concentration factor $K_t$**.

**Step 1:** Consider a beam of width $b$, thickness $B$, span $L$ with a V-notch of depth $a$ on the tension side, under three-point bend load $P$.

$$\sigma_{nominal} = \frac{M c}{I} = \frac{(P L / 4) (B/2)}{b B^3 / 12}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the geometric terms by cancelling $B$ in numerator and denominator.

$$\sigma_{nominal} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b B^2}$$

**Step 3:** The peak elastic stress at the notch root is the nominal stress amplified by the stress concentration factor.

$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the simplified nominal stress expression into the peak stress equation.

$$\sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \frac{3 P L}{2 b B^2}$$

**Step 5:** For a sharp V-notch ($0.25$ mm root radius), $K_t$ typically ranges from $3$ to $8$ in steels. Hence, **even modest loads create local stresses that exceed the yield strength**, forcing plastic deformation at the notch root.

This is precisely *why* the notch is used — it localizes failure to a known site, ensuring the measured energy is reproducible and represents the *worst-case* service condition.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Impact Energy

| Factor | Effect on Impact Energy | Physical Reason |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------|
| ↓ Temperature | Decreases (sharply in BCC) | Reduced dislocation mobility, cleavage becomes dominant |
| ↑ Strain rate | Decreases (sharps DBTT) | Less time for plastic flow, adiabatic heating reduced |
| ↑ Notch acuity | Decreases | Higher $K_t$, triaxial stress state |
| ↑ Grain size | Decreases (below DBTT) | Larger grains → longer cleavage paths (Griffith) |
| ↑ Carbon content | Decreases | Carbide network, increased hardness, lower ductility |
| ↑ Interstitial (N, O) | Decreases | Pin dislocations, embrittle ferrite |
| Refinement by alloying (Ni, Mn) | Increases (lowers DBTT) | Promotes ductile fracture, suppresses cleavage |
| Tempering (post-quench) | Increases | Relieves residual stress, spheroidizes carbides |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pendulum Impact Tester

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Release Mechanism] --> B[Pendulum at Height h1]
    B --> C{Specimen Placement}
    C -->|Charpy| D[Simply Supported Beam]
    C -->|Izod| E[Clamped Cantilever Beam]
    D --> F[Striker Impact]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Specimen Fracture Event]
    G --> H[Energy Absorption E = mg(h1 - h2)]
    H --> I[Analog Dial Gauge]
    I --> J[Digital Encoder Optional]
    J --> K[Result: CVN in Joules]
    H --> L[Fracture Surface Inspection]
    L --> M{Shear vs Cleavage Area}
    M -->|>%80 Shear| N[Ductile Fracture]
    M -->|<%20 Shear| O[Brittle Fracture]
    M -->|Intermediate| P[Transition Region]
```

### 4.2 Charpy vs Izod — Specimen Loading Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Charpy Test Geometry] --> A1[Support Span = 40 mm]
    A1 --> A2[Striker Behind Notch]
    A2 --> A3[Simply Supported Beam Bending]
    B[Izod Test Geometry] --> B1[Clamp at Top 22.4 mm]
    B1 --> B2[Striker Above Notch]
    B2 --> B3[Cantilever Beam Bending]
    A3 --> C[Both Measure Energy in J]
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Compare with Specified Minimum CVN]
    D --> E{Material Acceptance}
    E -->|Yes| F[Approved for Service]
    E -->|No| G[Rejected Reheat Treat]
```

### 4.3 Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Curve Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impact Energy J] --> B[Plot vs Temperature C]
    B --> C{Region Identification}
    C -->|Low T| D[Lower Shelf Energy]
    C -->|Mid T| E[Transition Zone]
    C -->|High T| F[Upper Shelf Energy]
    D --> G[Brittle Cleavage Fracture]
    E --> H[Mixed Mode Fracture]
    F --> I[Ductile Microvoid Coalescence]
    G --> J[Identify DBTT at Average Energy]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Set Service Temp > DBTT + Safety Margin]
```

### 4.4 Energy Flow During Pendulum Impact

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Potential Energy mgh1] --> B[Kinetic Energy 0.5mv2 at Impact]
    B --> C[Specimen Fracture Work]
    B --> D[Sound and Heat Losses]
    B --> E[Vibration Losses in Frame]
    C --> F[Final Swing Energy 0.5mvf2]
    C --> G[Plastic Deformation of Specimen]
    C --> H[New Surface Creation Cracks]
    D --> I[Final mgh2]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> J[Impact Energy Recorded]
    H --> J
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define impact toughness. Differentiate between Charpy and Izod impact tests in terms of specimen support and notch position.*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

**Impact toughness** is the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation under a sudden, dynamic load before fracturing. It is measured in **Joules (J)** and is a true indicator of *service performance* under shock loading, unlike static strength measures.

**Charpy Test:**
- Specimen: 55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm with 2 mm deep 45° V-notch
- Support: **Simply supported beam** with span 40 mm
- Striker hits **opposite side** of notch
- Specimen clamped at one end (cantilever)
- Notch on the same side as impact

**Izod Test:**
- Specimen dimensions similar
- Support: **Cantilever** — fixed at one end, free at other
- Striker hits **same side** as notch
- Notch located 22.4 mm from clamp line

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently write "Charpy is simply supported, Izod is cantilever" but forget to mention the **notch orientation** relative to the striker. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this omission. Always include *both* support type and notch-striker relationship.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)? Why is it more pronounced in BCC metals than in FCC metals?*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

**DBTT** is the temperature below which a material's fracture mode changes from ductile (energy-absorbing) to brittle (low-energy cleavage) under impact loading.

**Why BCC > FCC susceptibility:**

In **BCC metals** (ferritic steels, Cr, W, Mo at low T):
- The Peierls–Nabarro stress is *very high*, so dislocation motion is **thermally activated**.
- At low T, screw dislocations cannot glide easily → dislocations are "locked".
- Plastic flow is suppressed → fracture occurs by **cleavage** along $\{100\}$ planes.
- The yield strength rises sharply at low T, but fracture strength rises only slightly.
- When yield stress > fracture stress → brittle fracture.

In **FCC metals** (Cu, Al, austenitic SS, Ni):
- Dislocation motion is **not** thermally activated (no Peierls barrier).
- Multiple slip systems remain active even at cryogenic temperatures.
- Cleavage is essentially impossible in FCC due to slip geometry.
- Therefore, FCC metals remain ductile at all temperatures and show **no DBTT**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing "BCC has higher DBTT because it has a body-centered structure" is **incomplete**. Examiners expect the *Peierls stress* and *dislocation mobility* argument. Always link structure to *mechanism* for full 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]*

**(a)** *With a neat sketch, describe the Charpy V-notch impact test. Explain how the impact energy is measured using the pendulum principle.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A Charpy impact test was carried out on a steel specimen. The pendulum had a mass of 22.7 kg, was released from a height of 1.5 m, and rose to a height of 1.35 m after fracturing the specimen. Calculate: (i) Velocity of the pendulum just before impact, (ii) Velocity just after impact, (iii) Energy absorbed by the specimen in Joules. State the significance of this energy value.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Working Principle:** The Charpy V-notch impact test uses a pendulum of known mass released from a fixed height. The pendulum swings down, strikes a notched specimen held as a simply supported beam, and fractures it. The energy absorbed in fracture is calculated from the difference in swing heights.

**Procedure:**
1. Place the V-notched specimen (55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm, 2 mm notch depth) on the anvils with span 40 mm.
2. Raise the pendulum to the latched position (height $h_1$).
3. Release the pendulum; it swings down and strikes the specimen opposite the notch.
4. The specimen fractures; the pendulum continues to a lower final height $h_2$.
5. The dial gauge reads the energy lost directly.

**Equation Used:**

$$E_{absorbed} = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

This is the **measured impact toughness** in Joules.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:**
- $m = 22.7$ kg
- $h_1 = 1.5$ m
- $h_2 = 1.35$ m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Velocity just before impact:**

Using conservation of energy from release point to bottom of swing:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = m g h_1$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 g h_1}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}$$

$$v = \sqrt{29.43}$$

$$v = 5.425 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Velocity just after impact:**

The kinetic energy after fracture is converted to potential energy at height $h_2$:

$$\frac{1}{2} m v_f^2 = m g h_2$$

$$v_f = \sqrt{2 g h_2}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$v_f = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.35}$$

$$v_f = \sqrt{26.487}$$

$$v_f = 5.147 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Energy absorbed by the specimen:**

Using the height-based form:

$$E = m g (h_1 - h_2)$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$E = 22.7 \times 9.81 \times (1.5 - 1.35)$$

$$E = 22.7 \times 9.81 \times 0.15$$

$$E = 33.40 \text{ J}$$

**Significance:** An absorbed energy of **33.4 J** at room temperature indicates a **moderately tough steel**, suitable for general structural applications. For critical low-temperature service, the test would need to be repeated at sub-zero temperatures to confirm that the DBTT is below the design service temperature. A value below 20 J at 0°C would be flagged as borderline for ship plate or pressure vessel use per ASME standards.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2022]*

**(a)** *Define the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT). Sketch and explain the typical impact energy versus temperature curve for a low-carbon steel, clearly marking the upper shelf, lower shelf, and transition regions. Discuss the engineering significance of this curve.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss the metallurgical factors that influence the impact properties of steels. How does the addition of nickel and refinement of grain size affect the DBTT?* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Definition of DBTT:**

The **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)** is the temperature at which the fracture mode of a material transitions from ductile (microvoid coalescence) to brittle (cleavage) under impact loading conditions. It is typically identified as the temperature corresponding to the **average of the upper shelf and lower shelf energies** on the impact energy vs. temperature curve.

**Curve Description:**

```
Energy(J)  |
   USE ●━━━●━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  Upper Shelf
           ╲
            ╲
             ╲
              ╲  <-- Transition Zone
               ╲
                ╲
   LSE ●━━━━━━━━●━━━━━━━━━━━━━  Lower Shelf
            T1    T(DBTT)    T2
                       Temperature (°C)
```

- **Upper Shelf Energy (USE):** High and roughly constant; corresponds to fully ductile fracture via microvoid coalescence. Typical USE for mild steel: 200–300 J.
- **Lower Shelf Energy (LSE):** Low and roughly constant; corresponds to fully brittle cleavage fracture. Typical LSE: 5–20 J.
- **Transition Region:** Energy drops rapidly over a narrow temperature range; the fracture surface shows a mix of shear and cleavage.

**Engineering Significance:**

1. **Designers must ensure the service temperature is *above* the DBTT + safety margin** (typically + 30°C to + 50°C) to avoid catastrophic brittle fracture.
2. The *Liberty Ship* disaster of WWII — many of the 2,700 ships fractured in cold North Atlantic waters because designers ignored DBTT.
3. Modern codes (ASME, EN 10025) specify **minimum Charpy values at specified temperatures** for structural steels.
4. The USE indicates the *maximum toughness reserve* for overload situations.
5. The LSE gives the *worst-case toughness* at low temperatures.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Metallurgical Factors Affecting Impact Properties:**

| Factor | Effect on DBTT / Impact Energy |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| Carbon content | ↑ C → ↑ DBTT, ↓ impact energy (carbide network) |
| Grain size | Coarse grain → ↑ DBTT; fine grain → ↓ DBTT |
| Ni addition | ↓ DBTT significantly (most effective alloying element) |
| Mn addition | ↓ DBTT moderately; also desulfurizes |
| Phosphorus, Sulfur | ↑ DBTT (embrittling impurities) |
| Nitrogen | ↑ DBTT (interstitial locking) |
| Heat treatment | Tempering after quench → ↓ DBTT, ↑ impact energy |
| Cold work | ↑ DBTT (residual stresses, dislocation tangles) |
| Carbide morphology | Spheroidized → ↑ impact energy |

**Effect of Nickel (Ni):**

Nickel is the *most effective* alloying element for lowering the DBTT of ferritic steels. Adding **1 wt% Ni** can lower the DBTT by approximately **10–20°C**. The mechanism is twofold:

1. Ni *reduces the Peierls–Nabarro stress*, making dislocation motion easier at low temperatures.
2. Ni *stabilizes the ductile fracture path* by suppressing cleavage initiation.
3. Ni also lowers the *yield strength sensitivity to temperature*, keeping it from rising sharply at low T.

This is why **9% Ni steel** is used for LNG storage tanks operating at **-196°C**.

**Effect of Grain Refinement:**

Per the **Petch relationship** for cleavage stress:

$$\sigma_f = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$

where $d$ is the average grain diameter. **Smaller grains** mean **higher cleavage stress** → cleavage becomes harder → material remains ductile → **DBTT shifts to lower temperatures**.

**Quantitative rule:** Halving the grain size lowers the DBTT by approximately **15–20°C** in mild steels.

**Combined effect:** Modern HSLA (High-Strength Low-Alloy) steels achieve both grain refinement (via Nb, Ti, V microalloying) and Ni additions to push DBTT below -60°C, enabling Arctic pipeline and offshore platform applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. In Part B(a), students often draw the curve with *axes unlabeled* — KTU awards 0 marks for the sketch if neither axis has units or labels. Always mark **Energy (J)** on Y-axis and **Temperature (°C)** on X-axis.
> 2. In Part B(b), stating "Ni lowers DBTT" without a *mechanism* loses 2–3 marks. The Peierls stress argument is mandatory.
> 3. For the numerical problem, *forgetting to substitute g = 9.81 m/s²* is a frequent 1-mark loss. Always include units in your final answer.
> 4. Do **not** confuse Charpy and Izod specimen dimensions; mixing them up is an instant 2-mark deduction in design questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master Before Exam**

- [x] **Impact toughness** = energy absorbed (in Joules) under sudden dynamic load.
- [x] **Charpy test** uses a *simply supported beam* with striker opposite the notch.
- [x] **Izod test** uses a *cantilever beam* with striker on the same side as the notch.
- [x] **Standard specimen:** 55 × 10 × 10 mm, V-notch 2 mm deep at 45° with 0.25 mm root radius.
- [x] **Energy equation:** $E = mg(h_1 - h_2)$ — derived from pendulum energy balance.
- [x] **DBTT** is sharp in **BCC metals** (ferritic steel), absent in **FCC metals** (austenitic SS, Cu, Al).
- [x] **Mechanism behind DBTT:** Peierls stress rise at low T locks dislocations in BCC → cleavage becomes dominant.
- [x] **Notch Sensitivity Ratio (NSR):** $\text{NSR} = E_{notched}/E_{unnotched}$. NSR → 0 means highly notch-sensitive.
- [x] **Ni addition** is the most effective DBTT-lowering alloying element (1% Ni → -10 to -20°C).
- [x] **Grain refinement** lowers DBTT per Petch equation $\sigma_f \propto d^{-1/2}$.
- [x] **Engineering codes** (ASME, EN 10025) mandate minimum Charpy values at design temperatures.
- [x] **Fracture surface appearance:** Ductile = fibrous/dull; Brittle = crystalline/bright.
- [x] **Strain rate** shifts DBTT to higher temperatures — faster loading = more brittle behavior.
- [x] **Tempering** after quench improves impact energy dramatically (martensite → tempered martensite).
- [x] **Standards:** ASTM E23, ISO 148-1:2016, IS 1757 — know at least one for the exam.
- [x] **Liberty Ship lesson:** Always test for DBTT in service environment, never assume room-temp data is sufficient.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fatigue
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fatigue

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue** is defined as the **progressive, localized, and permanent structural damage** that occurs in a material subjected to **cyclic loading and unloading** at stress levels considerably **lower than the static ultimate tensile strength (UTS)** of the material. Failure ultimately occurs through the **initiation and propagation of a crack** until the remaining cross-section can no longer support the applied load, leading to **sudden and catastrophic fracture**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, fatigue is studied as a sub-category of **mechanical failure** alongside fracture, creep, and wear. The cyclic stress regime is fundamentally characterized by:

- **Maximum stress** ($\sigma_{max}$)
- **Minimum stress** ($\sigma_{min}$)
- **Mean stress** ($\sigma_m$)
- **Stress amplitude** ($\sigma_a$)
- **Stress range** ($\Delta\sigma$)
- **Stress ratio** ($R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$)

The fundamental governing relation between these parameters is:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

$$\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric for fatigue analysis is the **number of cycles to failure ($N_f$)**, which is plotted on the abscissa of the famous **S-N (Wöhler) Curve**. The **endurance limit (or fatigue limit, $\sigma_e$)** is defined as the stress amplitude below which the material can theoretically endure an **infinite number of cycles without failure** — typically applicable to **ferrous alloys (steels) and titanium alloys**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine bending a **steel paperclip** back and forth repeatedly. Each individual bend applies a stress far below the paperclip's ultimate strength — so theoretically, the clip should never break. Yet after 8–10 bends, the clip **snaps cleanly** with no visible plastic deformation. This is **fatigue in its purest form**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Whisper Until Shatter" Analogy:** Cyclic loading is like whispering a single word to a glass. One whisper does nothing. But say it **one billion times at the exact resonant frequency**, and the glass shatters. Fatigue is the structural equivalent — microscopic, sub-yield whispers accumulate damage at the **crystal lattice level** until the material gives way catastrophically.

**Historic Engineering Catastrophes Driven by Fatigue:**

| Incident | Year | Root Cause |
|---|---|---|
| De Havilland Comet (commercial jets) | 1954 | Fatigue crack propagation around square windows |
| Aloha Airlines Flight 243 (Boeing 737) | 1988 | Multi-site fatigue damage in fuselage lap joints |
| Versailles Railway Accident | 1842 | Fatigue fracture of a locomotive axle |
| Hyatt Regency Walkway Collapse | 1981 | Cyclic load-induced connection failure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Fatigue is Dangerous:** Unlike ductile overload failure, fatigue fracture surfaces often show **little to no macro plastic deformation**. A shaft can look perfectly fine visually, run smoothly for years, and then **shatter suddenly** in service — making fatigue a **silent, time-delayed, and often invisible killer** in mechanical design.

---

### 1.3 Standard Fatigue Terminology Snapshot

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fatigue Life | $N_f$ | cycles | Total cycles to failure |
| Fatigue Strength | $\sigma_f$ | MPa | Stress amplitude for failure at $N$ cycles |
| Endurance Limit | $\sigma_e$ | MPa | Stress below which $\infty$ life is expected |
| Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a$ | MPa | Half the stress range |
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m$ | MPa | Average of max and min stress |
| Stress Ratio | $R$ | — | $\sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, the **endurance limit for steels** is empirically approximated as: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5 \sigma_{UTS}$ (for polished specimens in air at room temperature). For non-ferrous metals like **aluminum and copper**, no true endurance limit exists — fatigue strength is quoted at a specific life (e.g., $10^7$ or $10^8$ cycles).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fatigue Regimes

The KTU syllabus mandates a clear bifurcation of fatigue into two principal regimes based on the number of cycles and the dominant deformation mechanism:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Cycle Fatigue (HCF):** $N_f > 10^4 - 10^5$ cycles. Loading is predominantly **elastic**. The stress-life (S-N) approach using **Basquin's Equation** governs. Typical in rotating shafts, bridges, aircraft wings.
>
> **Low Cycle Fatigue (LCF):** $N_f < 10^4 - 10^5$ cycles. Loading involves **macro-scale plastic strain** in each cycle. The strain-life approach using the **Coffin-Manson Equation** governs. Typical in pressure vessel startups/shutdowns, thermal fatigue, seismic loading.

A unifying **total strain-life** relation links both regimes:

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_{total}}{2} = \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_{elastic}}{2} + \frac{\Delta\varepsilon_{plastic}}{2}$$

---

### 2.2 The S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Stress-Life Framework

The S-N curve is the **backbone of fatigue design** in the KTU 2024 mechanical engineering curriculum. It is plotted with:
- **Y-axis (linear):** Stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (MPa)
- **X-axis (logarithmic):** Number of cycles to failure $N_f$

**Key Features of the S-N Curve for Steels:**

1. A **descending linear portion** in the high-cycle regime, fitted by **Basquin's equation**.
2. A **knee** at $N \approx 10^6$ to $10^7$ cycles, transitioning to a **horizontal plateau** — the **endurance limit $\sigma_e$**.
3. The curve **flattens indefinitely** for ferrous alloys, indicating infinite life below $\sigma_e$.

> [!NOTE]
> For **non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Mg, etc.)**, the curve continues to slope gently downward indefinitely. The "endurance limit" is replaced by a **fatigue strength at $N = 5 \times 10^8$ or $10^9$ cycles** — a design convention rather than a true material property.

---

### 2.3 Empirical & Analytical Fatigue Equations

**1. Basquin's Equation (High-Cycle Fatigue — Elastic Regime):**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$

where:
- $\sigma'_f$ = **fatigue strength coefficient** (intercept at $2N_f = 1$)
- $b$ = **fatigue strength exponent** (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.12$ for metals)
- $2N_f$ = **reversals to failure**

**2. Coffin-Manson Equation (Low-Cycle Fatigue — Plastic Regime):**

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$$

where:
- $\varepsilon'_f$ = **fatigue ductility coefficient**
- $c$ = **fatigue ductility exponent** (typically $-0.5$ to $-0.7$)

**3. Total Strain-Life Relation (Morrow Form):**

$$\frac{\Delta\varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma'_f}{E}(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$$

**4. Paris-Erdogan Crack Growth Law:**

$$\frac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^m$$

where:
- $a$ = crack length
- $\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = Y\Delta\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$
- $C$, $m$ = material constants (typically $m \approx 2$–$4$ for metals)

**5. Miner's Rule of Cumulative Damage:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$

where $n_i$ is the number of cycles applied at stress level $i$, and $N_i$ is the cycles to failure at that level.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation | Formula | Regime / Use | Critical Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min})/2$ | All regimes | $R$-ratio link |
| Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min})/2$ | All regimes | Reversed loading |
| Stress Ratio | $R = \sigma_{min}/\sigma_{max}$ | All regimes | $R=-1$ fully reversed |
| Basquin | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | HCF | Elastic S-N |
| Coffin-Manson | $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$ | LCF | Plastic strain |
| Morrow | $\Delta\varepsilon/2 = (\sigma'_f/E)(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon'_f(2N_f)^c$ | Total life | HCF + LCF |
| Paris Law | $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ | Crack growth | Subcritical flaw |
| Miner's Rule | $\sum n_i/N_i = 1$ | Variable amplitude | Cumulative damage |
| Goodman | $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_{UTS} = 1$ | Mean stress correction | Brittle assumption |
| Gerber | $(\sigma_a/\sigma_e) + (\sigma_m/\sigma_{UTS})^2 = 1$ | Mean stress correction | Ductile metals |
| Soderberg | $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_y = 1$ | Mean stress correction | Conservative design |
| Endurance Estimate (Steel) | $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\sigma_{UTS}$ | Quick design | Rotating beam |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Mean stress corrections are **NOT interchangeable**. **Soderberg** is the most conservative (uses yield strength), **Goodman** is a straight-line conservative approximation, and **Gerber** is a parabolic fit best for ductile metals. KTU examiners specifically test whether you choose the right one for the material system.

---

### 2.5 The Three Phases of Fatigue Failure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 1 — Crack Initiation (Nucleation):** Microscopic cracks nucleate at sites of **stress concentration** (inclusions, surface scratches, grain boundaries, slip bands). Takes up **80–90% of total life** in HCF.
>
> **Phase 2 — Crack Propagation:** Crack grows stably under cyclic loading. Produces characteristic **"beach marks" or "striations"** on the fracture surface — one striation per cycle.
>
> **Phase 3 — Final Fracture:** When crack reaches critical size ($K_I = K_{IC}$), sudden brittle-like fracture of the remaining ligament. Produces a **rough, dimpled overload zone**.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fatigue analysis is non-negotiable in industries where cyclic loading is endemic:

- **Aerospace:** Wing spars, turbine disks, landing gear
- **Automotive:** Crankshafts, connecting rods, suspension springs
- **Civil:** Bridges, offshore platforms, railway axles
- **Power Generation:** Steam/gas turbine blades, generator shafts
- **Biomedical:** Orthopedic implants (hip stems see $10^6$ gait cycles/year)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Basquin's Equation

The empirical S-N curve in the HCF regime appears as a straight line when plotted on a **semi-log scale** (log N on x-axis). The Basquin relation is derived from this observation.

**Step 1:** A linear relation between $\log \sigma_a$ and $\log N_f$ implies a power law:

$$\sigma_a = A \cdot N_f^B$$

**Step 2:** Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln \sigma_a = \ln A + B \ln N_f$$

**Step 3:** Differentiating with respect to $N_f$:

$$\frac{d(\ln \sigma_a)}{d(\ln N_f)} = B$$

**Step 4:** Identifying the slope $B$ as the **fatigue strength exponent $b$**, and rewriting the constant using **reversals to failure** ($2N_f$):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners commonly ask: "Define the fatigue strength exponent and explain its physical meaning." The answer: $b$ represents the **sensitivity of fatigue life to applied stress amplitude**. A more negative $b$ means a steeper S-N curve and greater sensitivity. Typical metallic values: $b \in [-0.12, -0.05]$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Miner's Cumulative Damage Rule

Miner's rule assumes **linear accumulation** of fatigue damage. The derivation follows a straightforward energy/fractional-life argument.

**Step 1:** Define the damage fraction at stress level $i$ as:

$$D_i = \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 2:** Assume damage accumulates linearly and independently at each stress level:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} D_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$

**Step 3:** Failure is postulated when total damage reaches unity:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> Miner's rule **ignores load sequence effects** (high-to-low vs. low-to-high loading order changes actual life). In KTU examinations, the assumption of $D = 1$ at failure is the standard textbook answer, but advanced study notes that real values range from $D \approx 0.3$ to $3.0$ depending on sequence.

---

### 3.3 Mean Stress Correction: Goodman, Gerber, Soderberg

These corrections modify the allowable stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ at a given mean stress $\sigma_m$.

**Step 1 — Goodman (Linear, Conservative):** Assumes brittle failure envelope.

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}} = 1$$

Solving for $\sigma_a$:

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Gerber (Parabolic, Ductile-Friendly):** Empirically fits ductile metals better.

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e}\right) + \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Soderberg (Linear, Most Conservative):** Uses yield strength $\sigma_y$ as the tensile limit.

$$\frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$$

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Heuristic for KTU Exam:**
> - **Soderberg** → conservative design, no yielding permitted
> - **Goodman** → quick conservative estimate, brittle materials
> - **Gerber** → best for ductile metals (Al, mild steel)

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: Mean Stress Correction

> **Given:** A steel shaft has $\sigma_e = 300$ MPa and $\sigma_{UTS} = 600$ MPa. It operates with $\sigma_m = 100$ MPa. Compute the allowable stress amplitude using Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg criteria. Take $\sigma_y = 420$ MPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Goodman:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right) = 300 \times \left(1 - \frac{100}{600}\right)$$

$$\sigma_a = 300 \times \frac{5}{6} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Gerber:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2\right] = 300 \times \left[1 - \left(\frac{100}{600}\right)^2\right]$$

$$\sigma_a = 300 \times \left[1 - 0.0278\right] = 300 \times 0.9722 = 291.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Soderberg:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}\right) = 300 \times \left(1 - \frac{100}{420}\right)$$

$$\sigma_a = 300 \times 0.7619 = 228.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | Conservatism |
|---|---|---|
| Soderberg | 228.6 | **Most conservative** |
| Goodman | 250.0 | Moderately conservative |
| Gerber | 291.7 | Least conservative (ductile-friendly) |

> [!NOTE]
> This example is a **direct KTU exam-style problem**. Master the calculation pattern: plug in mean stress, use the corresponding denominator ($\sigma_y$ for Soderberg, $\sigma_{UTS}$ for Goodman/Gerber), and apply the exponent (1 for Goodman/Soderberg, 2 for Gerber).

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example: Miner's Rule for Variable Loading

> **Given:** A component is subjected to the following loading spectrum:
> - $\sigma_1 = 200$ MPa for $n_1 = 10{,}000$ cycles, where $N_1 = 100{,}000$ cycles to failure
> - $\sigma_2 = 150$ MPa for $n_2 = 200{,}000$ cycles, where $N_2 = 1{,}000{,}000$ cycles to failure
>
> **Determine:** Whether the component fails.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute damage at each stress level:

$$D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{10{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.10$$

$$D_2 = \frac{n_2}{N_2} = \frac{200{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.20$$

**Step 2:** Sum the damage fractions:

$$D_{total} = D_1 + D_2 = 0.10 + 0.20 = 0.30$$

**Step 3:** Compare with failure criterion $D = 1$:

Since $D_{total} = 0.30 < 1$, the component **has not failed**. Remaining life fraction is $1 - 0.30 = 0.70$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Stress Amplitude Sigma_a (MPa)"] --> B["High Stress - Low Cycle Region"]
    B --> C["Basquin Linear Region - HCF"]
    C --> D["Fatigue Limit Knee at 10^6 to 10^7 cycles"]
    D --> E["Endurance Plateau Sigma_e"]
    E --> F["Infinite Life Region for Ferrous Alloys"]
    C --> G["Non-Ferrous Metals - No Plateau"]
    G --> H["Continuous Decay up to 10^9 cycles"]
    B --> I["Coffin-Manson Plastic Dominant Zone"]
    I --> J["LCF Region - Less than 10^4 to 10^5 cycles"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **operational zones of the S-N curve**. In KTU board answers, students are expected to **label both axes**, mark the **knee point**, identify the **endurance limit plateau** for steels, and explicitly note the **absence of a plateau for non-ferrous metals**.

---

### 4.2 Fatigue Failure Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Cyclic Load Application"] --> stepB["Microplastic Strain at Slip Bands"]
    stepB --> stepC["Persistent Slip Bands - PSB Formation"]
    stepC --> stepD["Stage 1: Crack Initiation at PSB"]
    stepD --> stepE["Stage 2: Stable Crack Propagation - Striations"]
    stepE --> stepF["Stress Intensity K reaches K_IC"]
    stepF --> stepG["Stage 3: Unstable Final Fracture"]
    stepG --> stepH["Component Catastrophic Failure"]
    stepA:::loadClass
    stepD:::crackClass
    stepE:::propClass
    stepG:::fracClass
    classDef loadClass fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef crackClass fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef propClass fill:#e8f5e1,stroke:#339933,color:#000
    classDef fracClass fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram maps the **three classical stages of fatigue failure**. Note the color-coding: blue (loading), orange (initiation), green (stable propagation), red (final fracture). The striations in Stage 2 are **diagnostic forensic markers** that engineers use in failure analysis to identify fatigue as the failure mode.

---

### 4.3 Mean Stress Effect on Fatigue Strength

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX["Sigma_m Mean Stress (MPa)"] --> axisY["Sigma_a Allowable Stress Amplitude (MPa)"]
    axisY --> M1["Soderberg Line: Uses Sigma_y as Tensile Limit - Most Conservative"]
    axisY --> M2["Goodman Line: Uses Sigma_UTS - Linear Conservative"]
    axisY --> M3["Gerber Parabola: Uses Sigma_UTS - Ductile-Friendly Curve"]
    M1:::consClass
    M2:::consClass
    M3:::libClass
    classDef consClass fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef libClass fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often present a **Haigh diagram** like this one. Students must (a) identify the **three constant-life lines**, (b) plot the **operating point** ($\sigma_m$, $\sigma_a$), and (c) state whether the point lies **inside the safe region** (below all three lines) or **outside** (unsafe).

---

### 4.4 Factors Affecting Fatigue Life — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    F["Fatigue Life N_f"] --> F1["Material Factors"]
    F --> F2["Geometric Factors"]
    F --> F3["Environmental Factors"]
    F --> F4["Loading Factors"]
    F1 --> F1a["Grain Size - Finer is Better"]
    F1 --> F1b["Surface Hardness"]
    F1 --> F1c["Microstructure - Martensitic Steels Excel"]
    F2 --> F2a["Surface Roughness - Polishing Improves Life"]
    F2 --> F2b["Notch Effects and Stress Concentrators"]
    F2 --> F2c["Size Effect - Larger Parts Have Lower Sigma_e"]
    F3 --> F3a["Temperature - Elevated Temp Reduces Life"]
    F3 --> F3b["Corrosive Environment - Synergistic Damage"]
    F3 --> F3c["Residual Stresses - Compressive is Beneficial"]
    F4 --> F4a["Mean Stress Level"]
    F4 --> F4b["Frequency and Waveform"]
    F4 --> F4c["Multiaxial vs Uniaxial Loading"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This functional architecture diagram is critical for KTU Part A questions like *"List any four factors affecting fatigue life."* A clear hierarchical structure earns full marks — students should always partition factors into **material, geometric, environmental, and loading categories** in their exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define fatigue failure. What is an endurance limit? Is it applicable to all metals?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fatigue failure** is the failure of a material under **repeated cyclic loading** at stress levels **below the static ultimate tensile strength**. It occurs through **progressive crack initiation and propagation**, ultimately leading to sudden fracture.
>
> The **endurance limit ($\sigma_e$)** is defined as the **maximum stress amplitude** below which a material can theoretically withstand an **infinite number of stress cycles** (typically $> 10^6$ cycles) without failure.
>
> A true endurance limit is exhibited **only by ferrous alloys (carbon steels, alloy steels) and titanium alloys** — metals that undergo strain-ageing. **Non-ferrous metals like aluminum, copper, and magnesium do not show a true endurance limit**; for them, fatigue strength is specified at a fixed cycle count (e.g., $10^8$ cycles).
>
> *(1 mark each for the three components — definition, endurance limit definition, applicability)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **List any four factors that affect the fatigue life of a component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!TIP]
> The four key factors affecting fatigue life are:
>
> 1. **Surface Finish:** Polished surfaces have higher fatigue life; rough surfaces act as stress raisers and nucleate cracks early.
>
> 2. **Mean Stress:** Higher tensile mean stress reduces fatigue life; compressive mean stress is beneficial (e.g., shot peening induces compressive residual stress).
>
> 3. **Size Effect:** Larger cross-sections have lower endurance limit (size factor $C_{size} < 1$ for $d > 8$ mm). This is because larger volumes statistically contain more flaws.
>
> 4. **Loading Frequency and Type:** Random, variable-amplitude loading is more damaging than constant-amplitude (Miner's rule governs cumulative damage). High frequencies combined with corrosive environment cause **corrosion fatigue**.
>
> *(0.75 marks per factor — 4 factors × 0.75 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **S-N curve** in detail. Discuss its salient features for **ferrous and non-ferrous** metals. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A steel specimen has an **ultimate tensile strength of 620 MPa** and an **endurance limit of 310 MPa**. The component operates at a **mean stress of 80 MPa**. Compute the allowable stress amplitude using **Goodman, Gerber, and Soderberg criteria**. Take $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 marks:**

The **S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)** is the fundamental graphical representation of fatigue behavior, plotting **stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (linear y-axis)** versus **number of cycles to failure $N_f$ (logarithmic x-axis)**.

**Salient Features:**

1. **Three distinct regions:**
   - **LCF region** ($N < 10^4$–$10^5$ cycles): Plastic strain dominant.
   - **HCF region** ($10^4 < N < 10^6$–$10^7$): Elastic strain dominant; **Basquin's law** applies.
   - **Endurance region** ($N > 10^6$–$10^7$): For steels, the curve becomes horizontal at $\sigma_e$.

2. **Ferrous metals (steels):**
   - Exhibit a clear **endurance limit** with a **distinct knee** at $10^6$–$10^7$ cycles.
   - Below $\sigma_e$, life is considered infinite.
   - Empirically: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\sigma_{UTS}$ (for polished, R = -1 specimens).

3. **Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, Mg):**
   - **No true endurance limit** — the curve continues to decline indefinitely.
   - **Fatigue strength** is reported at a specified life, e.g., $\sigma_{10^7}$ or $\sigma_{10^8}$.
   - Steeper S-N slope in HCF.

4. **Mathematical modeling:**
   - HCF: **Basquin's equation** $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$
   - LCF: **Coffin-Manson equation** $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$

**Mark split:** 2 marks for definition/axes, 2 marks for ferrous behavior, 2 marks for non-ferrous behavior, 1 mark for equations.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $\sigma_{UTS} = 620$ MPa
- $\sigma_e = 310$ MPa
- $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa
- $\sigma_y = 380$ MPa

**Step 1 — Goodman Criterion:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)$$

$$\sigma_a = 310 \times \left(1 - \frac{80}{620}\right) = 310 \times 0.8710 = 270.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Gerber Criterion:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left[1 - \left(\frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{UTS}}\right)^2\right]$$

$$\sigma_a = 310 \times \left[1 - \left(\frac{80}{620}\right)^2\right] = 310 \times \left[1 - 0.0166\right]$$

$$\sigma_a = 310 \times 0.9834 = 304.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Soderberg Criterion:**

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_e \left(1 - \frac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y}\right)$$

$$\sigma_a = 310 \times \left(1 - \frac{80}{380}\right) = 310 \times 0.7895 = 244.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison & Comment:**

| Criterion | Allowable $\sigma_a$ (MPa) | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Soderberg | 244.7 | Most conservative |
| Goodman | 270.0 | Moderate |
| Gerber | 304.8 | Least conservative |

**Comment:** Soderberg gives the most conservative estimate because it uses $\sigma_y$ (the actual elastic limit) as the failure boundary, ensuring no yielding occurs. Gerber is the least conservative and best represents ductile metals. Goodman is the typical design compromise.

**Mark split:** 1 mark formula, 1.5 marks each calculation (3 total), 0.5 marks comparison, 1 mark comment.

---

> **Q3. Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
> **(a)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the **three stages of fatigue failure**. Discuss the **fracture surface features** used in failure analysis to identify fatigue. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A component experiences a **two-level block loading**: 50,000 cycles at $\sigma_1 = 220$ MPa (where $N_1 = 200{,}000$ cycles to failure) and 100,000 cycles at $\sigma_2 = 160$ MPa (where $N_2 = 1{,}000{,}000$ cycles to failure). Using **Miner's rule**, determine if the component will fail. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q3(a) Alternative — 7 marks:**

**Stage 1 — Crack Initiation (3 marks):**

The process begins at microscopic sites of **stress concentration** such as slip bands, inclusions, grain boundaries, or surface defects. **Persistent slip bands (PSBs)** form within grains due to cyclic plastic strain at the micro-scale. Extrusions and intrusions develop on the surface, acting as crack nucleation sites. **This stage consumes 80–90% of total life in HCF**.

**Stage 2 — Crack Propagation (2 marks):**

The initiated micro-crack grows **stably** with each load cycle. The growth rate per cycle is governed by the **Paris-Erdogan equation**: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$. On the fracture surface, this stage leaves characteristic **"beach marks"** (macroscopic) and **"striations"** (microscopic, one per cycle). The crack propagates perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress.

**Stage 3 — Final Fracture (2 marks):**

When the crack reaches a **critical length** such that the stress intensity factor $K_I$ equals the **fracture toughness $K_{IC}$**, the remaining uncracked cross-section fails suddenly. The fracture surface in this zone appears **rough, fibrous, and dimpled** — characteristic of static overload failure. The transition from stage 2 to stage 3 is identified by a change in surface texture (smooth/striated → rough/dimpled).

**Mark split:** 3 marks for stage 1 with surface details, 2 marks for stage 2 with Paris law and beach marks, 2 marks for stage 3 with K_IC and dimpled surface.

---

**Model Solution for Q3(b) Alternative — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute Damage at Stress Level 1 (3.5 marks):**

$$D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{50{,}000}{200{,}000} = 0.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute Damage at Stress Level 2 (3.5 marks):**

$$D_2 = \frac{n_2}{N_2 = } = \frac{100{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Total Damage:**

$$D_{total} = D_1 + D_2 = 0.25 + 0.10 = 0.35$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Failure Criterion:**

Since $D_{total} = 0.35 < 1.0$, the component **has NOT failed**.

**Remaining damage capacity:** $1.0 - 0.35 = 0.65$ (i.e., 65% of life remains).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Common mistakes students make include:
> 1. Inverting the ratio as $N_i/n_i$ — the **damage** is **cycles applied / cycles to failure**, NOT the reverse.
> 2. Forgetting to sum the damages — both stress levels contribute additively.
> 3. Reporting "failure" when $D > 1$ but using wrong magnitudes — **always cross-check your arithmetic**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Fatigue"**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - Fatigue = failure under **cyclic stress below UTS**; failure is **sudden and brittle-like**.
> - S-N (Wöhler) curve is the **graphical fingerprint** of fatigue behavior.
> - Three stages: **Initiation (80–90% of life) → Propagation (striations) → Final fracture**.
> - **Endurance limit $\sigma_e$** exists for **ferrous alloys** only; non-ferrous have **fatigue strength at $N$ cycles**.
> - Empirical steel rule: $\sigma_e \approx 0.5\sigma_{UTS}$ (rotating beam, polished, R = -1).
>
> **Key Equations to Memorize:**
> - Basquin: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$
> - Coffin-Manson: $\Delta\varepsilon_p/2 = \varepsilon'_f (2N_f)^c$
> - Paris: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$
> - Miner: $\sum n_i/N_i = 1$
> - Goodman: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_{UTS} = 1$
> - Gerber: $(\sigma_a/\sigma_e) + (\sigma_m/\sigma_{UTS})^2 = 1$
> - Soderberg: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_y = 1$
>
> **Mean Stress Correction Selection (must know!):**
> - **Soderberg** = most conservative (uses $\sigma_y$)
> - **Goodman** = linear, conservative (uses $\sigma_{UTS}$)
> - **Gerber** = parabolic, ductile-friendly (uses $\sigma_{UTS}$)
>
> **Factors Affecting Fatigue Life (must list 4+):**
> - Surface finish (polished > rough)
> - Size effect (larger → lower $\sigma_e$)
> - Mean stress (tensile ↓ life, compressive ↑ life)
> - Temperature (high temp ↓ life)
> - Corrosion (corrosion fatigue is synergistic)
> - Microstructure (fine grain, martensitic for steels)
> - Residual stress (compressive from shot peening ↑ life)
>
> **Forensic Fracture Surface Markers:**
> - **Beach marks** (macroscopic) — progressive crack front positions
> - **Striations** (microscopic) — one per cycle
> - **Ratchet marks** — multi-crack initiation points
> - **Final overload zone** — rough, dimpled region at edge
>
> **Common KTU Exam Traps to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **stress amplitude** with **stress range** ($\Delta\sigma = 2\sigma_a$).
> 2. Forgetting to convert cycles to **reversals** ($2N$) in Basquin's equation.
> 3. Inverting fractions in Miner's rule.
> 4. Applying **endurance limit to aluminum** — incorrect; use fatigue strength at fixed N.
> 5. Choosing wrong mean stress correction for material type.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creep
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creep

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep** is defined as the **time-dependent permanent (plastic) deformation** of a material subjected to a **constant load or constant stress** at a **sustained elevated temperature**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, the operational threshold is rigorously defined as:

$$T_{creep} \geq 0.4 \, T_m \quad (\text{where } T_m \text{ is the absolute melting point in Kelvin})$$

The material continues to deform even though the applied stress is **below the yield stress** at room temperature. Three conditions must be simultaneously satisfied for creep to be a dominant failure mode:

1. **High Temperature** ($\geq 0.4 \, T_m$)
2. **Sustained Load** (constant stress, not cyclic)
3. **Sufficient Time** (deformation accumulates gradually over hours to years)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to distinguish between **creep (continuous deformation)** and **stress rupture (eventual fracture)**. They are two related but distinct concepts measured on the same curve.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **cold stick of chewing gum** versus the **same gum warmed in your palm**:

- The **cold gum** (low temperature) resists deformation. Even with force, it returns to shape or snaps if pushed too hard.
- The **warm gum in your palm** (high temperature + sustained gentle pressure) gradually sags, flattens, and slowly flows under its own weight — even though the force is tiny.

This slow, gravity-driven "flow" of a solid material over time is exactly what engineers call **creep**. The atoms inside the metal have enough thermal energy (vibration) to slowly move past each other, rearrange, and slide along crystallographic planes — something they cannot do at room temperature.

**Common Engineering Examples:**
- **Turbine blades** in jet engines (operate at $\sim 1000^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- **Steam boiler tubes** in thermal power plants
- **Nuclear reactor pressure vessels**
- **Lead pipes** sagging under their own weight over decades
- **Glass panes** in old windows (thicker at the bottom — classical creep)

## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024

| Term | Precise Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Creep** | Time-dependent strain under constant stress at high T |
| **Stress Rupture** | Eventual fracture after prolonged creep loading |
| **Rupture Life ($t_r$)** | Total time to fracture under given stress & temperature |
| **Steady-State Creep Rate ($\dot{\epsilon}_s$)** | Minimum constant strain rate during secondary creep |
| **Creep Limit** | Stress that produces $\epsilon = 1\%$ in 100,000 hours |
| **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)** | Combines temperature and time into a single design number |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classical Creep Curve ($\epsilon$ vs $t$)

When a material is loaded instantaneously at temperature $T$ to stress $\sigma$, and the strain is plotted against time, a characteristic **three-stage curve** is obtained:

### Stage I — Primary (Transient) Creep
- Strain rate **decreases** with time.
- Dominated by **strain hardening (work hardening)**.
- Dislocation density increases rapidly → material becomes harder → rate slows.
- Slope: $\dfrac{d\epsilon}{dt}$ continuously decreasing.

### Stage II — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep
- Strain rate is **constant and minimum** — the most important design region.
- Balance between **strain hardening** and **thermal recovery (annealing)**.
- Dislocations are created and annihilated at equal rates.
- This rate is used in all engineering design calculations.

### Stage III — Tertiary Creep
- Strain rate **accelerates rapidly** leading to fracture.
- Caused by: **internal void formation (cavitation)**, **necking**, and **grain boundary separation**.
- Ends at the **rupture point** (stress rupture).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** The most asked derivation is for the **steady-state creep rate equation** using the **power-law (Bailey-Norton) relationship**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Steady-State Creep Rate (Power Law)** | $\dot{\epsilon}_s = A \, \sigma^{n} \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-Q_c}{RT}\right)$ | $A$ = constant, $n$ = stress exponent (typically 3–8), $Q_c$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 2 | **Nabarro-Herring Creep (Lattice Diffusion)** | $\dot{\epsilon}_{NH} = \dfrac{A_{NH} \, \sigma \, \Omega \, D_L}{d^{2} \, k \, T}$ | $\Omega$ = atomic volume, $D_L$ = lattice diffusivity, $d$ = grain size, $k$ = Boltzmann constant |
| 3 | **Coble Creep (Grain Boundary Diffusion)** | $\dot{\epsilon}_C = \dfrac{A_{C} \, \sigma \, \Omega \, \delta \, D_{GB}}{d^{3} \, k \, T}$ | $\delta$ = grain boundary width, $D_{GB}$ = GB diffusivity |
| 4 | **Larson-Miller Parameter** | $LMP = T \left[ C + \log_{10}(t_r) \right]$ | $T$ in Kelvin, $t_r$ in hours, $C \approx 20$ for most metals |
| 5 | **Monkman-Grant Relation** | $\log t_r + m \log \dot{\epsilon}_s = K_{MG}$ | $m$ and $K_{MG}$ are material constants |
| 6 | **Diffusivity (Arrhenius form)** | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-Q}{RT}\right)$ | $D_0$ = pre-exponential factor |
