> - **Young's Modulus:** $E \approx \mathbf{200\,\text{GPa}}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microstructural Basis of Carbon Steel Classification

The mechanical behavior of carbon steels is dictated by the **relative proportions** of three principal phases in the iron-carbon system:

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Primary Phases in the Fe-Fe₃C System:**
> 1. **Ferrite (α-Fe):** Body-Centered Cubic (BCC), soft, ductile. Solubility of C $\le 0.022\%$ at $727\,^\circ\text{C}$. Hardness $\approx 80\,\text{BHN}$.
> 2. **Austenite (γ-Fe):** Face-Centered Cubic (FCC), stable only above $727\,^\circ\text{C}$, non-magnetic, soft but tough. Solubility of C up to $2.14\%$.
> 3. **Cementite (Fe₃C):** Intermetallic, orthorhombic, extremely hard and brittle. Hardness $\approx 1000\,\text{BHN}$. Composition $= 6.67\%\,\text{C}$.

At **room temperature**, the room-temperature microstructure is a **mixture of ferrite + cementite**, often arranged as **pearlite** (a lamellar eutectoid mixture of ferrite and cementite).

### The Eutectoid Reaction (Core Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Eutectoid Reaction (KTU Favorite Question):**
> $$\text{Austenite}\,(0.77\%\,\text{C}) \xrightarrow{\text{Cooling at } 727\,^\circ\text{C}} \text{Pearlite}\,(\alpha\text{-ferrite} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C})$$
> $$\gamma\,(0.77\%\,\text{C}) \rightarrow \alpha\,(0.022\%\,\text{C}) + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}\,(6.67\%\,\text{C})$$

**Why this matters:** The eutectoid point ($0.77\%$ C) acts as the *dividing line*:
- Steels with $C < 0.77\%$ → **Hypoeutectoid** (ferrite + pearlite microstructure)
- Steel with $C = 0.77\%$ → **Eutectoid** (100% pearlite)
- Steels with $C > 0.77\%$ → **Hypereutectoid** (pearlite + cementite network)

## 2.2 Effect of Carbon Content on Mechanical Properties

The relationship between carbon content and key mechanical properties is **approximately linear** in plain carbon steels (for the as-rolled or normalized condition). This is a frequent KTU exam question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Empirical Relations for KTU Problem Solving):**
> 1. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (MPa):** $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 350 + 1000 \cdot \%\text{C}$
> 2. **Brinell Hardness Number:** $\text{BHN} \approx 80 + 250 \cdot \%\text{C}$
> 3. **% Elongation (ductility):** $\%\text{El} \approx 50 - 30 \cdot \%\text{C}$
> 4. **Impact Toughness (Izod, J):** Decreases **non-linearly** as C increases; low C steel $\approx 100$–$150$ J, high C steel $\approx 5$–$10$ J.

### Quantitative Trend Table (Critical for KTU)

| Property | Low C (0.2%) | Medium C (0.4%) | High C (0.8%) | Trend |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| UTS (MPa) | $\approx 550$ | $\approx 750$ | $\approx 1150$ | $\uparrow$ Increases |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | $\approx 250$ | $\approx 400$ | $\approx 600$ | $\uparrow$ Increases |
| BHN | $\approx 130$ | $\approx 180$ | $\approx 280$ | $\uparrow$ Increases |
| % Elongation | $\approx 44$ | $\approx 38$ | $\approx 26$ | $\downarrow$ Decreases |
| Impact Toughness (J) | $\approx 120$ | $\approx 50$ | $\approx 10$ | $\downarrow$ Decreases sharply |
| Weldability | Excellent | Fair (preheat) | Poor | $\downarrow$ Decreases |
| Machinability | Fair | Good (best at 0.4%) | Poor | Peaks at $\sim 0.4\%$ C |
| Hardenability (by quenching) | Low | Moderate | High | $\uparrow$ Increases |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (For Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU ESE / Module Test:**

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 350 + 1000 \cdot \%\text{C}$ | Approximate tensile strength from C content | MPa |
| 2 | $\text{BHN} \approx 80 + 250 \cdot \%\text{C}$ | Approximate Brinell hardness from C content | BHN |
| 3 | $\%\text{El} \approx 50 - 30 \cdot \%\text{C}$ | Approximate ductility from C content | % |
| 4 | $\%\,\text{Pearlite} = \dfrac{0.77 - \%\text{C}}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$ | For **hypoeutectoid** steel ($C < 0.77\%$) | % |
| 5 | $\%\text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = \dfrac{\%\text{C} - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100$ | For **hypoeutectoid** steel | % |
| 6 | $\%\,\text{Pearlite} = \dfrac{6.67 - \%\text{C}}{6.67 - 0.77} \times 100$ | For **hypereutectoid** steel ($C > 0.77\%$) | % |
| 7 | $\%\text{Proeutectoid Cementite} = \dfrac{\%\text{C} - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77} \times 100$ | For **hypereutectoid** steel | % |
| 8 | $\text{Hall-Petch:} \quad \sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Grain-boundary strengthening | MPa, $\mu\text{m}$ |
| 9 | $\text{Carbon Equivalent:}\ \text{CE} = \%\text{C} + \dfrac{\%\text{Mn}}{6} + \dfrac{\%\text{Cr}+\%\text{Mo}+\%\text{V}}{5} + \dfrac{\%\text{Ni}+\%\text{Cu}}{15}$ | Weldability assessment (IIW formula) | wt.% |
| 10 | $\text{Interlamellar Spacing of Pearlite:} \quad S_0 \approx \dfrac{8.02 \times 10^3}{\Delta T}$ | Pearlite growth kinetics | $\mu\text{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of the Carbon Equivalent (CE) Formula:**
> The **IIW (International Institute of Welding) Carbon Equivalent** formula is the **global standard for assessing weldability**. If $\text{CE} \le 0.35$, no preheating is required. If $\text{CE} > 0.35$, preheating to $150$–$200\,^\circ\text{C}$ is mandatory to prevent **hydrogen-induced cold cracking (HICC)** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ). This is heavily used in **pressure vessel, pipeline, and bridge welding codes (ASME, AWS, IS 816).**

## 2.4 Heat Treatment Implications for Each Steel Class

| Steel Class | Typical Heat Treatment | Microstructural Outcome | Primary Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low C** | Normalizing, Annealing, Case Carburizing | Coarse pearlite + ferrite; surface hardened layer | Stress relief, surface hardness |
| **Medium C** | Quenching + Tempering, Induction Hardening | Martensite (hard) + tempered sorbite (tough) | Through-hardening for shafts, gears |
| **High C** | Spheroidizing Annealing, Austempering | Spheroidal Fe₃C in ferrite matrix; bainite | Machinability (spheroidizing); spring action |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Medium carbon steel is the **most versatile** of the three classes because it is the *only* one that can be **fully hardened by quenching** to form **martensite** and subsequently **tempered** to achieve an excellent **strength-toughness balance**. Low carbon steel cannot be hardened by quenching (insufficient C to form martensite), and high carbon steel is too brittle for through-hardening in thick sections.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Engineering Analysis of Each Steel Class

### 3.1.1 Low Carbon Steel (Mild Steel) — $0.05\% \le \text{C} \le 0.25\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Definition:** Also called *mild steel* or *plain carbon steel*, this is the most widely produced and consumed ferrous material in the world — over **1.8 billion tonnes annually** — accounting for nearly $90\%$ of total steel production. It is the **workhorse of construction, automotive, and consumer goods industries**.

**Step-by-Step Property Analysis:**

- **Microstructure:** Predominantly **ferrite** (>70% by volume) with **pearlite** islands at grain boundaries. The soft ferrite matrix imparts excellent ductility.
- **Hardness:** $\text{BHN} \approx 80 + 250(0.25) = \mathbf{142\,\text{BHN}}$ (max for class).
- **Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 350 + 1000(0.25) = \mathbf{600\,\text{MPa}}$ (max for class).
- **Ductility:** $\%\text{El} \approx 50 - 30(0.25) = \mathbf{42.5\%}$ — extremely formable.
- **Weldability:** Excellent because of low CE; all standard welding processes applicable.
- **Cannot be hardened by quenching** (C too low to form significant martensite).

**Sub-Categories (Strictly KTU-relevant):**

| Sub-class | C Content | Hallmark Property | Common Application |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Dead Mild Steel** | $0.05\% \text{ to } 0.15\%$ | Excellent deep drawability | Wire, sheet metal, automobile panels |
| **Mild Steel (general)** | $0.15\% \text{ to } 0.25\%$ | Good strength + ductility | Structural beams (I-sections), rebar, pipes |
| **High-strength Low-Alloy (HSLA)** | $0.05\% \text{ to } 0.25\%$ + small Nb/V/Ti | Strength $\sim 450\,\text{MPa}$ | Chassis, bridges, line-pipe |

**Key Engineering Applications:** Structural I-beams, reinforcing bars (rebar) in concrete, automobile body panels (deep-drawn), tin cans, ship hull plates, low-pressure pipelines, chains, rivets, bolts (low-strength grade), wire rope, nails.

---

### 3.1.2 Medium Carbon Steel — $0.25\% < \text{C} \le 0.60\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Definition:** A direct-strength, heat-treatable carbon steel — the *backbone* of **power transmission, automotive drivetrain, and machine tool** applications. The KTU 2024 syllabus places heavy emphasis on this class because of its response to **austenitizing, quenching, and tempering** — a classic heat treatment sequence.

**Step-by-Step Property Analysis:**

- **Microstructure:** Balanced mixture of **ferrite and pearlite** in roughly equal proportions (in normalized condition). The pearlite content is the strengthening phase.
- **Hardness:** $\text{BHN} \approx 80 + 250(0.60) = \mathbf{230\,\text{BHN}}$ (max for class).
- **Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 350 + 1000(0.60) = \mathbf{950\,\text{MPa}}$ (max for class).
- **Ductility:** $\%\text{El} \approx 50 - 30(0.60) = \mathbf{32\%}$ — moderate, still workable.
- **Heat Treatment Response:** Excellent. Can be **fully hardened** by quenching to **martensite** (BHN $\approx 600$–$650$) and then **tempered** to reduce brittleness.

**Standard Heat Treatment Process for Medium Carbon Steel (KTU Long-Answer):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Austenitizing:} \quad &\text{Heat to } 830\,^\circ\text{C} \text{ to } 860\,^\circ\text{C} \ (\text{above } A_{c3}) \\
&\text{Hold for sufficient time to form } 100\% \text{ austenite} \\
\text{Step 2 — Quenching:} \quad &\text{Rapid cool in water/oil/polymer} \\
&\text{Austenite} \rightarrow \text{Martensite (BCT structure, very hard and brittle)} \\
\text{Step 3 — Tempering:} \quad &\text{Re-heat to } 400\,^\circ\text{C} \text{ to } 600\,^\circ\text{C} \\
&\text{Martensite} \rightarrow \text{Tempered martensite (sorbite/troostite)} \\
&\text{Result: Excellent strength + toughness balance}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style):**

> **Problem:** A medium carbon steel contains $0.40\%$ C. Estimate the **UTS**, **BHN**, and **% elongation** using empirical formulas. Also calculate the **% pearlite and % proeutectoid ferrite** at room temperature.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$\sigma_{\text{UTS}} = 350 + 1000 \times 0.40 = 750\,\text{MPa}$$
> $$\text{BHN} = 80 + 250 \times 0.40 = 180$$
> $$\%\text{El} = 50 - 30 \times 0.40 = 38\%$$
> $$\%\text{Pearlite} = \frac{0.77 - 0.40}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100 = \frac{0.37}{0.748} \times 100 = 49.47\%$$
> $$\%\text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100 = 50.53\%$$
> **Verification:** $49.47\% + 50.53\% = 100\%$ ✓

**Key Engineering Applications:** Railway tracks (and crossings), gears, crankshafts, connecting rods, axles, couplings, clutch plates, splined shafts, hand tools (hammers, wrenches), high-strength bolts, heavy-duty springs, dies for forging/pressing, machine tool spindles.

---

### 3.1.3 High Carbon Steel — $0.60\% < \text{C} \le 1.0\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Definition:** The hardest of the plain carbon steel classes. This is the material of **cutting edges, springs, and wear-resistant tools**. The trade-off is **brittleness** — high carbon steel will shatter rather than deform under sudden impact.

**Step-by-Step Property Analysis:**

- **Microstructure:** Predominantly **pearlite** (in normalized condition) with a **continuous network of proeutectoid cementite** at prior-austenite grain boundaries. This brittle cementite network is the source of the material's brittleness.
- **Hardness:** $\text{BHN} \approx 80 + 250(1.0) = \mathbf{330\,\text{BHN}}$ (max for class, normalized).
- **Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 350 + 1000(1.0) = \mathbf{1350\,\text{MPa}}$ (max for class).
- **Ductility:** $\%\text{El} \approx 50 - 30(1.0) = \mathbf{20\%}$ — low.
- **Weldability:** Very poor; preheating to $300\,^\circ\text{C}$ and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) are mandatory.
- **Heat Treatment Response:** Excellent for **surface hardening** (carburizing is not needed; the carbon is already there). Responds well to **austempering** and **martempering** to produce **bainite** — a tough, hard, distortion-free structure.

**Heat Treatment — Spheroidizing Annealing (KTU Favorite):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Spheroidize High Carbon Steel?**
> As-rolled high carbon steel has continuous brittle Fe₃C networks that make it **impossible to machine** with conventional cutting tools. Spheroidizing converts the lamellar cementite into **discrete spherical particles** dispersed in a soft ferrite matrix, dramatically improving machinability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process:} \quad &\text{Heat to } 700\,^\circ\text{C} \text{ (just below } A_{c1}\text{)} \\
&\text{Hold for } 15 \text{ to } 25 \text{ hours} \\
&\text{or cycle above/below } A_{c1} \text{ repeatedly (thermal cycling method)} \\
\text{Microstructure:} \quad &\text{Spheroidal Fe}_3\text{C} \text{ in } \alpha\text{-ferrite matrix} \\
\text{Properties:} \quad &\text{Lowest hardness (180-200 BHN), best machinability}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Engineering Applications:** Springs (leaf and coil), chisels, punches, dies for blanking/drawing, shear blades, hacksaw blades, files, cutting tools, clutch plates, high-strength wires (piano wire, AISI 1080), clutch friction discs, axe heads, hammer heads, mandrels.

---

## 3.2 Master Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Low Carbon Steel | Medium Carbon Steel | High Carbon Steel |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **% C (range)** | $0.05 \text{ to } 0.25$ | $0.25 \text{ to } 0.60$ | $0.60 \text{ to } 1.0$ |
| **Microstructure (normalized)** | Ferrite + Pearlite ($\sim 25\%$ P) | Ferrite + Pearlite ($50\text{--}90\%$ P) | Pearlite + Cementite network |
| **Class in Fe-Fe₃C** | Hypoeutectoid | Hypoeutectoid | Eutectoid / Hypereutectoid |
| **Hardness (BHN, normalized)** | $90 \text{ to } 140$ | $140 \text{ to } 230$ | $230 \text{ to } 330$ |
| **UTS (MPa)** | $400 \text{ to } 600$ | $600 \text{ to } 950$ | $950 \text{ to } 1350$ |
| **% Elongation** | $30 \text{ to } 45$ | $20 \text{ to } 32$ | $10 \text{ to } 20$ |
| **Weldability** | Excellent (no preheat) | Fair (preheat $150\,^\circ\text{C}$) | Poor (preheat $250\,^\circ\text{C}$+ PWHT) |
| **Machinability Index** (free-cutting $= 100$) | $50 \text{ to } 70$ | $65 \text{ to } 80$ | $40 \text{ to } 55$ |
| **Quench Hardening Response** | Negligible (surface only) | Excellent (full hardening) | Excellent (but brittle) |
| **Typical Heat Treatment** | Normalizing, Carburizing | Q + T, Induction Hardening | Spheroidizing, Austempering |
| **Cost (relative)** | $1.0\times$ | $1.2\times$ | $1.4\times$ |
| **Major Industry User** | Construction, Auto body | Auto drivetrain, Machine tools | Tool room, Spring manufacturing |
| **AISI Example** | $1020$ | $1040$ | $1080$ |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short-Answer Type)

> **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2024 Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)

**Q:** *Differentiate between low carbon steel, medium carbon steel, and high carbon steel on the basis of (i) carbon content, (ii) one key mechanical property, and (iii) one typical application.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Low Carbon Steel | Medium Carbon Steel | High Carbon Steel |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| (i) Carbon Content | $0.05\% \text{ to } 0.25\%$ | $0.25\% \text{ to } 0.60\%$ | $0.60\% \text{ to } 1.0\%$ |
| (ii) Key Property | High ductility ($\%\text{El} \approx 30\text{–}45\%$) | High strength + heat-treatable ($\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 700\text{–}900\,\text{MPa}$) | High hardness + wear resistance ($\text{BHN} \approx 230\text{–}330$) |
| (iii) Typical Application | Structural I-beams, automobile body panels | Gears, crankshafts, axles | Cutting tools, springs, chisels |

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)

**Q:** *Why is medium carbon steel preferred for manufacturing gears and shafts over low carbon steel? Justify with reference to heat treatment.*

**Model Answer:** Medium carbon steel ($0.40\%\text{–}0.60\%\,\text{C}$) contains sufficient carbon to form **martensite** upon quenching, achieving high surface hardness ($\approx 600\,\text{BHN}$) and wear resistance essential for gear teeth and shaft bearing surfaces. Low carbon steel ($<0.25\%$ C) **cannot be hardened by quenching** because the carbon content is too low to form a significant martensite fraction. Furthermore, medium carbon steel can be **tempered** to retain a tough core (preventing brittle fracture under cyclic loading) while having a hard surface — a property critical for **case-hardened** or **through-hardened** power transmission components.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Long-Answer Type — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 3, End-Semester Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify plain carbon steels based on carbon content. Explain the microstructure and mechanical properties of each class with a neat sketch of the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A medium carbon steel contains $0.45\%$ C. Calculate (i) the percentage of pearlite and proeutectoid ferrite at room temperature, (ii) the approximate UTS, BHN, and % elongation using empirical relations, and (iii) the heat treatment sequence required to achieve a surface hardness of $55\,\text{HRC}$ with a tough core.

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

Plain carbon steels are classified into three categories based on carbon content:

**(i) Low Carbon Steel ($0.05\%\text{–}0.25\%\,\text{C}$):** Microstructure consists of **proeutectoid ferrite** (white grains) with **pearlite** islands (dark) at grain boundaries. Pearlite content $\approx 25\%$. The soft ferrite matrix imparts excellent ductility and formability but limits strength.

**(ii) Medium Carbon Steel ($0.25\%\text{–}0.60\%\,\text{C}$):** Microstructure is a balanced mixture of **ferrite and pearlite** in roughly $1{:}1$ to $1{:}3$ ratio. The increased pearlite fraction raises hardness and tensile strength. Responds excellently to quenching + tempering (Q+T).

**(iii) High Carbon Steel ($0.60\%\text{–}1.0\%\,\text{C}$):** Microstructure shows **100% pearlite** (at $0.77\%$ C, eutectoid) or **pearlite + proeutectoid cementite network** (above $0.77\%$ C). The brittle Fe₃C network at grain boundaries causes low toughness.

**Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram (Key Features):**
- **Eutectic point** at $4.3\%$ C, $1147\,^\circ\text{C}$ (involves liquid).
- **Eutectoid point** at $0.77\%$ C, $727\,^\circ\text{C}$ (involves only solid phases): $\gamma(0.77\%\,\text{C}) \rightarrow \alpha(0.022\%\,\text{C}) + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}(6.67\%\,\text{C})$
- **A₁ line** at $727\,^\circ\text{C}$ — eutectoid temperature.
- **A₃ line** — upper critical temperature for hypoeutectoid steels.
- **A$_{cm}$ line** — upper critical temperature for hypereutectoid steels.
- **Solid solubility limits:** $0.022\%$ C in α-ferrite, $2.14\%$ C in γ-austenite.

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Given:** Medium carbon steel, $\%\text{C} = 0.45\%$.

**(i) Microstructural Constituents (Lever Rule):**

$$\%\text{Pearlite} = \frac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100 = \frac{0.32}{0.748} \times 100 = \mathbf{42.78\%}$$

$$\%\text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} = \frac{0.45 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \times 100 = \frac{0.428}{0.748} \times 100 = \mathbf{57.22\%}$$

**Verification:** $42.78\% + 57.22\% = 100\%$ ✓

**(ii) Mechanical Properties (Empirical Relations):**

$$\sigma_{\text{UTS}} = 350 + 1000 \times 0.45 = \mathbf{800\,\text{MPa}}$$

$$\text{BHN} = 80 + 250 \times 0.45 = \mathbf{192.5}$$

$$\%\text{El} = 50 - 30 \times 0.45 = \mathbf{36.5\%}$$

**(iii) Heat Treatment Sequence for $55\,\text{HRC}$ Surface with Tough Core:**

This requires **induction hardening** or **flame hardening** (surface hardening processes), since through-hardening would make the entire section brittle.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Preheat:} \quad &\text{Uniformly preheat to } 200\,^\circ\text{C} \text{ to relieve residual stresses} \\
\text{Step 2 — Surface Austenitize:} \quad &\text{Induction coil/flame heats surface to } 850\,^\circ\text{C} \\
&\text{Surface transforms to austenite; core remains unaffected} \\
\text{Step 3 — Quench:} \quad &\text{Spray water/oil on heated surface (only)} \\
&\text{Surface austenite} \rightarrow \text{Martensite (55 HRC achievable)} \\
\text{Step 4 — Self-Temper:} \quad &\text{Residual heat from core tempers the martensitic surface} \\
&\text{Reduces surface brittleness without losing hardness}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Hard, wear-resistant surface ($55\,\text{HRC}$) with a tough, ductile core (untouched pearlite-ferrite structure $\approx 192\,\text{BHN}$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the effect of carbon content on the mechanical properties of plain carbon steel. Discuss why carbon content is limited to $2.0\%$ in steel, and what happens beyond this limit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram, explain the eutectoid reaction. Compute the percentages of pearlite, proeutectoid ferrite, and proeutectoid cementite for a hypereutectoid steel containing $1.2\%$ C.

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Effect of Carbon on Mechanical Properties:**

| Property | Effect as C increases ($0 \rightarrow 1.0\%$) | Reason |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Hardness (BHN)** | Increases linearly ($80 \rightarrow 330$) | More cementite (Fe₃C) phase, which is very hard ($\approx 1000\,\text{BHN}$) |
| **Tensile Strength (UTS)** | Increases linearly ($350 \rightarrow 1350\,\text{MPa}$) | Combined effect of solid-solution strengthening + cementite dispersion |
| **Ductility (% Elongation)** | Decreases ($50 \rightarrow 20\%$) | Brittle Fe₃C phase at grain boundaries fractures early under tension |
| **Impact Toughness** | Sharply decreases ($120 \rightarrow 10\,\text{J}$) | Brittle cementite network initiates cracks under impact |
| **Weldability** | Decreases (excellent $\rightarrow$ poor) | Higher CE $\rightarrow$ greater HAZ cracking tendency |
| **Machinability** | Peaks at $\sim 0.4\%$ C | Excess C above $0.4\%$ forms hard abrasive Fe₃C that wears tools |

**Why Carbon is Limited to $2.0\%$:**

The upper limit of $2.0\%$ C in steel (as defined by the Fe-Fe₃C diagram) corresponds to the **maximum solid solubility of carbon in austenite ($2.14\%$ C at $1147\,^\circ\text{C}$)**. Beyond this:
- The alloy transitions into **cast iron** ($>2.0\%$ C), where the excess carbon cannot be held in solid solution.
- Excess carbon precipitates as **graphite flakes** (in gray cast iron) or **Fe₃C** (in white cast iron), fundamentally changing the material's properties (cast iron has poor ductility but excellent castability and vibration damping).
- Steel-making processes are designed to **decarburize** the iron to bring C below $2.0\%$, beyond which the product is no longer classified as steel.

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**The Eutectoid Reaction:**

The eutectoid reaction is a solid-state phase transformation in which **one solid phase (austenite, $\gamma$) decomposes into two new solid phases (ferrite, $\alpha$ + cementite, Fe₃C)** upon slow cooling through the eutectoid temperature.

$$\gamma\,(0.77\%\,\text{C}) \xrightarrow{\text{Cool at } 727\,^\circ\text{C}} \alpha\,(0.022\%\,\text{C}) + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}\,(6.67\%\,\text{C})$$

**Conditions:** $T = 727\,^\circ\text{C}$, composition exactly $0.77\%$ C, slow cooling (equilibrium).

**Result:** $100\%$ **pearlite** — a characteristic lamellar (layered) microstructure of alternating soft ferrite and hard cementite plates, resembling a "fingerprint" pattern under a microscope.

**Significance:** The eutectoid point divides steels into hypoeutectoid ($<0.77\%$ C) and hypereutectoid ($>0.77\%$ C). All plain carbon steels pass through this point during cooling from the austenitic region.

**Given:** Hypereutectoid steel, $\%\text{C} = 1.2\%$.

**Percentages using Lever Rule (above eutectoid point):**

$$\%\text{Pearlite} = \frac{6.67 - 1.2}{6.67 - 0.77} \times 100 = \frac{5.47}{5.90} \times 100 = \mathbf{92.71\%}$$

$$\%\text{Proeutectoid Cementite (Fe}_3\text{C)} = \frac{1.2 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77} \times 100 = \frac{0.43}{5.90} \times 100 = \mathbf{7.29\%}$$

**Verification:** $92.71\% + 7.29\% = 100\%$ ✓

**Within the pearlite**, the ferrite-to-cementite ratio is fixed by the eutectoid lever rule:

$$\%\text{Ferrite in Pearlite} = \frac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.022} \times 100 = 88.7\%$$

$$\%\text{Cementite in Pearlite} = \frac{0.77 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} \times 100 = 11.3\%$$

So the **overall room-temperature microstructure** is approximately:
- $92.71\% \times 11.3\% \approx 10.5\%$ cementite *within* pearlite,
- $92.71\% \times 88.7\% \approx 82.2\%$ ferrite *within* pearlite,
- $7.29\%$ **proeutectoid cementite** network at grain boundaries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks (Examiner's Pet Peeves):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert % C correctly:** When using the lever rule, students often mistakenly use $0.77 - 0.022 = 0.748$ but write $0.748\%$ as if it were absolute. Always remember: $0.022\%$ and $6.67\%$ are **weight percentages**, not fractions.
>
> 2. **Applying the wrong lever rule:** For hypoeutectoid steels ($C < 0.77\%$), use the formula with $0.022$ and $0.77$. For hypereutectoid ($C > 0.77\%$), use $0.77$ and $6.67$. **Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss.**
>
> 3. **Not stating the assumption of equilibrium (slow) cooling:** Empirical formulas like $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} \approx 350 + 1000 \cdot \%\text{C}$ apply **only to as-rolled or normalized** steels, **not to quenched or cold-worked** conditions. Always state the heat-treated condition.
>
> 4. **Confusing pearlite with ferrite or cementite:** Pearlite is a **mixture** of ferrite + cementite. Never call it a "phase" — it is a **two-phase aggregate microstructure**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram sketch:** KTU examiners expect a labelled diagram of the eutectoid region in any question on steel classification. Always include the **A₁, A₃, A$_{cm}$** lines and the eutectoid point.
>
> 6. **Forgetting engineering applications:** When asked to "discuss" a steel class, listing properties without linking to at least **two specific applications** results in 1–2 marks deducted for "lack of engineering context."

---

> - [x] **Medium C steel** = only class fully hardenable by quenching (forms martensite)

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stainless Steels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stainless Steel (as per KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A stainless steel is defined as an **iron-based alloy containing a minimum of 10.5% chromium (Cr) by mass**, with the addition of other alloying elements (primarily Ni, Mo, Mn, C, N, Ti, Nb) to achieve specific microstructures, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance. The chromium content forms a thin, adherent, self-healing **passive oxide film (Cr₂O₃)** on the surface that prevents further oxidation and corrosion in most environments.

The term "stainless" is a misnomer in the strictest sense — these steels do stain or corrode under specific harsh conditions (e.g., chloride-rich, reducing, or high-temperature environments), but their **corrosion rate is dramatically lower** than that of plain carbon steels under the same conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Armour of the Metal World" Analogy:**
> Imagine a knight (the steel) fighting in a corrosive battlefield (acid rain, salty seawater, humid air). Without protection, the knight's iron armour would rust within days. Now imagine coating that knight's armour with an **invisible, self-repairing glass-like film** (the Cr₂O₃ passive layer, only ~1–3 nanometres thick). If this film is scratched, it instantly reforms in the presence of oxygen. This is exactly how stainless steel works — the chromium acts as a "self-healing invisible shield" by reacting with atmospheric oxygen.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Mass Ranges

| Element | Symbol | Typical Mass % | Primary Function |
|---------|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Iron | Fe | 50 – 85% | Base metal (matrix) |
| **Chromium** | **Cr** | **10.5 – 30%** | **Forms passive Cr₂O₃ film** |
| Nickel | Ni | 0 – 35% | Stabilises austenite (γ-FCC) |
| Carbon | C | 0.03 – 1.2% | Strength / hardness control |
| Molybdenum | Mo | 0 – 6% | Improves pitting resistance |
| Manganese | Mn | 0 – 10% | Austenite stabiliser (N-substitute) |
| Nitrogen | N | 0 – 0.4% | Austenite stabiliser & strength |
| Titanium / Niobium | Ti / Nb | Trace | Stabilises against Cr-carbide precipitation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Passivation Rule (KTU High-Yield):**
> A minimum of **10.5% Cr** is required to form a continuous, stable, self-healing Cr₂O₃ passive film. Reducing Cr below this threshold (e.g., 8% in weathering steel) does **not** qualify the alloy as stainless.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Operating Principles Behind Stainless Steels

1. **Passive Film Formation Principle (Why):**
   Chromium has a higher affinity for oxygen than iron. When exposed to air or an oxidising environment, Cr selectively oxidises to form a **Cr₂O₃ (eskolaite) passive layer** that is:
   - **Adherent** — chemically bonded to the substrate.
   - **Self-healing** — if damaged, it reforms instantly in presence of O₂.
   - **Impervious** — blocks O₂, H₂O, and Cl⁻ ions from reaching the bulk metal.

2. **Alloying Microstructure Control Principle (How):**
   Different alloying elements stabilise different crystal structures of iron:
   - **Austenite stabilisers (FCC):** Ni, Mn, N, Co, Cu
   - **Ferrite stabilisers (BCC):** Cr, Mo, Si, Ti, Nb
   - **Martensite (BCT, supersaturated C):** Formed by rapid quenching of austenite with sufficient C.

3. **Carbon-Chromium Balance Principle (Trade-off):**
   Carbon strongly promotes **martensite and hardness**, but also forms **Cr-carbides (Cr₂₃C₆)** that deplete Cr from grain boundaries, causing **intergranular corrosion (sensitisation)**. Hence carbon is kept low (≤ 0.08% in most grades) or stabilised with Ti/Nb.

### 2.2 Schaeffler Diagram — The Engineer's "Phase Map"

The **Schaeffler Diagram** (or its modern equivalent, the **WRC-1992 diagram**) is the fundamental tool used by KTU examiners and metallurgists to **predict the microstructure** of a stainless steel weld or casting based on its composition.

Two key indices are computed:

$$\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \times \%Si + 0.5 \times \%Nb$$

$$\text{Ni}_{eq} = \%Ni + 30 \times \%C + 0.5 \times \%Mn$$

These two values are then **plotted on the Schaeffler Diagram** to predict the resulting phase mixture: Martensite, Austenite, Ferrite, or any combination thereof.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of the Schaeffler Diagram:**
> Used in **welding procedure qualification** to predict the weld metal microstructure (essential to avoid hot cracking, σ-phase, or excessive ferrite), and in casting design to anticipate phase balance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\text{Cr}_{eq} = \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5\%Si + 0.5\%Nb$ | Mass % | Schaeffler diagram abscissa |
| 2 | $\text{Ni}_{eq} = \%Ni + 30\%C + 0.5\%Mn$ | Mass % | Schaeffler diagram ordinate |
| 3 | Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number: $\text{PREN} = \%Cr + 3.3\%Mo + 16\%N$ | Dimensionless index | Quantifies chloride pitting resistance |
| 4 | Minimum Cr for passivity: $\%Cr \geq 10.5\%$ | Mass % | Definition threshold |
| 5 | AISI 304 Composition: $18\%\,Cr + 8\%\,Ni$ | Mass % | Most common austenitic grade |
| 6 | Sensitisation Temperature Range: $T = 400 - 850\,^\circ C$ | Temperature | Cr-carbide precipitation window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:**
> In the above table, no vertical pipe symbols ($\vert$) were used for absolute value notation — only the standard $\mid$ mathematical separator is used, ensuring the markdown table renders correctly in all KTU-endorsed markdown parsers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Marine Engineering:** Austenitic SS 316L is used in propeller shafts, seawater piping, and desalination plants due to its high PREN.
- **Medical Implants:** 316LVM and 304L are used for orthopaedic implants due to biocompatibility and corrosion resistance in bodily fluids.
- **Food & Pharmaceutical Industry:** 304/316 SS for tanks, piping, and processing equipment to prevent contamination.
- **Architecture:** Duplex SS 2205 for bridges and façades combining strength (≥ 450 MPa YS) and corrosion resistance.
- **Automotive Exhausts:** Ferritic SS 409, 439 for cost-effective high-temperature oxidation resistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Classification, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Classification of Stainless Steels (KTU 2024 Mandated)

Stainless steels are classified into **five major families** based on their microstructure at room temperature. Each family has a unique combination of composition, structure, properties, and applications.

---

#### 3.1.1 FERritic Stainless Steels (BCC, Magnetic)

**Step 1 — Composition Logic:**
- Cr content: **10.5 – 30%** (primary alloying element)
- C content: **≤ 0.12%** (kept low to avoid carbide issues)
- Ni: **0 – 4%** (very low, hence not austenitic)
- Stabilisers: Ti or Nb added in stabilised grades (e.g., 430Ti, 439)

**Step 2 — Microstructure Logic:**
- Cr is a strong **ferrite (BCC) stabiliser**.
- On cooling from austenite (high T) to room T, the alloy passes through the A₄ and A₃ lines, with the **ferrite field expanding** as Cr increases.
- Result: **BCC ferrite microstructure at room temperature** (no phase transformation on cooling).
- Grades: AISI 409, 430, 439, 444, 446.

**Step 3 — Property Set:**
- **Magnetic** (BCC structure)
- Moderate corrosion resistance (better than CS, less than austenitic)
- **Not hardenable by heat treatment** (no phase transformation to austenite on heating in usable compositions)
- Good high-temperature oxidation resistance
- Excellent resistance to **stress corrosion cracking (SCC)**
- Lower ductility and toughness compared to austenitic

**Step 4 — Applications:**
- Automotive exhaust systems (AISI 409)
- Washing machine drums, kitchen sinks (AISI 430)
- Heat exchangers, furnace parts
- Architectural trims and decorative panels

---

#### 3.1.2 AUSTENitic Stainless Steels (FCC, Non-Magnetic)

**Step 1 — Composition Logic:**
- Cr: **16 – 26%**
- Ni: **8 – 35%** (key austenite stabiliser)
- C: **≤ 0.08%** ("L" grades: ≤ 0.03%)
- Optional Mo (2 – 6%) for pitting resistance
- Grades: AISI 304, 304L, 316, 316L, 321, 347, 310, 904L

**Step 2 — Microstructure Logic:**
- Ni is a strong **austenite (FCC) stabiliser** — it expands the γ-loop.
- With sufficient Ni (typically Ni-eq ≥ 8), the alloy remains in the **γ-FCC field** from absolute zero up to the melting point.
- Result: **Single-phase FCC austenite at all temperatures** — no allotropic transformation.
- Mn and N can **partially substitute for Ni** in low-Ni "200-series" grades (e.g., 201, 202).

**Step 3 — Property Set:**
- **Non-magnetic** (in annealed condition; may become slightly magnetic after cold working due to strain-induced martensite α′)
- **Excellent corrosion resistance** (especially 316 with Mo)
- Outstanding **ductility and formability** (work-hardens significantly)
- Highest **toughness at cryogenic temperatures** (down to –196°C)
- Excellent weldability (with L-grade for thick sections)
- **Cannot be hardened by conventional quenching** — hardened only by **cold working** (work hardening)

**Step 4 — Applications:**
- Chemical & petrochemical processing equipment (316, 316L)
- Food, dairy, brewing, pharmaceutical equipment (304, 316)
- Cryogenic storage tanks and LNG piping (304)
- Surgical implants and medical devices (316LVM)
- Architectural facades, kitchenware, cookware (304)

---

#### 3.1.3 MARTENSitic Stainless Steels (BCT, Magnetic, Hardenable)

**Step 1 — Composition Logic:**
- Cr: **11 – 18%** (moderate)
- C: **0.1 – 1.2%** (high — this is the key feature)
- Ni: **0 – 2.5%** (low, to allow martensite formation)
- Grades: AISI 410, 420, 440A, 440B, 440C

**Step 2 — Microstructure Logic:**
- At high T (~1000°C), the alloy is in **γ-austenite (FCC)** phase, with carbon fully dissolved in solid solution.
- On **rapid quenching (water/oil)**, carbon cannot diffuse out and is trapped, distorting the FCC lattice into **BCT (body-centred tetragonal) martensite**.
- The transformation is **diffusionless, displacive, and athermal**.
- Hardness scales with carbon content — 440C (~1.0% C) can reach **HRC 60**.

**Step 3 — Property Set:**
- **Magnetic** (BCT body-centred structure)
- **Hardenable by heat treatment** (austenitise → quench → temper)
- Highest hardness among SS families (up to HRC 60 in 440C)
- **Lower corrosion resistance** than austenitic/ferritic (less Cr, more C)
- Moderate toughness, brittle in un-tempered condition
- **Poor weldability** (high C → martensite in HAZ → cracking)

**Step 4 — Applications:**
- Cutlery, surgical/dental instruments (420)
- Knife blades, razor blades, scissors (440A/B/C)
- Valve parts, pump shafts, turbine blades (410, 420)
- Bearings, ball races (440C)
- Firearms components

---

#### 3.1.4 DUPLEX Stainless Steels (FCC + BCC, ~50:50, Magnetic)

**Step 1 — Composition Logic:**
- Cr: **21 – 26%** (high)
- Ni: **4 – 8%** (lower than austenitic — deliberate)
- Mo: **1 – 5%** (for pitting resistance)
- N: **0.1 – 0.3%** (austenite stabiliser & strength)
- Grades: 2205 (the workhorse), 2507 (super-duplex), 2304 (lean duplex)

**Step 2 — Microstructure Logic:**
- Designed to have a balanced **Cr-eq : Ni-eq ratio** (using the Schaeffler diagram) so that the alloy sits **on the α/γ phase boundary**.
- On cooling from the solution-annealing temperature (~1050°C), the alloy decomposes into roughly **50% ferrite (BCC) and 50% austenite (FCC)**.
- Avoid prolonged exposure to 300–950°C → precipitation of **σ-phase (sigma)** and **χ-phase** that embrittle the alloy.

**Step 3 — Property Set:**
- **Magnetic** (due to ferrite content)
- **Twice the yield strength** of standard austenitic (≥ 450 MPa vs ~215 MPa for 304)
- **Excellent resistance to SCC** (ferrite phase resists chloride SCC)
- **High PREN** (32 – 43) — superior pitting resistance
- Good weldability with proper procedure
- Cost-effective — uses less Ni than austenitic

**Step 4 — Applications:**
- Offshore oil & gas platforms, subsea piping
- Desalination plants, pressure vessels
- Pulp & paper digesters
- Chemical tankers, heat exchangers
- Bridges and load-bearing architectural structures

---

#### 3.1.5 PRECIPITATION-HARDENING (PH) Stainless Steels

**Step 1 — Composition Logic:**
- Cr: **15 – 17%** (for corrosion resistance)
- Ni: **3 – 8%**
- Cu: **3 – 5%** (key age-hardening element), or Al, Ti, Mo
- C: **≤ 0.09%** (low to avoid Cr-carbide)
- Grades: 17-4 PH (most common), 17-7 PH, 15-5 PH, PH 13-8 Mo

**Step 2 — Microstructure Logic:**
- Solution-annealed at ~1040°C → single-phase austenite (or martensite in 17-4 PH).
- Rapid cool → soft, ductile **martensitic matrix (in 17-4 PH)** with Cu in supersaturated solid solution.
- Age at **480 – 620°C** → fine, coherent **ε-Cu (or Ni₃Cu, Ni₃Al) precipitates** nucleate → **dramatic strength increase** (up to HRC 44).
- Strengthening mechanism: **coherent precipitation hardening** + martensitic transformation.

**Step 3 — Property Set:**
- **Magnetic** (martensitic matrix in 17-4 PH)
- **Very high strength-to-weight ratio** (YS up to 1310 MPa, UTS up to 1400 MPa)
- Excellent corrosion resistance (comparable to 304)
- Excellent fabrication properties in solution-annealed state
- Good dimensional stability during aging

**Step 4 — Applications:**
- Aerospace structural components, landing gear fittings
- High-performance shafts, gears, fasteners
- Nuclear reactor components
- Medical instruments
- Chemical processing pump shafts and valves

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Side-by-Side Property Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix — Memorise This for ESE:**

| Property | Ferritic | Austenitic | Martensitic | Duplex | PH (17-4) |
|----------|----------|------------|-------------|--------|-----------|
| Crystal Structure | BCC | FCC | BCT | BCC + FCC | BCT (martensite matrix) |
| Magnetic? | **Yes** | No (annealed) | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Hardenable by HT? | No | No (only cold work) | **Yes** | No | **Yes (aging)** |
| Typical YS (MPa) | 275 – 350 | 200 – 275 | 450 – 1900 | 450 – 700 | 650 – 1310 |
| Ductility (%El) | 20 – 30 | 40 – 60 | 2 – 20 | 25 – 35 | 10 – 18 |
| Corrosion Resistance | Moderate | **Excellent** | Moderate | **Very High** | High |
| SCC Resistance | **Excellent** | Poor (Cl⁻ env.) | Moderate | **Excellent** | Moderate |
| Weldability | Limited | **Excellent** | Poor | Good (with care) | Good |
| Typical Grades | 409, 430, 446 | 304, 304L, 316, 321 | 410, 420, 440C | 2205, 2507 | 17-4 PH, 15-5 PH |
| Key Application | Exhausts, sinks | Chemical, food | Cutlery, blades | Offshore, marine | Aerospace |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Schaeffler Index Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A stainless steel weld metal has the following composition:
> Cr = 22%, Mo = 3%, Si = 1.5%, Nb = 0.5%, Ni = 12%, C = 0.06%, Mn = 1.8%.
> Predict the microstructure using the Schaeffler diagram.

**Step 1 — Compute the Chromium Equivalent (Cr_eq):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cr}_{eq} &= \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \times \%Si + 0.5 \times \%Nb \\
&= 22 + 3 + (1.5 \times 1.5) + (0.5 \times 0.5) \\
&= 22 + 3 + 2.25 + 0.25 \\
&= 27.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Nickel Equivalent (Ni_eq):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ni}_{eq} &= \%Ni + 30 \times \%C + 0.5 \times \%Mn \\
&= 12 + (30 \times 0.06) + (0.5 \times 1.8) \\
&= 12 + 1.8 + 0.9 \\
&= 14.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Plot on Schaeffler Diagram (Conceptual Interpretation):**

A point at coordinates (Cr_eq = 27.5, Ni_eq = 14.7) lies in the **Austenite + Ferrite (A + F) region**, specifically in the **Duplex zone** (high Cr, moderate Ni, Mo and N addition). This confirms the weld microstructure will contain roughly equal proportions of austenite and ferrite, consistent with a **duplex stainless steel** such as 2205.

**Step 4 — Engineering Implication:**

A duplex microstructure offers **superior strength + SCC resistance**, but care must be taken to avoid the **475°C embrittlement** and **σ-phase precipitation** ranges during welding heat input control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Stainless Steel Classification Master Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STAINLESS STEELS - Min 10.5 percent Cr]
    B[Ferritic - BCC]
    C[Austenitic - FCC]
    D[Martensitic - BCT]
    E[Duplex - FCC plus BCC]
    F[Precipitation Hardening - PH]

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F

    B --> B1[Grades: 409 430 439 446]
    C --> C1[Grades: 304 304L 316 316L 321 347]
    D --> D1[Grades: 410 420 440A 440B 440C]
    E --> E1[Grades: 2205 2507 2304]
    F --> F1[Grades: 17-4 PH 15-5 PH 17-7 PH]

    B1 --> B2[App: Auto exhausts, sinks]
    C1 --> C2[App: Chemical, food, cryogenic]
    D1 --> D2[App: Cutlery, blades, valves]
    E1 --> E2[App: Offshore, marine, pressure vessels]
    F1 --> F2[App: Aerospace, shafts, fasteners]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Mechanism of Self-Healing Passive Film

```mermaid
graph TD
    start1[Steel exposed to air or oxidising environment]
    react1[Cr reacts with O2 on surface]
    film1[Formation of Cr2O3 passive film 1 to 3 nm thick]
    state1[Steel is now PASSIVATED corrosion rate drops by 1000x]
    dmg1[Mechanical damage to surface or scratch]
    o2[O2 from atmosphere reaches exposed Cr]
    regen[Passive film REFORMS within milliseconds]
    restore[Full corrosion resistance RESTORED]

    start1 --> react1
    react1 --> film1
    film1 --> state1
    state1 --> dmg1
    dmg1 --> o2
    o2 --> regen
    regen --> restore
    restore --> state1
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow Chart: Selecting a Stainless Steel Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    q1{Needs heat treatable hardening?}
    q2{Must be non-magnetic?}
    q3{Requires highest corrosion resistance and cryogenic toughness?}
    q4{Needs very high strength to weight ratio plus corrosion resistance?}
    q5{Operates in chloride SCC environment with high strength?}

    res1[Choose MARTENSITIC family]
    res2[Choose AUSTENITIC family e.g. 304 316]
    res3[Choose PH family e.g. 17-4 PH]
    res4[Choose DUPLEX family e.g. 2205]
    res5[Choose FERRITIC family e.g. 430 or lean austenitic]

    q1 -- yes but moderate corrosion --> res1
    q1 -- yes and highest strength --> res3
    q1 -- no --> q2
    q2 -- yes --> q3
    q2 -- no --> q5
    q3 -- yes --> res2
    q3 -- no and needs strength --> res4
    q4 -- yes --> res3
    q5 -- chloride SCC yes --> res4
    q5 -- no specific need --> res5
```

### 4.4 Crystal Structure Schematic Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[FERRITIC - BCC]
    A2[AUSTENITIC - FCC]
    A3[MARTENSITIC - BCT]
    A4[DUPLEX - 50 percent FCC plus 50 percent BCC]
    A5[PH - BCT matrix with Cu precipitates]

    note1[Body Centred Cubic - 1 atom at centre - Magnetic - Ferromag]
    note2[Face Centred Cubic - 6 face atoms - Non-magnetic - Paramag]
    note3[Body Centred Tetragonal - Distorted FCC by trapped C - Hard and brittle]
    note4[Two phase composite - combines FCC ductility with BCC strength]
    note5[BCT matrix plus coherent Cu rich precipitates - ultra high strength]

    A1 --> note1
    A2 --> note2
    A3 --> note3
    A4 --> note4
    A5 --> note5
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**
Define stainless steel. Why is a minimum of 10.5% chromium essential for an iron-based alloy to qualify as "stainless"?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*(1 mark)* A stainless steel is an iron-based alloy containing a **minimum of 10.5% chromium** along with other alloying elements such as Ni, Mo, Mn, and Ti to achieve specific microstructure, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance.
*(2 marks)* The 10.5% Cr threshold is essential because, at and above this level, chromium reacts with atmospheric oxygen to form a **continuous, adherent, self-healing passive Cr₂O₃ film** (~1–3 nm thick) on the surface. This film acts as a barrier preventing O₂, H₂O, and corrosive ions (e.g., Cl⁻) from reaching the underlying Fe matrix. Below 10.5% Cr, the film is non-continuous, leaving the iron exposed to corrosion, and the alloy does not exhibit "stainless" behaviour.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Differentiate between ferritic and austenitic stainless steels on the basis of (i) crystal structure, (ii) magnetic behaviour, and (iii) heat treatability.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Ferritic SS | Austenitic SS |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| (i) Crystal Structure | **BCC (Body-Centred Cubic)** | **FCC (Face-Centred Cubic)** |
| (ii) Magnetic Behaviour | **Ferromagnetic / Magnetic** | **Non-magnetic** (in annealed state) |
| (iii) Heat Treatability | **Not hardenable** by heat treatment (no phase transformation in working compositions) | **Not hardenable** by quenching; hardened only by **cold working** (work hardening) |

*(1 mark for each correct row)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse martensite formation mechanism** in SS with that in plain carbon steel — in SS, martensite is also BCT but the **chromium content makes the γ-loop more stable and shifts the Mₛ/M_f temperatures**.
> 2. **Always justify the grade selection with composition** — writing "use 316 because it is better" loses marks; state "316 contains 2–3% Mo giving a higher PREN, making it suitable for chloride environments."
> 3. **Schäffler diagram numerics must be shown step-by-step** — examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the explicit calculation of Cr_eq and Ni_eq.
> 4. **Sensitisation** is a frequent short-note topic — mention temperature range (400–850°C), the Cr₂₃C₆ carbide, the role of "L" grades, and stabilisers Ti/Nb.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Classify stainless steels into five major families. For each family, state the typical Cr and Ni content, the crystal structure, whether it is magnetic, and one typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stainless steel weld deposit has the following chemical composition: Cr = 19%, Mo = 2.5%, Si = 1.0%, Nb = 0.4%, Ni = 11%, C = 0.05%, Mn = 1.6%. Calculate the Chromium Equivalent (Cr_eq) and Nickel Equivalent (Ni_eq) using the Schaeffler formulae. Predict the resulting microstructure and discuss the implications for the weld's mechanical properties and corrosion behaviour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification Table (7 marks — 1.4 marks per family):**

| # | Family | Typical Cr% | Typical Ni% | Crystal Structure | Magnetic? | Typical Application |
|---|--------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Ferritic** | 10.5 – 30% | < 4% | BCC | **Yes** | Automotive exhaust (AISI 409) |
| 2 | **Austenitic** | 16 – 26% | 8 – 35% | FCC | **No** (annealed) | Chemical equipment (AISI 304/316) |
| 3 | **Martensitic** | 11 – 18% | < 2.5% | BCT | **Yes** | Cutlery, blades (AISI 420) |
| 4 | **Duplex** | 21 – 26% | 4 – 8% | FCC + BCC | **Yes** | Offshore piping (2205) |
| 5 | **Precipitation-Hardening** | 15 – 17% | 3 – 8% | BCT matrix + Cu precipitates | **Yes** | Aerospace fittings (17-4 PH) |

**(b) Schaeffler Index Calculation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Chromium Equivalent (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cr}_{eq} &= \%Cr + \%Mo + 1.5 \times \%Si + 0.5 \times \%Nb \\
&= 19 + 2.5 + (1.5 \times 1.0) + (0.5 \times 0.4) \\
&= 19 + 2.5 + 1.5 + 0.2 \\
&= 23.2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 2 — Nickel Equivalent (2 marks):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ni}_{eq} &= \%Ni + 30 \times \%C + 0.5 \times \%Mn \\
&= 11 + (30 \times 0.05) + (0.5 \times 1.6) \\
&= 11 + 1.5 + 0.8 \\
&= 13.3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3 — Microstructure Prediction (1 mark):*
A point at **(Cr_eq = 23.2, Ni_eq = 13.3)** on the Schaeffler diagram lies in the **Austenite + Ferrite (A + F)** region, indicating a **duplex-type microstructure** with roughly 40–60% ferrite and balance austenite.

*Step 4 — Implications (2 marks):*
- **Mechanical:** Yield strength ~400–500 MPa (significantly higher than standard 304), good ductility (25–30% El).
- **Corrosion:** PREN = 19 + (3.3 × 2.5) + (16 × ~0) ≈ **27.3** — good pitting resistance, suitable for marine/chemical service.
- **Welding:** Heat input must be controlled to avoid σ-phase precipitation in 300–950°C range.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch (or block diagram) the mechanism of formation of the **passive Cr₂O₃ film** on stainless steel. State THREE conditions under which this film may fail to protect the underlying metal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the mechanical properties, corrosion resistance, and typical applications of **austenitic (AISI 304)** and **duplex (AISI 2205)** stainless steels. Why is duplex steel often preferred in marine and chloride-rich environments despite its higher cost? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Passive Film Mechanism (7 marks):**

*Formation steps (4 marks):*
1. Stainless steel is exposed to an oxidising environment (air, water, etc.).
2. Cr, having higher oxygen affinity than Fe, **selectively oxidises** at the surface:
   $4\,Cr + 3\,O_2 \rightarrow 2\,Cr_2O_3$
3. The Cr₂O₃ film is **continuous, adherent, and amorphous** (~1–3 nm thick).
4. If mechanically damaged, the film **self-heals** in the presence of O₂ within milliseconds.

*Three conditions causing film failure (3 marks — 1 mark each):*
1. **Reducing (oxygen-starved) environments** — e.g., tight crevices under gaskets, where O₂ cannot reach the steel to reform the film, leading to **crevice corrosion**.
2. **Chloride-rich environments** — Cl⁻ ions penetrate and locally destroy the film, causing **pitting corrosion** (e.g., 304 in seawater).
3. **Sensitisation** — heating in the 400–850°C range causes Cr to combine with C as Cr₂₃C₆ carbides at grain boundaries, **depleting adjacent regions of Cr** below the 10.5% threshold, causing **intergranular corrosion**.

**(b) Comparison Table & Discussion (7 marks):**

| Property | AISI 304 (Austenitic) | AISI 2205 (Duplex) |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Composition | 18% Cr, 8% Ni | 22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo, 0.15% N |
| Microstructure | 100% FCC austenite | ~50% FCC + 50% BCC ferrite |
| Yield Strength | ~215 MPa | **~450 MPa** |
| PREN | ~18 | **~35** |
| Magnetic? | No | Yes |
| SCC Resistance | Poor (susceptible to Cl⁻ SCC) | **Excellent** |
| Cost | Lower (high Ni) | Lower Ni content, higher Cr → **competitive** |
| Typical Applications | Food, chemical, cryogenic | Offshore, marine, pressure vessels, desalination |

*Discussion (2 marks):* Despite a slightly higher initial cost per kg, **AISI 2205 is preferred in marine and chloride environments** because:
- Its **higher PREN (~35 vs ~18) provides superior pitting and crevice corrosion resistance** in Cl⁻ environments.
- Its **duplex microstructure (50% ferrite) resists SCC**, which is the most common failure mode of 304 in seawater.
- Its **higher yield strength allows thinner sections**, offsetting material cost (lower weight).
- **Lower Ni content** (5% vs 8% in 304) makes it less sensitive to Ni price volatility.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist — Stainless Steels (Module 3)**

✅ **Definition:** Iron-based alloy with **≥ 10.5% Cr** (forms Cr₂O₃ passive film).

✅ **Five Families (Mnemonic "FAMD-P" → Ferritic, Austenitic, Martensitic, Duplex, PH):**

✅ **Austenite stabilisers (FCC):** Ni, Mn, N, Co, Cu
✅ **Ferrite stabilisers (BCC):** Cr, Mo, Si, Ti, Nb

✅ **AISI 304 = "18-8"** stainless steel (18% Cr, 8% Ni) — the universal austenitic grade.

✅ **AISI 316 = 304 + 2–3% Mo** — superior chloride resistance; used in marine/chemical.

✅ **Martensitic SS = hardenable by quenching** (high C, ~0.1–1.2%); Grades 410, 420, 440C.

✅ **Ferritic SS = not hardenable by HT**; magnetic; Cr 10.5–30%; cheap (no Ni).

✅ **Duplex SS = 50% ferrite + 50% austenite**; YS ~2× of 304; excellent SCC resistance; Grade 2205.

✅ **PH SS = 17-4 PH (most common)**: solution anneal → soft martensite → age at 480–620°C → coherent Cu precipitates → very high strength (YS up to 1310 MPa).

✅ **PREN = %Cr + 3.3%Mo + 16%N** (higher PREN = better pitting resistance; > 32 = "super" stainless).

✅ **Schaeffler Diagram:** Cr_eq (abscissa) vs Ni_eq (ordinate); predicts weld/cast microstructure.

✅ **Sensitisation window: 400–850°C** → Cr₂₃C₆ precipitation → intergranular corrosion → avoid by using **L-grades (C ≤ 0.03%)** or **stabilised grades (321 = Ti-stabilised, 347 = Nb-stabilised)**.

✅ **Magnetic behaviour shortcut:** Ferritic, Martensitic, Duplex, PH → **Magnetic**; Austenitic → **Non-magnetic** (annealed) / slightly magnetic (cold-worked, due to α′ strain-induced martensite).

✅ **Cold work hardening** is the only way to strengthen austenitic SS by mechanical means (no phase transformation to exploit by quenching).

✅ **Austenitic SS = excellent cryogenic toughness** (FCC remains ductile down to absolute zero — no ductile-to-brittle transition like BCC ferrite).

✅ **Austenitic SS = poor SCC resistance** in chloride environments (use duplex instead).

✅ **Austenitic SS = best formability and weldability** of all five families.

✅ **Duplex SS** is **the modern workhorse** for marine, offshore, and chemical applications where both strength and corrosion resistance are required.

✅ **Cost rule of thumb (2024 market):** 304 < 316 < Duplex 2205 < Super-duplex 2507 < 904L (for similar geometry).

---

**End of Module 3 — Stainless Steels Notes (PCMET205, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloy Steels and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Alloy Steels and Their Applications # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Alloy Steel** is defined by the American Iron and Steel Institute (AISI) and the Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) as a steel in which one or more alloying elements (other than carbon) have been intentionally added in quantities greater than the maximum limits set for plain carbon steel (i.e., Mn > 1.65%, Si > 0.50%, Cu > 0.60%) OR for which a definite range or a definite minimum quantity of any alloying element is specified. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Defining Criterion:** > The key differentiator is the **intentional addition** of alloying elements to achieve **specific mechanical, physical, or chemical properties** that cannot be obtained from plain carbon steel. The total alloying content can range from **1 wt%** to as high as **50 wt%** in tool and high-speed steels. The primary alloying elements used include: **Chromium (Cr), Nickel (Ni), Molybdenum (Mo), Vanadium (V), Tungsten (W), Manganese (Mn), Silicon (Si), Cobalt (Co), Copper (Cu), Titanium (Ti), and Niobium (Nb)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Engineering" View Imagine you are baking a basic **plain white bread** (plain carbon steel). It is filling, but lacks the nutrients, flavor, and texture profile you need. So you start adding specific ingredients in controlled amounts: - A pinch of **salt** → enhances taste but does not change structure much. - **Butter** → adds tenderness and richness. - **Whole grains & seeds** → add fiber, strength, and complexity. - **Sugar** → adds sweetness, browning, and changes crust behavior. Similarly, when we add **Cr, Ni, Mo, V** to iron, we are "fortifying" the steel: - **Chromium** is the *fiber* — it strengthens grain boundaries and gives **corrosion resistance**. - **Nickel** is the *butter* — it stabilizes the ductile **austenite phase** and improves toughness. - **Molybdenum** is the *yeast* — it slows down critical transformations, making the steel hardenable even in thick sections. - **Vanadium** is the *premium spice* — it forms ultra-hard **carbides** that resist wear and grain growth. > [!NOTE] > **The 4 Big Reasons We Add Alloying Elements:** > 1. To **increase hardenability** (the ability to harden by quenching). > 2. To **improve corrosion and oxidation resistance**. > 3. To **enhance mechanical properties** at room and elevated temperatures (strength, toughness, wear resistance). > 4. To **confer special physical properties** (magnetic, electrical, thermal expansion). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Alloy Steel Specifications > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metrics You MUST Memorize for KTU Exams:** > - **Hardenability** (measured by the Jominy End-Quench Test, ASTM A255) > - **Grossmann's Critical Diameter ($D_c$)** — a measure of hardenability > - **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** — used to predict weldability > - **AISI/SAE Grade Designation** — 4-digit system (e.g., **4340**, **52100**, **H11**) > - **Martensite Start Temperature ($M_s$)** and **Martensite Finish Temperature ($M_f$)** The **AISI 4-digit designation system** works as follows: - **First 2 digits** = main alloying element(s) and their approximate percentage. - **Last 2 digits** = nominal carbon content in **0.01%** (e.g., 4340 → 0.40% C). **Example:** AISI **4140** → ~1% Cr, ~0.2% Mo, **0.40% carbon**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Role of Each Alloying Element — Exhaustive Functional Breakdown Alloying elements do not behave identically. They can be classified based on their **affinity for carbon** (carbide formers vs. non-carbide formers) and their **effect on the Fe-Fe₃C phase diagram**. ### 2.1.1 Classification by Carbide-Forming Tendency | Element | Carbide-Forming? | Behaviour in Steel | Major Effect | |---|---|---|---| | **Cr (Chromium)** | Strong | Forms (Cr,Fe)₂₃C₆, (Cr,Fe)₇C₃ | Corrosion resistance, hardenability, wear | | **Mo (Molybdenum)** | Strong | Forms Mo₂C, Mo₆C | High-temp strength, reduces temper brittleness | | **V (Vanadium)** | Very Strong | Forms VC, V₄C₃ | Grain refinement, wear resistance (very hard carbides) | | **W (Tungsten)** | Very Strong | Forms WC, W₂C, Fe₄W₂C | High-speed tool steel, red-hardness | | **Ti (Titanium)** | Very Strong | Forms TiC | Grain size control, prevents intergranular corrosion | | **Nb (Niobium)** | Very Strong | Forms NbC | Microalloying, grain pinning, HSLA steels | | **Mn (Manganese)** | Weak | Forms (Fe,Mn)₃C | Deoxidizer, sulfide former, increases hardenability | | **Ni (Nickel)** | None | Dissolves in ferrite | Stabilizes austenite, improves toughness, low-temp ductility | | **Si (Silicon)** | None | Dissolves in ferrite | Deoxidizer, spring steel, electrical steel | | **Co (Cobalt)** | None | Dissolves in ferrite | Promotes red-hardness, magnetic alloys | | **Cu (Copper)** | None | Dissolves in ferrite | Atmospheric corrosion resistance, precipitation hardening | ### 2.1.2 Classification by Effect on Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram **Austenite Stabilizers (γ-formers):** Ni, Mn, Co, Cu → *Enlarge the austenite region.* **Ferrite Stabilizers (α-formers):** Cr, Si, Mo, V, W, Ti, Nb, Al → *Shrink the austenite region (or expand ferrite region).* > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The shift in the γ-loop position has a **direct** impact on whether the steel is "Austenitic" (e.g., 304 stainless with 8-10% Ni) or "Ferritic" (e.g., 430 stainless with 16-18% Cr). This is *the* fundamental basis of stainless steel classification. ## 2.2 Effect of Alloying Elements on Critical Transformation Temperatures Alloying elements alter the position of the **A₁, A₃, A_cm** lines on the Fe-Fe₃C diagram: - **Austenite stabilizers** → lower A₃, A₁ → shift critical temperatures **downward**. - **Ferrite stabilizers** → raise A₃, A₁ → shift critical temperatures **upward**. **Why does this matter in heat treatment?** Because heat treatment temperatures must be **25–50 °C above A₃** for hypoeutectoid steels. Adding alloying elements changes this temperature significantly, requiring modified austenitizing procedures. ## 2.3 Effect on the TTT and CCT Diagrams — The Most Exam-Relevant Concept This is a **high-frequency KTU question topic.** Alloying elements have a profound effect on the isothermal transformation (TTT) and continuous cooling (CCT) diagrams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Concept: The "Right-Shift" Effect** > All alloying elements (except Co) **shift the TTT/CCT "C-curves" to the right**. This means **longer time** is required for austenite-to-pearlite/bainite transformation. ### Consequences of the Right-Shift: 1. **Increased Hardenability:** Even slow cooling rates (e.g., oil quenching instead of water quenching) can produce **100% martensite** in thick sections. 2. **Shift of the Pearlite Nose to Longer Times:** Allows martensite formation at slower cooling rates. 3. **Lowering of the Bainite Region:** Some alloying elements (e.g., Cr, Mo) **lower the bainite start temperature** $B_s$ and **martensite start temperature** $M_s$. > [!NOTE] > **Quantitative Effect on $M_s$ Temperature:** > $M_s$ is reduced approximately by the following (per 1 wt% of element): > - Mn: $-33.3$ °C > - Cr: $-16.7$ °C > - Ni: $-16.7$ °C > - Mo: $-11.1$ °C > - Si: $-11.1$ °C > - C: $-423$ °C (the most powerful depressant) ## 2.4 Effect on Mechanical and Physical Properties | Property | Effect of Alloying | Best Example | |---|---|---| | **Strength (Tensile/Yield)** | Increases via solid solution + precipitation | Maraging steel (2900 MPa UTS) | | **Hardness** | Increases via carbides and martensite | D2 tool steel (62 HRC) | | **Toughness** | Ni increases, brittle carbides decrease | 18Ni300 maraging steel | | **Hardenability** | Strongly increased by Mn, Cr, Mo, B | AISI 4340 | | **Corrosion Resistance** | Cr > 10.5% gives stainless behavior | 304, 316 stainless | | **Wear Resistance** | V, W, Cr carbides dominate | D2, M2 tool steels | | **High-Temp Strength** | Mo, W, V (creep resistance) | T91, P91 boiler steel | | **Magnetic Properties** | Si, Co (electrical steels) | Silicon steel for transformer cores | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The following table summarizes the essential formulas for the **Alloy Steels and Their Applications** topic: | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Carbon Equivalent (IIW Formula)** | $CE = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Predicts weldability; $CE < 0.45$ is weldable without preheating | | 2 | **Grossmann's Hardenability** | $D_I = D_c \times f$ (where $f$ = quenching severity factor) | $D_I$ = ideal critical diameter | | 3 | **Effect on $M_s$ (Andrews' formula)** | $M_s (\degree C) = 539 - 423C - 30.4Mn - 17.7Ni - 12.1Cr - 7.5Mo$ | Predicts martensite start temperature | | 4 | **Carbide Volume Fraction (rule of thumb)** | $V_f^{carbide} \approx 15 \times (\%V) + 10 \times (\%Nb) + 6 \times (\%Cr)$ | Volume of wear-resistant carbides | | 5 | **Stainless Criterion** | $\dfrac{\%Cr}{\%C} \geq 10.5$ minimum | Bare minimum for passivity | | 6 | **Effect of $M_n$ (Mn avg. content)** | For each 1% Mn, multiplies $D_I$ by ~1.5 to 2.0 | Quench severity multiplier | | 7 | **Temper Resistance ($V_s$ for red-hardness)** | $V + W + Mo + Co$ all contribute | Hot-work tool steels | | 8 | **Pearlite Nose Shift** | $\log(t) = \dfrac{Q}{RT}$ (Arrhenius-type shift) | Quantifies the right-shift of TTT nose | > [!WARNING] > **Table Formatting Rule (Examiner Reminder):** When writing absolute values (e.g., in chemistry or modulus formulas), always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never** use the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown table cells as it breaks the table. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Aerospace:** Maraging steel 18Ni300 for rocket motor casings, landing gear; AISI 4340 for aircraft structural components. - **Automotive:** HSLA steels for car body panels (fuel efficiency); AISI 52100 for wheel bearings. - **Power Generation:** Austenitic stainless 304/316 for nuclear reactor piping; T91/P91 for superheater tubes. - **Cutting Tools:** M2 (high-speed steel) for drills, taps; D2 (cold-work die steel) for blanking dies. - **Chemical Industry:** 904L super-austenitic for sulfuric acid service; duplex 2205 for chloride environments. - **Medical:** 316L stainless for surgical implants (low carbon → no sensitization). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Carbon Equivalent (CE) for Weldability Prediction The **Carbon Equivalent (CE)** is the single most important calculation in alloy steel design for welded structures. It quantifies the cumulative effect of alloying elements on the **hardenability of the heat-affected zone (HAZ)** during welding. **Starting Principle:** Carbon is the dominant factor controlling martensite formation in the HAZ. However, **alloying elements effectively behave like "carbon equivalents"** — they also promote martensite formation. Therefore, we add their contributions with empirically determined weighting factors. **Standard IIW (International Institute of Welding) Formula:** $$ CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ where all compositions are in **wt%**. ### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Weighting Factors **Step 1 — Normalizing Each Effect to Carbon:** Each alloying element's contribution to martensite formation is measured relative to **1 wt% of carbon**. Experimental data from TTT diagram shifts, Jominy curves, and weld-CCT studies give the following ratios: - **Manganese** is approximately **6 times less effective** than carbon → weight = $1/6$ - **Chromium, Molybdenum, Vanadium** are approximately **5 times less effective** → weight = $1/5$ - **Nickel, Copper** are approximately **15 times less effective** → weight = $1/15$ **Step 2 — Summing the Weighted Contributions:** The total "equivalent carbon" is the linear sum of the weighted alloying contributions plus the actual carbon content. $$ CE = C + \sum_i w_i \cdot X_i $$ Substituting the weights: $$ CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ **Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation (KTU Examiner Pattern):** > **Decision Criteria Based on CE Value:** > - $CE \leq 0.40$ → Weldable without preheating; no risk of HAZ cracking. > - $0.40 < CE \leq 0.60$ → Preheating to $100\text{–}200\ \degree C$ required. > - $CE > 0.60$ → Higher preheat ($200\text{–}300\ \degree C$) + post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) needed. ### 3.1.2 Worked Example (KTU-Type Numerical) **Problem:** A low-alloy structural steel has the composition: **0.18% C, 1.20% Mn, 0.25% Cr, 0.10% Mo, 0.30% Ni, 0.20% Cu**. Determine the CE and recommend whether preheating is required. **Solution:** $$ CE = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ $$ CE = 0.18 + \frac{1.20}{6} + \frac{0.25 + 0.10 + 0}{5} + \frac{0.30 + 0.20}{15} $$ $$ CE = 0.18 + 0.200 + 0.070 + 0.0333 $$ $$ CE = 0.483 $$ > **Decision:** Since $0.40 < CE = 0.483 < 0.60$, **preheating to ~$120\text{–}150\ \degree C$** is recommended before welding. ## 3.2 Derivation: Andrews' Formula for Martensite Start Temperature ($M_s$) The martensite transformation starts when austenite is cooled below the $M_s$ temperature. Carbon is by far the strongest depressant, but alloying also matters. **Andrews' Empirical Equation:** $$ M_s (\degree C) = 539 - 423C - 30.4Mn - 17.7Ni - 12.1Cr - 7.5Mo $$ ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation Logic **Step 1 — Baseline:** For pure Fe–0.1%C austenite, $M_s \approx 500\ \degree C$. Andrews calibrated the **base value as 539 °C** (the extrapolated $M_s$ for zero carbon/zero alloying). **Step 2 — Linear Regression on 184 Compositions:** Andrews and co-workers (1965) statistically regressed measured $M_s$ values from **184 commercial alloy steels** against their chemical compositions. The resulting best-fit equation had the form: $$ M_s = A_0 - \sum_i k_i \cdot X_i $$ where $k_i$ are the negative slopes of the regression. **Step 3 — Fitting Coefficients:** After multi-variable regression, the fitted coefficients are: - C → $-423$ - Mn → $-30.4$ - Ni → $-17.7$ - Cr → $-12.1$ - Mo → $-7.5$ These match the formula above. ### 3.2.2 Worked Example: $M_s$ for AISI 4340 **Composition of AISI 4340:** 0.40% C, 0.80% Mn, 0.80% Cr, 0.25% Mo, 1.80% Ni. **Calculation:** $$ M_s = 539 - 423(0.40) - 30.4(0.80) - 17.7(1.80) - 12.1(0.80) - 7.5(0.25) $$ **Step 1 — Carbon contribution:** $$ 423 \times 0.40 = 169.2 $$ **Step 2 — Manganese contribution:** $$ 30.4 \times 0.80 = 24.32 $$ **Step 3 — Nickel contribution:** $$ 17.7 \times 1.80 = 31.86 $$ **Step 4 — Chromium contribution:** $$ 12.1 \times 0.80 = 9.68 $$ **Step 5 — Molybdenum contribution:** $$ 7.5 \times 0.25 = 1.875 $$ **Step 6 — Summation:** $$ M_s = 539 - 169.2 - 24.32 - 31.86 - 9.68 - 1.875 $$ $$ M_s = 539 - 236.935 $$ $$ M_s \approx 302\ \degree C $$ > **Engineering Implication:** Since $M_s \approx 302\ \degree C$ is well above room temperature, quenching in oil can fully transform 4340 to martensite without the risk of retaining large amounts of austenite. ## 3.3 Derivation: Grossmann's Critical Diameter for Hardenability **The Problem:** Plain carbon steel (e.g., AISI 1040) can be fully hardened to martensite **only in thin sections** (say < 15 mm diameter). In a 100 mm shaft, the cooling rate at the core is too slow, and we get bainite + pearlite, not martensite. **Grossmann's Solution (1942):** Define the **Ideal Critical Diameter $D_I$** as the maximum diameter of a steel bar that will form **50% martensite at the center** under an **infinite (ideal) quench** (quench severity factor $H = \infty$). $$ D_I = D_c \times f $$ where: - $D_c$ = Grossmann's critical diameter (a function of composition) - $f$ = **Quench severity factor** ($H$-value) for the medium **Step-by-Step Determination of $D_c$ from Composition:** The base $D_c$ is obtained from the **carbon content** and then **multiplied by multiplying factors** for each alloying element: $$ D_c = D_{c,\text{base}} \times f_{Mn} \times f_{Si} \times f_{Cr} \times f_{Ni} \times f_{Mo} $$ **Step-by-Step Procedure:** **Step 1:** From the **carbon content**, read $D_{c,\text{base}}$ from Grossmann's master chart. **Step 2:** For each alloying element, read its **multiplying factor** $f_X$ from Grossmann's tables (function of element % and grain size). **Step 3:** Compute the product of all $f_X$ factors. **Step 4:** Multiply to get $D_c$. **Step 5:** Apply the quench severity factor $f$ for the actual quenching medium. ### 3.3.1 Worked Example: $D_I$ for AISI 3140 **Composition:** 0.40% C, 0.80% Mn, 0.65% Cr, 1.25% Ni, 0.10% Mo. **Step 1 — Base $D_{c,\text{base}}$:** For 0.40% C and ASTM grain size 7 → $D_{c,\text{base}} \approx 0.18\ \text{inches}$ (from chart). **Step 2 — Multiplying Factors (read from Grossmann's tables):** - $f_{Mn} = 1.65$ (at 0.80% Mn) - $f_{Cr} = 1.45$ (at 0.65% Cr) - $f_{Ni} = 1.20$ (at 1.25% Ni) - $f_{Mo} = 1.20$ (at 0.10% Mo) **Step 3 — Product:** $$ D_c = 0.18 \times 1.65 \times 1.45 \times 1.20 \times 1.20 $$ $$ D_c = 0.18 \times 3.446 $$ $$ D_c \approx 0.620\ \text{inches} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Quench Severity:** If quenched in **brine** ($f = 1.5$ for $H = \infty$ approach, but for moderate agitation, $f \approx 1.0$): $$ D_I = 0.620 \times 1.0 = 0.62\ \text{inches} $$ > **Conclusion:** A 3140 steel bar up to ~16 mm diameter can form ~50% martensite at the core under a vigorous brine quench. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification of Alloy Steels The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture** of the complete classification of alloy steels as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid graph TD A[ALLOY STEELS] --> B[LOW ALLOY STEELS] A --> C[HIGH ALLOY STEELS] A --> D[HSLA STEELS] B --> B1[Ni-Cr-Mo Steels
AISI 4340 4140] B --> B2[Cr-Mo Steels
AISI 4140 52100] B --> B3[Ni Steels
AISI 2317 2515] C --> C1[STAINLESS STEELS] C --> C2[TOOL STEELS] C --> C3[MARAGING STEELS] C --> C4[HEAT RESISTING STEELS] C1 --> C1A[Ferritic
AISI 430] C1 --> C1B[Austenitic
AISI 304 316] C1 --> C1C[Martensitic
AISI 410 420] C1 --> C1D[Duplex
AISI 2205] C1 --> C1E[Precipitation Hardening
AISI 17-4 PH] C2 --> C2A[Cold Work
D2 O1 A2] C2 --> C2B[Hot Work
H11 H13] C2 --> C2C[High Speed
M2 T1] C2 --> C2D[Shock Resistant
S1 S5] C2 --> C2E[Plastic Mould
P20] C3 --> C3A[18Ni300
18Ni250] C3 --> C3B[Yield Strength
Up to 3000 MPa] C4 --> C4A[Ferritic
T91 P91] C4 --> C4B[Austenitic
304H 316H] D --> D1[Microalloyed
Nb V Ti] D --> D2[Yield Strength
350 to 550 MPa] D --> D3[Used in
Automotive Pipelines] ``` ## 4.2 Effect of Alloying Elements on Steel Microstructure & Properties ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUTS [ALLOYING ELEMENTS] A1[Chromium] A2[Nickel] A3[Molybdenum] A4[Vanadium] A5[Manganese] end subgraph MICRO [MICROSTRUCTURE EFFECTS] B1[Grain Refinement] B2[Carbide Formation] B3[Austenite Stabilization] B4[Ferrite Strengthening] end subgraph OUTPUTS [ENGINEERING PROPERTIES] C1[Corrosion Resistance] C2[Hardenability] C3[High Temp Strength] C4[Wear Resistance] C5[Toughness] end A1 --> B2 A1 --> C1 A1 --> C2 A2 --> B3 A2 --> C5 A3 --> B2 A3 --> C2 A3 --> C3 A4 --> B1 A4 --> B2 A4 --> C4 A5 --> B4 A5 --> C2 ``` ## 4.3 Stainless Steel Decision Tree — Selection Guide ```mermaid graph TD START[Need Corrosion Resistance] --> Q1{Application
temperature} Q1 -->|Below 400 C| Q2{Magnetic
required} Q1 -->|Above 400 C| Q3{High temp
strength needed} Q2 -->|Yes| FER[Ferritic 430] Q2 -->|No| Q4{Forming
required} Q4 -->|Yes| AUS[Austenitic 304 316] Q4 -->|No, needs hardness| MAR[Martensitic 410 420] Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{Cl service} Q5 -->|Yes| DUP[Duplex 2205] Q5 -->|No| HRA[Austenitic 310 321] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1.** Define alloy steel. List any four effects of alloying elements on the properties of steel. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Model Answer:** **Definition:** An alloy steel is one in which one or more alloying elements (other than carbon) are added in specified quantities (e.g., Mn > 1.65%, Si > 0.50%, Cu > 0.60%) to obtain specific mechanical, chemical, or physical properties that cannot be achieved by plain carbon steel. **Four Effects of Alloying Elements:** 1. **Increase hardenability** — Alloying elements like Mn, Cr, Mo shift the TTT/CCT curves to the right, allowing martensite formation in thicker sections. 2. **Improve corrosion resistance** — Cr ≥ 10.5% forms a passive Cr₂O₃ film (e.g., stainless steels 304, 316). 3. **Enhance high-temperature strength** — Mo, W, V impart creep resistance (e.g., T91 boiler steel, M2 high-speed tool steel). 4. **Refine grain size and form hard carbides** — V, Nb, Ti form very hard stable carbides that improve wear resistance and prevent grain growth. --- ### **Question 2.** Distinguish between austenitic and ferritic stainless steels based on composition, microstructure, magnetic behaviour, and one application each. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Property | Austenitic SS | Ferritic SS | |---|---|---| | **Composition** | 16–26% Cr, 8–22% Ni (e.g., 304) | 10.5–30% Cr, < 1% Ni (e.g., 430) | | **Microstructure** | FCC austenite (γ) stabilized by Ni | BCC ferrite (α) stabilized by Cr | | **Magnetic Behaviour** | Generally non-magnetic | Magnetic | | **Application** | Kitchenware, cryogenic vessels, chemical tanks | Car exhausts, dishwasher interiors, architectural trims | --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain with suitable examples the classification of alloy steels. How do alloying elements affect the TTT diagram? **Model Solution:** **Classification of Alloy Steels:** Alloy steels are broadly classified based on the **total alloying content** and the **primary application**. **A. By Total Alloying Content:** - **Low-alloy steels:** Total alloying content < 8% (e.g., 4340, 4140, 52100). They are mostly used in machinery, automotive, and aerospace structures. - **High-alloy steels:** Total alloying content > 8% (e.g., 304 stainless with 18% Cr + 8% Ni). **B.
