By Application / Family:** 1. **Structural / Machinery Steels** (e.g., AISI 4340, 4140) — used in gears, shafts, axles. 2. **Stainless Steels** — corrosion-resistant (austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, duplex, PH). 3. **Tool Steels** — cutting, forming dies (W, Mo, V carbide formers). 4. **Heat-Resisting Steels** — boiler tubes, turbine blades (Cr, Mo, V). 5. **Maraging Steels** — ultrahigh-strength, 18% Ni maraging (2900 MPa UTS). 6. **HSLA (High-Strength Low-Alloy) Steels** — microalloyed with Nb, V, Ti for pipelines and structural applications. **Effect of Alloying Elements on the TTT Diagram:** The TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) diagram shows the kinetics of austenite decomposition into pearlite, bainite, or martensite. Alloying elements (except Co) **shift the entire "C-curve" to the right** along the time axis. This is called the **"right-shift effect"**. **Consequences of the right-shift:** 1. **Longer incubation time** is required for austenite to transform → slower cooling rates can still produce martensite. 2. **Hardenability increases** because the critical cooling rate is lowered. For example, a 1040 plain carbon steel needs water quenching, but a 4340 alloy steel can be fully hardened by **oil quenching** even in large sections. 3. **The pearlite nose shifts to longer times**, making it easier to bypass the nose during quenching. 4. **The bainite region may shift to lower temperatures**, but the relative position of the bainite and pearlite noses can change depending on the alloy. 5. **The $M_s$ and $M_f$ temperatures are lowered** by most alloying elements (especially C, Mn, Cr, Ni). **Mechanism:** Alloying elements slow down carbon diffusion and/or change the thermodynamics of austenite decomposition. For example, Mo and Cr form stable carbides that reduce the carbon available for cementite formation in pearlite, thereby delaying the transformation. #### (b) **[7 Marks]** What are the various purposes of adding alloying elements to steel? Describe the role of Cr, Ni, Mo, and V in detail with their specific effects. **Model Solution:** **Purposes of Adding Alloying Elements:** 1. To increase hardenability. 2. To improve corrosion and oxidation resistance. 3. To enhance strength and toughness. 4. To improve high-temperature properties (creep, hot strength). 5. To confer magnetic, electrical, or thermal-expansion properties. 6. To refine grain size. 7. To improve wear and abrasion resistance. **Role of Individual Elements:** **Chromium (Cr):** - Strong **carbide former** — forms (Cr,Fe)₂₃C₆, Cr₇C₃, Cr₂₃C₆. - At **≥ 10.5%** Cr, forms a **self-healing Cr₂O₃ passive film** → corrosion resistance. - Increases **hardenability** strongly. - Improves **wear resistance** through hard chromium carbides. - Used in **stainless steels (304, 316, 410)**, tool steels (D2, H11), and ball-bearing steel (AISI 52100 with 1.5% Cr). **Nickel (Ni):** - **Austenite stabilizer** — lowers A₁, A₃ temperatures, expands γ-field. - Does not form carbides → remains in solid solution. - **Improves toughness and impact strength**, especially at low temperatures. - Promotes ductility and corrosion resistance (combined with Cr). - Used in **austenitic stainless 304 (8% Ni)**, **maraging 18Ni300**, **Invar (36% Ni)** for low-expansion applications. **Molybdenum (Mo):** - **Strong carbide former** — forms Mo₂C, Mo₆C (very stable at high temperatures). - Provides **secondary hardening** during tempering (carbide precipitation at 500–600 °C). - **Reduces temper embrittlement** in Ni–Cr steels. - **Improves high-temperature strength and creep resistance**. - Used in **H11/H13 hot-work tool steel**, **T91/P91 boiler steel**, and **AISI 4140/4340** for deep hardening. **Vanadium (V):** - **Very strong carbide former** — forms VC and V₄C₃ (the hardest and most stable carbides in steel). - **Grain refinement** during austenitization — VC particles pin grain boundaries. - Dramatic improvement in **wear and abrasion resistance**. - Provides **secondary hardening** at high tempering temperatures. - Used in **M2 high-speed steel (1.8% V)**, **D2 cold-work die steel**, and as a **microalloying element in HSLA steels (0.05–0.15% V)**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Most Common Mistake:** > Students often confuse the **"right-shift" direction** of the TTT curve. Remember: **All alloying elements EXCEPT Co shift the TTT nose to the right** (longer times). A second common error is stating that **Ni forms carbides** — **Ni is a non-carbide former** (it dissolves in ferrite and austenite). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks per question. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### (a) **[7 Marks]** What are stainless steels? Classify them with composition, microstructure, properties, and applications of each class. **Model Solution:** **Definition:** Stainless steels are iron-based alloys containing a **minimum of 10.5% chromium** (per ISO 3506) that form a passive Cr₂O₃ surface film, giving them exceptional corrosion resistance. **Classification of Stainless Steels:** **1. Ferritic Stainless Steels** - **Composition:** 10.5–30% Cr, low C, < 1% Ni (e.g., AISI 430 → 16–18% Cr). - **Microstructure:** BCC ferrite (Cr is a ferrite stabilizer). - **Properties:** Magnetic, moderate corrosion resistance, good formability, low cost, **not hardenable by heat treatment** (can be hardened only by cold working). - **Applications:** Automotive exhausts, kitchenware, architectural panels, washing machine drums. **2. Austenitic Stainless Steels** - **Composition:** 16–26% Cr, 8–22% Ni (e.g., AISI 304 → 18% Cr, 8% Ni; AISI 316 → 16% Cr, 10% Ni, 2% Mo). - **Microstructure:** FCC austenite (Ni is an austenite stabilizer). - **Properties:** **Non-magnetic**, excellent corrosion resistance, excellent ductility and formability, **highest toughness at cryogenic temperatures**, hardenable only by cold working. - **Applications:** Chemical tanks, cryogenic vessels, kitchen sinks, medical implants (316L), food processing equipment, nuclear reactor piping. **3. Martensitic Stainless Steels** - **Composition:** 11.5–18% Cr, 0.1–1.2% C, low Ni (e.g., AISI 410 → 12% Cr; AISI 420 → 13% Cr). - **Microstructure:** Body-centered tetragonal (BCT) martensite on quenching. - **Properties:** **Magnetic**, high strength and hardness after heat treatment, **moderate corrosion resistance** (lower than austenitic), good wear resistance. - **Applications:** Cutlery, surgical instruments, turbine blades, shafts, valve parts, ball bearings (AISI 440C). **4. Duplex Stainless Steels** - **Composition:** 18–28% Cr, 4.5–8% Ni, 2.5–4% Mo (e.g., AISI 2205 → 22% Cr, 5% Ni, 3% Mo). - **Microstructure:** **Mixed austenite + ferrite** (~50:50) — hence the name "duplex." - **Properties:** Magnetic (due to ferrite), higher strength than austenitic (~2× yield), excellent resistance to **chloride stress corrosion cracking**, good weldability. - **Applications:** Offshore oil platforms, chemical tankers, heat exchangers, desalination plants. **5. Precipitation-Hardening (PH) Stainless Steels** - **Composition:** 15–17% Cr, 3–5% Ni, with Cu, Nb, Ti, Al additions (e.g., 17-4 PH → 17% Cr, 4% Ni, 4% Cu). - **Microstructure:** Low-carbon martensite or austenite matrix, with **fine intermetallic precipitates** (e.g., Cu-rich, Ni₃Ti) formed during aging. - **Properties:** Excellent combination of **high strength + corrosion resistance**, magnetic, can be heat treated to UTS up to 1310 MPa. - **Applications:** Aerospace structural parts, missile casings, high-performance shafts, valve stems. #### (b) **[7 Marks]** Discuss the composition, properties, and applications of tool steels. What is "red hardness" and which alloying elements impart it? **Model Solution:** **Tool Steels:** A specialized class of alloy steels designed for use as **cutting tools, dies, molds, and forming instruments**. They must possess high hardness (≥ 60 HRC), wear resistance, toughness, and dimensional stability. **AISI Classification of Tool Steels (T-Type Designations):** | Class | Symbol | Composition Highlights | Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **Cold-Work Die Steel** | D | 1.5–2.3% Cr, 1% Mo, 4% V (e.g., D2) | Blank dies, broaches, thread rollers | | | O | 0.9% C, 1% Mn, 0.5% Cr (e.g., O1) | Short-run dies, gauges | | | A | Air-hardening, 1% C, 5% Cr, 1% Mo (e.g., A2) | Medium-run blanking dies | | **Hot-Work Tool Steel** | H | 5% Cr, 1.5% Mo, 1% V (e.g., H11, H13) | Die casting dies, forging dies | | **High-Speed Steel** | M | Mo-based, e.g., M2 (6% W, 5% Mo, 2% V) | Drills, taps, milling cutters | | | T | W-based, e.g., T1 (18% W, 4% Cr, 1% V) | Heavy-duty cutting tools | | **Shock-Resistant** | S | 0.5% C, 1.5–2.5% Si (e.g., S1) | Chisels, punches, rivet sets | | **Plastic Mould** | P | 0.2% C, 2% Cr, 0.5% Mo (e.g., P20) | Plastic injection moulds | | **Water-Hardening** | W | 1% C, plain low-alloy (e.g., W1) | Woodworking tools, low-cost tools | **Properties of Tool Steels:** 1. **High hardness (60–65 HRC)** after heat treatment. 2. **Wear resistance** from hard carbide particles (V, Cr, W carbides). 3. **Toughness** to absorb impact loading (especially in S-type). 4. **Dimensional stability** during heat treatment. 5. **Hot hardness (red hardness)** for high-speed cutting. **Red Hardness (Hot Hardness):** **Definition:** The ability of a tool steel to **retain its hardness and cutting edge at elevated temperatures** (typically $500\text{–}600\ \degree C$) generated by friction during high-speed machining. **Elements That Impart Red Hardness:** - **Tungsten (W)** — forms stable W₂C and Fe₄W₂C carbides. - **Molybdenum (Mo)** — forms Mo₂C, Mo₆C. - **Vanadium (V)** — forms the extremely stable VC, V₄C₃. - **Cobalt (Co)** — does not form carbides, but raises the recrystallization temperature of the matrix, retaining strength at high temperatures. **Why it matters:** When a drill bit cuts steel at high speed, the cutting edge can heat up to **600 °C** or more due to friction. If the tool loses hardness at this temperature (which ordinary steel does), the cutting edge blunts rapidly. Tool steels with red hardness maintain their hardness, allowing them to continue cutting — hence the name **"high-speed steels"** (M2, T1, M35, M42). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B Specifics:** > - Students often forget that **"D", "O", "A" cold-work tool steels differ mainly in the quenching medium** (D = air, O = oil, A = air, W = water). Remember this triad. > - A common error is calling **maraging steels** as "tool steels" — they are **structural** ultrahigh-strength steels, not tool steels. > - The exact **% composition of carbides in D2 (~15% by volume)** is a frequent sub-question. D2 has about **12–15% Cr carbides and 4–5% V carbides** by volume. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:** - **Definition of alloy steel** → total alloying > specified limit OR specific alloying range mandated. - **AISI 4-digit rule** → first 2 digits = alloy type; last 2 digits = carbon in 0.01%. - **Austenite stabilizers (γ):** Ni, Mn, Co, Cu. **Ferrite stabilizers (α):** Cr, Si, Mo, V, W, Ti, Al. - **All elements except Co shift TTT nose to the RIGHT → ↑ hardenability.** - **Carbon Equivalent (CE) formula** — IIW equation. $CE > 0.45$ → preheat required. - **Andrews' $M_s$ equation** — 539 minus weighted sum of compositions. - **Martensitic SS** → magnetic, hardenable, used for cutlery/surgical tools. - **Austenitic SS** → non-magnetic, best corrosion resistance, cryogenic uses. - **Ferritic SS** → magnetic, low cost, automotive exhausts. - **Duplex SS** → 50% γ + 50% α, best for chloride + offshore applications. - **17-4 PH SS** → precipitation hardening, combines high strength + corrosion resistance. - **Tool steel families:** W (water), O (oil), A (air), D (high-Cr cold), H (hot work), M (Mo-HSS), T (W-HSS), S (shock), P (plastic mould). - **Red hardness** is given by **W, Mo, V, Co** in M2, T1, M42 grades. - **Maraging steel 18Ni300** → 18% Ni, UTS ≈ 2900 MPa, used in rocket motor casings. - **HSLA** → microalloyed with Nb, V, Ti; yield strength 350–550 MPa; used in pipelines and automobiles. - **AISI 52100** → 1% C, 1.5% Cr → the standard bearing steel. - **AISI 4340** → 0.4% C, Ni-Cr-Mo → the standard aerospace steel. - **Stainless criterion:** $\dfrac{\%Cr}{\%C} \geq 10.5$ (minimum for passivity). > [!NOTE] > **Quick Mnemonic for Effects of Alloying Elements — "SMART CHIPS":** > - **S**trength ↑ (solid solution) > - **M**echanical hardness ↑ (carbides) > - **A**ustenite stability (Ni, Mn) > - **R**ed hardness (W, Mo, V, Co) > - **T**oughness (Ni at cryogenic temps) > - **C**orrosion resistance (Cr ≥ 10.5%) > - **H**ardenability ↑ (all elements shift TTT right) > - **I**mpact resistance ↑ (Ni, Mn) > - **P**recipitation hardening (Cu, Al, Ti, Nb) > - **S**trength at high temp (Mo, V for creep)

topics.title: Properties and Applications of Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Properties and Applications of Composites # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Composites A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, engineered material composed of **two or more distinct phases** — a **matrix phase** (continuous binder) and a **reinforcement phase** (dispersed load-bearing constituent) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material with properties superior to those of the individual components acting alone. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a composite is defined as: > A multi-phase system where the **constituents retain their individual identities and boundaries** but act in concert to deliver synergistic mechanical, thermal, or electrical performance that no single constituent can achieve on its own. The three essential requirements for any composite system are: 1. **Matrix (Binder):** The continuous phase that transfers load to the reinforcement, protects it from environmental degradation, and maintains geometric stability. 2. **Reinforcement (Filler):** The discontinuous phase (fibers, particles, whiskers) that carries the principal load and imparts strength/stiffness. 3. **Interface (Interphase):** The critical boundary region where load transfer between matrix and reinforcement occurs via shear mechanisms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize this):** A composite is a *material system* — not a *compound* — meaning the constituents remain chemically distinct and mechanically separable, unlike in alloys or chemical compounds. ## 1.2 Classification of Composites The KTU syllabus requires familiarity with the following primary classification: | Class | Reinforcement Geometry | Typical Examples | |---|---|---| | **Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRC)** | Continuous or discontinuous fibers | Fiberglass, CFRP, Aramid-epoxy | | **Particle-Reinforced Composites** | Equiaxed particles | SiC/Al metal matrix, WC/Co cermets | | **Structural Composites** | Laminated or sandwich macrostructures | Plywood, CFRP laminates, honeycomb panels | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of **reinforced concrete**: concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension (it cracks easily if you try to bend it). To compensate, steel rebar (reinforcement) is embedded inside. The concrete (matrix) holds the rebar in place and protects it from rust, while the steel (reinforcement) carries the tensile loads. Together, they form a composite that is strong in **both** compression and tension — a property neither material possesses alone. **Another analogy:** A fiberglass boat hull is like a chocolate-coated wafer. The chocolate (polymer resin) is soft and weak; the crispy wafer (glass fibers) is brittle. But combine them, and you get a structure that is tough, lightweight, and durable. > [!NOTE] > **Why composites matter in engineering:** They allow engineers to **tailor properties** (anisotropy, stiffness, strength, weight) by adjusting the type, orientation, and volume fraction of the reinforcement — something impossible with monolithic metals or polymers. ## 1.4 The Three-Phase Concept ``` COMPOSITE = MATRIX (Continuous) + REINFORCEMENT (Dispersed) + INTERFACE ``` - The **interface** (often called the *interphase*) is where chemistry, mechanics, and physics meet. A weak interface = poor load transfer = premature failure. A strong interface = efficient load transfer = high strength. --- ## 1.5 Common Matrix and Reinforcement Materials **Matrix Materials:** - **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC):** Epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, PEEK (most common in KTU weightage). - **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC):** Aluminum, titanium, magnesium matrices reinforced with SiC or Al₂O₃. - **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC):** SiC, Al₂O₃ matrices with ceramic fibers — used in high-temperature applications. **Reinforcement Materials:** - **Glass fibers (E-glass, S-glass):** Cheap, good tensile strength, used in marine and automotive. - **Carbon fibers (PAN-based, pitch-based):** Very high stiffness, low density, aerospace-grade. - **Aramid fibers (Kevlar, Nomex):** Excellent impact resistance, used in body armor. - **Boron fibers:** High stiffness, used in military aircraft and sporting goods. - **Natural fibers:** Jute, flax, sisal — eco-friendly, growing in automotive interior applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Carbon fiber vs. Glass fiber — KTU Quick Fact:** Carbon fiber has higher specific stiffness and strength but is far more expensive. Glass fiber dominates in cost-sensitive applications like boat hulls, storage tanks, and car body panels. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constituents and Their Roles — Structured Logic Let us break down the *why* and *how* of each constituent: ### A. The Matrix Phase - **Why is it needed?** - Holds the reinforcement in proper geometric arrangement (alignment, spacing, orientation). - Transfers applied load to the reinforcement via **shear stress at the interface**. - Protects the reinforcement from surface damage, moisture, oxidation, and chemical attack. - Provides shape, surface finish, and dimensional stability to the composite. - **How does it work?** - When external load is applied, the matrix deforms and generates shear stresses at the matrix-fiber interface. - These shear stresses pull the fibers, which then carry the bulk of the tensile load. ### B. The Reinforcement Phase - **Why is it needed?** - Reinforcement is typically much **stronger and stiffer** than the matrix (e.g., glass fiber tensile strength ≈ 3.5 GPa vs. epoxy ≈ 0.07 GPa). - Fibers carry the principal load in the direction of fiber alignment. - **How does it work?** - In a unidirectional composite, the rule-of-mixtures model assumes that both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain condition). ### C. The Interface / Interphase - **Why is it needed?** - The interface governs the **efficiency of load transfer** between matrix and reinforcement. - A strong chemical/mechanical bond prevents fiber pull-out and debonding — the two most common failure modes. - **How does it work?** - Surface treatments (e.g., **silanization** of glass fibers) promote chemical bonding between organic matrix and inorganic reinforcement. ## 2.2 The Rule of Mixtures — Theoretical Foundation The **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** is the cornerstone KTU formula for predicting composite properties from constituent properties and volume fractions. ### A. Density of Composite ($\rho_c$) The mass of the composite equals the sum of the masses of the constituents: $$m_c = m_m + m_f$$ Since $m = \rho V$ and total volume $V_c = V_m + V_f$: $$\rho_c V_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$$ Dividing by $V_c$: $$\rho_c = \rho_m \left(\frac{V_m}{V_c}\right) + \rho_f \left(\frac{V_f}{V_c}\right)$$ Defining **volume fraction** $V_f = V_f / V_c$ and $V_m = 1 - V_f$: $$\boxed{\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f}$$ ### B. Young's Modulus — Iso-Strain (Voigt Model, Parallel Loading) When the load is applied **parallel to the fibers** (longitudinal direction), both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f$. The total load is shared: $$F_c = F_m + F_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f$$ With $A_i \propto V_i$ and $\sigma_i = E_i \varepsilon$: $$\boxed{E_{c,L} = V_m E_m + V_f E_f}$$ This is the **upper bound** (Voigt bound) and is applicable to **unidirectional continuous fiber composites loaded along the fiber direction**. ### C. Young's Modulus — Iso-Stress (Reuss Model, Transverse Loading) When the load is applied **perpendicular to the fibers** (transverse direction), both phases experience the **same stress** $\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f$. The total strain is the sum of individual strains: $$\varepsilon_c V_c = \varepsilon_m V_m + \varepsilon_f V_f$$ $$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}}$$ This is the **lower bound** (Reuss bound). ### D. Strength of Composite (Longitudinal Tensile) For brittle fiber + brittle matrix systems, the tensile strength under iso-strain is: $$\boxed{\sigma_{c,L} = V_m \sigma_m + V_f \sigma_f}$$ For ductile matrix systems, a **modified Kelly-Tyson model** applies with a critical fiber length factor, but the simple ROM form is what KTU expects in the 14-mark problems. ### E. Volume Fraction vs. Weight Fraction $$W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c} \quad ; \quad W_m = \frac{\rho_m V_m}{\rho_c}$$ And the conversion between them: $$V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Property** | **Formula (ROM)** | **Applicable Condition** | |---|---|---| | Density | $\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f$ | All composites | | Modulus (Longitudinal) | $E_{c,L} = V_m E_m + V_f E_f$ | Iso-strain, parallel loading | | Modulus (Transverse) | $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} + \dfrac{V_f}{E_f}$ | Iso-stress, perpendicular loading | | Strength (Longitudinal) | $\sigma_{c,L} = V_m \sigma_m + V_f \sigma_f$ | Brittle-brittle system | | Specific Strength | $\sigma_{c,L} / \rho_c$ | Weight-critical design | | Specific Modulus | $E_{c,L} / \rho_c$ | Stiffness-critical design | | Volume Fraction from Weights | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | Given weight data | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always explicitly state the **units** (e.g., GPa for modulus, kg/m³ for density) and the **assumptions** (iso-strain or iso-stress) before applying any ROM formula. Marks are awarded for assumptions, not just final numbers. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Domain** | **Composite Used** | **Why?** | |---|---|---| | Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) | CFRP laminates (≈50% by weight) | High specific strength, fatigue resistance, fuel savings | | Automotive (BMW i3, Tesla) | CFRP body panels | Crash energy absorption, weight reduction | | Marine (boat hulls, decks) | Glass fiber + polyester | Corrosion resistance, low cost, ease of fabrication | | Sports (tennis rackets, bicycles) | Carbon fiber reinforced polymer | Tunable stiffness, vibration damping | | Wind turbine blades | Glass + carbon hybrid | Fatigue resistance, large-span stiffness | | Spacecraft heat shields | Ceramic matrix composites | High-temperature stability above 1000°C | --- ## 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages of Composites (High-Yield KTU Content) **Advantages:** - **High strength-to-weight ratio** (specific strength) - **High stiffness-to-weight ratio** (specific modulus) - **Tailorable anisotropic properties** — properties can be designed by fiber orientation - **Corrosion resistance** (especially polymer matrix composites) - **Fatigue resistance** superior to most metals - **Thermal and electrical insulation** can be tuned - **Damping characteristics** — excellent vibration absorption **Disadvantages:** - **High cost** of raw materials (especially carbon and aramid fibers) - **Anisotropy** complicates analysis and design - **Difficult to recycle** — environmental concern - **Moisture absorption** in polymer matrices - **Complex manufacturing** (autoclave, filament winding, pultrusion) - **Difficult to inspect for internal defects** (delamination, voids) - **Brittle failure** without warning in some systems # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Density of a Composite We start from the **principle of conservation of mass** for the composite. **Step 1:** State the assumption. The composite contains no voids, so the volumes are additive: $$V_c = V_m + V_f$$ **Step 2:** State that mass is additive (no chemical reaction or mass loss at the interface): $$m_c = m_m + m_f$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $m = \rho V$: $$\rho_c V_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$$ **Step 4:** Divide both sides by $V_c$: $$\rho_c = \rho_m \left(\frac{V_m}{V_c}\right) + \rho_f \left(\frac{V_f}{V_c}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Define the volume fractions $V_m = V_m / V_c$ and $V_f = V_f / V_c$ with $V_m + V_f = 1$: $$\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt / Iso-Strain) **Step 1:** Apply load $F$ along the fiber direction. Because both phases deform together (perfect bonding), they share the same strain: $$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon$$ **Step 2:** The applied load is supported by the matrix and the fiber in parallel: $$F_c = F_m + F_f$$ **Step 3:** Using $F = \sigma A = E \varepsilon A$: $$\sigma_c A_c = E_m \varepsilon A_m + E_f \varepsilon A_f$$ **Step 4:** Factor out $\varepsilon$ and divide both sides by $A_c$: $$\sigma_c = \varepsilon \left(E_m \frac{A_m}{A_c} + E_f \frac{A_f}{A_c}\right)$$ **Step 5:** For uniform cross-section, $A_i / A_c = V_i / V_c = V_i$ (volume fraction, since lengths are equal in iso-strain): $$\sigma_c = \varepsilon (E_m V_m + E_f V_f)$$ **Step 6:** Since $E_c = \sigma_c / \varepsilon$: $$\boxed{E_{c,L} = E_m V_m + E_f V_f} \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Transverse Modulus (Reuss / Iso-Stress) **Step 1:** Apply load perpendicular to the fibers. The matrix and fiber experience the **same stress**: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f = \sigma$$ **Step 2:** The total elongation of the composite equals the sum of elongations of each phase: $$\delta_c = \delta_m + \delta_f$$ **Step 3:** With $\delta = \varepsilon L$ and equal length $L$: $$\varepsilon_c L = \varepsilon_m L + \varepsilon_f L \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m V_m + \varepsilon_f V_f$$ **Step 4:** Using $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$: $$\frac{\sigma}{E_{c,T}} = \sigma \left(\frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Cancel $\sigma$: $$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}} \quad \blacksquare$$ --- ## 3.4 Fully Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU Style, 7 marks):** A unidirectional glass fiber reinforced epoxy composite contains **60% by volume of glass fibers** and **40% by volume of epoxy resin**. Given the following data: - Glass fiber: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2580$ kg/m³, $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa - Epoxy resin: $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³, $\sigma_m = 70$ MPa **Calculate:** (i) Density of the composite, (ii) Longitudinal Young's modulus, (iii) Transverse Young's modulus, (iv) Specific strength in the longitudinal direction. --- ### Solution **Given:** $V_f = 0.60$, $V_m = 0.40$. #### (i) Density of the Composite Apply the density rule of mixtures: $$\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f$$ Substitute the values: $$\rho_c = (0.40)(1200) + (0.60)(2580)$$ Compute the matrix contribution: $$0.40 \times 1200 = 480 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ Compute the fiber contribution: $$0.60 \times 2580 = 1548 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ Add them: $$\rho_c = 480 + 1548 = 2028 \text{ kg/m}^3 \approx 2030 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ --- #### (ii) Longitudinal Young's Modulus $E_{c,L}$ Apply the Voigt (iso-strain) rule of mixtures: $$E_{c,L} = V_m E_m + V_f E_f$$ Substitute: $$E_{c,L} = (0.40)(3.5) + (0.60)(72)$$ Compute: $$E_{c,L} = 1.4 + 43.2 = 44.6 \text{ GPa}$$ --- #### (iii) Transverse Young's Modulus $E_{c,T}$ Apply the Reuss (iso-stress) formula: $$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}$$ Substitute: $$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{0.40}{3.5} + \frac{0.60}{72}$$ Compute each term: $$\frac{0.40}{3.5} = 0.114286 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{0.60}{72} = 0.008333 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$ Add: $$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = 0.122619 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$ Take the reciprocal: $$E_{c,T} = \frac{1}{0.122619} = 8.156 \text{ GPa} \approx 8.16 \text{ GPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical observation:** $E_{c,T}$ (8.16 GPa) is **5.5 times lower** than $E_{c,L}$ (44.6 GPa) — this dramatically demonstrates the **anisotropy** of unidirectional fiber composites. The examiner will reward you for noting this. --- #### (iv) Specific Strength in the Longitudinal Direction First, compute the longitudinal tensile strength using ROM: $$\sigma_{c,L} = V_m \sigma_m + V_f \sigma_f$$ Substitute (note unit conversion: $70$ MPa and $2400$ MPa are both in MPa, so the result is in MPa): $$\sigma_{c,L} = (0.40)(70) + (0.60)(2400)$$ Compute: $$\sigma_{c,L} = 28 + 1440 = 1468 \text{ MPa}$$ Now compute the specific strength: $$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{c,L}}{\rho_c} = \frac{1468 \text{ MPa}}{2028 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$ $$\text{Specific Strength} = 0.7239 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{m}^3/\text{kg} \approx 0.724 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ For comparison, structural steel (ASTM A36) has a specific strength of roughly $250/7850 \approx 0.032$ MPa·m³/kg — the composite is over **20× better**, explaining its use in aerospace. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result Interpretation (Marks-worthy):** This composite has a density comparable to aluminum (≈2700 kg/m³) but is **stronger and stiffer in the fiber direction**, making it ideal for structural panels, pressure vessels, and aircraft skin panels. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Composites ```mermaid flowchart TD A[COMPOSITE MATERIALS]:::root --> B[PARTICLE REINFORCED] A --> C[FIBER REINFORCED] A --> D[STRUCTURAL COMPOSITE] B --> B1[Large Particle
Concrete WC Co] B --> B2[Dispersion Strengthened
SiC in Al matrix] B --> B3[Orowan Mechanism
Nanoscale precipitates] C --> C1[Continuous Fiber
Unidirectional layup] C --> C2[Discontinuous Fiber
Chopped strand mat] C --> C3[Whisker Reinforced
SiC whiskers in Al] C1 --> C1a[Glass Fiber E Glass S Glass] C1 --> C1b[Carbon Fiber PAN Pitch] C1 --> C1c[Aramid Fiber Kevlar] C1 --> C1d[Boron Fiber] D --> D1[ Laminates
Plywood CFRP layers] D --> D2[ Sandwich Panels
Honeycomb core] D --> D3[ Filament Wound
Pressure vessels] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px; classDef leaf fill:#e8f1ff,color:#1f3a5f,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:1px; ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Composite ```mermaid flowchart LR A[EXTERNAL LOAD APPLIED]:::input --> B[MATRIX PHASE
Continuous Binder]:::matrix B --> C[INTERFACE
Interphase Region]:::inter C --> D[REINFORCEMENT PHASE
Fibers or Particles]:::fiber D --> E[LOAD CARRIED
Synergistic Response]:::out B --> E C --> E F[MOISTURE]:::env --> C G[TEMPERATURE]:::env --> C H[CHEMICAL ATTACK]:::env --> C classDef input fill:#fff4cc,color:#5a4500,stroke:#5a4500,stroke-width:2px; classDef matrix fill:#cce5ff,color:#003366,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px; classDef inter fill:#ffd6cc,color:#660000,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:2px; classDef fiber fill:#d4edda,color:#155724,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px; classDef out fill:#e2d4f0,color:#3d1a5e,stroke:#3d1a5e,stroke-width:2px; classDef env fill:#f8d7da,color:#721c24,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:1px; ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Representation of Unidirectional Fiber Composite (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Block1[UNIDIRECTIONAL FIBER COMPOSITE LAYUP] direction LR M1[EPOXY MATRIX]:::mat --> F1[GLASS FIBER]:::fib F1 --> M2[EPOXY MATRIX]:::mat M2 --> F2[GLASS FIBER]:::fib F2 --> M3[EPOXY MATRIX]:::mat M3 --> F3[GLASS FIBER]:::fib end LD[LOAD DIRECTION]:::load --> Block1 Block1 --> RESP[STIFF STRONG ALONG FIBER AXIS
WEAK IN TRANSVERSE DIRECTION]:::resp classDef mat fill:#cce5ff,color:#003366,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px; classDef fib fill:#d4edda,color:#155724,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:1px; classDef load fill:#fff4cc,color:#5a4500,stroke:#5a4500,stroke-width:2px; classDef resp fill:#f8d7da,color:#721c24,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px; ``` ## 4.4 Failure Modes in Composites (Engineering Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD LOAD[APPLIED LOAD INCREASES]:::in --> DM1{MATRIX CRACKING} LOAD --> DM2[INTERFACE DEBONDING]:::in LOAD --> DM3[FIBER FRACTURE]:::in LOAD --> DM4[DELAMINATION]:::in LOAD --> DM5[FIBER PULL OUT]:::in DM1 --> FINAL[COMPOSITE FAILURE]:::fail DM2 --> FINAL DM3 --> FINAL DM4 --> FINAL DM5 --> FINAL classDef in fill:#fff4cc,color:#5a4500,stroke:#5a4500,stroke-width:2px; classDef fail fill:#f8d7da,color:#721c24,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px; ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Diagram Tip:** In board answers, a simple hand-drawn block diagram showing **matrix + aligned fibers + load arrow** is sufficient to earn full diagram marks (≈2 marks) for 14-mark questions on composites. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a composite material. With a neat sketch, explain the role of the matrix and reinforcement phases.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A composite material is a heterogeneous, multi-phase material system composed of two or more distinct constituents — a **matrix phase** and a **reinforcement phase** — combined at the macroscopic level to achieve properties that are superior to those of the individual components. - **Matrix (continuous phase):** Holds the reinforcement in place, transfers applied load to the reinforcement through interfacial shear, protects the reinforcement from environmental damage, and provides the final shape and surface finish. - **Reinforcement (dispersed phase):** Carries the principal mechanical load. Typically much stronger and stiffer than the matrix (e.g., glass or carbon fibers in a polymer matrix). A simple sketch should show fibers aligned within a matrix with an external load arrow. *(Self-Assessment Rubric: Definition — 1 mark, Matrix role — 1 mark, Reinforcement role — 1 mark.)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between fiber-reinforced and particle-reinforced composites with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Aspect** | **Fiber-Reinforced** | **Particle-Reinforced** | |---|---|---| | Reinforcement shape | High aspect-ratio fibers (l/d > 10) | Equiaxed or near-spherical particles | | Load transfer | Along fiber axis (anisotropic) | Isotropic (more uniform) | | Strength mechanism | Load borne directly by fibers | Matrix strengthening by restricting dislocation motion | | Examples | Glass-fiber/epoxy, CFRP | SiC-particle/aluminum (MMC), WC/Co cermet | *(Self-Assessment Rubric: Two valid distinctions — 2 marks, Example for each — 1 mark.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` #### (a) State and derive the rule of mixtures for the density and longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The composite is free of voids. 2. Fibers are continuous, uniformly distributed, and perfectly aligned parallel to the loading direction. 3. Perfect bonding exists at the matrix-fiber interface (no slip or debonding). 4. Both matrix and fiber experience the **same strain** in the longitudinal direction (iso-strain / Voigt condition). **Derivation — Density (3 marks):** Step 1 — Mass conservation: $m_c = m_m + m_f$ Step 2 — Substitute $m = \rho V$: $\rho_c V_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$ Step 3 — Divide by $V_c$ and define volume fractions: $$\boxed{\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f}$$ **Derivation — Longitudinal Modulus (4 marks):** Step 1 — Iso-strain: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon$ Step 2 — Load equilibrium: $F_c = F_m + F_f$, hence $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f$ Step 3 — Substitute $\sigma = E\varepsilon$: $E_c \varepsilon A_c = E_m \varepsilon A_m + E_f \varepsilon A_f$ Step 4 — Divide by $A_c$ and use $A_i/A_c = V_i$: $$\boxed{E_{c,L} = V_m E_m + V_f E_f}$$ --- #### (b) A unidirectional carbon-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has a fiber volume fraction of 0.55. Calculate the longitudinal modulus, transverse modulus, and specific strength of the composite. Given: $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 1800$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1250$ kg/m³, $\sigma_f = 3000$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 60$ MPa. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** Given: $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$. **Step 1 — Density of composite:** $$\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f = (0.45)(1250) + (0.55)(1800)$$ $$\rho_c = 562.5 + 990 = 1552.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Longitudinal modulus (Voigt / iso-strain):** $$E_{c,L} = V_m E_m + V_f E_f = (0.45)(3.5) + (0.55)(230)$$ $$E_{c,L} = 1.575 + 126.5 = 128.075 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 3 — Transverse modulus (Reuss / iso-stress):** $$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f} = \frac{0.45}{3.5} + \frac{0.55}{230}$$ $$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = 0.128571 + 0.002391 = 0.130962 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$ $$E_{c,T} = 7.636 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4 — Longitudinal strength (ROM for brittle-brittle):** $$\sigma_{c,L} = V_m \sigma_m + V_f \sigma_f = (0.45)(60) + (0.55)(3000)$$ $$\sigma_{c,L} = 27 + 1650 = 1677 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5 — Specific strength:** $$\text{Specific Strength} = \frac{\sigma_{c,L}}{\rho_c} = \frac{1677}{1552.5} = 1.080 \text{ MPa·m}^3/\text{kg}$$ **Final Answer:** - $E_{c,L} = 128.08$ GPa - $E_{c,T} = 7.64$ GPa - Specific strength = $1.08$ MPa·m³/kg (≈34× that of structural steel) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse longitudinal and transverse formulae. If the problem says "load applied along the fiber direction" → use **Voigt** (sum form). If the load is **perpendicular** to the fibers → use **Reuss** (reciprocal sum form). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks instantly. > > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 2:** Many students forget to convert volume fractions correctly (must be in decimal form, not percentage). $V_f = 55\% = 0.55$, not $55$. Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` #### (a) Explain in detail the different types of fibers used in fiber-reinforced composites. Compare carbon, glass, and aramid fibers on the basis of density, modulus, strength, and cost. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The major classes of fibers used in fiber-reinforced polymer composites are: **1. Glass Fibers (E-glass, S-glass):** - Most widely used reinforcement (~90% of FRC market). - $E$-glass: density 2580 kg/m³, tensile strength 2400 MPa, modulus 72 GPa, low cost. - $S$-glass: higher strength and temperature resistance, used in aerospace. **2. Carbon Fibers (PAN-based, Pitch-based):** - Highest specific stiffness among common fibers. - PAN-based: tensile strength 3000–7000 MPa, modulus 230–600 GPa, density 1800 kg/m³. - Used in aerospace, racing, sporting goods. **3. Aramid Fibers (Kevlar 49, Nomex):** - Aromatic polyamide fibers with very high impact resistance. - Tensile strength ≈ 2750 MPa, modulus ≈ 130 GPa, density ≈ 1440 kg/m³. - Used in body armor, ropes, ballistic protection. **4. Boron Fibers:** - High stiffness (E ≈ 400 GPa), but very expensive. - Used in military aircraft and helicopter blades. **5. Natural Fibers (Jute, Flax, Sisal, Hemp):** - Eco-friendly, low cost, low density (~1.3–1.5 g/cm³). - Limited mechanical performance, used in automotive interiors and packaging. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Property | E-Glass | Carbon (PAN) | Aramid (Kevlar 49) | |---|---|---|---| | Density (kg/m³) | 2580 | 1800 | 1440 | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 2400 | 3500 | 2750 | | Young's Modulus (GPa) | 72 | 230 | 130 | | Specific Modulus (GPa·m³/kg) | 27.9 | 127.8 | 90.3 | | Cost (relative) | Low | High | Medium-High | | Major Application | Marine, automotive | Aerospace, sports | Ballistic, ropes | --- #### (b) Discuss the major applications of composite materials in aerospace, automotive, and sports industries. Justify why composites are preferred over conventional metals in these sectors. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Aerospace Industry:** - The Boeing 787 Dreamliner uses ~50% composite materials by weight (mostly CFRP). - The Airbus A350 XWB uses ~52% composites. - Components: fuselage skin, wing spars, tail sections, control surfaces, interior panels. - **Why composites?** Highest specific strength and stiffness → fuel efficiency, longer range, lower operating cost. Corrosion resistance → reduced maintenance. Fatigue resistance → longer service life. **Automotive Industry:** - BMW i3, i8 use CFRP passenger cells ("LifeDrive" architecture). - Tesla Model S uses aluminum + CFRP hybrid panels. - Formula 1 cars are ~85% composite (carbon-fiber monocoque). - **Why composites?** Crash energy absorption, weight reduction (→ better mileage and lower emissions), design flexibility (complex shapes). **Sports Industry:** - Tennis rackets, golf clubs, hockey sticks, racing bicycles, surfboards, ski poles, helmets. - **Why composites?** Tunable vibration damping, low weight, high impact resistance, ability to tailor stiffness along specific directions. **Justification Summary (Synthesis — for full 7 marks):** | Property | Composite (CFRP) | Aluminum 2024-T3 | Steel A36 | |---|---|---|---| | Density (kg/m³) | 1550 | 2780 | 7850 | | Specific Modulus (GPa·m³/kg) | 82.5 | 26.6 | 27.1 | | Specific Strength (MPa·m³/kg) | 1.08 | 0.17 | 0.032 | | Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Moderate | Poor | The composite is **3× stiffer per unit weight** and **20–30× stronger per unit weight** than the best aluminum alloy, justifying its premium cost in performance-critical applications. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall 3:** In application questions, students often give *vague* answers like "composites are used in aircraft." The KTU 2024 scheme expects **specific component names** (e.g., *Boeing 787 fuselage, F1 monocoque chassis, Airbus A350 wing spar*) and **a clear property-based justification** (specific strength, specific modulus, corrosion resistance, fatigue). Generic answers lose 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **Definition:** Composite = matrix (continuous) + reinforcement (dispersed) + interface. The constituents retain their identities — it is a *material system*, not a compound. - **Three main classes:** Fiber-reinforced, Particle-reinforced, Structural (laminates/sandwich). - **Rule of Mixtures (Density):** $\rho_c = V_m \rho_m + V_f \rho_f$ — always applies. - **Rule of Mixtures (Modulus, Longitudinal):** $E_{c,L} = V_m E_m + V_f E_f$ — Voigt / iso-strain. - **Rule of Mixtures (Modulus, Transverse):** $1/E_{c,T} = V_m/E_m + V_f/E_f$ — Reuss / iso-stress. - **Strength (Longitudinal, brittle-brittle):** $\sigma_{c,L} = V_m \sigma_m + V_f \sigma_f$. - **Voigt = upper bound; Reuss = lower bound.** - **Always state assumptions** before applying ROM: void-free, perfect bonding, uniform distribution, iso-strain or iso-stress. - **Volume fraction must be in decimal form** in all calculations. - **Specific strength = strength/density; Specific modulus = modulus/density** — these are the metrics that drive composite use in aerospace. - **Common fibers (rank by specific modulus):** Carbon > Aramid > Glass > Boron (high cost) > Natural fibers. - **Common failure modes:** Matrix cracking, fiber-matrix debonding, fiber fracture, fiber pull-out, delamination (in laminates). - **Real-world examples to memorize:** Boeing 787, Airbus A350, F1 chassis, wind turbine blades, fiberglass boat hulls, Kevlar body armor. - **Advantages:** High specific strength/modulus, anisotropy tailorability, corrosion resistance, fatigue resistance, vibration damping. - **Disadvantages:** High cost (especially carbon fiber), complex manufacturing, recyclability challenges, difficult inspection for internal defects, brittle failure.

topics.title: Super Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Super Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Superalloy** is defined as an alloy developed for elevated-temperature service, exhibiting excellent **mechanical strength**, **creep resistance**, **surface stability (oxidation and corrosion resistance)**, and **thermal fatigue resistance** at temperatures typically above **540 °C (1000 °F)** without losing its structural integrity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition (Recall-Ready):**
> "Superalloys are a class of high-performance metallic materials, based on group VIII elements (Fe, Ni, Co), that possess exceptional strength, resistance to surface degradation, and excellent creep and fatigue life at temperatures exceeding 540 °C, often retaining useful properties up to ~85 % of their absolute melting temperature."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module-3 Weightage):**
> Superalloys are frequently asked as **Part A (3 marks)** conceptual questions and as **Part B (14 marks)** descriptive + comparison questions. The most common long-answer pattern is: *Classify superalloys, explain strengthening mechanisms, and compare Fe/Ni/Co-based systems.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Visualization)

Imagine a **marathon runner** competing in the **Sahara Desert heat**:
- A normal steel bar is like an **ordinary runner** who can sprint well at room temperature but collapses after 10 minutes in the desert.
- A **superalloy** is like an **elite, heat-trained athlete** who:
  1. **Keeps running** even at 800 °C (extreme **creep resistance**).
  2. **Does not rust** in the harsh, oxygen-rich, sulfur-laden environment (**oxidation/corrosion resistance**).
  3. **Retains muscle mass** because its internal "fibres" (precipitates) are locked in place (**precipitation hardening**).
  4. **Resists repeated stress cycles** — the equivalent of sprint-rest-sprint cycles during the race (**thermal fatigue resistance**).

> **Just like a marathon runner is engineered through training, nutrition, and genetics, a superalloy is engineered through controlled composition, heat treatment, and microstructure design.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Remember)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **≥ 540 °C (1000 °F)** | Minimum definition threshold |
| Maximum Service Temperature | **~1200 °C** | Single-crystal Ni-superalloys (turbine blades) |
| Homologous Temperature Limit | **0.6 – 0.85 T_m** | Where T_m is the absolute melting point (K) |
| Typical Service Stress | **50 – 500 MPa** | High-stress, high-temperature zone |
| Density Range | **7.8 – 9.2 g/cm³** | Co-based ≈ Ni-based > Fe-based |

---

## 1.4 Why Superalloys are Considered "Super" — Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Superalloy Performance:**
> 1. **Pillar 1 — High-Temperature Strength:** Retains tensile, yield, and rupture strength well above 540 °C.
> 2. **Pillar 2 — Creep Resistance:** Resists time-dependent plastic deformation under constant load at elevated temperature.
> 3. **Pillar 3 — Surface Stability:** Forms a protective, adherent oxide layer (e.g., Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃) that prevents further degradation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Superalloys

Superalloys are broadly classified on the basis of their **principal matrix element**:

| Class | Matrix | Typical Max Use Temp | Key Characteristics | Example Alloys |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Iron-based (Fe)** | Fe-Ni-Cr | ~650 °C | Cheapest, moderate temperature, used in steam turbines | A-286, Incoloy 800/825 |
| **Nickel-based (Ni)** | Ni-Cr(-Al-Ti) | ~1100 °C | Best high-T strength, γ′/γ″ precipitation | Inconel 718, Waspaloy, Rene 80, CMSX-4 |
| **Cobalt-based (Co)** | Co-Cr-W(-Ni) | ~950 °C | Best hot-corrosion resistance, weldability | Stellite 6, Haynes 25, MAR-M 509 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"FeNCo" → **F**irst, **N**ickel is the **C**hampion, **O**nly then Cobalt* — meaning Ni-superalloys dominate 80 % of the market for high-temperature applications.

---

## 2.2 Four Strengthening Mechanisms in Superalloys (The "Why" Behind Strength)

### 2.2.1 Solid-Solution Strengthening
- **What:** Solute atoms (Mo, W, Cr, Co, Ti, Al) are dissolved in the γ (FCC matrix) phase, creating local lattice distortions.
- **How:** These distortions hinder dislocation motion, raising the **critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**.
- **Why it works at high T:** The solute atoms have a different size/modulus, creating a **misfit strain** that pins dislocations.
- **Effect:** Modest, but it remains stable up to ~0.6 T_m.

### 2.2.2 Precipitation Hardening (γ′ and γ″ Strengthening) — *The Dominant Mechanism*
- **γ′ (Gamma Prime) — Ni₃(Al, Ti):**
  - Ordered L1₂ (FCC-based, ordered) structure.
  - Coherently precipitates in the γ matrix with a **lattice misfit of ~0.1 – 1 %**.
  - Dislocations must either **cut** the precipitate (pair-coupling mechanism) or **bow around** it (Orowan looping).
  - **Strength increases with temperature up to ~0.7 T_m** — counter-intuitive because most alloys soften here.
- **γ″ (Gamma Double-Prime) — Ni₃Nb:**
  - Ordered DO₂₂ (BCT) structure.
  - Principal strengthener in **Inconel 718** (used in jet engines).
  - Less thermally stable than γ′ — limits upper use temperature to ~650 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Question Magnet:** *"Explain the role of γ′ precipitates in Ni-based superalloys."* — Always mention the **ordered L1₂ structure, coherent interface, anti-phase boundary (APB) energy, and the unique inverse temperature dependence of strength.**

### 2.2.3 Carbide Strengthening
- **What:** Carbides like **MC, M₂₃C₆, M₆C** form along grain boundaries.
- **How:** They **pin grain boundaries** against sliding and migration, preventing grain coarsening.
- **Why:** Grain-boundary creep (Coble creep) is severely retarded.

### 2.2.4 Oxide-Dispersion Strengthening (ODS)
- **What:** Inert oxide particles (Y₂O₃ — yttria) are dispersed in the matrix via **mechanical alloying**.
- **How:** These particles (~nm size) are **stable up to the melting point** and pin dislocations.
- **Result:** Exceptional creep resistance at **> 1000 °C**.

---

## 2.3 Microstructural Components of a Ni-Based Superalloy

| Phase | Composition | Crystal Structure | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **γ (Gamma)** | Ni matrix (FCC) | A1 (FCC) | Continuous ductile matrix |
| **γ′ (Gamma prime)** | Ni₃(Al, Ti) | L1₂ (ordered FCC) | Main strengthener |
| **γ″ (Gamma double-prime)** | Ni₃Nb | DO₂₂ (BCT) | Strengthener (Inconel 718) |
| **MC carbides** | TiC, NbC, HfC | B1 (NaCl-type) | Grain-boundary pinning |
| **M₂₃C₆ carbides** | Cr₂₃C₆ | Complex cubic | Grain-boundary strengthener |
| **TCP phases** | σ, μ, Laves | Topologically close-packed | **Detrimental** — must be avoided |
| **Oxide layer** | Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃ | Amorphous/crystalline | Surface protection |

---

## 2.4 Processing Routes for Superalloys

1. **Investment Casting (Conventional Cast):** Polycrystalline, equiaxed grains.
2. **Directional Solidification (DS):** Columnar grains aligned with stress axis — eliminates transverse grain boundaries.
3. **Single-Crystal (SX) Casting:** No grain boundaries at all → superior creep life (used in modern high-pressure turbine blades).
4. **Wrought Processing:** Forging + rolling — for compressor discs, rings, shafts.
5. **Powder Metallurgy (PM/ODS):** For alloys with high alloying content that cannot be cast.
6. **Additive Manufacturing (AM):** Emerging route for complex geometries (LPBF, EBM).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{sd}}{RT}\right)$ | Steady-state creep (Norton power-law) | $\dot{\epsilon}$ in s⁻¹, σ in MPa, Q in J/mol |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} \cdot t_f}{C} = \text{const}$ | Monkman-Grant relationship | Predicts rupture life from creep rate |
| 3 | $LMP = T \left[\log(t_f) + C\right]$ | Larson-Miller Parameter | T in K, $t_f$ in hours, $C \approx 20$ |
| 4 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | Hall-Petch strengthening | Grain-size effect, d in mm |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{\gamma_{APB}}{b}$ | Dislocation shearing stress in γ′ | $\gamma_{APB}$ = anti-phase boundary energy |
| 6 | $T_{homologous} = \dfrac{T_{service}}{T_{melting}}$ | Homologous temperature (K) | Service > 0.5 T_m defines "high-T" alloys |
| 7 | $R_{ox} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_{ox}}{RT}\right)$ | Oxidation rate (Arrhenius form) | Parabolic oxidation kinetics |
| 8 | $\Delta a / a = \dfrac{a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma}}{a_{\gamma}} \times 100\%$ | Lattice misfit (%) | Optimal range: 0.1 – 1 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)** is the most-asked engineering formula in superalloy questions, especially for estimating **stress-rupture life** in gas-turbine components.

---

|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace Jet Engines** | Turbine blades, combustion chambers, after-burners | Operates at 1100 °C under 250 MPa centrifugal stress |
| **Power Generation** | Gas & steam turbine blades | Long-term creep life, oxidation resistance |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Steady-State Creep Rate (Norton-Bailey Power Law)

The Norton-Bailey equation is the foundation for predicting the long-term deformation of superalloys at high temperature. We derive it from a thermally activated dislocation-glide model.

### Step 1: Start with the Arrhenius-Type Strain-Rate Expression

For a thermally activated process, the strain rate depends exponentially on temperature:

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

where:
- $A_0$ = structure-dependent constant
- $Q$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 2: Introduce Stress Dependence (Power Law)

Experimental data shows that strain rate increases as a power of stress:

$$
A_0 = A \cdot \sigma^{n}
$$

where $n$ = **stress exponent** (typically 3 – 8 for dislocation creep in superalloys).

### Step 3: Combine Both

$$
\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q_{sd}}{RT}\right)
$$

This is the **Norton-Bailey equation** for steady-state creep.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A small increase in stress (σ) causes an **n-fold** increase in creep rate — explaining why superalloy blades are designed with **single-crystal + γ′ precipitates** to raise the effective $n$ and $Q$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Larson-Miller Parameter (Stress-Rupture Life)

The Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP) is widely used to **extrapolate short-term creep-rupture data to predict long-term service life** (e.g., 100,000-hour life for a turbine blade).

### Step 1: Start from the Rate Equation for Rupture

The time to rupture, $t_f$, is inversely proportional to the minimum creep rate (empirical Monkman-Grant relation):

$$
t_f \cdot \dot{\epsilon}_{min}^{m} = C
$$

For practical design, $m \approx 1$, so:

$$
t_f \approx \frac{C}{\dot{\epsilon}_{min}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Arrhenius Creep Equation

$$
t_f = \frac{C}{A \cdot \sigma^{n}} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{Q_{sd}}{RT}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Take the Logarithm

$$
\log(t_f) = \log\!\left(\frac{C}{A \sigma^{n}}\right) + \frac{Q_{sd}}{2.303 \, RT}
$$

### Step 4: Multiply Both Sides by T

$$
T \cdot \log(t_f) = \frac{Q_{sd}}{2.303 \, R} + T \cdot \log\!\left(\frac{C}{A \sigma^{n}}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Group Constants into the Larson-Miller Parameter

Define the **LMP** as:

$$
LMP = T \left[\log(t_f) + C\right]
$$

where $C \approx 20$ (empirical, material-dependent constant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** For a given alloy and stress level, **LMP is constant**. So if rupture life is known at one temperature, it can be predicted at another temperature by:
>
> $$T_1 \left[\log(t_1) + 20\right] = T_2 \left[\log(t_2) + 20\right]$$

### Worked Example (For Practice):

**Q:** An Inconel 718 specimen has a rupture life of **1000 hours at 900 °C**. Predict the rupture life at **800 °C** (same stress). Use $C = 20$.

**Solution:**

$$
900 \, \left[\log(1000) + 20\right] = T_2 \left[\log(t_2) + 20\right]
$$

$$
900 \times [3 + 20] = 1073 \times [\log(t_2) + 20]
$$

$$
900 \times 23 = 1073 \times [\log(t_2) + 20]
$$

$$
20700 = 1073 \cdot \log(t_2) + 21460
$$

$$
\log(t_2) = \frac{20700 - 21460}{1073} = \frac{-760}{1073} = -0.7083
$$

$$
t_2 = 10^{-0.7083} \approx 0.196 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Wait — this contradicts intuition! Lower temperature should give LONGER life, not shorter. Let me re-verify:**

Let me re-check by using **T in Kelvin**: $T_1 = 900 + 273 = 1173$ K, $T_2 = 800 + 273 = 1073$ K.

$$
1173 \times [3 + 20] = 1073 \times [\log(t_2) + 20]
$$

$$
1173 \times 23 = 1073 \times [\log(t_2) + 20]
$$

$$
26979 = 1073 \cdot \log(t_2) + 21460
$$

$$
1073 \cdot \log(t_2) = 5519
$$

$$
\log(t_2) = 5.144
$$

$$
t_2 = 10^{5.144} \approx 1.39 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours} \approx 16 \text{ years}
$$

> ✅ **Correct answer (with T in Kelvin):** The rupture life **increases from 1,000 hours to ~140,000 hours** when the temperature is reduced from 900 °C to 800 °C — consistent with physical intuition.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Mistake:** Forgetting to convert °C to **Kelvin (K = °C + 273)** in the LMP equation. This single error can cause an order-of-magnitude error in the predicted life. **Always use T in Kelvin for LMP.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lattice Misfit Calculation

For a Ni-based superalloy, the lattice misfit (δ) controls the coherency strain and hence the γ′ strengthening.

### Given:
- Lattice parameter of γ matrix: $a_{\gamma} = 0.3584$ nm
- Lattice parameter of γ′ precipitate: $a_{\gamma'} = 0.3601$ nm

### Calculation:

$$
\delta = \frac{a_{\gamma'} - a_{\gamma}}{a_{\gamma}} = \frac{0.3601 - 0.3584}{0.3584}
$$

$$
\delta = \frac{0.0017}{0.3584} = 4.74 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\delta \approx +0.474 \%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **positive, small misfit (0.1 – 1 %)** is ideal — it creates a coherent strained interface that effectively pins dislocations, while avoiding loss of coherency (which would weaken the alloy).

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Logic — Why γ′ Strength Increases with Temperature

This is a **counter-intuitive** behavior unique to γ′-strengthened superalloys and is a **favorite KTU 14-mark question**.

### Step 1: Dislocation-Glide Mechanism
At low temperatures, dislocations move through γ′ by **cutting** — they form **anti-phase boundaries (APB)**. This requires energy $\gamma_{APB}$.

### Step 2: Role of APB Energy
The **APB energy is roughly temperature-independent**, but the **yield stress actually increases** with temperature up to ~0.7 T_m.

### Step 3: Kear-Wilsdorf Locking
At higher temperatures, dislocations **cross-slip** from the $\{111\}$ slip plane to the $\{100\}$ cube plane, where they are **immobilized** (Kear-Wilsdorf lock). The **CRSS increases** because cross-slip is thermally activated.

### Step 4: Peak Temperature
This continues until ~650 – 750 °C (depending on Al/Ti content), after which the γ′ begins to **coarsen** and the strength drops.

> **Result:** The yield stress vs. temperature curve has a **positive slope** up to the peak, opposite to most metals — and this is what makes superalloys "super."
