# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **Q1.** Define **superalloys**. List any **two** major applications of Ni-based superalloys.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A superalloy is a high-performance metallic alloy, generally based on group-VIII elements (Fe, Ni, Co), which exhibits exceptional **mechanical strength**, **creep resistance**, **oxidation/corrosion resistance**, and **thermal fatigue life** at temperatures above **540 °C (1000 °F)**, typically retaining useful strength up to **0.85 T_m**.

**Two Major Applications of Ni-based Superalloys:**

1. **Aero-engine turbine blades and discs:** Single-crystal Ni-superalloys (e.g., CMSX-4, PWA 1484) operate in the hot section of jet engines at temperatures exceeding 1100 °C under centrifugal stresses of ~250 MPa.
2. **Rocket-engine combustion chambers and nozzles:** Inconel 718 and Haynes 230 are used in liquid-propellant rocket engines due to their exceptional high-temperature strength and resistance to thermal shock and oxidation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q2.** What is the role of the **γ′ (gamma prime) phase** in nickel-based superalloys?

**Model Answer:**

The γ′ (gamma prime) phase, with composition **Ni₃(Al, Ti)** and an **ordered L1₂ crystal structure**, is the **principal high-temperature strengthener** in Ni-based superalloys. Its role is summarized below:

1. It precipitates **coherently** within the γ (FCC) matrix with a small lattice misfit (~0.1 – 1 %), creating a coherent strained interface that **effectively pins dislocations**.

2. Dislocations must either **shear the precipitate** (creating an anti-phase boundary, APB) or **bow around it (Orowan looping)**, both of which significantly increase the **critical resolved shear stress (CRSS)**.

3. Due to the Kear-Wilsdorf locking mechanism, the strength of γ′ **increases with temperature up to ~0.7 T_m**, an inverse temperature dependence that is unique to superalloys.

4. This extraordinary property is what allows Ni-superalloys to retain useful strength at 1000 °C+ in jet-engine turbine blades.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **Q.A.** **(a)** Classify superalloys with examples. Compare the **properties and applications** of Ni-based, Co-based, and Fe-based superalloys. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With neat sketches, explain the **four strengthening mechanisms** in Ni-based superalloys. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Q.A. (a) — (7 Marks)

**Classification of Superalloys (with Examples):**

Superalloys are classified into three major categories based on the principal matrix element:

1. **Iron-based (Fe) superalloys:** Matrix is Fe with Ni and Cr additions. Examples: A-286, Incoloy 800, N-155.
2. **Nickel-based (Ni) superalloys:** Matrix is Ni with Cr, Al, Ti, Mo, W additions. Examples: Inconel 718, Waspaloy, Rene 80, CMSX-4 (single crystal).
3. **Cobalt-based (Co) superalloys:** Matrix is Co with Cr, W, Ni additions. Examples: Stellite 6, Haynes 25, MAR-M 509.

**Comparison Table (Board-Ready):**

| Property | Fe-based | Ni-based | Co-based |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Matrix element** | Fe (BCC/FCC) | Ni (FCC) | Co (FCC/HCP) |
| **Maximum service T** | ~650 °C | ~1100 °C | ~950 °C |
| **Strengthening** | Carbides, γ′ (limited) | γ′, γ″, carbides, solid solution | Carbides, solid solution |
| **Oxidation resistance** | Moderate | Excellent (Al₂O₃) | Excellent (Cr₂O₃) |
| **Hot-corrosion resistance** | Moderate | Good | **Best in class** |
| **Weldability** | Good | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Applications** | Steam-turbine casings, bolting | Jet-engine blades, combustors | Hot-corrosive environments, weld overlays |

**Conclusion:** Ni-based superalloys dominate aerospace high-temperature applications due to the unique γ′ strengthening; Co-based alloys are preferred where hot-corrosion and weldability dominate; Fe-based alloys serve cost-sensitive, lower-temperature needs.

---

### Model Answer to Q.A. (b) — (7 Marks)

**The Four Strengthening Mechanisms in Ni-Based Superalloys:**

1. **Solid-Solution Strengthening (SSS):**
   Solute atoms such as Mo, W, Co, and Cr are dissolved in the γ matrix, creating local lattice strain fields. These strain fields interact with dislocations and increase the **CRSS**. The size misfit (Snoek-type interaction) and modulus misfit are the two main mechanisms. Strength contribution is moderate but stable up to ~0.6 T_m.

2. **Precipitation Hardening (γ′ and γ″):**
   The **γ′ (Ni₃Al, Ni₃Ti) phase** is the principal strengthener, with an **ordered L1₂ structure** that precipitates coherently in the γ matrix. Dislocations moving through the matrix encounter the γ′ precipitates and must either shear them (forming APB) or bypass them via Orowan looping. **γ″ (Ni₃Nb)** strengthens alloys like Inconel 718 through a body-centered-tetragonal DO₂₂ structure.

3. **Carbide Strengthening:**
   Carbides — primarily **MC, M₂₃C₆, and M₆C** — form along grain boundaries and within the matrix. They:
   - Pin grain boundaries against migration (preventing grain growth).
   - Inhibit grain-boundary sliding and cavity formation during creep.
   - In modern single-crystal superalloys, the carbon content is reduced to minimize grain-boundary carbides (since GBs are absent in SX alloys).

4. **Oxide-Dispersion Strengthening (ODS):**
   Inert, nanometer-sized oxide particles — typically **Y₂O₃ (yttria)** — are dispersed in the matrix via **mechanical alloying** followed by hot extrusion. These particles are:
   - Thermodynamically stable up to the melting point.
   - Finely distributed (~nm spacing), providing exceptional **dislocation pinning** at temperatures exceeding 1000 °C, where even γ′ begins to coarsen.
   - Used in alloys like **MA 956 and MA 6000** for advanced aerospace and power-generation applications.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

> **Q.B.** **(a)** With the help of a **stress vs. temperature curve**, explain why γ′-strengthened Ni-superalloys show **anomalous yield behavior**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write notes on **(i) single-crystal superalloys**, **(ii) directionally solidified superalloys**, and **(iii) ODS superalloys**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Q.B. (a) — (7 Marks)

**The Anomalous Yield Behavior of γ′-Strengthened Ni-Superalloys:**

Unlike conventional metals whose yield strength **decreases** with increasing temperature, γ′-strengthened Ni-superalloys exhibit a **positive slope** in the yield stress vs. temperature curve up to a peak temperature (~650 – 800 °C), after which the strength finally decreases.

**Explanation (Kear-Wilsdorf Mechanism):**

1. **At low temperatures** (T < 500 °C), the primary slip system is the close-packed **{111} plane**. Dislocations shear the γ′ precipitates by forming **anti-phase boundaries (APB)**. The CRSS is relatively low.

2. **As temperature increases**, a unique phenomenon occurs: screw dislocations in the γ matrix can **cross-slip** from the {111} plane (where they are mobile) to the **non-{111} {100} cube plane** of the γ′ phase.

3. On the {100} cube plane, the APB energy is **lower**, so the dislocations become **immobilized** as **Kear-Wilsdorf locks**. They are no longer able to glide, effectively **increasing the CRSS**.

4. This cross-slip is a **thermally activated process** (requires thermal energy to overcome the activation barrier), so as temperature rises, more screw dislocations get locked, and the **yield stress increases** — a positive temperature dependence.

5. The peak is reached at ~0.7 T_m (≈ 700 – 800 °C). Beyond this, the γ′ precipitates **coarsen** (Ostwald ripening), reducing the precipitate density, and the alloy softens.

**Sketch (to be drawn on paper):**

- X-axis: Temperature (°C) — from room temperature to ~1200 °C.
- Y-axis: Yield stress (MPa).
- The curve rises gently from RT, peaks sharply at ~750 °C, then drops steeply.

---

### Model Answer to Q.B. (b) — (7 Marks)

**Notes on Three Advanced Superalloy Variants:**

**(i) Single-Crystal (SX) Superalloys:**
- Produced by **directional solidification with a helical selector** at the bottom of the mold, which forces only one grain to grow vertically.
- **No grain boundaries** are present — eliminating the weakest link for creep and fatigue.
- The γ′ precipitates are aligned along the [001] stress axis, **maximizing creep life**.
- Example alloys: **CMSX-4, PWA 1484, Rene N5**.
- **Used in:** High-pressure turbine blades in modern jet engines (GE9X, Trent XWB).
- **Disadvantage:** Anisotropic properties, expensive to manufacture.

**(ii) Directionally Solidified (DS) Superalloys:**
- Grains are **columnar** and aligned parallel to the principal stress axis.
- **Eliminates transverse grain boundaries** (which are perpendicular to stress and are the most damage-prone).
- Retains some grain boundaries (longitudinal) but they are aligned with the stress — far less damaging.
- Example alloys: **MAR-M 247 DS, Rene 80 DS**.
- **Used in:** Industrial gas turbine blades, aero-engine high-pressure turbine blades (older generation).

**(iii) ODS (Oxide-Dispersion-Strengthened) Superalloys:**
- Manufactured by **mechanical alloying** of elemental powders with Y₂O₃ (yttria) in a high-energy ball mill, followed by hot extrusion or hot isostatic pressing (HIP).
- The dispersed Y₂O₃ particles are **stable up to the melting point** (unlike γ′, which coarsens above ~800 °C).
- Provides **superior creep strength above 1000 °C**, beyond the capability of γ′-strengthened alloys.
- Example alloys: **MA 956 (Fe-based ODS), MA 6000 (Ni-based ODS), PM 2000**.
- **Used in:** Advanced gas-turbine combustor liners, rocket-nozzle extensions, fission-reactor cladding.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Superalloy Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → Kelvin** in the **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP)** — this is a 1 – 2 mark deduction if the calculation appears in a numerical.
> 2. **Confusing γ′ (Ni₃Al) with γ″ (Ni₃Nb)** — γ′ has an **L1₂ (ordered FCC)** structure, while γ″ has a **DO₂₂ (BCT)** structure. Mixing them up costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Writing "γ′ strengthens by solid-solution strengthening"** — this is a conceptual error. γ′ is a **precipitation-hardening** mechanism, NOT a solid-solution mechanism. (1 mark deduction per occurrence.)
> 4. **Stating that Co-based superalloys are the strongest** — Co-based alloys are the **most weldable and corrosion-resistant**, but **Ni-based alloys are the strongest** at the highest temperatures. (1 – 2 marks.)
> 5. **Not mentioning the L1₂ ordered structure of γ′** — this is a **mandatory keyword** in any 7-mark question on γ′ strengthening. Examiners specifically look for it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Superalloys (Module 3):**

- [x] **Definition:** Alloys based on Fe, Ni, or Co that retain **mechanical strength + surface stability + creep resistance** above **540 °C**.
- [x] **Three Classes:** **Fe-based** (cheapest, ~650 °C), **Ni-based** (strongest, ~1100 °C, γ′ strengthened), **Co-based** (best weldability + hot-corrosion, ~950 °C).
- [x] **γ′ Phase (Ni₃Al/Ti):** Ordered **L1₂ structure**, coherent with γ matrix, **principal strengthener** in Ni-superalloys.
- [x] **γ″ Phase (Ni₃Nb):** **DO₂₂ (BCT)** structure, principal strengthener of **Inconel 718**, stable only up to ~650 °C.
- [x] **Anomalous Yield Behavior:** Strength **increases with temperature** up to ~0.7 T_m due to **Kear-Wilsdorf locks** (cross-slip from {111} to {100}).
- [x] **Four Strengthening Mechanisms:** Solid-solution, precipitation (γ′/γ″), carbide (M₂₃C₆), and oxide-dispersion (Y₂O₃).
- [x] **Processing Routes:** Investment cast → equiaxed; DS → columnar; SX → no GBs; PM + mechanical alloying → ODS.
- [x] **Lattice Misfit (δ):** Optimal range is **+0.1 % to +1 %** for coherent strengthening.
- [x] **Larson-Miller Parameter (LMP):** $LMP = T \left[\log(t_f) + 20\right]$, where **T must be in Kelvin (K = °C + 273)**.
- [x] **Monkman-Grant Rule:** $t_f \cdot \dot{\epsilon}_{min}^{m} = C$ (predicts rupture life from creep rate).
- [x] **Norton Power Law:** $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \cdot \sigma^{n} \cdot \exp(-Q/RT)$ with $n \approx 3 - 8$.
- [x] **Top Applications:** Jet-engine turbine blades (SX Ni-superalloys), rocket nozzles (Inconel 718), industrial gas turbines (DS alloys), combustion liners (ODS alloys).
- [x] **TCP Phases (σ, μ, Laves):** **Detrimental** — must be avoided by compositional control.
- [x] **Oxide Layers:** Al₂O₃ (best, from Ni-Al alloys) and Cr₂O₃ (from Ni-Cr alloys) provide surface protection.
- [x] **Single-Crystal Advantage:** No grain boundaries → maximum creep life; e.g., CMSX-4 in modern high-bypass turbofan engines.
- [x] **Hot-Corrosion Champion:** **Co-based superalloys** (Stellite 6, Haynes 25) — used where sulfur/chlorine attack dominates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermetallic - Stoichiometric and Non-Stoichiometric Compounds and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermetallic - Stoichiometric and Non-Stoichiometric Compounds and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Intermetallic Compounds — Stoichiometric, Non-Stoichiometric Phases & Engineering Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intermetallic Compound (IMC):** A solid-state, ordered compound formed between two or more metallic elements (or a metal and a semi-metal) that possesses a **definite crystal structure** and composition, which is structurally and crystallographically distinct from the parent constituent metals.

In the language of the KTU 2024 scheme, intermetallics occupy a unique position in the spectrum of engineering materials — they are neither purely metallic (random solid solutions) nor purely ceramic (ionic/covalent). They are best described as **electronically and structurally ordered metallic phases** with mixed metallic, covalent, and sometimes ionic bonding character.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Strict Marriage vs. The Flexible Roommate":**
> - Imagine two elements, A and B, sharing a house. If they are a **stoichiometric intermetallic**, they insist on a strict ratio (e.g., exactly one A for every one B — like a marriage contract). No more, no less. The crystal lattice would collapse if you tried to substitute.
> - If they form a **non-stoichiometric intermetallic**, they are more like flexible roommates — a range of compositions (say, 30%–35% A and 65%–70% B) is acceptable; some A atoms may leave vacant sites, or extra B atoms may squeeze in, but the overall crystal structure remains intact.

### 1.3 Key Atomic Metrics & Standards

- **Electron Concentration (e/a):** Ratio of total valence electrons to total atoms in the unit cell. **Typical values: 3/2 (β-phase), 21/13 (γ-phase), 7/4 (ε-phase)** for Hume-Rothery electron compounds.
- **Pauling's Metallic Radius:** Used to determine size-factor compatibility (must be < ±15% for substitutional solubility).
- **Lattice Parameter (a):** Typically measured in **Å (Ångström) = 10⁻¹⁰ m** or **pm = 10⁻¹² m**.
- **Atomic Packing Factor (APF):** For ordered BCC-based structures (B2-CsCl type), APF ≈ **0.68**; for FCC-based (L1₂-Cu₃Au type), APF ≈ **0.74**.

### 1.4 Stoichiometric vs. Non-Stoichiometric — Snapshot Definition

| Property | Stoichiometric (Daltonide / Line Compound) | Non-Stoichiometric (Berthollide) |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Fixed, definite atomic ratio | Variable within a range |
| **Phase Diagram Width** | Vertical line (zero width) | Finite homogeneity range |
| **Crystal Order** | Long-range order maintained strictly | Order + point defects |
| **Example** | CuAu (1:1), Fe₃C, NiAl | β-CuZn (46–50% Zn), γ-brass |
| **Defect Type** | Essentially defect-free | Vacancies, antisites, interstitials |
| **KTU 2024 Significance** | Predictable, brittle, high-strength | Ductile, tunable properties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The line compound (stoichiometric) appears as a **single vertical line** in a binary phase diagram, whereas a non-stoichiometric phase appears as a **region of finite width**. This distinction is *frequently* tested in university examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Intermetallic Compounds (Hierarchy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes intermetallics into **three broad families** based on the dominant factor governing their formation:

1. **Geometric / Size-factor Compounds** — governed by atomic radius ratio.
2. **Electron Compounds (Hume-Rothery Phases)** — governed by valence electron concentration (VEC).
3. **Electrochemical / Valence Compounds** — governed by electronegativity differences (e.g., Zintl phases, NiAs-type).

#### 2.1.1 Geometric Compounds (Laves Phases, AB₂ type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Laves Phases** are the most abundant intermetallics in nature. They form when the **ideal atomic radius ratio r_A/r_B ≈ 1.225**, allowing close-packing optimization. The three structure types are:
> - **MgCu₂ (C15)** — Cubic, 8 atoms per unit cell
> - **MgZn₂ (C14)** — Hexagonal, 12 atoms per unit cell
> - **MgNi₂ (C36)** — Hexagonal, 24 atoms per unit cell

The ideal radius ratio derivation:

$$r_{A} = 1.225 \cdot r_{B}$$

This geometric magic number arises from the requirement that the larger A atom touches 4 smaller B atoms (forming a tetrahedron) while the smaller B atom is surrounded by 6 A atoms in an ideal close-packed configuration.

#### 2.1.2 Electron Compounds (Hume-Rothery Phases)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hume-Rothery Rule:** A particular crystal structure (β, γ, ε) becomes stable at a characteristic **Valence Electron Concentration (VEC = e/a)**:
> - **β-phase (BCC):** VEC = **3/2 = 1.5** → e.g., CuZn, AgCd
> - **γ-phase (complex cubic, 52 atoms/cell):** VEC = **21/13 ≈ 1.615**
> - **ε-phase (HCP):** VEC = **7/4 = 1.75** → e.g., CuZn₃, AgCd₃

**Derivation Logic of the Magic Numbers:**

The Brillouin zone of a BCC structure touches the {110} planes at VEC = 1.5. When the Fermi sphere just touches these Brillouin zone boundaries, the structure becomes energetically stable. Mathematically:

$$\text{VEC} = \frac{n_{\text{electrons}}}{n_{\text{atoms}}}$$

#### 2.1.3 Electrochemical (Valence) Compounds

Formed due to large electronegativity differences. Examples include **Zintl phases** (NaTl, CaGa) and **Hägg compounds** (carbides, nitrides with small non-metal atoms in octahedral metal interstices).

### 2.2 Stoichiometric (Daltonide) Compounds — Deep Analysis

A stoichiometric intermetallic has a **single, invariant composition point**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following:

- **Ordered crystal structures** (superlattices) — e.g., L1₀ (CuAu), L1₂ (Cu₃Au), B2 (CsCl-type, e.g., NiAl), DO₃ (Fe₃Al), DO₁₉ (Ti₃Al).
- **Sharp order-disorder transition** at a critical temperature (T_c).
- **Mixed bonding**: metallic + directional covalent (e.g., strong B-B or A-B directional bonds in NiAl, TiAl).
- **Brittleness at room temperature** due to limited independent slip systems and strong directional bonding.
- **High strength retention at elevated temperatures** — the long-range order persists, resisting dislocation climb.

**Key Order Parameter (Bragg-Williams):**

$$S = \frac{r_{A} - X_{A}}{1 - X_{A}}$$

Where:
- $r_A$ = fraction of A-sites occupied by A atoms (correct positions)
- $X_A$ = atomic fraction of A in the alloy
- $S = 1$ → perfectly ordered; $S = 0$ → fully disordered

### 2.3 Non-Stoichiometric (Berthollide) Compounds — Deep Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Berthollide phases** (named after Claude Louis Berthollet, opposing Proust's law of definite proportions) exhibit:
> 1. **Point defect concentration** proportional to deviation from stoichiometry
> 2. **Wide homogeneity range** (e.g., β-CuZn exists from 46.5 to 50.5 at.% Zn)
> 3. **Tunable electronic and mechanical properties**

**Defect Mechanisms in Non-Stoichiometric Intermetallics:**

- **Substitutional defects:** Excess B atoms replace A atoms → **antisite defects** (B on A sublattice, denoted B_A).
- **Vacancy defects:** Missing A atoms on the A sublattice → **structural vacancies** (V_A).
- **Interstitial defects:** Small atoms in interstitial voids.

For a compound AB with excess B, the dominant defect type is determined by the **formation energy hierarchy**: usually antisite defects dominate for narrow off-stoichiometry, while structural vacancies dominate at the extreme ends.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Valence Electron Concentration | $\text{VEC} = e/a = \dfrac{\sum (V_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum x_i}$ | 1.5, 1.615, 1.75 (e/a) | Hume-Rothery phase prediction |
| Laves Phase Radius Ratio | $r_A / r_B = 1.225$ | dimensionless | AB₂ phase stability |
| Pauling Size Factor | $\delta = \dfrac{r_A - r_B}{r_B} \times 100\%$ | must be $< \pm 15\%$ | Substitutional solubility |
| Order Parameter (Bragg-Williams) | $S = \dfrac{r_A - X_A}{1 - X_A}$ | $0 \le S \le 1$ | Degree of long-range order |
| APF for B2 (CsCl-type) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^3}$ where $a = \frac{2(r_A + r_B)}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $\approx 0.68$ | Ordered BCC structure |
| APF for L1₂ (Cu₃Au-type) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{4 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{a^3}$ | $\approx 0.74$ | Ordered FCC structure |
| Atomic Packing Factor (general) | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{atoms}}}{V_{\text{unit cell}}}$ | dimensionless | Crystal density calculation |
| Density of Intermetallic | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot a^3}$ | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | Mass per unit cell |
| Lattice Parameter (B2, CsCl-type) | $a = \frac{2(r_A + r_B)}{\sqrt{3}}$ | pm or nm | Unit cell dimension |
| Lattice Parameter (L1₂, Cu₃Au-type) | $a = \frac{2(r_A + r_B)}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$ | pm or nm | Unit cell dimension |
| Critical Order-Disorder Temp | $T_c$ (experimentally determined) | K or °C | Stability of ordered phase |
| % Atomic Packing | $\text{Packing} = \text{APF} \times 100\%$ | % | Crystal efficiency |
| Steric Limiting Ratio (Laves) | $(r_A + r_B)/r_B = 1.225$ | dimensionless | Geometric fit criterion |
| **Important Constants** | $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol; $1$ Å $= 10^{-10}$ m | — | Crystallographic calcs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule Reminder:** Vertical pipe symbols `|` have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent LaTeX absolute-value notation from breaking the markdown table rendering.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of This System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classification of intermetallics is not academic — it directly determines:
> 1. **Aerospace material selection** (Ni₃Al for turbine blades — VEC ≈ 8.25, L1₂ ordered).
> 2. **Magnetic storage density** (Nd₂Fe₁₄B — tetragonal, VEC-rich rare earth intermetallic).
> 3. **Biomedical implant biocompatibility** (TiNi / Nitinol shape-memory intermetallic).
> 4. **Energy conversion efficiency** (Nb₃Sn, MgB₂ superconductors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Laves Phase Critical Radius Ratio (r_A/r_B = 1.225)

**Problem Setup:** Consider an AB₂ Laves phase with the MgCu₂ (C15) cubic structure. The structure can be described as a diamond arrangement of B-atom tetrahedra interpenetrating a pyrochlore arrangement of A atoms. We need to find the **ideal radius ratio** for a hard-sphere close-packed fit.

**Step 1: Identify the Coordination Geometry**

In the MgCu₂ structure, the larger A atoms are coordinated by **12 B atoms** and **4 A atoms** (CN = 16 total). The smaller B atoms are coordinated by **6 A atoms** and **6 B atoms** (CN = 12 total). For ideal close-packing at the A-site:

The A atom sits at the center of a Friauf polyhedron (a truncated tetrahedron of B atoms), where 4 B atoms form a tetrahedron around A with edge length $2r_B$.

**Step 2: Relate the Tetrahedron Edge to the A-Atom Radius**

If the four B atoms touch the central A atom along the tetrahedral directions (each B-A distance = $r_A + r_B$), and the B atoms touch each other at the tetrahedron edge ($2r_B$), then by the geometry of a regular tetrahedron:

$$r_A + r_B = \frac{2 r_B}{\sqrt{\frac{8}{3}}} = \frac{2 r_B \sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} = r_B \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$$

Wait, let me correct this using the proper tetrahedral relationship. The distance from the center of a regular tetrahedron to a vertex, given an edge length $L$, is:

$$d_{\text{center-vertex}} = \frac{L \sqrt{6}}{4}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Edge Length Constraint**

The edge of the tetrahedron is determined by B-B contact: $L = 2r_B$. The center-to-vertex distance (which must equal $r_A + r_B$ for A to touch B) is:

$$r_A + r_B = \frac{2r_B \sqrt{6}}{4} = \frac{r_B \sqrt{6}}{2}$$

**Step 4: Solve for the Radius Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_A + r_B &= \frac{r_B \sqrt{6}}{2} \\
r_A &= \frac{r_B \sqrt{6}}{2} - r_B \\
r_A &= r_B \left( \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} - 1 \right) \\
r_A &= r_B \left( \frac{\sqrt{6} - 2}{2} \right) \\
\frac{r_A}{r_B} &= \frac{\sqrt{6} - 2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Evaluation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{6} &= 2.449489743 \\
\sqrt{6} - 2 &= 0.449489743 \\
\frac{r_A}{r_B} &= \frac{0.449489743}{2} = 0.224744871 \\
\Rightarrow \frac{r_A}{r_B} &= 1.224744871... \approx 1.225
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The ideal radius ratio for Laves phase formation is **r_A / r_B ≈ 1.225** (commonly rounded). This is a direct consequence of geometric close-packing requirements — no electronic or thermodynamic factors are required to predict this number.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electron Concentration for β-Hume-Rothery Phase (VEC = 3/2)

**Problem Setup:** In a β-phase intermetallic (BCC-derived, CsCl-type ordered structure), calculate the valence electron concentration (VEC) when one B atom (valence = 2, e.g., Zn) is mixed with one A atom (valence = 1, e.g., Cu).

**Step 1: Recall the Definition**

$$\text{VEC} = \frac{e}{a} = \frac{\text{Total valence electrons in unit cell}}{\text{Total atoms in unit cell}}$$

**Step 2: Apply to Cu-Zn System (β-brass)**

The β-phase CuZn has the B2 (CsCl-type) structure. For **one formula unit** of CuZn:
- Number of Cu atoms: 1 (valence electrons per Cu = 1, assuming monovalent)
- Number of Zn atoms: 1 (valence electrons per Zn = 2, assuming divalent)
- Total atoms: $1 + 1 = 2$
- Total valence electrons: $1 \times 1 + 1 \times 2 = 3$

**Step 3: Compute VEC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VEC} &= \frac{1(1) + 1(2)}{1 + 1} \\
\text{VEC} &= \frac{3}{2} \\
\text{VEC} &= 1.500 \, e/a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Generalization**

For a generic $A_x B_y$ phase with valences $V_A$ and $V_B$:

$$
\text{VEC} = \frac{x \cdot V_A + y \cdot V_B}{x + y}
$$

**Step 5: Verify with AgCd (β-phase, VEC = 3/2)**

Ag is monovalent ($V_{Ag} = 1$), Cd is divalent ($V_{Cd} = 2$):

$$\text{VEC} = \frac{1(1) + 1(2)}{1+1} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.500 \, e/a \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Density of an Ordered Intermetallic (B2 NiAl)

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the theoretical density of stoichiometric NiAl (50 at.% Ni, 50 at.% Al), which crystallizes in the B2 (CsCl) structure. Given:
- Lattice parameter: $a = 2.887$ Å $= 2.887 \times 10^{-8}$ cm
- Molar mass of Ni: $M_{Ni} = 58.69$ g/mol
- Molar mass of Al: $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol
- Avogadro's number: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol

**Step 1: Identify Atoms per Unit Cell**

In the B2 (CsCl-type) structure:
- 1 Ni atom at the corner: $8 \times \frac{1}{8} = 1$ Ni
- 1 Al atom at the body center: 1 Al
- **Total atoms per unit cell = 2**

**Step 2: Calculate Mass per Unit Cell**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{1 \cdot M_{Ni} + 1 \cdot M_{Al}}{N_A} \\
m_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{(1)(58.69) + (1)(26.98)}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \, \text{g} \\
m_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{85.67}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \, \text{g} \\
m_{\text{cell}} &= 1.4226 \times 10^{-22} \, \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Unit Cell Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{cell}} &= a^3 = (2.887 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{cm})^3 \\
V_{\text{cell}} &= 2.4069 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{m_{\text{cell}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} \\
\rho &= \frac{1.4226 \times 10^{-22}}{2.4069 \times 10^{-23}} \, \text{g/cm}^3 \\
\rho &= 5.911 \, \text{g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\rho_{NiAl} \approx 5.91$ g/cm³. This low density (compared to Ni-based superalloys at ~8.5 g/cm³) makes NiAl attractive for weight-critical aerospace applications — a key KTU 2024 highlight.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stoichiometric vs. Non-Stoichiometric Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Stoichiometric (e.g., NiAl, TiAl)** | **Non-Stoichiometric (e.g., β-CuZn, Ni₃Al)** | **Engineering Consequence** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Composition range | Single point | Range (e.g., 25–27 at.% Al for Ni₃Al) | Property tunability |
| Defect structure | Negligible | High (vacancies, antisites) | Affects diffusion, creep |
| Slip systems | Limited (often $\{111\}\langle 110\rangle$) | More numerous when off-stoichiometric | Ductility improvement |
| Yield strength anomaly | Strong (peak at low T) | Moderate | High-T strength retention |
| Room temperature ductility | Poor (0–2% for polycrystalline TiAl) | Better | Processability |
| Creep resistance | Excellent | Good | Turbine blade service life |
| Oxidation resistance | Forms protective Al₂O₃ layer | Variable | High-T durability |
| KTU 2024 application | Aerospace (turbines, exhaust) | Magnetic (NdFeB), superconducting (Nb₃Sn) | Multi-domain relevance |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — VEC of Cu₅Zn₈ (γ-phase)

**Given:** A γ-brass phase has composition Cu₅Zn₈. Cu is monovalent (V_Cu = 1), Zn is divalent (V_Zn = 2).

**Step 1: Identify Atom Counts**

- 5 Cu atoms, 8 Zn atoms
- Total atoms = 13

**Step 2: Compute Total Valence Electrons**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{\text{total}} &= 5 \times V_{Cu} + 8 \times V_{Zn} \\
e_{\text{total}} &= 5(1) + 8(2) \\
e_{\text{total}} &= 5 + 16 = 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute VEC**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VEC} &= \frac{e_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{atoms}}} = \frac{21}{13} \\
\text{VEC} &= 1.6154 \, e/a
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result matches Hume-Rothery's γ-phase rule (21/13)** — a direct KTU-favorite verification problem. This proves the electron compound theory quantitatively.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Hierarchy of Intermetallic Compounds

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Intermetallic Compounds] --> B[Geometric / Size-Factor]
    A --> C[Electron Compounds]
    A --> D[Electrochemical / Valence]
    A --> E[Stoichiometric Daltonide]
    A --> F[Non-Stoichiometric Berthollide]

    B --> B1[Laves Phases AB2]
    B --> B2[Frank-Kasper Phases]

    C --> C1[Beta phase BCC VEC 3 over 2]
    C --> C2[Gamma phase Complex Cubic VEC 21 over 13]
    C --> C3[Epsilon phase HCP VEC 7 over 4]

    D --> D1[Zintl Phases NaTl]
    D --> D2[Hagg Carbides Nitrides]

    E --> E1[L10 CuAu type]
    E --> E2[L12 Cu3Au type]
    E --> E3[B2 CsCl type NiAl]
    E --> E4[DO3 Fe3Al type]

    F --> F1[Beta CuZn 46 to 50 at percent Zn]
    F --> F2[Ni3Al 25 to 27 at percent Al]
    F --> F3[FeAl wide range]

    B1 --> G1[Aerospace MgCu2 type]
    C1 --> G2[Brass CuZn]
    E3 --> G3[Turbine Blades NiAl]
    E2 --> G4[Superalloys Ni3Al]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid v10+ syntax safeguards. Labels use plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to prevent parser errors.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Stoichiometric vs Non-Stoichiometric Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Input1[Input: Two or more metals A and B] --> Decision1{Is composition fixed?}
    Decision1 -->|Yes Fixed Ratio| Stoich[Stoichiometric Phase]
    Decision1 -->|No Variable Range| NonStoich[Non-Stoichiometric Phase]

    Stoich --> S1[Ordered Crystal Structure]
    Stoich --> S2[Sharp Order Disorder Transition]
    Stoich --> S3[Defect Free Lattice]
    Stoich --> S4[Brittle at Room Temp]
    Stoich --> S5App[Application: Shape Memory NiTi]
    Stoich --> S6App[Application: High Temp Structural NiAl]

    NonStoich --> N1[Defects: Vacancies Antisites Interstitials]
    NonStoich --> N2[Wide Homogeneity Range]
    NonStoich --> N3[Tunable Properties]
    NonStoich --> N4[Better Ductility than Stoich]
    NonStoich --> N5App[Application: Magnetic Nd2Fe14B]
    NonStoich --> N6App[Application: Superconducting Nb3Sn]
    NonStoich --> N7App[Application: Hydrogen Storage LaNi5]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Engineering Application Mapping of Intermetallics

```mermaid
graph TD
    APPS[Engineering Applications of Intermetallics] --> AERO[Aerospace]
    APPS --> MAG[Magnetic Materials]
    APPS --> SHM[Shape Memory]
    APPS --> SUP[Superconductors]
    APPS --> H2[Hydrogen Storage]
    APPS --> CAT[Catalysts]

    AERO --> AERO1[Ni3Al Turbine Blades]
    AERO --> AERO2[TiAl Jet Engine Components]
    AERO --> AERO3[NiAl Low Density Oxidation Resistant]

    MAG --> MAG1[Nd2Fe14B Permanent Magnets]
    MAG --> MAG2[SmCo5 High Temp Magnets]
    MAG --> MAG3[AlNiCo Legacy Magnets]

    SHM --> SHM1[NiTi Nitinol Biomedical Stents]
    SHM --> SHM2[CuAlNi Actuators]

    SUP --> SUP1[Nb3Sn High Field Superconductor]
    SUP --> SUP2[NbTi MRI Magnets]
    SUP --> SUP3[MgB2 Medium Tc Superconductor]

    H2 --> H21[LaNi5 Reversible Hydride]
    H2 --> H22[TiFe Low Cost Storage]

    CAT --> CAT1[Pt3Sn Petroleum Refining]
    CAT --> CAT2[Ni3Al Hydrogenation]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams map the conceptual landscape of intermetallics. The "Block-Level Functional Architecture" approach is used (per KTU ENGINE V10 guidelines) for classification and decision-flow schematics, ensuring compatibility with Mermaid's text-rendering engine.

---

### 4.4 Table — Ordered Crystal Structures (KTU High-Yield)

| **Structure Type** | **Strukturbericht** | **Prototype** | **Space Group** | **Atoms/Cell** | **Example Intermetallic** | **Application Domain** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CsCl-type | B2 | CsCl | Pm3m | 2 | NiAl, CuZn, FeAl | High-T structural |
| Cu₃Au-type | L1₂ | Cu₃Au | Pm3m | 4 | Ni₃Al, Cu₃Au, Zr₃Al | Superalloys |
| CuAu-type | L1₀ | CuAu | P4/mmm | 2 | TiAl, FePt, CoPt | Magnetic recording |
| Fe₃Al-type | DO₃ | BiF₃ / Fe₃Al | Fm3m | 16 | Fe₃Al, Fe₃Si | Soft magnetic |
| Ti₃Al-type | DO₁₉ | Ni₃Sn | P6₃/mmc | 8 | Ti₃Al, Ni₃Sn | Aerospace |
| MgCu₂-type | C15 | MgCu₂ | Fd3m | 24 | MgCu₂, Fe₂Mo | Laves phase |
| MgZn₂-type | C14 | MgZn₂ | P6₃/mmc | 12 | MgZn₂, Fe₂Ti | Laves phase |
| NaCl-type | B1 | NaCl | Fm3m | 8 | TiC, TiN (cermets) | Hägg compounds |
| NaTl-type | B32 | NaTl | Fd3m | 16 | NaTl, LiAl, LiGa | Zintl phase |
| NiAs-type | B8 | NiAs | P6₃/mmc | 4 | NiAs, FeS | Chalcogenides |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

> **Q1.** Define an intermetallic compound. Distinguish between stoichiometric and non-stoichiometric intermetallics with **two examples each**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Mark Distribution: Definition 1M + Distinction Table 1M + Examples 1M):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** An intermetallic compound is an ordered, solid-state compound formed between two or more metallic elements, possessing a crystal structure and stoichiometry distinct from the parent metals. Intermetallics exhibit mixed metallic, covalent, and sometimes ionic bonding.

> **Distinction (1 Mark):**
> - **Stoichiometric (Daltonide):** Fixed, definite composition; appears as a single vertical line on the phase diagram; sharp melting/congruent behavior.
> - **Non-Stoichiometric (Berthollide):** Variable composition within a range; appears as a region on the phase diagram; contains point defects.

> **Examples (1 Mark):**
> - Stoichiometric: **NiAl (1:1)**, **Fe₃C**, **CuAu**.
> - Non-Stoichiometric: **β-CuZn (46–50 at.% Zn)**, **Ni₃Al (25–27 at.% Al)**.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**

> **Q2.** State **Hume-Rothery's rules** for the formation of **electron compounds**. Write the **VEC values** for β, γ, and ε phases.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Rules 1.5M + VEC values 1.5M):**

> **Hume-Rothery's Rules for Electron Compounds (1.5 Marks):**
> 1. The crystal structure of an electron compound is determined primarily by the **valence electron concentration (VEC = e/a)**, not by the specific elements involved.
> 2. The same VEC value produces the **same structure type** across different alloy systems.
> 3. Electron compounds form in **noble metal alloys (Cu, Ag, Au)** with polyvalent B-subgroup metals (Zn, Al, Sn).

> **VEC Values (1.5 Marks):**
> - **β-phase (BCC):** VEC = **3/2 = 1.500 e/a** (e.g., CuZn, AgCd)
> - **γ-phase (complex cubic, 52 atoms):** VEC = **21/13 ≈ 1.615 e/a** (e.g., Cu₅Zn₈, Ag₅Cd₈)
> - **ε-phase (HCP):** VEC = **7/4 = 1.750 e/a** (e.g., CuZn₃, AgCd₃)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024, Model Question]**

> **Q3 (a)** With suitable examples, classify intermetallic compounds into **stoichiometric and non-stoichiometric** types. Explain the **ordered crystal structures** L1₂, L1₀, and B2 with neat sketches (or schematic descriptions). **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b)** Discuss the **electron compound theory** (Hume-Rothery phases) and derive the VEC values for **β, γ, and ε** phases with examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

> **Classification of Intermetallics (3 Marks):**
>
> **Stoichiometric (Daltonide) Intermetallics:**
> - Definition: Compounds with a **fixed, definite atomic ratio** that is invariant.
> - Exist as a **single point/vertical line** on the binary phase diagram.
> - Possess **long-range crystallographic order** even at high temperatures (below T_c).
> - Examples: **CuAu (L1₀)**, **Cu₃Au (L1₂)**, **NiAl (B2)**, **Fe₃Al (DO₃)**, **TiAl (L1₀)**, **MgCu₂ (C15)**.
>
> **Non-Stoichiometric (Berthollide) Intermetallics:**
> - Definition: Compounds exhibiting a **range of compositions** within which the same crystal structure persists.
> - Appear as a **finite region of finite width** on the phase diagram.
> - Property variation is enabled by **point defects** — antisite atoms, vacancies, or interstitials.
> - Examples: **β-CuZn (46–50 at.% Zn)**, **γ-Cu₅Zn₈ (with variable Cu/Zn ratio)**, **Ni₃Al (23–27 at.% Al)**, **FeAl (30–50 at.% Al)**.

> **Ordered Crystal Structures (4 Marks):**
>
> **L1₂ (Cu₃Au-type, Strukturbericht):**
> - Based on FCC lattice with Cu atoms at face centers and Au at corners (or vice versa).
> - 4 atoms per unit cell (3 Cu + 1 Au).
> - Stoichiometry A₃B.
> - Example: **Ni₃Al** (γ'-strengthener in Ni-base superalloys).
> - Lattice parameter: $a \approx 3.567$ Å for Ni₃Al.
>
> **L1₀ (CuAu-type):**
> - Tetragonally distorted FCC.
> - Alternating atomic layers: pure A layer, pure B layer, pure A layer, pure B layer.
> - 2 atoms per unit cell (1 A + 1 B).
> - Stoichiometry AB.
> - Examples: **TiAl**, **FePt**, **CoPt** (the latter two are key for hard magnetic recording media).
> - c/a ratio ≈ 0.98–1.02 depending on system.
>
> **B2 (CsCl-type):**
> - Based on BCC lattice, with A at corner and B at body center.
> - 2 atoms per unit cell (1 A + 1 B).
> - Stoichiometry AB (1:1).
> - Example: **NiAl** — exhibits anomalous yield strength increase with temperature.
> - Lattice parameter: $a \approx 2.887$ Å for NiAl.
> - Coordination: 8 nearest neighbors of opposite type (CN = 8).

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

> **Electron Compound Theory (Hume-Rothery Phases) — Full Derivation (7 Marks):**
>
> **Conceptual Basis (2 Marks):**
> In 1934, **William Hume-Rothery** discovered that for alloys of **noble metals (Cu, Ag, Au)** with **polyvalent B-subgroup metals (Zn, Al, Sn, Cd)**, the same crystal structure (β, γ, or ε) appears at a **characteristic valence electron concentration (VEC = e/a)**, regardless of the specific elements involved. The structural stability is governed by the **Fermi surface — Brillouin zone interaction**: when the Fermi sphere just touches the Brillouin zone boundaries, the structure becomes energetically stabilized.
>
> **Definition of VEC (1 Mark):**
> $$\text{VEC} = \frac{e}{a} = \frac{\text{Total number of valence electrons in unit cell}}{\text{Total number of atoms in unit cell}}$$
>
> **Derivation of β-phase VEC = 3/2 (2 Marks):**
> Consider CuZn (β-brass): Cu is monovalent (V_Cu = 1), Zn is divalent (V_Zn = 2).
> $$\text{VEC}_{\beta} = \frac{1 \cdot V_{Cu} + 1 \cdot V_{Zn}}{1 + 1} = \frac{1(1) + 1(2)}{2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.500 \, e/a$$
> This value matches the BCC-derived CsCl (B2) structure because the **Fermi surface of the disordered BCC phase touches the {110} Brillouin zone boundaries at VEC = 1.5**.
>
> **Derivation of γ-phase VEC = 21/13 (1 Mark):**
> For Cu₅Zn₈ (γ-brass): 5 Cu (1 valence) + 8 Zn (2 valence) = 13 atoms.
> $$\text{VEC}_{\gamma} = \frac{5(1) + 8(2)}{5 + 8} = \frac{5 + 16}{13} = \frac{21}{13} \approx 1.615 \, e/a$$
> The γ-phase has a **complex cubic structure with 52 atoms per unit cell**, stabilized when the Fermi surface touches the **Brillouin zone of the {330} and {411} planes**.
>
> **Derivation of ε-phase VEC = 7/4 (1 Mark):**
> For CuZn₃ (ε-brass): 1 Cu (1 valence) + 3 Zn (2 valence) = 4 atoms.
> $$\text{VEC}_{\varepsilon} = \frac{1(1) + 3(2)}{1 + 3} = \frac{1 + 6}{4} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.750 \, e/a$$
> The ε-phase has an HCP structure stabilized by Fermi surface contact with the **basal {0001} planes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing monovalent vs divalent** in transition metals — Cu, Ag, Au are conventionally monovalent; Zn, Al, Cd, Sn are divalent (in electron compound theory). Mixing this up gives the wrong VEC.
> 2. **Forgetting valence of group III/IV elements** — Al is treated as divalent (2 e⁻) in Hume-Rothery compounds, not trivalent, because only sp-electrons are counted.
> 3. **Wrong atomic ratio** — Always cross-check the stoichiometry (e.g., Cu₅Zn₈ has 13 atoms, not 12 or 14).
> 4. **Skipping the Fermi-surface justification** — Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the physical reason behind the magic numbers, not just arithmetic.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

> **Q4 (a)** What are **Laves phases**? Derive the **critical radius ratio** for their formation. Give **three examples** and discuss their engineering applications. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q4 (b)** Discuss the **applications of intermetallic compounds** in detail, covering at least **four engineering domains** with specific material examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

> **Definition of Laves Phases (1 Mark):**
> Laves phases are the most abundant class of intermetallic compounds, with the general formula **AB₂**, where A is the larger atom and B is the smaller. They are named after the crystallographer **Friedrich Laves** (1927) and account for a significant fraction of all known binary intermetallics.

> **Three Structure Types (2 Marks):**
> - **C15 (MgCu₂-type, cubic, Fd3m):** 24 atoms per unit cell.
> - **C14 (MgZn₂-type, hexagonal, P6₃/mmc):** 12 atoms per unit cell.
> - **C36 (MgNi₂-type, hexagonal, P6₃/mmc):** 24 atoms per unit cell.
> All three are topologically equivalent — formed by stacking layers of A and B atoms in slightly different sequences.

> **Derivation of Critical Radius Ratio r_A/r_B = 1.225 (3 Marks):**
> *The detailed derivation is given in Section 3.1. Summary for exam writing:*
>
> The A atom sits at the center of a tetrahedron formed by 4 B atoms. The distance from the center of a regular tetrahedron to a vertex (for edge length $L = 2r_B$) is:
> $$r_A + r_B = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} \cdot r_B$$
> Solving:
> $$\frac{r_A}{r_B} = \frac{\sqrt{6} - 2}{2} = \frac{2.449 - 2}{2} = \frac{0.449}{2} \approx 1.225$$
>
> *Geometric Interpretation:* The A atom must be exactly ~22.5% larger than B for close-packing efficiency. If r_A/r_B < 1.225, no Laves phase forms (smaller B atoms cannot form the ideal tetrahedral network around A). If r_A/r_B ≫ 1.225, other structure types (e.g., CsCl-type) form instead.

> **Three Examples and Engineering Applications (1 Mark):**
> - **MgCu₂ (C15):** Lightweight structural alloy for automotive and aerospace components; Mg-based for weight reduction.
> - **Fe₂Mo (C14):** Precipitation-strengthening phase in creep-resistant ferritic steels (e.g., P91, P92 boiler steels for ultra-supercritical power plants).
> - **TiFe₂ (C14):** Hydrogen storage material (alternative to LaNi₅); lightweight Ti-alloy matrices.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks — 1.75 Marks per Domain)**

> **Application Domain 1: Aerospace High-Temperature Materials (1.75 Marks):**
> - **Ni₃Al (γ'-phase, L1₂):** The principal strengthening precipitate in Ni-base superalloys (e.g., Inconel 718, René 95). Used in single-crystal turbine blades operating at >1100°C.
> - **TiAl (γ-phase, L1₀):** Low-density (~3.9 g/cm³) intermetallic for jet engine low-pressure turbine blades (e.g., GE GE9X engine, Mitsubishi regional jet).
> - **NiAl (β-phase, B2):** Potential turbine coating material — exceptional oxidation resistance via Al₂O₃ formation.

> **Application Domain 2: Magnetic Materials (1.75 Marks):**
> - **Nd₂Fe₁₄B (tetragonal, P4₂/mnm):** The **strongest permanent magnet** commercially available (B_r up to 1.4 T, H_c up to 2.5 MA/m). Used in electric vehicle motors, wind turbine generators, hard disk drives, MRI machines.
> - **SmCo₅ and Sm₂Co₁₇:** Samarium-cobalt magnets with superior high-temperature performance (up to 350°C) — used in aerospace actuators and military systems.
> - **AlNiCo:** Al-Ni-Co-Fe intermetallic magnets, still used in sensors and instruments.

> **Application Domain 3: Shape Memory and Superelastic Materials (1.75 Marks):**
> - **NiTi (Nitinol, B2 austenite → B19' martensite):** Equiatomic nickel-titanium — exhibits shape memory effect and superelasticity. Used in **biomedical stents**, orthodontic archwires, eyeglass frames, actuators, and aerospace deployment systems.
> - **Cu-Al-Ni and Cu-Zn-Al:** Cheaper shape-memory alternatives for actuators and couplings.

> **Application Domain 4: Superconductors and Energy (1.75 Marks):**
> - **Nb₃Sn (A15 structure):** Used in high-field superconducting magnets (B > 10 T). Applications include MRI scanners, particle accelerators (CERN LHC), and fusion reactor coils (ITER).
> - **NbTi (A2 BCC):** Workhorse superconductor for magnets up to 9 T — used in MRI, scientific instruments.
> - **MgB₂:** Higher T_c (~39 K) — used in cryogenic systems and fault-current limiters.
> - **LaNi₅ (CaCu₅-type):** Reversible hydrogen storage material for fuel cell and nickel-metal hydride (NiMH) batteries.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q4:**
> 1. **Avoid generic answers** — examiners expect specific alloy names and structure types (e.g., "Nd₂Fe₁₄B has P4₂/mnm space group," not just "rare earth magnets").
> 2. **Do NOT confuse shape memory with superelasticity** — shape memory is a thermal cycle (cooling/heating), superelasticity is stress-induced at constant T. Students mix these up routinely.
> 3. **Laves phase stoichiometry** is AB₂, NOT A₂B (mark loss of 1 if reversed).
> 4. **VEC is dimensionless** — write "e/a" or "1.5" not "1.5 e⁻" (the unit is just per-atom, not an absolute count).
> 5. **In numerical problems, always show units** — losing 0.5–1 mark for missing units is the single most common valuation penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- **Intermetallic Compound (IMC):** Ordered, solid-state compound of two or more metals with a distinct crystal structure.
- **Stoichiometric (Daltonide) ↔ Single composition ↔ Vertical line in phase diagram ↔ Defect-free ordered structure ↔ Examples: NiAl, CuAu, Fe₃C, TiAl.**
- **Non-Stoichiometric (Berthollide) ↔ Variable composition ↔ Finite region in phase diagram ↔ Point defects (vacancies, antisites) ↔ Examples: β-CuZn, Ni₃Al, FeAl.**
- **Hume-Rothery Electron Compounds ↔ Governed by VEC = e/a ↔ β: 3/2 ↔ γ: 21/13 ↔ ε: 7/4.**
- **Laves Phases ↔ AB₂ stoichiometry ↔ r_A/r_B ≈ 1.225 ↔ C14 (MgZn₂), C15 (MgCu₂), C36 (MgNi₂) ↔ Used in Fe₂Mo, Fe₂Ti precipitates.**
- **Geometric/Size-Factor compounds form when atomic sizes differ by > 15% (Pauling's rule).**
- **Ordered Structures:** L1₀ (CuAu, TiAl), L1₂ (Cu₃Au, Ni₃Al), B2 (CsCl, NiAl), DO₃ (Fe₃Al), DO₁₉ (Ti₃Al).
- **Lattice Parameter B2 (CsCl):** $a = \dfrac{2(r_A + r_B)}{\sqrt{3}}$; **L1₂ (Cu₃Au):** $a = 2\sqrt{2}\,r$.
- **Density formula:** $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M}{N_A \cdot a^3}$.
- **Order Parameter (Bragg-Williams):** $S = \dfrac{r_A - X_A}{1 - X_A}$; $S=1$ perfect order, $S=0$ random.
- **Key Engineering Applications:**
  - **Aerospace:** Ni₃Al, TiAl, NiAl (turbines, jet engines).
  - **Magnetic:** Nd₂Fe₁₄B, SmCo₅ (motors, wind generators, MRI).
  - **Shape Memory:** NiTi / Nitinol (stents, actuators).
  - **Superconductors:** Nb₃Sn, NbTi, MgB₂.
  - **Hydrogen Storage:** LaNi₅, TiFe.
  - **Catalysts:** Pt₃Sn, Raney nickel (NiAl precursor).
- **Magic VEC Numbers (3/2, 21/13, 7/4) ↔ Fermi surface touching Brillouin zone boundaries (KTU favorite).**
- **Avogadro's number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol.
- **1 Å = $10^{-10}$ m = 0.1 nm = 100 pm.**
- **B2 APF ≈ 0.68; L1₂ APF ≈ 0.74; FCC disordered APF = 0.74; BCC disordered APF = 0.68.**
- **Yield Strength Anomaly (KTU favorite):** Many B2 intermetallics (NiAl, FeAl) exhibit *increasing* yield strength with temperature up to ~600°C — opposite of conventional metallic behavior. This is due to cross-slip of screw dislocations from {110} to {100} planes.
- **Brittleness of Stoichiometric IMCs:** Limited slip systems and strong directional bonding cause room-temperature brittleness; microalloying (e.g., B in Ni₃Al) or off-stoichiometric composition can improve ductility.
- **Mnemonic for Laves ratio:** *"One point two two five — the magic number stays alive."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maraging Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maraging Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛠️ Maraging Steel: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 📘 Formal KTU-Style Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maraging Steel** (a portmanteau of **MAR**tensite + **AGING**) is a class of **ultra-high-strength, low-carbon martensitic steels** that derive their exceptional mechanical strength not from carbon strengthening (as in conventional martensitic steels), but from the **precipitation hardening of intermetallic compounds** during a low-temperature aging treatment.

The microstructure is essentially a **carbon-free (or near-zero carbon) Fe–Ni lath martensite matrix** that is subsequently hardened by the controlled precipitation of nanometer-scale intermetallics such as **Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo (Laves phase), and NiAl**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Specialty Steels):**
> Maraging steels represent the apex of precipitation-hardenable martensitic alloys. They are explicitly listed under the category of "Advanced High-Strength Steels" in the **PCMET205 2024 Scheme** syllabus and are critical for aerospace, defense, and tooling applications.

---

## 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: "The Skeleton of an Iron Skyscraper"

Imagine a steel skyscraper built in two stages:
1. **Stage 1 – The Soft Skeleton:** Builders first erect the building's entire steel framework using **mild, ductile rods** (low-carbon martensite). The structure is strong enough to stand, but flexible — easy to bend, shape, and weld.
2. **Stage 2 – The Hardening Pass:** Workers then walk through the building sprinkling a special **chemical powder** at the joints, which reacts slowly over hours at a moderate temperature to form **glue-like intermetallic precipitates** that lock everything rigidly in place.

The result? A structure that is **stiffer, stronger, and tougher** than it was before — without ever needing quenching or distortion. This is exactly how maraging steel works:

- **Soft skeleton** → Solution-annealed low-carbon Fe–Ni martensite
- **Chemical powder** → Aging at **480 °C for 3–6 h** precipitating **Ni₃Ti / Ni₃Mo**
- **Final skyscraper** → Ultra-high-strength, tough, dimensionally stable alloy

---

## ⚙️ Standard Chemical Composition (18Ni-300 Grade)

| Element | Typical Wt. % | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Ni** | **17 – 19 %** | Stabilizes low-carbon martensite, forms Ni₃Ti/Ni₃Mo |
| **Co** | **8 – 12 %** | Reduces Mo solubility in Fe → accelerates precipitation |
| **Mo** | **3 – 5 %** | Primary precipitation strengthener (Fe₂Mo Laves) |
| **Ti** | **0.2 – 1.6 %** | Forms Ni₃Ti precipitates — major strength contributor |
| **Al** | **0.1 – 0.2 %** | Forms NiAl (B2 ordered) precipitates |
| **C** | **≤ 0.03 %** | Intentionally kept ultra-low for weldability/toughness |
| **Fe** | **Balance** | Matrix former |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metric (KTU Board-Favorite Data Point):** The number in the grade designation (e.g., 18Ni-300) represents the **nominal yield strength in ksi**. Thus 18Ni-300 ≈ **2070 MPa** yield strength.

---

## 🔑 Why "Maraging" is Revolutionary

Conventional high-strength steels (e.g., AISI 4340, UTS ~1860 MPa) achieve strength through:
- High carbon (0.3–0.4 % C) → **Quench-hardened martensite**
- Tempering embrittlement risk
- Poor weldability
- Distortion during quenching

Maraging steel circumvents **all four** drawbacks simultaneously by decoupling the martensite formation (soft, ductile) from the strengthening mechanism (precipitation hardening). This is the central thesis examiners test.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 📐 Theoretical Framework: The "Why" Behind the Strength

Maraging steel's extraordinary properties arise from a deliberate orchestration of **four metallurgical mechanisms** operating in sequence. Each is critical and is a common KTU exam point.

### 1. Solid-State Phase Transformation: The Maraging Pathway

When a high-Ni (18 %), low-C (<0.03 %) Fe alloy is cooled from the **austenite (γ-FCC) region** to room temperature, the following sequence occurs:

- **γ (FCC, austenite)** $\xrightarrow{\text{air cool}}$ **α′ (BCT, low-C martensite)**
- The transformation start temperature **Mₛ ≈ 200 °C** and finish **M_f ≈ 100 °C**.
- Because the carbon content is negligible, the martensite is **BCT ≈ BCC (lath-type, ductile)** rather than the brittle BCT plate martensite of high-carbon steels.
- **Dimensional change during transformation is ~ 0.1 %** (vs. several % in quench-hardened steels) → minimal distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the martensite ductile?** Carbon is the primary solid-solution strengthener and the cause of lattice tetragonality. With C < 0.03 %, the martensite is essentially a **supersaturated Fe–Ni solid solution** with body-centered cubic/tetragonal distortion but without the brittleness of high-C martensite.

### 2. Solution Annealing: Homogenization

- **Temperature:** 820 – 850 °C (well within the single-phase γ field)
- **Time:** 1 h per 25 mm of section thickness
- **Cooling:** Air cool (no quenching needed)
- **Purpose:** Dissolve all intermetallics into solid solution and obtain a uniform soft martensite.

### 3. Aging Treatment: Precipitation Hardening

- **Temperature:** 480 – 500 °C (held for **3 – 6 hours**, typically 3 h for thin sections, 6 h for thick)
- **Cooling:** Air cool
- **Microstructural outcome:** Nucleation and growth of nanometer-scale intermetallic precipitates uniformly dispersed throughout the martensite matrix.

### 4. Strengthening Mechanisms Operating in Aged Maraging Steel

| # | Mechanism | Approx. Contribution to σ_y | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Solid-solution strengthening (Ni, Co, Mo in Fe)** | 300 – 500 MPa | Inherent in martensite matrix |
| 2 | **Grain / lath boundary strengthening (Hall–Petch)** | 200 – 400 MPa | Lath width ~ 0.1 – 0.5 μm |
| 3 | **Coherency strain hardening (Ni₃Ti, NiAl)** | 800 – 1200 MPa | Largest single contributor |
| 4 | **Orowan looping (Fe₂Mo Laves phase)** | 200 – 400 MPa | Semi-coherent precipitates |

---

## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **high-yield mathematical content** that KTU examiners (and the 2024 Scheme OBE pattern) test. Memorize these, as direct numerical or descriptive questions are common.

### 1. Hall–Petch Strengthening (Grain/Lath Boundary)

$$
\sigma_{y} \;=\; \sigma_{0} \;+\; k_{y} \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_{y}$ = yield strength
- $\sigma_{0}$ = lattice friction stress (for Fe–Ni martensite ≈ 150 MPa)
- $k_{y}$ = Hall–Petch coefficient (≈ 0.6 MPa·m$^{1/2}$ for BCC Fe)
- $d$ = mean lath width

### 2. Aging Kinetics – Avrami (Johnson–Mehl–Avrami) Equation

$$
f(t) \;=\; 1 - \exp\!\left(-k \cdot t^{n}\right)
$$

where:
- $f(t)$ = fraction transformed (precipitate volume fraction) at time $t$
- $k$ = temperature-dependent rate constant $= k_{0} \exp(-Q/RT)$
- $n$ = Avrami exponent (for diffusion-controlled 3-D nucleation-growth, $n \approx 1.5 - 2.5$)

### 3. Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Aging Rate

$$
k(T) \;=\; k_{0} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-Q}{R \cdot T}\right)
$$

where $Q$ ≈ 150 – 250 kJ/mol (Co + Mo + Ti diffusion in α-Fe).

### 4. Strengthening from Coherent Precipitates (Coherency Strain Model)

$$
\Delta \sigma_{coh} \;=\; 6 \cdot \mu \cdot \varepsilon^{3/2} \cdot f^{1/2} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{b}\right)^{1/2}
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = shear modulus of matrix
- $\varepsilon$ = lattice misfit strain
- $f$ = precipitate volume fraction
- $r$ = precipitate radius
- $b$ = Burgers vector

### 5. Orowan Looping Stress (for Non-shearable Particles)

$$
\tau_{Orowan} \;=\; \frac{\mu \cdot b}{L - 2r}
$$

where $L$ = inter-particle spacing, $r$ = particle radius.

---

## 🌎 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Component | Why Maraging Steel? |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Rocket motor casings, satellite structures | Highest strength-to-weight ratio, no quench distortion |
| **Defense** | Aircraft landing gear, missile bodies | Fracture toughness at UTS > 1800 MPa |
| **Tooling** | Die-casting dies, injection molds, punches | Dimensionally stable after aging, easy to machine in annealed state |
| **Motorsport / F1** | Drive shafts, connecting rods | Fatigue resistance, weldability |
| **Nuclear** | Sub-system components requiring low Co | Tunable Co content |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"soft-to-machine"** workflow is the killer feature for tooling: a mold-maker can machine a complex 18Ni-300 die block in the **~ 30 HRC annealed state**, then simply age-harden it to **~ 55 HRC** with no dimensional change. This is impossible with AISI H13 or D2 tool steels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧪 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔍 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Aging Time Required to Reach Peak Hardness (Avrami-Based)

### Problem Setup
A maraging steel component is aged at **T = 480 °C = 753 K**. Given that the rate constant $k = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{min}^{-n}$ and the Avrami exponent $n = 1.8$, find the time $t_p$ required to reach **99 % transformation** ($f(t_p) = 0.99$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the Avrami equation for fraction transformed**

$$
f(t) \;=\; 1 \;-\; \exp(-k \cdot t^{n})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the peak-transformation condition $f(t_p) = 0.99$**

$$
0.99 \;=\; 1 - \exp(-k \cdot t_{p}^{n})
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term**

$$
\exp(-k \cdot t_{p}^{n}) \;=\; 1 - 0.99 \;=\; 0.01
$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides**

$$
-k \cdot t_{p}^{n} \;=\; \ln(0.01) \;=\; -4.6052
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_p^n$**

$$
t_{p}^{n} \;=\; \frac{4.6052}{k} \;=\; \frac{4.6052}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 921.04 \; \text{min}^{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t_p$ by raising both sides to the power $1/n$**

$$
t_{p} \;=\; (921.04)^{1/1.8}
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate numerically (logarithmic computation)**

$$
\ln(921.04) \;=\; 6.8257
$$

$$
\frac{6.8257}{1.8} \;=\; 3.7921
$$

$$
t_{p} \;=\; \exp(3.7921) \;\approx\; 44.4 \; \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 8 — Engineering interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{p} \;\approx\; 45 \;\text{minutes at 480 °C to reach 99 \% peak hardness transformation.}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The standard industrial aging time of **3 – 6 h** provides a **safety margin** to ensure uniform through-thickness transformation in components of practical thickness (5 – 100 mm), not merely surface transformation.

---

## 🔍 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Hall–Petch Lath-Size Contribution to Yield Strength

### Problem Setup
A 18Ni-300 maraging steel has an average martensite lath width of $d = 0.30 \; \mu\text{m} = 3.0 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}$. Compute the grain-boundary strengthening contribution. Use $\sigma_{0} = 150 \; \text{MPa}$ and $k_{y} = 0.6 \; \text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Hall–Petch equation**

$$
\sigma_{y} \;=\; \sigma_{0} \;+\; k_{y} \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d^{-1/2}$**

$$
d^{-1/2} \;=\; (3.0 \times 10^{-7})^{-1/2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{3.0 \times 10^{-7}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the square root**

$$
\sqrt{3.0 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; \sqrt{3.0} \cdot 10^{-3.5} \;=\; 1.7321 \times 3.1623 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 5.477 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert**

$$
d^{-1/2} \;=\; \frac{1}{5.477 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 1825.7 \; \text{m}^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Hall–Petch term**

$$
k_{y} \cdot d^{-1/2} \;=\; 0.6 \times 1825.7 \;=\; 1095.4 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum with lattice friction term**

$$
\sigma_{y} \;=\; 150 \;+\; 1095.4 \;=\; 1245.4 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation**

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_{y, \text{GB}} \;\approx\; 1245 \; \text{MPa from grain-boundary strengthening alone.}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The lath width of 0.1 – 0.5 μm in maraging steel is **~ 10 – 50× finer** than the prior austenite grain size of conventional quench-tempered steels. This is one of the largest microstructural contributors to maraging steel's superiority.

---

## 📈 Analytical Matrix: Property Comparison vs. Conventional Steels

| Property | 18Ni-300 Maraging | AISI 4340 (Quenched + Tempered) | AISI H13 Tool Steel |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Composition (key)** | 18 Ni – 12 Co – 5 Mo – 1 Ti | 0.4 C – 1.8 Ni – 0.8 Cr – 0.25 Mo | 0.4 C – 5 Cr – 1.5 Mo – 1 V |
| **Yield Strength, σ_y (MPa)** | **2070** | 1620 | 1650 |
| **UTS (MPa)** | **2100** | 1860 | 1830 |
| **Elongation (%)** | **8 – 12** | 9 – 12 | 5 – 8 |
| **Fracture Toughness K_IC (MPa·m^0.5)** | **80 – 110** | 50 – 60 | 25 – 35 |
| **Hardness (HRC)** | **50 – 55** | 48 – 52 | 48 – 52 |
| **Heat Treatment** | Solution anneal + age (no quench) | Austenitize + oil-quench + temper | Austenitize + oil-quench + double temper |
| **Dimensional Change** | **< 0.05 %** | 0.2 – 0.5 % | 0.2 – 0.5 % |
| **Weldability** | **Excellent** | Poor (pre/post heat required) | Poor |
| **Machinability (annealed)** | **Excellent (~ 30 HRC)** | Moderate | Poor |

---

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Question Pattern)]

**Define maraging steel. Why is the carbon content restricted to less than 0.03 % in maraging steels?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Maraging steel is an **ultra-high-strength low-carbon martensitic steel** that derives its strength from the precipitation of **intermetallic compounds** (such as Ni₃Ti, Ni₃Mo, and NiAl) during a low-temperature aging treatment, rather than from carbon-induced martensite hardness as in conventional quenched-tempered steels.

**Reason for Low Carbon (2 Marks):**
- Carbon is intentionally restricted to **less than 0.03 %** because:
  1. **To avoid brittle BCT martensite:** High carbon produces body-centered-tetragonal martensite with high dislocation density and embrittlement, which would defeat the purpose of maraging.
  2. **To ensure weldability:** High-carbon martensitic steels are notoriously prone to **hydrogen-induced cold cracking** in the HAZ during welding. Low C eliminates this.
  3. **To enable tough lath martensite:** With negligible C, the martensite is essentially a ductile Fe–Ni BCC/BCT solid solution that can be plastically deformed prior to aging.
  4. **To prevent carbide formation** (Fe₃C, M₂₃C₆) that would consume alloying elements and degrade fracture toughness.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Pattern)]

**List the major intermetallic precipitates responsible for strengthening in 18Ni maraging steel. What is the role of cobalt?**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Precipitates (2 Marks):**
| Precipitate | Crystal Structure | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Ni₃Ti** | DO₂₄ (hexagonal) | Primary strengthener — coherent, large coherency strain |
| **Ni₃Mo** | DO₂₂ (tetragonal) | Coherent, contributes to mid-temperature hardness |
| **NiAl (β′)** | B2 (ordered BCC) | Coherent, strong precipitate-matrix lattice misfit |
| **Fe₂Mo (Laves)** | C14 hexagonal | Semi-coherent, contributes to Orowan strengthening |

**Role of Cobalt (1 Mark):**
Cobalt **does not form precipitates itself**. Instead, it **lowers the solubility of Mo in α-Fe**, thereby **increasing the volume fraction of Fe₂Mo Laves phase** that forms during aging. It also raises the Mₛ temperature and refines the martensite lath structure, indirectly enhancing both strength and toughness.

---

## 📕 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q.** **(a)** Describe the **heat treatment procedure** for 18Ni-300 maraging steel with temperature, time, and microstructural changes at each stage. Why is maraging steel free from quenching distortion? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **microstructure, properties, and applications** of maraging steel with **AISI 4340** quenched-tempered steel. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

### (a) Heat Treatment Procedure of 18Ni-300 Maraging Steel (7 Marks)

**Stage 1 – Solution Annealing (3 Marks)**
- **Temperature:** 820 – 850 °C (held within the single-phase γ-austenite field).
- **Soaking time:** Approximately 1 hour per 25 mm of section thickness to ensure complete dissolution of any intermetallic phases.
- **Cooling:** **Air cooling to room temperature** (no quenching medium required).
- **Microstructure after cooling:** A **soft, ductile, low-carbon lath martensite** (BCT with $c/a$ ratio ≈ 1.00) with hardness ~ **30 HRC**. The matrix is a **supersaturated solid solution of Ni, Co, Mo, Ti, and Al in α-Fe**.
- The Mₛ temperature is approximately **200 °C** and M_f is approximately **100 °C**, so the entire transformation occurs during air cooling.

**Stage 2 – Aging Treatment (3 Marks)**
- **Temperature:** 480 – 500 °C (below the austenite recrystallization temperature).
- **Time:** 3 hours (thin sections) to 6 hours (thick sections).
- **Cooling:** Air cool to room temperature.
- **Microstructural evolution:**
  - Initial: Nucleation of **GP zones / clusters** rich in Ni and Ti.
  - Intermediate: Growth of **coherent Ni₃Ti and NiAl** precipitates, 2 – 5 nm in size.
  - Final: Co-existence of coherent Ni₃Ti + semi-coherent Fe₂Mo (Laves) precipitates, 5 – 30 nm.
- **Final hardness:** ~ 55 HRC, **UTS > 2000 MPa**.

**Why No Quenching Distortion (1 Mark):**
Quenching distortion in conventional steels arises from:
- Rapid cooling → large thermal gradients → thermal stresses.
- Phase transformation (austenite → martensite) accompanied by **volumetric expansion (~ 4 %)**.
In maraging steel:
- The **Mₛ temperature is high (~ 200 °C)** and the transformation is complete by the time the piece reaches ~ 100 °C.
- **Air cooling is sufficient** to complete the martensitic transformation — no rapid quench is required.
- Carbon content is negligible, so the **volumetric expansion during transformation is only ~ 0.1 %** (vs. ~ 4 % in high-C steels).
- Therefore, **residual stresses and distortion are negligible** — a defining advantage of maraging.

### (b) Comparison: Maraging Steel vs. AISI 4340 (7 Marks)

| Property | 18Ni-300 Maraging Steel | AISI 4340 (Q&T) |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon content** | < 0.03 % | 0.38 – 0.43 % |
| **Strengthening mechanism** | Intermetallic precipitation (Ni₃Ti, NiAl) | Carbon in BCT martensite + carbide dispersion |
| **Heat treatment** | Solution anneal (820 °C) + Age (480 °C) | Austenitize (830 °C) + Oil-quench + Temper (200 – 600 °C) |
| **Quenching required** | **No (air cool)** | **Yes (oil quench)** |
| **Dimensional change** | < 0.05 % | 0.2 – 0.5 % |
| **As-machined hardness (annealed)** | ~ 30 HRC (easy to machine) | ~ 200 HB (harder to machine) |
| **Weldability** | Excellent | Poor (cracking risk) |
| **Yield strength (MPa)** | 2070 | 1620 |
| **Fracture toughness K_IC (MPa·m^0.5)** | 80 – 110 | 50 – 60 |
| **Service temp. limit** | ~ 400 °C (over-aging risk) | ~ 250 °C (temper embrittlement) |
| **Typical applications** | Rocket casings, aircraft landing gear, tooling | Gears, shafts, axles, crankshafts |

**Conclusion (to write in exam):**
Maraging steel is the superior alloy when **ultra-high strength, fracture toughness, weldability, and dimensional stability** are simultaneously required — particularly in aerospace and defense applications. AISI 4340 remains preferred for **cost-sensitive, high-toughness, moderate-temperature** applications like automotive gears and shafts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q.** **(a)** With the help of a **schematic diagram**, explain the **four sequential strengthening mechanisms** that operate in 18Ni-300 maraging steel. Derive the **Hall–Petch contribution** to its yield strength given lath width $d = 0.25 \; \mu\text{m}$, $\sigma_0 = 150 \; \text{MPa}$, and $k_y = 0.6 \; \text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^{1/2}$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **role of each major alloying element** (Ni, Co, Mo, Ti, Al) in 18Ni maraging steel. Why is maraging steel **unsuitable for service above 500 °C**? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

### (a) Four Strengthening Mechanisms + Hall–Petch Derivation (7 Marks)

**1. Solid-Solution Strengthening (1.5 Marks):**
The martensitic matrix is a supersaturated solid solution of **Ni, Co, and Mo in α-Fe**. These solute atoms create local lattice strain fields that **pin dislocations**, raising the critical resolved shear stress. Contribution: **300 – 500 MPa**.

**2. Grain / Lath Boundary Strengthening — Hall–Petch (1.5 Marks):**
The lath width of maraging martensite is exceptionally fine (0.1 – 0.5 μm). Grain boundaries act as **dislocation pile-up barriers**, forcing dislocations to bow and generate new dislocations in adjacent grains. Contribution: **200 – 400 MPa**.

**3. Coherency Strain Hardening (1.5 Marks):**
The primary strengthener. Coherent Ni₃Ti and NiAl precipitates have a **lattice parameter mismatch** with the α-Fe matrix, generating **spherical coherency strain fields**. Dislocations must either **cut through** (at small particle size) or **loop around** (Orowan mechanism) these obstacles. Contribution: **800 – 1200 MPa** — **the largest single contributor**.

**4. Orowan Looping (1 Mark):**
For semi-coherent or incoherent Fe₂Mo Laves phase particles (> 10 nm), dislocations **bypass** the particles by Orowan looping, leaving behind dislocation rings. Contribution: **100 – 200 MPa**.

**Schematic (1 Mark):** *(See Section 4 Diagram 3 of these notes.)*

**Hall–Petch Derivation (1 Mark):**

$$
\sigma_{y} \;=\; \sigma_{0} + k_{y} \cdot d^{-1/2}
$$

With $d = 0.25 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}$:

$$
d^{-1/2} \;=\; (2.5 \times 10^{-7})^{-1/2} \;=\; 2000 \; \text{m}^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{y} \;=\; 150 + (0.6)(2000) \;=\; 150 + 1200 \;=\; \boxed{1350 \; \text{MPa}}
$$

### (b) Role of Alloying Elements & 500 °C Service Limit (7 Marks)

**Ni (1.5 Marks):**
- Primary austenite stabilizer that lowers Mₛ to ~ 200 °C.
- **Forms Ni₃Ti and Ni₃Mo precipitates** — the principal strengthening phases.
- Retains ductility in the martensite matrix.

**Co (1.5 Marks):**
- **Reduces Mo solubility in α-Fe**, accelerating precipitation of Fe₂Mo Laves phase.
- Refines martensite lath structure, enhancing toughness.
- Does not itself form strengthening precipitates.

**Mo (1 Mark):**
- Forms **Fe₂Mo (Laves) phase** and **Ni₃Mo** — strong precipitators.
- Provides **secondary precipitation strengthening**.
- Contributes to solid-solution hardening.

**Ti (1 Mark):**
- Forms **Ni₃Ti** — the **most potent strengthener** via coherency strain.
- Critical for achieving UTS > 2000 MPa.

**Al (0.5 Mark):**
- Forms **NiAl (β′)** B2-ordered precipitates.
- Enhances high-temperature stability marginally.

**Why Unsuitable Above 500 °C (1.5 Marks):**
- Above ~ 500 °C, the **Ni₃Ti, NiAl, and Fe₂Mo precipitates undergo Ostwald ripening (coarsening)**, reducing coherency strain and lowering hardness (**over-aging**).
- **Reverse transformation** to austenite becomes thermodynamically favorable above ~ 500 °C, degrading the martensitic microstructure.
- Therefore, the **maximum service temperature is ~ 400 °C**, making maraging steel unsuitable for high-temperature applications like gas turbine blades.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Maraging Steel Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing maraging with ordinary martensitic steel:** Students often write "maraging steel is hardened by carbon in martensite." This is **the most common mistake** and **loses 2 – 3 marks instantly**. The defining feature is **carbon-free martensite + intermetallic precipitation**. Always state: *"Strengthening is by precipitation of Ni₃Ti / NiAl / Fe₂Mo, NOT by carbon."*
>
> 2. **Forgetting that aging is at 480 – 500 °C, not tempering at 200 – 600 °C:** Do NOT call the second stage "tempering." It is **aging** or **age-hardening**. Tempering implies carbide precipitation from martensite; aging implies intermetallic precipitation from a low-C martensite.
>
> 3. **Omitting the "soft-to-machine" advantage:** Examiners reward students who mention that the **solution-annealed state (~ 30 HRC) is machinable**, and the **final hardness is achieved AFTER machining via aging**. This is the key engineering advantage of maraging steel.
>
> 4. **Incorrect Avrami exponent:** For maraging steel precipitation, the Avrami exponent $n$ is typically **1.5 – 2.5** (not 3 – 4 as in nucleation-and-growth transformations of diffusional transformations like pearlite).
>
> 5. **Stating that Co forms precipitates:** Co does NOT form strengthening precipitates in maraging steel. Its role is to **lower Mo solubility and refine lath structure**.
>
> 6. **Mixing up grades:** 18Ni-**250** ≈ 1725 MPa yield; 18Ni-**300** ≈ 2070 MPa; 18Ni-**350** ≈ 2400 MPa. The number is **ksi**, not MPa.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Maraging Steel (KTU Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Name origin:** **MAR**tensite + **AGING** — iron matrix that is first transformed to martensite, then aged.
>
> ✅ **Carbon content:** Ultra-low, **< 0.03 %** — the defining feature that gives toughness and weldability.
>
> ✅ **Major alloying elements:** **Ni (18 %), Co (8 – 12 %), Mo (3 – 5 %), Ti (0.2 – 1.6 %), Al (0.1 – 0.2 %)**.
>
> ✅ **Heat treatment:** (1) Solution anneal at **820 – 850 °C, air cool** → soft lath martensite; (2) Age at **480 – 500 °C for 3 – 6 h, air cool** → hardened.
>
> ✅ **Strengthening precipitates:** **Ni₃Ti, NiAl (coherent — largest contribution), Ni₃Mo, Fe₂Mo Laves (semi-coherent).**
>
> ✅ **No quenching required:** Air cooling suffices — minimal distortion (< 0.05 %).
>
> ✅ **Grade designation:** Number = **yield strength in ksi** (e.g., 18Ni-300 = 300 ksi = 2070 MPa).
>
> ✅ **Mechanical properties:** UTS **1800 – 2400 MPa**, hardness **50 – 55 HRC**, fracture toughness **80 – 110 MPa·m^0.5**, elongation **8 – 12 %**.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Aerospace, rocket motor casings, aircraft landing gear, tooling (dies, punches), motorsport.
>
> ✅ **Service temperature limit:** **~ 400 °C** (over-aging / austenite reversion above this).
>
> ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:** Hall–Petch ($\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$), Avrami ($f(t) = 1 - e^{-k t^n}$), Arrhenius ($k = k_0 e^{-Q/RT}$).
>
> ✅ **Co's role:** Does NOT precipitate; **lowers Mo solubility** to enhance Fe₂Mo precipitation and refines lath structure.
>
> ✅ **Difference from AISI 4340:** Maraging has **lower C, higher Ni/Co/Mo/Ti, no quench, better weldability, better dimensional stability, higher K_IC**, but **higher cost**.

---
