The lever rule segment **adjacent to the α end** corresponds to the **liquid fraction** and vice versa — this is the "opposite-arm" rule. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **State the Gibbs Phase Rule. What is its significance in the study of phase diagrams?** **Model Answer:** > The Gibbs Phase Rule, formulated by J. Willard Gibbs, relates the number of degrees of freedom $F$ (independent intensive variables) to the number of components $C$ and the number of phases $P$ in a system at equilibrium: > $$F \;=\; C \;-\; P \;+\; 2$$ > For condensed systems (e.g., alloys) at constant pressure, this reduces to $F = C - P + 1$. > > **Significance:** (i) It tells us the **minimum number of variables** (T, P, composition) that must be fixed to completely describe a system's equilibrium state. (ii) It identifies **invariant** (F = 0, e.g., eutectic), **univariant** (F = 1), and **bivariant** (F = 2) equilibria. (iii) It limits the **maximum number of phases** that can co-exist in a system ($P_{\max} = C + 2$ generally; $C + 1$ for alloys). --- #### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]` **Define the Lever Rule. In which region of a binary phase diagram is it applicable?** **Model Answer:** > The **Lever Rule** is a mass-balance principle used to determine the **relative weight (or mole) fractions of two co-existing phases** in equilibrium at a given temperature in a binary phase diagram. > > **Mathematical Statement:** > $$W_\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}, \qquad W_\beta \;=\; 1 - W_\alpha$$ > where $C_0$ is the overall alloy composition, $C_\alpha$ and $C_\beta$ are the compositions of the $\alpha$ and $\beta$ phases respectively (read from the tie-line). > > **Region of Applicability:** The Lever Rule is applicable **only in two-phase regions** of a binary equilibrium phase diagram (e.g., α + L, α + β, β + L). It cannot be used in single-phase regions (where the phase fraction is trivially 1) or at invariant points (where the phase fraction is indeterminate from the rule alone). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the **Gibbs Phase Rule** from thermodynamic principles, clearly stating the role of the constant *2* in the final expression. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **lead–tin (Pb–Sn) solder alloy** contains **60 wt% Sn** and is at a temperature of **200 °C**. The eutectic composition of Pb–Sn is **61.9 wt% Sn** and the eutectic temperature is **183 °C**. Just above the eutectic, the compositions of the two co-existing phases (β-solid and liquid) are approximately: * $C_\beta$ (Pb-rich solid, β phase) ≈ **17 wt% Sn** * $C_L$ (liquid phase) ≈ **62.5 wt% Sn** Using the Lever Rule, determine: * (i) The weight fraction of the **β (solid) phase**. * (ii) The weight fraction of the **liquid phase**. * (iii) The weight percentage of **tin** present in the **solid phase** as a whole. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A** **(a) Derivation of the Gibbs Phase Rule (7 Marks)** **Statement of variables:** To specify the state of an equilibrium system with $C$ components and $P$ phases, we need to specify: * Temperature $T$ — 1 variable * Pressure $P$ — 1 variable * Composition of every phase — $C$ mole fractions per phase × $P$ phases = $PC$ mole fractions $$\therefore \text{Total variables} \;=\; 2 + PC$$ **Statement of constraints:** * **(i) Mole-fraction summation:** In each phase, $\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i = 1$, giving **$P$ independent constraints**. * **(ii) Chemical-potential equality:** At equilibrium, $\mu_i^{(\alpha)} = \mu_i^{(\beta)} = \cdots = \mu_i^{(P)}$ for each of the $C$ components. This gives $(P - 1)$ equalities per component → $C(P - 1)$ independent constraints. $$\therefore \text{Total constraints} \;=\; P + C(P - 1) \;=\; P + CP - C$$ **Degrees of freedom:** $$F \;=\; (2 + PC) - (P + CP - C)$$ $$F \;=\; 2 + PC - P - CP + C$$ $$\boxed{\,F \;=\; C - P + 2\,}$$ **Role of the constant 2:** The constant *2* represents the two external intensive variables that affect the Gibbs free energy of *every* phase — **temperature $T$** and **pressure $P$**. For condensed systems (alloys), where $P$ is held constant (~1 atm), the constant reduces to *+1*: $$F \;=\; C - P + 1$$ --- **(b) Lever Rule Application on Pb–Sn Alloy (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Identify given values:** $$C_0 = 60 \, \text{wt\% Sn}, \quad C_\beta = 17 \, \text{wt\% Sn}, \quad C_L = 62.5 \, \text{wt\% Sn}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Lever Rule for weight fraction of β (solid) phase:** $$W_\beta \;=\; \dfrac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\beta - C_L} \;=\; \dfrac{60 - 62.5}{17 - 62.5} \;=\; \dfrac{-2.5}{-45.5} \;=\; 0.0549$$ $$\boxed{\,W_\beta \;\approx\; 5.49 \,\%\,}$$ **Step 3 — Weight fraction of liquid phase:** $$W_L \;=\; 1 - W_\beta \;=\; 1 - 0.0549 \;=\; 0.9451$$ $$\boxed{\,W_L \;\approx\; 94.51 \,\%\,}$$ **Step 4 — Verification of lever-rule internal consistency:** The arm opposite to β is $|C_0 - C_L| = 2.5$ and arm opposite to L is $|C_\beta - C_0| = 45.5$. Total tie-line = 48. $W_\beta = 2.5/48$? Wait — let me recompute using the correct denominator: $$W_\beta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_L}{C_\beta - C_L} = \dfrac{-2.5}{-45.5} \approx 0.0549$$ Cross-check: $W_L = (C_\beta - C_0)/(C_\beta - C_L) = (17-60)/(17-62.5) = (-43)/(-45.5) = 0.9451$ ✓ **Step 5 — Weight percentage of tin in the *solid phase* as a whole:** The question asks for the Sn content in the *solid phase*, which is simply $C_\beta$: $$\boxed{\,\text{Sn in solid } \beta = 17 \,\text{wt\% Sn}\,}$$ (Note: this is the equilibrium composition of the β phase as read from the phase boundary — it is *not* a lever-rule computation but a direct read-off.) > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** > * Many students confuse the **fraction of a phase** with the **composition of a phase**. They are entirely different quantities! $W_\beta$ is *how much* β phase is present (5.49 %), while $C_\beta$ is *what the composition of that phase is* (17 wt% Sn). > * Some students mistakenly use the *wrong arm* in the lever-rule formula. The arm length must correspond to the *opposite end* of the tie-line. A useful mnemonic: "**The lever arm opposite a phase is the segment of the tie-line on the other side of the fulcrum**." > * Always report fractions in the form the question asks — *decimal* (0.0549) or *percentage* (5.49 %). KTU typically expects the **percentage** form for full marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** What is the maximum number of phases that can co-exist in equilibrium for: * (i) A **pure component** system. * (ii) A **binary alloy** system at constant pressure. * (iii) A **ternary system** at variable temperature and pressure. Use the Gibbs Phase Rule in each case. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **brass (Cu–Zn) alloy** contains **35 wt% Zn** and **65 wt% Cu**. At a temperature of **900 °C**, the alloy lies in the **α + β'** two-phase region. The compositions of the two phases at 900 °C are: * $C_\alpha$ (Cu-rich α phase) = **32 wt% Zn** * $C_{\beta'}$ (Zn-rich β' phase) = **39 wt% Zn** Compute: * (i) The weight fraction of the α phase. * (ii) The weight fraction of the β' phase. * (iii) The total mass of the β' phase if the alloy sample weighs **2.5 kg**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B** **(a) Maximum Number of Co-existing Phases (7 Marks)** The maximum number of phases occurs when the degrees of freedom $F = 0$ (invariant equilibrium). From the Gibbs Phase Rule: $$F = C - P + 2 \;\Rightarrow\; P_{\max} = C + 2 \quad \text{(variable T, P)}$$ **Case (i) — Pure component ($C = 1$):** $$P_{\max} = 1 + 2 = 3$$ This is the famous **triple point** (e.g., water: ice, liquid, vapour at 0.01 °C, 0.006 atm). **Case (ii) — Binary alloy at constant pressure:** For condensed systems: $F = C - P + 1$. At invariant point $F = 0$: $$P_{\max} = C + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$ This is the **ternary invariant** (e.g., the **eutectic point** in a binary alloy, where liquid + α + β co-exist). **Case (iii) — Ternary system at variable T and P:** $$P_{\max} = C + 2 = 3 + 2 = 5$$ In a ternary system, up to **five phases** can co-exist at an invariant point (e.g., a quinary invariant in a ternary — this is a key insight in three-component ceramics and high-entropy alloys). **Summary Table:** | System | C | Constraint | $P_{\max}$ | Physical Example | |:------|:-:|:----------:|:----------:|:-----------------| | Pure element | 1 | T & P variable | **3** | Triple point of H₂O | | Binary alloy | 2 | P constant | **3** | Eutectic point of Pb-Sn | | Ternary | 3 | T & P variable | **5** | Five-phase invariant | --- **(b) Lever Rule on Brass (Cu-Zn) Alloy (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Identify values:** $$C_0 = 35, \quad C_\alpha = 32, \quad C_{\beta'} = 39 \quad \text{(all in wt\% Zn)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Lever Rule for the α phase:** $$W_\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{C_0 - C_{\beta'}}{C_\alpha - C_{\beta'}} \;=\; \dfrac{35 - 39}{32 - 39} \;=\; \dfrac{-4}{-7} \;=\; 0.5714$$ $$\boxed{\,W_\alpha \;\approx\; 57.14 \,\%\,}$$ **Step 3 — Weight fraction of the β' phase:** $$W_{\beta'} \;=\; 1 - W_\alpha \;=\; 1 - 0.5714 \;=\; 0.4286$$ $$\boxed{\,W_{\beta'} \;\approx\; 42.86 \,\%\,}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of the β' phase in a 2.5 kg sample:** $$m_{\beta'} \;=\; W_{\beta'} \times m_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.4286 \times 2.5 \;=\; 1.0715 \text{ kg}$$ $$\boxed{\,m_{\beta'} \;\approx\; 1.072 \text{ kg (or 1071.5 g)}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Cross-check:** * $W_\alpha + W_{\beta'} = 0.5714 + 0.4286 = 1.000$ ✓ * $m_\alpha + m_{\beta'} = 0.5714 \times 2.5 + 1.0715 = 1.4285 + 1.0715 = 2.500$ kg ✓ > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** > * **Most common mistake in Q3(b)(iii):** Students multiply the *composition* (39 wt%) by the total mass instead of the *phase fraction* (0.4286). The composition 39 wt% is a *concentration*; the phase fraction 0.4286 is a *fraction of the total mass*. Multiplying 39 % × 2.5 kg would yield the *mass of zinc in the β' phase*, not the *mass of the β' phase itself*. > * **Second common mistake:** Forgetting to subtract: $W_\alpha + W_{\beta'} = 1$ is a free correctness-check. Always perform it. > * **Third common mistake:** Reading the α and β compositions from the wrong side. The α phase is always the **Cu-rich** phase in brass (low Zn). The lever-arm formula uses **absolute** differences — sign cancels — but the student should still correctly identify which end is α and which is β. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Lever Rule & Gibbs Phase Rule** * **Lever Rule applies only in two-phase regions** of binary phase diagrams. * **Tie-line** is the horizontal isotherm connecting the two phase boundaries; it is the geometric basis of the Lever Rule. * **Lever Rule formula:** $W_\alpha = (C_0 - C_\beta)/(C_\alpha - C_\beta)$; always cross-check $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$. * **Mnemonic:** "**Opposite arm wins**" — the fraction of a phase is proportional to the length of the segment *opposite* to that phase on the tie-line. * **Gibbs Phase Rule (general):** $F = C - P + 2$ — use when **both T and P vary**. * **Gibbs Phase Rule (condensed):** $F = C - P + 1$ — use for **alloys at constant pressure** (this is the **default KTU form**). * **Number of components $C$:** $C = N - R$ (where $N$ = elements, $R$ = independent chemical reactions or stoichiometric constraints). * **Number of phases $P$:** Count distinct phases (e.g., α, β, liquid, gas) — polymorphic forms of the same phase count as one. * **Invariant point:** $F = 0$ → $P_{\max} = C + 2$ (general) or $C + 1$ (alloys). * **Triple point of water:** $C = 1$, $P = 3$ → $F = 0$ ✓ * **Eutectic point of binary alloy:** $C = 2$, $P = 3$ (α + β + L) → $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ ✓ * **Pure single-phase region:** $F = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1$ (only T can vary independently) — for condensed systems. * **F is always $\geq 0$**; if a calculation gives a negative $F$, the assumed combination of $C$ and $P$ is *physically impossible* — KTU examiners love this trick question. * **Phase fraction vs. Phase composition:** Phase fraction is a *quantity* (how much), phase composition is a *quality* (what is in it). Never mix them up. * **Always multiply decimal by 100** for percentage reporting in KTU answer sheets. * **Common KTU-PPT-style question:** "What is the maximum number of phases in a binary system at constant pressure?" → Answer: **3** (from $F = 0$). * **KTU favourite:** "Why is the constant *+1* and not *+2* in metallurgical problems?" → Answer: *Pressure is held constant at ~1 atm, removing one degree of freedom.*

topics.title: Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram with Microstructure and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram with Microstructure and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram (Fe-Fe₃C Phase Diagram):** A binary constitutional phase diagram that maps the equilibrium phases, phase boundaries, and microstructural constituents formed in iron–carbon alloys as a function of **temperature (°C)** on the ordinate (y-axis) and **weight percent of carbon (% C)** on the abscissa (x-axis), over the composition range **0% to 6.67% C**, under conditions of extremely slow cooling so that equilibrium is maintained.

It is a **meta-stable** diagram (the true equilibrium phase is graphite, but Fe₃C is the meta-stable cementite that engineers study). The diagram is read alongside **Gibbs Phase Rule**: $F = C - P + 1$ (for constant pressure, condensed systems), where $C = 2$ (Fe, C) and $P$ is the number of coexisting phases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Recipe of Steel" Analogy:**
> Think of the Fe–C diagram as a **"Master Recipe Chart"** for making steel, like a chef's mixing guide. Carbon is the *spice* added to pure iron (the *base ingredient*). The chart tells the chef:
> - At a given **spice level (carbon %)**, what is the **cooking temperature** the mixture must reach.
> - What is the **final texture (microstructure)** — soft ferrite, hard pearlite, or brittle cementite.
> - When to **quench (rapidly cool)** to lock in special structures like martensite.
>
> Just as too much spice ruins a dish, **>2% C** gives brittle cast iron instead of tough steel. The **727 °C horizontal line** is the famous "eutectoid line" — a magical temperature at which austenite decomposes into a beautiful lamellar mixture (pearlite) of ferrite and cementite.

## 1.3 Pure Iron & Allotropy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Allotropy of Iron:** The ability of pure iron to exist in more than one crystal structure depending on temperature. Three allotropes are critical to the diagram:

| Allotrope | Crystal Structure (BCC/FCC) | Stability Range | Lattice Parameter (nm) | Magnetic Behavior |
|-----------|------------------------------|------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **α-Ferrite (α-Fe)** | Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) | < **912 °C** | **0.2866** | Ferromagnetic up to **770 °C (Curie Temp)** |
| **γ-Austenite (γ-Fe)** | Face-Centered Cubic (FCC) | **912 °C – 1394 °C** | **0.3648** | Paramagnetic (Non-magnetic) |
| **δ-Ferrite (δ-Fe)** | Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) | **1394 °C – 1538 °C** | **0.2932** | Paramagnetic |

**Key Fact:** Pure iron melts at **1538 °C (1811 K)**.

## 1.4 Carbon Solubility — The Driving Force

> [!NOTE]
> Carbon is an **interstitial solute** in iron — its tiny atoms (radius ≈ 0.077 nm) fit *between* the larger iron atoms (radius ≈ 0.124 nm) at the octahedral or tetrahedral voids of the crystal lattice.

- **α-Ferrite (BCC):** Max solubility = **0.022 % C at 727 °C** (very low — BCC has fewer and smaller interstitial sites).
- **γ-Austenite (FCC):** Max solubility = **2.14 % C at 1147 °C** (very high — FCC has larger central octahedral voids).
- **δ-Ferrite (BCC):** Max solubility = **0.09 % C at 1493 °C** (low, similar to α-Fe).

This is **why FCC austenite can dissolve 100× more carbon than BCC ferrite** — a foundational fact that controls every reaction in the diagram.

## 1.5 The Three Principal Solid Phases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire diagram is built on **three single-phase fields**:

1. **Ferrite (α):** Soft, ductile, magnetic; BCC iron with very low carbon solubility. Essentially pure iron with trace carbon. **Tensile strength ≈ 280 MPa**, **Hardness ≈ 80 HB**.
2. **Austenite (γ):** Soft, ductile, non-magnetic, FCC iron with high carbon solubility. Stable only above 727 °C on the iron-rich side. **Tensile strength ≈ 400 MPa**.
3. **Cementite (Fe₃C):** Extremely hard, brittle intermetallic compound. **6.67 % C** (fixed composition). Orthorhombic crystal. **Hardness ≈ 950 HB**, **essentially zero ductility**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Critical Points, Lines, and Invariant Reactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot-Spot:** Examiners almost always ask for the coordinates and definitions of the **5 critical points (A-lines)** and **3 invariant reactions**. Memorize them.

### 2.1.1 The Five Critical "A" Points

| Point | Temperature (°C) | Carbon (%) | Physical Meaning |
|-------|------------------|------------|------------------|
| **A₁** (Eutectoid temp) | **727** | 0.77 | Lowest temp at which austenite is stable on heating |
| **A₂** (Curie / Magnetic) | **770** | ~0 | Loss of ferromagnetism of α-Fe (no phase change) |
| **A₃** (Upper critical) | varies (912 pure Fe) | varies | γ → α + γ boundary on cooling |
| **A_cₘ** (A-₄₃₇) | varies | varies | γ → γ + Fe₃C boundary on cooling (hypereutectoid side) |
| **A₄** (δ-transus) | **1394** | 0.10 | δ → γ + δ on cooling |

### 2.1.2 The Three Invariant Reactions

> [!NOTE]
> **Invariant Reaction:** A reaction occurring at a fixed temperature where **three phases coexist** in equilibrium. By Gibbs Phase Rule, $F = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$, meaning temperature and composition are *fixed*.

**Reaction 1 — Peritectic (at 1493 °C, 0.16 % C):**

$$\delta\text{-Fe} + L \;\xrightarrow[\text{Cooling}]{\text{At }1493\,°C}\; \gamma\text{-Austenite}$$

> A small amount of liquid iron reacts with δ-ferrite to form austenite. Industrially less important (left edge of diagram).

**Reaction 2 — Eutectic (at 1147 °C, 4.3 % C):**

$$L_{(4.3\%C)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{Cooling}]{\text{At }1147\,°C}\; \gamma\text{-Austenite}_{(2.14\%C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$$

> Liquid solidifies into a fine, intimate mixture of austenite and cementite called **Ledeburite**. Governs the structure of **cast irons**.

**Reaction 3 — Eutectoid (at 727 °C, 0.77 % C):** ⭐ THE MOST IMPORTANT REACTION IN STEEL

$$\gamma\text{-Austenite}_{(0.77\%C)} \;\xrightarrow[\text{Cooling}]{\text{At }727\,°C}\; \alpha\text{-Ferrite}_{(0.022\%C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$$

> Austenite decomposes into alternating lamellae of soft ferrite and hard cementite — the microstructure **Pearlite** ("Mother of Pearl" appearance under microscope).

### 2.1.3 Key Composition Classifications (KTU Definition Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classifying Iron–Carbon Alloys:**

| Carbon Range | Class Name | Industrial Use |
|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| **0 – 0.022 %** | Pure iron / Technical iron | Magnetic cores |
| **0.022 – 0.77 %** | **Hypoeutectoid steel** | Structural steel, rails, beams |
| **0.77 % (exact)** | **Eutectoid steel** | Razor blades, springs, piano wire |
| **0.77 – 2.14 %** | **Hypereutectoid steel** | Cutting tools, chisels, dies |
| **2.14 – 4.3 %** | **Eutectic / Hypoeutectic white cast iron** | Wear plates, crusher jaws |
| **4.3 % (exact)** | **Eutectic white cast iron** | (rarely pure) |
| **4.3 – 6.67 %** | **Hypereutectic white cast iron** | Linings, pump housings |

## 2.2 Phase vs. Microconstituent — A Subtle Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "phases" with "microconstituents." A **phase** is a physically and chemically distinct, homogeneous region (e.g., ferrite, austenite, cementite). A **microconstituent** is a *microscopic feature* observed under the microscope, which may contain one or more phases (e.g., pearlite contains both ferrite + cementite).

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Phase** | Uniform in composition, crystal structure, properties |
| **Microconstituent** | Distinct microstructural entity seen under microscope (may be a single phase or a mixture) |

## 2.3 The Levers & Tie-Lines — Applying Gibbs Phase Rule

For any alloy at any temperature on the diagram:
- **Pick a point (T, %C).**
- If inside a **single-phase field** → that phase exists with composition = alloy composition.
- If inside a **two-phase field** → draw a **tie-line (isotherm)**; the two end intersections give the compositions of the two phases; the **Lever Rule** gives their relative amounts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LEVER RULE:** For a binary alloy at temperature T inside a two-phase region, the fraction of each phase is proportional to the *opposite* arm of the tie-line.

$$W_{\alpha} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - C_{\beta}}{C_{\alpha} - C_{\beta}} \qquad W_{\beta} \;=\; \frac{C_{\alpha} - C_0}{C_{\alpha} - C_{\beta}}$$

Where:
- $C_0$ = overall alloy carbon content (%)
- $C_\alpha$ = carbon % in the $\alpha$-phase (left tie-line end)
- $C_\beta$ = carbon % in the $\beta$-phase (right tie-line end)
- $W_\alpha + W_\beta = 1$

**Mass Fraction of Total Cementite (Fe₃C) in a Hypoeutectoid Steel (using proeutectoid α and pearlite):**

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} \;\times\; 100\%$$

**Mass Fraction of Proeutectoid Ferrite in Hypoeutectoid Steel:**

$$W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; \frac{0.77 - C_0}{0.77 - 0.022} \;\times\; 100\%$$

**Mass Fraction of Pearlite in Hypoeutectoid Steel:**

$$W_{\text{pearlite}} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.77 - 0.022} \;\times\; 100\% \;=\; 1 - W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}}$$

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| **Lever Rule (any 2-phase)** | $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | dimensionless | Sum of W's = 1 |
| **% Proeutectoid Ferrite** | $\dfrac{0.77 - C_0}{0.748}$ | % | Hypoeutectoid steels |
| **% Proeutectoid Cementite** | $\dfrac{C_0 - 0.77}{5.90}$ | % | Hypereutectoid steels |
| **% Pearlite** | $100 - W_{\text{proeutectoid}}$ | % | For hypo/hyper steels |
| **% Eutectic (Ledeburite)** | $\dfrac{4.3 - C_0}{4.3 - 2.14}$ | % | Hypoeutectic cast iron |
| **% Proeutectic Cementite** | $\dfrac{C_0 - 4.3}{2.36}$ | % | Hypereutectic cast iron |
| **Gibbs Phase Rule** | $F = C - P + 1$ | — | Condensed system (P = const) |
| **Density of Fe₃C** | 7.66 | g/cm³ | Used in theoretical calc. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Treatment Selection:** Engineers use the diagram to choose austenitizing temperatures (just above A₃ or A₁) for normalizing, annealing, and hardening.
- **Alloy Design:** A 0.4% C hypoeutectoid steel is chosen for gears (tough + wear-resistant), while a 1.0% C hypereutectoid steel is chosen for razor blades (hard edge).
- **Welding & Casting:** The diagram predicts the risk of retained austenite or brittle ledeburite formation in weld/cast regions.
- **Failure Analysis:** Interpreting cracked components often traces back to a phase that formed unexpectedly — e.g., brittle cementite networks in overheated hypereutectoid steel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Cooling of a Hypoeutectoid Steel (0.4 % C) — Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU long-answer derivation** (frequently 7–14 marks). We trace what happens as a 0.4 % C steel is slowly cooled from the molten state to room temperature.

**Given:** Steel with $C_0 = 0.40\,\%\text{C}$, cooled very slowly from liquidus.

**Step 1 — Above Liquidus (> ~1500 °C):**
The alloy exists as a **100 % liquid** solution of C in Fe.

**Step 2 — Crossing Liquidus (~1480 °C):**
The first solid to form is **δ-ferrite** (BCC). Composition of this δ follows the **δ/(δ+L) solvus** on the left; liquid follows the **liquidus** curve.

**Step 3 — Crossing Peritectic Temperature (1493 °C, 0.16 % C):**
The remaining liquid + δ-ferrite react peritectically to form **γ-austenite**. For $C_0 = 0.40\,\%$ (right of peritectic), the alloy enters the **L + γ** field, then transitions to **100 % γ** once fully solidified.

**Step 4 — Single-Phase Austenite Field (~1400 °C → ~840 °C):**
The alloy is **100 % γ-austenite** with carbon content = 0.40 % throughout.

**Step 5 — Crossing the A₃ Line (γ / γ + α boundary) at ~840 °C:**
Now austenite becomes saturated with respect to ferrite. **Proeutectoid (pro = "before") α-ferrite** starts nucleating at the austenite grain boundaries.

The carbon in the rejected ferrite enriches the remaining austenite. As cooling continues, the **γ composition moves rightward along the A₃ line toward 0.77 % C** at 727 °C.

**Step 6 — Crossing the Eutectoid Temperature (727 °C):**
The remaining austenite (now 0.77 % C) undergoes the **eutectoid reaction**:

$$\gamma_{(0.77\%)} \;\longrightarrow\; \alpha_{(0.022\%)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{(6.67\%)}$$

This forms **Pearlite** — a lamellar mixture of α-ferrite and Fe₃C.

**Step 7 — Below 727 °C (down to 25 °C):**
The proeutectoid ferrite rejects a tiny amount of carbon (solubility drops from 0.022 % at 727 °C to ~0.008 % at RT), forming **tertiary cementite** (negligible, usually ignored).

**Final Microstructure at Room Temperature:**

$$\text{Proeutectoid Ferrite} \;+\; \text{Pearlite} \;(\alpha + \text{Fe}_3\text{C lamellae})$$

**Microstructural Sketch (verbal):**
- White **blocky patches** = proeutectoid ferrite (grain boundary network).
- Dark **"fingerprint" regions** = pearlite (alternating ferrite-cementite lamellae, appears dark at low magnification).

## 3.2 Mass-Fraction Calculation (Numerical Derivation) ⭐ HIGH-YIELD KTU PROBLEM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBLEM:** Calculate the mass fractions of **(a) proeutectoid ferrite**, **(b) pearlite**, and **(c) total cementite** in a slowly cooled steel containing **0.40 % C**.

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.40\,\%\text{C}$, Hypoeutectoid region (since 0.022 < 0.40 < 0.77).

**Step 1 — Identify the phases at room temperature:**
At RT, the alloy consists of **ferrite (α)** and **cementite (Fe₃C)** — only these are thermodynamically stable (Pearlite is a microconstituent, not a phase).

**Step 2 — Apply the Lever Rule on the RT tie-line:**
At room temperature, the two phases have:
- $\alpha$-ferrite composition: $C_\alpha = 0.022\,\%$
- Cementite composition: $C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = 6.67\,\%$

**Step 3 — Compute total ferrite fraction** (using the overall alloy 0.40 % C):

$$W_\alpha \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_0}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\alpha} \;=\; \frac{6.67 - 0.40}{6.67 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{6.27}{6.648}$$

$$W_\alpha \;=\; 0.9432 \;\approx\; \mathbf{94.3\,\%}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total cementite fraction:**

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} \;=\; 1 - W_\alpha \;=\; 1 - 0.9432 \;=\; \mathbf{0.0568 \;\approx\; 5.68\,\%}$$

*(Or directly: $W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} = \frac{0.40 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \frac{0.378}{6.648} = 0.0568$ ✓)*

**Step 5 — Compute Pearlite fraction** (using the 727 °C tie-line, where austenite at 0.77 % C is about to decompose):

Pearlite is the mixture formed at 727 °C; total ferrite = proeutectoid ferrite + ferrite *within* pearlite.

Using the tie-line at **727 °C** (just above eutectoid) on the ($\alpha + \gamma$) field, the total ferrite formed *up to* 727 °C = proeutectoid ferrite, because pearlite hasn't formed yet.

$$W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; \frac{0.77 - 0.40}{0.77 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.37}{0.748} \;=\; 0.4946$$

$$W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{49.5\,\%}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Pearlite fraction:**

$$W_{\text{pearlite}} \;=\; 1 - W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; 1 - 0.4946 \;=\; 0.5054$$

$$W_{\text{pearlite}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{50.5\,\%}$$

**Verification:** Proeutectoid ferrite (49.5 %) + Pearlite (50.5 %) = 100 % ✓

**Summary of Result for 0.40 % C Steel:**

| Constituent | Mass Fraction |
|-------------|---------------|
| **Proeutectoid Ferrite** | **49.5 %** |
| **Pearlite (α + Fe₃C)** | **50.5 %** |
| **Total Ferrite (overall)** | **94.3 %** |
| **Total Cementite (overall)** | **5.7 %** |

## 3.3 Worked Problem — Hypereutectoid Steel (1.2 % C)

**Given:** $C_0 = 1.20\,\%\text{C}$ (hypereutectoid, since 0.77 < 1.20 < 2.14).

**Step 1 — Phases at RT:** Ferrite (α) + Cementite (Fe₃C).
**Step 2 — Total Fe₃C fraction:**

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\alpha} \;=\; \frac{1.20 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{1.178}{6.648} \;=\; 0.1772$$

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{17.7\,\%}$$

**Step 3 — Total Ferrite fraction:**

$$W_\alpha \;=\; 1 - 0.1772 \;=\; \mathbf{82.3\,\%}$$

**Step 4 — Proeutectoid Cementite (forms along A_cₘ line, 727 °C → 900 °C):**
At 727 °C, the austenite is 0.77 % C. On cooling from above A_cₘ, proeutectoid cementite precipitates on austenite grain boundaries, leaving austenite at 0.77 % C.

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.77} \;=\; \frac{1.20 - 0.77}{5.90} \;=\; \frac{0.43}{5.90} \;=\; 0.0729$$

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{\text{pro}}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{7.3\,\%}$$

**Step 5 — Pearlite fraction:**

$$W_{\text{pearlite}} \;=\; 1 - W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; 1 - 0.0729 \;=\; \mathbf{92.7\,\%}$$

## 3.4 Worked Problem — Eutectoid Steel (0.77 % C) Pure Pearlite

For $C_0 = 0.77\,\%$ exactly:
- 100 % of the alloy transforms at 727 °C via the eutectoid reaction.
- **Microstructure = 100 % Pearlite** (no proeutectoid constituent).
- Within pearlite, ferrite fraction = $\dfrac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{5.90}{6.648} \approx 88.7\%$.
- Cementite fraction within pearlite = $\dfrac{0.77 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} = \dfrac{0.748}{6.648} \approx 11.3\%$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pearlite's Cementite is ~11.3 %** of the pearlite mass — a high-yield fact often asked in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Microstructure Summary Table

| Carbon (%) | Class | Phases Present | Microconstituents at RT |
|------------|-------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 0.00 | Pure Fe | α | 100 % Ferrite |
| 0.022 | (Boundary) | α | Ferrite + (trace) Fe₃C |
| 0.40 | Hypoeutectoid steel | α + Fe₃C | Proeutectoid Ferrite + Pearlite |
| 0.77 | Eutectoid steel | α + Fe₃C | 100 % Pearlite |
| 1.20 | Hypereutectoid steel | α + Fe₃C | Proeutectoid Cementite + Pearlite |
| 2.14 | (γ saturation limit) | γ + Fe₃C | (above 727 °C: γ + cementite) |
| 4.30 | Eutectic cast iron | — | Ledeburite (γ + Fe₃C) transforms to Pearlite + Fe₃C at 727 °C |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fe–C Phase Diagram Region Map

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a Mermaid block diagram showing the **major regions and invariant reactions** of the Iron–Carbon phase diagram. Use this as a mental map of the equilibrium diagram from the **iron-rich end (left)** through the **eutectoid region** to the **cast-iron end (right)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Liquid Region L above 1538C] --> A1[Cross Liquidus]
    A1 --> A2[Peritectic Reaction 1493C 0.16 percent C]
    A2 --> A3[Single Phase Austenite gamma field 912C to 1394C]
    A3 --> A4{Carbon percent decides path}
    A4 -- 0 to 0.022 --> B1[Cross A3 enter alpha plus gamma]
    A4 -- 0.022 to 0.77 --> B2[Hypoeutectoid steel path: alpha proeutectoid precipitates]
    A4 -- 0.77 exactly --> B3[Eutectoid reaction at 727C 100 percent Pearlite]
    A4 -- 0.77 to 2.14 --> B4[Hypereutectoid steel: proeutectoid Fe3C at grain boundaries]
    A4 -- 2.14 to 4.3 --> B5[Hypoeutectic white cast iron: proeutectic austenite]
    A4 -- 4.3 exactly --> B6[Eutectic reaction at 1147C forms Ledeburite]
    A4 -- 4.3 to 6.67 --> B7[Hypereutectic white cast iron: proeutectic cementite]
    B2 --> C1[727C eutectoid line: remaining gamma becomes Pearlite]
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Room temperature microstructure: alpha plus Fe3C]
    B1 --> D1
    B5 --> C2[1147C eutectic: Liquid becomes Ledeburite gamma plus Fe3C]
    B6 --> C2
    B7 --> C2
    C2 --> C3[727C: gamma within Ledeburite becomes Pearlite]
    C3 --> D2[Room temperature: Pearlite plus Fe3C plus Ledeburite]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Hypoeutectoid Steel Cooling Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: 0.4 percent C steel at 1500C Liquid] --> S2[Cool to 1480C first delta ferrite forms]
    S2 --> S3[Cool through 1493C peritectic: delta plus L to gamma]
    S3 --> S4[Pure austenite gamma field 0.4 percent C]
    S4 --> S5[Cross A3 line at 840C: alpha proeutectoid starts nucleating at grain boundaries]
    S5 --> S6[Continue cooling: gamma enriches in C moving along A3 toward 0.77]
    S6 --> S7[At 727C eutectoid: remaining gamma at 0.77 percent C decomposes to Pearlite]
    S7 --> S8[Final RT microstructure: Proeutectoid Ferrite plus Pearlite]
    S8 --> S9[Proeutectoid Ferrite = 49.5 percent]
    S8 --> S10[Pearlite = 50.5 percent]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Microstructure Comparison Chart (Optical Micrograph Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Hypoeutectoid Steel 0.4 percent C] --> M1[White blocky ferrite at grain boundaries plus dark pearlite colonies]
    N2[Eutectoid Steel 0.77 percent C] --> M2[100 percent Pearlite: all dark fingerprint lamellar structure]
    N3[Hypereutectoid Steel 1.2 percent C] --> M3[White Fe3C network at prior austenite grain boundaries plus dark pearlite inside]
    N4[Hypoeutectic White CI 3.0 percent C] --> M4[White dendrites of proeutectic austenite transformed to pearlite plus ledeburite matrix]
    N5[Hypereutectic White CI 5.0 percent C] --> M5[Long white plates of proeutectic cementite plus ledeburite matrix]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Three Invariant Reactions on the Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Peritectic 1493C: delta plus L to gamma"] --> P1[Horizontal line at 1493C from 0.10 to 0.50 percent C]
    R2["Eutectic 1147C: L to gamma plus Fe3C"] --> P2[Horizontal line at 1147C from 2.14 to 6.67 percent C]
    R3["Eutectoid 727C: gamma to alpha plus Fe3C"] --> P3[Horizontal line at 727C from 0.022 to 6.67 percent C]
    P3 --> PEARL[Pearlite formation governs all steels]
    P2 --> LEDE[Ledeburite formation governs all cast irons]
    P1 --> PERI[Peritectic governs pure iron to low carbon steel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in an exam:** When asked to draw or describe the Fe–C diagram, always label:
> 1. The **five critical A-temperatures** (A₁, A₂, A₃, A_cₘ, A₄).
> 2. The **three invariant horizontals** (peritectic 1493 °C, eutectic 1147 °C, eutectoid 727 °C).
> 3. The **key compositions** (0.022, 0.77, 2.14, 4.3, 6.67 % C).
> 4. The **phase fields** (α, γ, δ, L, Fe₃C, plus two-phase fields).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Eutectoid Reaction. Write the eutectoid reaction in the Fe–C system with temperature and composition.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **eutectoid reaction** is an invariant reaction in the solid state where a single solid phase decomposes on cooling into **two distinct solid phases** at a fixed temperature and composition.
>
> In the Fe–C system:
>
> $$\gamma\text{-Austenite}_{(0.77\%\,\text{C})} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Cooling at }727\,°C}\; \alpha\text{-Ferrite}_{(0.022\%\,\text{C})} \;+\; \text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{(6.67\%\,\text{C})}$$
>
> The product microstructure is called **Pearlite**. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for naming the product and conditions.)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Ferrite, Austenite, and Cementite on the basis of (a) crystal structure, (b) max carbon solubility, and (c) hardness.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — use a table):**
>
> | Property | Ferrite (α) | Austenite (γ) | Cementite (Fe₃C) |
> |----------|-------------|----------------|--------------------|
> | Crystal structure | **BCC** | **FCC** | **Orthorhombic** |
> | Max C solubility | **0.022 %** at 727 °C | **2.14 %** at 1147 °C | **6.67 %** (fixed stoichiometry) |
> | Hardness | ~80 HB (soft) | ~200 HB (moderate) | ~950 HB (very hard/brittle) |
>
> (1 mark each for the three distinguishing criteria.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Hypoeutectoid Steel Analysis** ⭐ Most Likely

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Draw the Iron–Carbon Equilibrium Diagram up to **6.67 % C** and label all critical points, lines, temperatures, and phase fields. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hypoeutectoid steel contains **0.35 % C**. Calculate the mass percentages of (i) proeutectoid ferrite, (ii) pearlite, and (iii) total cementite present at room temperature after slow cooling. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Diagram (7 marks):**

The student should draw a standard Fe–Fe₃C diagram with:
- Axes: Temperature (°C) vertical, %C horizontal (0 to 6.67).
- **Five critical points** labeled: A₁ (727 °C, 0.77 %C), A₂ (770 °C, ~0 %C), A₃ (912 °C, 0 %C), A_cₘ (1147 °C, 2.14 %C), A₄ (1394 °C, 0.10 %C).
- **Three invariant horizontals:** Peritectic at 1493 °C, Eutectic at 1147 °C, Eutectoid at 727 °C.
- **Phase fields labeled:** L, δ, δ+γ, γ, γ+L, α+γ, α, α+Fe₃C, γ+Fe₃C, Fe₃C, L+Fe₃C, etc.
- **Key compositions marked:** 0.022, 0.16, 0.77, 2.14, 4.30, 6.67 %C.

(Marking: 2 marks for correct shape, 2 marks for critical points, 1 mark for invariant reactions, 1 mark for phase fields, 1 mark for labeling.)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.35\,\%\text{C}$ (hypoeutectoid).

**(i) Proeutectoid Ferrite (using 727 °C tie-line on α + γ field):**

$$W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; \frac{0.77 - 0.35}{0.77 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.42}{0.748} \;=\; 0.5615$$

$$\boxed{W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;\approx\; 56.2\,\%}$$

**(ii) Pearlite:**

$$W_{\text{pearlite}} \;=\; 1 - W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; 1 - 0.5615 \;=\; 0.4385$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{pearlite}} \;\approx\; 43.8\,\%}$$

**(iii) Total Cementite (using room-temperature tie-line α + Fe₃C):**

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} - C_\alpha} \;=\; \frac{0.35 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{0.328}{6.648}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C}} \;\approx\; 4.93\,\%}$$

**Verification:** $W_{\alpha_{\text{pro}}} + W_{\text{pearlite}} = 56.2 + 43.8 = 100\,\%$ ✓

(Marking: 1 mark for identifying hypoeutectoid, 2 marks for proeutectoid ferrite with formula, 1 mark for pearlite calculation, 2 marks for total cementite with formula, 1 mark for correct numerical result.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Eutectic Cast Iron Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Eutectic reaction** in the Fe–C system. Discuss the formation and significance of **Ledeburite** in cast irons. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A white cast iron contains **3.0 % C**. Determine the **(i)** mass fraction of proeutectic austenite, **(ii)** mass fraction of ledeburite, and **(iii)** total cementite at room temperature after slow cooling. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Eutectic Reaction (7 marks):**

The **eutectic reaction** is an invariant reaction where, on cooling, a **single liquid phase** transforms into **two solid phases** simultaneously at a fixed temperature and composition.

In the Fe–C system at **1147 °C** and **4.30 % C**:

$$L_{(4.30\%\,C)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Cooling at }1147\,C}\; \gamma\text{-Austenite}_{(2.14\%\,C)} \;+\; \text{Fe}_3\text{C}$$

The product is called **Ledeburite** — a fine, intimate eutectic mixture of austenite and cementite.

**Formation of Ledeburite (3 marks):**
- Above 1147 °C: alloy exists as liquid (for $C_0$ between 2.14 and 6.67 %).
- At 1147 °C: liquid of eutectic composition (4.30 % C) solidifies.
- For $C_0 < 4.30\,\%$ (hypoeutectic), **proeutectic austenite** (dendritic) forms first as the liquid cools from liquidus to 1147 °C; remaining liquid (now 4.30 % C) forms ledeburite.
- For $C_0 > 4.30\,\%$ (hypereutectic), **proeutectic cementite** (long plates) forms first.
- At 727 °C: the austenite within ledeburite (2.14 % C → 0.77 % C) transforms to pearlite. So **transformed ledeburite = pearlite + cementite**.

**Significance (2 marks):**
- Ledeburite is responsible for the **hardness and brittleness** of white cast irons (HB 400–500).
- It forms a **continuous brittle matrix** in cast iron — gives excellent wear resistance but no ductility.
- Foundation for producing **malleable cast iron** (by long-time annealing of white CI to decompose Fe₃C).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $C_0 = 3.0\,\%\text{C}$ (hypoeutectic white CI, since 2.14 < 3.0 < 4.30).

**(i) Proeutectic Austenite (using 1147 °C tie-line, $\gamma$ + L field):**

$$W_{\gamma_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; \frac{4.30 - 3.0}{4.30 - 2.14} \;=\; \frac{1.30}{2.16} \;=\; 0.6019$$

$$\boxed{W_{\gamma_{\text{pro}}} \;\approx\; 60.2\,\%}$$

**(ii) Ledeburite (the eutectic mixture formed at 1147 °C):**

$$W_{\text{Ledeburite}} \;=\; 1 - W_{\gamma_{\text{pro}}} \;=\; 1 - 0.6019 \;=\; 0.3981$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{Ledeburite}} \;\approx\; 39.8\,\%}$$

**(iii) Total Cementite at Room Temperature:**

At RT, the alloy contains: proeutectic cementite (zero, since hypoeutectic) + cementite *within transformed ledeburite*.

Since ledeburite at 1147 °C consists of $\gamma$ (2.14 %C) + Fe₃C, and at 727 °C that γ becomes pearlite, the cementite in transformed ledeburite is calculated using the eutectic composition (4.30 %) on the overall RT tie-line of pearlite + Fe₃C. But for the *whole alloy* at RT, the phases are still just α + Fe₃C:

$$W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} \;=\; \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{6.67 - 0.022} \;=\; \frac{3.0 - 0.022}{6.648} \;=\; \frac{2.978}{6.648} \;=\; 0.4480$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{Fe}_3\text{C, total}} \;\approx\; 44.8\,\%}$$

(Marking: 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for conditions, 1 mark for ledeburite definition, 2 marks for formation sequence, 2 marks for significance; in part b: 1 mark for classification, 2 marks for austenite, 2 marks for ledeburite, 2 marks for total cementite.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "proeutectoid" with "proeutectic":** "Proeutectoid" applies to **steels** (solid → solid, e.g., α or Fe₃C formed before 727 °C in austenite). "Proeutectic" applies to **cast irons** (solid formed before 1147 °C in liquid). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting the two temperature tie-lines:** Many students apply the room-temperature tie-line for *all* constituents, getting wrong proeutectoid/pearlite fractions. You need the **727 °C tie-line** for proeutectoid ferrite/cementite, but the **RT tie-line** for total ferrite/total cementite.
> 3. **Mixing up phases and microconstituents:** Pearlite is **not a phase**; it is a microconstituent consisting of two phases (α + Fe₃C).
> 4. **Not converting 0.77 % to "eutectoid carbon content" properly:** The eutectoid composition is **0.77 % C** (not 0.8 or 0.76). Use exactly **0.77** for KTU exams.
> 5. **Wrong sign in Lever Rule:** Always double-check: $W_\alpha$ uses opposite arm — numerator is the *far* end minus $C_0$.
> 6. **Skipping units and significant digits:** Mark off 0.5 mark if you write "0.0568" instead of "**5.68 %**".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TOPIC RECAP — Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram**

- [x] The Fe–Fe₃C diagram is a **meta-stable** binary diagram from **0 to 6.67 % C** (max carbon in cementite).
- [x] Three solid phases: **α-ferrite (BCC)**, **γ-austenite (FCC)**, **Fe₃C cementite (orthorhombic)**.
- [x] Pure iron allotropes: **α (BCC) → γ (FCC) at 912 °C → δ (BCC) at 1394 °C → melts at 1538 °C**.
- [x] Carbon solubility: **0.022 %** in α-Fe (727 °C), **2.14 %** in γ-Fe (1147 °C), **0.09 %** in δ-Fe (1493 °C).
- [x] **Peritectic** at **1493 °C, 0.16 %C**: δ + L → γ.
- [x] **Eutectic** at **1147 °C, 4.30 %C**: L → γ + Fe₃C (forms **Ledeburite**).
- [x] **Eutectoid** at **727 °C, 0.77 %C**: γ → α + Fe₃C (forms **Pearlite**) ⭐.
- [x] **Pearlite** = lamellar (α + Fe₃C) — about **88.7 % ferrite + 11.3 % cementite** by mass.
- [x] **Steels**: 0.022 – 2.14 %C. **Cast irons**: 2.14 – 6.67 %C.
- [x] Hypoeutectoid steel (0.022–0.77 %C): **proeutectoid ferrite + pearlite**.
- [x] Eutectoid steel (0.77 %C): **100 % pearlite**.
- [x] Hypereutectoid steel (0.77–2.14 %C): **proeutectoid cementite + pearlite**.
- [x] Hypoeutectic white CI (2.14–4.30 %C): **proeutectic austenite (→ pearlite) + ledeburite**.
- [x] Eutectic white CI (4.30 %C): **100 % ledeburite**.
- [x] Hypereutectic white CI (4.30–6.67 %C): **proeutectic cementite + ledeburite**.
- [x] **A₁ = 727 °C** (eutectoid temp); **A₃ = upper critical** for hypoeutectoid; **A_cₘ = upper critical** for hypereutectoid; **A₄ = 1394 °C**; **A₂ = 770 °C** (Curie).
- [x] **Lever Rule** for mass fractions in two-phase regions; **RT tie-line** for total phases, **727 °C tie-line** for microconstituents.
- [x] Cementite (Fe₃C) is **hardest (~950 HB), most brittle**; ferrite is **softest (~80 HB), most ductile**; austenite is **intermediate, non-magnetic, ductile**.
- [x] **Gibbs Phase Rule** $F = C - P + 1$ — invariant reactions have $F = 0$ (T and compositions fixed).
- [x] Diagram is read with **slow cooling** (equilibrium); rapid cooling gives **non-equilibrium structures** like **martensite** (BCT, very hard, formed by quenching austenite).
- [x] **Industrial use of diagram:** designing heat treatments (annealing, normalizing, hardening), alloy selection, predicting weld/cast microstructures.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram with Microstructure and Properties**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Treatment - TTT and CCT Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Treatment - TTT and CCT Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat Treatment: Formal Definition

**Heat Treatment** is a controlled sequence of heating and cooling operations applied to a metal or alloy in the solid state to alter its internal microstructure and thereby achieve desired mechanical, physical, and chemical properties (such as hardness, tensile strength, ductility, toughness, and wear resistance) without changing the overall shape of the component.

Within the broad family of heat-treatment operations, two foundational graphical tools are prescribed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMET205, Module 4)** syllabus for predicting phase transformations in steels:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isothermal Transformation (TTT) Diagram:** A plot of the *time* required for the undercooled austenite to transform into specific products (pearlite, bainite, ferrite, cementite) as a function of *temperature*, when held isothermally (at a constant temperature) below the eutectoid temperature.
>
> **Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) Diagram:** A plot that depicts the *time* and *temperature* paths of transformation products when a steel is cooled *continuously* from the austenitising temperature at various cooling rates.

These diagrams are often collectively called **"S-Curves"** or **"C-Curves"** because of the characteristic "C"-shape traced by the transformation-start and transformation-finish loci.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have a tray of molten chocolate (representing **austenite at high temperature**, ~800 °C). You pour it onto a cold marble slab.

*   If you let the slab cool **very slowly** (a constant, low rate) — the chocolate has a long time to organise into coarse, soft crystals (this is like **coarse pearlite** in steel).
*   If you cool it **moderately fast** — you get fine, hard crystals (this is like **fine pearlite** or **bainite**).
*   If you dunk the tray into ice water (an extreme, very fast quench) — the atoms have *no time at all* to rearrange, and the chocolate freezes into an amorphous, glassy, super-hard slab (this is **martensite** in steel).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Analogy:** A TTT diagram answers the question: *"If I hold my steel at exactly 540 °C, how long until 50 % becomes pearlite?"* A CCT diagram answers: *"If I cool the steel from 800 °C down to room temperature at 50 °C per second, what microstructure will I end up with?"*

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Austenite (γ-Fe)** | High-temperature face-centred cubic (FCC) phase of iron; can dissolve up to **2.0 % C** at 1147 °C. | γ |
| **Eutectoid Temperature** | The temperature (727 °C for plain-carbon steel) at which austenite decomposes into ferrite + cementite. | A₁ |
| **Martensite** | A supersaturated, body-centred tetragonal (BCT) phase formed by diffusionless shear when austenite is rapidly quenched. | α' |
| **Pearlite** | A lamellar mixture of soft ferrite (α) and hard cementite (Fe₃C) formed by diffusion-controlled decomposition. | P |
| **Bainite** | A non-lamellar, feathery/needle-like mixture of ferrite and cementite, formed at temperatures between pearlite and martensite. | B |
| **Ms Temperature** | Temperature at which martensite *starts* forming on cooling. | Mₛ |
| **Mf Temperature** | Temperature at which martensite formation is *finished* (≈ 95–99 %). | M_f |
| **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** | The *minimum* cooling rate that just suppresses the nose of the TTT curve and produces 100 % martensite. | V_c |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "sketch" a TTT diagram in an examination, always label the **A₁ (727 °C)**, **Mₛ**, and **M_f** lines, plus the **upper critical (nose) temperature (~550 °C)**. Missing any of these markers is the #1 cause of losing 1–2 marks in Part A descriptive questions.

---

## 1.4 Why Are TTT and CCT Diagrams Important?

Engineers use these diagrams to:
1.  **Predict microstructure** before heat-treating a real component.
2.  **Select the correct quenching medium** (water, oil, brine, polymer, air) to achieve a target hardness.
3.  **Estimate hardness** using empirical correlations such as the **Grange-Baughman** chart.
4.  **Design heat-treatment cycles** (annealing, normalising, hardening, tempering, austempering, martempering).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Thermodynamic Driver

Every transformation in the TTT diagram is governed by two competing factors:

> **Driving Force (ΔG):** The free-energy difference between the *parent* austenite and the *product* phase. The greater the undercooling (ΔT = A₁ − T_holding), the larger the driving force.

> **Atomic Mobility (Diffusion):** The rate at which carbon and iron atoms can rearrange. Mobility is *high* near A₁ and *collapses* as temperature approaches room temperature.

The competition produces the characteristic **C-shape**:

*   **Near A₁:** Low driving force → slow transformation (curve is pushed to the *right*).
*   **At the "Nose" (~550 °C):** Best compromise of high driving force + still-acceptable diffusion → *fastest* transformation (curve bends to the *left*).
*   **Near Mₛ (~250 °C):** Diffusion becomes negligible, so a *new* diffusionless (martensitic) transformation takes over.

---

## 2.2 Construction of a TTT Diagram — Step-by-Step Logic

1.  **Austenitise** a small steel specimen at ~30–50 °C above A_c3 for a time sufficient to dissolve all carbides.
2.  **Rapid transfer** to a salt bath held at a *constant* sub-eutectoid temperature (e.g., 650 °C, 600 °C, 550 °C, 500 °C, 400 °C, 300 °C, 200 °C).
3.  **Hold isothermally** and interrupt the hold after predetermined times (quench to room temperature).
4.  **Measure** the fraction of transformation using metallography, hardness, dilatometry, or magnetic response.
5.  **Plot** the time to 1 % transformation (start), 50 % transformation, and 99 % transformation (finish) on a logarithmic time axis versus temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The TTT diagram assumes **isothermal** transformation, but **real heat treatment is continuous cooling** — that is why the **CCT diagram** exists. In CCT, the cooling curve *crosses* the transformation fields instead of intersecting them horizontally.

---

## 2.3 Interpretation of the Three C-Curve Regions

| Region | Temperature Range | Product | Mechanism | Morphology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Upper C (Pearlite Nose)** | 727 °C → ~550 °C | Coarse → Fine Pearlite | Diffusional | Lamellar α + Fe₃C |
| **Intermediate (Bainite Bay)** | ~550 °C → Mₛ (~250 °C) | Upper / Lower Bainite | Partially diffusional | Feathery / Acicular |
| **Lower Plateau (Martensite)** | Below Mₛ | Martensite (BCT) | Diffusionless shear | Lath / Plate |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **only** equations KTU examiners can directly pose numerical questions on for this topic. Memorise the variables and units, not the symbols.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta T = A_1 - T_{hold}$ | Undercooling below eutectoid temperature | °C or K |
| 2 | $V_c = \dfrac{A_1 - M_f}{t_{nose}}$ | Critical (minimum) cooling rate for 100 % martensite | °C/s |
| 3 | $t_{nose}$ | Time coordinate at the nose of the TTT curve (read from diagram) | seconds |
| 4 | $Y = 1 - \exp(-k t^{n})$ | **Avrami (Johnson-Mehl) equation** for fraction transformed | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\ln\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-Y}\right)\right] = \ln k + n \ln t$ | Linearised Avrami form for plotting | dimensionless |
| 6 | $HRC \approx f(\text{cooling rate at } 700\,°\text{C})$ | Empirical hardness–cooling-rate correlation | HRC |
| 7 | $T_{holding} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{T_m} + \dfrac{R}{\Delta H}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)}$ | Van't Hoff / thermodynamic driving-force relation (conceptual) | K |

> **Where:**
> * $Y$ = Volume fraction transformed (0 ≤ Y ≤ 1)
> * $k$ = Rate constant (temperature dependent)
> * $n$ = Avrami exponent (typically 1 to 4)
> * $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> * $T_m$ = Melting temperature (K)
> * $\Delta H$ = Latent heat of transformation (J·mol⁻¹)
> * $x$ = Atomic fraction of solute in austenite

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **Automotive gear manufacturing** — case-hardened gears are designed by referring to the CCT diagram to predict case/core hardness gradients.
*   **Aerospace alloy development** — TTT/CCT data feeds into **JMatPro** and **Thermo-Calc** simulations to certify new alloy compositions.
*   **Railway wheel and rail metallurgy** — pearlitic rails (UIC 700 grade) are controlled-cooled using CCT data to obtain a fine pearlite with hardness 360–400 BHN.
*   **Power-plant boiler tubes (T/P91, T/P92)** — creep-resistant steels require precise bainitic transformation windows to avoid retained austenite.

---

## 2.6 Effect of Alloying Elements on the TTT Diagram

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloying elements shift the TTT diagram to the right** (longer transformation times) — this is the entire metallurgical basis of **"hardenability."**

| Element | Effect on Nose | Effect on Mₛ | Practical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo** | Shifts right (slower transformation) | Lowers Mₛ | ↑ Hardenability, less brittle quench |
| **Co, Al** | Shifts left (faster transformation) | Raises Mₛ | ↓ Hardenability |
| **C (Carbon)** | Shifts right up to ~0.8 % C, then back | Strongly lowers Mₛ | Eutectoid (0.77 % C) is reference |
| **V, Nb, Ti** | Form stable carbides; very strong right-shift | Markedly lowers Mₛ | Used in HSLA / microalloyed steels |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Avrami (Johnson–Mehl) Equation for Transformation Kinetics

The Avrami equation relates the volume fraction of a transformed phase to the isothermal hold time. It is a **direct consequence of nucleation-and-growth theory** and is fundamental for KTU Part-B derivations.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions

1.  Random (homogeneous) nucleation throughout the parent phase.
2.  Constant nucleation rate per unit volume, $\dot{N}$ (nuclei/m³·s).
3.  Constant isotropic growth rate, $G$ (m/s).
4.  No preferred nucleation sites on grain boundaries (ideal case).

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation

The extended volume of transformed material, $V_{ex}$, in time $t$ inside a single grain of original volume $V_0$, considering nuclei that appeared at time $\tau$ and grew for $(t - \tau)$ is:

$$
V_{ex} = \int_{0}^{t} \dot{N} V_0 \cdot \dfrac{4\pi}{3} G^{3}(t-\tau)^{3} \, d\tau
$$

Integrating this expression with respect to $\tau$ from 0 to $t$:

$$
V_{ex} = \dot{N} V_0 \cdot \dfrac{4\pi}{3} G^{3} \int_{0}^{t} (t-\tau)^{3} \, d\tau
$$

Let $u = t - \tau$, then $du = -d\tau$. When $\tau = 0$, $u = t$; when $\tau = t$, $u = 0$. Substituting:

$$
V_{ex} = \dot{N} V_0 \cdot \dfrac{4\pi}{3} G^{3} \int_{t}^{0} u^{3} (-du) = \dot{N} V_0 \cdot \dfrac{4\pi}{3} G^{3} \int_{0}^{t} u^{3} \, du
$$

Evaluating the elementary integral $\int_{0}^{t} u^{3} \, du = \dfrac{t^{4}}{4}$:

$$
V_{ex} = \dot{N} V_0 \cdot \dfrac{4\pi}{3} G^{3} \cdot \dfrac{t^{4}}{4} = \dot{N} V_0 \cdot \dfrac{\pi G^{3} t^{4}}{3}
$$

Since *phantom* nuclei can form in already-transformed regions, the *actual* transformed volume $V$ is less than $V_{ex}$. Accounting statistically for this overlap:

$$
dV = (1 - Y) \, dV_{ex}
$$

where $Y = V / V_0$ is the actual volume fraction transformed. Substituting and integrating:

$$
\int_{0}^{Y} \dfrac{dY}{1 - Y} = \int_{0}^{V_{ex}/V_0} d\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ex}}{V_0}\right)
$$

The left side yields $-\ln(1 - Y)$, giving the famous **Avrami equation**:

$$
Y(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-k t^{n}\right)
$$

where $k = \dfrac{\pi \dot{N} G^{3}}{3}$ and $n = 4$ for the case of constant nucleation rate and 3-D growth.

### 3.1.3 Linearised Form for Experimental Verification

Taking the natural log twice:

$$
-\ln(1 - Y) = k t^{n}
$$

$$
\ln\!\left[-\ln(1 - Y)\right] = \ln k + n \ln t
$$

> A plot of $\ln\!\left[-\ln(1 - Y)\right]$ versus $\ln t$ yields a **straight line** with slope $n$ and intercept $\ln k$. This is the experimental procedure used in KTU laboratories to construct the 50 %-transformation line of a TTT diagram from dilatometric data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)

The Critical Cooling Rate is the slowest cooling rate that just bypasses the nose of the TTT diagram, producing 100 % martensite. It is **defined geometrically** as the slope of the line from the austenitising temperature $(T_A)$ to the point $(t_{nose}, M_f)$ on a temperature-vs-log(time) plot.

**Starting from geometry:**

A straight line drawn on a semi-log plot through two points $(t_1, T_1)$ and $(t_2, T_2)$ has slope:

$$
m = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\log_{10} t_1 - \log_{10} t_2}
$$

For the critical cooling line, take:

*   Point 1: austenitising start, $(t = 0, T = T_A)$. Note $\log_{10} 0$ is undefined, so we use a small reference time.
*   Point 2: nose of TTT, $(t = t_{nose}, T = T_{nose})$.

In the KTU textbook approximation, the **critical cooling rate** is taken as:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{T_A - M_f}{t_{nose} - t_0} \quad \text{°C/s}
$$

Since $t_0 \to 0$ on the log axis is taken as 1 second reference:

$$
V_c \approx \dfrac{A_1 - M_f}{t_{nose}} \quad \text{°C/s}
$$

> This is the **formula that appears most frequently in KTU Part-B numerical questions** (3–4 marks).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Modelled on KTU Dec 2023 style):**
A plain-carbon eutectoid steel (0.77 % C) is austenitised at 760 °C. From the TTT diagram, the nose is at 550 °C and $t_{nose}$ = **1 second**. $M_s$ = 240 °C, $M_f$ = 50 °C. Calculate the **minimum (critical) cooling rate** required to obtain 100 % martensite.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant temperature drop.

The cooling path must drop from the austenitising temperature down to just below $M_f$ before the austenite has a chance to decompose at the nose.

$$
\Delta T = T_A - M_f = 760 - 50 = 710\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the time budget.

The available time is bounded by the nose, i.e., $t = t_{nose}$ at the nose.

$$
\Delta t = t_{nose} - 0 = 1 - 0 = 1\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the critical cooling rate formula.

$$
V_c = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{710\,°\text{C}}{1\,\text{s}} = 710\,°\text{C/s}
$$

**Step 4:** Physical interpretation.

> A cooling rate **slower than 710 °C/s** would allow the cooling curve to touch the nose → some pearlite/bainite would form. A rate **faster than 710 °C/s** would bypass the nose entirely → 100 % martensite.

**Final Answer:** $V_c = 710\,°\text{C/s}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TTT vs CCT — Direct Comparison

| Parameter | TTT (Isothermal) | CCT (Continuous Cooling) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process condition** | Hold at constant $T$ | Cool at continuous rate |
| **Curve type** | Two C-curves (start, finish) | Single C with shifted fields |
| **Transformation products** | Pearlite + Bainite + Martensite | Pearlite + Bainite + Martensite (shifted to *lower* T and *longer* t) |
| **Nose position** | Reference (~550 °C, ~1 s) | **Shifted right and down** (typically ~3 s, ~500 °C) |
| **Critical cooling rate** | Read directly | **Always lower** than TTT estimate (because continuous cooling gives more "time budget" at each temperature) |
| **Experimental difficulty** | Moderate (salt-bath quench) | High (requires controlled cooling + dilatometry) |
| **Industrial use** | Lab reference | **Directly used in production** |
| **Cooling curve** | Horizontal isothermal line | Sloped line on log-time axis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mermaid cannot physically draw a temperature-vs-time curve with parabolic C-shapes. Following the **Diagram Fallback Rule** of the KTU engine, the following block diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of the transformation logic — a methodology that mirrors the cooling path a real TTT/CCT diagram encodes.

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Isothermal (TTT) Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[A1 AUSTENITISING 760 C] --> B1{QUENCH RAPIDLY}
    B1 --> C1[SALT BATH 650 C ISOTHERMAL HOLD]
    C1 --> D1[TIME 1 SAMPLE QUENCH]
    C1 --> D2[TIME 10 SAMPLE QUENCH]
    C1 --> D3[TIME 100 SAMPLE QUENCH]
    D1 --> E1[METALLOGRAPHY HARDNESS]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[PLOT START 50 FINISH CURVES]
    F1 --> G1[TTT S CURVE RESULT]
```

*   `A1` — Heating stage above the upper critical temperature.
*   `B1` — Decision node: rapid quench to bypass proeutectoid region.
*   `C1` — Isothermal holding at a chosen sub-eutectoid temperature.
*   `D1`, `D2`, `D3` — Interruption of hold at progressively longer times.
*   `E1` — Quantitative measurement of fraction transformed.
*   `F1` → `G1` — The TTT diagram is the *output* of this experimental sequence.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Continuous Cooling (CCT) Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[AUSTENITISING 800 C] --> Q1{SELECT COOLING RATE}
    Q1 --> R1[FAST QUENCH WATER]
    Q1 --> R2[MEDIUM QUENCH OIL]
    Q1 --> R3[SLOW COOL AIR]
    R1 --> S1[COOLING CURVE BYPASSES NOSE]
    R2 --> S2[COOLING CURVE TANGENT TO NOSE]
    R3 --> S3[COOLING CURVE CROSSES PEARLITE FIELD]
    S1 --> T1[100 PERCENT MARTENSITE]
    S2 --> T2[MARTENSITE PLUS FINE PEARLITE]
    S3 --> T3[COARSE PEARLITE PLUS FERRITE]
    T1 --> U1[PLOT COOLING CURVES ON TTT GRAPH]
    T2 --> U1
    T3 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[CCT S CURVE WITH COOLING PATH OVERLAY]
```

*   `P1` — Austenitising stage.
*   `Q1` — Branching on the cooling medium.
*   `R1`, `R2`, `R3` — Three representative cooling media.
*   `S1`, `S2`, `S3` — Three distinct geometric outcomes on the diagram.
*   `T1`, `T2`, `T3` — Resulting microstructures.
*   `U1` → `V1` — The CCT diagram is the *superposition* of cooling curves onto the transformation fields.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Microstructure Selector — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[START QUENCH FROM AUSTENITE] --> X2{TEMPERATURE OF HOLD}
    X2 -->|ABOVE 550 C| Y1[PEARLITE FORMS]
    X2 -->|550 C TO MS| Y2[BAINITE FORMS]
    X2 -->|BELOW MS| Y3[MARTENSITE FORMS]
    Y1 --> Y1A[COARSE IF SLOW]
    Y1 --> Y1B[FINE IF FAST]
    Y2 --> Y2A[UPPER BAINITE 400 TO 550]
    Y2 --> Y2B[LOWER BAINITE 250 TO 400]
    Y3 --> Y3A[NEED TEMPERING]
```

This decision tree is the **mental model** every KTU student should internalise before walking into the exam hall. A cooling path that:
*   Spends *more time* in the upper region → **pearlite**.
*   Crosses the middle region → **bainite**.
*   Bypasses both → **martensite**.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hardenability Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[LOW ALLOY STEEL] --> H2[NOSE AT 1 S]
    H3[HIGH ALLOY STEEL] --> H4[NOSE AT 100 S]
    H2 --> H5[CRITICAL COOLING RATE HIGH]
    H4 --> H6[CRITICAL COOLING RATE LOW]
    H5 --> H7[WATER QUENCH ONLY]
    H6 --> H8[OIL OR AIR QUENCH SUFFICES]
```

*   **Key Insight:** Alloying moves the nose to the right → reduces the required cooling rate → improves *hardenability*. (Note: hardenability ≠ hardness; a low-alloy steel can be equally hard, but only in thin sections.)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Modelled on `KTU University Exam – Dec 2023`)

**Q1.** What is a **TTT diagram**? Why is it also called an **"S-curve"** or **"C-curve"**? Mention *any two* characteristic temperatures marked on it.

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword scoring):**

A TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) diagram is a graphical plot of the time required for the isothermal decomposition of undercooled austenite into various products as a function of temperature. **(1 mark)**

It is called an S-curve or C-curve because the start and finish transformation lines take the shape of the alphabet letter "C" (or two stacked "S" shapes) on a temperature versus logarithmic-time plot. **(1 mark)**

Two characteristic temperatures:
1.  **A₁ = 727 °C** (eutectoid temperature — upper boundary).
2.  **Mₛ** = martensite-start temperature (lower boundary, ≈ 240 °C for eutectoid steel). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 (Modelled on `KTU University Exam – May 2024`)

**Q2.** Differentiate between a **TTT diagram** and a **CCT diagram**. State the principal reason why the CCT diagram is preferred for industrial heat treatment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TTT | CCT |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling type | Isothermal hold | Continuous cooling |
| Transformation nose | Reference position | Shifted right and down |
| Practical use | Academic / lab | Industrial heat treatment |

**(1 mark each for any two clear points above = 2 marks**

**Principal reason:** In real heat treatment, components are not held at a single temperature — they are cooled continuously through a range of temperatures. Hence, the **CCT diagram directly predicts the microstructure that will result from a chosen cooling rate (e.g., water, oil, air quench)**, which is exactly the input an industrial heat treater controls. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks on TTT/CCT questions because they:
> 1.  **Forget to label $A_1$, $M_s$, $M_f$** on a sketched diagram.
> 2.  **Confuse hardenability with hardness** in concept questions.
> 3.  **Use linear time axis** instead of the correct *logarithmic* time axis (this is a syllabus-mandated convention).
> 4.  **Mix up bainite and pearlite temperature ranges.**
> 5.  In numerical questions, **fail to convert minutes to seconds** when applying the critical cooling rate formula.
> Memorise the relationship: $\text{1 minute} = 60\,\text{s}$ and $\log_{10}(60) \approx 1.78$.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks (Modelled on `KTU University Exam – May 2023`)

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, describe the **TTT diagram of a eutectoid plain-carbon steel**. Explain the **three distinct transformation regions** and the **structural products** formed in each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):**

*(Student is expected to draw a temperature–log(time) graph with the C-curve, $A_1 = 727\,°\text{C}$, $M_s \approx 240\,°\text{C}$, $M_f \approx 50\,°\text{C}$, the upper C representing pearlite, the lower bay for bainite, and the lower plateau for martensite.)*

**Three transformation regions (5 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 2):**

1.  **Pearlite Region (727 °C → ~550 °C, the upper C):** Austenite decomposes by a *diffusional* mechanism into alternate lamellae of soft ferrite ($\alpha$) and hard cementite ($Fe_3C$). Coarse pearlite forms near 727 °C, fine pearlite near 550 °C. **(1.5 marks)**

2.  **Bainite Region (~550 °C → M_s, the intermediate bay):** Transformation is *partially* diffusional. The microstructure is a feathery or needle-like (acicular) mixture of ferrite and dispersed cementite. *Upper bainite* (feathery, formed 400–550 °C) and *lower bainite* (acicular, formed 250–400 °C) are distinguished. **(1.5 marks)**

3.  **Martensite Region (Below M_s):** Diffusion is suppressed; austenite transforms to martensite by a *diffusionless shear* mechanism. The product is a supersaturated, body-centred tetragonal (BCT) phase that is extremely hard and brittle. **Tempering is required** to relieve stresses. **(2 marks)**

---

**Q.A (b)** A eutectoid steel is austenitised at 760 °C. From the TTT diagram, the **nose of the curve is at 550 °C and $t_{nose} = 1\,\text{s}$**. The $M_s$ temperature is 240 °C and $M_f$ is 50 °C.

*   **(i)** Calculate the **critical cooling rate (CCR)** required to obtain 100 % martensite. **(3 marks)**
*   **(ii)** If the steel is cooled at exactly this critical rate and then **tempered at 400 °C for 1 hour**, list the **microstructural changes** that occur during tempering. **(4 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Critical cooling rate calculation (3 marks):**

The critical cooling rate is the rate of cooling that takes the steel from the austenitising temperature to just below $M_f$ in time $t_{nose}$:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{A_1 - M_f}{t_{nose}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_c = \dfrac{760\,°\text{C} - 50\,°\text{C}}{1\,\text{s}} = 710\,°\text{C/s}
$$

> A cooling rate *greater* than 710 °C/s is required to **fully bypass the nose** of the TTT diagram. **(3 marks — formula 1 mark, substitution 1 mark, final 1 mark.)**

**(ii) Microstructural changes during tempering at 400 °C (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1.  **Stage 1 (≤ 250 °C):** Precipitation of *epsilon carbide* ($\varepsilon$-Fe₂.₄C) from martensite — reduces some brittleness.
2.  **Stage 2 (250–400 °C):** Decomposition of **retained austenite** into bainite/ferrite + cementite. *(This is the dominant stage at 400 °C.)*
3.  **Stage 3 (400–500 °C):** Cementite ($\text{Fe}_3\text{C}$) precipitation and growth, and **recovery of ferrite** — marked reduction in hardness, increase in toughness.
4.  **Net result at 400 °C / 1 h:** The microstructure becomes **tempered martensite (sorbite)** — a fine, dispersed carbide-ferrite aggregate that is **hard yet tough**, ideal for engineering components like gears, shafts, and axles.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice — `KTU University Exam – Dec 2022`)

**Q.B (a)** Draw and explain the **CCT diagram** of a plain-carbon eutectoid steel. Compare it with the TTT diagram and explain why the **C-curve of the CCT diagram is shifted to the right and downwards** compared to the TTT diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):** Temperature (vertical) vs log-time (horizontal); single C with pearlite nose shifted right; bainite region beneath; $M_s$, $M_f$ lines at the bottom; **cooling curves** of water/oil/air superimposed.

**Explanation (3 marks):**
The CCT diagram is constructed by cooling austenite at *continuous* rates and recording the start and finish of transformation. The transformation products and their temperature ranges are:
*   **Pearlite** at high temperatures (just below A₁).
*   **Bainite** in the intermediate range.
*   **Martensite** below the $M_s$ line.

Cooling curves of different rates are superimposed on the diagram; the *intersection* of each curve with the C determines the final microstructure.

**Why CCT is shifted right and down (2 marks):**
Under continuous cooling, the time at *each* temperature is finite but cumulative. By the time the temperature reaches the nose, austenite has already had time to begin transforming at *higher* temperatures. Hence, the *effective* transformation-start is delayed to *longer times* and *lower temperatures* than in the isothermal case. The TTT diagram therefore *over-estimates* the cooling rate required for 100 % martensite.

---

**Q.B (b)** A medium-carbon steel (0.4 % C) component of **50 mm diameter** is to be **oil-quenched** to obtain a **hardness of 55 HRC at the core**.

*   **(i)** Define **hardenability** and state **two factors** that affect it. **(3 marks)**
*   **(ii)** Using the **Grossmann end-quench test concept**, explain how the **ideal critical diameter ($D_I$)** is determined for this steel. Why is the **ideal critical diameter larger than the actual critical diameter**? **(4 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hardenability (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Hardenability is the *ease* with which a steel can be hardened to a given depth below the surface by quenching. It is a property of the *steel composition* and is **not the same as hardness** — a low-hardenability steel can be made very hard in thin sections, but a high-hardenability steel can be made hard in thick sections.

**Two factors (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1.  **Alloy content** — additions of Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni shift the TTT nose to the right, increasing hardenability.
2.  **Austenite grain size** — coarser grains have fewer grain-boundary nucleation sites, delaying pearlite formation and increasing hardenability.
3.  *(Bonus: Carbon content up to 0.8 % increases hardenability.)*

**(ii) Grossmann End-Quench Test and $D_I$ (4 marks):**

The **Grossmann end-quench test (Jominy end-quench)** is the standard experimental method. A cylindrical specimen (25 mm dia × 100 mm long) is austenitised and then quenched *only at one end* by a column of water. This produces a continuously *decreasing* cooling rate along the length. **(1 mark)**

After quenching, the specimen is ground flat and hardness is measured at intervals from the quenched end. The hardness is plotted versus distance (Jominy distance, in 1/16 inch units). **(1 mark)**

The **ideal critical diameter $D_I$** is the *largest* bar diameter that would yield **50 % martensite at the centre** under an *ideal* (infinitely severe) quench. It is read off the Jominy curve and converted to a bar diameter using a published **Grossmann chart**. **(1 mark)**

**Why $D_I > D_{actual}$:** Real quenching media (water, oil, polymer) cannot extract heat as fast as the "ideal quench" assumed by Grossmann. Heat extraction is limited by:
*   The Leidenfrost effect (vapour blanket formation).
*   The thermal conductivity of the steel.
*   The temperature and agitation of the quenchant.

Therefore, in practice, the *actual* bar diameter that achieves 50 % martensite ($D_{actual}$) is **always smaller** than $D_I$. The ratio $D_{actual} / D_I$ is called the **severity of quench (H-value)**, and it is **largest for brine (H ≈ 2.0)**, intermediate for water (H ≈ 1.0), and smallest for oil (H ≈ 0.3). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it on your study wall — it covers ~80 % of what is asked in KTU Module 4 viva + Part A.**

### Conceptual Foundation
*   TTT = **isothermal** hold, time is log scale, C-curve.
*   CCT = **continuous** cooling, cooling curves superimposed.
*   The C-curve exists because of the **trade-off** between **driving force** and **atomic mobility**.

### Three Regions
*   **Upper C:** Pearlite (coarse at top, fine at nose).
*   **Intermediate Bay:** Bainite (upper = feathery; lower = acicular).
*   **Below $M_s$:** Martensite (BCT, brittle, must temper).

### Critical Temperatures (memorise)
*   $A_1 = 727\,°\text{C}$ (eutectoid)
*   $M_s \approx 240\,°\text{C}$ (eutectoid)
*   $M_f \approx 50\,°\text{C}$ (eutectoid)

### Key Formula
$$
V_c = \dfrac{A_1 - M_f}{t_{nose}} \quad \text{°C/s}
$$

### Avrami Equation
$$
Y = 1 - \exp(-k t^{n}), \quad \ln\!\left[-\ln(1 - Y)\right] = \ln k + n \ln t
$$

### Effects to Memorise
*   **Mn, Ni, Cr, Mo** → shift nose **right** → ↑ hardenability.
*   **Coarse austenite grains** → ↑ hardenability.
*   **Faster quench** → ↑ martensite content → ↑ hardness, ↓ toughness.

### Industrial Heat Treatments Mapped to TTT/CCT
| Treatment | TTT/CCT Path |
|---|---|
| **Full Annealing** | Slow cool → coarse pearlite (soft) |
| **Normalising** | Air cool → fine pearlite (moderate hardness) |
| **Quench Hardening** | Water/oil → martensite (hard + brittle) |
| **Tempering** | Re-heat below $A_1$ → tempered martensite / sorbite |
| **Austempering** | Hold in bainite bay → bainitic structure (no tempering needed) |
| **Martempering** | Quench to just above $M_s$, hold, then air cool → minimised distortion |

### Common Exam Buzzwords
*   "Hardenability" ≠ "Hardness" — *the most common viva trap.*
*   "TTT stands for Time-Temperature-Transformation" — never say "Time-Temp-Transformation-Temperature."
*   "CCT curves are shifted to the right and down" — *this is the single most asked conceptual question.*

### Last-Minute Memory Aid
> **Mnemonic — "PMB"** for the three transformation products from top to bottom on the TTT diagram:
> **P**earlite (top), **M**artensite (bottom, with **B**ainite in between).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
