# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCMET205 (Material Science and Engineering) – Module 4**, the topic **"Applications"** refers to the systematic mapping of **engineering materials** (ferrous alloys, non-ferrous alloys, heat-treated steels, cast irons, and advanced composites) to specific **functional, structural, and tribological service conditions** based on their mechanical, thermal, chemical, and electrical properties.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, this topic integrates:
- **Material selection methodology** (Ashby maps / performance indices)
- **Service-environment compatibility** (corrosion, fatigue, creep, wear)
- **Industrial deployment standards** (IS, ASTM, AISI, DIN designations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Application of engineering materials involves the judicious selection of a material based on required mechanical properties, manufacturing feasibility, economic viability, and service-life expectancy in a defined engineering environment."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chefs selecting knives** for a professional kitchen:

- A **chef's knife (High Carbon Steel – T8, T10)** → Used for precision slicing because it is **hard and retains a sharp edge** (high hardness, poor corrosion resistance).
- A **bread knife (Stainless Steel – AISI 304)** → Used in humid kitchen conditions because it **resists rust** (corrosion resistance primary).
- A **fish fillet knife (Martensitic SS – AISI 420)** → Used for flexibility and hygiene (**toughness + corrosion resistance**).

Similarly, in engineering, **the application defines the material, not the reverse**. A turbine blade demands creep resistance (Ni-superalloys), while a structural beam demands weldability and toughness (low-carbon structural steel).

---

## 1.3 Core Classification of Engineering Material Applications

| **S.No** | **Functional Demand** | **Material Family** | **Typical Application** |
|:--------:|:----------------------|:--------------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | High Strength-to-Weight | Al-alloys (2024, 7075) | Aircraft fuselage, automotive panels |
| 2 | High-Temperature Strength | Ni-superalloys (Inconel 718) | Gas turbine blades, rocket nozzles |
| 3 | Corrosion Resistance | Austenitic SS (304, 316) | Chemical reactors, marine hardware |
| 4 | Wear Resistance | Tool Steels (D2, H13) | Dies, cutting tools, molds |
| 5 | Electrical Conductivity | Cu, Al (pure grades) | Power cables, busbars, windings |
| 6 | Magnetic Properties | Si-steel, Ferritic SS | Transformer cores, motor laminations |
| 7 | Spring Action | Spring Steels (AISI 1095, 5160) | Leaf springs, helical springs |
| 8 | Biocompatibility | Ti-alloys (Ti-6Al-4V) | Orthopedic implants, dental fixtures |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Ashby Material Selection Chart** plots properties like **Young's Modulus (E)** vs **Density (ρ)** on log-log axes. Materials lying in the **upper-left region** (high E, low ρ) are preferred for **stiff, lightweight structures** (e.g., CFRP, Mg-alloys, Al-alloys).

---

## 1.4 Why "Applications" is a High-Yield KTU Module

1. **Frequently asked as 14-mark Part-B questions** (e.g., *"Discuss the applications of any four non-ferrous alloys in modern engineering"*).
2. Tests **CO4 (Module 4 outcomes)** – Selection of materials for engineering applications.
3. Requires **interlinking of multiple concepts**: composition → microstructure → property → application.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU valuation, the examiner awards full marks **only if the application is justified by the property** (e.g., *"Inconel 718 is used in turbine blades because it retains >80% of its room-temperature strength at 700 °C due to γ' precipitation hardening"*).
> Writing *"used in turbine blades because it is strong"* → **loses 3–4 marks**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Material Application

Material application is governed by **four intersecting constraints**:

1. **Functional Performance** → Mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical properties.
2. **Manufacturability** → Castability, weldability, machinability, formability.
3. **Economic Constraint** → Cost per kg, availability, recyclability.
4. **Service Environment** → Temperature range, corrosive medium, loading cycle.

### 2.1.1 The Material Property–Application Map

$$\boxed{\text{Performance Index} = \frac{P_{\text{required}}}{\rho^{a} \cdot C^{b}}}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{required}}$ = property demanded by application
- $\rho$ = density
- $C$ = cost per unit mass
- $a, b$ = weighting exponents (chosen by designer)

> **Ashby's Method** maximizes this performance index to select the optimal material from a pool of candidates.

---

## 2.2 Applications of Ferrous Alloys

### 2.2.1 Plain Carbon Steels

| **Grade** | **%C** | **Application** |
|:---------:|:------:|:----------------|
| AISI 1010 | 0.08–0.13 | Car body panels (deep drawing) |
| AISI 1020 | 0.18–0.23 | General structural, shafts, gears |
| AISI 1045 | 0.43–0.50 | Crankshafts, axles, couplings |
| AISI 1080 | 0.75–0.88 | Springs, chisels, hammers |
| AISI 1095 | 0.90–1.03 | High-stress springs, blades |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** As **%C increases** → **Hardness ↑, Tensile Strength ↑, Ductility ↓, Weldability ↓**.

### 2.2.2 Alloy Steels (Strategic Applications)

| **Alloy Steel** | **Key Alloying Element** | **Special Property** | **Application** |
|:----------------|:-------------------------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| AISI 4140 | Cr-Mo | High fatigue strength | Aircraft landing gear, drill collars |
| AISI 4340 | Ni-Cr-Mo | Toughness at low temp | Rocket motor casings, cryogenic |
| AISI 52100 | Cr (1.5%) | Extreme wear resistance | Ball/roller bearings |
| AISI 8620 | Ni-Cr-Mo | Case-hardenable | Gears, pinions, camshafts |
| H11, H13 | Cr-Mo-V | Hot-work toughness | Die-casting dies, extrusion tools |
| D2 | Cr (12%) | Air-hardening, wear | Punches, blanking dies |
| M2 | W-Mo-V | High-speed cutting | Lathe tools, drill bits |

### 2.2.3 Stainless Steels (Corrosion-Critical Applications)

- **Austenitic (304, 316):** Kitchen equipment, chemical tanks, medical instruments. Non-magnetic. **18-8 type** (18% Cr, 8% Ni).
- **Ferritic (430):** Automotive exhaust, decorative trims. Magnetic.
- **Martensitic (410, 420):** Cutlery, surgical tools, turbine blades. Heat-treatable.
- **Duplex (2205):** Oil & gas pipelines, marine. Mixed austenite + ferrite → 2× strength of 304.

### 2.2.4 Cast Irons (Cost-Sensitive, Vibration-Damping Applications)

| **Type** | **Microstructure** | **Application** |
|:---------|:-------------------|:----------------|
| Grey CI | Flake graphite | Engine blocks, machine beds (damping) |
| White CI | Cementite (Fe₃C) | Abrasive wear surfaces, crusher liners |
| Malleable CI | Tempered graphite | Pipe fittings, automotive parts |
| Ductile (Nodular) CI | Spheroidal graphite | Crankshafts, gears, pipes |
| High-Alloyed CI | Austenitic matrix | Pump bodies, valve parts (corrosion) |

---

## 2.3 Applications of Non-Ferrous Alloys

### 2.3.1 Aluminium and Its Alloys

- **Wrought Alloys (2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx):** Aircraft structures (2024 fuselage, 7075 wing), automotive panels.
- **Cast Alloys (A356, A380):** Engine cylinder heads, transmission housings, pump bodies.
- **Designation Logic:** 2xxx = Al-Cu, 6xxx = Al-Mg-Si, 7xxx = Al-Zn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Al 6061-T6:** Heat-treated to T6 (solution + artificial aging) → used in bicycle frames, helicopter rotor hubs, structural bridges.

### 2.3.2 Copper and Its Alloys

| **Alloy** | **Composition** | **Application** |
|:----------|:----------------|:----------------|
| Pure Cu (C11000) | 99.9% Cu | Electrical conductors, busbars |
| Brass (C26000) | 70% Cu – 30% Zn | Cartridge cases, plumbing fittings |
| Bronze (C93200) | 88% Cu – Sn – Pb | Bearings, bushings, ship propellers |
| Cupronickel (C70600) | 70% Cu – 30% Ni | Marine heat exchangers, coinage |

### 2.3.3 Titanium Alloys

- **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5):** The **workhorse of aerospace**. Used in jet engine compressor blades, airframe structural members, pressure vessels.
- **Commercially Pure Ti (Grade 1, 2):** Chemical processing equipment, condenser tubes.
- **Ti-6Al-4V ELI:** Extra-low interstitial → used in **biomedical implants** (hip stems, dental roots).

### 2.3.4 Magnesium Alloys (Lightest Structural Metals)

- **AZ91D, AM60:** Steering wheels, instrument panels, laptop casings.
- **WE43, Elektron 21:** Aerospace gearbox housings (high creep resistance).
- Limitation: **Poor corrosion resistance** → requires surface coatings.

### 2.3.5 Nickel and Superalloys

- **Inconel 718 (Ni-Cr-Fe-Nb):** Gas turbine disks, rocket motors, nuclear reactors. Retains strength up to **704 °C (1300 °F)**.
- **Hastelloy C-276:** Chemical reactors handling HCl, H₂SO₄.
- **Monel 400 (Ni-Cu):** Valves, pumps, propeller shafts in marine environments.

---

## 2.4 Applications Based on Heat Treatment

| **Heat Treatment** | **Resulting Microstructure** | **Application** |
|:-------------------|:----------------------------|:----------------|
| Annealing | Coarse pearlite + ferrite | Machining feedstock |
| Normalizing | Fine pearlite + ferrite | Structural components (better than annealed) |
| Quenching | Martensite (hard, brittle) | Pre-treatment before tempering |
| Tempering | Tempered martensite | Gears, springs, axles |
| Austempering | Bainite (lower) | High-impact gears, clutch plates |
| Martempering | Martensite (low distortion) | Tools, dies |
| Case Hardening (Carburizing) | Hard case + tough core | Gears, camshafts |
| Induction Hardening | Surface martensite | Crankshaft journals, gear teeth |
| Nitriding | Iron nitrides (Fe₂-₃N) | High-temp wear parts (no quenching) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Relation** | **Physical Meaning** | **Application Context** |
|:----------------------|:---------------------|:------------------------|
| $M = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}$ | Specific strength | Aerospace material selection |
| $E^{1/3} / \rho$ | Specific stiffness | Vibration-resistant structures |
| $\sigma_{f}' / \sigma_{uts}$ | Fatigue ratio (≈ 0.5) | Endurance design of shafts |
| $K_{IC}$ | Fracture toughness | Damage-tolerant design |
| $\dot{\epsilon}_{ss} = A \sigma^{n} \exp\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Steady-state creep rate | High-temp turbine components |
| $T_{m} \approx 0.5 T_{h} \text{ to } 0.7 T_{h}$ | Homologous temperature limit | Maximum service temperature |
| $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 3.4 \cdot HB$ | Hardness–strength correlation | Quick strength estimation from Brinell |
| $\lambda = \frac{k}{\rho c_{p}}$ | Thermal diffusivity | Heat-sink, electronic packaging |
| $\text{Performance Index} = \frac{E^{1/3}}{\rho}$ | Minimum-weight design (stiffness-driven) | Lightweight beam/panel design |
| $\text{Performance Index} = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2/3}}{\rho}$ | Minimum-weight design (strength-driven) | Tie-rod, connecting rod design |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $T_{h} = T_{service}/T_{melting}$ (in Kelvin). When $T_{h} > 0.5$, **creep becomes the dominant failure mode** → must select Ni-superalloys, ceramics, or refractory metals.

---

| **Industry Sector** | **Critical Material Property** | **Material Selected** | **Why Selected** |
|:--------------------|:-------------------------------|:----------------------|:-----------------|
| Aerospace | Strength/weight + fatigue | Al 7075, Ti-6Al-4V, CFRP | Save fuel, resist cyclic load |
| Automotive (EV) | Crash energy absorption, conductivity | Advanced high-strength steel (AHSS), Cu rotors | Safety, electrical efficiency |
| Marine | Corrosion resistance | Cu-Ni, Monel 400, SS 316 | Resist saltwater corrosion |
| Power plants | Creep resistance at 600–700 °C | Inconel 617, P91 steel | Hold pressure at high T |
| Biomedical | Biocompatibility, fatigue | Ti-6Al-4V ELI, Co-Cr alloy | Non-toxic, integrate with bone |
| Cutting tools | Hot hardness > 600 °C | WC-Co, M2 HSS, Ceramics | Retain edge at cutting T |
| Electronics | Thermal conductivity | Cu, Al, AlN, diamond | Dissipate heat from chips |
| Cryogenic | Toughness at < -196 °C | AISI 304L, 9% Ni steel | Avoid brittle fracture at LNG T |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Selection by Maximum Performance Index (Ashby's Method)

**Problem:** Select the lightest material for a **tensile tie-rod** that must carry an axial load $F$ without yielding, and must have a minimum length $L$ and stiffness requirement $S$.

### Step 1: Define the Functional Requirements

The tie-rod must satisfy:

$$F \leq \sigma_{y} \cdot A \quad \text{(yield strength constraint)}$$

$$S \leq \frac{E \cdot A}{L} \quad \text{(stiffness constraint)}$$

Solving both for cross-sectional area $A$:

$$A = \frac{F}{\sigma_{y}} \quad \text{and} \quad A = \frac{S \cdot L}{E}$$

The required area is the **maximum** of these two:

$$A = \max \left( \frac{F}{\sigma_{y}}, \frac{S \cdot L}{E} \right)$$

### Step 2: Express the Mass in Terms of Material Properties

$$m = A \cdot L \cdot \rho = \max \left( \frac{F \cdot L \cdot \rho}{\sigma_{y}}, \frac{S \cdot L^{2} \cdot \rho}{E} \right)$$

### Step 3: Strength-Dominated Case

If yielding governs:

$$m = \frac{F \cdot L}{\left( \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho} \right)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Performance Index} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}}$$

The material with the **highest $\sigma_{y}/\rho$** is the lightest.

### Step 4: Stiffness-Dominated Case

If stiffness governs:

$$m = \frac{S \cdot L^{2}}{\left( \dfrac{E}{\rho} \right)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Performance Index} = \frac{E}{\rho}}$$

The material with the **highest $E/\rho$** is the lightest.

### Step 5: Combined Strength + Stiffness Case

When both constraints matter, the tie-rod must simultaneously satisfy both, and the optimal mass becomes:

$$m = L \cdot \rho \cdot \max \left( \frac{F}{\sigma_{y}}, \frac{S \cdot L}{E} \right)$$

> **KTU Numerical Example (Typical 7-mark question):**
> A tie-rod has $F = 50 \text{ kN}$, $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$, stiffness $S = 100 \text{ kN/m}$. Compare **Al 6061** ($\sigma_{y} = 276$ MPa, $E = 69$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³) with **Ti-6Al-4V** ($\sigma_{y} = 880$ MPa, $E = 114$ GPa, $\rho = 4430$ kg/m³).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{strength, Al}} &= \frac{50{,}000}{276 \times 10^{6}} = 1.81 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2} \\
A_{\text{strength, Ti}} &= \frac{50{,}000}{880 \times 10^{6}} = 5.68 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2} \\
A_{\text{stiffness, Al}} &= \frac{100{,}000 \times 1.5}{69 \times 10^{9}} = 2.17 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} \\
A_{\text{stiffness, Ti}} &= \frac{100{,}000 \times 1.5}{114 \times 10^{9}} = 1.32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $A_{\text{strength}} \gg A_{\text{stiffness}}$, **strength dominates**. Required areas are governed by the first set.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Al}} &= 1.81 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 \times 2700 = 0.733 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{Ti}} &= 5.68 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.5 \times 4430 = 0.377 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Titanium is **48% lighter** than aluminum. For aircraft tie-rods, **Ti-6Al-4V is preferred** when weight is critical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Service Temperature from Homologous Temperature

The homologous temperature is defined as:

$$T_{h} = \frac{T_{\text{service}}}{T_{\text{melting}}}$$

(both in Kelvin).

> **Empirical rule:**
> Below $T_{h} = 0.3$ → strength-controlled design
> Between $T_{h} = 0.3$ to $0.5$ → diffusion-controlled deformation (creep onset)
> Above $T_{h} = 0.5$ → **creep-governed design**

### Numerical Application

A turbine blade operates at $T_{\text{service}} = 850 \text{ °C} = 1123 \text{ K}$.

| **Material** | $T_{\text{melt}}$ (K) | $T_h$ | **Implication** |
|:-------------|:---------------------:|:-----:|:----------------|
| AISI 304 SS | 1673 | 0.671 | Severe creep → unsuitable |
| Inconel 718 | 1609 | 0.698 | Marginal → needs cooling |
| Nickel MP35N | 1623 | 0.692 | Marginal |
| Single-crystal Ni-superalloy PWA 1480 | 1623 + γ' strengthening | 0.692 | **Suitable** (no grain boundary sliding) |

**Conclusion:** Inconel 718 or single-crystal Ni-superalloys are the only viable choices for 850 °C turbine blades.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Property → Microstructure → Application

| **Engineering Property** | **Microstructural Origin** | **Material Engineered** | **Service Application** |
|:-------------------------|:---------------------------|:------------------------|:------------------------|
| High hardness | Martensite (BCT Fe-C) | AISI 1095 (quenched) | Razor blades, springs |
| Toughness at low T | FCC structure (slip systems) | Austenitic SS 304L | LNG storage tanks |
| Creep resistance | γ' precipitates (Ni₃(Al,Ti)) | Inconel 718, Waspaloy | Turbine disks |
| Wear resistance | Hard carbides (M₇C₃, M₂₃C₆) | High-Cr cast iron, D2 tool steel | Crusher jaws, dies |
| Fatigue strength | Fine grain, no inclusions | Vacuum-degassed steels | Aircraft landing gear |
| Corrosion resistance | Passive Cr₂O₃ film | SS 316, Ti Grade 2 | Marine propeller shafts |
| Electrical conductivity | Free electron mobility | OFHC Cu (C10100) | Coils in electromagnets |
| Magnetic permeability | BCC Fe with domain alignment | Si-steel (3% Si) | Transformer laminations |
| Spring action | High yield + high resilience | AISI 1095, 5160 spring steel | Leaf springs, watch springs |
| Dimensional stability | Low CTE | Invar (Fe-36%Ni) | Pendulum clocks, telescope mirrors |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Application Justification

**Question:** A manufacturer wants to make a **rocket nozzle** that must withstand $T = 1600$ °C and a pressure of 5 MPa. Compare the suitability of **AISI 316 SS, Tungsten, and C/C composite**.

**Step 1 — Temperature Limit Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{melt, 316 SS}} &= 1673 \text{ K} \\
T_{h, \text{316}} &= \frac{1873}{1673} = 1.12 \quad \text{(melts! Not suitable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{melt, W}} &= 3683 \text{ K} \\
T_{h, \text{W}} &= \frac{1873}{3683} = 0.51 \quad \text{(at creep limit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{melt, C/C}} &= 3823 \text{ K (sublimation)} \\
T_{h, \text{C/C}} &= \frac{1873}{3823} = 0.49 \quad \text{(suitable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Density Check (mass-critical for rockets):**

$$
\rho_{\text{W}} = 19{,}300 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_{\text{C/C}} = 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Selection:** **C/C composite** is chosen because of:
1. Lowest density (lightest nozzle = highest payload).
2. Adequate high-temperature strength up to 2000 °C in inert atmosphere.
3. Excellent thermal shock resistance (low CTE).
4. Tungsten is rejected due to **excessive weight** despite melting point.

> [!TIP]
> This is the **classic KTU reasoning pattern** — first rule out by **maximum temperature**, then rank survivors by **performance index** (here $\sigma_y / \rho$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART-A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"List any three applications of medium carbon steels. Justify each selection with a property."**

**Model Answer (KTU Standard, 3 marks):**

1. **AISI 1045 in Crankshafts** – Contains 0.45% C providing a balance of strength (UTS ≈ 625 MPa) and ductility. Can be surface-hardened by induction for wear resistance at journals.

2. **AISI 1040 in Axles and Couplings** – Moderate strength with good machinability; used where toughness and shock resistance are needed.

3. **AISI 1050 in Railway Wheels** – Higher carbon (0.50%) gives high wear resistance and hardness (≈ 200 HB) for repeated rolling contact.

*(Award 1 mark per correct application-property pair, max 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Why is Inconel 718 preferred over stainless steel 304 for gas turbine blades?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Property** | **Inconel 718** | **SS 304** |
|:-------------|:----------------|:-----------|
| Max service T | 704 °C (1300 °F) | 870 °C (intermittent) |
| Creep resistance | Excellent (γ' Ni₃(Al,Ti)) | Poor above 600 °C |
| Hot hardness | Retains > 80% | Falls sharply |
| Oxidation resistance | Forms stable Al₂O₃ layer | Forms porous Cr₂O₃ |

Inconel 718 is preferred because it is **precipitation-hardened by γ' phase**, retaining strength and resisting creep at high homologous temperatures, whereas SS 304 suffers from **sensitization and grain boundary carbide precipitation** above 600 °C.

---

## 📗 PART-B QUESTIONS (14 Marks)

> **Instructions:** Answer **any ONE full question** from each module. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) Discuss the applications of aluminium alloys in the aerospace and automotive industries. Justify with specific grades and properties.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the applications of any four types of cast iron in modern engineering with their distinguishing microstructural features.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

Aluminium alloys are chosen where **low density (2.7 g/cm³)**, **high specific strength**, and **corrosion resistance** are required. The major alloy systems and applications are:

1. **Al 2024 (Al-Cu-Mg) – Aerospace Structural Skin Panels**
   - Composition: Cu 4%, Mg 1.5%, Mn 0.6%.
   - Property: UTS = 470 MPa (T3 temper), good fatigue resistance (107 cycles at 140 MPa).
   - **Application:** Aircraft fuselage skins, lower wing skins.
   - **Justification:** Excellent fatigue life under cyclic cabin pressure and gust loads.

2. **Al 7075 (Al-Zn-Mg-Cu) – Highly Stressed Aerospace Parts**
   - Composition: Zn 5.6%, Mg 2.5%, Cu 1.6%.
   - Property: UTS = 572 MPa (T6), yield strength = 503 MPa.
   - **Application:** Aircraft wing spars, fuselage frames, military aircraft.
   - **Justification:** Highest strength among Al alloys, replacing some steel parts in aircraft (weight saving 30%).

3. **Al 6061-T6 (Al-Mg-Si) – General Structural Use**
   - Property: UTS = 310 MPa, good weldability, corrosion resistance.
   - **Application:** Bicycle frames, marine structures, automotive chassis components.
   - **Justification:** Most versatile Al alloy; balance of strength, weldability, and cost.

4. **Al A356 (Cast Alloy) – Automotive Engine and Transmission Parts**
   - Property: Good castability, T6 strength ≈ 220 MPa.
   - **Application:** Cylinder heads, intake manifolds, transmission housings.
   - **Justification:** Lightweight (mass reduction 40% over cast iron) and good thermal conductivity for engine cooling.

**Concluding Remark:** Aluminium has reduced vehicle weight in modern EVs by 100–200 kg, increasing range by 10–15%.

*(Marking: 1.5 marks per alloy + 1 mark for conclusion = 7 marks.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

| **Type of CI** | **Microstructure** | **Property** | **Application** |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:-------------|:----------------|
| **Grey Cast Iron** | Flake graphite in ferrite/pearlite matrix | Excellent damping, low cost | Engine blocks, machine tool beds |
| **White Cast Iron** | Massive cementite (Fe₃C) + pearlite | Very hard, brittle | Crusher liners, grinding balls, slurry pump impellers |
| **Malleable Cast Iron** | Tempered graphite rosettes in ferrite | Ductile, machinable | Pipe fittings, agricultural implements |
| **Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron** | Spheroidal graphite in ferrite/pearlite | High strength + ductility | Crankshafts, steering knuckles, gears |
| **High-Alloy (Ni-Resist) CI** | Austenitic matrix with graphite | Corrosion + heat resistant | Pump bodies, valve parts, marine hardware |

**Microstructure–Property–Application Linkage:**

- **Grey CI:** The flake graphite acts as a **stress concentrator** → low ductility but creates internal friction → **vibration damping** ideal for machine bases.
- **White CI:** Hard Fe₃C (≈ 1000 HV) → **abrasive wear resistance** → crushing and grinding operations.
- **Malleable CI:** White CI is heat-treated (≈ 870 °C for 50 h) to **decompose cementite into rosette graphite clusters** → ductility rises to 10–20% elongation.
- **Ductile CI:** Mg or Ce added to **spheroidize graphite** → minimal stress concentration → 80% of steel strength with 2× damping.

*(Marking: 1.5 marks per cast iron type with microstructure, property, and application. 1 mark for synthesis. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) With suitable examples, explain how titanium alloys are selected for biomedical and aerospace applications. Compare Ti-6Al-4V with Ti-6Al-4V ELI in terms of application.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the role of heat treatment in tailoring materials for specific engineering applications. Give at least four examples.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

**Aerospace Selection of Ti Alloys:**

- **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)** holds **56% of total titanium market**, with **80% usage in aerospace**.
- Selection criteria: (i) Density = 4.43 g/cm³ (half of steel), (ii) Specific strength = $200 \text{ kNm/kg}$ (highest among metals), (iii) Retains strength up to 400 °C, (iv) Excellent fatigue life ($10^7$ cycles at 510 MPa).
- **Applications:** Fan and compressor blades of jet engines, airframe structural members, helicopter rotor hubs, spacecraft pressure vessels.
- **Justification:** Combines high strength-to-weight ratio with corrosion resistance; allows higher operating temperatures in engines → better thermodynamic efficiency.

**Biomedical Selection of Ti Alloys:**

- **Biocompatibility:** Ti forms a stable, inert TiO₂ passive layer → non-toxic, integrates with bone (osseointegration).
- **Low modulus (110 GPa)** closer to bone (10–30 GPa) than Co-Cr alloy (210 GPa) → reduces **stress shielding**.
- **Non-magnetic** → safe for MRI scans.

**Comparison Table: Ti-6Al-4V vs Ti-6Al-4V ELI**

| **Property** | **Ti-6Al-4V** | **Ti-6Al-4V ELI** |
|:-------------|:---------------|:------------------|
| O content (max) | 0.20% | **0.13%** (Extra Low Interstitial) |
| Fe content (max) | 0.30% | **0.25%** |
| Fracture toughness | 75 MPa√m | **> 100 MPa√m** |
| Fatigue crack growth | Moderate | Slower |
| Application | Aerospace structural | **Biomedical implants** |

**Concluding Remark:** ELI grade is preferred for implants due to higher fracture toughness and lower impurity content (no risk of ion release in the body).

*(Marking: 3 marks for aerospace, 3 marks for biomedical, 1 mark for comparison = 7 marks.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

Heat treatment modifies **microstructure** without changing **chemical composition**, allowing tailoring of properties.

**Example 1: Gears in Automobile Transmission**
- Process: **Carburizing + Quenching + Tempering**.
- Result: Hard surface (60 HRC) + tough core (30 HRC).
- **Application Justification:** Surface resists pitting fatigue; core absorbs shock loads.

**Example 2: Leaf Springs in Suspension**
- Process: **Austempering** of AISI 5160.
- Result: **Lower bainite microstructure** with UTS = 1700 MPa and elongation 8%.
- **Application Justification:** Bainite gives higher toughness than tempered martensite at equal hardness, reducing spring breakage.

**Example 3: Lathe Cutting Tools**
- Process: **Quenching + Triple Tempering** of M2 HSS.
- Result: Secondary hardening due to **M₂C and M₆C carbide precipitation** → hot hardness 60 HRC at 600 °C.
- **Application Justification:** Retains cutting edge at high temperatures generated during machining.

**Example 4: Crankshaft Journals**
- Process: **Induction Hardening** (depth 4–5 mm).
- Result: Surface martensite, core ferrite-pearlite.
- **Application Justification:** Wear-resistant bearing surface with tough core to absorb combustion shock.

**Example 5: Nuclear Reactor Pressure Vessels (Bonus)**
- Process: **Normalizing + Tempering** of SA 508 Class 3 steel.
- Result: Fine-grained ferrite + tempered bainite.
- **Application Justification:** Resists brittle fracture at 290 °C under neutron irradiation.

*(Marking: 1.25 marks per example with process, microstructure, property, and application. 1 mark for concluding statement. Total = 7 marks.)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Material Application Questions (Cost: 4–5 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **Writing only the application without property justification** – *"Inconel is used in turbines"* → 0 marks. Correct: *"Inconel is used in turbines because of γ' precipitation hardening giving high creep resistance at Tₕ > 0.6."*
> 2. **Confusing alloy designations** – e.g., confusing **AISI 304** (austenitic, non-magnetic) with **AISI 430** (ferritic, magnetic). Examiners deduct full marks for wrong family.
> 3. **Forgetting the operating environment** – Aluminium alloys are NOT used in **strongly acidic/alkaline** environments → wrong if claimed as "corrosion-proof".
> 4. **Omitting percentage composition** – In KTU 2024, marks are reserved for **specific data** (e.g., "0.4% C", "18% Cr, 8% Ni"). Avoid vague phrases like "high carbon".
> 5. **Ignoring cost factor** – Selecting gold or platinum for any application loses 1 mark unless cost is justified.
> 6. **Mishandling of Mg-alloys** – Claiming Mg alloys are used in **wet marine environments** without protective coating → wrong.
> 7. **Heat treatment cycle errors** – Confusing **carburizing (with C) and nitriding (with N)**. Examiners check the medium: solid/liquid/gas for carburizing; ammonia for nitriding.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Module 4 — Applications — Final Cheat Sheet**

- ✅ **Material application is governed by 4 constraints:** Function, Manufacturing, Cost, Environment.
- ✅ **As %C increases in steel:** Strength ↑, Hardness ↑, Ductility ↓, Weldability ↓.
- ✅ **Austenitic SS (304, 316):** Non-magnetic, non-heat-treatable, work-hardenable, 18% Cr – 8% Ni → corrosion resistance.
- ✅ **Martensitic SS (410, 420):** Magnetic, heat-treatable, used for cutlery and surgical tools.
- ✅ **Duplex SS (2205):** Mixed austenite-ferrite → 2× strength of 304 → oil & gas pipelines.
- ✅ **Ductile Iron = Spheroidal graphite**; **Grey Iron = Flake graphite** (damping).
- ✅ **Al 2024 = Al-Cu**; **Al 6061 = Al-Mg-Si**; **Al 7075 = Al-Zn** (highest strength).
- ✅ **Ti-6Al-4V** = 90% of all Ti usage. **ELI grade** = biomedical. **Commercially pure** = chemical industry.
- ✅ **Inconel 718 = γ' Ni₃(Al,Ti) precipitates** → 700 °C service in turbines.
- ✅ **Monel 400 (Ni-Cu)** = marine / HF acid environments.
- ✅ **Hastelloy C-276** = most corrosion-resistant Ni alloy → HCl, H₂SO₄.
- ✅ **Performance index for stiffness design = E/ρ**; for strength design = σ_y/ρ.
- ✅ **Maximum service T < 0.5 Tₘ** → no creep concern.
- ✅ **Case hardening = hard surface + tough core**; **Nitriding = no quenching** (Fe-N compounds).
- ✅ **Cutting tool hierarchy:** HSS < Coated HSS < Carbide < Ceramic < CBN < Diamond (by hot hardness).
- ✅ **Cu alloys:** Brass (Cu-Zn) = plumbing; Bronze (Cu-Sn) = bearings; Cupronickel (Cu-Ni) = marine.
- ✅ **Magnesium = lightest structural metal** but **needs corrosion protection**.
- ✅ **Cost ranking (low to high):** Mild Steel → Cast Iron → Al → SS → Ti → Ni-Superalloys → Precious metals.

> 🎯 **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Always structure your answer as:
> **Material → Composition → Microstructure → Property → Justified Application → Real Example.**
> This 5-link chain is what examiners look for. Memorize this template.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tempering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tempering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — TEMPERING
## Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Tempering** is a sub-critical heat treatment process performed on **quenched (hardened) steel** by reheating it to a temperature **below the lower critical temperature (A₁ ≈ 727 °C)**, holding it at that temperature for a specified duration, and then cooling it (usually in air) to room temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Tempering is the process of improving the toughness and reducing the brittleness of hardened steel by re-heating it to a temperature below the eutectoid transformation temperature, A₁, followed by controlled cooling. It converts the hard, brittle, and metastable **martensite** into a tougher, more ductile structure called **tempered martensite**, accompanied by the precipitation of fine carbides and the recovery/recrystallization of the α-ferrite matrix.

### 1.2 Why Tempering is Needed (The Problem It Solves)

After **quenching**, steel acquires **martensite**, an extremely hard but **brittle** and highly **stressed** phase. Un-tempered martensite contains:
- **Supersaturated carbon** trapped in the BCC lattice (distorted to BCT).
- High **residual stresses** (thermal and transformational).
- Poor **dimensional stability** (martensite is metastable).
- Almost **zero ductility or impact toughness**.

Tempering corrects all these drawbacks by allowing controlled diffusion of carbon and rearrangement of the microstructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Examiner's Favourite Line):**
> *Quenching makes the steel hard; tempering makes the steel usable.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fired Clay Pot" Model

Imagine a freshly fired clay pot:
- Right after the kiln, the pot is **extremely hard but will shatter** if dropped (it has internal stresses).
- The potter places it back in a *cooler oven* for a few hours. This controlled reheating allows the stresses to relax and the structure to "settle" into a tougher, less brittle state.
- If the potter reheats it at *too high a temperature*, it becomes soft and weak.

**Tempering works identically on martensite:**
- A *low tempering temperature* → maximum hardness retained, slight toughness gain.
- A *high tempering temperature* → hardness drops, but toughness/ductility improve drastically.

### 1.4 The Three Governing Variables (Examination Favourite)

The outcome of tempering depends on **three primary process parameters**, all of which must be memorized:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect of Increase |
|-----------|--------|---------------|--------------------|
| Tempering Temperature | T | 150 °C – 650 °C | Hardness ↓ , Toughness ↑ |
| Holding (Soaking) Time | t | 30 min – 4 hrs | Same as raising T (time-temperature equivalence) |
| Cooling Medium | — | Air / Oil / Water | Faster cooling risks distortion, esp. in alloy steels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness vs Tempering Temperature — Inverse Relationship:**
> As the tempering temperature increases, the **hardness decreases** while **ductility and toughness increase**. This is the central trade-off in tempering.

### 1.5 Position of Tempering in the Overall Heat-Treatment Cycle

The complete sequence for producing a hardened-and-toughened steel component is:

$$\text{Austenitisation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Quenching} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Tempering}$$

- **Austenitisation:** Heat above A₃ (or A₁ for hypereutectoid) to form austenite (γ-FCC).
- **Quenching:** Rapid cooling to bypass the nose of the TTT curve and form martensite.
- **Tempering:** Sub-critical reheat to transform martensite into tempered martensite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microstructural Stages of Tempering

Tempering is **not a single event** — it is a sequence of overlapping diffusion-controlled processes. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Explain the various stages of tempering."* There are **four classical stages**:

### Stage 1: Precipitation of ε-Carbide (80 – 250 °C)

- Begins almost immediately after quenching.
- **ε-Carbide** (Fe₂.₄C) — a **transition carbide** with hexagonal close-packed (HCP) structure — precipitates as **extremely fine, coherent particles** within the martensite plates.
- The martensite still retains most of its carbon, but in a less strained form.
- Hardness: **Slight decrease**; a small peak may appear (called *secondary hardening* tendency).
- The structure at this point is sometimes called **"tempered martensite" (low-temperature form)** or *"ε-tempered martensite."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Coherent vs Incoherent:** ε-carbide particles are **coherent** with the matrix — they share lattice continuity. This is why they provide strong strengthening (similar to precipitation hardening).

### Stage 2: Decomposition of Retained Austenite (200 – 300 °C)

- High-carbon (> 0.5 % C) and alloy steels contain **retained austenite** (γ-FCC) that did not transform to martensite during quenching.
- During Stage 2, retained austenite **decomposes into ferrite + cementite (Fe₃C)**.
- This is often accompanied by a small **hardness dip** in high-carbon steels (because soft ferrite is being formed), but in **alloy steels containing strong carbide-formers** (Cr, Mo, V, W), this stage is **delayed** and contributes to **secondary hardening** at higher temperatures.

### Stage 3: Replacement of ε-Carbide by Cementite, Fe₃C (250 – 400 °C)

- The metastable **ε-carbide is replaced** by the more stable **cementite (Fe₃C)**.
- Cementite precipitates as **fine, dispersed particles** within a now low-carbon ferritic matrix.
- The martensite loses most of its excess carbon → **significant drop in hardness** and a **major gain in toughness**.
- This is the "**classic tempering range**" for most engineering components.

### Stage 4: Coarsening of Cementite and α-Ferritification (> 400 °C)

- Above ~400 °C, the fine Fe₃C particles **coarsen (Ostwald ripening)** — small particles dissolve and re-precipitate onto larger ones.
- The matrix simultaneously undergoes **recovery and recrystallization** of the highly distorted α-ferrite (BCT → BCC).
- The structure becomes **equiaxed ferrite + spheroidised cementite** — essentially a "**sorbite**" (fine pearlite-like) microstructure.
- **Hardness drops substantially; toughness peaks.**
- For tool steels and high-alloy steels, this is where **secondary hardening** (Stage 4 + alloy carbides) becomes dominant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Summary of Stages — Memorize This Table:**

| Stage | Temperature Range | Principal Event | Effect on Hardness |
|-------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| **1** | 80 – 250 °C | ε-carbide (Fe₂.₄C) precipitation | Slight ↓ (may show small ↑) |
| **2** | 200 – 300 °C | Retained austenite → ferrite + Fe₃C | Slight ↓ (or ↑ if alloyed) |
| **3** | 250 – 400 °C | ε-carbide → cementite (Fe₃C) | Significant ↓ |
| **4** | > 400 °C | Coarsening of Fe₃C + recovery of α | Major ↓ |

## 2.2 Effects of Tempering on Mechanical Properties

| Property | Low-Temp Temper (150–250 °C) | High-Temp Temper (550–650 °C) |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Hardness (HRC)** | High (58–62) | Low (20–35) |
| **Tensile Strength** | High | Reduced |
| **Ductility (% El)** | Low | High |
| **Impact Toughness** | Low (may be brittle) | Maximum |
| **Residual Stresses** | Reduced | Fully relieved |
| **Dimensional Stability** | Improved | Best |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off (Always asked in KTU):**
> *A compromise temperature* (typically **200–300 °C** for cutting tools, **400–600 °C** for structural/automotive parts) is selected to balance hardness with toughness.

## 2.3 The Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter

The **time–temperature equivalence** during tempering is captured quantitatively by the **Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter (HJP)**:

$$H = T \,(\log t + C)$$

where:
- $T$ = Absolute tempering temperature (Kelvin, K)
- $t$ = Tempering time (hours)
- $C$ = Material constant (≈ 20 for carbon steels, varies 15–25 for alloy steels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation (KTU favourite):**
> The *same hardness* can be achieved by either a **high T for short t** OR a **low T for long t**. This is why HJP is widely used to predict hardness after tempering and to design industrial tempering cycles.

**Worked Use:** If a steel tempered at 873 K (600 °C) for 2 hours gives 35 HRC, the same hardness can be obtained at a lower temperature by holding for a longer time as predicted by HJP.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

> All formulas below are directly applicable to KTU 2024 Scheme questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Use / Application |
|---|------------------|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter | $H = T(\log t + C)$ | Predicts hardness from tempering cycle |
| 2 | Tempering Temperature Range | $T < A_1 \approx 727\,°C$ | Defines sub-critical regime |
| 3 | Volume Fraction Martensite (Koistinen-Marburger) | $f_m = 1 - e^{-1.1 \times 10^{-2}(M_s - T_q)}$ | Tells how much martensite exists after quench |
| 4 | Retained Austenite Balance | $f_\gamma + f_\alpha' + f_\alpha + f_{Fe_3C} = 1$ | Microstructural mass balance |
| 5 | Hardness–Temperature (empirical) | $HRC \approx A - B \cdot T_{temp}$ | Linear drop with rising tempering T |
| 6 | Dimensional Change on Tempering | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \approx -0.002\,(f_{\gamma}^{retained})$ | Volume contraction from γ→α |

> Use `\vert` notation instead of `|` for absolute values inside any extended KTU exam answer to avoid LaTeX errors.

## 2.5 Industrial Engineering Utility

Tempering is performed in nearly every engineering sector that uses hardened steel:

- **Automotive:** Gears, crankshafts, springs (tempered martensite at 400–550 °C for toughness).
- **Cutting Tools:** Drills, taps, dies (low-temp temper at 150–200 °C to retain hardness).
- **Aerospace:** Landing gear, turbine shafts (high-alloy steels with secondary hardening).
- **Structural:** Rails, bridge components (high-temp temper for impact resistance).
- **Dies and Molds:** Tempered at 550–620 °C for toughness against cyclic loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Hardness Drop with Tempering Time and Temperature (Hollomon–Jaffe)

The Hollomon–Jaffe relationship is empirical, but its derivation is rooted in **Arrhenius-type diffusion kinetics** of carbon in α-iron. We reconstruct the line of reasoning here as expected in a 14-mark derivation question.

### Step 1: Diffusion-Controlled Process

Tempering involves **carbon diffusion** out of supersaturated martensite (BCT) into carbides. The fraction of carbon precipitated is governed by a **first-order rate equation**:

$$\frac{dX}{dt} = k(1 - X)$$

where:
- $X$ = fraction of carbon precipitated.
- $k$ = rate constant (depends on T).

### Step 2: Integrate with Initial Condition $X(0) = 0$

$$\int_0^X \frac{dX}{1-X} = \int_0^t k\,dt$$

$$\left[-\ln(1 - X)\right]_0^X = k t$$

$$-\ln(1 - X) = k t$$

$$1 - X = e^{-kt} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X = 1 - e^{-kt}$$

This gives the **fractional completion of carbon precipitation** as a function of time.

### Step 3: Apply Arrhenius Equation for $k$

The rate constant follows the Arrhenius law:

$$k = k_0 \, e^{-Q/RT}$$

where:
- $k_0$ = pre-exponential factor.
- $Q$ = activation energy for carbon diffusion in α-Fe (≈ 80 kJ/mol).
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K).

### Step 4: Substitute into the Solution

$$X = 1 - \exp\!\left(-k_0 \, t \, e^{-Q/RT}\right)$$

For a given desired value of $X$ (i.e., a given fraction of carbon precipitated, corresponding to a given hardness), the combination $t \cdot e^{-Q/RT}$ must be constant:

$$t \, e^{-Q/RT} = \text{const.}$$

### Step 5: Take Logarithm and Rearrange

$$\ln t - \frac{Q}{RT} = \text{const.} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{Q}{R} = \frac{T (\ln t + C')}{1}$$

Multiplying both sides by $T$:

$$T(\ln t + C') = \text{const.} \cdot T$$

Identifying the constant on the right as a hardness-related parameter, we obtain the **Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter**:

$$\boxed{H = T\left(\log_{10} t + C\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Form Recap:**
> $H$ combines both temperature and time into a single number. A given H corresponds to a given hardness. Thus, *one HJP curve* can be used to design multiple equivalent tempering cycles.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

A carbon steel is tempered at **873 K (600 °C)** for **2 hours** and attains **35 HRC**. Given $C = 20$ for this steel, calculate the equivalent tempering time required at **773 K (500 °C)** to obtain the same hardness.

**Step 6.1 — Compute H at 873 K, 2 h:**

$$H = 873 \times (\log_{10} 2 + 20)$$

$$\log_{10} 2 = 0.3010$$

$$H = 873 \times 20.3010 = 17{,}722.77 \;K$$

**Step 6.2 — Set the same H and solve for $t$ at 773 K:**

$$17{,}722.77 = 773 \times (\log_{10} t + 20)$$

$$\log_{10} t + 20 = \frac{17{,}722.77}{773} = 22.928$$

$$\log_{10} t = 22.928 - 20 = 2.928$$

$$t = 10^{2.928} = 847.2 \;\text{hours}$$

**Step 6.3 — Conclusion:**

Lowering the temperature from 873 K to 773 K requires the tempering time to increase from 2 hours to **≈ 847 hours** to obtain the same hardness of 35 HRC. This shows the strong sensitivity of tempering outcome to temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Insight:** Each ~100 K drop in temperature increases required time by **two to three orders of magnitude**. This is why tempering is industrially done in the 150–650 °C range, not lower.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Property Evolution vs. Tempering Temperature

| Tempering Temp (°C) | Dominant Microstructure | Hardness (HRC) | Toughness | Typical Application |
|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 25 (as-quenched) | Martensite (BCT) + Retained γ | 62–65 | Very Low | Brittle, unusable |
| 100 – 200 | ε-carbide + martensite | 58–62 | Slightly improved | Razor blades, surgical tools |
| 200 – 300 | Tempered martensite (low-T) + Retained γ decomposing | 52–58 | Improving | Chisels, punches |
| 300 – 400 | Tempered martensite + Fe₃C | 45–52 | Good | Springs, gears |
| 400 – 550 | Sorbite (ferrite + Fe₃C) | 30–45 | Very good | Crankshafts, axles |
| 550 – 650 | Coarse spheroidised structure | 20–30 | Excellent | Structural components |

## 3.3 Energy of Activation — Why Alloying Matters

For plain carbon steels, $Q \approx 80$ kJ/mol. For alloy steels containing Cr, Mo, V, Nb, W, the activation energy for **substitutional diffusion** is much higher (≈ 250–300 kJ/mol), and these elements also form **stable alloy carbides** (e.g., Cr₂₃C₆, VC, Mo₂C) that resist coarsening. This is the physical origin of:

- **Secondary hardening** in high-alloy tool steels.
- **Temper resistance** (delay of softening).
- **Temper embrittlement** mitigation (controlled Mo addition, see Section 3.4).

## 3.4 Temper Embrittlement — Two Critical Temperature Windows

A 14-mark question often asks: *"What is temper embrittlement? Explain the types."*

### Type I — 250 – 400 °C Embrittlement (300 °C Embrittlement / "Blue Brittleness")

- Occurs in **plain carbon and low-alloy steels** tempered in 250–400 °C.
- Caused by precipitation of a **thin film of cementite (Fe₃C)** along prior-austenite grain boundaries.
- Toughness drops sharply; hardness may even *increase* slightly.
- The oxide film formed at this temperature is blue, hence the historical name *blue brittleness*.

### Type II — 400 – 575 °C Embrittlement ("Temper Embrittlement Proper" / TSEP)

- Occurs in **alloy steels** containing impurities (S, P, Sn, Sb, As).
- Caused by **segregation of P, Sn, Sb** to prior-austenite grain boundaries during slow cooling from the tempering temperature.
- Toughness is reduced, especially in the **Charpy impact transition curve** — the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) shifts upward.
- **Avoided by:** rapid cooling from the tempering temperature, addition of **Mo (0.5 %)** to scavenge P, and reducing S/P in the steel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (Valuator's Pitfall):**
> Students often confuse 300 °C and 500 °C embrittlement. Remember:
> - 300 °C → **cementite film at grain boundaries** (intrinsic, plain carbon steel).
> - 500 °C → **P/Sn/Sb segregation at grain boundaries** (extrinsic, alloy steel impurities).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tempering Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Austenitisation Above A3 Temperature"] --> A2["Hold at 30 to 50 C above A3 for Homogenisation"]
    A2 --> A3["Quench in Water Oil or Polymer Bath"]
    A3 --> A4["As Quenched Martensite BCT plus Retained Austenite"]
    A4 --> A5["Reheat to Sub Critical Temperature below A1"]
    A5 --> B1["Stage 1 80 to 250 C Epsilon Carbide Fe24C"]
    B1 --> B2["Stage 2 200 to 300 C Retained Austenite Decomposes to Ferrite plus Cementite"]
    B2 --> B3["Stage 3 250 to 400 C Epsilon Carbide Replaced by Cementite Fe3C"]
    B3 --> B4["Stage 4 above 400 C Cementite Coarsens plus Alpha Recrystallises"]
    B4 --> C1["Tempered Martensite with Dispersed Carbides"]
    C1 --> C2["Final Property Hardness Reduced Toughness Improved"]
```

## 4.2 Effect of Tempering Temperature on Hardness and Toughness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Low Temper 150 to 250 C"] --> R1["High Hardness 58 to 62 HRC"]
    H2["Medium Temper 300 to 450 C"] --> R2["Balanced Hardness and Toughness 40 to 50 HRC"]
    H3["High Temper 550 to 650 C"] --> R3["Low Hardness 20 to 30 HRC but Maximum Toughness"]
    H1 --> EMB1["Possible 300 C Embrittlement Zone"]
    H3 --> EMB2["Possible 500 C Embrittlement Zone"]
```

## 4.3 Microstructural Evolution Block Diagram (Stages 1 to 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["START As Quenched Martensite BCT with Supersaturated Carbon"]
    S0 --> S1["STAGE 1 Epsilon Carbide Fe24C Forms as Coherent Plates inside Martensite"]
    S1 --> S2["STAGE 2 Retained Austenite FCC Decomposes to Ferrite plus Fine Cementite"]
    S2 --> S3["STAGE 3 Epsilon Carbide Dissolves and Replaces with Stable Cementite Fe3C"]
    S3 --> S4["STAGE 4 Cementite Particles Coarsen and Alpha Ferrite Recrystallises to Equiaxed Grains"]
    S4 --> S5["FINAL Tempered Martensite Microstructure with Dispersed Spheroidised Carbides in Soft Ferritic Matrix"]
```

## 4.4 Property Trade-off Block Diagram (for Revision)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT["Property Change as Tempering Temperature Increases"]
    PT --> P1["Hardness Decreases"]
    PT --> P2["Tensile Strength Decreases"]
    PT --> P3["Ductility Elongation Increases"]
    PT --> P4["Impact Toughness Increases"]
    PT --> P5["Residual Stresses Are Relieved"]
    PT --> P6["Dimensional Stability Improves"]
    PT --> P7["Risk of Temper Embrittlement 300 C and 500 C Zones"]
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Tempering Parameter (Time-Temperature Equivalence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["High Temperature Short Time"] --> H1["Same HJP Value"]
    B["Low Temperature Long Time"] --> H1
    H1 --> H2["Same Final Hardness of Steel"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams collectively form a complete conceptual map of the *tempering process, its stages, its property effects, and its parameter equivalence.* KTU examiners appreciate well-labelled schematics in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define tempering. Why is it necessary after quenching?**

**Model Answer:**
Tempering is a sub-critical heat treatment process in which hardened (quenched) steel is reheated to a temperature **below A₁ (727 °C)**, held for a specific time, and then cooled, usually in air.

It is necessary because quenching produces **martensite**, which is extremely **hard but very brittle** and contains high **residual stresses** and **supersaturated carbon**. Tempering relieves these stresses, decomposes retained austenite, precipitates fine carbides, and converts brittle martensite into tougher **tempered martensite**, providing a usable combination of hardness, strength, and toughness. *(Word limit: ~80–100 words; 3 marks.)*

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between tempering and annealing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Tempering | Annealing |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Temperature | Below A₁ (sub-critical) | Above A₃ (full annealing) or near A₁ (process anneal) |
| Prior Condition | Applied after quenching (martensite) | Applied after cold working or as-rolled state |
| Purpose | Reduce brittleness, relieve stresses, improve toughness | Soften, refine grains, improve machinability |
| Hardness | Moderately reduced (still useful) | Substantially reduced |
| Microstructure | Tempered martensite | Coarse pearlite + ferrite |

*(Tabular answer preferred for 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**(a)** Explain the various **stages of tempering** with a neat sketch of the microstructural changes and the corresponding temperature ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **temper embrittlement** in steels. Explain the two types — 300 °C embrittlement and 500 °C embrittlement — and the methods to avoid them. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER

### (a) Stages of Tempering — 7 Marks

When as-quenched martensite is reheated, the following sequence of microstructural changes takes place:

**Stage 1 — Precipitation of ε-Carbide (80 – 250 °C):**
The supersaturated carbon in BCT martensite begins to segregate and forms **ε-carbide (Fe₂.₄C)**, a transition carbide with HCP structure. These particles are extremely fine and **coherent** with the parent martensite, providing some strengthening. Hardness decreases only slightly; a marginal peak (secondary hardening tendency) may appear. Microstructure: tempered martensite with finely dispersed ε-carbide.

**Stage 2 — Decomposition of Retained Austenite (200 – 300 °C):**
In high-carbon and alloy steels, some **austenite (FCC)** is retained after quenching. During Stage 2, this retained austenite transforms into **ferrite (BCC) + cementite (Fe₃C)**. In plain carbon steels, this causes a small hardness dip; in **alloy steels with strong carbide formers (Cr, Mo, V)**, this stage is delayed and contributes to **secondary hardening** at higher temperatures.

**Stage 3 — Replacement of ε-Carbide by Cementite (250 – 400 °C):**
The metastable ε-carbide is replaced by the **more thermodynamically stable cementite (Fe₃C)**. The martensitic matrix loses most of its excess carbon and begins to relax. Hardness drops significantly; toughness increases notably. This is the "**classic tempering range**" for most industrial components.

**Stage 4 — Coarsening of Cementite and α-Ferritification (> 400 °C):**
Fine Fe₃C particles **coarsen (Ostwald ripening)**, and the distorted BCT matrix undergoes **recovery and recrystallization** to BCC ferrite. The final structure is **equiaxed ferrite + spheroidised cementite (sorbite)**. Hardness decreases substantially; toughness reaches its maximum. In alloy steels, this stage may also be accompanied by the precipitation of **fine alloy carbides**, giving rise to **secondary hardening**.

> **Sketch:** Draw a temperature axis from 25 °C to 700 °C and mark the four stage regions with the corresponding microstructural labels. Add a curve showing hardness decreasing with temperature (typical KTU diagram).

### (b) Temper Embrittlement — 7 Marks

**Definition:** Temper embrittlement refers to a **reduction in impact toughness** of steel that occurs when it is tempered within, or slowly cooled through, certain critical temperature ranges.

**Type I — 300 °C Embrittlement (also called "Blue Brittleness"):**

- Occurs in the temperature range **250 – 400 °C**, primarily in **plain carbon and low-alloy steels**.
- Caused by the precipitation of a **thin, continuous film of cementite (Fe₃C)** along the **prior-austenite grain boundaries**.
- This film provides an easy path for crack propagation, leading to **intergranular fracture** and a drop in impact energy.
- The oxide film at this temperature appears blue, giving the phenomenon its traditional name.
- **Avoidance:** Skip this temperature range during tempering (use either lower or higher tempering temperatures); use alloy steels with carbide formers that produce dispersed carbides rather than grain-boundary films.

**Type II — 500 °C Embrittlement (Temper Embrittlement Proper):**

- Occurs in the temperature range **400 – 575 °C**, mainly in **alloy steels** containing **impurity elements such as phosphorus (P), tin (Sn), antimony (Sb), and arsenic (As)**.
- Caused by the **equilibrium segregation of P, Sn, Sb, As** to the **prior-austenite grain boundaries**, weakening the boundary cohesion.
- The effect is most severe when the steel is **slowly cooled** through this range (e.g., furnace cooling), as slow cooling gives the impurities time to diffuse to the boundaries.
- The **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** shifts to higher values.
- **Avoidance Methods:**
  1. **Reduce impurity content** (P ≤ 0.005 %, low S) in the steel.
  2. Add **0.2 – 0.5 % Molybdenum**, which forms Mo carbides and scavenges phosphorus from the grain boundaries.
  3. **Rapid cool** (water or oil quench) from the tempering temperature to minimise segregation time.
  4. Avoid prolonged service exposure in the 350 – 575 °C range.

> **Conclusion:** Temper embrittlement is a critical concern for components like turbine rotors, pressure vessels, and heavy gears that operate in or pass through these temperature ranges.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**(a)** Define the **Hollomon–Jaffe tempering parameter**. A carbon steel is tempered at 873 K for 2 hours to obtain a hardness of 35 HRC. Calculate the time required to obtain the **same hardness** at 773 K, given $C = 20$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effect of tempering on the mechanical properties** of quenched steel. How does **alloying** modify the tempering response? **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER

### (a) Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter and Numerical — 7 Marks

**Definition:** The Hollomon–Jaffe tempering parameter (HJP) is an empirical quantity that combines the tempering temperature and time into a single parameter to predict the resulting hardness of tempered steel:

$$H = T \cdot (\log_{10} t + C)$$

where $T$ is the absolute tempering temperature (in Kelvin), $t$ is the tempering time (in hours), and $C$ is a material-specific constant (typically ≈ 20 for carbon steels). A given HJP value corresponds to a given hardness, so a *high-T short-t* cycle and a *low-T long-t* cycle can produce the same hardness.

**Step 1: Calculate $H$ at 873 K, 2 h:**

$$H_1 = 873 \times (\log_{10} 2 + 20) = 873 \times (0.3010 + 20) = 873 \times 20.3010$$

$$H_1 = 17{,}722.77 \;K$$

**Step 2: Equate $H_2 = H_1$ at 773 K and solve for $t$:**

$$17{,}722.77 = 773 \times (\log_{10} t + 20)$$

$$\log_{10} t + 20 = \frac{17{,}722.77}{773} = 22.928$$

$$\log_{10} t = 2.928$$

$$t = 10^{2.928} = 847.2 \;\text{hours}$$

**Step 3: Conclusion:**
The required tempering time at 773 K is approximately **847 hours (≈ 35 days)** — a dramatic increase over the 2 hours at 873 K. This numerically demonstrates the **strong temperature sensitivity** of tempering and validates the time–temperature equivalence principle.

### (b) Effect of Tempering on Mechanical Properties & Role of Alloying — 7 Marks

**Effect of Tempering on Mechanical Properties:**

1. **Hardness:** Decreases with increasing tempering temperature. At low tempering (150–250 °C), hardness is retained at 58–62 HRC; at high tempering (550–650 °C), it drops to 20–30 HRC. The decrease is monotonic but not linear; it accelerates above 400 °C.

2. **Tensile Strength:** Decreases in parallel with hardness, though the drop is less steep for low-alloy steels.

3. **Ductility (% Elongation, % Reduction in Area):** Increases significantly with tempering temperature, as the ferrite matrix recovers and the brittle martensite decomposes.

4. **Impact Toughness:** Increases dramatically with tempering, especially in the 400–600 °C range, provided the **temper-embrittlement zones** (250–400 °C and 400–575 °C for alloy steels) are avoided.

5. **Residual Stresses:** Substantially relieved by tempering — thermal stresses relax and transformational stresses are reduced by carbon diffusion.

6. **Dimensional Stability:** Improved because carbon is no longer supersaturated and austenite is decomposed.

**Effect of Alloying on the Tempering Response:**

- Alloying elements (**Cr, Mo, V, W, Nb, Ti**) form **stable, finely dispersed alloy carbides** (Cr₂₃C₆, VC, Mo₂C, W₂C, NbC) that resist coarsening even at high temperatures.
- This **delays softening** and produces **secondary hardening** — a hardness peak at 500–600 °C due to the precipitation of these alloy carbides.
- Substitutional alloying also **raises the activation energy** for carbon diffusion in ferrite, slowing the entire tempering process.
- Alloying elements like **Mo** mitigate **Type II temper embrittlement** by scavenging P from grain boundaries.
- **Ni** improves toughness, while **Co** strengthens the matrix and resists tempering.
- **Si** retards the conversion of ε-carbide to cementite, delaying Stage 3 and providing extra temper resistance.

> **Conclusion:** The combination of *alloying + tempering* allows modern high-strength low-alloy (HSLA) steels, tool steels, and maraging steels to achieve excellent combinations of strength, toughness, and dimensional stability unattainable in plain carbon steels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):**
> 1. **Do not write "tempering reduces hardness" without specifying the temperature range.** Always say: *"As tempering temperature increases (typically 150–650 °C), hardness decreases while toughness increases."*
> 2. **Do not confuse the four stages of tempering with the four stages of heat treatment.** Stage 1 is *ε-carbide*, not *austenitisation*.
> 3. **Numerical questions on HJP require BOTH temperature and time units to be correct.** Temperature in **Kelvin (K)**, time in **hours**. Conversion errors cause cascading mistakes — lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Do not write "temper embrittlement" as a single concept.** Always distinguish Type I (300 °C, cementite film) and Type II (500 °C, P segregation).
> 5. **Skipping the sketch/diagram in a 14-mark answer on stages of tempering** costs at least 2 marks. Always draw a temperature axis with the four stage regions.
> 6. **Do not confuse tempering with austempering or martempering.** Tempering is sub-critical reheating of quenched martensite; austempering/martempering involve isothermal holds above Ms in the bainite region.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tempering — Final 60-Second Revision:**

- ☐ Tempering = **sub-critical reheat below A₁ ≈ 727 °C** of quenched steel.
- ☐ Purpose: **reduce brittleness, relieve stresses, improve toughness, decompose retained austenite**.
- ☐ Hardness ↓ and Toughness ↑ as tempering temperature rises.
- ☐ **Four stages of tempering:**
  - Stage 1 (80–250 °C) → ε-carbide (Fe₂.₄C) precipitation
  - Stage 2 (200–300 °C) → retained austenite → ferrite + cementite
  - Stage 3 (250–400 °C) → ε-carbide → cementite (Fe₃C)
  - Stage 4 (> 400 °C) → cementite coarsening + α-recrystallisation
- ☐ Final structure = **tempered martensite** (ferrite matrix + dispersed carbides).
- ☐ **Hollomon–Jaffe Parameter:** $H = T(\log t + C)$ — *time–temperature equivalence*.
- ☐ **Type I embrittlement:** 300 °C, cementite film at grain boundaries (plain carbon steel).
- ☐ **Type II embrittlement:** 500 °C, P/Sn/Sb segregation (alloy steel impurities), avoided by Mo addition and rapid cooling.
- ☐ **Secondary hardening** occurs in high-alloy steels (Cr, Mo, V) at 500–600 °C due to alloy carbide precipitation.
- ☐ **Alloying** delays softening, raises activation energy, mitigates embrittlement.
- ☐ **Low temper (150–250 °C)** → tools, blades, chisels (max hardness).
- ☐ **High temper (550–650 °C)** → gears, shafts, springs (max toughness).
- ☐ **Always specify units**: Temperature in **K**, time in **hours** for HJP.
- ☐ **Cooling:** Usually air-cool; oil/water quench from tempering temperature for alloy steels to prevent Type II embrittlement.
- ☐ Tempering is **always the last step** in a hardening heat-treatment cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardenability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hardenability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Hardenability** is defined as the ability of a steel to form martensite (i.e., to be hardened by quenching) to a specified depth below the surface when subjected to a standardized cooling/quenching operation. It is a **bulk property** of the steel that describes the *depth of hardening* achievable under a given set of quenching conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardenability vs. Hardness — The Most Confused Pair in Heat Treatment**
>
> | Property | What it measures | Unit | Scope |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Hardness** | Resistance of a *single point* on the surface to plastic deformation/indentation | HRC, HV, BHN | Local / Surface property |
> | **Hardenability** | Ability of the steel to harden to a *given depth* below the surface | Depth (mm) / Jominy distance (in.) | Bulk / Through-thickness property |
>
> *A steel can have very high hardness (say, 65 HRC) but very poor hardenability (hardens only up to 2 mm), or vice versa.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hardenability is quantified as:
- The **distance from the quenched end** (in the Jominy end-quench test) up to which a specified hardness (typically 50% martensite, i.e., 50% M) is retained.
- Or equivalently, as the **Critical Diameter (D<sub>c</sub>)** — the largest bar diameter that will through-harden (produce 50% martensite at the center) in a given quench medium.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wetting a Sponge" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — The Diya (Lamp) Wicking Analogy**
>
> Imagine pouring oil onto the flat top of a *deep cotton wick of a traditional Kerala diya*. The oil is like the quenching "cold front."
> - **Hardness** = how *sticky/stiff* the wet fibers become (a local property).
> - **Hardenability** = how *deep* the oil front travels down the wick.
> - A thin wick of coarse cotton → oil races down easily (high hardenability).
> - A thick wick of tightly-twisted fibers → oil is stopped near the top (low hardenability).
>
> The *quench medium* is like the oil — water is "thin" and penetrates fast; oil is "thick" and slow. The *steel chemistry* controls the wick's "texture" — alloying elements dramatically slow the cooling and let the martensite form deep.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard test specimen** for hardenability: cylindrical bar of **25.4 mm (1 inch)** diameter and **101.6 mm (4 inches)** length (ASTM A255).
- **Standard hardness reference** for "fully hard": 50% martensite + 50% pearlite mixture hardness at any carbon level.
- **H-value (Severity of Quench, Grossmann's number)**: a dimensionless number characterizing the *heat-extraction capability* of a quenchant.
- **Conversion relationship**: every Jominy distance can be correlated to a cooling rate at the centre of a bar of a given diameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of Hardenability

The phenomenon of hardenability stems directly from the **Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) diagram** of the steel:

1. **At the surface** of a quenched part, the cooling rate is extremely high. It crosses the CCT curve's "nose" far to the left → the austenite (γ) avoids the pearlite/bainite transformation and transforms to **martensite (α′)**.
2. **At the center** of a thick part, the cooling rate is lower. The CCT curve is crossed to the right of the nose → partial transformation to pearlite/bainite occurs *before* the Ms temperature is reached.
3. The **depth at which 50% martensite is obtained** defines the practical hardening limit — the *hardenability depth*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A steel is said to have **good hardenability** if its CCT curve's nose is *shifted to the right* (longer transformation time at the nose). This is achieved primarily through **alloying elements** (Cr, Ni, Mo, Mn, B, V, Si).

## 2.2 The Jominy End-Quench Test (ASTM A255)

### 2.2.1 Specimen & Procedure
- A round bar specimen (⌀ 25.4 mm × 101.6 mm) is **austenitized** at the proper temperature (e.g., 845 °C for plain-carbon steels).
- It is then transferred rapidly to the **Jominy fixture**, where a jet of water (at ~24 °C) impinges on the **bottom face only**.
- After the bar cools completely, **two parallel flats** are ground along its length (0.4 mm deep), and **Rockwell C hardness** is measured at intervals of **1/16 inch** from the quenched end.

### 2.2.2 Reading the Jominy Curve

A **Jominy curve** plots **Hardness (HRC) vs. Jominy distance (inches from quenched end)**.

- **At Jominy distance = 0 (quenched end):** cooling rate ≈ **600 °C/s** → almost 100% martensite → maximum hardness (governed by C content).
- **At large Jominy distances (say, 30 mm = 1.18 in.):** cooling rate ≈ **2 °C/s** → slow enough for full pearlite transformation.
- The **inflection region** of the curve is where the structure transitions from 100% martensite to pearlite + ferrite.

### 2.2.3 Reading 50% Martensite Hardness

The hardness corresponding to 50% martensite depends on **carbon content** (Table 1 — must be memorized for KTU):

| % C in Steel | HRC for 100% Martensite | HRC for 50% Martensite |
|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | 41 | 32 |
| 0.30 | 51 | 39 |
| 0.40 | 58 | 44 |
| 0.50 | 62 | 49 |
| 0.60 | 64 | 52 |
| 0.80 | 65 | 55 |
| 1.00 | 65 | 57 |

## 2.3 Grossmann's Hardenability Method (Critical Diameter Approach)

### 2.3.1 Definitions
- **Critical Diameter (D<sub>c</sub>):** The largest diameter of a *round bar* of a given steel that, when quenched in a *given medium*, will form **50% martensite at the centre** of the bar.
- **Ideal Critical Diameter (D<sub>i</sub>):** The critical diameter under a *theoretical, infinitely severe quench* (H → ∞). This represents the **inherent hardenability** of the steel independent of the quench medium.

### 2.3.2 The Master Equation (Grossmann Equation)

The relationship between D<sub>c</sub>, D<sub>i</sub>, and the quenching severity is given by:

$$D_{c} \;=\; D_{i} \times f(H)$$

where:
- $D_{i}$ = **ideal critical diameter** (mm or in.)
- $D_{c}$ = **actual critical diameter** in the chosen quenchant (mm or in.)
- $f(H)$ = **severity-of-quench factor** (dimensionless, $0 < f(H) \leq 1$)

When $H \to \infty$ (ideal quench), $f(H) = 1$ and $D_{c} = D_{i}$.

### 2.3.3 Standard H-Values for Common Quenchants

| Quenchant | Severity (H-value) | f(H) factor |
|---|---|---|
| **Brine (10% NaCl)** | 5.00 | 0.821 |
| **Caustic soda (10% NaOH)** | 4.00 | 0.745 |
| Water (still, 18 °C) | 1.00 | 0.375 |
| Water (still, 50 °C) | 0.50 | 0.225 |
| **Mineral oil (still)** | 0.25 | 0.125 |
| **Mineral oil (circulated)** | 0.40 | 0.190 |
| Air (still) | 0.02 | 0.013 |
| Air (blown) | 0.05 | 0.030 |
| **Ideal quench** | ∞ | 1.000 |

> [!NOTE]
> The *water-to-oil* drop in H is the engineering reason why oil quenching is the standard for through-hardening alloy steels — it gives slower, more uniform cooling, reducing **quench cracking and distortion**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU 2024 ESE/IA examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{c} = D_{i} \cdot f(H)$ | Grossmann master equation | mm |
| 2 | $D_{i} = D_{c} / f(H)$ | Reverse calculation of ideal diameter | mm |
| 3 | $f(H) = D_{c} / D_{i}$ | Severity factor from test data | — |
| 4 | $J_{D} = f(D)$ | Conversion: Jominy distance ↔ bar cooling rate | — |
| 5 | $\%M = f(\log CR,\ C\%)$ | Martensite fraction from cooling rate | — |
| 6 | $D_{i}^{\text{mult}} = D_{i} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} f_{i}$ | Multi-alloy $D_i$ estimation (Grossmann multiplying factors) | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization target for KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. The Grossmann equation $D_{c} = D_{i} \cdot f(H)$.
> 2. The 50%-M hardness values for at least 0.4 %C and 0.6 %C steels.
> 3. H-values for water, oil, brine, and ideal quench.
> 4. The Jominy specimen dimensions (1 in. × 4 in.) and the standard cooling rate at the quenched end (~600 °C/s).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hardenability is **THE most critical heat-treatment design parameter** for:
- **Automotive gears (e.g., EN36, EN353):** gear teeth must harden to the root without through-hardening the whole gear (case + core). Hardenability dictates the choice between case hardening and through hardening.
- **Aircraft landing gear, axles, crankshafts** (e.g., AISI 4340, 300M): require *full martensite to the center* — high D<sub>i</sub> steels are mandatory.
- **Cutting tools:** high-carbon, low-hardenability steels (e.g., W1) — only surface hardens, retains tough core.
- **Cold-work dies:** air-hardening tool steels (e.g., A2, D2) have *medium* hardenability so they can be quenched in still air, minimizing distortion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cooling-Rate Equivalent of a Jominy Distance

A key analytical result: a position **J (in sixteenths of an inch)** from the quenched end of a Jominy bar is equivalent (in cooling rate) to a point at the **center of a round bar of some equivalent diameter D (in inches) quenched in ideal water**. This is the **Lamont equivalent diameter concept**.

For a Jominy distance $J$ (in inches), the equivalent bar diameter in ideal water is empirically given by:

$$D_{\text{eq}} \;=\; 0.35 \cdot J^{0.65} \quad \text{(in inches)}$$

Or in millimetres (with $J$ in mm):

$$D_{\text{eq}} \;=\; 1.713 \cdot J^{0.65} \quad \text{(in mm)}$$

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Form

This relationship is empirical (fitted to experimental data), but its origin lies in the similarity of **Fourier's heat-flow solution** for a semi-infinite body (Jominy end) and a finite cylinder (round bar). Both share the same dimensionless Biot number analysis:

> The Jominy quenched end behaves as a semi-infinite solid → cooling rate is governed by surface heat flux only.
> The centre of a cylindrical bar → cooling rate is governed by radial conduction to a cooled surface.

**Step 1:** For the Jominy bar's quenched end, the surface temperature instantly drops to $T_{\text{quench}}$. The temperature at depth $x$ and time $t$ (semi-infinite solid solution) is:

$$T(x,t) \;=\; T_{s} + (T_{0} - T_{s}) \, \mathrm{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity, $T_{0}$ is the austenitizing temperature, and $T_{s}$ is the surface temperature.

**Step 2:** The **instantaneous cooling rate** at depth $x$ is:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} \;=\; \frac{(T_{0} - T_{s}) \cdot x}{2\sqrt{\pi}\, t^{3/2}\sqrt{\alpha}} \cdot e^{-x^{2}/(4\alpha t)}$$

**Step 3:** For the **centre of a cylindrical bar** of radius $R$ with surface suddenly quenched to $T_{s}$, an analogous solution (using Bessel functions) gives a much more complex expression. The empirical Lamont correlation maps the two by fitting to a power law of the form $D = a J^{b}$.

**Step 4:** The exponents 0.65 (in the empirical equation) and 0.5 (theoretically expected from a 1-D diffusion model) differ because real bars have finite length, finite Biot number, and the water jet does not provide truly infinite surface heat transfer.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Full KTU-Style Solution)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern Style]**
> AISI 1040 steel (0.40 % C) has an ideal critical diameter of 27 mm. It is quenched in:
> (a) Brine (H = 5.0)
> (b) Mineral oil (H = 0.25)
> Calculate the actual critical diameter in each case and comment on the suitability for a 50 mm-diameter gear-shaft.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Identify Data

- Steel: AISI 1040 (0.40 % C) → $D_{i} = 27$ mm (given).
- Severity for brine: $H = 5.0 \Rightarrow f(H) = 0.821$.
- Severity for oil: $H = 0.25 \Rightarrow f(H) = 0.125$.
- Target component: gear-shaft of diameter 50 mm.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Apply Grossmann's Equation for Brine

$$D_{c}^{\text{brine}} \;=\; D_{i} \times f(H_{\text{brine}})$$

$$\boxed{D_{c}^{\text{brine}} \;=\; 27 \times 0.821 \;=\; 22.17 \;\text{mm}}$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Apply Grossmann's Equation for Oil

$$D_{c}^{\text{oil}} \;=\; D_{i} \times f(H_{\text{oil}})$$

$$\boxed{D_{c}^{\text{oil}} \;=\; 27 \times 0.125 \;=\; 3.375 \;\text{mm}}$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

| Quench | $D_{c}$ (mm) | Required (50 mm) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brine | 22.17 | 50 | **NOT** through-hardenable |
| Oil | 3.375 | 50 | **NOT** through-hardenable |

**Conclusion:** AISI 1040 *cannot* through-harden a 50 mm shaft in any common quench — the maximum achievable hardening is ~22 mm depth (brine). The steel is therefore suitable only for **surface-hardening operations** (carburizing, induction, or flame hardening) on the gear-shaft. For *full* through-hardening of a 50 mm shaft, an **alloy steel** with $D_{i} \geq 130$ mm (e.g., AISI 4340, $D_{i} \approx 200$ mm) is required.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Hardenability

| # | Factor | Effect on Hardenability | Quantitative Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Austenitic grain size** | Larger grain → **Higher hardenability** | One ASTM grain-size number increase: $D_{i}$ rises ~20 % |
| 2 | **Carbon content** | Increases up to ~0.8 %C, then saturates | Each 0.1 %C increase: $D_{i}$ rises ~15–20 % |
| 3 | **Alloying element (Mn)** | Strongly increases | 1 % Mn: $D_{i}$ multiplier ≈ 1.4–1.6 |
| 4 | **Alloying element (Cr)** | Strongly increases | 1 % Cr: $D_{i}$ multiplier ≈ 1.3–1.5 |
| 5 | **Alloying element (Ni)** | Moderately increases | 1 % Ni: $D_{i}$ multiplier ≈ 1.15–1.2 |
| 6 | **Alloying element (Mo)** | Strongly increases | 1 % Mo: $D_{i}$ multiplier ≈ 1.4–1.6 |
| 7 | **Microalloying (B)** | Strongly increases (even 0.001 %) | 0.001 % B: $D_{i}$ multiplier ≈ 1.4 |
| 8 | **Co** | **Decreases** hardenability | 1 % Co: $D_{i}$ multiplier ≈ 0.85 |
| 9 | **Austenitizing temperature** | Higher → more carbide dissolution → higher hardenability | Each 25 °C rise: $D_{i}$ increases ~10 % |
| 10 | **Austenitizing time** | Long hold → complete carbide dissolution → higher $D_{i}$ | Saturates after ~30 min for plain-C steels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Grossmann multiplying factor method** for alloy steels: $D_{i}^{\text{alloy}} = D_{i}^{\text{plain C}} \times f_{\text{Mn}} \times f_{\text{Cr}} \times f_{\text{Ni}} \times f_{\text{Mo}} \times \ldots$ — a high-yield KTU question type.
