# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hardenability Test Workflow

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the complete flow of hardenability assessment using the Jominy end-quench test, mapped as a process architecture (since the physical Jominy apparatus cannot be drawn in Mermaid directly).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Receive Steel Specimen] --> A1[Cut Round Bar 25.4 mm x 101.6 mm]
    A1 --> A2[Austenitize at 845 deg C for 30 min]
    A2 --> A3[Quench bottom face with water jet at 24 deg C]
    A3 --> A4[Cool specimen to room temperature]
    A4 --> A5[Grind two parallel flats 0.4 mm deep]
    A5 --> A6[Measure Rockwell C hardness at 1 by 16 inch intervals]
    A6 --> A7[Plot HRC vs Jominy distance curve]
    A7 --> A8[Read 50 percent martensite hardness from carbon table]
    A8 --> A9[Determine Jominy distance for 50 percent M]
    A9 --> A10[Read Ideal Critical Diameter Di from conversion chart]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Actual Critical Diameter Dc using Grossmann equation]
    A11 --> A12[Compare Dc with required bar diameter of part]
    A12 --> A13{Is Dc greater than required}
    A13 -- Yes --> A14[Steel SUITABLE for through hardening]
    A13 -- No --> A15[Steel UNSUITABLE use surface hardening or higher alloy steel]
    A14 --> A16[End Report]
    A15 --> A16
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Hardenability (Architecture Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0[Hardenability Influencing Factors] --> F1[Steel Chemistry]
    F0 --> F2[Thermal Treatment Variables]
    F0 --> F3[Quench Medium Severity]
    F0 --> F4[Microscopic Structure]
    F1 --> F1a[Carbon content]
    F1 --> F1b[Alloying elements Mn Cr Ni Mo V B]
    F1 --> F1c[Impurity Co decreases]
    F2 --> F2a[Austenitizing temperature]
    F2 --> F2b[Holding time]
    F2 --> F2c[Prior austenitic grain size]
    F3 --> F3a[Brine H 5.0]
    F3 --> F3b[Water H 1.0]
    F3 --> F3c[Oil H 0.25 to 0.40]
    F3 --> F3d[Air H 0.02 to 0.05]
    F4 --> F4a[Homogeneity of austenite]
    F4 --> F4b[Carbide dissolution completeness]
    F1a --> X0[Result Ideal Critical Diameter Di]
    F1b --> X0
    F1c --> X0
    F2a --> X0
    F2b --> X0
    F2c --> X0
    F3a --> Y0[Result Actual Critical Diameter Dc]
    F3b --> Y0
    F3c --> Y0
    F3d --> Y0
    F4a --> X0
    F4b --> X0
    X0 --> Z0[Engineering Decision Material Selection for Heat Treatment]
    Y0 --> Z0
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Hardness vs. Hardenability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Property Identification] --> H1{What is being measured}
    H1 -- Surface resistance to indentation --> H2[Hardness]
    H1 -- Depth of martensite formation --> H3[Hardenability]
    H2 --> H2a[Units HRC HV BHN]
    H2 --> H2b[Test Methods Rockwell Vickers Brinell]
    H2 --> H2c[Depends mainly on Carbon content]
    H3 --> H3a[Unit mm or Jominy distance in inches]
    H3 --> H3b[Test Method Jominy end quench or Grossmann]
    H3 --> H3c[Depends on alloying and grain size]
    H2c --> H2d[Example W1 water hardening 65 HRC at surface only]
    H3c --> H3d[Example AISI 4340 hardens to 100 mm depth]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**Differentiate between hardness and hardenability of a steel. Why is hardenability considered a more critical design parameter than hardness?** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Hardness** is a surface property — the resistance of a *single point* on the surface to localized plastic deformation, measured in units such as HRC, HV, or BHN. [1 Mark]
2. **Hardenability** is a *bulk property* — the ability of a steel to form martensite to a *depth* below the surface on quenching, measured in mm (critical diameter) or Jominy distance. [1 Mark]
3. Hardenability is more critical because it determines whether *the entire cross-section* of a component (e.g., a 50 mm shaft) can be hardened, which dictates the **fatigue strength, toughness, and wear resistance** of the part in service. A high-hardness, low-hardenability steel is unsuitable for thick sections. [1 Mark]

### Question A.2
**Name the standard specimen dimensions and the cooling rate at the quenched end used in the Jominy end-quench test for hardenability.** [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Specimen:** A round bar of diameter **25.4 mm (1 inch)** and length **101.6 mm (4 inches)**. [1 Mark]
2. **Quenching:** The bottom face is quenched by a vertical water jet at room temperature (~24 °C). [1 Mark]
3. **Cooling rate at the quenched end:** approximately **600 °C/s** (very high, approaching the ideal quench). [1 Mark]

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question B.1 — Choice A

**A. (a)** Explain the **Jominy end-quench test** in detail. How is the hardenability of a steel assessed from the resulting Jominy curve? Discuss with a neat sketch the typical shape of the curve for a plain-carbon steel and an alloy steel. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Test setup and procedure [2 Marks]:**
   - A standard cylindrical specimen (25.4 mm dia × 101.6 mm long) is austenitized at the correct temperature (e.g., 845 °C for plain-carbon steel, 870 °C for alloy steel).
   - It is rapidly transferred to the Jominy fixture, where a controlled water jet impinges on the **bottom face only**.
   - After complete cooling, two parallel flats (0.4 mm deep) are ground.
   - Rockwell C hardness is measured at **1/16-inch intervals** from the quenched end.

2. **Typical Jominy curve interpretation [3 Marks]:**
   - **Near the quenched end (J = 0–4 mm):** cooling rate is extremely high (≈ 600 °C/s at J=0). 100% martensite forms; hardness is maximum and is governed **only by carbon content** (not by alloying).
   - **At large Jominy distances (J > 25 mm):** cooling rate is very slow; the structure is fully pearlitic + ferritic, with very low hardness.
   - The **steepness of the drop** in the inflection region is the visual signature of hardenability:
     - Plain-carbon steel (e.g., 1040): **steep drop** → poor hardenability.
     - Alloy steel (e.g., 4340): **gradual drop, plateau extends far** → excellent hardenability.

3. **Assessing hardenability from the curve [2 Marks]:**
   - Read the **%C of the steel**, then from the standard table find the hardness corresponding to **50% martensite**.
   - On the Jominy curve, locate the Jominy distance at which the measured HRC equals this 50%-M value.
   - This Jominy distance, using the Lamont equivalent-diameter chart, is then converted to the **ideal critical diameter D<sub>i</sub>**.

---

**A. (b)** Discuss the various **factors affecting hardenability** of steels. What is the role of boron in enhancing hardenability? [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Chemical-composition factors [3 Marks]:**
   - **Carbon content:** up to 0.8 %, $D_i$ rises sharply; beyond 0.8 %C, $D_i$ remains nearly constant.
   - **Alloying elements (Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo, Si, V):** all **increase** hardenability by shifting the CCT nose to longer times. Effect order (strongest to weakest): Mo ≈ Mn > Cr > Ni > Si.
   - **Cobalt (Co):** the *only common element* that **decreases** hardenability.
   - **Boron (B):** even 0.001–0.003 % B significantly enhances hardenability by segregating to austenite grain boundaries and suppressing the nucleation of ferrite and pearlite.

2. **Thermal/structural factors [2 Marks]:**
   - **Austenitizing temperature:** higher temperature → more complete carbide dissolution → more carbon and alloy in solution → higher $D_i$.
   - **Austenitizing time:** longer time (up to ~30 min) gives more uniform austenite → higher $D_i$.
   - **Prior austenitic grain size:** larger grains → fewer grain-boundary nucleation sites for ferrite/pearlite → **higher** $D_i$. One ASTM grain-size increase ≈ 20 % rise in $D_i$.

3. **Role of boron — detailed mechanism [2 Marks]:**
   - Boron atoms segregate to austenite grain boundaries during austenitizing.
   - They lower the grain-boundary energy and reduce the nucleation rate of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite.
   - This effectively shifts the CCT nose to the right, *similar to a much larger addition of Mn or Cr*, but at a fraction of the cost.
   - Boron is "consumed" by combining with N as BN, so **Ti or Al is added** to "fix" the nitrogen and keep boron in solution.

### Question B.2 — Choice B (Internal Choice)

**B. (a)** Explain **Grossmann's method** of determining hardenability. Define the terms **critical diameter (D<sub>c</sub>)** and **ideal critical diameter (D<sub>i</sub>)**. State the master equation relating them. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Critical diameter (D<sub>c</sub>) [2 Marks]:**
   - D<sub>c</sub> is the largest diameter of a round bar of a *given steel* that, when quenched in a *given medium*, will produce **50% martensite at the centre** of the bar.
   - It is therefore a combined property of the steel *and* the quench.

2. **Ideal critical diameter (D<sub>i</sub>) [2 Marks]:**
   - D<sub>i</sub> is the maximum bar diameter that will produce 50% martensite at the centre under a *theoretically perfect* (infinite-severity) quench.
   - D<sub>i</sub> is a property of the steel *alone* and is independent of the quenching medium.

3. **Grossmann's master equation [2 Marks]:**

$$D_{c} \;=\; D_{i} \times f(H)$$

where $f(H)$ is the severity-of-quench factor (0 < f(H) ≤ 1) and $H$ is the dimensionless Grossmann severity number for the quenchant. Common H-values: brine = 5.0, water = 1.0, oil = 0.25–0.40, ideal = ∞.

4. **Procedure [1 Mark]:** A series of bars of progressively larger diameters are quenched in the chosen medium; the largest bar that shows 50% martensite at its centre is D<sub>c</sub>.

---

**B. (b)** A gear-shaft of diameter 80 mm must be **through-hardened** in **oil** (H = 0.30). The steel selected has an ideal critical diameter of 65 mm. Determine: **(i)** the actual critical diameter achievable in oil, and **(ii)** whether the steel is suitable. If not, suggest a remedy. [7 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data [1 Mark]:**
- $D_{i} = 65$ mm, $H_{\text{oil}} = 0.30$.
- From the H-to-f(H) table, $H = 0.30 \Rightarrow f(H) \approx 0.155$ (interpolating between 0.25 → 0.125 and 0.40 → 0.190, the value 0.30 gives ~0.155).

**Step 2 — Compute actual critical diameter [2 Marks]:**

$$D_{c} \;=\; D_{i} \times f(H)$$

$$D_{c} \;=\; 65 \times 0.155$$

$$\boxed{D_{c} \;\approx\; 10.1 \;\text{mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with required diameter [1 Mark]:**
Required bar diameter = 80 mm. Since $D_c \approx 10$ mm ≪ 80 mm, the steel is **not suitable** for through-hardening this shaft in oil.

**Step 4 — Remedy [3 Marks]:**
- *Option 1 (Material change):* Select an **alloy steel with a much higher $D_i$** (e.g., AISI 4340, $D_i \approx 200$ mm). For $D_c \geq 80$ mm, $D_i \geq 80/0.155 = 516$ mm — no commercial steel reaches this; therefore oil quench is **fundamentally unsuitable** for this size.
- *Option 2 (Quenchant change):* Switch to **water** (H = 1.0, f(H) = 0.375): $D_c = 65 \times 0.375 = 24.4$ mm — still not enough. Switch to **brine** (H = 5.0, f(H) = 0.821): $D_c = 65 \times 0.821 = 53.4$ mm — still less than 80 mm.
- *Option 3 (Practical engineering solution — recommended):* Use **surface hardening** — induction hardening, flame hardening, or **carburizing + quenching**. The 50 mm-deep case is hardened, while the tough core remains unhardened. This is the *standard industrial practice* for gear-shafts > 50 mm diameter.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. In a Grossmann numerical problem, students frequently **forget to apply the f(H) factor** and just write $D_c = D_i$. This costs 2 of 7 marks immediately.
> 2. Many students confuse **H-value with f(H)**. Remember: $H$ is the *quenchant property* (severity), and $f(H)$ is the *multiplier* used in the Grossmann equation. They are *not* the same number.
> 3. Always state the *units* (mm or in.) explicitly in the final answer box. Omitting units = −½ mark.
> 4. When the problem says "ideal quench," it means $H \to \infty$ and $f(H) = 1$; do **not** plug an infinite number into your calculator.
> 5. In 14-mark questions, the *engineering interpretation* (whether the steel is suitable) carries **at least 1–2 marks** — never end with just a numerical answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Hardenability ≠ Hardness.** Hardenability = depth of hardening; Hardness = surface indentation resistance.
- [ ] **Jominy test** specimen: ⌀ 25.4 mm × 101.6 mm; quenched end cooling rate ≈ **600 °C/s**.
- [ ] **Jominy curve** = HRC vs. Jominy distance in 1/16-inch increments. Steeper drop = poorer hardenability.
- [ ] **Grossmann equation** (must memorize symbol-by-symbol): $D_c = D_i \cdot f(H)$.
- [ ] **D<sub>i</sub>** = property of steel alone; **D<sub>c</sub>** = property of steel + quench medium.
- [ ] **H-values** to memorize: Brine = 5.0; Caustic = 4.0; Water = 1.0; Oil = 0.25–0.40; Air = 0.02–0.05; Ideal = ∞.
- [ ] **50%-M hardness** for 0.4 %C steel ≈ 44 HRC; for 0.6 %C ≈ 52 HRC.
- [ ] **Alloying elements** (Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo, B, V, Si) **increase** hardenability. **Co** uniquely *decreases* it.
- [ ] **Boron** (0.001–0.003 %) is a powerful, low-cost hardenability booster — but Ti/Al must be added to "fix" nitrogen.
- [ ] **Coarse austenitic grain** ⇒ **higher** hardenability (fewer grain-boundary nucleation sites).
- [ ] **Larger bar diameter** ⇒ **lower** cooling rate at centre ⇒ **less martensite** ⇒ practical limit of through-hardening.
- [ ] **Lamont equivalent diameter** for Jominy-to-bar correlation: $D_{\text{eq}} \approx 1.713 \cdot J^{0.65}$ mm.
- [ ] For thick sections (> 50 mm) requiring through-hardness, always use **alloy steels with high D<sub>i</sub>** (e.g., 4340, EN24, EN353).
- [ ] For gear-shafts and large parts, **surface hardening** (carburizing, induction, nitriding) is often the only practical route.
- [ ] KTU 2024 Scheme **HOT question format**: "Calculate D<sub>c</sub> from D<sub>i</sub> and H" — practice at least 5 numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jominy End Quench Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Jominy End Quench Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardenability** is defined as the ability of a steel to form martensite when quenched, characterized by the depth of hardness penetration beneath the surface, and **NOT** by the actual hardness value attained. It is a property of the steel's composition and grain size, not of a particular piece.

The **Jominy End Quench Test** (also called the **Jominy Bar Test** or **Jominy Hardenability Test**) is a **standardized laboratory test** developed by **Walter E. Jominy and A.L. Boegehold in 1938** (ASTM A255, ISO 642, and IS 3848 standards) to determine the **hardenability** of ferrous alloys (steels). It involves quenching one end of a standardized cylindrical specimen with a controlled water jet and then measuring the resulting hardness along the length to construct a hardenability curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardenability vs. Hardness — The Critical Distinction (Board Exam Favourite!)**
> - **Hardness** = Resistance of a material surface to localized plastic deformation (a *property of the piece* after heat treatment).
> - **Hardenability** = Capacity of a steel to be hardened to a certain depth upon quenching (a *property of the alloy composition*).
> - High hardenability does NOT mean high hardness — it means martensite forms **deeper** into the cross-section.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice-Cream Stick Gradient" Model

Imagine you dip one end of a wooden ice-cream stick into a glass of ice-cold water. The dipped end cools *almost instantly* (think: ultra-fast cooling, like water-quenched steel), but the heat from the far end takes a long time to travel out through the wood (think: slow, air-cooling rate). If you measured the temperature every few millimeters along the stick right after dipping, you would record a **steep thermal gradient** — extreme cold at the wetted end, mild warmth at the dry end.

Now, if the wood were instead a piece of **austenitized steel**, the very tip dipped in water would transform entirely to **martensite (very hard)**, while the far end would cool so slowly that it would transform to **pearlite + ferrite (soft)**. The "Jominy test" does exactly this — it is a beautifully simple experiment that creates a **continuous spectrum of cooling rates along a single bar**, allowing metallurgists to determine, in one test, how the steel responds to cooling rates ranging from ~600°C/s down to ~5°C/s.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use one bar to study many cooling rates?**
> If we quenched separate bars in different media (water, oil, air), we would need *dozens* of bars and many furnaces. The Jominy test is brilliantly efficient: **one specimen = one furnace cycle = hundreds of effective cooling rates** sampled simultaneously.

## 1.3 Standard Specimen (KTU Board Specification)

| Parameter | Specification (Standard) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Shape** | Cylindrical bar with a small protruding "head" / lug |
| **Diameter** | **25.4 mm (1 inch)** |
| **Length** | **101.6 mm (4 inches)** |
| **Head / Lug** | 12.7 mm length × 28.6 mm diameter at one end |
| **Test surface** | Two parallel flats ground on opposite sides, **0.4 mm deep** below the original surface |
| **Hardness measurement points** | At 1.5 mm intervals from the quenched end, then progressively wider intervals up to 50 mm |

## 1.4 Why Hardenability Matters (Engineering Utility)

- **Gear, shaft, and bearing selection** — components require through-hardening (high hardenability steels) for high fatigue resistance.
- **Heat-treatment process design** — helps decide whether water, oil, brine, or polymer quenchant is needed.
- **Alloy development** — multiplying factors (Grossmann) help predict hardenability of new compositions.
- **Failure analysis** — Jominy data explains why some components fail due to **untempered martensite cores** or **soft pearlite centres**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Metallurgical Principle

The Jominy test is fundamentally a **controlled-velocity quench** that exploits **Fourier's law of heat conduction**. When water sprays onto the lug end, the heat flux (q) drawn out of the bar creates a steep temperature gradient:

$$q = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where **k** is thermal conductivity. As the cooling front propagates along the bar, the **local cooling rate** at any point $x$ mm from the quenched end is determined by the **transient 1-D heat conduction** in a semi-infinite cylinder. The empirical cooling rate at the centreline follows a relationship that is **steep near the quenched end** and **flattens out** as $x$ increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cooling rate at any Jominy position is **inversely related** to the distance from the quenched end. This single fact allows the Jominy curve to act as a **"cooling-rate generator"** for predicting phase transformations along the entire spectrum from martensite to pearlite.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Test Procedure (KTU Board-Critical)

1. **Specimen Preparation** — Machine a 25.4 mm × 101.6 mm bar with a lug of 12.7 mm length.
2. **Normalizing (Pre-treatment)** — Heat the bar to ~930°C, air cool. *This ensures a uniform, fine-grained microstructure before testing.*
3. **Austenitizing** — Re-heat to the proper austenitizing temperature (typically **830–870°C** for plain-carbon and low-alloy steels) and hold for 30–45 minutes to ensure **complete austenitization** and **carbide dissolution**.
4. **Transfer to Fixture** — Within **5 seconds**, place the bar in the Jominy fixture with the lug-end down, centred above a **water jet of 12.7 mm diameter column, 65 mm height of free water**.
5. **Quench** — Water at ~24°C sprays ONLY on the bottom face of the lug. Quench for **at least 10 minutes** until the bar is fully cold.
6. **Flat Grinding** — Grind two parallel flats **0.4 mm deep** on opposite sides along the entire bar length (this removes the decarburized surface and provides a flat surface for hardness indentation).
7. **Hardness Survey** — Measure **Rockwell C hardness (HRC)** at 1.5 mm, 3 mm, 5 mm, 7 mm, 9 mm, 11 mm, 13 mm, 15 mm, 20 mm, 25 mm, 30 mm, 35 mm, 40 mm, 45 mm, 50 mm from the quenched end. (Spacing increases with distance because the hardness gradient flattens.)
8. **Plotting** — Plot **HRC (Y-axis) vs. Distance from Quenched End in mm (X-axis)** → this curve is the **Jominy Hardenability Curve**.

## 2.3 Typical Cooling Rates at Various Jominy Positions

| Jominy Position (mm) | Cooling Rate at 700°C (°C/s) | Approximate Microstructure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1.5** | ~600 | 100% Martensite |
| **6** | ~150 | Martensite + minor bainite |
| **12** | ~70 | Bainite + martensite mix |
| **25** | ~20 | Bainite dominant |
| **50** | ~6 | Bainite + fine pearlite |
| **75** | ~3 | Coarse pearlite + ferrite |

> [!TIP]
> The cooling rate at **700°C** (just above the nose of the TTT diagram) is the most diagnostic — this is where the **pearlite nose** sits. Faster rates skip the nose and form martensite; slower rates form pearlite/bainite.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Numerical & ESE question on hardenability.**

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $D_I = D \cdot \frac{H}{H_{\text{actual}}}$ | **Grossmann's Critical Diameter Equation** — relates ideal critical diameter $D_I$ to actual bar diameter $D$, ideal quench severity $H = \infty$, and actual $H$ of the medium |
| 2 | $\log D_I = \frac{\log \left(D \cdot H\right) - \log H_{\text{actual}}}{1}$ | Rearranged form of Grossmann equation for numerical substitution |
| 3 | $H_{\text{water (still)}} = 1.0$ | Reference quench severity — still water bath |
| 4 | $H_{\text{water (agitated)}} = 1.5$ | Mildly agitated water |
| 5 | $H_{\text{oil (still)}} = 0.20$ | Standard still mineral oil |
| 6 | $H_{\text{oil (agitated)}} = 0.35$ | Agitated oil |
| 7 | $H_{\text{brine}} = 2.0$ | Salt/brine water quench |
| 8 | $H_{\text{air blast}} = 0.05$ | Forced air quench |
| 9 | $D_I^{\text{(steel)}} = D_I^{\text{(base)}} \cdot \prod f_i$ | **Grossmann's Multiplying Factor Equation** — $f_i$ is the multiplying factor for each alloying element $i$ |
| 10 | $D_J \approx \text{Jominy equivalent round bar diameter}$ | Empirical chart conversion from Jominy distance $D_J$ to equivalent bar diameter |

## 2.5 Grossmann's Multiplying Factors for Common Alloying Elements (Key KTU Data)

| Element | % Present (Mass) | Multiplying Factor (approx.) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| C | 0.6 – 0.8 | 0.18 – 0.22 (acts as base $D_I$) |
| Mn | 0.6 | 1.67 |
| Mn | 1.0 | 2.30 |
| Cr | 1.0 | 1.73 |
| Cr | 2.0 | 2.30 |
| Ni | 1.0 | 1.50 |
| Ni | 3.0 | 1.97 |
| Mo | 0.3 | 1.67 |
| Mo | 1.0 | 2.20 |
| V | 0.2 | 1.50 |
| Si | 1.0 | 1.20 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Application**: To predict the hardenability of a new alloy, take the base ideal critical diameter for plain carbon steel of the same carbon content and **multiply** by the factor for each alloying element present.

## 2.6 Why the Jominy Test is Preferred Over Other Methods

| Method | Drawback |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hardness survey on a quenched round bar** | Requires destructive sectioning; only one cooling rate tested per bar |
| **TTT (Time-Temperature-Transformation) diagram** | Requires isothermal hold at each temperature — laborious |
| **Grossmann's quench-severity test on multiple bars** | Needs 10–20 specimens to map a single steel |
| **Jominy End Quench Test** | **One bar, one heat treatment, ~50 hardness readings = complete hardenability spectrum** |

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive industry**: Selection of 41xx, 43xx, 86xx, 93xx series steels for through-hardened gears, camshafts, and axles.
- **Tool steel industry**: Predicts cross-section depth of HRC 60+ zones.
- **Aerospace**: Critical for landing gear (e.g., 300M, 4340 steels) where through-thickness tensile strength of 1800 MPa is mandatory.
- **Forensic failure analysis**: Confirms whether a component's core remained unhardened due to low hardenability + poor quench.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Cooling Rate and Jominy Distance

The cooling rate at a point $x$ from the quenched end of a semi-infinite cylinder of radius $R$, subject to a convective boundary with heat transfer coefficient $h$, is given by the **transient heat conduction solution**. The empirical fit (developed by French & Klier, 1958) for the cooling rate at 700°C is:

$$\log R_c(x) = -1.413 \log x + 4.802 \quad \text{(for } x \text{ in mm, } R_c \text{ in °C/s)}$$

### Step-by-step demonstration at $x = 5$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } \log R_c(5) &= -1.413 \times \log(5) + 4.802 \\
\text{Step 2: } \log(5) &= 0.69897 \\
\text{Step 3: } -1.413 \times 0.69897 &= -0.9877 \\
\text{Step 4: } \log R_c(5) &= -0.9877 + 4.802 = 3.8143 \\
\text{Step 5: } R_c(5) &= 10^{3.8143} \approx 652 \text{ °C/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: At just 5 mm from the quenched end, the steel cools at ~652 °C/s — far faster than the critical cooling rate of plain-carbon steel (~100 °C/s), so the structure is **100% martensite** in this region.

### Step-by-step demonstration at $x = 40$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } \log R_c(40) &= -1.413 \times \log(40) + 4.802 \\
\text{Step 2: } \log(40) &= 1.60206 \\
\text{Step 3: } -1.413 \times 1.60206 &= -2.2638 \\
\text{Step 4: } \log R_c(40) &= -2.2638 + 4.802 = 2.5382 \\
\text{Step 5: } R_c(40) &= 10^{2.5382} \approx 345 \text{ °C/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of the derivation:** The inverse-logarithmic relationship explains why the Jominy curve has a steep drop in hardness near the quenched end and a long flat tail. Each additional millimetre of distance multiplies the cooling rate by a smaller and smaller factor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Grossmann's Critical Diameter Equation

Starting from the definition of **ideal critical diameter** $D_I$ — the diameter of a round bar that would form **50% martensite at the centre** under an *infinitely severe* quench ($H = \infty$):

$$H = \frac{h}{2k} \cdot R \quad \text{(dimensionless quench severity)}$$

For a cylindrical bar of diameter $D = 2R$:

$$H = \frac{h \cdot D}{4k}$$

Rearranging for the bar diameter that would yield 50% martensite under the *actual* quench $H_{\text{actual}}$:

$$D = \frac{4k \cdot H_{\text{actual}}}{h}$$

But $D_I$ corresponds to $H \to \infty$, which is a theoretical limit. The empirical Grossmann relationship is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_I &= D \cdot \left( \frac{H}{H_{\text{actual}}} \right) \\
\log D_I &= \log D + \log H - \log H_{\text{actual}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example:

> **Problem**: A 50 mm diameter steel bar quenched in still water ($H = 1.0$) forms 50% martensite at the centre. Estimate $D_I$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } D_I &= 50 \times \frac{H_{\text{ideal}}}{H_{\text{actual}}} \\
\text{Step 2: } H_{\text{ideal}} &\approx \infty \quad \Rightarrow \text{ use Grossmann master curve: } D_I \text{ from } D, H \\
\text{Step 3: From Grossmann chart at } D = 50 \text{ mm, } H = 1.0 &\rightarrow D_I \approx 75 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Step 4: Verification via } \log D_I &= \log 50 + \log H - \log 1.0 \\
\log D_I &= 1.699 + 0 - 0 = 1.699 \\
D_I &= 50 \text{ mm (lower bound, since } H_{\text{ideal}} = \infty)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Plugging $H_{\text{ideal}} = \infty$ directly into the formula. You must read the value from Grossmann's master chart or use the reciprocal relationship correctly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Grossmann's Multiplying Factor Method

For alloyed steels, $D_I$ is computed by:

$$D_I^{\text{alloy}} = D_I^{\text{plain-C}} \times f_{\text{Mn}} \times f_{\text{Cr}} \times f_{\text{Ni}} \times f_{\text{Mo}} \times f_{\text{V}} \times f_{\text{Si}}$$

### Worked Example:

> **Problem**: Steel composition 0.4% C, 0.8% Mn, 1.0% Cr, 0.3% Mo. Plain-carbon base $D_I = 16$ mm. Find $D_I^{\text{alloy}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{Mn}}(0.8\%) &\approx 2.10 \\
f_{\text{Cr}}(1.0\%) &\approx 1.73 \\
f_{\text{Mo}}(0.3\%) &\approx 1.67 \\
D_I^{\text{alloy}} &= 16 \times 2.10 \times 1.73 \times 1.67 \\
&= 16 \times 6.066 \\
&\approx 97 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: Alloying multiplies the hardenability by **~6×** — the alloyed bar of 97 mm diameter can be through-hardened, while the plain-carbon version is limited to 16 mm.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Jominy Position ↔ Equivalent Round Bar Diameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This empirical conversion is **exam-favourite** — examiners love asking "A bar of X mm diameter is equivalent to which Jominy position?"

| Jominy Position (mm) | Equivalent Round Bar Diameter (mm, in oil) | Equivalent Round Bar Diameter (mm, in water) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1.5 | 5 | 7 |
| 5 | 10 | 12 |
| 10 | 15 | 20 |
| 15 | 20 | 27 |
| 25 | 30 | 40 |
| 40 | 50 | 65 |
| 50 | 65 | 80 |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb**: A Jominy position of $x$ mm corresponds approximately to a round bar of diameter $\approx 1.3 x$ mm (in oil) or $\approx 1.6 x$ mm (in water).

## 3.5 Critical Cooling Rate — Link to TTT Diagram

The **critical cooling rate** ($R_{cc}$) is the slowest cooling rate that **entirely avoids the pearlite nose** of the TTT diagram. It is defined as:

$$R_{cc} = \frac{700 - 550}{t_{\text{nose}} - 0}$$

where $700°C$ and $550°C$ bracket the nose, and $t_{\text{nose}}$ is the time at the nose.

> If the **local cooling rate at the centre of a bar** > $R_{cc}$ → martensite forms → through-hardened.
> If the local rate < $R_{cc}$ → pearlite nose is intercepted → soft core.

The Jominy test simply **moves a probe** along the bar, identifying the **exact distance** at which the local cooling rate equals $R_{cc}$. Beyond this distance, the structure is no longer fully martensitic.

## 3.6 Summary of Master Equations Used in KTU Numericals

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_c(x) &= 10^{-1.413 \log x + 4.802} \quad \text{°C/s at 700°C} \\
D_I &= D \cdot \left( \frac{H}{H_{\text{actual}}} \right) \\
D_I^{\text{alloy}} &= D_I^{\text{plain-C}} \cdot \prod f_i \\
\text{Jominy position} &\equiv \text{equivalent bar diameter via empirical chart} \\
\text{Martensite \%} &\text{ determined from TTT diagram and } R_c(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1, 3 Marks]**
>
> **Q: Differentiate between hardenability and hardness of a steel.**
>
> **Model Answer**:
>
> | Aspect | Hardness | Hardenability |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Definition** | Resistance of a material surface to localized plastic deformation | Ability of a steel to form martensite to a certain depth upon quenching |
> | **Type of property** | Property of the *piece* after heat treatment | Property of the *alloy* (composition & grain size) |
> | **Measured by** | Rockwell, Brinell, Vickers tests | Jominy end quench test, Grossmann method |
> | **Affected by** | Quench rate, % martensite, carbon content | Alloying elements, austenite grain size, carbon content |
> | **Indicates** | Surface wear resistance | Depth of hardening / through-thickness strength |
>
> High hardenability does not guarantee high hardness, but high hardness almost always requires sufficient hardenability.

> - **Bar length** = 101.6 mm (4 inches)

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Model Paper Q3, 14 Marks]**

### Question A:

**(a)** Describe the Jominy end-quench test for measuring the hardenability of steel. Sketch the test specimen, the apparatus, and a typical hardenability curve. List the standard test conditions. **  [7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 50 mm diameter steel bar is quenched in still water ($H = 1.0$) and forms 50% martensite at the centre. Using the Grossmann method, calculate the ideal critical diameter $D_I$ of the steel. If the same steel is quenched in oil ($H = 0.25$), what is the new bar diameter that would yield 50% martensite at the centre? **  [7 Marks]**

### Model Answer:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**:

**Test description:**
The Jominy end-quench test is a standardized method to evaluate the hardenability of steels, developed by W. E. Jominy in 1938 (ASTM A255).

**Standard specimen** (sketch essential):
- Cylindrical bar 25.4 mm dia × 101.6 mm length
- Lug of 12.7 mm length, 28.6 mm dia at the quenched end
- Two flat surfaces ground 0.4 mm deep for hardness indentation

**Apparatus**:
- A vertical specimen holder (fixture) supporting the bar with the lug-end down
- A water jet 12.7 mm in diameter, 65 mm column height, at 24°C, impinging on the lug face
- Austenitizing furnace (with controlled atmosphere)
- Rockwell C hardness testing machine

**Procedure** (4 key steps):
1. Normalize specimen at 930°C → air cool → ensures uniform starting structure
2. Austenitize at 850°C for 30 min → complete carbide dissolution
3. Transfer in less than 5 sec → end-quench with water for at least 10 min
4. Grind two flats → measure HRC from quenched end at 1.5 mm, 3 mm, 5 mm, … 50 mm

**Typical hardenability curve**:
- X-axis: Distance from quenched end (mm)
- Y-axis: Rockwell C hardness (HRC)
- Starts high (HRC 60–65) at quenched end, drops steeply, then flattens to HRC 20–30 at 50 mm
- Three zones: full martensite (high) → transition → soft pearlite/ferrite (low)

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution**:

**Step 1**: From Grossmann's chart, for $D = 50$ mm and $H = 1.0$, the **ideal critical diameter** is read as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_I &= D \cdot \left( \frac{H}{H_{\text{actual}}} \right) \\
\text{From chart, at } D &= 50 \text{ mm, } H = 1.0: \\
D_I &\approx 75 \text{ mm (or use Grossmann master curve interpolation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: For oil quench, $H_{\text{oil}} = 0.25$. We need the bar diameter $D_{\text{oil}}$ that will give 50% martensite at the centre, which is by definition $D_I$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_I &= D_{\text{oil}} \cdot \left( \frac{H}{H_{\text{oil}}} \right) \\
75 &= D_{\text{oil}} \cdot \left( \frac{1.0}{0.25} \right) \\
D_{\text{oil}} &= 75 \times 0.25 = 18.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: A bar of only ~18.75 mm diameter in oil will form 50% martensite at the centre, compared to 50 mm in water. **Oil quenching is far less effective at producing deep hardening** — illustrating why water-quenched carbon steels are used for thin sections, while alloy steels in oil are used for thick sections.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q4, 14 Marks]**

### Question B:

**(a)** What are the limitations of plain-carbon steels for through-hardening? Explain how the addition of alloying elements improves hardenability, citing the Grossmann multiplying factor method with a suitable example. **  [7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 0.4% C, 1.0% Mn, 1.5% Cr, 0.4% Mo steel has a plain-carbon base ideal diameter $D_I^{\text{base}} = 16$ mm. Compute the alloyed steel $D_I$ and comment on the size of round bar that can be through-hardened in oil ($H = 0.25$) and water ($H = 1.0$). **  [7 Marks]**

### Model Answer:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**:

**Limitations of plain-carbon steels for through-hardening:**

1. **High critical cooling rate** ($R_{cc} \approx 100$–$200$ °C/s) — only water or brine quench can suppress pearlite formation
2. **Shallow hardening** — only the surface hardens; the core remains soft pearlite/ferrite
3. **Quench-cracking and distortion** — high thermal stresses due to aggressive water quench
4. **Section-size limitation** — through-hardening only in bars of 10–15 mm diameter
5. **Toughness poor in as-quenched martensite** — requires tempering, which further reduces hardness

**How alloying elements improve hardenability**:
- Alloying elements (Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo, V, Si) **shift the TTT nose to the right** — i.e., they increase the time available for the austenite to transform, so slower quench rates can still avoid pearlite.
- They form stable carbides that resist dissolution and grain growth, preserving fine austenite grain size.
- Grossmann's method quantifies this via the multiplying factor equation:

$$D_I^{\text{alloy}} = D_I^{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{Mn}} \times f_{\text{Cr}} \times f_{\text{Ni}} \times f_{\text{Mo}} \times f_{\text{V}} \times f_{\text{Si}}$$

**Worked example (cited)**: A 0.4% C steel has $D_I^{\text{base}} = 16$ mm. Adding 1.0% Mn, 1.5% Cr, 0.4% Mo:
- $f_{\text{Mn}}(1.0) = 2.30$
- $f_{\text{Cr}}(1.5) \approx 2.00$
- $f_{\text{Mo}}(0.4) \approx 1.80$

$$
D_I^{\text{alloy}} = 16 \times 2.30 \times 2.00 \times 1.80 = 16 \times 8.28 = 132.5 \text{ mm}
$$

This 8.3× improvement means the steel can be through-hardened in sections **8 times larger** than the plain-carbon version.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution**:

**Step 1 — Multiplying factors** (from standard table):
- $f_{\text{Mn}}(1.0\%) = 2.30$
- $f_{\text{Cr}}(1.5\%) = 2.00$
- $f_{\text{Mo}}(0.4\%) = 1.80$

**Step 2 — Compute alloyed $D_I$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_I^{\text{alloy}} &= D_I^{\text{base}} \times f_{\text{Mn}} \times f_{\text{Cr}} \times f_{\text{Mo}} \\
&= 16 \times 2.30 \times 2.00 \times 1.80 \\
&= 16 \times 8.28 \\
&= 132.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum through-hardening diameter in oil ($H = 0.25$)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\max}^{\text{oil}} &= D_I \times \left( \frac{H_{\text{actual}}}{H} \right) \\
&= 132.5 \times 0.25 \\
&= 33.1 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum through-hardening diameter in water ($H = 1.0$)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\max}^{\text{water}} &= 132.5 \times 1.0 \\
&= 132.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment**: The alloy steel can be **through-hardened in oil up to ~33 mm diameter** and **in water up to ~132 mm diameter**. This is why alloy steels are preferred for gears, axles, and large machinery components — they achieve deep hardening in **mild oil quench**, reducing distortion and quench-cracking risk while delivering uniform through-thickness hardness.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Jominy/Hardenability Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hardness with hardenability** — Examiners dock 2 marks if you write "high hardenability means high hardness."
> 2. **Forgetting to state specimen dimensions** — A sketch without dimensions loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the "distance from quenched end" axis label** — Always label X-axis as "Distance from quenched end (mm)" and Y-axis as "HRC" on any curve.
> 4. **Wrong H-value for still vs. agitated water** — Still water $H = 1.0$, Agitated $H = 1.5$. Don't mix them up.
> 5. **Plugging $H = \infty$ into Grossmann formula directly** — You must use the chart/table or invert the relationship.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify "Rockwell C" scale** — Always mention HRC, not generic "hardness."
> 7. **Confusing $D_I$ with $D_{\max}$** — $D_I$ is the *ideal* diameter (theoretical max), while $D_{\max}$ in a given medium is smaller.
> 8. **Missing the role of austenitizing time and temperature** — Examiners expect 30 min hold and 850°C as standard.
> 9. **Plotting a Jominy curve with linear Y-axis and forgetting the steep initial drop** — A "flat" Jominy curve is wrong.
> 10. **Saying "HRC 60" without specifying distance** — Hardness values are valid only at a stated Jominy position.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Jominy End Quench Test**

- **Definition**: Standardized test (ASTM A255) to measure the **hardenability** of steels via a single quenched bar.
- **Specimen**: 25.4 mm dia × 101.6 mm length cylindrical bar with 12.7 mm lug.
- **Procedure**: Normalize → Austenitize (850°C, 30 min) → End-quench (water jet, 24°C, 10 min) → Grind flats → Measure HRC every 1.5 mm up to 50 mm.
- **Output**: Jominy curve — HRC vs. distance from quenched end.
- **Hardenability ≠ Hardness**: Hardenability is *depth*; hardness is *value*.
- **Cooling rate at 700°C** ranges from ~600 °C/s at 1.5 mm to ~5 °C/s at 50 mm.
- **Critical cooling rate** ($R_{cc}$) — slowest rate that avoids the pearlite nose; determined from TTT diagram.
- **Grossmann's equation**: $D_I = D \cdot (H / H_{\text{actual}})$.
- **H values**: still water = 1.0, agitated water = 1.5, still oil = 0.20, agitated oil = 0.35, brine = 2.0, air = 0.05.
- **Multiplying factors**: $D_I^{\text{alloy}} = D_I^{\text{base}} \times \prod f_i$ where $f_i$ depends on % alloying element.
- **Key alloying effect**: Mn, Cr, Ni, Mo, V, Si all shift TTT nose to the right → increase hardenability.
- **Jominy position ↔ equivalent round bar diameter** (rule of thumb): $D_{\text{bar}} \approx 1.3 \times D_J$ in oil.
- **Three zones of Jominy curve**: full martensite (high) → transition → pearlite/ferrite (low).
- **Standard hardness measurements**: at 1.5, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 mm.
- **Why Jominy is preferred**: One bar, one quench → maps entire cooling-rate spectrum from 600 °C/s to 5 °C/s.
- **Engineering applications**: Gear steel selection (41xx, 43xx, 86xx), tool steel, aerospace alloys (300M, 4340), failure analysis.
- **Limitations**: Only for steels; not directly applicable to non-ferrous alloys; assumes standard 850°C austenitization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Hardening Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET205 - MATERIAL SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING (MECHANICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surface Hardening Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Hardening** is a category of metallurgical processes used to induce hardness, wear resistance, and fatigue strength on the **outer skin** of a ferrous (or sometimes non-ferrous) component, while deliberately retaining the **toughness and ductility of the inner core**. The resulting structure exhibits a hard *case* (outer layer) over a softer, tougher *core*.

Mathematically, the requirement is:
$$\text{Profile: } H(x)\Big\vert_{x=0 \text{ (surface)}} \gg H(x)\Big\vert_{x \to \infty \text{ (core)}}$$

where $H(x)$ is the hardness as a function of depth $x$ from the surface, and the **Effective Case Depth (ECD)** is defined by the depth at which hardness falls to a specific threshold (e.g., **50 HRC**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why surface hardening?**
> Most engineering failures (fatigue cracking, wear, pitting, scuffing, galling) initiate at the surface, not the core. A hardened skin resists these while a tough core absorbs impact loads. This is the *composite optimization principle* in tribology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: A Knight in Armour**
> Imagine a medieval knight. His body (the **core**) is soft flesh and bone — flexible and shock-absorbing, allowing him to move and survive blows. His armour (the **case**) is a hard, rigid shell that resists arrows, swords, and wear. He can fight all day because the *hardness is on the outside* and the *toughness is on the inside*. Surface hardening of steel works on exactly this principle: a hard skin + tough core = optimum service life.

Another simple analogy: think of a hardened **cricket bat handle** — the grip is soft (rubber, comfort) but the outer surface of the bat's blade is hard (resists ball impact). Same engineering philosophy.

### 1.3 Broad Classification of Surface Hardening Methods

| S.No | Category | Basic Mechanism | Typical Case Depth |
|------|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Thermal (Hardenability-based)** | Rapid heating of surface above Ac₃, followed by quenching — no chemistry change | 0.5 – 6 mm |
| 2 | **Thermochemical (Diffusion-based)** | Heating in a reactive medium → atoms (C, N, B) diffuse inward → chemical + microstructural change | 0.1 – 3 mm |
| 3 | **Mechanical (Cold-work based)** | Plastic deformation of surface → strain hardening + compressive residual stress | 0.1 – 1 mm |
| 4 | **Coating / Overlay** | Depositing a hard layer on top of the substrate | 5 µm – few mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Note for KTU 2024 Scheme:** The 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the first two categories (thermal and thermochemical). The other two are mentioned for completeness but carry lower weightage.

### 1.4 The Three "Hardness-Defining" Quantities (Must Memorize)

1. **Surface Hardness** — Hardness measured directly on the outer face (typically 58–65 HRC for hardened steels).
2. **Effective Case Depth (ECD)** — Perpendicular distance from surface to the location where hardness equals a defined limit (**most often 50 HRC**, per ISO 18203).
3. **Total Case Depth (TCD)** — Perpendicular distance from surface to the location where the microstructure / chemistry is indistinguishable from the core (e.g., where carbon equals core carbon in carburizing).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Category 1: Thermal Surface Hardening Methods

These rely on **austenitization + quenching** confined to a thin surface layer.

#### 2.1.1 Flame Hardening

- **Process:** An oxy-acetylene (or oxy-propane) flame is swept across the steel surface. Temperature rises above $A_{c3}$ (typically **850–950 °C**). Water spray follows immediately for quenching.
- **Equipment:** Oxy-fuel torch, water-quench ring, traverse mechanism.
- **Steels:** Medium-carbon steels (**0.4–0.6 % C**), some alloy steels.
- **Depth:** 0.5 – 6 mm depending on flame dwell time.
- **Merits:** Cheap, portable, suited for very large parts (e.g., crane wheels, large shafts).
- **Demerits:** Manual operation → inconsistent, surface oxidation, possible distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> The hardness obtained depends on (i) carbon content of steel, (ii) cooling rate, (iii) austenitizing temperature. Plain medium-carbon steel can reach **55–60 HRC**.

#### 2.1.2 Induction Hardening

- **Process:** The component is placed inside (or in proximity to) a **water-cooled copper coil** carrying high-frequency AC (1 kHz – 1 MHz). The alternating magnetic field induces **eddy currents** in the surface — by the **skin effect**, these currents are concentrated in a thin outer layer, which heats rapidly (within seconds). Quenching follows immediately (often by the same coil or a built-in spray).
- **Steels:** Medium-carbon, medium-alloy steels (e.g., **EN8, EN9, 4140, 4340**).
- **Frequency vs. Depth (critical relation):**

$$d_{\text{heated}} \approx K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\mu \cdot f}}$$

where:
- $d_{\text{heated}}$ = depth of heated layer (m)
- $\rho$ = electrical resistivity of steel (Ω·m)
- $\mu$ = magnetic permeability (H/m)
- $f$ = frequency of the AC supply (Hz)
- $K$ = geometric constant (typically 500 in practical units, yielding depth in mm)

| Frequency | Application | Typical Depth |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 – 10 kHz (low) | Deep heating, large shafts | 5 – 25 mm |
| 100 – 500 kHz (medium) | Gears, pins | 0.5 – 3 mm |
| 1 – 10 MHz (high) | Shallow, fine parts | 0.1 – 0.5 mm |

- **Merits:** Fast (seconds), clean, no decarburization, easily automated, repeatable.
- **Demerits:** High capital cost, requires accurately machined parts (uniform air gap), limited to conductive materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** "In induction hardening, the *only* variable the engineer truly controls to set the case depth is the **frequency of the inductor current**." This is the most tested point on this topic.

#### 2.1.3 Laser Surface Hardening

- **Process:** A high-power **CO₂ or Nd:YAG laser** (typically 1–10 kW) scans the surface. Only the top 0.1–1 mm reaches austenitization; quenching is **self-quenching** by the cold substrate mass.
- **Merits:** Very localized, minimal distortion, can harden specific spots (e.g., bearing seat of a shaft).
- **Demerits:** Very expensive equipment, requires absorptive coating (e.g., graphite) on shiny steel.

#### 2.1.4 Electron Beam Hardening

- Similar to laser hardening, but uses a focused beam of electrons in a vacuum. Used for very small, precise parts (e.g., valve seats).

### 2.2 Category 2: Thermochemical Surface Hardening

These rely on **diffusion of interstitial atoms** (C, N, B) into the surface at elevated temperature.

#### 2.2.1 Carburizing

- **Definition:** Diffusion of carbon into a low-carbon steel surface (**0.15–0.25 % C core**) at **850–950 °C**, in a carbon-rich environment, to produce a high-carbon surface (**0.8–1.1 % C**).
- **Quenching is required after carburizing** to form martensite in the case.
- **Methods:**

| Method | Medium | Typical Case Depth | Application |
|--------|--------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Pack Carburizing** | Solid — steel parts sealed in boxes with **charcoal + BaCO₃ energizer** | 0.5 – 4 mm | Low volume, heavy parts |
| **Gas Carburizing** | Gaseous — **methane, propane, endothermic gas (endo-gas)** | 0.5 – 4 mm | Mass production (e.g., gears) |
| **Liquid Carburizing** | Molten salt bath (e.g., NaCN → NaCNO) — also called **cyaniding** | 0.1 – 2 mm | Smaller parts |
| **Vacuum Carburizing** | Low-pressure hydrocarbon gas (e.g., C₂H₄) | 0.5 – 3 mm | High-end, no oxidation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Energizers** (BaCO₃, Na₂CO₃) in pack carburizing release CO₂ which reacts with the carbon source to form CO, which is the actual carburizing agent:
> $$\text{C (charcoal)} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$
> $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{C (charcoal)} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$$
> $$2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{C (atomic)} \rightarrow \text{Steel surface}$$

#### 2.2.2 Nitriding

- **Definition:** Diffusion of **nitrogen** into the surface of **pre-hardened (often alloy) steel** at **500–575 °C** (below $A_{c1}$).
- **Steels:** Alloy steels containing **Cr, Al, Mo, V, Ti** (the strong nitride formers). Examples: **EN41B (40 Cr Al Mo 6)**, Nitralloy 135.
- **Medium:** Ammonia gas ($\text{NH}_3$) — dissociates to give atomic N:
> $$2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{N (atomic)} + 3\text{H}_2$$
- **No quenching required** — case hardens on slow cooling.
- **Hardness:** Very high surface hardness (**> 1000 HV / 70+ HRC**), retained up to **500 °C** (better than carburizing).
- **Merits:** Low distortion (low temperature), no quench, very high surface hardness, good fatigue resistance.
- **Demerits:** Very long treatment time (**20–90 hours**), only effective on nitride-former alloy steels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Nitriding is a **sub-critical** process — temperature is *below* $A_{c1}$. The core does NOT re-austenitize. This is the reason there is no quenching step and no distortion.

#### 2.2.3 Carbonitriding

- Simultaneous diffusion of **carbon AND nitrogen** at **700–850 °C** in a gaseous atmosphere of $\text{CH}_4$ + $\text{NH}_3$.
- Lower temperature than carburizing → less distortion.
- Faster than nitriding.
- Case depth: 0.1 – 1 mm.

#### 2.2.4 Boriding (Boronizing)

- Diffusion of **boron** at **800–1000 °C** (solid, liquid, or gas medium).
- Forms **FeB + Fe₂B** boride layer.
- Extreme surface hardness (**1400–2000 HV**).
- Used for dies, agricultural parts.

#### 2.2.5 Chromizing, Aluminizing, Siliconizing

- Diffusion of **Cr, Al, Si** — primarily for **corrosion and oxidation resistance** rather than pure hardness. Mentioned for completeness in KTU Module 4.

### 2.3 Category 3 & 4: Brief Notes

- **Shot Peening:** Surface hammered by steel/ceramic shot → plastic deformation → **compressive residual stress** in surface, very effective for fatigue life (e.g., leaf springs, gears, aircraft wing panels).
- **Surface Rolling / Burnishing:** Similar principle, applied to shafts and bores.
- **Hard Chrome Plating, CVD, PVD, Thermal Spray, HVOF:** Hard coating deposited on the surface. Modern high-performance tools and aerospace parts.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most tested** on this topic in KTU ESE. Memorize with units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** | $$J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$ | $J$ = flux (kg/m²·s), $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $C$ = concentration |
| 2 | **Arrhenius Diffusivity** | $$D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ | $Q$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ = absolute temperature (K) |
| 3 | **Case Depth vs Time (parabolic)** | $$x \approx K\sqrt{t}$$ | $x$ = case depth, $t$ = time, $K$ = process constant (depends on T) |
| 4 | **Induction Skin Depth** | $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\mu \omega}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$$ | $\omega = 2\pi f$, gives current penetration depth |
| 5 | **Solution to Fick's 2nd Law (semi-infinite, constant surface concentration)** | $$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$ | erf = error function; $C_s$ = surface concentration, $C_0$ = initial bulk concentration, $C_x$ = concentration at depth $x$ |
| 6 | **Jominy equivalent depth of hardening** | Related to cooling rate, not an exact formula but uses Jominy curves | Used in flame / induction selection |
| 7 | **Nitriding growth rate (empirical)** | $$x \approx K\sqrt{t} \;\; \text{with } K = 0.1 \text{–} 0.3 \;\mu\text{m}/\sqrt{\text{h}} \text{ at } 500\text{–}550\;°\text{C}$$ | Used to estimate long treatment time |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, note that the **erf function** appears in the diffusion equation. It is defined as:
> $$\text{erf}(z) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^z e^{-u^2}\, du$$
> Common values: $\text{erf}(0) = 0$, $\text{erf}(1) = 0.8427$, $\text{erf}(0.5) = 0.5205$, $\text{erf}(2) = 0.9953$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive gears** → induction or carburized.
- **Crankshafts** → induction hardened journals.
- **Tappets & camshafts** → induction or laser.
- **Aircraft landing gear** → nitrided alloy steel.
- **Cutting tools, dies** → coated (CVD TiN/TiAlN) or borided.
- **Leaf springs** → shot peened.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> *(Detailed continuation emphasizing analytical depth, applicable mathematics, and the "Why / How" behind every step.)*

### 2.1 The Physics Behind Surface Hardening

All surface hardening processes are governed by **three fundamental phenomena** of materials science:

1. **Thermal Diffusion of Heat** — the rapid surface heating required for thermal methods (flame, induction, laser) is fundamentally a transient heat-conduction problem solved by the 1-D heat equation:
> $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$
> where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity (m²/s) of steel ≈ $8 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s at 900 °C.

2. **Mass Diffusion of Atoms** — thermochemical methods are governed by **Fick's Second Law**:
> $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$
> which is mathematically analogous to the heat equation. The analogous variables are: temperature $\leftrightarrow$ concentration; thermal diffusivity $\leftrightarrow$ mass diffusivity.

3. **Metastable Phase Formation (Martensitic Transformation)** — final hardness arises from quenching austenite to martensite, governed by the **TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) diagram**. The cooling rate at the surface must exceed the **critical cooling rate** to form martensite.

### 2.2 Why Surface Hardening Works — The "Why / How" Breakdown

> [!TIP]
> **Why the case hardens:** During rapid heating, only the outer layer crosses $A_{c3}$ and forms austenite. Upon quenching, this layer transforms to martensite (hard), while the core never crossed $A_{c1}$ and remains tough (ferrite + pearlite/bainite, depending on alloy).
>
> **How chemistry is engineered in thermochemical methods:** The steel is heated in an environment that supplies C, N, or B atoms. These atoms *diffuse* inward along a concentration gradient, enriching the surface. After quenching, the case becomes martensite of a different (higher carbon/nitrogen) chemistry → harder than the core.

### 2.3 Detailed Process-Parameter Comparison (Master Table)

| Parameter | Flame Hardening | Induction Hardening | Laser Hardening | Carburizing | Nitriding | Carbonitriding |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|------------------|--------------|------------|----------------|
| **Temperature (°C)** | 850 – 950 | 850 – 950 | 900 – 1100 (local) | 850 – 950 | 500 – 575 | 700 – 850 |
| **Heating Source** | Oxy-fuel flame | Induction coil | Laser beam | Furnace (gas/solid) | NH₃ furnace | Furnace (CH₄ + NH₃) |
| **Heating Time** | Seconds to minutes | 1 – 30 s | Milliseconds | Hours (4 – 12 h) | Long (20 – 90 h) | 1 – 4 h |
| **Quenching Required** | Yes | Yes | Self-quench | Yes (after) | No | Yes (after) |
| **Case Depth** | 0.5 – 6 mm | 0.5 – 25 mm | 0.1 – 1 mm | 0.5 – 4 mm | 0.1 – 1 mm | 0.1 – 1 mm |
| **Core Carbon** | 0.4 – 0.6 % | 0.4 – 0.6 % | 0.4 – 0.6 % | 0.15 – 0.25 % | 0.3 – 0.5 % | 0.2 – 0.4 % |
| **Surface Hardness (HRC)** | 55 – 60 | 55 – 62 | 55 – 62 | 58 – 63 | 70+ (> 1000 HV) | 58 – 62 |
| **Distortion** | High | Moderate | Very low | Moderate | Very low | Low |
| **Best for** | Large parts, repairs | Mass production, gears | Selective hardening | Gears, shafts | High-precision, hot parts | Moderate batch sizes |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick-Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below is the **consolidated, high-yield formula reference** — every KTU-asked equation for this topic.

| # | Equation | Use |
|---|---------|-----|
| 1 | $$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$ | Fick's 1st law — flux of diffusing atoms |
| 2 | $$D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)$$ | Arrhenius diffusivity — temperature dependence |
| 3 | $$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$ | Fick's 2nd law solution — case concentration profile |
| 4 | $$x = K\sqrt{t}$$ | Parabolic growth law (carburizing, nitriding) |
| 5 | $$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$$ | Induction skin (heating) depth |
| 6 | $$\dot{Q} = m \, c_p \, \frac{dT}{dt}$$ | Heating power requirement per unit mass |
| 7 | $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$ | Transient heat conduction (1-D) |
| 8 | $$\% \text{ Cold Work} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$ | Strain hardening (shot peening) |

> [!NOTE]
> In all diffusion problems, notice the **square-root-of-time** dependence of case depth. *Doubling the time does NOT double the case depth — it only increases it by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.* This is a **frequently tested KTU concept** in 3-mark questions.

### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- The **Fick's 2nd law solution** lets a heat-treatment engineer **predict** the case depth vs. time at a chosen temperature, before running the actual furnace cycle. It saves enormous cost.
- The **Arrhenius relation** tells us that *doubling the absolute temperature* can **multiply the diffusivity by 100× or more**, which is why carburizing at 950 °C takes hours, but analogous diffusion at 1200 °C would take minutes.
- The **skin depth formula** tells the induction engineer which frequency to select for a target case depth.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fick's Second Law Solution for Semi-Infinite Body with Constant Surface Concentration

This is the most important derivation in the thermochemical surface hardening section.

**Problem Setup:**
A steel of initial carbon concentration $C_0$ (mass fraction, throughout the bulk) is held at temperature $T$ for time $t$ in a carburizing atmosphere that maintains a constant surface carbon concentration $C_s$. Find the carbon concentration $C_x$ at depth $x$ from the surface.

**Governing PDE (Fick's Second Law, 1-D):**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Boundary Conditions (BCs):**

1. At $t = 0$ (initial condition), for all $x \geq 0$: $C(x, 0) = C_0$
2. At $x = 0$ (surface), for all $t \geq 0$: $C(0, t) = C_s$
3. As $x \to \infty$ (deep bulk), for all $t \geq 0$: $C(\infty, t) = C_0$

**Step 1: Non-dimensionalize.** Define a dimensionless concentration:

$$\theta = \frac{C_s - C}{C_s - C_0}$$

With this substitution, the BCs become $\theta(0,t)=0$, $\theta(\infty,t)=1$, $\theta(x,0)=1$, and the PDE is unchanged in form (since $D$ and the derivatives are preserved).

**Step 2: Use a similarity variable.** Because the system has a semi-infinite domain with a step BC in time, we use Boltzmann's similarity variable:

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

Apply chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} \cdot \left(-\frac{x}{4\sqrt{D} \, t^{3/2}}\right) = -\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}$$

$$\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2} = \frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = \frac{1}{(2\sqrt{Dt})^2} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} = \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the PDE:**

$$-\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}$$

Cancel $1/t$ from both sides:

$$-\frac{\eta}{2} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}$$

Rearrange to a 2nd-order ODE:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \, \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve the ODE.** Let $p = d\theta/d\eta$. Then:

$$\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta \, p = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta \, d\eta$$

Integrate:

$$\ln p = -\eta^2 + A \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = A' e^{-\eta^2}$$

Integrate again:

$$\theta = A' \int_0^\eta e^{-u^2}\, du + B = A' \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \text{erf}(\eta) + B$$

**Step 5: Apply BCs.**
- At $\eta = 0$ → $\theta = 0$ → $B = 0$.
- At $\eta = \infty$ → $\theta = 1$ → $1 = A' \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot 1$ → $A' = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}$.

**Step 6: Final solution:**

$$\theta = \text{erf}(\eta) = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

Back-substitute $\theta$:

$$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** To find the time $t$ to achieve a given case depth $x$ at a given concentration $C_x$, we evaluate the error function and solve for $t$:
> $$t = \frac{x^2}{4D \cdot [\text{erf}^{-1}(\cdot)]^2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Induction Heating Skin Depth

**Setup:** A flat, semi-infinite conductor is placed in a uniform alternating magnetic field $H_0 e^{i\omega t}$ parallel to its surface. Find how the induced current density $J$ decays with depth.

**Step 1: Maxwell's equation (Faraday's law):**

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

For 1-D variation in $x$ (depth) and field in $y$ direction, the curl reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial x} = i\omega \mu H$$

**Step 2: Ampère-Maxwell law (neglecting displacement current at low frequency):**

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

which gives:

$$\frac{\partial H}{\partial x} = \sigma E$$

**Step 3: Combine the two.** Differentiate the second equation w.r.t. $x$ and substitute the first:

$$\frac{\partial^2 H}{\partial x^2} = \sigma \frac{\partial E}{\partial x} = \sigma \cdot i\omega \mu H = i\omega \mu \sigma H$$

Define the **complex propagation constant**:

$$\gamma^2 = i\omega \mu \sigma \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \gamma = (1+i)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

**Step 4: Solve.** The general solution is $H = H_0 e^{-\gamma x}$. Since $\gamma$ has equal real and imaginary parts, this means both $H$ and $J$ decay with depth as $e^{-x/\delta}$ where:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\rho}{\mu \omega}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$$

because $\sigma = 1/\rho$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning:** $\delta$ is the depth at which the induced current density has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value. About **63% of the heating** occurs in the top layer of thickness $\delta$. By selecting frequency, the engineer controls the case depth.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Question:** A low-carbon steel gear (initial carbon $C_0 = 0.20\%$) is gas-carburized at 927 °C. The surface concentration is held at $C_s = 1.0\%$. The diffusivity of carbon in austenite at 927 °C is $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-11}$ m²/s. Estimate the time required to obtain a carbon concentration of $0.40\%$ at a depth of 0.5 mm.

**Step 1:** Compute the concentration ratio:

$$\frac{C_s - C_x}{C_s - C_0} = \frac{1.0 - 0.40}{1.0 - 0.20} = \frac{0.60}{0.80} = 0.75$$

**Step 2:** Set this equal to the error function:

$$\text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 0.75$$

**Step 3:** Look up the inverse error function: $\text{erf}^{-1}(0.75) \approx 0.8134$.

**Step 4:** Solve for $t$:

$$t = \frac{x^2}{4D \cdot [\text{erf}^{-1}(0.75)]^2}$$

Convert units: $x = 0.5\,\text{mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, so $x^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m².

$$t = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-11} \times (0.8134)^2}$$

$$t = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{2.645 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 9450 \;\text{s} \approx 2.62 \;\text{hours}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **2.6 hours** are required.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Last-Minute KTU Revision)

| Feature | Flame Hardening | Induction Hardening | Carburizing | Nitriding |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|--------------|------------|
| **Principle** | Heat + Quench | Heat + Quench | Diffusion of C | Diffusion of N |
| **Temp (°C)** | 850 – 950 | 850 – 950 | 850 – 950 | 500 – 575 |
| **Time** | Minutes | Seconds | Hours | 20 – 90 h |
| **Quench** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Core carbon** | 0.4 – 0.6 | 0.4 – 0.6 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.3 – 0.5 |
| **Hardness (HRC)** | 55 – 60 | 55 – 62 | 58 – 63 | 70+ (HV) |
| **Distortion** | High | Moderate | Moderate | Very low |
| **Cost per part (low vol.)** | Low | High | Moderate | High |
| **Cost per part (mass prod.)** | High | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Selectivity (localized)** | Possible | Easy | Difficult (whole furnace) | Difficult |
| **KTU 2024 weightage** | Medium | High | High | High |
