## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **flame hardening** and **induction hardening**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flame Hardening | Induction Hardening |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| Heat source | Oxy-acetylene / oxy-propane flame | High-frequency AC electromagnetic induction |
| Heating speed | Slower (seconds to minutes) | Very fast (1–30 s) |
| Case depth control | By flame dwell time (manual) | By **frequency of AC current** (precise) |
| Surface cleanliness | Some scale / oxidation possible | Clean — no oxidation (inert heating) |
| Operator skill | High (manual) | Low (automatable) |
| Suitable for | Large, heavy, irregular parts (e.g., crane wheels) | Mass-produced regular parts (e.g., gears, shafts) |
| Cost (low volume) | Low capital | High capital |
| Cost (high volume) | High (labour-intensive) | Low (automated) |

**A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Q:** What is **case depth** in carburizing? How is it controlled in practice?

**Model Answer:**
Case depth is the perpendicular distance from the surface to a location in the steel where a defined property (carbon content or hardness) reaches a specified limit. Two definitions are used:

1. **Total Case Depth (TCD):** depth at which carbon concentration equals the **core carbon** (e.g., 0.20 %).
2. **Effective Case Depth (ECD):** depth at which hardness falls to a defined limit — typically **50 HRC** (per ISO 18203).

**Control parameters in practice:**
- **Time** of carburizing: deeper case with longer time, but only as $\sqrt{t}$.
- **Temperature:** higher T → much faster (Arrhenius).
- **Carbon potential** of the atmosphere: higher surface $C$ → faster inward diffusion.
- **Steel composition:** alloying elements (Cr, Mo) reduce $D$, slowing case growth.
- **Quenching after carburizing** is required to convert the high-carbon case to martensite (otherwise case would be soft pearlite).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 style model paper]**

**(a)** Explain the principle, working, and applications of **induction surface hardening** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of frequency in controlling case depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **carburizing**? Explain **pack carburizing** and **gas carburizing** in detail. Mention their relative merits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER A(a) — Induction Surface Hardening

**Principle (2 marks):**
A high-frequency alternating current passed through a water-cooled copper coil generates a rapidly alternating magnetic field around the component. By **Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction**, **eddy currents** are induced in the steel. Due to the **skin effect**, these currents flow predominantly in a thin outer layer. The I²R losses in this layer heat it rapidly (within seconds) above the austenitizing temperature ($A_{c3}$). The coil itself is cooled by water, so the bulk of the steel remains cold and acts as a heat sink. Water (or polymer) quench is applied within milliseconds — the surface transforms to martensite.

**Working Procedure (2 marks):**
1. The component is positioned inside the inductor coil with a uniform air gap of 1.5–3 mm.
2. High-frequency AC is applied for 1–30 s.
3. Quench is applied (either by an integrated spray ring or by dropping the part into a bath).
4. The component is tempered if needed (180–200 °C).

**Frequency vs. Case Depth (2 marks):**
Skin depth:
$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$$
- **Low frequency (1–10 kHz):** $\delta$ is large → deep heating (5–25 mm) — used for large shafts.
- **Medium frequency (100–500 kHz):** $\delta$ ≈ 0.5–3 mm — for gears, pins.
- **High frequency (1–10 MHz):** $\delta$ ≈ 0.1–0.5 mm — for shallow cases on small parts.

**Applications (1 mark):**
Automotive crankshafts, transmission gears, cam lobes, splined shafts, bearing surfaces, piston pins.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER A(b) — Carburizing

**Definition (1 mark):**
Carburizing is a thermochemical surface hardening process in which **carbon** is diffused into the surface of a **low-carbon steel** (0.15–0.25 % C) at **850–950 °C** in a carbon-rich environment, to produce a high-carbon surface layer (0.8–1.1 % C). Quenching is required after carburizing.

**Pack Carburizing (3 marks):**
- The parts are packed in a steel box together with a **solid carbonaceous material** (charcoal, coke, bone meal) and an **energizer** (BaCO₃, Na₂CO₃ at 10–40 %).
- The box is sealed with fire clay, then placed in a furnace at 900–950 °C for 4–12 hours.
- Reactions:
> $$\text{BaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{BaO} + \text{CO}_2$$
> $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{C (charcoal)} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}$$
> $$2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{C (atomic)}$$
- The atomic C dissolves in austenite and diffuses inward.
- **Merits:** Cheap, simple, suitable for very large parts, no special equipment.
- **Demerits:** Slow, uneven heating, limited to batch production, energy-inefficient.

**Gas Carburizing (3 marks):**
- The parts are heated in a sealed furnace at 900–950 °C with a flowing **carburizing gas** — typically an **endothermic gas (endo-gas)** enriched with **methane (CH₄)** or **propane (C₃H₈)**.
- Reactions:
> $$\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{C (atomic)} \;\;\text{on the steel surface}$$
- The **carbon potential** of the gas is precisely controlled by dew-point measurement or IR CO₂ analysis.
- **Merits:** Uniform heating, controllable C potential, suited to continuous furnaces, faster than pack.
- **Demerits:** Requires gas generator, more capital-intensive, fire/explosion hazard.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 style model paper]**

**(a)** What is **nitriding**? Explain the process, mechanism, and the role of alloying elements. Why is nitriding done at sub-critical temperature? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a comparative account of **case hardening vs. through hardening**, and explain the practical importance of the **"hard case + tough core"** composite structure in engineering components. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER B(a) — Nitriding

**Definition (1 mark):**
Nitriding is a thermochemical surface hardening process in which **nitrogen** is diffused into the surface of a **pre-hardened alloy steel** at **sub-critical temperature (500–575 °C)** using **ammonia gas**, producing a hard nitride-rich surface (≥ 1000 HV / 70 HRC) **without any quenching step**.

**Process (2 marks):**
1. The pre-hardened (quenched + tempered) steel part is placed in a sealed furnace.
2. Ammonia gas (NH₃) is passed at 500–575 °C for 20–90 hours.
3. NH₃ dissociates at the steel surface: $2\text{NH}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{N} + 3\text{H}_2$.
4. Atomic N diffuses inward following Fick's laws.
5. Surface nitrides form: $\text{Fe}_2\text{N}$, $\text{Fe}_4\text{N}$, and (more importantly) **stable alloy nitrides** like CrN, AlN, MoN.
6. Slow cool in the furnace — no quench, minimal distortion.

**Role of Alloying Elements (2 marks):**
Plain carbon steels do NOT respond well to nitriding — the iron nitrides decompose at high temperature. Strong **nitride-formers** (Cr, Al, Mo, V, Ti) form extremely stable, finely dispersed nitrides in the case, producing the characteristic very high hardness and **retention of hardness up to 500 °C** (vs. ~200 °C for carburized steel). This is why **EN41B / Nitralloy 135** (containing ~1 % Al + Cr + Mo) is the classic nitriding steel.

**Why sub-critical temperature? (2 marks):**
Nitriding is performed **below $A_{c1}$** (723 °C) to:
- Avoid re-austenitization of the core → no phase change → **no distortion**.
- Keep the core's pre-hardened tempered structure intact.
- Allow very slow diffusion of N → precise, uniform case.
- Eliminate the need for a quench step.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER B(b) — Case Hardening vs Through Hardening

**Definitions (1 mark):**
- **Case hardening:** Produces a hard outer layer (case) over a relatively soft, tough interior (core).
- **Through hardening:** Hardens the **entire cross-section** uniformly (e.g., quenching a thin medium-carbon steel).

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Case Hardening | Through Hardening |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Hardness profile | Gradient: hard outside, soft inside | Uniform across section |
| Toughness of core | High (retained) | Lower (sacrificed for hardness) |
| Carbon of steel | Low (0.15–0.25 % C) for diffusion methods; medium for flame/induction | Medium / high (0.4–0.6 % C or alloy) |
| Quench | Yes (or not, e.g., nitriding) | Always yes |
| Distortion | Less (especially nitriding) | More (uniform quench shrinkage) |
| Cost | Higher (multi-step) | Lower (single step) |
| Size limitation | None | Limited to thin sections (hardenability constraint) |
| Typical applications | Gears, shafts, camshafts, bearings | Springs, blades, cutting edges (thin) |

**Why "Hard Case + Tough Core" is Optimum (3 marks):**
- **Wear resistance:** Hard surface resists abrasion, adhesive wear, and contact fatigue.
- **Fatigue resistance:** Hard surface (often with compressive residual stress from quenching or shot peening) resists crack initiation.
- **Impact resistance:** Tough core absorbs sudden shock loads without catastrophic brittle fracture.
- **Cost efficiency:** Only a thin layer is hardened — saves expensive alloying, energy, time.
- **Example:** A gear tooth has a hard contact surface (resists pitting/wear) and a tough root (resists bending fatigue fracture). Through-hardened gear would either be too soft (wears) or too brittle (breaks at root).
- **Failure analysis insight:** Most engineering failures initiate at the surface (≈ 80 % of fatigue cracks start at the surface). A hardened skin prevents or delays this initiation.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing case depth definitions** — examiners *will* deduct if you say "case depth is 0.5 mm" without specifying *total* or *effective* (50 HRC criterion).
> 2. **Forgetting that carburizing REQUIRES a subsequent quench** — many students describe the process and omit the quench. This is an automatic 1–2 mark loss.
> 3. **Stating that nitriding "does not need alloy steel"** — false. Plain carbon steels do not develop significant nitrided hardness. Always emphasize **Cr, Al, Mo**.
> 4. **Confusing induction "skin depth" with "case depth"** — skin depth is where eddy current density falls to 1/e; the actual hardened depth is somewhat larger (≈ 2–3 × skin depth).
> 5. **Wrong temperature for nitriding** — students often say "900 °C" or "1200 °C". It is **500–575 °C**.
> 6. **Skipping the diffusion equation** when asked to explain carburizing — KTU wants you to *quantitatively* mention $x = K\sqrt{t}$ or the error function.
> 7. **Not comparing carburizing vs. nitriding** when asked — even if the question is about only one, a comparison table adds a great impression in part (b).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision summary — Surface Hardening Methods (Module 4)**

- [x] **Two main categories** to master: **Thermal (no chemistry change)** and **Thermochemical (chemistry change by diffusion)**.
- [x] **Flame hardening:** cheap, manual, large parts; case depth 0.5–6 mm; quench needed.
- [x] **Induction hardening:** precise control of case depth via **frequency**; $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi \mu f)}$; very fast (seconds); automated; clean.
- [x] **Laser / electron beam hardening:** very localized, minimal distortion, expensive, self-quench by substrate.
- [x] **Carburizing:** carbon diffusion at **850–950 °C**, low-carbon steel, must be **quenched after**; case up to 0.8–1.1 % C.
- [x] **Pack carburizing** uses charcoal + BaCO₃; **gas carburizing** uses CH₄ / endo-gas; **vacuum carburizing** is the modern, clean variant.
- [x] **Nitriding:** nitrogen diffusion at **500–575 °C** (sub-critical); needs **alloy steel with Cr, Al, Mo**; **no quench**; surface ≥ 70 HRC; very long (20–90 h).
- [x] **Carbonitriding:** C + N diffusion at 700–850 °C; middle ground between carburizing and nitriding.
- [x] **Boriding:** boron diffusion; very high hardness (1400–2000 HV); used for dies.
- [x] **Shot peening:** mechanical, imparts **compressive residual stress** → excellent for fatigue.
- [x] **Fick's 1st law:** $J = -D \, \partial C/\partial x$.
- [x] **Arrhenius diffusivity:** $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$.
- [x] **Fick's 2nd law solution (semi-infinite, constant surface conc.):** $(C_s - C_x)/(C_s - C_0) = \text{erf}(x / 2\sqrt{Dt})$.
- [x] **Parabolic growth:** case depth $\propto \sqrt{t}$ — doubling time increases depth by $\sqrt{2}$.
- [x] **Induction skin depth:** lower frequency → deeper heating; higher frequency → shallower heating.
- [x] **Hard case + tough core = optimum engineering** — surface resists wear, core absorbs impact.
- [x] **Effective Case Depth (ECD):** depth to 50 HRC (most common definition).
- [x] **Total Case Depth (TCD):** depth to core composition.
- [x] **Carburizing vs. nitriding** key contrast: high-T + quench + plain low-C steel vs. low-T + no-quench + alloy steel with nitride formers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonics:**
> - "**C**arburizing uses **C**arbon at high T, needs **C**arbon-rich furnace" (3 C's).
> - "**N**itriding is **N**ot-quenched, **N**itrogen, **N**itralloy" (3 N's).
> - "**Frequency goes up = Depth goes down**" (induction).
